VPM-75.measurement_goal: Measure coverage, detection, prioritization, and remediation of technical vulnerabilities: percent of assets scanned/covered, count of open vulnerabilities by CVE and severity, and remediation velocity (MTTR) against policy-defined targets and ownership assignments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (managing vulnerabilities and ensuring remediation coverage), so percentage metrics (coverage, percent remediated within policy) best show compliance; counts and trends supplement with raw vulnerability volumes and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and status of vulnerability findings (grouped by CVE and severity) per asset and asset tier; percentage of assets scanned and percentage of vulnerabilities remediated within policy timeframes by severity; remediation ticket timestamps and asset owner assignment to demonstrate tracking and timely closure; scan cadence and scan success/failure records to show ongoing vulnerability identification.
data_signals: vulnerability scan execution timestamps and scan results (CVE identifiers)
vulnerability severity (CVSS score) and classification per finding
open vulnerability count by asset, by severity, and by age
patch/application update status per asset (package/version inventory)
remediation ticket creation and closure timestamps and assignee (ticketing system)
scan coverage ratio vs. authoritative asset inventory
overdue remediation count relative to policy-defined remediation windows
asset owner / responsibilities mapping (asset-to-owner registry)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, frequent vulnerability scan logs correlated with asset inventory and ticketing timestamps are strong evidence.
Remediation tickets with assignee and closure timestamps tied to the vulnerability finding are strong; manual email threads or one-off pen-test reports without tracking are weak.
Consistent scan coverage over time and decreasing open vulnerability counts by severity strengthen evidence; single point-in-time scans are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Management of technical vulnerabilities' implies proactive prevention via identification and remediation (management and tracking) rather than purely post-facto detection.

VPM-75.differentiation_note: This control specifically emphasizes operational management and tracking of technical vulnerabilities (scanning, prioritization, remediation and ownership) rather than related topics in the risk set like contact maintenance, project planning, or remote-work controls; measurement must therefore focus on scan coverage, CVE/severity tracking, remediation SLAs, and asset ownership mappings. (Control description was minimal; taxonomy infers typical vulnerability program measurements.)

AC-1.measurement_goal: Measure existence, approved status, currency (last review date), dissemination, and implementation mapping of the access control policy and associated procedures; and the proportion of systems/process owners that have acknowledged or adopted the procedures against the organizational policy requirement.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AC-1 is a preventive governance control concerned with coverage and currency of policy and procedures; percentage metrics (e.g., percent of systems with mapped procedures or percent of owners who have acknowledged policy) directly show coverage. Counts and trends help show scope and review cadence over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Presence of a formal access control policy document and associated procedures with approval metadata, the date of last review, list of distribution/recipients or attestation records, and mapping between policy statements and implemented technical controls (systems/assets). Also measure number and proportion of systems or owners with signed/recorded acknowledgement or procedural adoption.
data_signals: policy document version and approval record (document name, version, approver, approval timestamp)
policy/procedure last review timestamp and scheduled next review date
policy distribution list or attestation/acknowledgement records by role or system owner
procedure implementation mapping matrix linking policy statements to systems/controls
number of systems/assets with documented procedures vs. total in asset registry
exceptions or waiver records referencing the access control policy
training/comms logs showing dissemination of policy changes (email/learning completion timestamps)
audit findings or compliance assessment results referencing policy adherence
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal document repository entries with approval signatures and machine-readable metadata, automated attestation records, and a maintained mapping between policy statements and system implementations with last-review dates.
Weak: single point-in-time screenshots, email chains without approval metadata, or undocumented verbal approvals; absence of mapping between policy and technical controls weakens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Policy and Procedures' indicates a governance/preventive control focused on establishing rules and processes prior to access activity.

AC-1.differentiation_note: This entry focuses specifically on policy governance (existence, approval, review, dissemination, and mapping to systems) rather than operational enforcement metrics (e.g., authentication logs) which would be covered by other access-related controls.

AC-2.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and timeliness of account lifecycle activities: percent of accounts with valid owners and last activity, percent of provisioning/deprovisioning actions completed within policy timeframes, and count of orphaned or inactive accounts requiring remediation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Account management is a preventive control focused on coverage and correctness (e.g., percent of accounts compliant with lifecycle policy); supplemental counts and trends show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of user accounts with a recorded owner/steward and last-activity timestamp; count and percent of account creations and disablements performed within policy timeframes; list and count of orphaned accounts (accounts without owner or with terminated owners).
data_signals: user account creation timestamps
user account disablement timestamps
account owner/steward mapping records
last login / last activity timestamp per account
access review completion records and timestamps
orphaned account list (accounts without owner or stale owner)
active account count by account type (user/system/privileged)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated account lifecycle event logs (creation/disable with timestamps and actor) are strong evidence; periodic manual spreadsheets without machine timestamps are weak.
Complete owner/steward mappings and machine-readable access-review records are strong; missing owner fields or ad-hoc reviews are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Account Management' describes preventative lifecycle and coverage activities (provision/deprovision/access review).

AC-2.differentiation_note: High-level lifecycle and review control focused on overall account coverage and ownership, rather than automation, privileged specifics, or session policies covered by siblings.

AC-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of system/service accounts managed by automated lifecycle processes: percentage under automated provisioning/deprovisioning, count of manual exceptions, and time-to-disable for stale system accounts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated system account controls are preventive and best measured as coverage percentages (what percent of system accounts are under automation); counts and trends show exception volumes and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of system/service accounts that have lifecycle events (provision/modify/disable) executed by automation tooling with timestamps; number of system accounts flagged as manually managed; mean time between detection of stale system account and automated disablement.
data_signals: system account provisioning logs showing automation tool as actor
system account disablement timestamps and actor (automation vs manual)
automation run/execution receipts for account lifecycle actions
list of system accounts flagged as manual-exception
stale system account detection events
automation error/failure logs for account operations
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automation execution logs with deterministic actor fields and timestamps are strong evidence; ad-hoc manual change tickets or screenshots are weak.
Low manual-exception count and consistent automated disable events for stale accounts strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The parenthesized title 'Automated System Account Management' implies preventive automation of system account lifecycle.

AC-2(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on system/service accounts and whether lifecycle is automated, unlike AC-2 which covers all accounts at a policy level.

AC-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure issuance and expiry enforcement for temporary and emergency accounts: count of temporary/emergency accounts created, percent with expiration timestamps enforced, and mean time to automatic removal after expiration.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive (controls issuance and lifetime of temporary/emergency accounts); percent of temp/emergency accounts with enforced expirations best shows coverage, with counts indicating volume and trends showing improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of temporary/emergency accounts created with an expiration timestamp and automated deprovisioning event; count of temporary/emergency accounts active beyond their expiration and count of emergency accounts created without required approval records.
data_signals: temporary/emergency account creation timestamps
expiration timestamp field per temporary/emergency account
automated deprovisioning events for expired temporary accounts
approval/justification records for emergency account creation
overdue temporary account list (expired but still active)
manual exception records for temporary/emergency accounts
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated expiry enforcement logs and automated deprovision events are strong evidence; manual follow-up emails or unchecked spreadsheets are weak.
Presence of approval records at creation and automated expiry enforcement increases evidence strength.

affinity_keywords: Automated Temporary and Emergency Account Management

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title references managing temporary and emergency accounts (issuance and expiry), a preventive lifecycle control enforced by automation.

AC-2(2).differentiation_note: Targets short-lived and emergency accounts with expiration and approval enforcement, distinct from general system or privileged account management.

AC-2(3).measurement_goal: Measure responsiveness of disablement actions: mean time to disable accounts after termination/request, percent disabled within SLA, and count of active accounts belonging to terminated or departed users.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Disablement is a corrective action; response speed (rates/MTTR) is the primary auditor concern, while percent within SLA and counts provide complementary coverage information.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Mean time between account disablement-triggering event (e.g., HR termination timestamp or deprovision request) and account disablement timestamp; percent of disablements completed within SLA; count of accounts still active after termination beyond SLA.
data_signals: termination / deprovision request timestamps (HR or ticketing system)
account disablement timestamps and actor
SLA target for disablement by event type
list of accounts active for terminated/departed users
disablement exception or backlog records
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated correlation of HR termination events to automated disablement with timestamps is strong; reliance on manual emails or unsynchronized spreadsheets is weak.
Low number of accounts active beyond SLA and consistent MTTR under SLA indicate strong evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The specific focus on 'Disable Accounts' implies corrective action in response to termination or events, where response time (MTTR) is key.

AC-2(3).differentiation_note: Specifically measures responsiveness of deprovision/disable actions and SLA adherence, unlike sibling controls that focus on automation, privileged accounts, or session policies.

AC-2(4).measurement_goal: Measure volume and coverage of automated audit actions for account events: count of automated audit alerts for create/modify/disable events, percent of audit alerts triaged/acknowledged, and false positive rate of automated audit rules.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control (automated audit actions); counts of audit events/alerts and their triage rates are the primary measurable indicators of detection coverage and effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of automated audit events for account lifecycle changes (create/modify/disable), timestamps of generated alerts, acknowledgement/triage timestamps, and classification of alerts (true positive/false positive).
data_signals: audit logs for account create/modify/disable with actor and timestamp
automated audit alert generation timestamps and rule identifiers
alert acknowledgement/triage timestamps and analyst IDs
alert disposition labels (true positive, false positive, benign)
audit rule coverage matrix mapping rules to account event types
alert volume over time (daily/weekly)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous machine-generated audit logs with alert rule identifiers and triage records are strong evidence; ad-hoc manual log reviews without timestamps are weak.
Low false positive rate and high triage/acknowledgement within target windows strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Automated Audit Actions' describes detection/monitoring of account events and forwarding/alerting, a classic detective control.

AC-2(4).differentiation_note: Focused on automated detection/audit of account events and alerting/triage metrics, unlike other controls that emphasize lifecycle automation or policy enforcement.

AC-2(5).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement of inactivity logout settings: percent of applications/systems enforcing configured inactivity logout, count of sessions terminated due to inactivity, and number of sessions exceeding inactivity thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Inactivity logout is a preventive configuration; percent of systems/applications with enforced timeout settings indicates coverage, while counts show actual session terminations and policy violations.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of managed applications/systems with configured inactivity logout values that match policy; count of session logout events generated by inactivity timeouts; count of sessions that exceeded the inactivity threshold without logout.
data_signals: session start and end timestamps per user session
logout events labeled as inactivity timeout
configured inactivity timeout value per application/system
session events where idle time exceeded policy threshold
application/system configuration inventory indicating timeout settings
exceptions or waiver records for inactivity logout
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-logged session events with explicit inactivity-logout reason and configured timeout values are strong evidence; screenshots of settings or one-off tests are weak.
High percentage of systems with enforced timeouts and low count of sessions exceeding thresholds strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Inactivity Logout' indicates preventive enforcement of session timeouts to prevent unauthorized access from idle sessions.

AC-2(5).differentiation_note: Specifically measures session timeout enforcement and actual inactivity-triggered logouts, not general account lifecycle or privilege controls.

AC-2(6).measurement_goal: Measure how privileges are adjusted dynamically: percent of privilege elevations/de-escalations executed by dynamic/JIT controls vs manual, mean time to revoke elevated privileges, and count of manual privilege overrides.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Dynamic privilege management is primarily preventive (ensuring privileges are granted only as needed); percentage coverage of automated JIT/dynamic mechanisms best evidences correct enforcement, with rates showing volume and reversions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of privilege elevation/de-escalation events performed by dynamic/automated mechanisms with timestamps and automatic revoke times; count of manual privilege grants and manual override incidents; mean time between revoke-scheduled and actual revoke.
data_signals: privilege elevation request logs with actor and timestamp
dynamic/JIT grant issuance and automatic revocation timestamps
manual privilege grant/change tickets and timestamps
privilege rollback/revoke events
list of manual overrides and justification records
policy mapping of privilege tiers to dynamic controls
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated JIT grant records with automatic revoke timestamps are strong; manual ticket entries without automatic revoke evidence are weak.
Low manual override count and fast automatic revocation strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The term 'Dynamic Privilege Management' implies preventative, just-in-time privilege enforcement to minimize standing privileges.

AC-2(6).differentiation_note: Addresses dynamic/temporary privilege changes and automated revocation specifically, rather than static privileged account inventory or lifecycle automation.

AC-2(7).measurement_goal: Measure control and monitoring of privileged user accounts: percent of privileged accounts with required controls (MFA, least-privilege role), completeness of privileged account inventory, and count of privileged accounts with no owner or with anomalous activity.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Privileged account management is preventive — auditors need coverage metrics (percent of privileged accounts meeting required controls) while counts and trends show volume and behavioral anomalies.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of accounts classified as privileged that have enforced controls (MFA enabled, role/labelling correct), completeness of privileged account inventory versus authoritative source, and count of privileged accounts without owners or with no recent access-review evidence.
data_signals: privileged account inventory with role labels
MFA enablement status per privileged account
privileged account owner/steward mapping
privileged account last-login / last-activity timestamp
privileged access review records and completion timestamps
privileged session monitoring/recording logs (where applicable)
evidence_strength_indicators: Authoritative privileged account inventory and automated control status checks (MFA, role) are strong evidence; ad-hoc lists or unchecked designations are weak.
Regular, machine-recorded access-review completions and low counts of unmanaged privileged accounts strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Privileged User Accounts' indicates preventive controls to restrict and monitor high-privilege accounts.

AC-2(7).differentiation_note: Concentrates on privileged accounts and control/monitoring specific to high-risk identities, distinct from system or temporary account controls.

AC-2(8).measurement_goal: Measure the extent and correctness of dynamic account management: percent of account lifecycle events triggered automatically by identity attribute/policy changes, count of exceptions, and time from attribute change to lifecycle action.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Dynamic account management is preventive/automation-focused; percent coverage of lifecycle actions executed dynamically best demonstrates correct enforcement, with counts and trends for exception handling and velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of provisioning/deprovisioning/attribute-driven account actions executed by automation triggered by identity attribute or policy change; count of lifecycle exceptions and mean time from trigger (attribute change) to lifecycle action.
data_signals: attribute change events (HR/identity source) with timestamps
automated provisioning/deprovisioning event logs and timestamps
policy-trigger mappings linking attributes to lifecycle actions
exception/override records for dynamic actions
orphaned account list (accounts not updated after attribute change)
evidence_strength_indicators: End-to-end automated event chains (attribute change → automation execution → lifecycle event logged) are strong evidence; manual reconciliation or delayed batch updates are weak.
Low exception counts and short time from trigger to action strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Dynamic Account Management' implies preventative automation of account lifecycle based on dynamic attributes/policies.

AC-2(8).differentiation_note: Emphasizes attribute- or policy-driven automation of account lifecycle events, differing from static automation of system accounts or temporary account policies.

AC-2(9).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement of restrictions on shared/group accounts: percent of shared/group accounts that are authorized and documented, count of unauthorized shared account usage events, and percent of shared accounts with accountable owners.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Restrictions on shared/group accounts are preventive; percent-authorized coverage of shared accounts shows adherence to restrictions, with counts of unauthorized uses and distribution of use patterns as supporting detective metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of shared/group accounts that have documented authorization/approval and assigned owner; count of login events using shared/group accounts and count of unauthorized or unapproved shared account uses.
data_signals: shared/group account inventory with owner/approval metadata
approval/authorization records for shared/group account use
login/authentication events attributed to shared/group accounts
exceptions/waivers for shared account usage
session logs showing actions performed by shared/group accounts
anomalous or unauthorized usage alerts for group/shared accounts
evidence_strength_indicators: Authoritative shared-account inventory linked to approval records and machine-logged authentication events is strong evidence; informal knowledge or undocumented shared credentials are weak.
Low unauthorized-use counts and high percentage of shared accounts with owners/approvals strengthen evidence.

affinity_keywords: Restrictions on Use of Shared and Group Accounts

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title calls out 'Restrictions on Use of Shared and Group Accounts', indicating preventive restrictions and authorization requirements.

AC-2(9).differentiation_note: Focuses on restrictions, authorization and monitoring of shared/group accounts (who may use them and whether usage is approved), unlike sibling controls that target automation, privileged accounts, or session enforcement.

AC-2(10).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of shared and group accounts that have had their credentials changed according to the organization's credential-change schedule and identify accounts overdue for credential change, including the method and actor of the change.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control about compliance with credential-change policy (coverage %) — auditors need percent of compliant accounts; counts of overdue accounts and trends over time help show remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of shared/group accounts with a credential-change event within the policy interval (e.g., last 90 days), plus per-account last_change_timestamp, change_method (automated vault rotation vs manual reset), actor identity, and list of accounts with no change beyond policy interval.
data_signals: inventory of shared and group account identifiers (account_id, account_type)
credential change event logs (timestamp, account_id, actor, change_method)
last_credential_change_timestamp per account
credential rotation/vault job execution records (job_id, success/failure, timestamp)
credential-change policy/configuration (required interval in days)
failed rotation attempts and error reasons
exceptions/waivers for credential-change policy (account_id, approver, expiry)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated vault rotation job logs with immutable timestamps and job IDs are strong evidence; ad-hoc screenshots or one-off manual resets are weak evidence.
Complete shared/group account inventory mapped to change events is strong; partial inventory or unknown shared accounts is weak.
Records that include actor identity and change_method (automated vs manual) strengthen evidence versus anonymous or aggregated counts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrasing 'Credential Change' for shared/group accounts indicates a preventive enforcement of credential hygiene (proactive credential rotation).

AC-2(10).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on credential rotation/change for shared and group accounts (inventory + rotation events) rather than individual user account management.

AC-2(11).measurement_goal: Measure how consistently account usage conditions are enforced: percentage of sessions that met declared usage conditions (e.g., allowed sources, MFA, session constraints) and count of usage-condition enforcement events (denials/exceptions) over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive enforcement control — auditors need a compliance percentage (sessions compliant with usage conditions); counts of noncompliant sessions and trends provide detective context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of access sessions evaluated against usage-condition rules that passed (e.g., MFA present, source allowed, time-of-day) versus total sessions; logs of policy evaluation decisions, denials, exceptions (with approver and expiry), and configuration of the stated usage conditions.
data_signals: usage conditions policy artifact or configuration (rules: allowed sources, MFA requirement, session constraints)
access/session logs with attributes (session_id, account_id, source_ip, geolocation, timestamp, MFA status)
policy-evaluation/authorization decision logs (permit/deny/exception, rule matched)
exception approvals and expiry records (account_id, approver, justification, expiry)
count of enforcement actions (denials) and their reasons
configuration deployment records for usage-condition enforcement (policy applied to which systems)
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time policy-evaluation logs that tie each session to a permit/deny decision and the rule used are strong evidence; a static policy document without enforcement logs is weak.
Coverage mapping showing which systems have the usage conditions enabled is strong evidence; absence of deployment records is weak.
Exception records with approver and expiry make evidence credible; undocumented exceptions or verbal approvals are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Usage Conditions' implies preventive constraints on how accounts may be used and requires enforcement of conditions at access time.

AC-2(11).differentiation_note: This control is about enforcement of allowed-use rules per session (policy evaluation and enforcement) rather than credential lifecycle or anomaly detection.

AC-2(12).measurement_goal: Measure detection coverage and effectiveness for atypical account usage: count of atypical-usage alerts per period, percent of accounts monitored, alert-to-investigation conversion rate, and average time from alert to investigation/closure.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective controls require event/alert counts (volume of atypical usage detections); rates (alerts per 1k sessions) and trends over time show detection effectiveness and reduction in anomalies.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of atypical-usage/anomaly detection alerts mapped to account_id and timestamp, percent of active accounts with monitoring coverage, number and timestamps of alerts escalated to investigation tickets, and mean time from alert creation to investigation/closure.
data_signals: authentication and session logs (timestamp, account_id, device, source_ip, geolocation, session_duration)
anomaly detection alerts (alert_id, account_id, alert_type, score, timestamp)
baseline user behavior profiles or models (profile_id, training_period, feature sets)
alert investigation/ticket records (ticket_id, alert_id, investigator, status, timestamps)
monitoring coverage list (which accounts/systems have anomaly detection enabled)
false positive classification labels for alerts (true_positive/false_positive)
evidence_strength_indicators: Immutable alert logs with linked investigation tickets and dispositions (TP/FP) are strong; periodic manual reviews without linked tickets are weak.
Complete mapping showing which accounts have monitoring (coverage %) is strong; unknown coverage is weak.
Presence of scored anomaly outputs and model baselines (to explain why alert triggered) is strong; black-box alerts without context are weaker.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Account Monitoring for Atypical Usage' explicitly describes a detective capability to observe and flag anomalous account activity.

AC-2(12).differentiation_note: This control is focused on continuous anomaly detection and monitoring coverage for atypical account behavior rather than policy enforcement or credential lifecycle.

AC-2(13).measurement_goal: Measure the rate and timeliness of disabling accounts for identified high-risk individuals: percent of identified high-risk accounts disabled within policy timeframes and average time from risk identification to disable action.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is an action-oriented control; auditors need rates (disable actions per identified high-risk individual) and timeliness (how quickly disables occur), with percentage coverage as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percent of high-risk individuals identified that had associated accounts disabled within the policy time window, per-account disable timestamps, identification-to-disable time delta, re-enable events, and any approval/exception records.
data_signals: high-risk individual identification records (risk_id, account_id, identification_timestamp, reason)
account disable action logs (action_id, account_id, actor, timestamp, reason)
policy defining required timeliness for disabling high-risk accounts (time-to-disable SLA)
re-enable/reactivation logs (account_id, timestamp, approver)
exceptions or approved delays (account_id, approver, justification, expiry)
incident or risk-case tickets linking identification to disable action
evidence_strength_indicators: Immutable disable action logs linked to the identifying risk record and with timestamps are strong evidence; verbal confirmations or undated spreadsheets are weak.
Documentation of SLA/time-to-disable and consistent measurement against it is strong; lack of SLA makes timeliness assessment weak.
Records showing re-enable approvals and expiry demonstrate controlled handling; undocumented re-enables are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The action 'Disable Accounts for High-risk Individuals' describes a responsive corrective activity to mitigate identified risk by disabling accounts.

AC-2(13).differentiation_note: Unlike routine account policy enforcement, this control measures responsive disable actions tied to identification of high-risk individuals and their timeliness.

AC-3.measurement_goal: Measure enforcement coverage and effectiveness of access control decisions: percent of authorization decisions enforced by policy configurations, count of unauthorized access attempts blocked, and number of enforcement misconfigurations detected per period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Access enforcement is primarily about coverage/compliance (percent of access governed by enforceable controls); counts and rates (blocked attempts, enforcement errors) are useful secondary signals to show event volumes and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of access control decisions executed by enforced policy (enforced allow/deny), count of denied unauthorized attempts, and count of policy enforcement configuration items mapped to in-scope systems; include timestamps and system identifiers so queries can group by system and time window.
data_signals: access decision logs (allow/deny) with user, resource, action, timestamp
access control policy/configuration exports (ACLs, PDP/PIP/PAP rules)
unauthorized access attempt logs and alert records
enforcement exceptions and approved overrides with approver and expiration
system inventory mapping to enforcement mechanisms (which systems enforce AC policies)
configuration drift / misconfiguration scan results for access controls
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous enforcement logs and machine-readable policy exports (strong) vs. ad-hoc screenshots or manual attestations (weak)
Correlation of denied attempts to policy rules (strong) vs. counts without mapping to policies (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is about enforcing access decisions and preventing unauthorized access, which is a preventive function.

AC-3.differentiation_note: This is the broad enforcement control focused on overall decision enforcement and coverage; siblings specify particular enforcement mechanisms (e.g., RBAC, MAC) that require separate, mechanism-specific signals.

AC-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of privileged functions that are restricted to authorized accounts and the count of privileged function invocations that lacked required restrictions or approvals during a period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A preventive control focused on restricting privileged functions is best measured by coverage/percentage (how many privileged functions have restrictions) with counts/rates of unrestrained or anomalous privileged invocations as event evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of identified privileged functions that have enforced access restrictions (by role, approval, or technical control), plus the count of privileged function invocations without a matching authorization record, annotated by user and timestamp.
data_signals: inventory of privileged functions and mapping to enforcing controls
privileged account list and assigned privileges with timestamps
privileged-session logs (function invoked, user, timestamp, source)
approval/exception records for privileged function access
alerts for privileged function invocations outside of policy
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated privileged session logs correlated with enforcement policies and approval records (strong) vs. manual lists of privileged functions without invocation telemetry (weak)
Low count of unapproved privileged invocations and 100% mapping of functions to restriction controls (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Restricting privileged functions prevents misuse and is therefore a preventive control.

AC-3(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on privileged functions and their enforced restrictions rather than general access decisions or role mappings.

AC-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure compliance with dual-authorization requirements: percent of transactions or access actions requiring dual authorization that had two distinct authorized approvals and count of single-approval occurrences.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Dual-authorization is a preventive policy; percent compliance (transactions with two approvals) is primary, with counts of non-compliant or exceptional actions as supporting metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of access actions/transactions that required dual authorization and that have two distinct approvers recorded, plus the count of actions with missing or single approvals, including timestamps, approver IDs, and transaction IDs.
data_signals: approval workflow records with approver IDs, timestamps, and transaction IDs
policy mapping indicating which actions require dual authorization
transaction logs showing actions flagged as requiring dual authorization
exceptions/waiver records and rationale
orphaned or single-approval transaction audit entries
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-enforced workflow records showing two distinct approvers correlated to each transaction (strong) vs. retrospective attestation emails or manual approvals recorded in free text (weak)
Low and decreasing trend of single-approval exceptions (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Dual authorization prevents single-person risky actions and is therefore preventive in nature.

AC-3(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures approval workflow compliance for dual-signoff requirements rather than general role or policy enforcement.

AC-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure deployment and enforcement of Mandatory Access Control (MAC) policies: percentage of systems with MAC enabled and percentage of access requests evaluated against MAC labels/policies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: MAC is about ensuring technical enforcement across systems (coverage % is primary); counts of systems and distribution of label assignments help show scope and correctness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope systems with MAC enforcement enabled and configured, plus count of access requests evaluated against MAC labels (user/object labels) with decision outcomes and system identifiers.
data_signals: system configuration inventory indicating MAC enabled/disabled
policy repository of MAC rules and label definitions
access request evaluation logs showing label comparisons and decision (permit/deny)
label assignment records for users and objects
exceptions or disablement tickets for MAC on systems
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated system configuration scans confirming MAC enabled and decision logs correlated to policy rules (strong) vs. manual checklist attestations (weak)
High percent coverage of MAC-enabled systems and low count of MAC exceptions (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: MAC enforces access decisions at the system level to prevent unauthorized flows, a preventive control function.

AC-3(3).differentiation_note: Requires measurement of label-based enforcement and system-level MAC configuration, unlike sibling controls that focus on RBAC or DAC policies.

AC-3(4).measurement_goal: Measure correctness and use of Discretionary Access Control (DAC): percent of resources with DAC entries conforming to owner-defined policies and count of owner-granted accesses that violated policy constraints.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: DAC measurement focuses on coverage and compliance to owner-specified ACLs (percentage) while counts of violations and distribution of ACL sizes provide supporting insight.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of resources with owner-managed ACLs aligned to policy, plus counts of access events where owner-granted privileges exceed allowed scopes, including resource ID, owner ID, grantee ID, and timestamp.
data_signals: resource ACL exports and owner assignments
resource owner policy statements or documented owner rules
access event logs showing grantee, action, resource, timestamp
audit findings of owner-granted excessive privileges
exception/approval records for DAC overrides
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-readable ACL exports matched to owner policy documents and correlated with access logs (strong) vs. paper or email lists of owner grants (weak)
Low count of owner-granted violations and high percent alignment to owner policy (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: DAC is about setting and enforcing owner-level permissions to prevent unauthorized access, which is preventive.

AC-3(4).differentiation_note: Measures owner-managed permissions (ACLs) and owner decisions, distinct from mechanism-specific enforcement like MAC or role mappings like RBAC.

AC-3(5).measurement_goal: Measure classification and protective handling of security-relevant information: percent of security-relevant information items labeled/classified and percent of those protected (encrypted/marked) per policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about labeling and applying protections (coverage %), with counts of unprotected items and distribution by data type useful as secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of identified security-relevant information objects that are classified/labeled per policy and that have required protections applied (e.g., encryption, marking), plus counts of unclassified/unprotected items by repository.
data_signals: data inventory entries tagged as security-relevant with classification labels
DLP/encryption policy application logs showing protection status
file/repository metadata indicating classification and protection flags
exceptions/waiver records for unlabeled or unprotected items
audit logs of access to security-relevant items
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated discovery and label/protection status correlated to data inventory (strong) vs. manual spreadsheets listing classified items (weak)
High percent labeled and protected with decreasing trend of unprotected items (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes handling to prevent disclosure of security-relevant information, which is preventive.

AC-3(5).differentiation_note: Focuses on classification and protective controls for security-relevant data, not on general access enforcement or role assignments.

AC-3(6).measurement_goal: Measure protections applied to user and system information: percent of user/system information stores encrypted or access-restricted per policy and count of unauthorized accesses to those stores.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Protective controls are best expressed as coverage percentages (percent of information stores protected) with counts/rates of unauthorized access as secondary detective indicators.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of user and system information stores that meet protection requirements (encryption at rest/in transit, access restrictions) and counts of access events to those stores flagged as unauthorized, with system identifiers and timestamps.
data_signals: inventory of user/system information stores with protection status (encryption, ACLs)
encryption key management logs and encryption status reports
access logs to information stores with user, action, timestamp
DLP or data access alerts for sensitive information access
exceptions or documented compensating controls for unprotected stores
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated inventory showing protection flags and correlated access logs with low unauthorized access counts (strong) vs. ad-hoc attestations of protection (weak)
Evidence of encryption key usage and management logs (strong)

affinity_keywords: Protection of User and System Information

control_type_classification.reasoning: This control mandates protective measures (encryption, restrictions) to prevent unauthorized access to information, so it is preventive.

AC-3(6).differentiation_note: Targets protection of stored user/system information (encryption/access controls) rather than access model specifics like RBAC or MAC.

AC-3(7).measurement_goal: Measure RBAC implementation and correctness: percent of accounts assigned roles (not direct privileges) per policy, count of role-to-permission mappings violating least privilege, and distribution of users per role.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: RBAC adoption and correctness are coverage-based (percent of accounts using roles) and benefit from distribution metrics (users per role) and counts of violations to identify over-privileged roles.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of user accounts assigned roles rather than direct permissions, count of role-to-permission mappings that exceed policy-defined least privilege, and distribution of user counts per role with timestamps for assignments.
data_signals: identity directory exports: account -> role assignments with timestamps
role definitions and role -> permission mappings
access control lists showing direct privilege assignments (to detect bypass of RBAC)
privilege review findings showing role violations or excessive permissions
role assignment approval records
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-readable directory exports showing role assignments and role-permission mappings (strong) vs. manual spreadsheets or ad-hoc lists (weak)
Decreasing trend of direct privilege assignments and low count of over-privileged roles (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: RBAC structures and role assignments are preventive controls designed to constrain access and enforce least privilege.

AC-3(7).differentiation_note: Measures role assignment and mapping correctness (RBAC) rather than mechanism enforcement or data classification.

AC-3(8).measurement_goal: Measure timeliness and completeness of access revocation: rate of access revocations completed within defined SLA (e.g., hours/days) and mean time to revoke access after termination/role change.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Revocation is corrective (removing access after an event); rates (timely revocation compliance) and MTTR trends are the most useful measures, with percentage coverage as a secondary signal.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate (percent) of revocation actions completed within SLA and mean time-to-revoke measured from triggering event (termination/role change) to access removal, by account and system; include timestamps for event and revocation.
data_signals: HR/system event logs indicating termination, role change, or trigger event with timestamp
access revocation logs with timestamp, actor, and systems affected
ticketing/automation records initiating revocation and completion status
account status changes in identity directory (disabled/deleted) with timestamps
exceptions and approved delays for revocation with rationale
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated revocation records and directory state changes correlated to trigger events with short MTTR (strong) vs. email/manual attestations without timestamps (weak)
High percent of revocations meeting SLA and decreasing MTTR trend (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Revoking access after an event is a corrective action to remediate residual access and reduce risk.

AC-3(8).differentiation_note: Focuses on timeliness and completeness of removing access (MTTR and SLA compliance) rather than on initial access granting or role design.

AC-3(9).measurement_goal: Measure adherence to controlled release processes: percent of releases of protected resources that had required approvals and controls applied, and count of unauthorized or unapproved releases detected.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Controlled release is preventive — percent of releases with required approvals shows process compliance; counts/rates of unauthorized releases are key detective signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of releases of controlled/protected resources that followed the approved release workflow (approvals, masking/encryption, recipient validation) and count of releases lacking required approvals or controls, with timestamps and approver IDs.
data_signals: release request and approval records with approver IDs, timestamps, and checklist items
transfer logs showing data/resource release events with destination and timestamp
controls applied at release (redaction, encryption) recorded in release artifacts
exceptions/waiver records for releases
alerts or incidents related to unauthorized releases
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated workflow records showing approvals and applied controls tied to each release event (strong) vs. ad-hoc communication approving releases without artifacts (weak)
Low and decreasing counts of unauthorized releases and high percent of releases with required controls applied (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Controlled release policies prevent improper disclosure by requiring approvals and controls prior to release, making this a preventive control.

AC-3(9).differentiation_note: Targets the release/disclosure process and artifacts (approvals, applied protections) rather than role assignments, enforcement mechanisms, or revocation processes.

AC-3(10).measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of access-control overrides, the percentage of overrides with an auditable justification and approver recorded, and the time between override occurrence and reviewer/approval to demonstrate audited override controls are applied and reviewed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a detective/audit capability; counts of override events and rates over time show frequency, while percentage of overrides with complete audit records and trends show process adherence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of override events of access controls per system per period, number/percentage of those events with recorded justification, approver ID and timestamp, and median time-to-review for overrides; include source system and control mechanism id to confirm coverage.
data_signals: override event logs with timestamp and control mechanism identifier
override justification text/field
override approver ID and approval timestamp
related session or transaction ID
review/audit log entries referencing override event IDs
indicator flags for whether the override was pre-approved or emergency
change or exception ticket IDs linked to overrides
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous machine-generated override logs with immutable timestamps and approver IDs are strong;
Periodic manual spreadsheets or ad-hoc email approvals without linkage to event IDs are weak;
Presence of review entries that reference specific override IDs and include reviewer conclusions is strong evidence of audited review.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Audited Override' indicates a monitoring/audit function to detect and record overrides rather than preventing them.

AC-3(10).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on logging, approval and review of overrides to access controls (audit trail and timeliness), unlike sibling controls that address access model or enforcement coverage.

AC-3(11).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of data stores and resources containing specific classified information types that have access restrictions applied, the count of access attempts denied per information type, and the number of exceptions granted to demonstrate enforcement of restrictions by information type.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control verifying coverage: percentage coverage of resources mapped to information-type-specific rules is the primary signal, with counts of denied accesses and exception trends as secondary signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets/resources that are tagged with an information type and have a corresponding access control rule applied; count of denied access requests grouped by information type and count of granted exceptions with justification and approver.
data_signals: data inventory entries with information type/classification labels
access control policy mappings linking information type to enforcement rule
ACL/policy application records showing enforcement status per resource
access request/authorization logs indicating deny/allow by information type
exception/waiver tickets referencing information types with approver and expiry
resource coverage report showing untagged or unprotected resources
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated data classification and policy-to-resource enforcement mapping showing per-resource enforcement is strong;
Manual lists of sensitive resources without programmatic linkage to enforcement rules are weak;
Low counts of denied requests for a protected information type may be weak unless accompanied by high classification and enforcement coverage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The wording 'Restrict Access to Specific Information Types' describes applying access restrictions (preventive enforcement) by data type.

AC-3(11).differentiation_note: Targets enforcement tied to information classification labels and per-information-type policy coverage, rather than model type or identity mapping addressed by siblings.

AC-3(12).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of applications configured to assert identity and enforce authorization decisions, count of application-level access enforcement failures, and time-to-fix for enforcement misconfigurations to demonstrate application-level access controls are asserted and enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage metrics (percentage of applications asserting/enforcing access) best show whether applications enforce access controls; counts of failures detect gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope applications with assertion (authentication) integrated and active authorization enforcement configured; count of application sessions where authorization was not applied or returned errors and remediation tickets created with timestamps.
data_signals: application configuration records indicating assertion/enforcement enabled
authentication/authorization decision logs (PDP/PAP) per application
SSO/OAuth/OpenID Connect integration status per app
application error logs showing missing authorization checks
access denied vs allowed counts at application layer
remediation/change tickets for app enforcement fixes with resolution timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-readable app configuration and decision logs showing consistent authorization decisions per request are strong;
Developer attestations without supporting enforcement logs are weak;
Low error/unauthorized counts plus 100% of apps reporting enforcement enabled is strong evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Assert and Enforce Application Access' describes implementation/configuration activities that prevent unauthorized access at the application layer.

AC-3(12).differentiation_note: Specifically measures application-layer assertion/enforcement coverage and failures, unlike sibling controls that address data-type restrictions or access models.

AC-3(13).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of protected resources governed by attribute-based access control (ABAC) policies, the distribution of attributes used in decisions, and the count of authorization decisions that referenced attributes to prove ABAC is implemented and effective.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: ABAC is a preventive configuration and coverage problem; percentage coverage of ABAC policy application is primary, while distributions of attributes and counts of attribute-driven decisions help validate operation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of governed resources with ABAC policies applied, count of authorization decisions that evaluated attribute-based rules, list and coverage of attributes used (subject, resource, environment) and the frequency each attribute is present in decisions.
data_signals: ABAC policy repository entries and enforcement mappings to resources
authorization decision logs showing attribute names and values used
attribute source/system inventory (LDAP/IDP/HR) with timestamps
count of decisions falling back to non-ABAC rules
policy-to-resource coverage report indicating unprotected resources
evidence_strength_indicators: Decision logs that include attribute values and policy IDs for each authorization are strong evidence;
Policy definitions without decision logs or attribute sources are weak;
High percentage of resources with ABAC policies plus consistent attribute population is strong.

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'Attribute-based Access Control' is a configuration/architecture control intended to prevent unauthorized access via attribute-driven policies.

AC-3(13).differentiation_note: Focuses on attribute policy coverage and decision logs for ABAC specifically, instead of generic access enforcement or model choices.

AC-3(14).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of active accounts mapped to individual identities (non-shared), count of shared or generic accounts, and time-to-deprovision for accounts after role changes or termination to demonstrate individual access is enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about unique individual mapping and coverage; percentage of accounts uniquely mapped is the key coverage metric, with counts of shared accounts and deprovisioning trends as complementary signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of user accounts with a unique individual owner identifier, count of accounts labeled as shared/generic, last authentication timestamp for each such account, and average time from termination or role change to deprovisioning.
data_signals: identity directory records with owner/user-id attribute
account type flags (service/shared/individual) in provisioning system
authentication logs with user identifiers and timestamps
provisioning/deprovisioning ticket timestamps and actor IDs
roster of orphaned/stale accounts with last login
HR termination/change events correlated to account status
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated directory records tying each account to a single person plus audit trails for provisioning/deprovisioning are strong;
Manual spreadsheets or undocumented shared accounts are weak;
Rapid deprovisioning times after HR events and near-zero shared accounts indicates strong control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Individual Access' implies enforcing per-individual accounts and preventing shared accounts — a preventive identity control.

AC-3(14).differentiation_note: Unique focus on per-account individual ownership and deprovisioning metrics, unlike siblings that focus on policy models or attribute/policy enforcement.

AC-3(15).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of resources that are configured under the correct access control model (Discretionary or Mandatory) per policy, the count of access attempts that violated DAC/MAC policy expectations, and exceptions granted to demonstrate proper application of DAC and MAC.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires verifying correct model configuration across resources; percentage compliance shows coverage while counts of violations and comparisons to expected model assignments validate enforcement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope resources labelled with the intended access-control model (DAC or MAC) and configured accordingly; count of access decisions or violations inconsistent with the assigned model; list of exceptions with justifications and approvers.
data_signals: resource inventory entries with access-control model attribute (DAC/MAC)
OS/configuration files showing ACLs or mandatory labels (e.g., SELinux contexts, label assignments)
access decision logs indicating enforcement mode (ACL vs label enforcement) and violations
exception/waiver records for model misalignment
policy mapping of resource classification to required model
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-checked configuration snapshots showing DAC/MAC settings applied as per policy are strong;
Manual developer assertions without configuration evidence are weak;
Low counts of violations and full labeling/configuration per policy are strong indicators.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control names 'Discretionary and Mandatory Access Control' indicate configuration and enforcement of specific access models, a preventive activity.

AC-3(15).differentiation_note: Requires verifying resource-level access model (DAC vs MAC) and configuration compliance, distinct from sibling controls that focus on overrides, attributes, or application-level assertion.

AC-4.measurement_goal: Measure enforcement coverage and effectiveness of information flow controls: percent of systems and data channels with active flow enforcement, count/rate of blocked unauthorized flows, and number of approved exceptions outstanding.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AC-4 is primarily preventive (enforcing flows), so coverage/compliance percentages are most useful; counts and rates of blocked/allowed flows and trending of enforcement events provide operational visibility.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of relevant systems and data channels with active information-flow enforcement configured, plus counts of allow/deny decisions over a defined period and number of outstanding exceptions/overrides. Measurements should be grouped by system/zone/processing domain and include timestamps for enforcement decisions.
data_signals: information flow enforcement configuration list (system/zone -> enforcement enabled flag)
flow allow/deny decision logs with timestamp, source, destination, protocol
exception/override records with approver and expiry
enforcement rule inventory (rule id, scope, rule text)
blocked flow event count by source/destination over period
flow enforcement deployment inventory (components with enforcement agents)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous allow/deny enforcement logs correlated to an authoritative enforcement-rule inventory and system inventory.
Weak: one-off configuration screenshots, manual statements, or ad-hoc exception emails without timestamped enforcement logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control enforces how information moves (enforcement implies prevention of unauthorized flows).

AC-4.differentiation_note: This base control defines overall enforcement coverage and decision logging; siblings focus on specific enforcement attributes (e.g., attributes, metadata, encryption) that require more granular data signals.

AC-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of objects (files, messages, records) that are tagged with security and privacy attributes and the rate at which those attributes are used in enforcement decisions (allow/deny).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement requires coverage metrics (what percent of objects carry attributes) and event rates showing attributes being evaluated in flow decisions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of objects in scope that have security/privacy attribute metadata applied, count of enforcement decisions that referenced object attributes, and number of attribute-less objects transferred between domains over time.
data_signals: object attribute tag presence (object id -> attribute keys/values)
enforcement decision logs showing referenced object attribute values
count of objects transferred without attributes by source/destination
attribute taxonomy/registry (allowed attribute names and semantics)
attribute application/change timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated enforcement logs that include the exact object attribute values used in each allow/deny decision and a canonical attribute registry.
Weak: manual spreadsheets of tagged objects or periodic lists without logs tying attribute use to enforcement decisions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring object-level attributes to be enforced prevents inappropriate flows by policy at source/target.

AC-4(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on object-level tagging and use of those attributes in enforcement decisions, unlike the base control which focuses on general enforcement coverage.

AC-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure mapping coverage of processing domains and percent of inter-domain flows that are authorized versus denied according to domain-to-domain policies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Processing domain mapping is a coverage/authorization problem best measured as percentage of mapped domains and percent-authorized flows; counts of inter-domain denies provide operational insight.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of systems and assets assigned to processing domains with an authoritative mapping, counts of inter-domain flow attempts and their allow/deny outcomes, and list of domain-to-domain policy rules.
data_signals: processing domain registry (system -> domain)
domain-to-domain policy rule set (authorized flows table)
flow logs labeled by source domain and destination domain with allow/deny
unauthorized inter-domain attempt count and details
domain mapping change history
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative domain registry and continuous domain-labeled flow logs showing policy enforcement.
Weak: undocumented domain mappings or sampling-based domain labels without continuous flow correlation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mapping processing domains and enforcing allowed inter-domain flows prevents unauthorized cross-domain data movement.

AC-4(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures domain mapping accuracy and inter-domain authorization rates, unlike other enhancements which focus on object attributes or metadata.

AC-4(3).measurement_goal: Measure the usage and effectiveness of dynamic information flow control: count/rate of dynamic policy decisions, percent of flows where dynamic attributes influenced the decision, and rate of policy overrides.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Dynamic controls generate event decisions; counts/rates of dynamic policy evaluations and overrides are the most direct evidence, with percentages for coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of dynamic flow-control evaluations per time period, fraction of flow decisions that used dynamic inputs (context, attributes), details of conditions that triggered dynamic decisions, and count of manual overrides.
data_signals: dynamic policy evaluation logs (decision id, inputs, outcome, timestamp)
contextual inputs used (user context, time, location, object attributes) referenced in logs
override/exception records for dynamic decisions
policy trigger definitions and rule versions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: timestamped dynamic policy evaluation logs showing inputs and outcomes correlated to enforcement actions.
Weak: descriptions of dynamic policies without logs of actual evaluations or manual summaries.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Dynamic flow control enforces policy decisions in real time, preventing unauthorized flows based on changing context.

AC-4(3).differentiation_note: Measures real-time policy decisions and use of contextual inputs, unlike static rule coverage in the base control.

AC-4(4).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement controls for encrypted flows: percent of encrypted flows inspected/enforced per policy, counts of encrypted flows allowed/blocked, and coverage of cryptographic handling rules.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control verifying coverage of encrypted flow controls is best expressed as percentage of flows/systems covered; counts of blocked encrypted flows and distribution by cipher provide operational detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of encrypted flows subject to flow-control policy (inspection or handling), counts of encrypted flow allow/deny events, cryptographic parameters seen (cipher, TLS version), and exceptions where encrypted flows bypass control.
data_signals: encrypted flow logs with allow/deny, timestamp, source, destination, cipher/TLS metadata
list of endpoints/systems with encrypted-flow controls enabled
policy rules for handling encrypted information
exception records for encrypted flows bypassing controls
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous encrypted-flow logs showing enforcement decisions and captured cryptographic metadata tied to policy rules.
Weak: policy text alone or periodic scans without per-flow enforcement evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Controlling flow of encrypted information is a preventive enforcement to stop unauthorized data movement even when encrypted.

AC-4(4).differentiation_note: Focuses on handling and inspection of encrypted flows and capturing cryptographic metadata, which requires different signals than plaintext flow controls.

AC-4(5).measurement_goal: Measure detection and enforcement for embedded data types within flows: count of flows containing embedded sensitive types detected, percent of detections blocked or redirected per policy, and classification coverage of embedded types.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detecting embedded data types yields count/rate metrics (how many flows contained specific embedded types) plus percentages for handling outcomes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Counts of flows flagged for embedded data types (e.g., documents with embedded PII), the classification label assigned, and the enforcement outcome (block, quarantine, allow) with timestamps and rule IDs.
data_signals: embedded data type detection logs (flow id, detected types, classifier confidence, timestamp)
enforcement outcome logs for those flows (action taken, rule id)
embedded-type classification taxonomy
false-positive/false-negative review records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated detection logs with classification confidence and correlated enforcement actions; documented review of false positives.
Weak: manual reports of embedded data incidents without machine-detected logs or classifier metadata.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Detecting embedded data types is primarily a detective activity that informs enforcement actions.

AC-4(5).differentiation_note: Targets detection/classification of embedded data within content and links detection outputs to enforcement outcomes, unlike domain-level or attribute-level controls.

AC-4(6).measurement_goal: Measure coverage of metadata tagging and the percent of flow enforcement decisions that reference metadata tags (and their correctness), plus counts of metadata-mismatch denials.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Metadata coverage is a coverage/compliance measure best shown as percentage; counts/distributions of metadata mismatches and denials provide operational detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets and flows tagged with required metadata keys, count of enforcement decisions that referenced metadata tags, and count of flows denied due to missing or incorrect metadata.
data_signals: metadata tag registry and required keys per object type
object/flow metadata presence logs (object id -> tags)
enforcement logs referencing metadata values in decision rationale
mismatch/denial events tied to metadata issues
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: enforcement logs that explicitly include metadata values used for each decision and a canonical metadata registry.
Weak: periodic inventories of metadata without per-decision correlation or manual spot-checks only.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Using metadata to control flows enforces constraints before or during transfer, preventing policy violations.

AC-4(6).differentiation_note: Emphasizes metadata tagging coverage and its direct use in enforcement decisions, distinct from object attributes (AC-4(1)) and embedded content detection (AC-4(5)).

AC-4(7).measurement_goal: Measure inventory and deployment coverage of one-way flow mechanisms (diodes) and count of data transfers attempted that should have used one-way mechanisms but did not.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: One-way mechanism coverage is a preventive coverage metric best expressed as percentage of required channels protected; counts of misplaced transfers are critical operational indicators.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of required uni-directional channels implemented with one-way mechanisms, inventory of deployed one-way devices with configuration status, and count of attempted bi-directional transfers where a one-way mechanism was required.
data_signals: one-way mechanism inventory (device id, location, channel supported)
configuration status and health checks for one-way devices
transfer attempt logs showing directionality and whether one-way mechanism was used
exceptions where one-way mechanism requirement was waived
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative device inventory and continuous transfer logs demonstrating directional enforcement.
Weak: procurement records alone or absence of transfer logs verifying directionality.

control_type_classification.reasoning: One-way mechanisms physically prevent flows in one direction and thus operate as preventive controls.

AC-4(7).differentiation_note: Requires hardware/architecture inventory and directional transfer logs rather than policy or metadata inspection used by other enhancements.

AC-4(8).measurement_goal: Measure the deployment and effectiveness of security/privacy policy filters: percent of enforced filters deployed to required points and rate of filtered flow denials or quarantines matching policy expectations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Filter deployment and coverage are coverage problems (percent deployed); counts/rates of filter matches and enforcement outcomes show operational effect.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of required enforcement points with active security/privacy filters, count of filter matches and resulting enforcement actions (block/quarantine/allow) with rule identifiers, and exception counts.
data_signals: policy filter inventory (filter id, scope, rules)
filter match logs (flow id, filter id, action taken, timestamp)
deployment map of filters to enforcement points
filter rule version history
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous logs tying each filter match to a deployed filter rule and documented deployment map.
Weak: filter definitions without evidence of live matches or inconsistent deployment maps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Policy filters are configured to block or modify flows proactively, which is preventive in nature.

AC-4(8).differentiation_note: Measures filter deployment and live match logs (rule-level enforcement), unlike metadata/attribute tagging which deals with object metadata.

AC-4(9).measurement_goal: Measure frequency and coverage of required human reviews: percent of flagged flows reviewed by humans within required timeframes and counts of corrective actions arising from reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Human review is a detective/corrective activity; review rates (reviews per flagged item) and timeliness are primary indicators, with counts and percentages for coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number and percent of flagged flows that received documented human review within defined SLAs, time-to-review distribution, and count of remediation/corrections performed as a result of review.
data_signals: flagged-flow queue records (flow id, reason flagged, timestamp)
human review logs/approvals (reviewer id, outcome, timestamp)
time-to-review measurements per flagged item
remediation action records resulting from reviews
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: timestamped human review records linked to the original flagged-flow event and documented remediation actions within SLA.
Weak: informal notes or retrospective summaries without linkage of reviewer, timestamp, and original event.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Human reviews detect and validate flows and trigger corrective actions; they are primarily detective with corrective outputs.

AC-4(9).differentiation_note: Focuses on human review timeliness and linkage to flagged flows and remediation, distinct from automated enforcement measures in other enhancements.

AC-4(10).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and timeliness of security/privacy policy filters that are enabled or disabled according to approved change requests and the count of unauthorized filter state changes detected.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily preventive (ensuring correct enable/disable state) so percentage (coverage/compliance) is most useful; counts of state changes and trends over time are useful secondary signals to detect misconfiguration or churn.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of policy filters (by device/interface/application) whose enabled/disabled state matches the approved configuration within the required SLA, plus count of unapproved or out-of-policy state changes with timestamps and responsible actor. Include change request IDs, approval timestamps, and configuration commit records.
data_signals: filter configuration state per policy (enabled/disabled) with timestamp
configuration change logs (who/what/when) for policy filters
change request/approval records referencing filter changes
filter rule deployment status and success/failure logs
unauthorized filter state change alerts
baseline approved filter configuration manifest
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated configuration management records + continuous detection of state drift and approved change links; Weak: manual screenshots or one-off audits without change-traceability

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on enabling/disabling policy filters (configuration enforcement) which is a preventive activity to control information flow.

AC-4(10).differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on the enable/disable state and change governance of policy filters, unlike other AC-4 variants that address detection, metadata, or attribute binding.

AC-4(11).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of policy/traffic filters that are correctly configured per policy templates and the distribution of configuration deviations by device, policy type, and severity.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires measuring configuration correctness and coverage (percent compliant); distributions and counts help prioritize misconfigurations by device/policy.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of filters whose runtime configuration matches the approved policy template; number and distribution of configuration deviations (rule mismatches, missing constraints) by policy and system component. Include configuration diffs, template versions, and remediation tickets.
data_signals: approved policy filter templates and versions
actual filter configuration snapshots per device/system
configuration diff reports (expected vs actual)
configuration audit logs with timestamps and user IDs
configuration drift alerts
remediation/ticket status for configuration deviations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated config scanning with template version reconciliation and tracked remediation tickets; Weak: manual checklists or ad-hoc device reports

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control emphasizes configuration of policy filters (a preventive control ensuring correct enforcement settings).
