AC-4(11).differentiation_note: Unlike AC-4(10) which measures enable/disable state changes, this control measures configuration correctness against templates and detects configuration drift.

AC-4(12).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage of data type identifiers applied to information flows and the count of unidentified or misclassified data flows by system and data sensitivity level.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: ensuring data flows are labeled/identified; percentage coverage shows completeness while counts/distribution show where gaps occur.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of data flows or data objects that have assigned data type identifiers (e.g., PII, PHI, confidential) and the count of flows lacking identifiers or with conflicting identifiers. Include flow source/destination, identifier tag, classification method, and timestamp.
data_signals: data flow inventory with source/destination and ports
data type/classification tags on flows and files
classification tool scan results (file-level and flow-level)
unclassified data flow count by system
metadata tags (sensitivity labels) and last-applied timestamp
mismatched or conflicting identifier alerts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated classification feeds applied in-line with continuous tagging and low unclassified flow counts; Weak: one-time manual labeling or sample-based inventories

control_type_classification.reasoning: Applying data type identifiers is a preventive measure to enforce information handling rules and classify flows before enforcement.

AC-4(12).differentiation_note: This control uniquely requires measuring classification/tagging coverage of data flows, not filter configuration or detection of violations.

AC-4(13).measurement_goal: Measure the extent to which systems and information flows are decomposed into policy-relevant subcomponents (percent of flows mapped to subcomponents) and the count of policy gaps where decomposition is missing.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive/organizational control is best measured via coverage percentage (how many flows/assets have been decomposed) with counts of unmapped items and comparisons to expected architecture.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems and information flows that have been decomposed into policy-relevant subcomponents with mapping artifacts (component ID, policy mapping, owner). Count of flows/systems without decomposition and evidence of policy gaps or exceptions.
data_signals: system/component inventory with decomposition mapping
information flow maps tied to subcomponent identifiers
policy-to-component mapping documents
unmapped flow/system list
owner/maintenance records for subcomponents
architecture change requests and approval logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: maintained mapping repository with automated linkage between flows and policy subcomponents and low unmapped counts; Weak: architectural diagrams without up-to-date mappings or no owner assignment

control_type_classification.reasoning: Decomposing systems into policy-relevant subcomponents prevents ambiguous enforcement boundaries and supports preventive policy application.

AC-4(13).differentiation_note: Focuses on mapping and architecture decomposition for policy application, not on runtime filter configuration or metadata validation.

AC-4(14).measurement_goal: Measure adherence to defined security/privacy policy filter constraints by quantifying the percentage of filters enforcing required constraints and the count of constraint violations observed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percentage compliance with constraint rules is most relevant; counts and trends highlight recurring violations or constraint bypass.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of policy filters configured with mandated constraints (e.g., allowed protocols, rate limits, data redaction rules) and count of filter instances that violate those constraints, with evidence showing device, rule ID, and timestamps.
data_signals: policy constraint definitions (allowed protocols, ports, redaction rules)
filter rule configurations and parameters per device
constraint violation alerts or logs
filter rule test results or simulation logs
remediation tickets for constraint non-compliance
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated assertions/tests verifying constraints in production and low violation counts; Weak: manual checklist reviews or absent runtime verification

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires enforcing policy constraints on filters, a preventive configuration and enforcement activity.

AC-4(14).differentiation_note: This control emphasizes measurement of constraint enforcement (specific rule parameters) rather than general filter configuration or labeling.

AC-4(15).measurement_goal: Measure detection performance by counting unsanctioned information flow events detected per period, and the percentage of detected events that are confirmed and triaged within target timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control (detecting unsanctioned information) so counts of detection events are primary; rates and percentages (triage/true-positive rates) are useful to measure effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of unsanctioned information detection alerts (e.g., sensitive data leaving environment, unauthorized transfers), time-to-triage/confirm for each alert, and percent of alerts validated as true incidents. Include alert details, source/destination, detection rule ID, and investigator disposition.
data_signals: unsanctioned-information detection alerts with timestamps and rule IDs
DLP/IDS/monitoring logs showing source/destination and data patterns
alert triage and disposition records (true/false/false-positive)
time-to-confirm and time-to-contain metrics per alert
policy exceptions or approved transfers list
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous DLP/inspection logs with low false-positive rate and documented triage workflow; Weak: periodic manual log reviews or ad-hoc investigations without timestamps

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name explicitly includes 'Detection of Unsanctioned Information', indicating a detective detection capability.

AC-4(15).differentiation_note: This entry targets runtime detection event metrics (counts, triage times) for unsanctioned flows, unlike configuration or labeling controls.

AC-4(16).measurement_goal: Measure the number and percentage of information transfers between interconnected systems that comply with approved interconnection policies, and count unauthorized or non-compliant transfer events by partner system.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective/preventive hybrid: counting transfer events and unauthorized transfers is primary to detect incidents; percentage compliance indicates preventive posture across interconnections.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of inter-system transfer events (by system pair) and percent of those transfers that match approved interconnection agreements/policy (including allowed ports, protocols, encryption). Include transfer metadata, interconnection agreement ID, and any deviation flags.
data_signals: interconnection agreements and policy definitions
network flow logs (source/destination IP, port, protocol, bytes, timestamp)
transfer audit logs with encryption/authentication metadata
non-compliant transfer alerts (policy violations)
whitelist/approved partner system list
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous network flow telemetry correlated to interconnection policy mappings and automated blocking/alerting; Weak: periodic manual reviews of interconnection agreements without traffic correlation

control_type_classification.reasoning: Focuses on monitoring transfers on interconnected systems to detect non-compliant transfers; therefore largely detective though preventive interconnection agreements exist.

AC-4(16).differentiation_note: This control specifically measures cross-system transfers and their compliance with interconnection agreements, rather than internal filter configuration or metadata.

AC-4(17).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of information flows that are authenticated to an approved domain authentication mechanism and the count of flows failing domain authentication or using weak authentication.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percentage of flows authenticated by approved domain mechanisms (coverage) is primary; counts of failures and distribution by auth method help prioritize remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of flows where domain authentication was performed and succeeded using approved methods (e.g., mutual TLS, Kerberos), plus count of authentication failures, expired credentials, or weak cipher usage. Include auth method, timestamp, source/destination, and failure reason.
data_signals: authentication logs tied to flow events (method, result, timestamp)
TLS/mTLS certificate validation logs (issuer, expiry, cipher suite)
authentication failure counts and error codes
inventory of approved domain authentication mechanisms
exceptions/allowlist for unauthenticated flows
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: flow-level authentication logs correlated with network flows showing near 100% compliance; Weak: spot-checks of auth configurations or lack of linkage between auth logs and flows

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires domain authentication of flows (prevention of unauthenticated flows), a preventive access control mechanism.

AC-4(17).differentiation_note: This control demands measurement of authentication coverage and failures tied to flows, distinct from filter configs or metadata validation.

AC-4(18).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of information objects and flows bound to required security attributes (e.g., owner, classification, handling instructions) and count of flows missing required attribute bindings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percentage coverage for attribute binding shows compliance; counts and distributions reveal gaps by system or data type.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of data objects and flows with required security attribute bindings (classification, owner, handling, retention) and list/count of items missing bindings. Include attribute values, timestamps of last binding operation, and system/component IDs.
data_signals: metadata attribute tags on files/flows (classification, owner, retention)
binding application logs (who/when/what attribute applied)
inventory of required attributes per data type
unbound item lists and counts
policy exceptions or overrides
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated metadata tagging integrated with flows and storage showing high binding coverage; Weak: manual spreadsheets of attributes or intermittent tagging exercises

control_type_classification.reasoning: Binding security attributes to data/flows is a preventive measure to ensure correct handling and enforcement.

AC-4(18).differentiation_note: Focuses on metadata/attribute binding coverage for enforcement rather than filter configuration, authentication, or detection.

AC-4(19).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of information flows whose metadata is validated against schema/policy rules and the count of flows rejected or flagged due to invalid or missing metadata.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive/detective hybrid: percentage of validated metadata indicates preventive coverage; counts of validation failures are important detective signals to trigger enforcement or remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of flows with metadata validated at enforcement points (matching expected schema and required fields), and count of flows flagged/rejected for invalid/missing metadata with reasons. Include metadata schema version, validation timestamp, flow identifiers, and validation result codes.
data_signals: metadata validation logs (schema version, result, error codes)
flows with metadata fields and values (source/destination, tags)
validation failure counts and top failure reasons
policy definitions for required metadata fields
rejection/quarantine events and remediation tickets
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: inline metadata validation with low failure rates, automated quarantine and remediation workflows; Weak: offline validation scripts or sampling without enforcement linkage

control_type_classification.reasoning: Validating metadata before enforcement prevents mis-tagged or unactionable flows, which is primarily preventive.

AC-4(19).differentiation_note: This control uniquely focuses on runtime metadata schema validation and measurement of validation failures versus attribute binding or filter configuration.

AC-4(20).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of information flows that are processed only by 'approved solutions' and the count of blocked or redirected flows that attempted to use unapproved solutions; demonstrate an authoritative inventory of approved solutions and enforcement rule coverage against that inventory.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive enforcement control where auditors need coverage/ compliance (percentage of flows using approved solutions). Counts of unapproved attempts and trending show events over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active information flows that terminate or transit only through items on the approved-solutions inventory (by solution id/version) and the count of blocked/denied flow attempts attributed to unapproved solutions during the reporting period.
data_signals: approved solutions inventory (solution id, version, approval timestamp)
flow enforcement logs (flow id, source, destination, solution id used, timestamp, allow/deny action)
blocked/denied flow events attributed to unapproved solution fingerprints
exceptions/waiver records tied to approved-solutions inventory
configuration state of enforcement points (policy version applied to each enforcement point)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated enforcement logs continuously correlated with an authoritative approved-solutions inventory and no recorded flows through non-approved solutions.
Weak: one-off manual attestations or screenshots of the inventory without correlated enforcement logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title emphasizes enforcing information flows through 'Approved Solutions', a preventive enforcement action restricting allowed solutions.

AC-4(20).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on using an authoritative list of 'approved solutions' as the enforcement reference, unlike siblings that address separation, sanitization, or audit of filters.

AC-4(21).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required information flows that are implemented with the mandated physical or logical separation (segmentation) and count cross-boundary flows that violate the separation policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about enforcing separation/segmentation; auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of flows/segments compliant) and counts of cross-segment violations.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of network/system interfaces and information flows mapped to required separation zones (physical or logical) that are configured and enforced as specified, and the count of observed cross-zone flows that lack the required separation controls.
data_signals: zone/segment inventory (zone id, type: physical|logical, intended flows)
network/firewall/ACL rules mapping flows to zones
VLAN/subnet/routing configuration records
flow logs showing source/destination zone and policy decision
cross-zone flow violation events and alerts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous flow logs correlated to current zone configs showing zero unauthorized cross-zone flows and automated compliance checks of segmentation rules.
Weak: manual network diagrams or infrequent segmentation attestations without telemetry showing actual flow enforcement.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates physical/logical separation of information flows, which is an architectural preventive control to stop unauthorized flow.

AC-4(21).differentiation_note: Specifically measures enforcement of segmentation (physical or logical) and cross-zone violations, rather than solution approval, sanitization, or pipeline orchestration.

AC-4(22).measurement_goal: Measure the rate and count of flows that are restricted to 'access-only' operations versus flows that perform disallowed operations (e.g., modify, exfiltrate), and the percentage of enforcement points configured to restrict flows to access-only actions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: auditors need coverage (% of enforcement points enforcing access-only) and counts/rates of disallowed modification or non-access events.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of enforcement points and active flows configured to allow only permitted access operations (read/list) versus the count of detected flows that attempted disallowed operations (writes, deletes, exfiltration) during the period.
data_signals: enforcement point configuration (operation allowlist per flow)
object access logs with operation type (read, write, delete, execute) and flow id
deny/alert events for attempted non-access operations
exceptions or policy waivers permitting non-access operations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous enforcement logs showing operation types, automatic blocking of disallowed operations, and up-to-date configurations at all enforcement points.
Weak: ad-hoc manual checks that do not include operation-type telemetry or only occasional sampling.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Access Only' implies preventing disallowed operations on flows, a preventive enforcement mechanism.

AC-4(22).differentiation_note: Targets enforcement of permitted operation types on flows (access-only) rather than separation, sanitization, or auditing of filter actions.

AC-4(23).measurement_goal: Measure counts and rates of modification attempts (successful and blocked) against information explicitly marked as 'non‑releasable', and the percentage of non‑releasable items protected by enforcement controls preventing modification.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires counting modification attempts and measuring protection coverage; counts of attempts (and successes) are critical, while coverage % shows preventive completeness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of modification events and attempted modifications against assets/data labeled as 'non-releasable' (by data id) separated into blocked vs. allowed, and percentage of non-releasable items that have active modification-blocking policies applied.
data_signals: data classification labels/metadata indicating 'non-releasable'
modification audit logs (data id, actor, action, timestamp, allow/deny)
enforcement rule bindings mapping non-releasable labels to deny-modify policies
exceptions/waiver entries for permitted modifications
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous correlation of classification metadata with enforcement logs showing zero successful modifications without documented exception.
Weak: periodic manual attestations of classifications without audit logs or records of enforcement actions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control aims to prevent modification of 'non-releasable' data via enforcement, indicating a preventive classification.

AC-4(23).differentiation_note: Specifically ties enforcement to data classification ('non-releasable') and measures modification attempts against that label, unlike sibling controls focused on separation or sanitization.

AC-4(24).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of inbound/outbound information flows that are converted to the mandated internal normalized format and the count of normalization failures or schema validation errors.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive/operational control: auditors want coverage (% normalized) and counts of failures or unnormalized items to detect gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of processed flows that were successfully transformed to the internal normalized format (by flow id) and the count of normalization/schema validation failures with error reasons and affected data ids.
data_signals: data transformation logs (flow id, source format, target normalized format, success/failure, timestamp)
schema/format validation error records (error type, record id)
mapping/version metadata for normalization rules
reconciliation reports showing percentage normalized vs. total processed
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated transformation logs with success/failure flags and per-flow mapping versions correlated to downstream consumers.
Weak: manual notes or one-time conversion reports without per-flow telemetry or failure reasons.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mandating conversion to an internal normalized format is a preventive/transformational enforcement to ensure only normalized data flows proceed.

AC-4(24).differentiation_note: Unique focus on data transformation/format enforcement (normalization) and measurement of conversion success and validation errors, unlike filtering or separation controls.

AC-4(25).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of flows requiring sanitization that were sanitized successfully and the count of residual sensitive data detections after sanitization; track sanitization failures and exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Sanitization is preventive/transformative; auditors need coverage (% sanitized) while counts/rates of failures or residual detections indicate control effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of flows marked for sanitization that completed sanitization successfully (by flow id), number of sanitization failures, and number of post-sanitization scans that still detect sensitive or non-releasable data.
data_signals: sanitization process logs (flow id, transformation applied, timestamp, success/failure)
post-sanitization sensitive-data scan results (DLP findings with text snippets/ids)
original vs sanitized artifact hashes or redaction metadata
exceptions/waiver records for unsanitized flows
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous sanitization logs plus automated post-sanitization DLP scans showing zero residual sensitive data and documented exception handling.
Weak: periodic manual redaction reports without automated post-sanitization verification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Sanitization of information flows is an active preventative control to remove sensitive content prior to release or transit.

AC-4(25).differentiation_note: Focused strictly on the sanitization step and post-sanitization validation (residual sensitive data), unlike siblings that govern separation, operation restrictions, or orchestration.

AC-4(26).measurement_goal: Measure the count and completeness of audit records for filtering actions (create/update/delete filter rules, allow/deny events, filter failures) and the percentage of filter actions that include required audit fields (filter id, action, operator, timestamp).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective/audit-focused control; primary evidence is counts of audit events and completeness (percentage of events with required fields).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of audit log entries generated for filter-related actions in the period, and the percentage of those entries that include required metadata (filter id, action type, actor, timestamp, outcome).
data_signals: filter action audit logs (filter id, action: allow/deny/create/update/delete, actor/user/service, timestamp, outcome)
log integrity/hash records and retention metadata
missing-field/error counts in audit records
audit pipeline delivery success/failure metrics
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, tamper-evident audit logs with complete required fields for every filter action and automated alerting on missing fields.
Weak: sporadic manual audit samplings or logs that lack required metadata fields.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The focus on 'Audit Filtering Actions' is inherently detective — collecting audit records of filter actions to detect and review behavior.

AC-4(26).differentiation_note: Unique auditing/detection focus: measures audit log generation and completeness for filter actions rather than configuration coverage or sanitization.

AC-4(27).measurement_goal: Measure the presence and coverage of redundant and independent filtering mechanisms for critical flows (percentage of critical flows protected by >=2 independent filters) and count of single points of filter failure.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive resiliency control, auditors need coverage measures (% of critical flows with redundant filters) and counts of single-failure points; distribution shows where redundancy exists.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of identified critical information flows that have at least two independent filtering mechanisms applied (by flow id), and the count of flows with only a single filtering mechanism or with known dependencies creating single points of failure.
data_signals: filter topology/inventory (filter id, type, deployment point, dependency mapping)
flow-to-filter mappings showing independent filter chains
failover/failure test logs and results
filter health and availability metrics (uptime, error rates)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system topology records plus successful periodic failover tests and live telemetry showing multiple independent filters active for each critical flow.
Weak: architectural diagrams listing redundant filters without evidence of independence or failover testing.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring redundant/independent filtering mechanisms is a preventive resiliency control to avoid single points of failure.

AC-4(27).differentiation_note: Emphasizes redundancy and independence of filters and requires failover/test evidence, different from controls that focus on single-filter behavior, audit, or sanitization.

AC-4(28).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of filter pipelines implemented as linear ordered pipelines per design and count instances where pipeline order was bypassed or non-linear behavior occurred.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive/operational control: auditors need a coverage percentage of pipelines conforming to the linear design plus counts of bypasses; comparisons against intended pipeline definitions are useful.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of deployed filter pipelines that match the approved linear pipeline definition (ordered filter sequence) and the count of flow traces or events that bypass or execute filters out-of-order.
data_signals: filter pipeline definitions (ordered sequence of filter ids and expected order)
pipeline execution logs / flow traces showing order of filters applied per flow
bypass/skip events and exceptions records
pipeline configuration change history
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: per-flow execution traces correlated to pipeline definitions with zero out-of-order or bypass events and automated drift detection.
Weak: static pipeline diagrams without execution logs or occasional manual checks.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mandating linear ordered filter pipelines is a preventive design/control to ensure filters are applied in sequence.

AC-4(28).differentiation_note: Unique requirement to verify filter execution order per pipeline design and detect bypass/out-of-order events, unlike sibling controls that focus on redundancy, auditing, or sanitization.

AC-4(29).measurement_goal: Measure orchestration engine effectiveness: deployment success rate of filter policies, time-to-deploy policy changes, and count of orchestration failures or reconciliations required.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Orchestration is preventive/operational — primary metric is percentage of successful automated deployments (coverage/consistency), with rates and counts showing velocity/failure frequency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of filter policy changes successfully deployed by the orchestration engine to target enforcement points without manual intervention, average time-to-deploy (change request to applied), and count of failed or reconciled deployments.
data_signals: orchestration engine deployment logs (policy id, target, start/complete timestamps, success/failure)
reconciliation reports (drift detection results, manual fixes)
policy versioning metadata and deployment history
incident records for orchestration failures or rollbacks
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated deployment logs with high success rate, short deployment times, and low reconciliation counts correlated to target enforcement points.
Weak: manual deployment records, ad-hoc scripts, or one-off deployment spreadsheets without machine-generated logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on orchestration engines deploying/enforcing filters, a preventive/configuration control to ensure consistent enforcement.

AC-4(29).differentiation_note: Measures orchestration/deployment success, time-to-deploy, and reconciliation count for filter policies — operational automation metrics not required by other sibling controls focused on policy content or audit.

AC-4(30).measurement_goal: Measure the extent and effectiveness of process-based filter mechanisms applied to information flows: percent of enforced flows covered by multi-process filter chains, count of filter process instances mapped to flows, and the rate of flow decisions (allowed/blocked) attributable to those processes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive enforcement control; auditors need coverage and compliance metrics (coverage % of flows enforced by multi-process filters) plus counts/rates of enforcement actions (blocked/allowed) to validate operation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of active information flows with an associated configured multi-process filter chain, count of filter process instances per flow, number of allowed vs. blocked flow decisions attributed to each filter process, and instances where a flow lacked an assigned process-based filter. Measurements must include flow identifier, assigned process IDs, policy rule IDs, timestamps, and enforcement decision.
data_signals: flow enforcement logs with flow ID, timestamp, enforcement decision (allowed/blocked) and process ID
filter process instance registry (process ID, binary/version, host, start/stop timestamps)
policy rule-to-process assignment table (policy/rule ID -> process ID mapping)
configuration snapshots showing deployed filter chains per flow
inter-process communication audit logs referencing filtered flows
filter configuration version and deployment timestamp
enforcement error/failure logs where filter process failed or bypassed
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous enforcement logs linking each flow decision to a process ID plus automated configuration management records showing policy->process mappings and versioned deployments.
Weak: point-in-time screenshots or annual manual test results without linkage of flow decisions to running process instances.
Strong: consistent filter process start/stop telemetry and low rate of enforcement errors; Weak: frequent unassigned flows or manual overrides recorded only in change tickets.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name 'Information Flow Enforcement | Filter Mechanisms Using Multiple Processes' indicates preventative enforcement (filters) applied to flows to stop undesired transfers.

AC-4(30).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on multi-process filter implementations mapped to flows (process-to-flow enforcement) rather than transfer integrity checks or process policy adherence.

AC-4(31).measurement_goal: Measure prevention of failed or corrupted content transfers by tracking the percentage and count of transfers that were blocked or stopped due to detected transfer failures or corruption, plus the rate of integrity-verification events and prevented-transfer incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A preventive requirement to stop failed content transfers is best evidenced by coverage/percentage metrics (portion of transfers validated/blocked) with counts and rates of blocked or prevented transfers to show operational effect.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of file/data transfer transactions where integrity checks were performed and transfers were blocked on integrity failure; count of blocked failed-transfer events (with transfer ID, source/destination, timestamp, error code), retransmission attempts, and any DLP/content-inspection results tied to blocked transfers.
data_signals: transfer transaction logs with transfer ID, timestamp, source/destination, status (blocked/allowed/completed)
integrity verification results (checksum/HMAC/signature pass/fail) tied to transfer ID
blocked transfer event records and error codes
content-inspection/DLP match events correlated to transfer IDs
retransmission attempt logs and outcomes
encryption status flag for the transfer
incident or alert records indicating prevented corrupted transfers
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated transfer logs that include integrity verification results and explicit blocked-transfer records with full metadata (IDs, timestamps, error codes).
Weak: ad-hoc manual reports or periodic tests without per-transfer integrity results or lacking correlation between integrity checks and blocked outcomes.
Strong: low or zero occurrences of undetected corrupted transfers in monitoring/incident reports; Weak: only summary counts without transfer-level evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Failed Content Transfer Prevention' explicitly indicates a control to prevent (not just detect) failed/corrupted transfers, so it is preventive in nature.

AC-4(31).differentiation_note: Targets prevention of corrupted/failed transfers and integrity verification at the transfer level, rather than the filter chain implementation or general process-policy adherence.

AC-4(32).measurement_goal: Measure adherence to mandated process requirements for information transfers: percent of transfers executed with required process artifacts (approvals, mandated transfer method, required protections) and count of transfers executed outside the required process (exceptions/waivers).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires demonstrating process compliance and coverage (percent of transfers following required processes); counts and trends help show exceptions and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of outbound/inbound transfers where required process artifacts exist and were executed: approval record present (ID/timestamp/approver), mandated transfer channel used, required protection flags (encryption, integrity check), and any waiver/exception records; also count of transfers missing one or more required artifacts.
data_signals: transfer request and approval records (request ID, approver ID, timestamps)
transfer audit trail showing method/protocol used (SFTP/HTTPS/etc.) and protection flags (encryption enabled)
policy compliance evaluation results per transfer (compliant/non-compliant) with rule IDs
exception/waiver records tied to transfer IDs
authorization lists for allowed recipients/destinations correlated to transfer logs
transfer start/end timestamps and elapsed approval-to-transfer time
audit logs showing enforcement or manual override events
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated per-transfer approval and audit records showing required artifacts and enforcement decisions (with timestamps and IDs) across a continuous period.
Weak: only policy documents or one-time attestations without per-transfer audit trails or lacking linkage between approvals and executed transfers.
Strong: low exception counts and improving trend in percent-compliant transfers; Weak: many manual overrides recorded only in ticketing systems without enforcement metadata.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Process Requirements for Information Transfer' implies preventive controls (requirements and enforcement) to ensure transfers follow mandated processes.

AC-4(32).differentiation_note: Emphasizes process and procedural compliance for transfers (approvals, mandated channels, protections) rather than implementation details of filters or integrity-blocking mechanisms.

AC-5.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and count of user identities and accounts that have role/permission assignments which create prohibited separation-of-duties (SoD) conflicts, track exception approvals, and show that automated enforcement or compensating controls prevent unauthorized dual-role combinations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Separation of duties is primarily about coverage/compliance (what percent of accounts violate SoD rules), so percentage is the best primary metric; counts (number of conflicts), rates (exceptions granted per time), and trends (conflicts over time) are useful secondary views to show exposure and remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of active user accounts with combinations of roles/permissions that match the organization's prohibited SoD pairs; number and age of approved SoD exceptions; presence and result of automated enforcement (role-assignment rejection events) and results of periodic SoD attestations. Measurements should be grouped by system/application and by role-criticality.
data_signals: user-to-role assignment records with timestamps
role-to-permission (role-permission) mapping
defined prohibited SoD role-pair list (policy table)
SoD violation detection alerts or reports (automated)
SoD exception/waiver records with approver, justification, and expiry date
role assignment change audit logs (who, when, source system)
periodic attestation results (attestation completion status and findings)
privileged account / privileged role inventory
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, continuous detection events showing zero or policy-compliant number of active SoD conflicts plus up-to-date exception records and enforcement logs.
Moderate: periodic attestation reports combined with role assignment change logs that demonstrate remediation actions within policy timeframes.
Weak: one-time manual spreadsheets or an annual attestation without audit logs or automated detection (these do not demonstrate ongoing enforcement).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Separation of duties is primarily preventive—designed to prevent conflicting role/privilege assignments that enable misuse of functions.

AC-5.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on measuring role/permission conflicts, exception tracking, and enforcement of SoD rules (prevention of dual-role combinations), rather than general access review completeness or authentication controls; due to the input lacking a detailed description, keywords and signals are centered on role assignments and exception records.

AC-6.measurement_goal: Measure enforcement and coverage of least-privilege across accounts and resources: percentage of accounts assigned only required privileges, count of privileged accounts, and number of privilege escalation incidents over the audit period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Least-privilege is a preventive control; auditors want coverage and compliance percent metrics (coverage of policy), with counts and trends for incident/context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of user and service accounts that have privileges scoped to documented role requirements (accounts with any privilege beyond role baseline), total count of privileged accounts, and count of privilege escalation events with timestamps and affected resources.
data_signals: account inventory with assigned role and effective privileges
privileged role definitions / role baseline mapping
privilege escalation events (logs) with user, timestamp, target resource
PAM enrollment/coverage list and timestamps
MFA enablement status for privileged accounts
access request and approval records
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated account entitlement reports and continuous privilege scanning (strong) versus one-off spreadsheets or screenshots of roles (weak).
Correlated logs showing privilege escalation events with remediation tickets (strong) versus anecdotal incident reports (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: AC-6 establishes restrictions on access rights to prevent excessive privileges (least privilege principle), which is preventive in nature.

AC-6.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall least-privilege program and coverage metrics, while child enhancements (AC-6(x)) target specific aspects like logging, reviews, or code-execution privileges.

AC-6(1).measurement_goal: Measure that security functions are only authorized to approved accounts: percentage of security-function accounts explicitly authorized and count of unauthorized attempts to access security functions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement is preventive (authorizing access); percentage coverage of authorized accounts is the most direct proof, with counts of unauthorized attempts as supplementary detective signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of accounts authorized to use security functions (compare actual authorized list to policy-approved list), and counts of denied or unauthorized access attempts to security functions including user, timestamp, and function targeted.
data_signals: approved security-function account list (authorization records)
access control lists (ACLs) for security functions
access attempt logs for security functions (allow/deny, user, timestamp)
security function role-to-account mappings
approval/justification records for each authorization
evidence_strength_indicators: Centralized allowlist of authorized accounts with automated enforcement and real-time deny/allow logs (strong) vs. manual approval emails without enforcement evidence (weak).
Deny logs with correlated change-control approvals for removed access show effectiveness (strong).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires authorization of access to security functions (explicitly preventive—restricting who may perform security actions).

AC-6(1).differentiation_note: Focused specifically on authorization for security functions (who can perform security operations), unlike AC-6 which covers general least-privilege across all resources.

AC-6(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of non-security functions and accounts that operate with non-privileged rights and the count of non-security accounts granted privileged rights improperly.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is preventive: auditors want coverage/percentage metrics showing nonsecurity functions/accounts are configured as non-privileged; counts of exceptions complement that view.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of non-security function accounts whose effective privileges do not exceed non-privileged baseline, and count/list of exceptions where non-security functions have elevated privileges including justification and approvals.
data_signals: catalog of non-security functions and associated accounts
effective privilege lists by account/function
exceptions/waiver records with approvals and timestamps
configuration snapshots showing privilege assignments
access request/approval logs for privilege elevation
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated entitlement scans showing non-security functions mapped to non-privileged roles (strong) vs. manual inventory without privilege validation (weak).
Documented approved exceptions with expirations are stronger than undocumented exceptions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement mandates limiting non-security functions to non-privileged access—an enforced prevention of excessive rights.

AC-6(2).differentiation_note: Targets separation between security and non-security function privileges (ensures nonsecurity functions are non-privileged), rather than overall privileged account management.

AC-6(3).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement of network-level restrictions for privileged commands: count of privileged command executions over network interfaces and percentage of privileged commands blocked or allowed according to network access policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily preventive (restrict network access to privileged commands); percentage enforcement (policy compliance) is most useful, with counts of actual network-executed privileged commands as detective context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of privileged commands that are executed only over approved network paths (or blocked), plus counts of privileged commands executed remotely including source IP, user, command, and timestamp to detect policy violations.
data_signals: network access control policy for privileged commands
network/SSH/RDP session logs with executed commands and user context
firewall/NAC deny/allow logs for privileged ports
privileged command execution audit logs (user, timestamp, target)
approved remote access lists (approved hosts/IPs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Network session logs tied to command audits and enforcement (NAC/firewall denies) are strong; periodic manual attestations without logs are weak.
Low count of remote privileged command executions with documented approvals indicates strong enforcement.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control restricts network access paths for privileged commands to prevent unauthorized remote privileged operations (preventive enforcement).

AC-6(3).differentiation_note: Unique focus on network-level controls and remote execution of privileged commands versus other entries that focus on account or role management.

AC-6(4).measurement_goal: Demonstrate separation of processing domains: percentage of systems/applications where processing domains are isolated per policy and count of cross-domain access events that violate separation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Separation of processing domains is preventive (configuration/enforcement); percentage of systems/computing environments compliant with domain separation policy is the clearest evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems and applications configured with required processing-domain separation (e.g., VLANs, hypervisor/tenant isolation, container namespaces), and count of cross-domain access/communication events that are not allowed by policy.
data_signals: network segmentation/VLAN configuration records
hypervisor/tenant isolation configuration reports
container namespace and RBAC configuration
cross-domain traffic logs (flows) with source/destination and port
exceptions/allowlist records for cross-domain communications
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated configuration scans showing enforced separation and flow logs demonstrating no unauthorized cross-domain flows (strong) vs. architecture diagrams without configurations or logs (weak).
Documented approved exceptions with expirations are stronger than undocumented cross-domain communications.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires separating processing domains (configuration/enforcement), which prevents unauthorized lateral access.

AC-6(4).differentiation_note: Emphasizes architectural and configuration evidence for domain isolation (network/hypervisor/container) rather than per-account privilege metrics.

AC-6(5).measurement_goal: Measure the lifecycle and protection of privileged accounts: percentage of privileged accounts under PAM, percentage with MFA enabled, and count of unregistered privileged accounts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Managing privileged accounts is preventive; percentage metrics (PAM coverage, MFA enablement) directly show enforcement, supported by counts and remediation trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of privileged accounts enrolled in PAM with configured session recording, percentage of privileged accounts protected by MFA, and a count/list of privileged accounts not covered by controls with justification and remediation tickets.
data_signals: privileged account inventory
PAM enrollment/coverage report (with session records)
MFA enablement status for each privileged account
privileged account creation/modification logs
exception/waiver and remediation ticket records
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated PAM enrollment reports with session recordings and MFA enforcement (strong) vs. spreadsheets listing privileged accounts without evidence of protection (weak).
Low number of unprotected privileged accounts and documented remediation plans strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement concerns management and protection of privileged accounts (PAM, MFA), which prevent misuse of privileges.

AC-6(5).differentiation_note: Specifically measures privileged account lifecycle and protective controls (PAM/MFA) rather than general privilege assignments or logging.

AC-6(6).measurement_goal: Prove that non-organizational users (contractors/third parties) have only necessary privileges: count of non-org users with privileged access, percentage of non-org privileged accesses with valid approvals, and frequency of access reviews for these users.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement is preventive—restricting privileges for non-org users—so percentage coverage of approvals/compliance is most relevant, supported by counts of non-org privileged accounts and trends of reviews.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of non-organizational user accounts with privileges aligned to documented job/contract requirements and with an active approval record, plus count of non-org privileged accounts and timestamps of last access and last review.
data_signals: non-organizational user account inventory (contractor/vendor list)
access approval records for each non-org user (scope, approver, timestamp)
effective privileges assigned to non-org users
access logs showing non-org user activity (timestamps, resources)
privilege review schedule and completion records for non-org users
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated IAM reports correlating non-org accounts to approvals and recent access activity (strong) vs. manual lists without approval metadata (weak).
Frequent reviews with remediation tickets for out-of-scope privileges strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control restricts privileges for non-organizational users to prevent excessive access, making it preventive.

AC-6(6).differentiation_note: Focuses on third-party/contractor account controls and approvals, unlike sibling controls that target internal privileged accounts or logging.

AC-6(7).measurement_goal: Show that periodic privilege reviews occur and drive remediation: count of scheduled privilege reviews completed on time, number of privilege adjustments (revocations/reductions) resulting from reviews, and mean time to remediate excessive privileges.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Privilege review is primarily detective (periodic detection of excessive privileges); counts of completed reviews and adjustments are the primary evidence, with percentage coverage and trends as supporting metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of scheduled privilege review events completed within the defined cadence (e.g., quarterly), count of access rights adjustments (revocations or reductions) per review, and average time from identification to remediation of excessive privileges.
data_signals: privilege review schedule and completion records (timestamps, reviewer)
user account inventory snapshots before/after review
access rights adjustment logs (who, what changed, timestamp)
remediation tickets linked to review findings with resolution timestamps
percentage of users flagged with excessive privileges per review
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated review workflows with completed review records and linked remediation tickets (strong) vs. ad-hoc/manual reviews without artifacts (weak).
Declining trend in percentage of users with excessive privileges across review cycles indicates effective control (strong).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires periodic reviews to detect excessive privileges (detective), as indicated by frequency and remediation tracking.

AC-6(7).differentiation_note: Specifically measures the review cadence, findings, and remediation velocity (time to remediate), unlike other entries focused on enforcement or logging.

AC-6(8).measurement_goal: Measure that code execution contexts operate with least privilege: percentage of processes/applications executing under least-privileged accounts, count of code execution privilege escalations, and frequency of sandbox/whitelist enforcement failures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is preventive (restrict code execution privileges); percentage of code execution contexts complying with least-privilege policies is the clearest evidence, with counts of escalations as detective signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of running processes/applications that execute under non-privileged contexts per policy, counts of privilege escalation attempts by processes (with process name, host, timestamp), and counts of sandbox or application-whitelisting violations.
data_signals: process execution logs with effective user/privilege context
application whitelist/sandbox enforcement logs (allow/deny, timestamp)
privilege escalation detection alerts (process, host, timestamp)
configuration of RBAC/ABAC for execution environments
inventory of code execution environments and assigned privilege levels
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time process execution telemetry showing effective privileges and sandbox denials (strong) vs. periodic manual code reviews without execution logs (weak).
Low count of escalation alerts and documented remediation flows strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement enforces minimal privileges for code execution environments (a preventive configuration control).

AC-6(8).differentiation_note: Targets runtime application/process privileges and enforcement (whitelisting/sandboxing) rather than human user account privileges.

AC-6(9).measurement_goal: Demonstrate that privileged function use is logged and reviewed: count of privileged-function executions logged, percentage of privileged executions with complete audit fields (user, timestamp, function), and count of anomalous privileged uses detected.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Logging is detective; counts of logged privileged-function executions and counts of missing/unlogged events are primary, with percentage completeness for log quality.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of privileged function executions recorded in centralized logs over the period (with user identity, timestamp, and function name), percentage of privileged-function events that include required audit fields, and count of anomalies or unauthorized privileged executions discovered during log review.
data_signals: centralized privileged function execution logs (user, timestamp, function)
log ingestion/collection metrics (success/failure counts)
audit log retention records and integrity checks
privileged function definitions list
alerts from log analysis for anomalous privileged function use
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time centralized logs with immutable storage, complete audit fields, and automated alerting on anomalies (strong) vs. local system logs with missing fields or inconsistent retention (weak).
High log ingestion success rate and low missing-event count strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement requires logging and review of privileged function use, which is detective by design (detecting misuse after/when it occurs).

AC-6(9).differentiation_note: Focused on logging and audit completeness for privileged function usage (detection and review) rather than on preventive restrictions or account lifecycle.

AC-6(10).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of privileged-function executions performed by authorized privileged accounts and the coverage of RBAC assignments for privileged functions, plus counts of denied/non-privileged attempts and the on-time completion rate of privileged access reviews. An auditor should see privileged-action audit coverage, authorization mapping completeness, and denial counts over the review period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage and compliance verification (e.g., percent of privileged actions performed by authorized roles, percent of privileged accounts with required protections). Counts and rates (denials, unauthorized attempts, execution rates) are useful secondary measures; trends show improvement or regression over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of privileged-function executions associated with accounts/roles that are authorized for those functions (authorized-execution / total privileged-function executions). 2) Percentage of privileged accounts with required controls (e.g., MFA, documented role assignment). 3) Count of denied or blocked attempts by non-privileged users to execute privileged functions and completion rate / timeliness of scheduled privileged access reviews.
data_signals: privileged-action audit logs (timestamp, user_id, action_id, outcome)
access control / RBAC role-to-permission mapping records (privileged function → authorized roles)
privileged account inventory with authentication posture (MFA enabled flag, last password change)
access review records (review id, reviewer, scope, completion timestamp, findings)
access control enforcement events (deny/allow counts for privileged functions)
identity governance change logs (role assignment additions/removals with timestamps and approver)
exceptions/approved elevation records (request id, justification, duration, approver)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, centralized privileged-action audit logs correlated with RBAC mappings and identity governance records are strong evidence.
Automated deny/allow enforcement events and timestamps with consistent coverage across all privileged functions are strong.
Periodic manual spreadsheets of role assignments without timestamps or correlating audit logs are weak evidence.
Ad-hoc exception paperwork without automated enforcement or expiration is weak; documented, time-bound, approved exceptions with enforcement controls are moderate.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on restricting access and ensuring only authorized users can perform privileged functions (establish RBAC, grant only legitimate need), which are preventive measures.

AC-6(10).differentiation_note: This control specifically targets prohibition of non-privileged execution of privileged functions — measurement must focus on privileged-action execution vs. authorization mapping and denial events, versus sibling controls that might focus only on role provisioning, authentication strength, or general access-review scheduling. Note: no associated_risks provided, so risk-derived categories are not available.

AC-7.measurement_goal: Measure the volume and distribution of unsuccessful logon attempts across systems, the count and frequency of account/IP lockouts, the average lockout duration and time-to-resolution, and the coverage of lockout/delay policies across operating system and application layers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily detective (monitoring failed logins), so event volumes (counts of failures and lockouts) are the most direct evidence; rates and percentages help normalize the counts and show coverage/trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of failed authentication events per account/device/IP per configurable time window; count of lockout events and whether lockout was automatic or administrative; lockout start and end timestamps to compute duration and time-to-resolve; configuration records showing threshold and delay algorithm settings per system; coverage matrix of systems/applications with lockout policy applied.
data_signals: authentication event logs (successful and failed) with timestamp, user/account identifier, source IP
failed_attempt_count per account/device within configured time window
lockout_event records (timestamp, account/device/IP, trigger type automatic/manual)
lockout_release records (automatic timer expiry or administrative intervention) with timestamps
configured lockout threshold and delay_algorithm parameters per system/application
policy documents / configuration files indicating lockout policy applied at OS and application levels
alerts generated for excessive failed logon attempts or lockouts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous, timestamped authentication logs and automated lockout event records correlated with configuration artifacts (thresholds and delay settings).
Moderate: Periodic exported logs showing failed attempts and lockouts but lacking real-time correlation to configuration/state.
Weak: Point-in-time screenshots or manual reports of a few lockout incidents without historical logs or policy/configuration evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control description emphasizes monitoring and tracking failed logon attempts and maintaining logs for analysis, which is detective in nature.

AC-7.differentiation_note: This baseline AC-7 entry covers overall monitoring and measurement of failed logons and lockout policy coverage across systems, whereas sub-controls focus on specific enforcement actions (automatic lock, device wipe, biometric limits, alternate factors).

AC-7(1).measurement_goal: Measure the number of automatic account lockout events, the percentage of accounts/systems configured for automatic unlock after a defined time, the count of successful logins following lockouts, and average time-to-unlock (automatic or administrative).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control that produces discrete events (lockouts), counts of lockout events are the most direct measure; percentages show coverage of auto-unlock configuration and normalization across the user base.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of account lockout events (automatic) per system per period; configuration state indicating max unsuccessful attempts and automatic unlock timer per system; timestamps showing unlock method (automatic expiry vs administrative unlock) to compute average unlock time; counts of successful logins immediately following unlock to validate legitimate recovery.
data_signals: account lockout event logs with timestamp, account id, system/application identifier
automatic_unlock timer events and timestamps
manual administrative unlock records with operator id and timestamp
configuration records specifying max unsuccessful attempts (e.g., 3-5) and unlock duration (e.g., 15/30 minutes)
alerts generated to security personnel when lockouts occur
successful_login_after_lockout logs (timestamped)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: System-generated lockout and auto-unlock logs correlated to stored configuration thresholds and automated alerts to security staff.
Moderate: Regular exports of lockout counts and unlock times without live correlation to config settings.
Weak: Occasional manual reports or user support tickets without machine-readable lockout or unlock timestamps.

affinity_keywords: maximum number of allowed unsuccessful logon attempts
time taken to resolve locked accounts

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly describes detecting repeated failed attempts and triggering automatic account lockouts and alerts, which are detective actions producing event data.

AC-7(1).differentiation_note: AC-7(1) specifically demands automatic account lock enforcement and associated unlock timing/alerts, so measurements must show lockout event counts, auto-unlock behavior, and coverage—more operationally focused than the parent AC-7.

AC-7(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of managed mobile devices with MDM-enforced wipe-on-failed-authentication configured, the count and rate of device wipes triggered by failed logon thresholds, and the average time to restore legitimate user access after a wipe.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive enforcement control is about coverage and configuration (MDM wipe policy), so percentage coverage of devices with the policy is primary; counts of wipes and trends show operational impact and incidents.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of mobile devices in inventory with MDM policy enforcing device wipe after N failed logon attempts; event logs of device wipe actions including device ID, timestamp, and triggering failed attempt count; records of successful logon resetting unsuccessful count; ticketing/enrollment records showing time-to-restore for wiped devices.
data_signals: MDM policy records indicating wipe threshold and enforcement status per device
device wipe event logs (device id, timestamp, cause: failed logon threshold)
unsuccessful_logon_count reset events on successful authentication
number of devices wiped per reporting period
device restoration or re-enrollment ticket timestamps and durations
asset inventory linking mobile device to user and sensitivity classification
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Machine-generated MDM policy state and device wipe event logs correlated to inventory and policy artifacts, plus restoration ticket metrics.
Moderate: Periodic MDM reports showing wipes but lacking event-level correlation to failed auth counts.
Weak: Manual statements that device wipe policy exists without machine logs of enforcement or associated thresholds.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control enforces an automated destructive action (device wipe) after failed attempts via MDM, which is preventive enforcement to remove data exposure risk.

AC-7(2).differentiation_note: AC-7(2) targets mobile endpoints and MDM-triggered wipes; measurement must include MDM policy state and device wipe event logs, which differs from account lockout metrics in sibling controls.

AC-7(3).measurement_goal: Measure the number and rate of biometric authentication failures and biometric-specific lockouts, the percentage of biometric systems enforcing configured attempt limits, and average time-to-recovery for biometric lockouts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires verified enforcement of configured attempt limits on biometric systems (coverage %), while counts/rates of biometric failures quantify operational exposure.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of biometric authentication systems/devices configured with max-failed-attempt thresholds and lockout behavior; logs of biometric authentication failures with timestamps, device id, and user id; biometric lockout events and recovery action timestamps; config records stating lockout duration and recovery procedures.
data_signals: biometric authentication failure logs (timestamp, device id, user id)
biometric lockout event records with timestamps and recovery method
biometric system configuration files indicating max attempts and lockout duration
account recovery action logs (who performed recovery and when)
frequency of lockouts per user/device over time
audit/review reports of biometric system settings
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Device-level biometric logs and configuration artifacts showing enforced attempt limits and automated lockout events with recovery records.
Moderate: Periodic audit reports confirming settings but lacking event-level failure logs.
Weak: Policy statements about biometric limits without device logs or recovery timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires enforcing maximum failed biometric attempts and triggering lockout/additional verification, which prevents further unauthorized attempts.

AC-7(3).differentiation_note: AC-7(3) is biometric-specific and requires evidence from biometric system logs and configuration, unlike sibling controls that focus on general account lockouts, device wipes, or alternate factors.

AC-7(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of authentication flows that escalate to an alternate authentication factor after the configured failed-attempt threshold, count of alternate-factor challenges issued, the success rate of alternate-factor recovery, and time-to-account-recovery using alternate factors.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control enforces escalation to alternate authentication; coverage and percent of escalations (and successful recoveries) indicate whether the policy is applied and effective, while counts/rates show event volumes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems/accounts configured to require alternate authentication factor after N failed attempts; event logs showing escalation events (failed attempts → alternate factor challenge) with timestamps and outcome; counts of successful account recoveries via alternate factor and time from escalation to successful authentication.
data_signals: authentication policy config records specifying escalation to alternate authentication factor
events where alternate authentication factor was requested/used (timestamp, user id, system id)
failed_attempt_count leading to escalation (event correlation logs)
MFA challenge and response result logs
alerts/log entries for repeated failed attempts that triggered escalation
metrics showing percentage of lockouts resolved via alternate factor and time-to-recovery
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Correlated policy configurations and per-event logs showing failed attempts, escalation to alternate factor, and successful recovery timestamps.
Moderate: Aggregated stats on escalations without per-event correlation to configured thresholds.
Weak: Descriptive policy documents or isolated MFA logs without linkage to preceding failed-attempt events.

affinity_keywords: temporary locked or require an alternate authentication factor
percentage of lockouts that lead to successful account recovery

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates escalation to an alternate authentication factor after failures (a preventive enforcement to stop brute-force success), so it is primarily preventive.

AC-7(4).differentiation_note: AC-7(4) focuses on escalation to alternate/MFA methods after failed attempts and requires measurement of escalation events and recovery success, which differs from lockout counts, device wipes, or biometric-specific enforcement in siblings.

AC-8.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of active users who have acknowledged the current system use notification at required login points, the timeliness of notification updates (versioning and last update), and the frequency of related policy violations or unauthorized access attempts to assess effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control where auditors want coverage/acknowledgment rates (percentage) to show policy reach; counts of acknowledgments, policy breaches, and trends over time supplement coverage with volume and direction of effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of currently active user accounts with a recorded, time-stamped acknowledgment of the currently-deployed system use notification; count and rate of login attempts flagged as unauthorized or policy-violating; notification banner/version deployed at each login point and its last update timestamp; accessibility compliance test results for the notification content.
data_signals: user acknowledgment records (user_id, timestamp, notification_version, login_point)
login banner/version identifier deployed per system/interface
notification last_update_timestamp and change history
accessibility compliance test results (WCAG pass/fail, remediation items)
system access logs showing failed/unauthorized login attempts (timestamp, user_id, source_ip, reason)
policy-violation incident records referencing misuse tied to notification acknowledgment status
training attendance/completion records for sessions that reinforce notification content
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, auditable acknowledgment logs tied to authenticated user IDs with notification_version and timestamp; banner enforcement at authentication flow; continuous collection of access log events correlated to acknowledgment status.
Strong: maintained change history showing notification updates and corresponding re-acknowledgment campaigns.
Weak: one-time manual attestations or screenshots of banners without per-user timestamped acknowledgments.
Weak: periodic training attendance alone without evidence of in-flow acknowledgement at login points.

affinity_keywords: links to the organization's acceptable use policy

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes informing users to prevent misuse and unauthorized access (e.g., 'inform users of their responsibilities', 'foster a culture of security awareness'), which is characteristic of a preventive control.

AC-8.differentiation_note: This control is focused on user-facing notification coverage and measurable acknowledgments (visibility, versioning, accessibility, and re-acknowledgment) rather than technical enforcement or incident response metrics used by sibling controls; measurement must tie per-user acknowledgments to login points and notification versions.

AC-9.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and operational effectiveness of previous-logon notifications by counting accounts with the feature enabled, the number of notifications displayed at successful logon, and the count and response time of user-reported suspicious logons.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily detective (notifications and reports generate discrete events) so counts of notifications and user reports are most useful; percentage coverage of accounts with the feature enabled is a valuable secondary metric to show policy enforcement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of user accounts with previous-logon notification enabled (by account type), number of notifications shown per successful logon per day, count of user-initiated suspicious logon reports, and mean/median time from user report to investigation start.
data_signals: feature_enabled flag per user account (previous logon notification)
authentication event logs with last-logon timestamp and notification delivery event
notification delivery logs (timestamp, user id, channel)
user incident/report tickets referencing previous-logon discrepancies
investigation start timestamps for reported events
audit log entries recording last successful logon and originating IP/device
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated authentication and notification logs correlated with delivered-notification receipts are strong evidence.
Point-in-time screenshots or manual attestations are weak evidence.
High feature_enabled percentage with low missed-delivery counts and timely investigation of reports indicates strong control operation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes informing users of prior logons and monitoring user reports ("inform users", "report suspicious activity"), which are detective activities.

AC-9.differentiation_note: This entry is the baseline previous-logon notification control focused on overall enablement, notification delivery, and user reporting; siblings add unsuccessful-attempts, combined success/failure details, account-change alerts, or extra logon metadata.

AC-9(1).measurement_goal: Measure the volume and delivery of notifications that include unsuccessful logon attempts, plus user engagement: count of notifications sent that include unsuccessful attempts, user response rate, and subsequent account remediation actions taken.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective nature: counts of notifications and unsuccessful-attempt events show detection activity; rates/percentages (response rate, percent of users acting) measure user engagement and effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of notifications sent that include unsuccessful logon attempt details (per day/week), count of unsuccessful logon events per account captured by auth logs, user response rate to those notifications (acknowledgement or reported), and count of remedial actions (password resets, account lockouts) triggered by notifications.
data_signals: authentication failure event logs with timestamps and source IP/location
notification generation logs indicating inclusion of unsuccessful attempt details
notification delivery channel logs (email/in-app) and delivery status
user acknowledgement or click/response events
helpdesk or incident records for password changes or account remediation after notification
counts of unsuccessful logon attempts correlated to subsequent notification timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated correlation between auth-failure events and generated notifications with delivery receipts is strong evidence.
Sampling notifications without cryptographic/log correlation is weak.
High delivery success and measurable user response/remediation actions strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires notifying users of unsuccessful logon attempts and tracking responses ("notify users", "unsuccessful logon attempts", "response rate"), a detective function.

AC-9(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on unsuccessful authentication events and the notification/response workflow (delivery, user acknowledgement, remediation), unlike the base AC-9 which centers on last-logon display and overall coverage.

AC-9(2).measurement_goal: Measure capture and notification of both successful and unsuccessful logon attempts by counting authentication events (success/failure) per account, verifying notifications include event success/failure status, and tracking user reporting latency after such notifications.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective control: counts of both successful and failed authentication events and notifications surface anomalous behavior; trends and percentages help show change over time and coverage/compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-account counts of authentication events labeled success/failure with timestamps and location, number of notifications generated that include status (success/failure), rate of user reports following such notifications, and integrity controls for log data (checksums or write-once logs).
data_signals: authentication event logs containing success/failure flag, timestamp, source IP and device
notification payloads showing success/failure status included
log integrity/hash records or WORM storage indicators
