user report timestamps referencing a specific notification
notification delivery logs (channel, timestamp, user id)
audit logs of access to authentication logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Tamper-evident authentication logs and automated notification records correlated to events are strong evidence.
Manual export of logs without integrity metadata is weaker evidence.
Consistent trending reduction in unexplained failures or fast reporting latency indicates effective control operation.

affinity_keywords: integrity and confidentiality of log data

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control specifies capturing and notifying both successful and unsuccessful authentication events ("success or failure of each attempt"), a detective monitoring capability.

AC-9(2).differentiation_note: Emphasizes preserving log integrity and including both success and failure status in notifications; measurement must validate log integrity and correct status propagation to notifications, unlike variants focusing only on failures or account changes.

AC-9(3).measurement_goal: Measure automated notifications for account-status changes by counting account-change notification events (password changes, lockouts, unrecognized device/logon) and the proportion that trigger user verification or remediation actions within a defined timeframe.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective/control-alerting function: count of account-change notifications shows alerting volume; rates/percentages capture user verification and remediation behavior after alerts.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of account-change notification events generated (password change, account lockout, logon from unrecognized device/location), count of users who confirm or take remediation steps after notification, and average time from notification to remediation action.
data_signals: IAM system events for password changes and account lockouts
device recognition logs indicating unrecognized device or location flags
notification generation and delivery logs for account-change alerts
user verification/response records (ACK, support ticket, password reset request)
incident tickets opened as a result of account-change notifications
timestamps linking notification to remediation action
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated linkage between IAM events and delivered notifications with recorded user remediation actions is strong evidence.
Ad-hoc manual notifications or user surveys without event correlation are weak evidence.
High proportion of notifications that lead to timely remediation (per policy_threshold) indicates effective control operation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires automatic notification on account changes ("automatically send notifications" for "password changes, account lockouts") — an alerting/detection mechanism.

AC-9(3).differentiation_note: Specifically focuses on account-status change alerts (password changes, lockouts, unrecognized devices) and measuring resultant user verification/remediation rather than general last-logon display.

AC-9(4).measurement_goal: Measure that notifications include additional logon metadata (originating IP, remote access/mobile context) and assess coverage: percent of authentication interfaces (remote, mobile, web) that display the extra logon information and counts of discrepancies reported by users.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This variant requires verifying coverage and presence of additional metadata across interfaces (a preventive/coverage measurement); percentage coverage across interfaces is primary, with counts of discrepancy reports as detective support.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of authentication interfaces (desktop, remote access, mobile, web) that include additional logon metadata in notifications (originating IP, device), counts of notifications that included IP/device metadata, and counts of reported discrepancies tied to those notifications.
data_signals: notification payloads indicating included fields (last logon date/time, originating IP, device)
inventory of authentication interfaces and their notification configuration status
delivery logs showing interface/channel (remote access solution, mobile app) and notification content
user reports/incidents referencing additional logon information discrepancies
correlation between displayed IP/device and auth logs for the same event
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated verification that notification payloads include IP/device fields across all interfaces with delivery receipts and log correlation is strong evidence.
Manual UI screenshots for a subset of interfaces are weaker evidence.
High percent coverage and low mismatches between displayed metadata and auth logs indicate strong evidence.

affinity_keywords: display a notification upon each successful logon
scripts that pull logon data from system logs
integrity and confidentiality of the logon data

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control emphasizes ensuring the feature (additional metadata in notifications) is enabled across interfaces ("ensure this feature is enabled across all user interfaces"), which is a preventive/coverage requirement; however it also produces detective output.

AC-9(4).differentiation_note: Unique need to verify inclusion of specific metadata fields (IP/device) across different authentication interfaces and measure coverage percentage, unlike siblings that focus on notification content or response behavior.

AC-10.measurement_goal: Measure enforcement and compliance with defined concurrent session limits: (1) percentage of user accounts whose active session count never exceeds their role-based maximum during a reporting period, (2) count and rate of blocked or terminated session attempts due to concurrency limits, and (3) distribution of concurrent sessions per role and system to identify outliers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires demonstrating coverage and compliance with a policy-defined threshold (best measured as percentages of accounts/systems in compliance). Counts and rates are useful secondary metrics for blocked attempts and event volumes, while distributions show per-role/session patterns.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Collect per-user active session counts (timestamped), IAM/application configuration records that map roles to their max concurrent-session settings, session termination and blocked-attempt logs, and incident reports referencing session misuse. Produce metrics by role and system: percent of users exceeding limits, count of blocked session attempts per day, and average/peak sessions per user.
data_signals: IAM configuration: role-to-max-concurrent-session mapping entries
active session records: user identifier, session id, start timestamp, end timestamp, source IP
session termination logs and reason codes (e.g., 'concurrency_limit_enforced')
blocked or rejected session attempt logs with timestamps and user id
per-user peak concurrent session counts per reporting interval
policy document revision and effective date for session limits
MFA integration status per account/system (as referenced for enhanced controls)
incident reports referencing 'session hijacking' or 'session misuse'
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, timestamped session logs correlated with IAM configuration showing automatic enforcement events (e.g., termination or rejection) and per-role compliance rates.
Moderate: periodic exported session summaries plus IAM config snapshots and attestations of enforcement.
Weak: one-off screenshots or manual attestations without correlated session logs or enforcement event records.

affinity_keywords: limit the number of simultaneous sessions

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description frames AC-10 as a measure to 'limit' simultaneous sessions and enforce configuration/policy, which are preventive actions (e.g., 'define the maximum number of concurrent sessions' and 'enforcing session limits').

AC-10.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measuring per-user concurrent session counts and enforcement events (blocked/terminated sessions) and validating IAM/application configuration for max-session settings, rather than broader authentication or logging controls which may focus on credentials or audit retention.

AC-11.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of managed endpoints that have automatic device lock enabled with timeout settings meeting policy (e.g., ≤15 minutes), the distribution of configured lock timeouts, and counts of incidents or unauthorized accesses attributable to unlocked devices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and configuration compliance—percentage metrics show coverage of enforced device locks, while counts and trends surface raw incident volumes and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of endpoints (desktops, laptops, mobile) where device_lock_enabled = true AND lock_timeout_minutes <= policy_timeout (e.g., 15). Also capture average lock_timeout_minutes per device_type, count of endpoints lacking strong_authentication, and number of incidents citing unlocked devices as contributing factor over the audit period.
data_signals: endpoint inventory with device_type (desktop/laptop/mobile) and management_status
MDM policy record: device_lock_enabled flag and configured lock_timeout_minutes per device
authentication configuration per device (password_enabled, biometric_enabled)
lock/unlock event logs with timestamps (inactivity timestamp → lock event timestamp)
incident records citing 'unlocked device' or 'unauthorized access due to unlocked device'
user training completion records referencing device lock policy
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous MDM-enforced device configuration reports and lock/unlock event logs showing lock events within configured timeout for >95% of managed endpoints.
Weak: single point-in-time screenshots, manual attestations, or annual audit checklists without machine-enforced policy evidence.
Moderate: periodic (weekly/monthly) device configuration reports without per-event lock timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description states the control 'automatically secure devices by locking them' and focuses on configuring settings to prevent unauthorized access, indicating a preventive control.

AC-11.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on broad device-lock coverage, timeout configuration, authentication settings, and incident counts for unlocked devices — not on display-obscuring features or specialized UI protections.

AC-11(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of endpoints with automatic locking enabled plus pattern-hiding display features active (screen-obscuring when locked), the average lock timeout, counts of unauthorized access attempts against locked endpoints, and MDM enforcement success rate for both lock and pattern-hiding settings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, percentage metrics demonstrate coverage of both automatic locking and pattern-hiding display settings; counts show unauthorized attempts and distribution reveals timeout variability across device types.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percent of managed devices where device_lock_enabled = true AND lock_timeout_minutes ≤ policy AND pattern_hiding_display_enabled = true. Measure MDM_enforcement_success_rate (applied policy vs reported device state), count of lock_activation_events, count of unauthorized_access_attempts against devices with lock enabled, and average time from inactivity to lock event.
data_signals: MDM policy records: device_lock_enabled, lock_timeout_minutes, pattern_hiding_display_enabled per device
endpoint reported configuration state (periodic inventory) showing applied vs desired settings
lock activation event logs (timestamp of inactivity start and lock event)
screen state or display-obscure events (pattern-hiding activation logs) where available
unauthorized access attempt logs referencing locked devices or failed unlock attempts
audit or compliance scan results showing devices missing pattern-hiding or failing enforcement
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous MDM telemetry showing applied policy matches device-reported state and event logs for pattern-hiding activation on lock events.
Weak: manual attestations that pattern-hiding is available without telemetry showing it actually activates when locked, or occasional spot-checks.
Moderate: nightly inventory reports indicating configuration but lacking per-lock activation events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires configuring devices to automatically lock and obscure sensitive display content—actions taken to prevent unauthorized access rather than detect or correct incidents.

AC-11(1).differentiation_note: Unlike AC-11 which centers on lock coverage and timeout compliance, this control adds measurement for pattern-hiding/display-obscuring behavior and MDM enforcement of UI-level protections, requiring telemetry of display state changes in addition to lock configuration.

AC-12.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of sessions that are terminated automatically per the organization's timeout policy, the distribution of session lengths (average and median), and the count of systems/applications lacking configured timeout settings to demonstrate coverage and effectiveness of automatic session termination.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AC-12 is a preventive control verifying coverage and policy adherence; percentage metrics show compliance (e.g., % of sessions terminated per policy), distributions show session length spread, and counts/trends surface gaps over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percent of total sessions that ended with an automated termination reason (inactivity/system trigger) vs manual logout; count of applications/systems with configured session timeout values; average session length per application and per sensitivity tier. Include token invalidation and termination event timestamps to prove sessions were ended and tokens revoked.
data_signals: session start and end timestamps
last-activity timestamp per session
session termination event logs with termination reason (inactivity, logout, system shutdown)
session timeout configuration value by application/system
session token invalidation events (token ID, timestamp)
average session length by application and user role
count of systems without session timeout configured
frequency of session terminations (per day/week)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated termination event logs with timestamps and token invalidation records covering all production applications; weak: periodic manual screenshots or one-off audit notes without event-level logs.
Strong: per-application timeout configuration inventory mapped to policy; weak: undocumented or partially populated configuration lists.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control text calls it a 'preventive control designed to automatically end user sessions,' indicating prevention of unauthorized access.

AC-12.differentiation_note: This entry covers broad, organization-wide timeout configuration and coverage measurement (percentage of sessions terminated by policy and systems lacking timeout settings), whereas siblings focus on specific triggers, messages, or UI behaviors.

AC-12(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of user-initiated logout commands that result in immediate session termination and token invalidation, and the count/rate of any post-logout access attempts to prove logout behavior is enforced and audited.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires verifying that logout actions reliably end sessions; percentage shows successful logout terminations, while counts and rates capture frequency of failures and post-logout access attempts.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each logout event, record if the session termination occurred and whether session tokens were invalidated and session data cleared. Compute percent successful logout terminations (successful termination + token invalidation / total logout commands) and count of post-logout authentication or resource access attempts within a short window (e.g., 5 minutes).
data_signals: logout command event logs (user ID, timestamp, UI element invoked)
session termination events tied to logout (session ID, termination timestamp, reason=logout)
session token invalidation logs (token ID, invalidation timestamp)
session data clearing/cleanup events
post-logout access attempts (authentication or resource requests referencing invalidated session token)
mapping of user interfaces that expose logout option (application inventory)
user education/training completion records referencing logout best practices
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: event-level linkage of logout command → immediate session termination → token invalidation across applications, plus low/no post-logout access attempts; weak: self-reported logout behavior or occasional manual verification without logs.
Strong: automated audits showing 100% logout-to-termination mapping per application; weak: evidence limited to UI presence without backend enforcement logs.

affinity_keywords: user interfaces provide a straightforward logout option

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description mandates systems 'automatically terminate user sessions upon receiving a logout command'—a preventive enforcement of logout behavior.

AC-12(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on user-initiated logout mechanics and token invalidation (backend enforcement and UI availability), unlike the parent control which measures general inactivity timeouts and coverage.

AC-12(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of sessions terminated explicitly due to inactivity per configured timeout values (e.g., 15 minutes), verify termination messages were displayed, and track user feedback/clarity incidents to ensure termination messaging and enforcement align with policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control centers on policy enforcement (timeout values and messages); percentage shows compliance with configured timeouts, while counts and trends reveal frequency and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Record configured timeout values per application, and for each termination record the termination reason and whether a termination message was presented. Compute percent of terminations that align with the configured policy (terminated after the configured inactivity period) and count of terminations where the termination message was not logged/displayed or flagged in user feedback.
data_signals: predetermined timeout configuration values per application (e.g., 15 minutes)
session termination logs with reason=timeout/inactivity and timestamps
termination message display logs or UI render events (message ID, timestamp, session ID)
user feedback/clarity incident reports referencing termination messages
count of sessions terminated due to inactivity vs total sessions
overdue termination counts (sessions exceeding configured timeout before termination)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated logging that ties termination events to configured timeout values and records message display events; weak: ad hoc user complaints or screenshots without system logs linking reason to configured policy.
Strong: low count of overdue sessions and consistent message-render logs; weak: inconsistent or missing termination message logs.

affinity_keywords: number of sessions terminated due to inactivity

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control enforces automatic termination after a defined inactivity period and requires messaging—both preventive measures to avoid unattended sessions.

AC-12(2).differentiation_note: This control emphasizes the presence and clarity of the termination message and adherence to a specific configured timeout (e.g., 15 minutes), whereas siblings either cover logout behavior or timeout warnings.

AC-12(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of timeout warning prompts delivered prior to session termination and the percentage of users who successfully extend their session in response (interaction success rate), plus the count of unauthorized access attempts following warnings and terminations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive but measures user interaction with warnings; percentage captures prompt delivery and extension success, while rates/counts capture attempts and behavioral trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each session where inactivity approaches the timeout, log the warning prompt event (timestamp, session ID, warning template), record user interaction (extend/ignore) and whether the session remained active. Compute percent of warnings delivered on schedule (e.g., 5 minutes before timeout) and percent of warnings that resulted in successful session extension. Track counts of sessions that ended after warning without extension and any post-termination access attempts.
data_signals: timeout warning prompt event logs (timestamp, session ID, warning template shown)
configured warning timing per application (e.g., 5 minutes before timeout)
user interaction records with warning (extend clicked, password entered, timestamp)
session extension success/failure events (session continued vs terminated)
count of sessions terminated after warning without extension
unauthorized access attempts after termination (post-warning)
user feedback regarding warning clarity
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: event logs linking warning prompt → user interaction → session extension with timestamps and low post-warning access attempts; weak: UI mockups or periodic manual testing without production event logs.
Strong: consistent timing of warnings (e.g., 5 minutes prior) recorded across applications; weak: variable or missing warning-timing logs.

affinity_keywords: display a clear and explicit warning message
percentage of users who extend their sessions

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control proactively warns users prior to termination to prevent unattended session exposure, which is a preventive behavior enforced by the system.

AC-12(3).differentiation_note: This control specifically measures pre-termination user prompts and extension interactions (timing and success rates), unlike AC-12 which focuses on termination enforcement and AC-12(1)/(2) which focus on logout/token invalidation and termination messaging respectively.

AC-13.measurement_goal: Measure the completion and coverage of scheduled access reviews, the count and rate of unauthorized access attempts detected via log monitoring, and the mean time to remediate access control violations so an auditor can verify only authorized individuals retain access and principle of least privilege is enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective control focused on detecting events and occurrences (access review completions, unauthorized attempts) — raw counts (and counts per period) are most direct; rates and percentages are useful secondarily for normalization (attempts per 1k logins, % reviews completed).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of access reviews executed and completed with timestamps and reviewer IDs per scheduled cycle; count of unauthorized access attempts and anomaly alerts generated by log analysis tools with event details (user, resource, timestamp); remediation ticket timestamps showing time-to-remediate access violations and entitlement change records demonstrating least-privilege adjustments.
data_signals: access review schedule and completion records (review date, reviewer ID, scope, systems covered)
user access rights audit snapshots / entitlement export (user, role, permissions, timestamp)
access event logs with timestamps, user IDs, resource accessed, action (login, privilege elevation), source IP
anomaly/unauthorized access alerts from automated log analysis tools (alert id, rule, severity, timestamp)
privilege assignment/change records and justifications (who changed, when, previous vs new entitlement)
remediation ticket records for access violations (ticket open/close timestamps, owner, remediation actions)
coverage metrics showing % of accounts/systems/data stores included in each review cycle
role definitions and role-to-permission mappings (documented RBAC/roles inventory)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated log analysis alerts with raw event records and timestamps are strong evidence
Complete access review records (schedule, scope, reviewer sign-off, artifacts) and closed remediation tickets with timestamps strengthen evidence
Ad-hoc/manual spreadsheets or single point-in-time screenshots without event logs or change history are weak evidence
Lack of entitlement snapshots or missing coverage metrics (systems/accounts excluded) weakens assurance

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description emphasizes monitoring and review ("monitoring access logs for anomalies", "auditing user access rights"), which are detective activities.

AC-13.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on ongoing supervision and review (frequency, log monitoring, remediation time) rather than provisioning or change controls — measurement must combine periodic review completion, continuous log-derived detection counts, and remediation timing.

AC-14.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of documented permitted (unauthenticated) actions: percent of unauthenticated actions that are authorized in the permitted-actions registry and the count/rate of unauthenticated actions that access non-public or sensitive resources and/or result in misuse incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AC-14 is primarily a preventive control that requires documented scope and policy coverage (best expressed as percentages of permitted actions covered by policy). Counts/rates and trends are useful secondary metrics to detect anomalies and measure volume of unauthenticated actions over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of unauthenticated actions that appear in the approved permitted-actions registry (documented exceptions) and the count of unauthenticated actions per action-type that accessed data classified as non-public or sensitive; link each action record to: timestamp, source (IP/session), resource accessed, and policy approval ID. Also capture incidents tied to these actions and outcomes of periodic reviews.
data_signals: permitted-actions registry entries (action id, scope, approval date, approving authority)
application/service audit logs with action type, timestamp, source IP/session id, resource identifier
data classification tag for the resource accessed (sensitivity level)
incident/ misuse reports referencing unauthenticated actions (ticket id, severity, root cause)
periodic review/audit records showing review date and reviewer conclusions
user feedback/complaint records referencing unauthenticated functionality
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous machine-generated audit logs that correlate each unauthenticated action to a permitted-actions registry entry and data classification.
Strong: linkable approval artifacts and recent risk assessment documents for each permitted action.
Weak: one-off screenshots or manual attestations without machine logs tying actions to approvals.
Weak: aggregated counts without resource-level sensitivity context or lack of incident linkage.

affinity_keywords: Permitted Actions Without Identification or Authentication

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes defining scope, policies and approvals for permitted unauthenticated actions (preventive controls) and reducing exposure through policy and documented approvals.

AC-14.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on the governance, policy coverage and audit evidence for permitted unauthenticated actions (registry, approvals, coverage %) rather than the technical session/timeout or compensating-control mechanisms addressed in its enhancement.

AC-14(1).measurement_goal: Measure whether documented 'necessary uses' for unauthenticated actions are enforced by technical safeguards and monitored: percent of necessary-use exceptions with required compensating controls (e.g., session timeouts, activity logging) in place, plus the count/rate of unusual patterns or unauthorized access incidents tied to these exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The enhancement requires documented approvals and technical compensating controls (coverage/compliance is best expressed as a percentage). Counts/rates of anomalies and incident trends are necessary secondary detective measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of necessary-use (unauthenticated) exceptions that have an associated technical control artifact (session timeout configuration, activity logging enabled, compensating control implementation) and the daily count of unauthenticated actions flagged as unusual or that triggered monitoring alerts; include timestamps of exception approvals and last review dates.
data_signals: necessary-use exception registry (exception id, justification, approver, approval timestamp, expiration/review date)
configuration records: session timeout values and enforcement for affected services
activity/audit logs with action name, timestamp, source IP/session id, and alert flags
monitoring/alert tickets for unusual patterns of behavior referencing exception ids
evidence of compensating controls (WAF rules, rate-limiting configs, IP allowlists) linked to exception id
periodic exception review/audit records and remediation actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated correlation between exception registry entries and live configurations/logging showing session timeout and activity logging enabled, plus generated alerts when thresholds are exceeded.
Strong: time-stamped approval and review artifacts with enforcement evidence (config snapshots) and low incident counts tied to exceptions.
Weak: documented exceptions without technical configuration evidence, or manual attestations without logs/alerts.
Weak: incident reports lacking mapping to exception identifiers or absent monitoring timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: AC-14(1) prescribes approved 'necessary uses' plus technical measures (session timeouts, logging) and compensating controls which are preventive by design, with detective elements for monitoring.

AC-14(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement emphasizes technical enforcement and monitoring for approved exceptions (session timeouts, logging, compensating controls) rather than the higher-level policy coverage and audit-frequency focus of the base AC-14 control.

AC-15.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of new and modified data objects that receive an automated classification/tag matching the applicable classification policy, plus counts of misclassified or untagged items and the average time from creation/modification to final classification to demonstrate consistent, timely automated marking.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control the primary auditor view is coverage/compliance (percent of data correctly classified). Counts of untagged/misclassified items and trending of classification accuracy over time complement coverage by showing volume and improvement/decay.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and timestamp of classification events per data object, classification label value and policy ID applied, whether the label matches the policy-determined classification, time delta between object creation/modification and final tag, and the number of exceptions or manual overrides recorded. Break these metrics down by data store, data type, and criticality tier to allow policy-targeted measurement.
data_signals: classification event logs (object id, timestamp, classifier id, policy id, assigned label)
data object metadata showing 'sensitivity'/'classification' tag values
creation/modification timestamps for data objects
classification decision outcome (automated_accept / automated_override / manual_tag)
audit findings listing misclassified or untagged objects
incident tickets for misclassified data and remediation timestamps
SLA or policy threshold for time-to-classify by data tier
training completion records for users/operators involved in classification rules
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated classification event logs that show immediate tag assignment with policy_id and no manual override, low and declining count of audit-identified misclassifications, and time-to-classify within policy SLA.
Weak: periodic manual inventory spreadsheets or one-off audit reports without time-stamped classification events or evidence of automated enforcement.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control' that 'automatically tag[s] data as it is created or modified' which prevents mis-handling before it occurs.

AC-15.differentiation_note: Unlike nearby access or logging controls, AC-15 focuses specifically on automated labeling accuracy, coverage and timeliness (classification event metadata and SLA adherence) rather than access enforcement or audit log retention.

AC-16.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and accuracy of data classification and security/privacy attribute assignments across systems and applications (percentage of data assets classified), track incidents tied to unclassified or misclassified data (count), and monitor completion of periodic attribute risk assessments and training (rate/percentage).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control emphasizes coverage and compliance (e.g., % of data classified, % compliance with classification policy), so percentage metrics best show coverage; counts and rates supplement with incident and activity volumes and trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of data assets in the authoritative data inventory/catalog that have an assigned security and privacy classification tag; count incidents referencing unclassified data and the number of completed risk assessments per system per reporting period; measure training completion rates for staff on data handling.
data_signals: data inventory / catalog entries with classification tag
data discovery/classification tool output (asset id, tag, timestamp)
incident records referencing 'unclassified' or 'misclassified' data
risk assessment reports and timestamps
SDLC integration records showing attribute requirements
training completion records for data handling courses
periodic audit reports showing classification coverage
evidence_strength_indicators: High: automated scan outputs and data catalog showing >90% assets tagged with timestamped assignments and audit trail.
High: linkage between SDLC requirement artifacts and attribute assignments.
Weak: one-off manual spreadsheet inventories or annual manual reports without timestamps.
Weak: anecdotal training evidence without completion records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description directs establishing policies, classification, and SDLC integration to prevent improper handling of sensitive data.

AC-16.differentiation_note: This parent control focuses on enterprise-wide classification coverage and governance, not on the mechanics of dynamic assignment, change control, or output displays covered by its children.

AC-16(1).measurement_goal: Measure the accuracy and timeliness of dynamic attribute associations (percentage of attribute associations applied correctly within required latency) and the rate/count of unauthorized access attempts attributable to incorrect or missing dynamic attributes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control that requires coverage and correctness of dynamic attribute application; percentage shows accuracy/coverage while counts/rates capture errors and unauthorized attempts.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure the percent of attribute association events that match policy-defined attribute mappings (by subject/object type) and the median latency from creation/composition event to attribute association; count unauthorized access attempts that occurred while attributes were missing or incorrect.
data_signals: attribute association event logs (subject, object, attribute, timestamp)
policy-to-attribute mapping records
contextual triggers (role change, device enrollment) with timestamps
attribute update latency measurements
real-time monitoring/alert logs for failed or mismatched associations
unauthorized access attempt logs referencing missing/incorrect attributes
audit/review findings on dynamic associations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous logs showing automated associations with sub-minute latency and low mismatch percentage vs. policy.
Strong: correlation between contextual trigger events and immediate attribute updates.
Weak: manual change tickets with irregular timestamps and no automated event logs.
Weak: quarterly manual spot-checks without real-time monitoring.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Emphasizes automated, real-time association to prevent unauthorized access by ensuring attributes are applied correctly as objects are created/combined.

AC-16(1).differentiation_note: Unlike the parent AC-16, this control requires measurement of real-time association accuracy and latency for automated/contextual attribute application.

AC-16(2).measurement_goal: Measure the enforcement of authorization for attribute value changes by tracking the percentage of attribute changes performed by authorized roles, count of unauthorized change attempts, and mean time to detect and revert unauthorized modifications.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Control prevents unauthorized attribute changes so percentage of changes by authorized individuals indicates enforcement; counts/rates capture attempts and detection/remediation activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percent of attribute modification events where the actor held the required authorization role at the time of change; count failed or blocked attribute change attempts and measure MTTR (time from unauthorized change detection to remediation).
data_signals: attribute change logs with actor, prior value, new value, timestamp
RBAC permission assignments snapshot at change time
approval workflow records for attribute changes
MFA or authentication logs correlated to attribute change events
SIEM alerts for unusual attribute modification activity
audit trail entries showing approvals and reverts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: immutable, timestamped audit trail linking actor identity, RBAC role at time of change, and approval workflow records.
Strong: MFA enforcement logs tied to change events.
Weak: manual spreadsheets of changes without authentication linkage or approval records.
Weak: periodic manual reviews alone without live logs.

affinity_keywords: time taken to review and approve changes

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires restricting who can change attributes using RBAC and MFA—an inherently preventive enforcement mechanism.

AC-16(2).differentiation_note: This control is specifically about authorization and proof-of-approval for attribute value changes (actors and workflows), not about automated association or interpretation.

AC-16(3).measurement_goal: Measure the integrity and reconciliation of attribute associations maintained by the system (percentage of attributes consistent between authoritative stores and consuming systems; count of attribute mismatches detected and resolved per period).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control ensuring attribute integrity across lifecycle; percentage indicates consistency/coverage while counts and trends capture mismatch events and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure the percent of attribute records that match between the authoritative attribute store and downstream enforcement points; report counts of attribute drift/mismatches detected during periodic audits and mean time to reconcile mismatches.
data_signals: sync logs between attribute store and IAM/enforcement systems (timestamps, record counts)
attribute mismatch counts from reconciliation jobs
periodic audit reports on attribute associations
frequency of attribute updates per asset
access enforcement logs demonstrating attribute-based decisions
discrepancy resolution ticket timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated reconciliation jobs with low mismatch rates and documented resolution timestamps.
Strong: synchronized logs showing consistent attribute values across systems with verified enforcement in access logs.
Weak: ad-hoc manual reconciliation or inconsistent snapshots without timestamps.
Weak: lack of reconciliation evidence or unresolved discrepancies.

affinity_keywords: integration with identity and access management (IAM)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Focuses on ensuring attributes are maintained and protected by system processes to prevent incorrect access decisions.

AC-16(3).differentiation_note: Emphasizes automated system-level maintenance and reconciliation of attribute associations across stores/systems, unlike sibling controls focused on assignment or interpretation.

AC-16(4).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of attribute assignment actions performed by authorized personnel (percentage), count unauthorized assignment attempts, and audit completion of attribute assignment reviews (rate).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control—percentage metrics show authorized assignment compliance while counts/rates show unauthorized attempts and review frequency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percent of attribute assignment events where the assigning actor held the authorized role and followed the documented approval workflow; count unauthorized assignment attempts and measure the rate of periodic assignment reviews completed on schedule.
data_signals: attribute assignment logs with actor, object, attribute, timestamp
authorization/role mapping for assignment permissions
audit logs of assignment-related approvals
unauthorized assignment attempt records
periodic review sign-off records
IAM integration logs showing assignment enforcement
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: assignment logs linked to RBAC permission snapshots and approval workflow tickets.
Strong: automated enforcement preventing unauthorized assignment attempts (blocked events logged).
Weak: manual assignment spreadsheets without approval or authentication metadata.
Weak: infrequent manual audits without continuous logs.

affinity_keywords: integration with identity and access management (IAM)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires limiting assignment capability to authorized roles and auditing assignment activity, a preventive enforcement.

AC-16(4).differentiation_note: Focused specifically on the actor/permission side of initial attribute assignment (authorization and auditability), distinct from change control or system maintenance controls.

AC-16(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of data outputs (reports, logs, exported files) that include required security/privacy attribute metadata (coverage %), and count incidents where missing attributes on outputs contributed to unauthorized disclosure.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control requiring consistent labeling of outputs; percentage shows coverage/completeness while counts capture missing-tag incidents and trends show improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of output artifacts (by report template, log stream, export job) that contain required attribute metadata fields (classification level, handling instructions); count output artifacts discovered missing attributes and any resulting access incidents.
data_signals: output file/report metadata showing attribute tags (classification, handling instructions)
report template registry indicating attribute fields
automated tagging logs showing tag application on output generation
scans of storage/export locations for untagged outputs
incident records citing missing attribute on output
audit results verifying attribute display on outputs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: integration between data classification system and output templates producing tagged artifacts with immutable metadata.
Strong: continuous scanning of outputs with low missing-tag percentages and audit verification.
Weak: sample-based manual checks of a few reports with no systemic tagging evidence.
Weak: self-reported compliance without artifacts or metadata.

affinity_keywords: Attribute Displays on Objects to Be Output
incidents of unauthorized access to sensitive data

control_type_classification.reasoning: Ensures outputs are labeled at generation time to prevent unauthorized disclosure — a preventive labeling requirement.

AC-16(5).differentiation_note: Unique requirement to verify attributes are visible on output artifacts (reports/logs) rather than in backend stores or assignment processes.

AC-16(6).measurement_goal: Measure the consistency and timeliness of attribute association maintenance (percentage of attributes current vs. stale, count of attribute mismatches found in audits, mean time to resolve association discrepancies).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control emphasizes consistent association across lifecycle; percentage metrics capture currentness/coverage, while counts and comparisons surface mismatches and reconciliations.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure percent of attribute associations verified as accurate in the last audit cycle (authoritative store vs. enforcement points), count attribute mismatches detected during ongoing reconciliation, and measure average time to resolve association discrepancies.
data_signals: authoritative attribute store snapshots and hashes (value, timestamp)
reconciliation job outputs with mismatch counts
audit logs showing association verification results
ticketing records for discrepancy resolution with timestamps
attribute update/change history with actor and timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated reconciliation with low mismatch rates and documented resolution times tied to tickets.
Strong: regular audit snapshots showing consistent attribute associations across lifecycle stages.
Weak: irregular manual spot-checks without reconciliation logs.
Weak: unresolved discrepancies or missing resolution evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Focuses on ongoing maintenance and policy-driven updates to prevent stale/mismatched attribute associations.

AC-16(6).differentiation_note: Overlaps with AC-16(3) but emphasizes process and lifecycle policy (reviews and training) for maintaining associations, not only system reconciliation.

AC-16(7).measurement_goal: Measure the organizational consistency of attribute definitions and application (percentage of systems using centralized definitions, count of interpretation-related incidents, and training completion rates for attribute interpretation).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control requiring standardization; percentage shows adoption of centralized definitions while counts/scores measure incidents and assessment results.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of systems and applications referencing the centralized attribute-definition repository; count incidents or audit findings attributed to inconsistent attribute interpretation and measure completion rates of attribute-interpretation training across stakeholders.
data_signals: centralized repository of attribute definitions with versioning
system/source references linking to repository definitions
training completion records for attribute interpretation
incident reports citing attribute misinterpretation
audit assessment matrices comparing attribute use across systems
review logs showing updates to definitions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized, versioned definition repository with >90% system references and training tied to acceptance quizzes.
Strong: audit comparisons showing consistent application across systems and low incident counts.
Weak: multiple divergent local definitions or undocumented interpretation guidance.
Weak: sporadic training attendance without assessment results.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Establishes standardized definitions and training to prevent misinterpretation and mishandling — a preventive governance control.

AC-16(7).differentiation_note: This control is uniquely concerned with semantic consistency and training (interpretation of attributes), not with assignment mechanics or enforcement technologies.

AC-16(8).measurement_goal: Measure deployment and effectiveness of attribute association technologies (percentage of data assets inventoried and classified by tools, distribution of attributes applied automatically vs. manually, and count of classification inaccuracies found in audits).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control focusing on tools/techniques: percentage demonstrates tool coverage and automation rates; distribution shows how attributes are applied across methods.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure percent of data assets present in the inventory that have classification attributes applied by approved data classification tools (automated) versus manual tagging; track counts of assets reclassified during audits and number of incidents tied to misclassification.
data_signals: comprehensive data asset inventory with sensitivity tags
classification tool outputs (asset id, assigned attribute, confidence score)
mapping of attributes to controls (encryption/access controls)
distribution of automated vs manual assignment events
audit findings on classification accuracy
incident logs referencing classification failures
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated classification outputs with confidence scores and integration into enforcement controls (ACLs/encryption).
Strong: inventory showing high automated coverage and low manual override rates.
Weak: manual inventories without tool outputs or confidence metrics.
Weak: isolated point-in-time classification exercises.

affinity_keywords: identify, manage, and protect sensitive data

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires deployment of tools and techniques to prevent misclassification and ensure attributes are applied consistently — a preventive technology control.

AC-16(8).differentiation_note: Focuses on the specific tools and automation techniques used to associate attributes and their coverage/accuracy, not on assignment authorization or interpretation.

AC-16(9).measurement_goal: Measure the timeliness and accuracy of attribute reassignments/regrading (percentage of required regrades completed within policy timeframes, median time-to-reassign, and count of regrading-related incidents).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive/control-of-change focus: percentage shows compliance with reassignment policies and timeliness; rates/counts capture frequency and incidents.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of attributes flagged for reassignment that were updated within the policy-defined timeframe; measure median time from triggering event (context/risk/compliance change) to attribute reassignment and count misclassification incidents discovered after regrade windows.
data_signals: reassignment event logs with reason code, actor, timestamp
approval workflow records and timestamps
policy criteria for reassignment with timeframe
audit reports on regrading accuracy
ticketing records for reassignment requests and completion times
incidents related to misclassified data post-change
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated monitoring that logs trigger events and completed reassignment with timestamps showing SLA compliance.
Strong: approval workflows and audit trails linking rationale to changes and resolution times.
Weak: manual email approvals without timestamps or reason codes.
Weak: overdue reassignment items without documented remediation.

affinity_keywords: reassigned based on changes in context
criteria for when and how attributes should be modified
automated tools for monitoring attribute changes
frequency and accuracy of attribute reassignments
percentage of attributes reviewed within a specified timeframe

control_type_classification.reasoning: Specifies policy-driven reassignment workflows and SLAs to prevent stale or inappropriate attribute assignments—a preventive change control.

AC-16(9).differentiation_note: Specifically measures regrading/reassignment timeliness and SLA compliance following contextual or risk changes, unlike siblings that focus on assignment, maintenance, or interpretation.

AC-16(10).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of security/privacy attribute configuration actions performed by authorized roles and the controls around those changes: percentage of attribute changes with an approval record, count of unauthorized modification attempts, average time from configuration request to approved change, and percentage of attributes with an assigned owner and recent review.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (enforcing RBAC and approvals) so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rates) best show policy adherence; counts and rates capture unauthorized attempts and approval velocity while trends show changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track every attribute configuration change as a discrete record: include actor identity and role, timestamp, attribute name and prior/after values, approval ticket/record, and the request-to-approval time. From those records compute: (a) % of changes executed by roles listed in the RBAC policy, (b) count/rate of unauthorized or failed modification attempts, (c) median time-to-approve, and (d) % of attributes with an assigned owner and review within policy window.
data_signals: attribute configuration change log entries (user ID, role, timestamp, attribute name, old value, new value)
RBAC role-to-permission mapping (list of roles authorized to modify attributes)
approval workflow records/ticket IDs linked to each configuration change (approver ID, approval timestamp)
failed or denied modification attempts and corresponding audit log entries
attribute inventory with owner and last-reviewed timestamp
training completion records for authorized personnel regarding attribute configuration
change management records linking attribute changes to change requests
security attribute management tool enforcement logs (policy enforcement actions)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, automated change logs with authenticated user IDs, role mapping, before/after values, and linked approval ticket IDs; enforcement logs from attribute management tools showing blocked unauthorized attempts.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or annual attestations without per-change audit logs, screenshots of approvals, or reviewer signatures lacking timestamps or link to the change record.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates RBAC and restriction of who can modify attributes (prevention of unauthorized changes) and establishment of approval workflows, which are preventive controls.

AC-16(10).differentiation_note: This control specifically targets authorization and change controls for security/privacy attribute configuration (who may change attribute values and how changes are approved/audited), rather than broader access control topics like session management or authentication; measurement must center on attribute-change records, approval linkage, and RBAC mappings.

AC-17.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and enforcement of remote access policy: percentage of remote sessions that comply with policy controls (MFA, approved VPN/gateway, endpoint posture), count of unauthorized access attempts, and mean time to respond to remote-access incidents against defined SLA targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AC-17 is primarily preventive (policy, MFA, approved gateways); auditors want coverage percentages (policy compliance, MFA adoption). Counts and rates are useful secondarily for incident volumes and response times.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of remote sessions compliant with the organization's remote access policy (sessions protected by MFA + routed through approved VPN/gateway and with compliant endpoint posture) per day; count of failed/unauthorized remote access attempts and average incident response time from detection to remediation (minutes/hours).
data_signals: VPN/gateway session logs with session_id, user_id, source_ip, timestamp
MFA challenge logs (challenge issued, success/failure, timestamp)
endpoint posture/compliance checks (agent heartbeat, last scan timestamp, patch level)
successful and failed remote login events (RDP/SSH/VPN) with outcome and timestamp
remote access policy document/version and enforcement configuration
incident ticket timestamps for remote-access alerts (detection and remediation times)
user remote access training completion records
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated logs from VPN concentrators and MFA providers are strong evidence;
Endpoint posture telemetry and correlated SIEM alerts strengthen evidence versus isolated manual audit snapshots;
Policy documents alone are weak unless mapped to enforcement logs and session telemetry.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing policy, MFA, VPNs and least privilege — all preventive measures to control remote access.

AC-17.differentiation_note: This base control focuses on broad remote access policy coverage and enforcement metrics (coverage %, incident response times) rather than the specific monitoring, encryption, or protocol-blocking details covered in its enhancements.

AC-17(1).measurement_goal: Measure detection effectiveness and monitoring coverage for remote access: count of anomalous remote access events detected (per day/week), percentage of remote sessions audited/logged, and time-to-investigate remote-access anomalies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AC-17(1) centers on monitoring and control—detective metrics like counts of anomalies/alerts are primary; rates and coverage percentages are useful to show detection density and audit coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of remote access anomalies (SIEM alerts correlated to VPN/RDP/other remote session logs) per time window, percent of remote sessions that are fully logged/retained for audit, and median investigation time from alert creation to triage.
data_signals: VPN/RDP/remote-session logs with timestamps and user_id
SIEM alert records for remote access anomalies (alert_id, severity, timestamp)
authentication logs showing successful/failed logins
endpoint telemetry correlated to remote session (device_id, posture status)
audit log retention and collection configuration (enabled/disabled)
access log review schedules and completed review records
evidence_strength_indicators: High confidence when SIEM alerts are automatically correlated with source session logs and include context (user, device, source IP) vs. manual log reviews;
Regular, automated log collection and retention with timestamped analyst actions is strong; ad-hoc screenshot evidence is weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly calls out monitoring and log review to identify and respond to suspicious activities, which is detective in nature.

AC-17(1).differentiation_note: AC-17(1) specifically demands continuous monitoring/anomaly detection and audit coverage metrics, differentiating it from policy/coverage metrics in AC-17 and encryption-specific metrics in AC-17(2).

AC-17(2).measurement_goal: Measure the degree to which remote sessions use approved encryption: percentage of remote access sessions negotiated with approved protocols/cipher suites (e.g., TLS/IPsec) and count of encryption policy violations or sessions using deprecated ciphers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive (ensure encryption). Percentage of sessions using approved encryption is the most direct evidence; counts/trends show violations and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of remote-access session records where negotiated protocol and cipher suite match the approved list (e.g., TLS 1.2+/strong cipher) per day; number of sessions flagged for unsupported/insecure ciphers and inventory of certificates/keys nearing expiry.
data_signals: remote session TLS/IPsec negotiation logs (protocol version, cipher suite, session timestamp)
VPN configuration and allowed cipher-suite lists
certificate inventory with expiry dates and issuer
key management logs showing key rotations and access
recorded encryption policy violations (session_id, user, timestamp)
penetration test or audit reports noting unencrypted flows
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong evidence: automated session-level telemetry showing negotiated protocol/cipher for every session and keyed to an approved-cipher inventory;
Weak evidence: periodic penetration test reports or manual attestations without session-level telemetry.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The text mandates use of encryption protocols and key management to prevent interception—a preventive control.

AC-17(2).differentiation_note: AC-17(2) focuses narrowly on cryptographic protections and key/cipher hygiene for remote sessions, so measurements must include protocol/cipher-at-session granularity unlike broader policy or logging controls.

AC-17(3).measurement_goal: Measure centralization and correct enforcement of remote access control points: percentage of remote access entry points managed by a centralized access gateway (RBAC + MFA enforced) and frequency of permission review completions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control about managed access points (centralization, RBAC, MFA), coverage percentage of access points behind managed gateways is most useful; counts/trends show review frequency and changes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of remote access endpoints (VPN concentrators, gateways, proxy points) that enforce RBAC + MFA and are in the centralized access management inventory; count of access permission reviews completed on schedule per quarter.
data_signals: inventory of access gateways with management status (managed/unmanaged)
RBAC role assignments and last review timestamp
MFA enforcement logs per gateway (enabled/disabled flags)
VPN/gateway configuration export showing centralized enforcement
records of periodic security assessments and pentest results for gateways
frequency and results of access permission review records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: configuration exports showing centralized enforcement and system-of-record evidence that RBAC/MFA are enforced for each gateway, plus automated review logs;
Weak: checklist attestations that lack configuration evidence or per-gateway logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires deployment of centralized access management and RBAC/MFA enforcement—measures that prevent unauthorized access.

AC-17(3).differentiation_note: AC-17(3) is distinct because it measures centralization and enforcement at access control points (gateway-level coverage and RBAC reviews) rather than session encryption or anomaly detection.

AC-17(4).measurement_goal: Measure control and oversight of privileged remote access: count of privileged remote sessions, percentage of privileged sessions protected by MFA and session recording, and number of unauthorized privileged command attempts (ideally zero).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive emphasis on limiting privileged commands makes percentage metrics (e.g., percent of privileged sessions with MFA/recording) primary; counts of privileged sessions and unauthorized attempts are useful detective complements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of privileged accounts/sessions that require MFA and have session recording enabled; count of privileged command executions per week with user_id, command, start/end timestamps; count of unauthorized privileged access attempts.
data_signals: privileged account inventory and role mapping
privileged session logs (session_id, user, commands executed, timestamps)
MFA logs tied to privileged session authentication
session recording logs or video/command audit trails
access right review records and last review timestamp
incident records for privileged misuse and remediation timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: session-level command audit trails correlated to authentication/MFA events and automated privilege revocation logs;
Weak: periodic spreadsheets listing privileged accounts without session-level activity or recording.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires defining and restricting privileged commands and rights and using MFA/controls to prevent misuse, which is primarily preventive.

AC-17(4).differentiation_note: AC-17(4) specifically targets privileged command execution measurement (command-level auditing and session recording), unlike sibling controls that focus on general session encryption or gateway management.

AC-17(5).measurement_goal: Measure detection of unauthorized remote connections: count of unauthorized connection attempts detected, mean time-to-detect and mean time-to-respond for unauthorized connections, and percentage of connection events matching baseline behavior.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control, counts of unauthorized connection attempts and detection alerts are primary; rates and percentages show detection effectiveness and false positive rates.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of unauthorized remote access attempts detected by monitoring tools (VPN, RDP, Tailscale) per day; median time from detection alert to mitigation action; percentage of remote sessions flagged as anomalous against established baseline behavior.
data_signals: VPN/RDP/Tailscale connection logs (source_ip, destination, user, timestamp, success/failure)
SIEM alerts for unauthorized connections (alert_id, severity, timestamp)
anomaly detection outputs and baseline behavior profiles
incident ticket timestamps (detection, triage, mitigation)
MFA failure records tied to connection attempts
blocked/terminated session records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous SIEM correlation of access logs to anomaly detection with timestamps for detection and automated mitigation actions recorded;
Weak: occasional manual log inspection or one-off reports without event timestamps and correlation to response actions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly states it 'aims to detect and monitor unauthorized remote access connections,' which is a detective function.

AC-17(5).differentiation_note: AC-17(5) is narrowly focused on detecting unauthorized/ anomalous remote connections (counts, detection times) rather than on policy coverage or encryption.

AC-17(6).measurement_goal: Measure protection and hygiene of remote access mechanisms: percentage of remote access mechanisms that have secure configurations (encrypted, patched, keys rotated) and count of outdated/unpatched remote access tools.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive and configuration-oriented; a percentage showing mechanisms meeting secure-configuration and key-management requirements is the clearest evidence, with counts of exceptions as a secondary signal.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of remote access mechanisms (VPN servers, gateways, remote access tools) that are patched to policy level, have approved encryption configured, and have current keys/certificates rotated per policy; count of mechanisms with insecure/default configurations.
data_signals: inventory of remote access mechanisms and versions
patch management records for remote access software (last_patch_date)
configuration snapshots showing encryption settings and allowed protocols
key/certificate inventory with rotation history and expiry dates
IDS/IPS/Firewall configurations related to remote access
vulnerability scan findings for remote access tooling
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated configuration scans and patch management records tied to the canonical inventory and key-management system;
Weak: ad-hoc/manual attestations or one-time penetration tests without ongoing evidence of key rotation and patch status.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires safeguarding mechanism information through encryption, patching, and authentication mechanisms—preventive measures to reduce exploitation risk.

AC-17(6).differentiation_note: AC-17(6) emphasizes security hygiene of the remote access mechanisms themselves (patching, keys, configs) rather than per-session encryption or anomaly detection.

AC-17(7).measurement_goal: Measure additional protections for access to security functions: percentage of security-function access attempts protected by mandatory MFA and privileged access controls, and count of failed MFA attempts for security functions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive focus on additional protections (MFA for security functions) makes percentage (coverage of MFA and controls) primary; counts/rates track failures and enforcement events.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of accesses to identified security functions (console, SIEM admin, firewall management) that required and succeeded MFA and RBAC authorization; count of MFA failures and anomalous access attempts to security functions.
data_signals: access logs for security functions (user, function/resource, timestamp)
MFA success/failure logs tied to security-function sessions
RBAC role assignments for security functions
session records for security-function access (duration, commands executed)
user behavior analytics flags for anomalous security-function access
training/compliance records for personnel authorized to access security functions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: per-session authentication logs linking MFA success to security-function actions and automated enforcement of RBAC rules;
Weak: manual approvals or spreadsheets listing authorized users without per-session authentication evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates stronger access controls (MFA, logging) specifically for security functions, which prevents unauthorized use of critical functions.

AC-17(7).differentiation_note: AC-17(7) is specialized for security-function access and requires metrics tied to those privileged admin functions (MFA success rates and RBAC enforcement) rather than general user remote access.

AC-17(8).measurement_goal: Measure elimination of insecure remote protocols: percentage of remote access services/configurations that have nonsecure protocols (Telnet, FTP) disabled and count of any detected connections using prohibited protocols.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive configuration control—percentage of services/configs that do not allow insecure protocols is the clearest evidence; counts of prohibited-protocol detections are useful to find exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Point-in-time percentage of remote access services/configurations where insecure protocols (Telnet, FTP, legacy RDP without encryption) are disabled; list and count of endpoints still offering insecure protocols.
data_signals: service inventory with enabled protocols per host (Telnet/FTP/SSH/RDP flags)
firewall and ACL rules blocking nonsecure protocols
network traffic logs showing protocol types and connections
configuration management snapshots showing disabled services
vulnerability scan or configuration scan results identifying insecure protocols
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated configuration scans and network traffic evidence showing zero active insecure-protocol listeners and firewall blocks recorded;
Weak: manual attestations or occasional scans without inventory reconciliation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires disabling insecure protocols and enforcing secure protocols—this is a preventive configuration control.

AC-17(8).differentiation_note: AC-17(8) is a configuration/compliance check focused on disabling specific insecure protocols (Telnet/FTP) and is best evidenced by point-in-time inventory and firewall rules, unlike continuous-session controls.

AC-17(9).measurement_goal: Measure the organization's ability to disconnect or disable remote access: average time to disconnect a remote session or disable an account after detection/authorization removal, and percentage of incidents where disconnect/removal occurred within SLA.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is corrective/response-focused; time-based rate metrics (mean/median time-to-disconnect or to disable access) directly measure corrective performance; counts of terminations and percentages meeting SLA supplement evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Median and 95th-percentile time (minutes) from alert or authorized request to actual session disconnect or account disablement; count of sessions disconnected and count of access privileges revoked per incident, and percent of disconnections completed within defined SLA.
data_signals: active session inventory with start_time and disconnect_time
disconnect command or automation run logs with timestamp and operator_id
account disablement/change logs with timestamps
incident/ticket timestamps indicating detection and remediation actions
automation logs for forced disconnect or revocation actions
SLA definitions for time-to-disconnect per incident type
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated orchestration logs showing immediate disconnects with timestamps and correlation to alert IDs and incident tickets;
Weak: retrospective manual reports or attestations without precise timestamps tying detection to disconnect action.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on the ability to terminate sessions or disable access in response to incidents, which is corrective action.

AC-17(9).differentiation_note: AC-17(9) is focused on response metrics (time-to-disconnect/disable) and automation of session termination, unlike other controls that focus on prevention or detection.

AC-17(10).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of remote command executions that were authenticated according to policy (e.g., MFA/hardware token), the count and rate of failed or unauthenticated remote command attempts, and the mean time to revoke remote access after user offboarding or role change.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive — auditors need coverage/compliance percentages (e.g., % of commands authenticated) as the primary measure; counts and rates of failed/unauthenticated commands and trends over time provide supporting detection and remediation context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage = (number of remote command executions with a successful policy-compliant authentication / total remote command executions) * 100; (2) counts and rate of failed authentication attempts associated with command executions by user and source IP; (3) mean and distribution of time-to-revoke remote access after termination/permission removal; (4) enforcement of session timeouts by measuring session durations vs configured timeout.
data_signals: remote command execution records correlated with authentication token/credential IDs (timestamp, userID, commandID)
MFA challenge/response logs and outcome (success/failure) per session
failed authentication attempt logs tied to command requests (count, user, source IP)
session start/end timestamps and session timeout enforcement events
access revocation/deprovisioning timestamps and confirmation of session termination
inventory of remote access endpoints and their IAM/PAM integration status
authentication protocol/configuration change history (protocol versions, applied updates)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous machine-parsed authentication + command-execution logs that can be correlated by session/command ID and show MFA success/failure and revocation timestamps
Strong: automated PAM/IAM telemetry showing enforcement of session timeouts and immediate session termination on deprovision
Weak: periodic manual attestations or screenshots of settings without correlated command-level logs
Weak: standalone MFA configuration records without evidence of command-level enforcement or session correlation

affinity_keywords: inventory of all remote access points

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires that commands be authenticated before execution (prevention) and emphasizes MFA, unique credentials, and session timeouts to stop unauthorized actions.

AC-17(10).differentiation_note: This control targets command-level authentication and session enforcement for remote access (command execution correlation, MFA validation, and revoke timing), not general network remote-access inventory or periodic access reviews; measurements must tie authentication events directly to command execution records.

AC-18.measurement_goal: Measure wireless configuration and operational compliance across the estate (percentage of APs/devices using approved encryption/authentication and patched firmware), plus counts of wireless-related security incidents and unauthorized connection attempts over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AC-18 is primarily preventive (verify coverage and correct configuration) so percentage metrics (compliance %) are most useful; counts of incidents and trends over time are helpful secondary detective measures referenced by the control.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of wireless access points and endpoint devices configured with approved encryption/authentication (e.g., WPA3, 802.1X), percentage with up-to-date firmware, count of unauthorized wireless access attempts and wireless-related incidents, and compliance rate of devices connecting to the network versus inventoryed devices.
data_signals: access point configuration records (encryption protocol field, auth method field)
endpoint inventory with wireless capability flag and connection compliance status
firmware/software version list for WAPs and date of last update
authentication logs (802.1X/RADIUS success/failure counts) with timestamps
unauthorized access attempt counts from wireless monitoring tools
incident/ security event records labeled 'wireless' with timestamps and resolution status
guest network isolation configuration and traffic logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, continuous collection of AP configs, auth logs and WAP firmware inventory (strong).
Regularly scheduled scans/site surveys showing coverage and signal leakage (strong).
One-off manual checklist or screenshots without logs (weak).
Incident counts with full audit trails and remediation timestamps (strong) vs. summary emails without timestamps (weak).
