control_type_classification.reasoning: Control focuses on establishing secure configurations, encryption, segmentation and policy to prevent unauthorized wireless access.

AC-18.differentiation_note: This entry covers broad wireless control objectives (policy, encryption, firmware, segmentation and general monitoring) rather than a single technical enforcement or detection mechanism.

AC-18(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of wireless devices and APs enforcing strong authentication/encryption (e.g., WPA3, 802.1X with RADIUS) and track counts of devices using legacy/insecure protocols and frequency of encryption key rotations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement is preventive (verify enforcement of auth/encryption). Percentage coverage of approved auth/encryption is the strongest indicator; counts of legacy protocol instances and trends of key rotation complement it.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of APs and connecting devices configured for WPA3 or enterprise 802.1X (RADIUS) vs total wireless-capable devices; count of APs or clients still permitting legacy protocols; timestamps of last encryption key rotation and audit frequency.
data_signals: AP configuration export showing encryption protocol (WPA3/WPA2/legacy) per AP
802.1X/RADIUS authentication logs (successful auths, failures, client identifiers)
device connection telemetry indicating negotiated cipher suites
inventory of devices by supported protocol (legacy vs WPA3)
encryption key rotation schedule and rotation timestamps
audit/assessment reports of wireless security settings
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous capture of auth protocol negotiated per session and RADIUS logs (strong).
Automated configuration baselines showing zero APs using legacy protocols (strong).
Single manual audit snapshot without historical auth logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text requires enforcing and configuring strong authentication/encryption (preventive configuration controls such as disabling legacy protocols and using 802.1X).

AC-18(1).differentiation_note: Focuses narrowly on authentication and encryption enforcement (protocols, key rotation and disabling legacy protocols) rather than broad wireless policy or physical signal management.

AC-18(2).measurement_goal: Measure detection coverage and responsiveness: count of unauthorized wireless connection attempts detected, median and 95th percentile response/remediation time after alerts, and false positive rate against a baseline of legitimate devices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is explicitly detective; counts of unauthorized connection attempts and rates of detection/remediation are the primary evidence; tracking remediation time and trends are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of unauthorized connection attempts detected by WIDS/WIPS per time period, device types detected, timestamps for detection and remediation, and baseline legitimate device list to calculate false positive/true positive rates.
data_signals: WIDS/WIPS alert logs with timestamp, detected device MAC, SSID, signal strength
baseline inventory of legitimate devices and authorized MACs
alert acknowledgement and remediation timestamps (ticket or incident record)
classification of device types from monitoring (e.g., phone, laptop, IoT)
counts of recurring unauthorized devices and location/AP mapping
frequency of unauthorized access events per site or per AP
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time WIDS/WIPS alerts with device identifiers and automatic correlation to inventory (strong).
Recorded remediation tickets with timestamps showing closure times (strong).
Manual ad-hoc reports without alert metadata or timestamps (weak).
High fidelity device-type attribution and location mapping (strong) vs. generic 'unknown device' entries (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description specifies continuous monitoring and detection of unauthorized devices via WIDS and alerting, which are detective activities.

AC-18(2).differentiation_note: This entry is focused on detection and response metrics (WIDS alerts, response times, false positive rates) rather than configuration or physical signal controls.

AC-18(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of inventoryed devices with wireless interfaces disabled where not required and count of exceptions maintained; track unauthorized wireless enablement events and periodic audit completion rates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percentage of devices compliant (wireless disabled when not required) best demonstrates enforcement; counts of non-compliant devices and trends are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of managed endpoints with wireless network interfaces disabled in the device management inventory, number and justification of devices on the exceptions list, and counts/timestamps of audits verifying wireless-disabled state.
data_signals: endpoint management inventory showing wireless capability enabled/disabled flag
centralized management tool change logs showing wireless interface disablement actions
exceptions registry (device IDs and business justification)
audit logs/results from periodic device configuration reviews
logs of unauthorized attempts to enable wireless interfaces
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated endpoint management records showing disabled interfaces across inventory (strong).
Exception registry with approved justifications and periodic revalidation (strong).
Ad-hoc manual checks without centralized inventory correlation (weak).
Device-side logs showing attempts to re-enable wireless and corresponding enforcement action (strong).

affinity_keywords: unauthorized attempts to access the network wirelessly

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control requires disabling wireless interfaces on devices that do not need them, a preventive configuration/enforcement activity.

AC-18(3).differentiation_note: Targets endpoint-level enforcement (disabling radios and managing exceptions) rather than AP configuration, authentication, or monitoring activities.

AC-18(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of wireless management accounts and systems using RBAC and MFA, count/config-change events by non-authorized users, and frequency of access rights reviews completed on schedule.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control focusing on limiting configuration changes; percentage coverage of RBAC/MFA and scheduled reviews are primary. Counts of unauthorized config changes are useful secondary detective metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of wireless management consoles/users assigned least-privilege RBAC roles and protected by MFA; counts of configuration change events, identity of change initiator, and timestamps; completion rate of periodic access rights reviews.
data_signals: wireless management system user role assignments and last review timestamp
authentication configuration (MFA enabled flag) for admin accounts
configuration change logs with user, timestamp and change diff
access rights review records and attestation timestamps
alerts for configuration changes from unauthorized accounts
evidence_strength_indicators: Immutable change logs with user identity, diff and ticket linkage (strong).
Automated RBAC enforcement and MFA status exported from management systems (strong).
Manual attestations without logs (weak).
Regularly completed, timestamped access rights reviews with remediation actions (strong).

affinity_keywords: logging and monitoring of configuration changes

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires restricting configuration changes via RBAC and controls on administrative privileges, which are preventive controls.

AC-18(4).differentiation_note: Specifically measures governance of wireless configuration changes (RBAC/MFA and change logs) rather than network or endpoint wireless settings.

AC-18(5).measurement_goal: Measure signal containment and configuration compliance: distribution of measured signal strength outside intended coverage, percentage of WAPs with documented power output settings matching survey baselines, and counts of APs with directional vs omnidirectional antenna misconfigurations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about physical/technical signal characteristics; distributions of signal strength and power output across locations are primary to show containment, with percentage compliance of configured power/antenna settings as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Distribution of signal strength (dBm) measured at sample points inside and outside intended coverage, per-AP configured transmission power outputs and antenna types, and results of site survey/spectrum analysis showing signal leakage and compliance with documented settings.
data_signals: wireless site survey output (signal strength measurements by location, dBm)
AP configuration records: transmit power setting, antenna type (directional/omni)
spectrum analyzer reports
mapping of AP coverage polygons vs intended coverage areas
change logs for power/antenna setting adjustments and timestamps
counts of unauthorized detections correlated with signal leakage zones
evidence_strength_indicators: Comprehensive site survey and spectrum analysis data tied to AP configuration (strong).
Automated monitoring of AP transmit power/settings and alerting on deviations (strong).
Single visual inspection without measurement artifacts (weak).
Historical comparisons showing adjustments after environmental changes (strong).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control mandates design and configuration of antennas and power levels to prevent signal leakage—preventive configuration and physical control.

AC-18(5).differentiation_note: Unique focus on physical RF characteristics and site-survey measurable signal containment (dBm distributions) rather than auth, endpoint, or configuration governance.

AC-19.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of organization-controlled mobile devices enrolled in MDM with required security configurations (MFA, approved connection protocols), count of unauthorized access attempts from mobile devices, and frequency of mobile-device-related security incidents and policy audit findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage/compliance (device enrollment, MFA, configuration) so percentage metrics best show coverage; counts/rates of unauthorized attempts and trends show residual risk and incident frequency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) % of organization-controlled mobile devices actively enrolled in MDM with MFA enforced and required configuration profile applied; (2) count and rate of unauthorized access attempts by mobile device per period; (3) number of mobile-device-related security incidents and results of most recent mobile device policy audits.
data_signals: MDM enrollment records and timestamps
configuration profile compliance flags (MFA enabled, allowed connection protocols)
authentication logs showing MFA success/failure for mobile device accounts
unauthorized access attempt logs filtered by device type
mobile-device incident tickets and incident classification
mobile device inventory (device ID, owner, managed/unmanaged)
policy audit reports and findings for mobile devices
app update/vetting timestamps and app inventory for mobile applications
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated MDM enrollment and configuration compliance logs, authentication logs showing MFA enforcement, and recent automated audits.
Weak: annual/manual inventory spreadsheets, ad-hoc screenshots of MDM console, or user attestations without system logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description repeatedly frames the control as preventive and mandates policy, MDM, and MFA to prevent unauthorized access.

AC-19.differentiation_note: General mobile-device access control focused on organization-controlled devices and overall enforcement; other siblings specialize on portable storage, personal devices, classified-data restrictions, or encryption specifics.

AC-19(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of portable storage devices (organization-owned) inventoried and compliant with encryption and endpoint protection standards, the count/frequency of portable-media policy violations, and time-to-remediate non-compliant devices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive emphasis on coverage/compliance of portable storage and encryption makes percentage (coverage/compliance) the primary measure; counts/rates capture violations and incident frequency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) % of portable storage devices (from inventory) with approved encryption at rest/in transit and endpoint protection agents active; (2) number of logged portable-media policy violations per period; (3) mean time to remediate encryption or configuration deficiencies on portable devices.
data_signals: portable storage device inventory (serial/ID, owner, assigned status)
encryption status for device (data at rest / data in transit flags)
endpoint protection agent telemetry on portable storage access
device usage/access attempt logs referencing portable storage
policy violation logs or incidents tied to portable devices
remediation ticket timestamps and resolution status
audit reports showing compliance with encryption standards
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory and encryption state from endpoint/MDM and tamper-resistant logs showing portable device usage plus remediation records.
Weak: manual checklists or occasional audits without system-sourced logs, or uncorroborated user reports of device usage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text specifies policies, encryption standards, and endpoint protection to prevent data breaches from portable storage, indicating a preventive control.

AC-19(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on portable storage/media (organization-owned) and encryption/endpoint-protection compliance rather than broader mobile device configuration or personal devices.

AC-19(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of personally owned mobile and portable devices enrolled or controlled by MDM/endpoint policy, percentage compliant with required configurations (VPN, encryption, MFA), and count of security incidents attributable to personally owned devices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control governing personally owned devices, percentage coverage/enrollment is the primary signal; incident counts and trends help show residual risk from BYOD.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) % of personally owned devices that are enrolled in the MDM or covered by required controls (VPN usage, encryption, MFA); (2) count of reported security incidents traced to personally owned devices; (3) frequency of security audits of BYOD devices and percentage passing configuration standards.
data_signals: MDM enrollment records with device ownership attribute (personal/organization)
VPN connection logs showing device IDs
encryption/compliance flags for personally owned devices
authentication logs showing MFA usage from personal devices
incident reports/tickets linked to personally owned devices
percentage of personally owned devices meeting security configuration standards
training completion records related to BYOD policy
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system-sourced MDM enrollment and compliance telemetry for personal devices, VPN logs, and correlated incident tickets.
Weak: user attestations of compliance, spreadsheets listing devices without automated compliance verification.

affinity_keywords: percentage of devices meeting security configuration standards

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control prescribes policy and MDM enrollment for personally owned devices to prevent data exposure, fitting a preventive classification.

AC-19(2).differentiation_note: Targets personally owned (BYOD) devices and enrollment/compliance of those devices specifically, unlike sibling controls that focus on organization-owned devices or portable media without owner.

AC-19(3).measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of connections from portable storage or mobile devices with no identifiable owner, percentage of network connections blocked/isolated due to unknown-device policy, and number of unauthorized-access detections tied to unapproved devices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control emphasizes preventing unknown/unapproved devices from connecting; counts of unidentifiable-device connections and rates of blocking are primary detective/preventive signals while percentage coverage shows enforcement breadth.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) count of connection events from devices with no owner identifier blocked or quarantined by network/MDM per period; (2) % of device connection attempts classified as unapproved and successfully prevented; (3) incident records for data loss or unauthorized access linked to unowned portable storage.
data_signals: network NAC/MDM logs showing device authentication failures or unknown device identifiers
connection attempt records with device ID blank/unknown
blocked/quarantined device event logs
portable storage device discovery logs with no owner attribute
unauthorized access alerts correlated to unknown-device events
policy enforcement logs showing restriction actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated NAC/MDM logs showing unknown-device detection and automated blocking/quarantine actions with timestamps and outcomes.
Weak: anecdotal reports of unapproved devices or one-off manual removals without system logs.

affinity_keywords: portable storage devices with no identifiable owner

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text directs restriction of unapproved/unowned devices and use of authentication/monitoring to prevent unauthorized access, which is preventive in intent.

AC-19(3).differentiation_note: Specifically addresses devices lacking owner attribution and enforcement (detection/blocking) of unapproved connections, which requires telemetry on unknown-device connection events rather than general MDM compliance.

AC-19(4).measurement_goal: Measure the count of access attempts to classified information from mobile devices (authorized vs. unauthorized), percentage of mobile devices with role/classification-based access controls applied, and percentage of devices capable of remote wipe/encryption for classified data.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control enforcing role & classification-based restrictions is best evidenced by percent coverage of devices with appropriate controls; counts of unauthorized access attempts are useful secondary detective metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) % of mobile devices with access controls mapped to user roles and information classification levels (ACL/policy applied); (2) count of access attempts to classified datasets from mobile devices and count of unauthorized attempts; (3) presence and success logs for remote wipe and encryption capability for devices handling classified information.
data_signals: access control lists/role-based access mapping records tied to device IDs
classification labels on data and policy bindings enforcing device restrictions
access attempt logs to classified resources with device identifiers and outcome
remote wipe execution logs and device wipe capability flags
device encryption status and encryption protocol metadata
policy review records and audit logs for classified-data access
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: correlated access logs showing role/classification enforcement, system-enforced ACLs, and remote-wipe/encryption telemetry for devices handling classified data.
Weak: manual role assignment documents without log evidence, or paper-based policy statements without technical enforcement records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control prescribes role/classification-based enforcement, encryption, MFA and remote wipe to prevent unauthorized access to classified information, indicating preventive measures.

AC-19(4).differentiation_note: Unique in its focus on classification-aware access controls for classified information and remote-wipe/encryption capability, requiring data classification and ACL enforcement telemetry rather than general device config compliance.

AC-19(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of mobile devices with full-device or container-based encryption enabled and compliant with approved algorithms (e.g., AES-256), count of devices with missing/invalid encryption keys, and incidents where unencrypted sensitive data on mobile devices was exposed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control mandating encryption coverage, percentage of devices encrypted is primary; counts of missing/invalid keys and trend of encryption compliance support remediation tracking.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) % of devices with full-device encryption or approved container-based encryption enabled and reporting compliant algorithm/version (e.g., AES-256); (2) count of encryption key management issues (missing, expired, or improperly stored keys); (3) number of incidents involving unencrypted sensitive data on mobile devices.
data_signals: device encryption status flags (full-device or container-based) and algorithm/version metadata
encryption key management logs (key issuance, rotation, expiry, failures)
MDM reports showing encryption compliance by device
incident reports listing exposure of unencrypted mobile data
audit logs from periodic encryption compliance checks
device inventory with encryption compliance timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated MDM/endpoint telemetry showing encryption algorithm and key management events, plus periodic scan reports confirming full coverage.
Weak: self-reported encryption status, one-off encryption certificates without ongoing telemetry, or lack of key-management logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mandates encryption and key management to prevent data exposure on mobile devices, which is a classic preventive control.

AC-19(5).differentiation_note: Specifically requires measuring encryption state and key-management health (algorithm/version, key lifecycle), unlike siblings that focus on enrollment, ownership, or classification-based access.

AC-20.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of external systems that are inventoried and assessed against the organization's security criteria, the count of security incidents involving external systems, and time-to-remediate vulnerabilities or incidents originating from external systems.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is primarily preventive/governance-focused: auditors need coverage and compliance rates (percentage of vendors/systems meeting criteria). Counts and trends are secondary to show incident volumes and remediation velocity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of all known external systems that have a completed risk assessment and current security posture score; count of incidents attributed to external systems with timestamps and remediation ticket lifecycle (open -> resolved); average and median time-to-remediate for external-system-originated findings.
data_signals: external system inventory records (vendor id, system id, classification)
risk assessment completion timestamps and assessment results
security assessment / audit result records for each external system
contractual clauses / data handling controls presence flag per vendor
incident records tagged with 'external system' with timestamps and remediation ticket ids
encryption / data transfer configuration evidence (e.g., TLS enabled flags)
continuous monitoring alerts related to external-system connections
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated inventory and assessment records with machine-readable timestamps and severity scores (strong);
Continuous monitoring alerts correlated to inventory entries and incident tickets (strong);
One-off manual assessment reports or ad-hoc spreadsheets without timestamps or linkage to inventory (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing policies, inventories, and risk assessments to prevent insecure integrations ('establishing a framework for evaluating and managing external systems').

AC-20.differentiation_note: This control is the broad governance/assessment requirement for all external systems (inventory, risk assessments, contractual controls) whereas the siblings focus on specific allowed/restricted/prohibited device or ownership scenarios.

AC-20(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of external-system access assignments that are authorized via the defined approval workflow and the count of unauthorized access incidents per review period; measure timeliness of access revocation requests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on authorization and policy compliance, so percentage of access assignments in compliance is the primary verifier; counts of unauthorized accesses and trend over review cycles are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active external-system accounts that have an associated approved access request and role justification; counts of access requests, approvals, denials, and revocations with timestamps; count of unauthorized access incidents and time from detection to revocation.
data_signals: access request and approval workflow logs for external systems (requestor, approver, timestamps)
access control lists and role assignments for external systems
access revocation records with timestamps
authentication logs showing external-system logins tagged by user id and system id
EDR or endpoint logs where external-system usage is monitored
training completion records for users authorized to use external systems
audit logs showing periodic access reviews and their results
evidence_strength_indicators: System-generated access request/approval records linked to IAM and external-system accounts (strong);
Manual spreadsheets or anecdotal approvals without timestamps or approver identity (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing policies, approval workflows, and restrictions to ensure only authorized personnel may access external systems ('process for granting and revoking access').

AC-20(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on authorization workflows and access revocation metrics for external-system use, unlike AC-20 which is broader governance of external integrations.

AC-20(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of portable storage device uses that comply with policy (encrypted, authorized user, allowed device) and count incidents of unauthorized portable-device usage or data transfers to portable devices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive (restricting permitted portable device use), so coverage/ compliance percentages (allowed vs unauthorized uses) are primary; counts of violations and trend over time are needed to detect residual risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of portable storage device connections to managed endpoints that are from approved device IDs and show encryption-enabled; count of connection attempts by unauthorized devices and incidents of data copied to portable devices with timestamps and user ids.
data_signals: endpoint device connection logs showing USB/portable device IDs and host ids
MDM/endpoint policy records indicating approved device lists
encryption status flags for portable device mounts
DLP logs showing file transfer events to portable devices
audit records of periodic portable device policy reviews
training completion records for users on portable device policy
incident records for data loss events involving portable devices
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated endpoint logs correlated with approved-device registry and DLP events (strong);
Periodic manual spot-checks or user self-reports without logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates policies and technical restrictions (encryption, permitted devices) to restrict use of portable storage devices ('establish a clear policy... which portable storage devices are permitted').

AC-20(2).differentiation_note: Specifically targets portable-storage device usage and requires device-level logs and DLP evidence, unlike other siblings which address broader external-system or network storage policies.

AC-20(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of users accessing non-organizationally owned systems in compliance with policy (approved, using VPN/encryption) and the count and severity of incidents originating from non-organizational systems.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily preventive (restricting and conditioning use of non-org-owned systems) so compliance percentages (approved use, VPN/encryption usage) are primary; counts/rates of incidents are secondary to detect policy breaches.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of non-organizational-system access events that were performed over approved controls (VPN, encryption) and had prior risk assessment approval; counts of unauthorized connections from non-organizational systems and incident records with timestamps and user ids.
data_signals: access logs showing connections from non-organizational system IPs/hosts with VPN flag
VPN session logs (user id, start/end, destination system)
risk assessment/approval records for non-organizational system usage
DLP or network-monitoring alerts for data transfers to/from non-org systems
training completion records on acceptable use of non-organizational systems
incident/ticket records tagged 'non-organizational system' with remediation timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Correlated VPN/session logs, risk-approval records, and incident tickets indicating blocked or mitigated attempts (strong);
User attestations or periodic surveys without log correlation (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes policies, approvals and technical controls (VPN, encryption, access restrictions) to prevent unsafe use of non-organizational systems ('establish a clear policy... conditions under which such systems may be used').

AC-20(3).differentiation_note: Focuses on non-organization-owned systems and requires VPN/session and approval evidence rather than device-level or network-storage-specific signals.

AC-20(4).measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of attempted or successful connections to prohibited network-accessible storage devices and the percentage of network segments enforcing the prohibition (blocked vs allowed connections).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is a prohibition with technical enforcement; percentage of enforcement coverage (network segments enforcing prohibition) is the primary preventive metric, while counts/rates of detected/prohibited connection attempts are useful detective/operational metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of network segments and endpoints where access to network-accessible storage devices is blocked per policy; counts of detected unauthorized device connections or attempted mounts with timestamps, source/destination IPs, and enforcement action (blocked/allowed).
data_signals: network access control / NAC policies and enforcement logs per segment
network traffic logs showing SMB/NFS mounts and destination storage IPs
DLP events for transfers to network-mounted storage
endpoint protection logs indicating attempted mounts of network storage
inventory of approved vs prohibited network storage device IPs/hostnames
incident reports for breaches involving network-accessible storage devices
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time NAC and network traffic logs showing enforcement actions (block) correlated with inventory of prohibited storage devices (strong);
Periodic manual network scans without enforcement logs or missed linkage to incidents (weak).

affinity_keywords: Network Accessible Storage Devices — Prohibited Use

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires prohibiting specific device classes and enforcing that prohibition via network segmentation and access controls ('prohibiting the use of such devices... technical measures ... to enforce this prohibition').

AC-20(4).differentiation_note: Targets network-accessible storage specifically and requires network/ NAC/traffic-level enforcement and logs, differing from portable-device or general external-system controls.

AC-20(5).measurement_goal: Measure the count of attempts to connect organization-controlled portable storage devices to external systems, the rate at which such attempts are blocked, and the percentage of endpoints with USB/portable device ports disabled or enforced by policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a prohibition that should be enforced at endpoints, the proportion of endpoints with enforcement (percentage) is the primary preventive metric; counts of blocked attempts and comparison of blocked vs allowed provide detective context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of managed endpoints with USB ports disabled or with endpoint policies that block mounting of organization-controlled portable storage on external systems; counts of connection attempts and DLP events showing attempted data transfers to external-system hosts with timestamps and device ids.
data_signals: MDM/endpoint configuration inventory showing USB/port policy status per endpoint
endpoint logs showing USB mount attempts, device serial/ID and host identifiers
DLP events showing attempted file write to removable media when connected to external systems
incident/ticket records for policy violations involving portable storage on external systems
audit logs of periodic checks for disabled USB ports and policy enforcement
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated endpoint configuration records and real-time USB mount logs correlated with DLP actions and blocked outcomes (strong);
User-reported incidents or manual audits without machine logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control directs organizations to prohibit use and implement technical measures (disable ports, endpoint blocks) to prevent prohibited device connections ('explicitly prohibits the use... disabling USB ports... endpoint protection').

AC-20(5).differentiation_note: Specifically prohibits organization-controlled portable devices on external systems and therefore requires endpoint configuration state and USB mount logs rather than network or vendor-assessment evidence.

AC-21.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of formal information sharing: percent of relevant stakeholder groups covered by formal agreements, frequency of intelligence exchanges per partner, and count of incidents where shared intelligence contributed to mitigation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., percent of partners under formal agreements) while counts and trends show volume and activity; quality can be represented as a score.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of defined internal and external stakeholder groups covered by formal information sharing agreements; count of intelligence exchanges (by partner and channel) per period; number of incidents mitigated where shared intelligence was cited as a contributing factor; stakeholder engagement (attendance/participation) rates for sharing forums and trainings.
data_signals: inventory of formal information sharing agreements with partner identifiers and effective dates
threat intelligence exchange logs (timestamps, sender, recipient, channel)
participation/attendance records for ISACs or sharing forums
incidents with attribution field indicating 'mitigated_by_shared_intel' or linkage to shared advisory IDs
training completion records for information sharing processes
message/channel encryption/secure channel usage logs
frequency counts of intelligence items shared per partner per month
evidence_strength_indicators: High strength: automated exchange logs tied to formal agreement records and documented incident mitigations referencing shared intel.
Medium strength: periodic manual reports of sharing activity combined with meeting minutes.
Weak evidence: one-off email threads or anecdotal claims without logged deliveries, timestamps, or linkage to incident outcomes.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description labels the control as preventive and emphasizes establishing agreements, communication methods, and training to proactively enable sharing.

AC-21.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on governance and participation coverage (agreements, partner coverage, training) rather than technical integration or retrieval capabilities covered by its siblings.

AC-21(1).measurement_goal: Measure the integration and effectiveness of automated decision-support: percent of relevant feeds integrated and normalized, quality/precision score of automated indicators, and change in mean decision/response time attributable to automated shared intelligence.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated decision-support requires qualitative/quantitative scoring of intelligence relevance and precision (score) while rates and counts show integration volume and response-time improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate integration coverage (percent of defined intelligence sources normalized into the platform), an automated intelligence quality/precision score (e.g., true positive rate of indicators), time-to-decision or response before vs after automation, and the count of incidents where automated dissemination triggered an action.
data_signals: list of integrated threat feeds with ingestion timestamps and normalization status (STIX/TAXII endpoints, feed IDs)
automation event logs showing alerts/decisions generated (timestamp, rule/indicator ID, downstream action)
decision/response time metrics (timestamp of intel receipt → timestamp of decision/action)
mapping of shared intelligence items to incident records (indicator ID → incident ID)
privacy/compliance flags for shared data (GDPR/PII markers)
counts of false positives/false negatives for automated indicators (labelled outcomes)
evidence_strength_indicators: High strength: automated ingestion logs with STIX/TAXII endpoint telemetry, measured reduction in decision/response time, and incident records showing automated-intel-driven actions.
Medium strength: manual SLA reports and periodic metrics without end-to-end linkage between feed ingestion and response.
Weak evidence: manual summaries of decisions or anecdotal improvement claims without timestamps or feed integration logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control text emphasizes automated aggregation and dissemination to enable proactive decisions, which is preventive in nature.

AC-21(1).differentiation_note: Unlike the base AC-21, this control requires machine-readable feed integration (STIX/TAXII), automated event logs, and measured impact on decision/response times rather than governance/participation coverage.

AC-21(2).measurement_goal: Measure secure availability and access success for shared information: percent of critical shared assets searchable/retrievable by authorized roles, success/failure rate of information retrieval attempts, and frequency of data-access audits for shared repositories.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: primary measurement is access/coverage percentages (authorized access and encryption coverage); rates and counts capture retrieval attempts and failures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure percent of critical shared datasets/sources that are encrypted in transit/at rest and accessible to authorized roles, retrieval success rate (successful vs failed retrieval attempts) by user role, number of access audit events and number of policy violations found during audits.
data_signals: secure data sharing platform access logs (user ID, role, timestamp, resource ID, retrieval outcome)
encryption-at-rest and in-transit configuration records for shared repositories
data classification tags for shared items (sensitivity labels)
counts of successful vs failed information-sharing attempts
data access audit reports and findings (audit timestamps, violations)
gap analysis outputs against NIST CSF Implementation Tiers
evidence_strength_indicators: High strength: continuous access logs with role-based authorization checks, encryption configuration records, and audit reports showing remediation of identified gaps.
Medium strength: periodic manual audits and sample retrieval tests without full log coverage.
Weak evidence: anecdotal assertions of secure sharing or one-time tests without logged retrieval events or classification metadata.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing access controls, encryption, and procedural safeguards to prevent unauthorized access—typical preventive controls.

AC-21(2).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on secure retrieval, access controls, encryption, and auditability of shared data rather than agreement coverage (AC-21) or automated feed processing (AC-21(1)).

AC-22.measurement_goal: Measure the size and coverage of the publicly accessible asset inventory (count of assets and percent with sensitivity classification), the frequency and coverage of audits/reviews, the count and rate of unauthorized access/data exposure incidents related to public content, and the median time-to-remediate identified public exposures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage and compliance measurements (e.g., percent of public content reviewed/classified). Counts (assets, incidents) and trending (audit cadence, remediation time over time) supplement coverage metrics to show scope and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A queryable inventory of publicly accessible assets (URLs, hosts, buckets) with sensitivity classification and latest scan timestamp; audit/review records with reviewer and timestamp; access/authorization snapshots for each public asset; incident records and remediation tickets with timestamps to compute time-to-remediate and overdue items against policy.
data_signals: inventory of publicly accessible assets (host/URL/S3 bucket identifiers) with sensitivity classification tags
web crawler / external scan results and execution timestamps for each public URL/endpoint
storage ACL/public flag snapshots (e.g., S3 bucket public flag, blob container permissions)
audit/review logs indicating review date, reviewer, and coverage (assets reviewed)
access logs/authorization checks showing unauthorized access attempts to public assets (timestamps, source IP)
incident tickets for data exposure related to public content with open/close timestamps
remediation/change request records showing remediation action and completion timestamp
encryption-at-rest status or metadata for public assets
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped inventory and external scan/crawler logs showing asset discovery and last-scan time combined with access logs and remediation ticket timestamps.
Strong: periodic snapshots of storage ACLs/public flags showing no unintended public exposures over time.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or one-off screenshots of public pages without timestamps or audit trail.
Weak: annual manual reviews only, without continuous monitoring or access logs correlating incidents to public assets.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing policy, inventory, classification, and preventive controls (access controls, encryption, audits) to avoid inadvertent exposure.

AC-22.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets management, inventory, and monitoring of intentionally public content (discovery, classification, remediation SLAs), unlike sibling access controls that focus on internal access provisioning or authentication metrics.

AC-23.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of controls that prevent unauthorized data mining: percentage of sensitive data assets classified and protected by DLP/encryption, count and rate of DLP and ML-detected anomalous access/exfiltration attempts (blocked and successful), and remediation/closure rate of data leakage incidents against policy thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage and compliance measurements (percentage of classified/protected assets, encryption coverage) as primary evidence; counts and rates of DLP/ML alerts and incident trends are necessary secondary measures to show operational effectiveness and detection activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of data stores/records tagged with sensitive classification and covered by active DLP policies and encryption; counts and timestamps of DLP policy violations and ML anomaly detections (including user ID, resource, destination, action) and whether the events were blocked or allowed; incident records with remediation timestamps and root cause classification; employee training completion rate for data handling.
data_signals: sensitive data inventory with classification tags and asset identifiers
DLP policy assignment and enforcement status per data store/bucket/database
DLP alert logs (timestamp, user ID, resource path, action, destination, policy triggered, block/allow outcome)
access logs/file activity logs with user role, source IP, timestamp and query/extract details
encryption status flags/metadata for storage (at-rest) and transport (in-transit) per asset
ML anomaly detection alerts with score, model version, user/entity, and validation status
network egress logs and data exfiltration indicators (large transfers, uncommon destinations)
incident/ticket records for data leakage with detection time, remediation time, and closure reason
training attendance/completion records for data handling and DLP awareness
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, automated DLP and access logs with full metadata (user, resource, action, timestamp) are strong evidence versus occasional manual reports.
A complete sensitive-data inventory mapped to DLP/encryption coverage (full coverage) is strong; partial or missing inventory is weak.
ML anomaly alerts that include score, validation/false-positive rate, and correlated block action are strong; unvalidated model outputs or standalone alerts without blocking/remediation are weak.
Incident records with end-to-end timestamps (detection -> containment -> remediation) and repeatable remediation actions are stronger than anecdotal or undocumented responses.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly labels this a 'preventive control' and prescribes DLP, encryption and access restrictions to prevent unauthorized data mining.

AC-23.differentiation_note: This control uniquely focuses on preventing data mining/exfiltration via DLP, data classification, encryption and ML-based anomaly detection, rather than general access review or incident response — measurements must emphasize coverage (%) and blocked vs. allowed exfiltration event counts.

AC-24.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of access control decisions: percentage of accounts and resources governed by IAM policies (RBAC/least-privilege), percentage of scheduled access reviews completed on time, count and rate of access violations, and mean time to revoke or adjust access after role changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive governance control where auditors need coverage and compliance ratios (percentage of users/resources under correct RBAC, percent of reviews completed). Counts (requests, violations) and trends over time supplement coverage metrics for operational visibility.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of active user accounts and resources with assigned RBAC roles and documented policy mappings; count of access requests and access violations with timestamps and outcomes; time interval between role change event and access revocation per user; percentage of scheduled access reviews completed within the review window.
data_signals: IAM user and group inventory with assigned role IDs and effective permissions
access request logs (request_id, user_id, resource_id, timestamp, decision_granted/denied)
access violation/denial events with reason codes and timestamps
access review completion records (review_id, scope, reviewer_id, completion_timestamp)
provisioning/deprovisioning events and timestamps (role_change_event_id, revoke_timestamp)
automated policy decision logs (PDP/PIP/PAP request and decision entries)
MFA enablement flag per account
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous automated IAM logs showing request→decision→enforcement events and provision/deprovision timestamps; machine-readable access review outputs tied to user IDs.
Weak: Periodic spreadsheets or one-off audit reports without timestamped enforcement events or lacking linkage between role changes and revoke events.

affinity_keywords: time taken to revoke access after role changes

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description emphasizes preventive mechanisms (RBAC, least privilege, IAM framework, and proactive access reviews) to ensure only authorized access.

AC-24.differentiation_note: This is the broad governance/control-level requirement focused on overall IAM coverage, periodic reviews, and revocation timelines — unlike its siblings which focus on transmission of authorization info or identity verification specifics.

AC-24(1).measurement_goal: Measure how access authorization information is evaluated, transmitted and logged: percent of access requests evaluated against predefined authorization policies before granting, percent of decisions logged for audit, count of automated vs manual decisions, and average processing time per request.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires verifying that authorization information is transmitted and used before access is granted, so coverage/compliance percentages (requests evaluated, decisions logged) are primary; counts of requests and processing rate/time provide operational context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percent of access requests that underwent policy-based authorization evaluation (policy_id recorded) prior to granting; count of decisions logged (request_id, decision, policy_version, timestamp); average request processing time (request_timestamp → decision_timestamp); percent of requests that were granted vs denied and percent processed automatically vs manually.
data_signals: authorization request and response logs (request_id, user_id, resource_id, timestamp, policy_version, decision)
policy repository records (policy_id, effective_date, criteria)
audit logs indicating decision transmission events and recipients
inventory of user roles and mapped authorization criteria
metrics of automated policy engine vs manual approvals (auto_decision_flag)
request processing time metrics (decision_timestamp - request_timestamp)
logged outcomes: granted_count, denied_count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Machine-generated authorization request/decision logs with policy/version identifiers and downstream enforcement confirmation; consistent logging for every request.
Weak: Manual approval emails or ad-hoc ticket notes without structured request/decision logs or lacking policy references.

affinity_keywords: percentage of requests granted or denied

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text labels this a 'preventive security control' ensuring access requests are evaluated against authorization criteria before granting access.

AC-24(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on transmission, evaluation and logging of authorization decisions (PDP/PAP/PIP interactions) and request processing metrics rather than overall IAM coverage or identity verification mechanics.

AC-24(2).measurement_goal: Demonstrate that no actions occur without a verified identity by measuring percentage of access attempts tied to authenticated identities (MFA enabled where required), count and rate of unauthenticated/anonymous attempts, privileged account usage audit coverage, and time to revoke access for orphaned or terminated accounts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Control forbids actions without verified identity (zero-tolerance semantic) so coverage % (accounts with verified identity/MFA) is primary; counts of unauthorized attempts and rates of revocation measure enforcement and corrective action.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of access events associated with authenticated identities (user_id or process_id) and percent of accounts with MFA/identity verification enabled; count of access attempts that lacked a verifiable identity or were blocked; mean time from account termination to access revocation; audit trail coverage for privileged (PAM) account actions.
data_signals: authentication logs with user_id/process_id, method (MFA flag), timestamp, success/failure
session creation records tied to a verified identity
blocked/unauthenticated attempt logs (anonymous_access_attempts)
privileged account (PAM) session logs and justification entries
account lifecycle events (provision_date, termination_date, revoke_timestamp)
access review and orphaned account reports
MFA enrollment and enforcement status per account
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous authentication logs showing request/session linked to a verified identity and enforced MFA; PAM session records with audit trail for privileged actions.
Weak: Periodic manual attestations of identity verification or spreadsheets listing verified accounts without timestamped authentication events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control explicitly requires prevention of actions by entities lacking a verified identity and recommends MFA, RBAC, and PAM—classic preventive authentication controls.

AC-24(2).differentiation_note: This control is narrowly focused on the requirement that every action be tied to a verified identity (authentication/MFA/PAM), unlike siblings that emphasize policy transmission or broad IAM governance.

AC-25.measurement_goal: Measure enforcement coverage and correctness of the Reference Monitor by reporting the percentage of access requests to sensitive resources that are evaluated by the Reference Monitor, the percentage denied vs allowed, MFA enforcement rate on evaluated requests, and the mean time to revoke access for terminated users against policy expectations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and policy adherence — auditors need percentages (coverage, deny-rate, MFA-enforcement %) to verify enforcement; counts and rates (requests, unauthorized attempts) and trends over time are useful secondary signals to identify gaps and changes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of access requests to classified/sensitive resources that have an associated Reference Monitor decision (allow/deny) and the decision outcome; percentage of those requests protected by MFA; time interval between employee termination event and access revocation. Include mappings from resource to sensitivity classification and the policy/rule IDs evaluated for each request so enforcement coverage and correctness can be validated.
data_signals: access request evaluation logs with timestamp, subject_id, resource_id, decision (allow/deny), and policy_rule_id
sensitive data / resource classification mapping (resource_id → sensitivity_tier)
MFA challenge/enforcement logs correlated to access request IDs
user role-to-permission assignments and RBAC mappings (role_id → permissions)
provisioning/deprovisioning events and timestamps (employee termination, access_revocation_timestamp)
counts of unauthorized access attempts and corresponding alert records
access control policy definitions and version history
audit log integrity checks, e.g., log hashes or tamper-evidence records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, machine-generated access decision logs that include policy_rule_id, resource_id, subject_id, and cryptographic integrity/hash of logs; automated correlation between termination events and access_revocation timestamps; consistent MFA challenge records tied to access grants.

Weak: ad-hoc screenshots or one-off manual attestations of policy existence, periodic manual reviews without request-level decision logs, or sampled logs that do not include policy_rule_id or resource mapping.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls it a 'preventive mechanism designed to enforce access control policies' and focuses on preventing unauthorized access and validating requests.

AC-25.differentiation_note: This control specifically measures runtime enforcement and decision coverage of access requests (request-level allow/deny, policy_rule_id, MFA enforcement and revoke timing), unlike sibling identity or provisioning controls that focus primarily on account lifecycle or role assignment records.

AT-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of the workforce who have acknowledged the current cybersecurity policies and completed required policy-related training on schedule, the count of active policy exceptions with approvals, and the number and rate of reported policy violations per reporting period to demonstrate policy adoption and oversight.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily about coverage and adherence (e.g., policy acknowledgment rates and training completion rates), which are best expressed as percentages; counts and trends are useful secondary measures for exceptions and violation volumes, and a score can summarize policy maturity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A dashboardable metric set: (1) percentage of employees (by org unit and role) with a recorded acknowledgment of the current policy version and timestamp; (2) percentage of employees who completed required policy training (course ID, completion timestamp, score) within the required timeframe; (3) count and status of active policy exception requests with approver, rationale, and expiration; (4) count and severity classification of documented policy violations/incidents per period; (5) policy document version history with review/update timestamps and reviewer identities.
data_signals: policy document version identifiers and publish timestamps
policy acknowledgment records (user id, policy version, acknowledgment timestamp)
training/LMS completion records (user id, course id, completion timestamp, score)
policy exception requests and approvals (request id, approver id, approval timestamp, expiration date, justification)
policy review logs or meeting minutes (review date, reviewer, summary of changes)
policy violation or incident reports tagged as 'policy violation' (report id, reporter id, date, outcome)
distribution/communication records (email send logs or intranet receipt confirmations tied to policy publications)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated LMS completion records and signed electronic policy acknowledgments tied to the current policy version and user identity, plus timestamped, versioned policy documents and an approvals log for exceptions.
Weak: ad-hoc manual attestations, undated policy documents, or one-off training sign-in sheets without linkage to policy versions or user identities.
Strong: recorded exception approvals with expiration and periodic re-approval; Weak: undocumented or informal exceptions without approver identity or expiration.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes policy, training, and governance to prevent noncompliant behavior and mitigate human error (phrases: 'establishes a framework', 'ensure that all employees... understand their roles').

AT-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on governance, documentation, and human adoption metrics (acknowledgment, training, exceptions) rather than technical controls or detection — measurements must tie to policy versions, acknowledgments, and exception approvals rather than system logs.

AT-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of employees who completed required onboarding and recurring security training, the average improvement in pre- and post-training assessment scores, and the trend in security incidents attributable to human error (e.g., phishing clicks, misconfiguration) to demonstrate training effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and completion (best evidenced by percentages), with supporting assessment score deltas and trends in incident counts to show effectiveness over time as required by the description (participation rates, pre- and post-training assessments, incident trends).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of active employees who have completed required onboarding and refresher training modules within the required interval; compute average pre-training and post-training assessment scores and their delta; count monthly security incidents attributed to human error and phishing clicks to correlate training completion with incident reduction.
data_signals: training completion records (employee_id, module_id, completion_timestamp)
training enrollment/assignment records (employee_id, required_by_role, due_date)
pre-training and post-training assessment scores (employee_id, module_id, score, timestamp)
simulated phishing campaign metrics (emails_sent, clicks, reports, timestamps)
incident reports tagged 'human error' or 'phishing' (incident_id, date, root_cause)
training material revision timestamps and version identifiers
employee role mapping / target population (employee_id -> role/department)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated LMS completion logs, timestamped assessment scores, and correlated incident records spanning multiple periods
Weak: annual attestations or self-reported completion without timestamps or per-module records
Strong: reduction in phishing-click rates and human-error incidents after training cycles; Weak: single snapshot completion report without assessment or incident correlation

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description repeatedly labels this as a "preventive control" focused on training to reduce human error and prevent incidents.

AT-2.differentiation_note: This entry covers the program-wide management and effectiveness metrics (coverage, assessment improvement, incident correlation) rather than specific simulation or threat-topic modules covered by child controls.

AT-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure participation and performance in hands-on exercises: percent of employees completing simulated phishing and incident response drills, click-through and report rates for simulations, and average detection/response times observed during drills.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Practical exercise effectiveness is primarily demonstrated by participation/coverage percentages; counts of simulation events and trending detection/response metrics provide operational evidence as described (simulated phishing, incident response drills, participation and response times).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of targeted employees who participated in each practical exercise (simulated phishing, response drills) within the reporting period; simulation outcomes (emails delivered, clicks, reports) and drill metrics (time-to-detect, time-to-respond, tasks completed); pre/post exercise assessment score changes.
data_signals: simulated phishing campaign logs (campaign_id, emails_sent, emails_delivered, clicks, reports, timestamps)
incident response drill logs (drill_id, participants, start_time, detection_time, containment_time, closure_time)
exercise attendance/completion records (employee_id, exercise_id, completion_timestamp)
pre/post drill assessment or quiz scores (employee_id, exercise_id, score_before, score_after)
participant feedback submissions (exercise_id, responses, timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated simulation telemetry and drill timing logs with participant identifiers and objective response metrics
Weak: manual attendance sheets or anecdotal drill summaries without timestamps or measurable detection/response times
Strong: observed improvement in drill performance and reduced click rates over successive campaigns; Weak: single exercise result without historical trend

control_type_classification.reasoning: Described as a "preventive control" using practical exercises (simulated phishing, drills) to reduce human error before incidents occur.

AT-2(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on measurable simulation and drill outcomes (click/report rates, drill response times) rather than overall training program completion or topic-specific training.

AT-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of employees who completed insider-threat-specific training, the number and timeliness of reported suspicious behaviors, and the change in insider-related incidents before and after training deployment.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive awareness for insider threats is validated by completion coverage (percent); counts of reports and trend analysis of insider incidents demonstrate whether training changed behavior as described (reported incidents, refresher frequency).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of employees assigned and completing the insider-threat training module within required intervals; monthly counts of insider threat reports and confirmed insider incidents; average time from report to investigation initiation to show reporting effectiveness.
data_signals: insider threat module completion records (employee_id, module_id, completion_timestamp)
records of reported suspicious behavior (report_id, reporter_id, date_reported, category)
investigation initiation timestamps (report_id, investigation_start_time)
confirmed insider incident logs (incident_id, date, root_cause)
refresher course schedule adherence (module_id, scheduled_date, actual_date)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: timestamped training completion for the insider threat module and a measurable increase in timely reports followed by investigative actions
Weak: training completion attested without linkage to report volumes or investigation timelines
Strong: demonstrable reduction in confirmed insider incidents post-training; Weak: unchanged incident rates without contextual investigation

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes proactive training to reduce insider risk and instructs employees on reporting—characteristics of preventive controls.

AT-2(2).differentiation_note: Targets measurement of reporting volumes and investigation timeliness specific to insider threats rather than generic phishing or APT simulations.

AT-2(3).measurement_goal: Measure participation in social engineering training, simulated social-engineering campaign outcomes (phishing click and report rates), and pre/post assessment score changes to show improved recognition and reporting of social engineering attempts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is demonstrated by coverage percentages (completion) and by simulation outcome rates (click/report) and assessment scores, which directly reflect recognition of social engineering tactics called out in the description.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assigned employees who completed social engineering modules; simulated social-engineering campaign click rates and report rates per campaign; average change in assessment quiz scores before and after training.
data_signals: social engineering / phishing simulation logs (campaign_id, delivered, clicks, reports, timestamps)
module completion records (employee_id, module_id, completion_timestamp)
assessment/quiz scores pre and post training (employee_id, score_before, score_after)
counts of reported social engineering attempts (report_id, date, reporter_id)
ad hoc training session attendance records (session_id, attendees, date)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: repeated automated simulation metrics showing declining click rates and increasing report rates aligned with training cycles
Weak: isolated anecdotal reports or single-scan results without historical context or assessment linkage
Strong: measurable improvement in assessment scores and lower incident rates after training; Weak: completion logs without performance or behavior indicators

control_type_classification.reasoning: Described as training to "equip employees" to recognize social engineering, which is inherently preventive.

AT-2(3).differentiation_note: Specifically measures social-engineering recognition and simulation outcomes (phishing click/report rates) rather than broader training coverage or insider-threat reporting.

AT-2(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of employees trained to identify suspicious communications and anomalous system behavior, the number and rate of employee-reported suspicious communications and anomalous events, and simulation performance (phishing results) to demonstrate detection/reporting capability.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage (percentage trained) is primary; the control text emphasizes reporting and recognition (best measured by rates/counts of reported suspicious communications, simulated phishing statistics, and anomalous activity reports).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of employees completing modules on suspicious communications and anomalous system behavior; monthly count and rate of suspicious communications reported by employees; simulated phishing click/report rates and number of anomalous activity reports escalated by employees to security teams.
data_signals: training completion records for suspicious communications module (employee_id, completion_timestamp)
reported suspicious communications log (report_id, reporter_id, message_metadata, date_reported)
simulated phishing campaign metrics (delivered, clicks, reports)
anomalous system behavior reports escalated by users (report_id, system_affected, timestamp)
timestamps of policies and reporting mechanism publication/updates
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated reporting logs and SIEM escalations triggered by employee reports correlated with training cycles
Weak: manual email reports collected ad-hoc without structured logging or timestamps
Strong: increasing report rates and decreasing successful phishing clicks after training; Weak: static completion rates without behavior change

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control trains employees to identify and report suspicious communications and anomalies — a preventive activity intended to reduce successful attacks.

AT-2(4).differentiation_note: Emphasizes the link between human reporting of suspicious communications/anomalies and detection pipelines (reports -> SIEM/ops) versus generic phishing or insider training metrics.

AT-2(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of employees who completed APT-focused training modules, average assessment scores on APT TTP recognition, and performance in simulated APT exercises (detection, containment, and response times).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage (percentage trained) is essential for awareness; TTP recognition is best evidenced by assessment scores and trends in simulated APT exercise performance as the control specifies TTP-focused practical exercises.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of relevant employees completing APT/TTP training within required intervals; average and distribution of assessment scores for TTP recognition; simulated APT exercise metrics including detection time, containment time, and number of missed indicators.
data_signals: APT module completion records (employee_id, module_id, completion_timestamp)
assessment results for TTP recognition (employee_id, question_id, score, timestamp)
simulated APT exercise logs (exercise_id, detection_time, containment_time, participant_ids, exercise_outcome)
feedback/survey responses on training relevance (survey_id, responses, timestamp)
incident records tagged with APT-like tactics (incident_id, tactics, date)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: timestamped completion and assessment data plus objective exercise telemetry showing reduced detection/containment times
Weak: narrative exercise summaries or training slides without participant performance data
Strong: measurable improvements in simulated APT response metrics across iterations; Weak: static completion counts without performance or simulation linkage

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is preventative training focused on APT recognition and response, intended to prevent successful sophisticated attacks by educating personnel.

AT-2(5).differentiation_note: Targets APT/TTP recognition and simulated APT exercise performance metrics, which are more specialized than generic phishing or insider-focused training measures.

AT-2(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of mandated employees completing threat-environment training, the recency/timeliness of training material updates against current threat intelligence, and trends in incident indicators (e.g., phishing success rates) to show training content currency and impact.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control evidence centers on coverage percent (completion), while trend metrics and counts show whether training content aligned to the evolving threat environment produces measurable behavior change and incident reduction.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of employees completing the 'cyber threat environment' training module in the required interval; timestamps of when training content was updated versus dates of relevant threat intelligence; trend in phishing simulation success and reported security concerns over time after updates.
data_signals: training completion records (employee_id, module_id, completion_timestamp)
training content update metadata (module_id, version, update_timestamp, change_summary)
threat intelligence integration records (threat_feed_id, ingestion_timestamp, mapping_to_training_changes)
phishing/simulation trend metrics (campaign_id, clicks, reports, date)
counts of reported security concerns or incidents (report_id, date, reporter_id)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: linked timestamps showing training updates shortly after new threat intelligence plus improved simulation/incident metrics thereafter
Weak: training materials with no update metadata or stale content even if completion rates are high
Strong: demonstrated downward trend in successful attacks or click rates after curriculum updates; Weak: static incident rates despite claimed training updates

control_type_classification.reasoning: Described as preventive training that must reflect current threats; the control emphasizes up-to-date materials and mandatory participation to prevent incidents.

AT-2(6).differentiation_note: Emphasizes measurement of curriculum currency (content update timestamps and linkage to threat intelligence) in addition to participation and incident trends, which is unique among sibling controls.

AT-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of personnel per role with current, documented role-specific training completed on schedule; average assessment scores by role; and the count and rate of security incidents attributable to human error per period to demonstrate training effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires proof of coverage and completion (best expressed as percentages of roles trained); assessment performance is a score; counts and rates capture incident frequency tied to human error, aligning with the control's emphasis on completion, assessments, and incident reduction.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each defined role, compute the percentage of incumbent personnel with a recorded completion timestamp for the required role-based training within the current policy period; compute average and distribution of assessment scores per role; count security incidents tagged as 'human error' and link to training status of involved personnel.
data_signals: LMS training completion records with user ID, role, course ID, and completion timestamp
training curriculum version or course revision timestamp
assessment/quiz scores by user and course
training schedule and required frequency per role (policy)
incident reports flagged as human error with implicated user ID and timestamp
training enrollment and attendance logs
training feedback survey responses and timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated LMS logs with user IDs, role mappings and timestamps (strong) vs. ad-hoc spreadsheets or scanned sign-in sheets (weak)
Correlation of incident reports to trained/untrained status (strong) vs. anecdotal or unlinked incident descriptions (weak)
Up-to-date curriculum versioning and timestamps showing periodic updates (strong) vs. undated or rarely updated materials (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control' focused on ensuring personnel receive tailored training to prevent human-error incidents.

AT-3.differentiation_note: General program-level control focused on agency-wide role coverage, completion rates, and assessment scoring rather than domain-specific content or exercise formats.

AT-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of OT personnel with completed, role-specific training on environmental/OT controls, the frequency of OT-focused practical exercises, and any change in OT incident/error rates after training.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control emphasizes coverage of OT-specific training (best shown as percentage coverage); exercise frequency and incident counts/rates show operational impact; assessment scores demonstrate competency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For all roles identified as OT-relevant, compute percent with up-to-date environmental controls training completed within the required cycle; count OT-focused exercises/drills executed per period; track OT incidents/errors by role and training status to observe pre/post training changes.
data_signals: LMS records tagged with 'OT' or 'operational technology' course IDs and completion timestamps
role-to-training mappings identifying OT-relevant roles
exercise/drill logs (incident response drills, environmental controls exercises) with participant lists and dates
incident reports for OT systems with root-cause tagging and implicated personnel
training curriculum notes referencing 'environmental controls' and version timestamps
assessment scores from OT-specific modules
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated tagging of OT courses and role mappings with completion timestamps (strong) vs. manual, untagged records (weak)
Documented drill logs with participant lists and outcomes (strong) vs. undocumented or informal exercises (weak)
Measured reduction in OT incidents linked to trained roles (strong) vs. no linkage between incidents and training status (weak)
