control_type_classification.reasoning: Control text frames this as a 'preventive security control' intended to equip personnel for OT environmental controls, indicating prevention via training.

AT-3(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on OT/environmental controls and measures OT-specific exercises and OT incident trends rather than general role training coverage.

AT-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of roles with assigned physical-security training completed, counts of physical security drills performed, and the number of physical-access incidents attributable to insufficient training.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires proof of role coverage and completion for physical security topics (expressed as percentages); counts and rates capture drills and incidents; scores measure competency from assessments.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each role identified as requiring physical security training (security staff, facility managers, IT), report the percent with recorded completion within policy period; count scheduled and completed physical security drills; count physical-security incidents and link to training completion status.
data_signals: LMS or training attendance logs for 'physical security' course IDs with user ID and completion timestamp
role inventory identifying security-related roles (security personnel, facility managers)
drill/exercise logs for access control or emergency response drills with attendees and outcomes
incident reports for unauthorized access, theft, or breaches with root-cause and implicated personnel
assessment or practical exercise outcomes for physical security modules
training material revision history referencing 'physical security protocols' and 'emergency response procedures'
evidence_strength_indicators: Signed drill logs and incident linkage to trained/untrained personnel (strong) vs. generic incident counts without training linkage (weak)
Automated LMS completion with course IDs and timestamps (strong) vs. manual attestations without timestamps (weak)
Consistent curriculum updates reflecting policy or technology changes (strong) vs. stale materials (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description describes this as a preventive control to ensure personnel understand physical security measures and responsibilities.

AT-3(2).differentiation_note: Targets physical asset protection and access-control procedures; measurement must include drill logs and physical-incident linkage rather than cyber-focused simulations.

AT-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of roles completing practical exercises (simulated phishing, incident response drills) within policy periods, frequency/count of simulations per role, and pre/post exercise assessment score improvements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control emphasizes completion and currency of practical exercises (percent coverage); counts of exercises and score deltas measure frequency and learning/retention; trends show improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each role, calculate the percent of personnel who completed required practical exercises within the required period, count the number of simulations/drills performed per role per period, and measure change in assessment scores before and after exercises.
data_signals: simulation/phishing campaign results with user IDs, click/interaction outcomes, and timestamps
incident response drill logs with participant lists and timestamps
pre- and post-exercise assessment scores by user and exercise ID
exercise schedule and required frequency per role
training completion records for practical exercise modules
materials or certificates showing completion of hands-on exercises
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated simulation logs with user-level outcomes and timestamps (strong) vs. self-reported exercise completion (weak)
Documented pre/post assessments showing score improvement (strong) vs. one-off exercise without assessment (weak)
Regularly scheduled exercises with participant rosters (strong) vs. ad-hoc, undocumented drills (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control specifies practical exercises and simulations as preventive measures to build competency and reduce human-error risk.

AT-3(3).differentiation_note: Emphasizes practical simulation metrics (phishing campaign outcomes, pre/post assessment deltas) and exercise frequency, unlike sibling controls that focus on curriculum or domain-specific content.

AT-3(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of employees in targeted roles who completed training on recognizing suspicious communications and anomalous system behavior, the phishing simulation click rate and report rate, and the change in reported suspicious events versus successful incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage (percentage trained) is primary; detection-related outcomes (phishing click rates, report rates) are best expressed as rates; counts capture number of reported anomalies and incidents.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track percent of targeted roles with completed suspicious-communications/anomaly detection training; measure phishing simulation click-through rate and user report-to-phish ratio; count reported suspicious communications and subsequent confirmed incidents to evaluate training impact.
data_signals: LMS course completions for 'suspicious communications' or 'anomalous system behavior' modules with user IDs and timestamps
phishing simulation campaign results: click-through counts, report/submit counts, user IDs, timestamps
security operations center (SOC) or helpdesk reported suspicious communication tickets with timestamps and resolution status
confirmed incident counts where suspicious communications led to compromise
post-training assessment scores for relevant modules
feedback or survey responses on training relevance
evidence_strength_indicators: User-level simulation outcomes tied to training completion dates showing reduced click rates post-training (strong) vs. organization-level only stats without linkage to training (weak)
High report-to-phish ratio and decreased confirmed incidents after training (strong) vs. unchanged or untracked reporting behavior (weak)
Automated ticketing records for reported suspicious communications with SOC confirmation (strong) vs. ad-hoc email reports (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is described as preventive training to equip employees to identify and respond to suspicious communications and anomalies, preventing incidents.

AT-3(4).differentiation_note: Specifically targets detection-oriented human behaviors (phishing click/report rates, SOC ticketing) and requires measurement of simulation outcomes and report rates rather than general completion alone.

AT-3(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of personnel handling PII who have completed PII-specific training, average assessment/compliance quiz scores for PII handling, and the count/rate of PII mishandling incidents linked to trained vs. untrained staff.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control requiring proof of role-specific training for PII handlers (coverage percentage); assessment scores measure comprehension; counts/rates capture PII incidents and can be tied to training status.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For all roles that interact with PII, calculate percent with completed PII handling training within the required cycle; report average assessment scores for PII modules; count PII mishandling incidents and map to the training status of involved employees.
data_signals: LMS completion records for 'PII' or 'data handling' courses with user ID, role, completion timestamp
assessment/quiz results for PII modules by user and timestamp
role inventory identifying employees with PII handling responsibilities
incident logs for data breaches or PII mishandling with root cause and implicated personnel
training curriculum referencing GDPR/HIPAA and version timestamps
audit logs showing access to PII systems correlated to training status
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated linkage between PII access logs, role inventory, and LMS completion showing trained personnel access patterns (strong) vs. separate, unlinked datasets (weak)
Documented assessments with passing thresholds and timestamps (strong) vs. completion without assessment evidence (weak)
Reduction in PII incidents post-training with direct mappings to trained vs. untrained staff (strong) vs. unclassified incident cause (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly targets prevention by ensuring employees handling PII receive role-specific training to mitigate data breach risk.

AT-3(5).differentiation_note: Focused on PII/data-protection requirements and regulatory alignment (GDPR/HIPAA); requires linking training records to PII access and incident logs, unlike more general training controls.

AT-4.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of personnel (by role) who complete required onboarding and ongoing cybersecurity training on schedule, the distribution of assessment/simulation scores, and the count and age of overdue or missing training records to demonstrate program coverage and effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and completion: auditors want completion rates (percentage) by role and policy; supporting metrics include counts of overdue/missing records, assessment score distributions, and trends over time to show improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute completion rate per required training module and per role (count completed ÷ count required), distribution of assessment/simulation scores (mean, median, failures), number and age (days overdue) of outstanding training assignments, and timestamps of last material update to show currency. Include mappings from personnel to required role-specific trainings and remediation assignment records for failed assessments.
data_signals: LMS enrollment and completion logs with timestamps
participant attendance records and sign-in timestamps
assessment and simulation (e.g., phishing) scores and pass/fail outcomes
role-to-training requirement mapping (role assignments)
training module version and last updated timestamp
remediation assignment records and completion timestamps for failed assessments
overdue training count and days-overdue per user
exportable training report artifacts used in audits (PDF/CSV) with record hashes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated LMS logs showing enrollment, timestamped completions, assessment scores, and role mappings with automated reports; continuous exports or dashboards.
Moderate: periodic LMS reports or CSV exports that include timestamps and user IDs but require manual correlation.
Weak: ad-hoc paper sign-in sheets or single point-in-time attestations without timestamps, assessment details, or role mapping.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control' to ensure personnel receive required cybersecurity training and prevent human-error incidents.

AT-4.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on training record completeness, timeliness, and effectiveness (completion rates and assessment scores) rather than technical controls like patching or access management; measurement must tie individuals and roles to required modules and show coverage over time.

AT-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of employees completing required security engagement activities (training, acknowledgements) and the percent of departments with an assigned security liaison; track count and timeliness of reported security concerns recorded in the centralized communications repository and audit adherence to established communication protocols.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control focuses on coverage and compliance (training completion, liaison assignment) which are best expressed as percentages; counts capture volume of reports and repository entries and a score can capture audit/maturity assessments.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percentage of employees who completed mandated security training within the required period, percentage of organizational units with a recorded security liaison, weekly/monthly count of security concerns reported to the centralized repository, and audit compliance rate for adherence to the documented communication protocols. Include timestamps and ownership for each report and repository entry so timeliness and follow-up can be measured.
data_signals: training completion records with employee ID and completion timestamp
training schedule frequency and curriculum version records
HR directory entries indicating assigned security liaisons by department
centralized communications repository records (entry timestamp, reporter ID, summary, disposition/status)
ticketing/incident records labeled 'security concern' with acknowledgement and escalation timestamps
audit reports showing adherence to established communication protocols and findings with remediation status
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated training completion logs with timestamps and unique employee identifiers are strong evidence; ad-hoc self-reported completion or undocumented verbal confirmations are weak.
Centralized repository entries and ticketing records with acknowledgement/escalation timestamps are strong; isolated email threads or undocumented reports are weak.
Documented HR records of designated security liaisons and periodic verification/audit entries are strong; temporary or informal liaison assignments without HR record are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly lists prevention activities (training, establishing liaisons, protocols, centralized reporting) which are preventive controls to reduce insider risk.

AT-5.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about human-centric communication, liaison assignments, and documented reporting channels (people/process coverage), distinguishing it from technical controls that measure system configuration or vulnerability remediation.

AT-6.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and responsiveness of the feedback process (survey response rate), the distribution and average of satisfaction/confidence scores (pre/post when available), and the percentage of training items updated as a direct result of participant feedback within defined review cycles.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control emphasizes response rates and percentages (e.g., 'feedback response rates', 'percentage of participants reporting increased confidence') and also expects aggregated satisfaction/confidence scores and trends over time, so percentage is primary with score and rate useful for detail and trending.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) survey response rate = number of participants who submitted feedback / number of training attendees per session; (2) average satisfaction score and average self-reported confidence gain (pre/post) per course; (3) percent of training modules updated with timestamped change records linked to feedback items; (4) mean time from feedback submission to incorporated change or documented decision.
data_signals: training roster/attendance records (participant IDs, session IDs, timestamps)
post-training survey responses with timestamps and question-level scores (relevance, clarity, applicability)
pre-training and post-training confidence score records (when used)
catalog of feedback items (free-text responses) with categorization tags and timestamps
training content change log / version history with change timestamps and references to feedback items/action IDs
action item / remediation tracker entries created from feedback with status and closure timestamps
survey delivery and reminder events (to compute response rate and delivery lag)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated post-training surveys tied to attendance records and tracked content-change tickets showing feedback-linked updates; pre/post confidence scores enabling measured learning gain.
Moderate: regular aggregated satisfaction scores and response rates without explicit linkage to content changes.
Weak: occasional manual focus-group notes or annual retrospective summaries with no timestamps or participant coverage data.

affinity_keywords: percentage of participants reporting increased confidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires integrating feedback to continuously improve training and reduce human-error risk ('ensure training programs are effective and continuously improved'), which is preventive in nature.

AT-6.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about measuring feedback effectiveness and closing the loop (linking feedback to content updates), unlike sibling controls that might measure training completion or access; it requires tracking both participant responses and documented content changes tied to that feedback.

AU-1.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, currency, and organizational adoption of documented cybersecurity policies and procedures: percent of required policies with current review dates and approvals, percent of employees who completed and attested to required policy/training, and counts of policy-related audit findings and incidents attributable to policy non-compliance.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control that verifies coverage and adherence (coverage expressed as percentages). Counts (e.g., number of updates, findings) and trends (review cadence and training completion over time) provide complementary evidence of ongoing maintenance and adoption.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Maintain a policy inventory table (policy id, topic, owner, version, last_review_date, approval_record) and link to employee training/attestation records (employee id, policy ids attested, training completion timestamp). Measure: percent of policies reviewed within policy-defined cadence, percent of active employees with recent attestation/training, count of audit findings and incidents citing policy non-compliance and time-to-remediate those findings.
data_signals: policy document inventory with version, owner, approval record, and last_review_date
policy review schedule / governance meeting minutes recording reviews and approvals
employee training completion records tied to specific policies (employee_id, completion_timestamp, course_id)
signed policy attestation records (user, policy_id, attestation_timestamp)
internal audit reports listing policy adherence findings (finding_id, policy_id, severity, status, remediation_timestamp)
incident reports or tickets tagged with 'policy violation' or citing a specific policy
count of policy updates per year with change rationale
open vs closed corrective actions from policy non-compliance
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized policy inventory with machine-readable last_review_date and approval records, automated linkage to LMS/training and attestation logs, and time-stamped audit findings and remediation timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc Word/PDF files without metadata, one-off presentation-based trainings, or annual manual attestations without timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing and enforcing policies, governance structure, and training to prevent human-error and non-compliance (phrases: 'establishes a comprehensive framework', 'ensure that all employees understand their roles').

AU-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on governance, documentation, review cadence, and workforce adoption (policy inventory, training/attestation and audit findings) rather than technical detection or remediation metrics; measurement must emphasize coverage and evidence of organizational adoption rather than system logs.

AU-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical systems, applications, and network devices with logging configured to policy, plus the percentage of critical event types captured and retained according to defined retention periods and protected against tampering.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive and focuses on coverage and adherence to policy (e.g., retention periods, what to log); percentage metrics best express coverage/compliance while counts and trends support volume and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets (by criticality tier) with logging enabled and configured per the organization's logging policy; percentage of required event types (critical events) actually generated and retained for the policy-defined retention period; presence of access controls and integrity protections for stored logs.
data_signals: logging policy document (defined event types and retention periods)
asset inventory with logging-enabled flag and asset criticality
log generation volume and timestamps per asset
configured retention settings per log store
log access control / ACL entries and audit of log access
log integrity checksums or WORM/immutable store evidence
ingestion success/failure counts from centralized log collector
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated collection into a centralized log store with ingestion timestamps and integrity checks is strong evidence.
Point-in-time screenshots of settings or ad-hoc manual exports are weak evidence.
Complete asset-to-log-source mapping and daily ingestion success metrics strengthen confidence vs intermittent scan reports.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls AU-2 a "critical preventive control" that defines what to log and retention, indicating prevention via coverage and policy.

AU-2.differentiation_note: AU-2 is the overarching policy-and-coverage control (what to log, retention, protection) while its enhancements focus on aggregation, selection, reviews, or privileged logging specifics.

AU-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of log sources successfully aggregated into the centralized log store, the number of incidents detected via aggregated logs, and ingestion latency and parsing error counts to prove comprehensive compilation and usable analysis.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control focused on aggregation and detection, raw counts of sources/alerts and rates of ingestion/parsing errors directly show whether logs are compiled and producing detections; trends are useful for operational health.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of distinct log sources (systems, applications, network devices) forwarding to the centralized collector and the count of incidents/alerts generated from compiled records; ingestion success rate and parser/error counts per source, plus time-to-ingest latency.
data_signals: list of log sources registered with the centralized collector and timestamps of last successful ingestion
ingestion success/failure counts per source
parsing/error counts and failure reasons from log pipeline
alerts/incidents generated from centralized log analysis correlated to sources
log aggregation configuration records and retention settings
frequency of log reviews triggered by aggregation (review timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: High-confidence evidence: continuous ingestion metrics with per-source success rates, low parsing error counts, and alerts generated from correlation rules.
Weak evidence: occasional manual log copies or one-off exports without sustained ingestion or parser telemetry.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is described as a "critical detective control designed to aggregate and analyze audit logs," explicitly indicating detective classification.

AU-2(1).differentiation_note: AU-2(1) specifically targets aggregation/centralization and the health of ingestion/pipeline (sources, parser errors, ingestion latency), unlike AU-2 which focuses on policy and coverage.

AU-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of components (by type/criticality) configured to generate required event types (e.g., logins, sensitive-data access, configuration changes) as defined in policy, and count missing or misconfigured event types per component.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This detective enhancement verifies configuration coverage per component; percentage coverage (components compliant with required event selection) is the most direct signal, with counts and trends showing noncompliance and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of components (servers, applications, network devices) that have the policy-required event types enabled; list/count of components missing each required event type; mapping of event-type requirements to component configuration snapshots.
data_signals: component inventory with expected required event-type checklist
configuration snapshots or exported logging settings per component
enabled event-type lists per component (e.g., login events, data access, config change)
counts of components missing required event types
timestamps of configuration changes for auditability
log records showing required event fields (timestamp, user, event type) present
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong evidence: automated configuration snapshots and per-component enabled-event-type lists demonstrating required events are enabled across components.
Weak evidence: manual attestations or occasional sample checks without full component configuration exports.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on ensuring components 'identify and log significant events' and verifying logging configuration, which is a detective/configuration-check control.

AU-2(2).differentiation_note: AU-2(2) is uniquely about per-component event-type selection and configuration compliance (which events are enabled) rather than aggregation, review cadence, or privileged-specific logging.

AU-2(3).measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and timeliness of scheduled log reviews (e.g., daily/weekly), the percentage of required reviews completed on schedule, and the count of logging configuration updates resulting from reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This detective control emphasizes review cadence and update activity; rates (reviews per period, review completion rates) show operational execution, while percentages and trends show adherence and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate of scheduled log reviews completed (per schedule), percent of log sources reviewed within required timeframe, average time to complete a review, and number of logging configuration updates made as a result of reviews.
data_signals: log-review schedule and completion timestamps (daily/weekly review logs)
tickets or change records created from review findings
counts of reviews completed vs. scheduled per period
number of anomalies identified during reviews
timestamps of subsequent logging-configuration updates
percentage of logs retained in accordance with policy after reviews
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong evidence: automated review workflows with timestamps, documented findings, and linked change tickets showing updates; consistent completion rates over time.
Weak evidence: informal or ad-hoc review notes, infrequent/manual reviews without traceable outcomes.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires scheduled reviews and updates to logging configurations to detect anomalies, which is intrinsically detective in nature.

AU-2(3).differentiation_note: AU-2(3) focuses on the operational cadence and evidence trail of log reviews and resulting configuration updates, distinguishing it from configuration selection (AU-2(2)) and aggregation (AU-2(1)).

AU-2(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of privileged actions and privileged accounts whose actions are fully recorded with required fields (user, timestamp, action, target), the count of anomalous privileged events, and the percentage of privileged logs reviewed within the defined timeframe.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control mandates that all actions by privileged accounts be recorded; percentage coverage of privileged action logging is the clearest primary metric, with counts of events and rates of review as secondary signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of privileged accounts with complete audit logging enabled and percentage of privileged actions recorded with required attributes (user, action, timestamp, target); counts of privileged-access alerts and time-to-detection for privileged anomalies.
data_signals: privileged account inventory (list of accounts with privileged role)
privileged function audit logs (records showing admin actions, timestamps, affected systems)
counts of privileged access events logged
alerts for anomalous privileged activity
SIEM correlation rules triggered by privileged events
percentage of privileged logs reviewed within policy timeframe
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong evidence: continuous SIEM ingestion of privileged-event logs with per-account coverage metrics, correlation alerts, and immutable storage.
Weak evidence: occasional manual reviews or reliance on administrative recollection without persistent logged records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires systematic recording of privileged-account actions and detection of misuse, which is described as a detective logging requirement.

AU-2(4).differentiation_note: AU-2(4) specifically requires logging and monitoring of privileged functions and accounts (complete coverage of admin actions), unlike other AU-2 enhancements which address aggregation, event selection, or review cadence.

AU-3.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and integrity of audit records by counting generated audit events against the policy-defined expected events, measuring retention compliance, and tracking investigation timeliness (e.g., average time to investigate incidents discovered via logs).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily detective (logs are used to detect events), so event counts (and rates) show observable activity; percentage and trend metrics are useful secondarily to express coverage and improvement over time (e.g., % of critical events logged).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of audit events generated per log source and per event type compared to the set of events required by the logging policy; retention metadata showing retention/archival timestamps; integrity/tamper-evidence records for log stores; and investigation timestamps linking logged events to incident investigations (to compute investigation latency).
data_signals: audit event records with timestamp, event ID, user identity, action performed, and resource affected
log source inventory and configuration (which systems send logs: syslog, application logs, network devices)
central ingestion receipts/ingest timestamps per source (to show events were received)
log retention and archival metadata (retention period, deletion/archival timestamps)
log storage integrity checksums or tamper-evidence markers and change history
access control logs for log repositories showing who accessed/modified logs
counts of critical events logged vs. expected critical event types
time-to-investigation timestamps linking a logged event to an investigation/incident record
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated ingestion into a centralized, immutable store with integrity hashes and documented retention settings; automated schema validation and alerts for missing event types.
Weak: periodic manual collection of logs, ad-hoc screenshots or single exports, or missing integrity/tamper-evidence for stored logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes recording and retaining security events to support monitoring and investigation ('logged and retained...supports effective monitoring, investigation'), which is detective in nature.

AU-3.differentiation_note: This control defines the overall expectation for audit record content, integrity, and retention; sibling controls focus more narrowly on specific fields, centralization, or PII minimization.

AU-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of log-generating systems and log entries that include the required audit fields (e.g., timestamp, user identity, action, resource) and track the time and count of log reviews that verify schema completeness and accuracy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires verifying that logs include specific fields and sufficient context; percentage metrics (schema compliance %, % of systems meeting schema) directly express coverage while counts and trends show volume and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of log entries (by source and by critical system) that include required fields (timestamp, user identity, action, resource affected); count of systems emitting logs in the standardized format; time between log generation and availability to reviewers; and number of incidents detected where required fields enabled forensic analysis.
data_signals: log schema compliance reports (field presence per log entry: timestamp, user identity, action, resource affected)
list of critical systems and 'percentage generating required logs' per system
sampled log entries showing the 'standardized format' and required elements
log review records and review timestamps (who reviewed, when, and findings)
counts of incidents where audit trail contained sufficient context for forensics
PII redaction flags for fields excluded/removed from logs
references to NIST SP 800-92 or internal logging standard mapped to observed fields
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated schema validation that flags and blocks non‑conforming log entries and produces compliance reports; continuous monitoring showing high % of entries with required fields.
Weak: manual spot-checks of a few logs or one-time mapping exercises without automated enforcement or regular validation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement to ensure logs include specific fields and standardized format is aimed at preventing inadequate records (schema omissions) and thus is primarily preventive though it supports detection.

AU-3(1).differentiation_note: Unlike AU-3 which covers broad content/integrity, AU-3(1) specifically requires and measures presence of particular audit fields and schema compliance across systems.

AU-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure centralized collection coverage and ingestion health by counting the number of planned log sources and event types that successfully forward expected audit records to the centralized store, tracking ingestion latency and ingestion error rates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This detective control depends on observable receipts of events in a centralized system; counts (number of sources/events ingested) and ingestion rates make it possible to detect missing sources or missing event types quickly, with percentage providing coverage context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of planned log sources (servers, applications, network devices) vs. number actually sending logs to the centralized collector; per-source ingestion timestamps and latency; counts of ingestion failures/errors; and mapping of planned event types to received event types to show completeness.
data_signals: centralized ingestion receipts per source with timestamp and event type
log source inventory of planned sources (servers, applications, network devices) and 'last seen' timestamps
ingestion failure/error counts and error messages from collectors
time-to-ingest (latency) distribution per source
mapping/table of planned event types vs. received event types showing gaps
configuration review records for logging settings and periodic review frequency
evidence of tamper-protection for centralized store (access logs, immutability settings)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized SIEM or log lake showing continuous receipts for all planned sources, automated alerts for missing sources or event types, and documented tamper-protection and retention.
Weak: manual aggregation of logs, inconsistent source mappings, or intermittent exports that do not show continuous ingestion and mapping by planned event type.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires centralized capture and management of audit records to enable detection and investigation ('captures and manages audit records from various sources'), indicating a detective control.

AU-3(2).differentiation_note: This control is specifically about centralized capture and ingestion health (coverage, latency, errors) rather than field-level schema or PII concerns addressed by other siblings.

AU-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure the degree to which PII is excluded or sanitized from audit records by tracking the percentage of log entries containing PII (by PII type), count of redaction/anon rules applied, and number of incidents or findings where PII was exposed in logs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive (minimizing PII in logs); percentage metrics (e.g., % of entries containing PII) directly express compliance with a 'minimum necessary' policy, while counts and trends show volume and improvement or regression.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of audit records that contain PII tokens (by type: SSN, email, phone, etc.) and counts of redaction/anonymization actions applied at ingestion; number and severity of incidents involving PII exposure via logs and audit results from log content reviews.
data_signals: log entries flagged by PII detection (regex/token match) with PII type label
PII inventory mapping (which PII types should never appear in logs vs. which are permitted)
counts of redaction/filtering rules and number of log entries processed by those rules
audit results showing volume of audit records containing PII and examples
incident/ticket records for any PII exposures in logs
logging system configuration showing automatic filtering/anonymization at ingest
records of policy defining 'minimum necessary' logging fields and related training records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, pre-ingest PII detection and redaction in the pipeline with measurement dashboards showing very low % of entries containing PII and configuration proof of rule application.
Weak: manual redaction after the fact, sample-based audits only, or lack of a mapped PII inventory and no automated detection.

affinity_keywords: inventory of types of data collected
volume of audit records containing PII

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control directs limiting or excluding PII from logs ('establish a policy that dictates the minimum necessary information...anonymizing or redacting any PII'), which is preventive in nature.

AU-3(3).differentiation_note: AU-3(3) uniquely targets privacy and PII minimization in audit records (measurement of PII presence and redaction), rather than general completeness, schema, or centralization concerns of the other AU-3 controls.

AU-4.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of log stores that have sufficient provisioned storage and retention mapping against policy, the total and per-source log volume growth rate, and the count of storage capacity incidents (e.g., full stores or dropped/failed writes) over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires verifying coverage and compliance (e.g., percent of log stores meeting retention and provisioning). Secondary metrics (counts of capacity incidents, trends in log volume) show operational behavior and growth that inform capacity planning.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track per-log-source daily/weekly log volume (MB/GB), storage provisioned vs. used (GB and % utilized) per log store, retention policy assigned per log type, and the number and timestamps of capacity-related events (failed writes, dropped logs, full volumes). Also capture log rotation and archival event records to prove older logs are moved according to policy.
data_signals: per-log-source daily log volume (MB/GB) with timestamp
storage provisioned size and current storage utilization percentage per log store
retention policy mapping by log type (system, application, security) and required retention duration
log rotation and archival event timestamps and target archive identifiers
count and timestamps of failed log writes or dropped events due to capacity
inventory of log management systems and cloud storage buckets with region and encryption status
log access and retrieval request audit records (who/when/which logs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous storage utilization metrics, automated ingestion success/failure logs, and archival job logs with byte counts and checksums; documented mapping of log types to retention policies.
Weak: occasional manual screenshots of storage dashboards or one-off reports without historical utilization, or annual statements without automated ingest/rotation logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description frames the control as preventing loss of logs by ensuring storage capacity and retention mapping (e.g., 'preventive control', 'maintains sufficient storage').

AU-4.differentiation_note: This control focuses on provisioning and retention coverage across log sources (capacity and mapping) rather than the mechanics of moving logs offsite or to archives, which is handled by AU-4(1).

AU-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of transfer jobs that successfully move logs from primary to alternate storage within defined thresholds, the frequency and volume of transfers by log source, and the average time-to-transfer when primary storage thresholds are reached.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive/operational control needs coverage and SLA compliance (e.g., percent of transfers completed within target time). Counts and rates of transfer jobs and trends in transfer volume/time provide operational visibility into the archival process.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Record each archival/transfer job with start/end timestamps, byte counts transferred, source and alternate storage identifiers, success/failure status, and elapsed time. Measure the fraction of transfers completed within the defined threshold and maintain logs of configuration (thresholds), encryption and access-control settings for the alternate storage, plus documentation of the transfer process.
data_signals: transfer job logs with start/end timestamps and bytes transferred
primary storage utilization snapshots and configured primary storage threshold values
alternate storage location identifiers, encryption at-rest status, and access control lists
transfer job success/failure status and error codes
frequency of transfers per log source (jobs/day or jobs/week)
time-to-transfer (elapsed seconds/minutes) per job
documentation of configured thresholds and transfer schedules
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated transfer job logs with start/end timestamps, byte counts, job success status, and stored checksums; documented thresholds and periodic audits confirming transfer integrity and access controls.
Weak: manual ad-hoc file copies, unlogged transfers, or only occasional attestations without machine-generated transfer logs or integrity verification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is described as ensuring preservation of logs by transferring when primary storage threshold is reached (preventing data loss), i.e., preventive action.

AU-4(1).differentiation_note: Unlike AU-4 which measures provisioned capacity and retention coverage, AU-4(1) specifically measures the operational transfer/archival process — success rates, transfer latency, and integrity of moving logs to alternate storage.

AU-5.measurement_goal: Measure the frequency of audit logging process failures, the mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to respond/recover (MTTR) to those failures, and the percentage of expected logs successfully captured and stored within defined retention periods.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires measuring response effectiveness (time-based remediation metrics), so rate (e.g., MTTD/MTTR rates) is primary; counts of failures and percentage of logs captured are useful secondary metrics to show coverage and volume trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of audit logging failures (with timestamps and affected system identifiers), time-to-detect and time-to-resolve per failure, and ratio of events generated by systems vs. events successfully persisted to logging storage within the configured retention window (grouped by system and storage target).
data_signals: audit logging failure events with timestamps and system IDs
alerting/notification logs for logging-process failures
detection timestamp and resolution/close timestamp for each failure incident
log ingestion/success metrics (events generated vs. events stored) by source system
storage health metrics (capacity used, read/write errors) for logging storage
inventory of systems generating audit logs and their assigned storage targets
test results from regular logging infrastructure resilience tests
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous automated logging of failures, alert histories, and incident records with timestamps showing MTTD/MTTR and persisted log counts across retention windows.
Weak: One-off manual summaries, occasional test reports, or screenshots without event-level timestamps and persistent storage verification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description centers on detecting, responding to, and recovering from failures ('detect, respond to, and recover from failures'), indicating corrective/response activities.

AU-5.differentiation_note: This parent control is broad — it mandates end-to-end detection and recovery metrics (MTTD/MTTR and capture percentage) across the logging estate, whereas the enhancements focus on specific mechanisms (storage thresholds, real-time alerts, thresholds for traffic, shutdowns, and alternate logging).

AU-5(1).measurement_goal: Measure storage utilization for audit log targets (percentage used), the frequency of storage capacity threshold alerts (by threshold tier), and the response times and actions taken after capacity warnings to prevent overwriting or data loss.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control centers on storage utilization thresholds and prevention of overwrite; percentage used (capacity %) is the most direct measure, with counts of alerts and trending capacity growth as useful supplements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-log-target storage capacity used as a percentage (e.g., current used/allocated), timestamps of threshold-crossing events (75%, 90%, 100%), alert notification records for each threshold, and documented actions taken (archival, increased allocation, retention policy enforcement) with timestamps.
data_signals: storage used percentage per log storage target (real-time and historical)
configured capacity threshold values (75%, 90%, 100%) per target
threshold-crossing events with timestamps
alert/notification records showing recipients and escalation steps
records of retention/archival actions taken (job run logs)
overwriting incidents or dropped-log counts after capacity events
storage allocation change events (provisioning/expansion)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated capacity metrics and threshold alerts with documented, timestamped remediation actions and no post-event log loss.
Weak: Manual capacity checks or occasional reports without timestamped alerts or action logs; absence of configured thresholds.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control proactively prevents log loss by alerting on storage capacity thresholds and defining escalation and archival actions, aligning with preventive controls.

AU-5(1).differentiation_note: AU-5(1) is narrowly focused on storage-capacity thresholds and associated alert/escalation actions, unlike the parent AU-5 which covers detection and recovery across all failure modes.

AU-5(2).measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of real-time audit logging process failure alerts, the MTTD and MTTR for those alerts, and the health/redundancy status of logging storage systems tied to those alerts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Real-time alerts and failure events are event-oriented; counts of alerts and rates (alerts per hour/day) are most useful to show detection activity and volume, with rate/trend for responsiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Event-level real-time alert logs for logging-process failures (alert type, source, timestamp, severity), detection and response timestamps to compute MTTD/MTTR, mapping of alerts into incident response cases, and redundancy/health status indicators for logging storage during incidents.
data_signals: real-time alert events for logging-process failures with timestamps
alert severity/type labels (e.g., ingestion failure, write error)
MTTD and MTTR calculations per alert (detection and resolution timestamps)
incident response ticket IDs and escalation logs linked to alerts
redundancy/replication health metrics for logging storage
results of regular tests of alert mechanisms and alert delivery success rates
records of designated personnel notified and acknowledgement timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Real-time alert streams stored with event timestamps, linked incident tickets showing response timelines, and test logs proving alert delivery to designated personnel.
Weak: Aggregated daily summaries or ad-hoc email threads without event-level timestamps, or tests that are not frequent enough to validate real-time behavior.

control_type_classification.reasoning: This enhancement emphasizes prompt identification via real-time monitoring and alerts ('triggers alerts when audit logging processes fail'), which is characteristic of detective controls.

AU-5(2).differentiation_note: AU-5(2) specifically requires real-time detection and integration with incident response (MTTD/MTTR tracking), unlike AU-5(1) which focuses on storage capacity thresholds and AU-5 which is broader.

AU-5(3).measurement_goal: Measure baseline logging traffic volumes, count and rate of threshold breaches for configured traffic-volume thresholds, the response actions triggered (throttling/rejection), and the false positive rate of threshold-triggered actions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about detecting threshold breaches and actions taken; counts of breaches and actions are primary, with rates and percentages (false positive %) to assess calibration and effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Historical baseline traffic volume per log source, configured threshold values, per-threshold breach events (timestamp, source, volume), automated response actions executed (throttle/reject) with timestamps, and classification of breaches as true incidents vs false positives to compute false positive rate.
data_signals: historical log traffic volume by source (events/sec or MB/sec) and baseline windows
configured threshold values and effective dates
threshold breach events with timestamps and measured volume
automated response action logs (throttle, reject) with duration and affected sources
false positive classification records (post-incident analysis)
frequency of threshold breaches over time
reviews and updates to threshold configuration (who changed, when, why)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated event-level records showing traffic volumes, exact threshold crossing metrics, executed automated responses, and post-event analysis labeling true vs false positives.
Weak: Manual notes of threshold breaches, or action summaries without raw traffic metrics and without false-positive analysis.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on detecting anomalies when traffic volumes exceed thresholds and triggering alerts/actions ('trigger alerts or initiate predefined responses'), which is detective in nature with automated responses.

AU-5(3).differentiation_note: AU-5(3) is uniquely focused on traffic-volume baselining, threshold configuration, and managing false positives for logging traffic — a distinct telemetry/volume calibration need compared with other enhancements.

AU-5(4).measurement_goal: Measure occurrences where the shutdown-on-failure mechanism was triggered, the success rate of safe shutdowns (and pre-shutdown preservation actions), and the MTTD/MTTR for restoring logging services and ensuring logs were preserved to backups before shutdown.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A preventive action (shutdown) is best measured by success/compliance percentage (successful safe shutdowns and preserved logs) with rates/counts of shutdown events and recoveries as secondary indicators.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of shutdown-on-failure triggers with timestamps and affected systems, percentage of shutdowns that executed pre-shutdown backup/preservation actions, and time-to-restore logging services (MTTR) after shutdown events.
data_signals: shutdown-on-failure trigger events with timestamps and reasons
pre-shutdown backup/preservation job logs and completion status
shutdown execution logs and outcome (successful/failed)
post-shutdown restoration events with timestamps (service restore MTTR)
hardware/software health metrics leading to shutdown (e.g., write errors, capacity thresholds)
records of procedures followed for shutdown (notifications, approvals)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: System-generated shutdown and backup logs showing backups completed before shutdown and successful restoration with timestamps; formal runbooks and automated enforcement.
Weak: Anecdotal reports of shutdowns or undocumented manual shutdowns without backup verification or post-incident restoration evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates a preventive action (triggering a system shutdown to prevent further data loss) and pre-shutdown preservation steps, aligning it with preventive controls.

AU-5(4).differentiation_note: AU-5(4) is distinguished by enforcing an automated preventive action (shutdown) with required pre-shutdown backups, unlike other enhancements that focus on alerts or failover.

AU-5(5).measurement_goal: Measure the existence and readiness of alternate logging capability, count and frequency of failovers to the alternate system, the time-to-switch (failover time), and the completeness/integrity of logs captured during failover periods.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control emphasizes continuity and recovery via failover; rate metrics (failover success rate, time-to-switch rates) measure operational effectiveness, with counts of failovers and percentage completeness as complements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Records showing automatic activation of the alternate logging mechanism when primary fails (failover events with timestamps), time elapsed between primary failure and alternate activation (time-to-switch), success rate of alternate writes (percentage of expected events captured), and periodic test results of failover capability.
data_signals: failover activation events with timestamps and triggering primary-failure IDs
time-to-switch/failover duration measurements
counts of events written to alternate logging store during failover
comparisons of expected vs. actual event counts during failover windows (completeness)
security controls protecting alternate store (encryption, access logs)
regular failover test run logs and verification results
capacity and performance metrics for alternate logging solution
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated failover logs showing immediate activation, short time-to-switch, event-level evidence that events were captured in the alternate store with integrity checks and test run history.
Weak: Ad-hoc manual failover, absent test records, or lack of event-level comparison between primary and alternate stores.

affinity_keywords: capacity and performance of the alternate

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement requires activating an alternate logging mechanism when the primary fails ('activates when the primary audit logging system fails'), indicating corrective/continuity behavior.

AU-5(5).differentiation_note: AU-5(5) focuses on continuity via a secondary logging system and measures of failover success and completeness, a unique continuity requirement compared with capacity alerts, real-time detection, or shutdown behaviors.

AU-6.measurement_goal: Measure log collection coverage for critical systems (percentage of critical systems whose logs are ingested), the count of audit records reviewed per review cycle, the count and severity distribution of anomalies detected from logs, and mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to respond (MTTR) for incidents identified via log review.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a detective control that requires counting reviewed records, anomalies and incidents; percentage metrics (coverage, percent reviewed) and trends over time are useful secondary indicators of effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of audit records ingested and reviewed per log source and per review cycle; ingestion timestamps and retention records; count and severity of anomalies detected from log analysis; timestamps for detection and incident ticket creation to compute MTTD/MTTR. Group by log source (application, OS, network, firewall) and criticality tier.
data_signals: audit log ingestion timestamps by source
log source identifiers and criticality tags (e.g., server, firewall, application)
number of audit records reviewed per reviewer/per review cycle
alert/anomaly records produced by log analysis (with severity and source)
incident/ticket creation timestamps linked to log findings
log integrity / tamper check results (checksums, WORM flags)
log retention policy records (retention start/end timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, timestamped ingestion and analysis logs (SIEM ingestion + analyzer output) are strong evidence; periodic spreadsheets or ad-hoc screenshots are weak.
Automated linkage from alert to incident ticket (showing timestamps) is strong; manual, unaudited email reports are weak.
Immutable/tamper-evident storage records (checksums, WORM) are strong; editable logs without integrity metadata are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description emphasizes reviewing and analyzing audit logs to identify incidents and anomalies, which is characteristic of a detective control.

AU-6.differentiation_note: This entry covers the broad, program-level audit record review and reporting requirements (coverage, review counts, MTTD/MTTR) rather than specific integrations, correlation or privileged-command analyses addressed by siblings.

AU-6(1).measurement_goal: Measure percentage of relevant systems whose logs are centrally ingested (log ingestion coverage), ingestion success rate, parser/normalization failure rate, and the percentage of anomalies initially detected by automated analysis (automation detection rate).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Control is described as preventive/automated integration; coverage and compliance (percent of systems centrally logging) are primary indicators. Counts of alerts and parser failures are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of defined relevant systems (inventory list) with successful log ingestion into the centralized logging system; ingestion success/failure counts per source; number of integrated log sources; automated analysis alert counts and percentage of anomalies detected by automation vs manual review.
data_signals: list of relevant system identifiers and expected log sources
log ingestion success/failure timestamps and error codes
number of integrated log sources (by type: server, firewall, app)
parser/normalization failure counts and reasons
automated analysis/automation-generated alert records
timestamps for log delivery and earliest available log for each source
percentage of incidents first detected by automated tools
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, machine-recorded ingestion metrics and parser logs (with success/failure codes) are strong evidence; manual attestations that systems are 'connected' without ingestion logs are weak.
Automated alerts with provenance back to raw logs and ingestion timestamps are strong; one-off exported reports without traceability are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control is labeled and described as a preventive automated process integration that focuses on centralized aggregation and automated detection to prevent missed events.

AU-6(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on centralized automated ingestion and integration (coverage and parser reliability) rather than manual review, correlation logic, or privileged-command analysis.

AU-6(2).measurement_goal: Count security alerts generated by automated analysis (by severity) per time period, measure the percentage of alerts that are actionable/validated, and measure mean time to acknowledge (MTA) and mean time to respond (MTTR) for automated alerts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control focused on automated alerts, raw counts of alerts (and counts by severity) are primary; percentages of actionable alerts and trends are important secondary measures of quality and tuning.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of automated security alerts generated per alert rule (with severity and source), count of alerts validated as actionable and escalated to incident response, timestamps for alert generation/acknowledgement/closure to compute MTA/MTTR, and false positive/true positive labeling.
data_signals: automated alert records from SIEM (alert ID, rule name, severity, timestamp, source)
alert validation/escalation flags (actionable vs false positive)
incident/ticket IDs linked to alerts and associated timestamps
average time-to-acknowledge and time-to-resolve per alert
alert threshold and rule configuration records
percent of alerts that led to confirmed incidents
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time SIEM alert streams correlated to incident tickets (with timestamps) are strong evidence; periodic email summaries without ticket linkage are weak.
Low false positive rate and high percentage of alerts mapped to incidents indicate strong tuning; large volumes of untriaged alerts are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control explicitly describes configuring automated alerts and measuring alert generation and response, which are hallmark detective activities.

AU-6(2).differentiation_note: This entry specifically targets automated alert generation and alert-handling metrics (alert counts, actionability, MTA/MTTR) rather than ingestion coverage or manual review frequency.

AU-6(3).measurement_goal: Count of correlated events produced by correlation rules (and distribution by correlation type), percentage of correlated events that generate alerts, and coverage of contextual enrichment fields (e.g., user role, access level) included in correlation outputs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective correlation activities are best evidenced by counts of correlated events and counts of alerts triggered; percentages of successful enrichments and distributions across correlation types help assess quality.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of correlated events produced per correlation rule (with inputs listed), number of source repositories contributing to correlations, presence of enrichment/context fields (user role, access level, system config), and count/timestamps of incidents opened due to correlated events.
data_signals: correlation engine output records (correlated_event_id, rule_id, input_event_ids)
list of log repositories ingested and last-ingest timestamps
enrichment fields present (user role, access level, system configuration)
anomaly/correlation severity and timestamp
incident tickets opened as a result of correlated events (with timestamps)
baseline normal activity profile used for correlation
evidence_strength_indicators: Correlation outputs with provenance linking back to raw log events and enrichment attributes are strong evidence; manually compiled correlation spreadsheets are weak.
High percentage of correlated events that lead to validated incidents indicates effective correlation; many correlations never triaged are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires correlating audit records across sources to detect anomalies and incidents, which is detective in nature.

AU-6(3).differentiation_note: Focuses on cross-repository correlation and enrichment coverage (presence of contextual fields) as opposed to single-source ingestion, automated alerting, or privileged-command parsing.

AU-6(4).measurement_goal: Measure adherence to scheduled central reviews (percentage of scheduled reviews completed on time), count of audit records analyzed centrally per review cycle, false positive rate in analysis, and average time from detection to reporting to stakeholders.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This detective control is evidenced by counts (reviews performed, records analyzed) while percentages (on-time review completion, false positive rate) and trends over review cycles are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of centralized log reviews conducted per scheduled interval and timestamps, count of audit records analyzed centrally (by source), percent of scheduled reviews completed on-time, number of findings reported to stakeholders, and time from detection to stakeholder report.
data_signals: central review schedule and completion logs (review ID, reviewer, timestamp)
count of audit records processed during each central review
reported findings and distribution to stakeholders (report IDs, timestamps)
false positive / confirmed incident counts from central analysis
integration records between central review tools and incident response
log scope definition and retention policy entries
evidence_strength_indicators: Centralized review logs with reviewer identity and timestamps plus automated processing evidence are strong; ad-hoc or undocumented reviews are weak.
Consistent reduction in overdue reviews and decreasing MTTD over multiple periods indicates strengthening controls; one-off reviews show weaker evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Emphasis on scheduled central reviews and analysis to detect and report incidents indicates a detective function.

AU-6(4).differentiation_note: Emphasizes scheduled, centralized human+automated review cadence and reporting metrics (on-time completion and stakeholder reporting) rather than ingestion, correlation or privileged-command specifics.

AU-6(5).measurement_goal: Measure percentage of defined log types/sources ingested into SIEM (ingestion coverage), count of anomalies detected by integrated SIEM analytics per period, and percentage of incidents resolved within defined remediation timeframe.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective SIEM-based analysis is best evidenced by counts of anomalies and incidents; percentage metrics for ingestion coverage and remediation SLAs complement counts.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number and percentage of expected log sources successfully ingested into the SIEM, counts of anomalies flagged by SIEM analytics (with severity), timestamps for detection and remediation to compute mean time to remediate by severity, and documentation of baseline behavior models used for detection.
data_signals: SIEM ingestion metrics (source id, last ingest timestamp, ingestion success/failure)
anomaly/alert records with severity and analytic rule id
baseline behavioral model identifiers and versions
incident/ticket records linked to SIEM alerts with timestamps
remediation timestamps to calculate MTTR and percent resolved within timeframes
documentation of log types collected (system access, application, network)
evidence_strength_indicators: SIEM logs with provenance linking alerts to raw events and remediation tickets are strong; manual correlation without SIEM audit trails is weak.
High ingestion coverage plus low MTTR and high percent resolved within SLA indicate strong evidence; missing ingestion or unresolved alerts indicate weaknesses.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Describes SIEM-based, integrated analysis and tracking of anomalies and remediation—core detective functions.

AU-6(5).differentiation_note: Targets SIEM-specific ingestion, analytics and remediation tracking (coverage, anomalies, resolution SLA) rather than general review cadence or cross-repository correlation.

AU-6(6).measurement_goal: Measure count and percentage of security events that are correlated across physical monitoring (e.g., badge/CCTV) and digital logs, count of actionable incidents resulting from such correlation, and average response time for correlated incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective correlation requires counting correlated events and incidents; percentages (e.g., percent actionable) and trends help assess effectiveness of cross-domain correlation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of correlation events that link physical monitoring records (badge swipes, CCTV events) with digital audit logs (authentication, application access), percentage of correlated events flagged as actionable, timestamps for correlation and incident creation to compute response time.
data_signals: physical monitoring logs (badge access timestamps, CCTV event timestamps)
digital audit logs (authentication logs, session start/stop with timestamps)
correlation engine outputs linking physical and digital events (correlated_event_id)
incident/ticket records spawned from correlated events (with timestamps)
percentage of correlated alerts deemed actionable after triage
log source ingestion timestamps for both physical and digital sources
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated correlation outputs with provenance to both physical and digital raw events plus incident linkage are strong; manual cross-checks between CCTV footage and logs without automated records are weak.
High percent actionable correlations and short response times strengthen evidence; many unmatched or unanalyzed correlated results weaken it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control requires correlating audit logs with physical monitoring to detect incidents, a detection-oriented activity.

AU-6(6).differentiation_note: Unique requirement to link digital audit logs with physical monitoring sources (badge/CCTV), so measurement must include physical-log ingestion and cross-source correlation counts, unlike purely digital log reviews.

AU-6(7).measurement_goal: Measure percentage of defined log scope reviewed within the designated timeframe, count of audit findings related to unauthorized or non-permitted actions, and mean time to investigate findings related to permitted-action violations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective review of permitted actions is best evidenced by counts of items reviewed and violations found; percentages (percent of logs reviewed on schedule, percent violations) are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of log records within the defined scope reviewed per timeframe, count of detected unauthorized/non-permitted actions (with user and timestamp), percent of logs reviewed within the required retention/period timeframe, and time from detection to investigation closure.
data_signals: scope definition of logs to be reviewed (log types and systems)
review completion records (review_id, reviewer, timestamp)
records of detected unauthorized actions (user id, action, timestamp)
percentage of logs reviewed within designated timeframe
incident/ticket records for investigations spawned from permitted-action findings
retention timeframe entries for logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, timestamped review logs mapping reviewers to reviewed record sets are strong evidence; untracked manual reviews are weak.
High percent of scheduled reviews completed and low time-to-investigate for findings are strong indicators; infrequent reviews and unresolved findings are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control requires systematic review to identify unauthorized or suspicious permitted actions, which is detective in nature.

AU-6(7).differentiation_note: Specifically targets detection of unauthorized or non-permitted actions within the reviewed scope, requiring measurement of review completeness and counts of permissions-related violations rather than general ingestion or SIEM analytics.

AU-6(8).measurement_goal: Count privileged-command execution records captured (by user and system), count of anomalous privileged commands flagged (and percent flagged vs total privileged commands), and mean time to investigate flagged privileged-command incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective analysis of privileged commands is best evidenced by counts of commands captured and counts of anomalous detections; percent anomalous and trends over time are useful complements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of privileged-command executions logged (with full command text, user id, host, and timestamp), count of commands flagged by full-text analysis or ML as anomalous (with anomaly score), timestamps for alert generation and investigation start/closure to compute response times, and list of definitions/classification of what constitutes a privileged command.
data_signals: full command text logs (command string, user id, host, timestamp)
privileged-command classification list (which commands are privileged)
anomaly detection outputs (flag, score, rule/model id)
session recordings or shell history linkages
investigation/ticket records for flagged commands (timestamps)
access controls restricting log access (who can view logs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous capture of full command text with provenance and automated anomaly flags (with scores) is strong evidence; periodic manual audits of command history without parsing are weak.
Clear mapping from flagged command to investigation ticket with closure timestamps strengthens evidence; no ticketing or ad-hoc notes is weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Focuses on analyzing logs of privileged commands to detect misuse—detection through analysis is central to the control.

AU-6(8).differentiation_note: Unique focus on capturing and analyzing full command text and applying text/ML analysis to privileged commands, requiring command-level logging and anomaly scoring rather than general event correlation or ingestion coverage.

AU-6(9).measurement_goal: Measure percentage of organizational assets inventoried and classified (by asset type and criticality), frequency of inventory updates, and count of assets missing owner or classification tags.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is described as a preventive control (asset identification/classification); percentage coverage of inventory and classification completeness are primary indicators, with counts and trends as secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets recorded in the authoritative inventory vs expected assets (by discovery scans and CMDB), classification labels (sensitivity/criticality) present for each asset, timestamps of last inventory update per asset, and count of assets without assigned owner or classification.
data_signals: authoritative asset inventory records (asset id, type, owner, classification, last_update_timestamp)
automated discovery scan outputs (discovered asset id, discovery timestamp)
classification labels and criticality tiers
percentage of assets with assigned owner field populated
frequency/timestamp of inventory updates by asset
unauthorized access attempt counts per asset (linked to inventory)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated discovery results reconciled to the authoritative inventory with timestamps and classification fields are strong; manual spreadsheets or stale inventories are weak.
High percent inventoried and classified with recent update timestamps is strong; many assets lacking classification or owner is weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description emphasizes establishing and enforcing inventory and classification to prevent unmanaged assets and prioritize controls, which is preventive.

AU-6(9).differentiation_note: Although included under AU-6 variants, this item is an asset-management preventive control (inventory/classification) rather than an audit log analysis/control—measurements must focus on inventory coverage and classification completeness.

AU-6(10).measurement_goal: Measure the volume and coverage of audit records reviewed, the frequency and timeliness of analytic reviews and reports, and the number and timeliness of audit-level adjustments made based on risk assessments (e.g., per system or application). An auditor should be able to see counts of records reviewed, detected anomalies, review cadence, time-to-response for flagged items, and a record of audit-level changes tied to risk decisions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control focused on event detection and review activity: counts (e.g., audit records reviewed, anomalies detected) directly evidence detection, while rates (alerts per volume) and percentages (coverage of systems forwarding logs) provide additional context on effectiveness and coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of audit records ingested and the subset that were reviewed/annotated per review cycle, per system/application; number of anomaly/alert events generated from audit analysis; timestamps for review actions and time-to-first-response for flagged items; audit level setting per log source and timestamped changes with justification/risk assessment. Also measure log source coverage (percentage of critical systems forwarding logs) and frequency of scheduled reviews executed.
data_signals: audit log ingestion timestamps and source identifiers
log source inventory with assigned audit_level or logging_detail setting
audit review events (reviewer ID, review timestamp, reviewed_record_count, review_notes)
anomaly detection/alert records referencing audit logs (alert id, severity, timestamp)
time-to-acknowledge and time-to-action timestamps for flagged audit items
audit level change requests/approvals with timestamps and linked risk assessment
scheduled review execution records (planned vs executed review timestamps)
documentation of findings and actions taken (report artifacts, ticket IDs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated ingestion logs + timestamped, auditable review events and actions tied to alerts and audit-level change records
Strong: full log-source inventory showing audit_level settings and recent automated reviews for each critical system
Weak: ad-hoc manual summaries or one-off screenshots of logs without reviewer identifiers or timestamps
Weak: absence of log-source inventory or missing timestamped evidence of audit-level adjustments and tied risk assessments
