metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on baseline coverage and compliance; auditors want percent-compliant coverage as the primary view, with counts of deviations and trends to show change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets (by asset ID) with an associated baseline configuration document or template and current configuration values matching that baseline; count of deviation events and remediation ticket timestamps to compute mean time-to-remediate.
data_signals: asset inventory records with asset IDs and types
baseline configuration document IDs and revision timestamps
configuration item (CI) values exported from systems (system settings, installed applications, network configurations)
automated configuration scan / compliance check results (pass/fail per CI)
deviation event records (detected_drift events) with detection timestamps
remediation/change request records with open/close timestamps and approvals
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated configuration scans matching asset inventory and baseline templates (strong)
Continuous CMDB-synced configuration values vs. manual spreadsheet snapshots (weak)
Time-stamped remediation tickets linked to detected deviations (strong)
Ad-hoc one-off audit reports without machine-readable scan output (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description frames establishing and maintaining baselines and minimizing attack surface, which are preventive activities ('establishes and maintains a documented baseline configuration').

CM-2.differentiation_note: This is the broad, organization-wide baseline control focused on coverage and remediation metrics; siblings are narrower (reviews, automation, retention, software-specific, environment-specific).

CM-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the count and frequency of baseline configuration review events and the percentage of assets whose configurations were reviewed and updated on schedule, plus counts of detected configuration drift during those reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control (reviews/updates); counts of review events and detected drifts are primary signals, with compliance percentage and trend to show coverage and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of documented baseline review events (with reviewer and timestamp), percentage of assets reviewed against baseline in each review period, and number of drift findings per review with remediation start timestamps.
data_signals: baseline review schedule and event logs (review_id, reviewer, timestamp)
baseline revision timestamps and change notes
configuration comparison/audit reports (current vs baseline) with detected discrepancies
asset lists included in each review
remediation/change request records created as a result of reviews
frequency/calendar of scheduled reviews vs. actual executed reviews
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-generated comparison reports for each review period with per-asset discrepancy counts (strong)
Signed manual review checklists without comparison artifacts (weaker)
Consistent timestamps showing reviews executed on the documented schedule (strong)
Irregular or undocumented review events (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly requires reviews and audits of baseline configurations ('reviews and updates'), which are detective activities to find drift and discrepancies.

CM-2(1).differentiation_note: Focused on the review/update process, so measurement must capture scheduled review events and findings rather than baseline coverage alone.

CM-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems under automated discovery and continuous configuration monitoring, the frequency of automated compliance checks, and the count/rate of configuration-drift incidents detected by automation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automation is preventive (ensuring accuracy/currency across systems); percentage coverage of automated tooling is the primary metric, with counts of drift detections and trends to show effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of inventoryed systems discovered by configuration management tools and subject to continuous automated checks, number of automated compliance runs per system per day/week, and count/time-to-detect for automated drift alerts.
data_signals: configuration management tool discovery logs (discovery_timestamp, asset_id)
automated compliance scan results (scan_id, asset_id, pass/fail, timestamp)
integration logs showing tool-to-incident-response handoffs
configuration drift alerts generated by automation with detection timestamps
synchronization timestamps between CMDB and automated tools
percentage of assets lacking automation coverage
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, timestamped discovery and scan outputs correlated to asset inventory (strong)
Automated, machine-readable drift alerts with linked remediation tickets (strong)
Manual spot-checks or ad-hoc scans reported only in documents (weak)
Gaps between CMDB and automation discovery (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description centers on automating discovery/monitoring to keep baselines accurate and current, which prevents misconfiguration and drift.

CM-2(2).differentiation_note: This sibling is specific to automation coverage and accuracy; measurement must show tool discovery rates, sync timestamps, and automated drift detections rather than manual audit counts.

CM-2(3).measurement_goal: Measure the count of retained configuration snapshots per asset, adherence to retention policy (percent of assets with required retention metadata), and the rate/success of restoring previous configurations within target timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control enables corrective actions (rollback/restore); restoration success rate and rate of being able to retrieve prior configurations are most relevant, with counts of retained versions as supporting metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number and timestamps of stored configuration snapshots per asset/version (version_id), metadata indicating retention period and owner, records of restore attempts with success/failure and restore duration to compute restore success rate and mean time-to-restore.
data_signals: configuration repository entries (snapshot_id, asset_id, timestamp, version)
version control audit trails and retention policy metadata
restore execution logs (restore_id, snapshot_id, start_time, end_time, success_flag)
backup/replication status for configuration repository
change request references linking config captures to deployment/change events
periodic audits comparing repository contents to required retention policy
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-readable repository with versioned snapshots and successful restore test logs (strong)
Documented retention policy plus periodic restore exercise evidence (strong)
Ad-hoc manual copies or undocumented retained configs (weak)
Restore attempts failing or lacking timestamps (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on retaining prior configurations to enable recovery/rollback, which is a corrective capability ('enable quick recovery from configuration errors').

CM-2(3).differentiation_note: Unlike other baselines, this control requires measurable versioning/retention and restore-success metrics rather than just compliance coverage.

CM-2(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of endpoints compliant with the approved software list and the count and rate of unauthorized software instances detected and remediated (with median time-to-remediation).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, percent compliance with an approved software list is the primary indicator; counts and rates of unauthorized installations provide detective/remediation context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of endpoints whose installed application inventory matches the approved/authorized software list (including version), count of unauthorized software detection events, and timestamps for detection-to-removal to compute remediation time.
data_signals: approved applications inventory / whitelist (application name, version, approval_id)
endpoint software inventory scans (host_id, installed_packages, timestamps)
unauthorized software detection alerts with host_id and timestamp
remediation/change tickets with detection-to-resolution timestamps
audit logs showing removal/uninstallation events
frequency of unauthorized software installations per time period
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated endpoint inventory scans correlated to approved list with per-host mismatch reports (strong)
Automated remediation actions or documented removal tickets linked to detection alerts (strong)
Periodic manual audit-only findings without automation evidence (weak)
Untracked unauthorized installs or stale whitelist (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control intends to prevent installation/execution of unauthorized software through inventories and enforcement ('prevent the installation and execution of unauthorized software').

CM-2(4).differentiation_note: This sibling targets unauthorized software detection and remediation metrics specifically at the application/endpoint level, not general baseline settings.

CM-2(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems matching the authorized software inventory (by name and version), the count of deviations (unauthorized or mismatched versions), and average time-to-approve new software requests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percent of systems aligned with the authorized software baseline is the primary compliance metric; counts of deviations and approval-time trends supplement it.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of hosts whose installed packages/version tuples exactly match entries in the authorized software inventory; count of systems with unauthorized or out-of-date authorized software; time between software approval request and approval/compliance deployment.
data_signals: authorized software inventory with version numbers (software_name, version, approval_date)
deployment/package manager logs showing installed versions per host
compliance scan outputs showing mismatched or missing authorized software
software approval request and approval timestamps
audit reports of periodic baseline audits
counts of unauthorized software installations detected during audits
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated inventory versus authorized list matching with version-level checks (strong)
Audit logs showing software approvals and deployment records (strong)
Manual inventories or stale authorized lists with no versioning (weak)
Lack of timestamps linking approval to deployment (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is about establishing and enforcing an authorized software baseline to prevent unauthorized installs and configuration drift, which is preventive in nature.

CM-2(5).differentiation_note: While CM-2(4) focuses on detecting and removing unauthorized software, this control focuses on documenting and maintaining the authorized software baseline and approval lifecycle.

CM-2(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of development and test environment systems with documented baseline configurations that are segregated from production, and the count of unauthorized changes in those environments per period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires verifying baseline coverage and segregation for dev/test; percent coverage of dev/test baseline plus counts of unauthorized changes best evidence adherence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of dev/test hosts with an assigned baseline configuration template and network/ACL evidence of segregation from production; number of unauthorized or unapproved changes recorded in dev/test environment change logs and time to remediate.
data_signals: environment tagging (environment=dev/test/production) in asset inventory
baseline templates for dev/test with revision timestamps
network segmentation configs and ACLs showing segregation
configuration change logs for dev/test hosts (change_id, approver, timestamp)
automated config scan outputs for dev/test hosts
deployment pipeline/config-as-code run logs for dev/test
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-readable environment tags plus automated scan evidence of segregation and baseline matching (strong)
Change logs showing approvals tied to dev/test baseline templates (strong)
Informal or undocumented segregation (weak)
Manual notes without machine-backed evidence of baseline compliance (weak)

affinity_keywords: baseline configurations for development and test
segregation of development and test from production
integration of security testing into development lifecycle

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is about establishing and maintaining baselines and segregation for dev/test to prevent insecure changes reaching production, which is preventive.

CM-2(6).differentiation_note: Targets configuration controls specifically for dev/test environments—measurements must capture segregation, environment tagging, and plumbed CI/CD pipeline evidence rather than org-wide baselines.

CM-2(7).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of high-risk systems configured to the documented hardened baseline and the count/rate of unauthorized configuration changes in high-risk areas, plus time-to-detect and remediate those changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control for high-risk assets requires percent-compliant hardened configuration as primary evidence, plus counts and distribution of deviations to prioritize remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets in the defined high-risk asset list whose current configurations match the hardened baseline; number of unauthorized configuration changes detected in high-risk assets and detection-to-remediation durations.
data_signals: high-risk asset inventory/list (asset_id, risk_flag)
hardened baseline configuration documents for those assets
compliance scan results for high-risk assets (pass/fail per control)
change approval logs filtered for high-risk assets
unauthorized change alerts for high-risk asset IDs with timestamps
frequency and timestamps of baseline reviews for high-risk assets
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous monitoring and automated hardening checks for high-risk asset set with per-asset compliance status (strong)
Change logs showing required approvals for high-risk assets and enforcement events (strong)
Periodic manual review only for high-risk assets without automation (weaker)
High variance in compliance distribution across high-risk asset classes (weak)

affinity_keywords: Configure Systems and Components for High-risk Areas

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates establishing and enforcing hardened baselines for high-risk areas to mitigate vulnerabilities and unauthorized changes, which is preventive.

CM-2(7).differentiation_note: This sibling scopes baseline hardening and enforcement to a high-risk asset subset; metrics must be filtered to the high-risk asset list rather than org-wide coverage.

CM-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of configuration changes that are authorized, documented in the CMDB, tested in a pre-production environment, and reviewed within defined approval timeframes; report count of unauthorized or undocumented changes and mean approval-to-deploy time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., percent of changes documented/approved). Counts and trends supplement by showing volumes and improvement over time, while rates capture approval velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of deployed configuration changes that have a linked change request, approval record, CMDB entry, and test validation record; count of detected unauthorized changes and average approval-to-implementation time (timestamp delta between approval and deployment).
data_signals: change request records with unique IDs and submitter
approval workflow timestamps (requested, approved, rejected)
CMDB entries/records linked to change IDs
test/validation results and test environment deployment timestamps
deployment/change log entries (deployment timestamp, change ID, executor)
unauthorized/unscheduled change alerts or incident tickets
change audit/review logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated linkage between change requests, approvals, CMDB, and deployments (strong).
Continuous change logs with timestamps and IDs (strong) vs. ad‑hoc spreadsheets or manual snapshots (weak).
Presence of test validation artifacts and rollback plans for each change (strong).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description emphasizes managing and documenting changes before implementation (authorized, tested, approved) which is preventive in nature.

CM-3.differentiation_note: This is the parent control covering the end-to-end change process (authorization, testing, CMDB linkage) whereas sub-controls focus on automation, testing details, roles, cryptography, or prohibition specifics.

CM-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of change requests that are automatically documented and notified to approvers, the count of prohibited/blocked unauthorized changes by the system, and average time to notify designated authorities after submission.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive automation control, coverage percentages (automated documentation/notification coverage) show compliance; counts of blocked/prohibited changes and notification latency provide operational insight.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of change submissions that generate an automated documentation record and approver notification; count and timestamps of automatic prohibition/block actions for unauthorized change attempts; notification latency (submission→notified approver).
data_signals: automated change request creation logs (system-generated entries)
notification events and delivery receipts to designated approval authorities
prohibition/block action logs (change attempt blocked, reason code)
integration logs between change system and CMDB
approval/rejection records correlated to automated notifications
security impact analysis artifacts linked to automated workflows
evidence_strength_indicators: System-generated records and delivery receipts proving automated notifications (strong).
Automated block/prohibition events recorded with reason codes (strong).
Manual emails/screenshots without system logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text requires automated documentation/notification and prohibition of unauthorized changes before they occur, clearly preventive.

CM-3(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on automation of documentation, notification, and automated prohibition/blocking — measurement must capture system-generated notifications and block events rather than manual approvals.

CM-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of changes that undergo testing and validation in a dedicated test environment before deployment, the first-pass test success rate, and the count/mean time of rollbacks triggered by post-deployment failures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Testing coverage and pass-rate percentages are primary to demonstrate preventive validation; rates and counts capture rollback frequency and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of change records linked to test environment execution with pass/fail outcomes, first-pass pass rate, timestamped test execution and validation artifacts, and count/timestamp of rollbacks or failed deployments.
data_signals: change request IDs linked to test execution logs
test environment deployment timestamps and test results
test-case identifiers and pass/fail status
rollback/rollback-plan execution logs
change documentation and approval records
time to resolve post-change issues (ticket creation → resolution)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated test execution logs with pass/fail and artifacts (strong).
Presence of rollback execution logs correlated to change IDs (strong).
Manual sign-off without test artifacts (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires pre-deployment testing and validation of changes (prevention of erroneous changes).

CM-3(2).differentiation_note: Emphasizes pre-deployment testing artifacts and rollback readiness — measurement must capture test execution records and first-pass success rather than only approvals.

CM-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of configuration changes deployed via automated pipelines, success rate of automated deployments, and the count/time of human interventions or manual deployments due to automation failures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control focused on automation coverage: percent of changes executed by approved automation and success rates are primary; counts and rates measure failures and human overrides.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of changes executed through automated deployment pipelines linked to change IDs, automated deployment success/failure counts, timestamps for automated deployment start/finish, and number of manual rollbacks or manual interventions per period.
data_signals: CI/CD pipeline logs with change ID and job status
deployment success/failure events and error codes
integration records linking pipeline runs to CMDB updates
manual deployment records and justification notes
rollback execution logs and timestamps
approval-to-deploy automation audit trails
evidence_strength_indicators: End-to-end automated pipeline logs showing successful deployment and CMDB update (strong).
Automated validation post-deploy reports (strong).
Manual screenshots or undocumented manual deployments (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Specifies automated execution of changes to reduce human error and enforce policy before or during deployment (preventive).

CM-3(3).differentiation_note: Unique measurement need: track automated pipeline artifacts (CI/CD logs) and measure automation coverage and failure/override counts versus manual deployments.

CM-3(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of configuration changes that received explicit security and/or privacy representative approval prior to deployment, and track incidents linked to changes that lacked such approvals.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Demonstrating percent of changes approved by security/privacy reps shows preventive policy enforcement; counts and trends show scale and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of change requests with a recorded security/privacy approver ID and approval timestamp; count of deployed changes missing security/privacy approval and any resulting security incidents linked to those changes.
data_signals: change request approval records with approver role (security/privacy)
approval timestamps and comments
change deployment logs correlated to approval records
security/privacy impact assessment artifacts
incident tickets referencing changes lacking approval
training records for change approvers (when cited)
evidence_strength_indicators: Signed/automated approval records showing approver role and timestamp (strong).
Linkage between approval record and deployed change in CMDB (strong).
Oral confirmations or undated approvals (weak).

affinity_keywords: security incidents related to configuration changes

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires approval by security/privacy roles before implementation, preventing risky changes.

CM-3(4).differentiation_note: Measurement must specifically verify presence and identity of security/privacy approvers and link approvals to deployments — unlike generic change coverage, this requires role-specific approval evidence.

CM-3(5).measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of automated security responses triggered by unauthorized or unapproved configuration changes, average time-to-detect and time-to-remediate these changes, and percent of unauthorized changes remediated automatically versus manually.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Corrective/preventive hybrid: measuring rate and speed of automated responses (time-based) is crucial; counts and percentages show scale and automation coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate and count of automated alerts/actions (e.g., revert, quarantined) executed against unauthorized change attempts, detection-to-remediation time intervals, and percent of unauthorized changes remediated by automation versus manual handling.
data_signals: automated response/action logs (revert, block, quarantine) with timestamps
unauthorized change detection alerts with source/target system
remediation tickets and resolution timestamps
audit/change logs showing pre/post automated remediation state
policy violation reason codes
evidence_strength_indicators: System logs showing automated response execution and success with timestamps (strong).
Correlation between detection alert and automated remediation within SLA (strong).
Manual incident reports without automation logs (weak).

affinity_keywords: frequency of changes leading to security incidents

control_type_classification.reasoning: Although it includes automated responses (reactive), the control is framed as preventing risks by integrating automated security response into change control, so classified preventive with some corrective characteristics.

CM-3(5).differentiation_note: Combines change control with automated security responses — measurement must capture automated detection and remediation execution logs and timing, not just approvals.

CM-3(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of cryptographic configuration changes that are recorded in the cryptographic asset inventory, assessed for security impact, approved, and logged; track frequency of unauthorized or out-of-policy cryptographic changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive controls around cryptography need coverage percentages (what fraction of crypto changes are properly managed); counts and distributions (by algorithm/key type) supplement risk posture.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of cryptographic configuration changes linked to cryptographic asset inventory entries, with approval and security assessment artifacts; counts of unauthorized or undocumented cryptographic changes and time to remediate or roll back those changes.
data_signals: cryptographic asset inventory records (key IDs, algorithms, expiration)
change requests for cryptographic configuration items with approval stamps
audit logs showing cryptographic config modifications (who/when/what)
security review/impact assessment documents for crypto changes
automated policy enforcement events for crypto settings
key lifecycle events (generation, rotation, revocation) correlated to change records
evidence_strength_indicators: Immutable audit logs of cryptographic config changes and linkage to asset inventory (strong).
Recorded security assessments and approval artifacts for each crypto change (strong).
Manual notes without cryptographic asset linkage (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires pre-change identification, assessment, approval and documentation of cryptographic settings, preventing insecure crypto changes.

CM-3(6).differentiation_note: Unique focus on cryptographic assets — measurement must include crypto-specific inventory, key lifecycle events, and algorithm/configuration details rather than generic change logs.

CM-3(7).measurement_goal: Measure the count and percentage of changes reviewed by the Change Advisory Board (CAB) or equivalent, percentage of changes requiring additional security assessment, and average dwell time in review awaiting CAB decision.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control focused on review coverage — percentage of changes reviewed by CAB is primary; counts and time comparisons track backlog and decision timeliness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of change requests that were presented to and recorded as reviewed by the CAB (including decision and rationale), count of changes flagged for additional security assessment, and average time from proposal to CAB decision.
data_signals: CAB meeting minutes or decision logs linked to change IDs
change request review status fields and decision rationale
security impact analysis templates indicating additional assessment required
timestamps for proposal→CAB review→decision
change request form content and attached documentation
evidence_strength_indicators: CAB-authored decision logs with rationale and change ID cross-references (strong).
Recorded timestamped decisions and linkage to subsequent deployment (strong).
Informal reviewer notes without formal CAB record (weak).

affinity_keywords: frequency of incidents related to configuration changes

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires pre-implementation evaluation by CAB and documented rationale, which prevents risky changes.

CM-3(7).differentiation_note: Measurement must capture CAB-specific artifacts (meeting minutes/rationale and timestamps) and percent of changes routed to CAB, as opposed to automated or role-based approvals in other sub-controls.

CM-3(8).measurement_goal: Measure the count and percentage of configuration changes that were blocked or required explicit approval before modification, number of unauthorized changes detected, and average time to remediate unauthorized changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive focus on restriction/enforcement means coverage percentages (what proportion of changes are restricted/approved) are primary; counts/rates show frequency of blocked or unauthorized changes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of configuration items protected by enforcement controls (e.g., role-based locks, change windows) that require explicit approval prior to modification; count and remediation time of unauthorized configuration changes detected and reverted.
data_signals: policy enforcement logs (locks, ACLs, change windows)
blocked change attempt logs with reason codes
approved change records vs. blocked/unauthorized change records
incident or remediation tickets tied to unauthorized changes
user/role access records showing who attempted changes
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated enforcement logs showing block actions and successful prevention (strong).
Consistent audit trails linking attempts to user identities and remediation actions (strong).
Periodic manual spot-checks without enforcement logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Emphasizes preventing unauthorized changes by restricting actions and requiring approvals before implementation; clearly preventive.

CM-3(8).differentiation_note: Specifically requires enforcement/prevention (blocking) of configuration changes — measurement must include enforcement/block logs and counts of prevented unauthorized attempts, not just approval coverage.

CM-4.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of documented impact analyses for critical assets and quantify the percentage of risks identified by those analyses that have assigned mitigations and are remediated within target timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and remediation rates; percentage metrics (e.g., percent of critical assets analyzed, percent of identified risks mitigated) best demonstrate completeness, while counts and trends show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of impact analyses performed per asset criticality tier, percentage of critical assets with a completed impact analysis in the last 12 months, average time (hours/days) to complete an impact analysis, and percentage of identified risks from those analyses that have mitigation plans created and closed within defined targets.
data_signals: impact analysis report identifiers and timestamps
asset inventory with criticality classification
list of risks identified per analysis (risk ID, severity)
mitigation plan records with assignment and closure timestamps
time-to-complete metrics for each analysis (start/end timestamps)
cross-functional sign-off/approver entries in analysis documentation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: documented impact analysis artifacts with timestamps, asset criticality mapping, recorded approvers, and linked mitigation tickets showing remediation and closure dates.
Weak: periodic summary statements or annual slides without per-asset analysis documents, or manual attestations without supporting timestamps and remediation links.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control' and focuses on evaluating potential consequences to prioritize risk management.

CM-4.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall programmatic measurement of impact analyses (coverage, timeliness, and mitigation rates), whereas sibling controls focus on specific environments and verification activities.

CM-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of non-emergency changes that were executed in a dedicated test environment that mirrors production, track defect discovery rate during testing, and measure the rate of production incidents attributable to changes not tested in the separate environment.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control ensuring testing coverage before deployment, percentage metrics (coverage of changes tested in separate environments) are primary; counts of issues found and rates of production incidents are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of approved change requests that include a test execution record in a separate test environment, count of issues/defects identified during that test per change, time between test completion and resolution of test-identified issues, and count/rate of production incidents traced to changes that skipped separate environment testing.
data_signals: change request records with environment designation (test vs production)
test environment inventory and configuration snapshots
test execution logs and test result artifacts (pass/fail, defect IDs)
defect/issue tickets created during testing with timestamps
approval records showing pre-production sign-off
deployment records and any rollback/incident correlation to recent changes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CI/CD pipeline logs showing tests executed in separate environments, test artifacts, defect tickets linked to change IDs, and pre-deploy approvals.
Weak: isolated screenshots or verbal attestations that testing occurred, or reports without links between change requests, test artifacts, and deployment records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates evaluating changes in controlled test environments before production deployment, a classic preventive measure to avoid introducing vulnerabilities or disruptions.

CM-4(1).differentiation_note: This control specifically measures pre-deployment testing and environment mirroring (test coverage and defect discovery) rather than general impact analysis coverage or control verification.

CM-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and coverage of impact analyses performed to verify control effectiveness, the control effectiveness score or rating across the control inventory, and the percentage of identified control deficiencies remediated within target timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires assessing 'effectiveness' of controls; a quantifiable control effectiveness score summarizes test outcomes while percentages show remediation coverage and trends show improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Control inventory coverage (count of controls in scope), frequency of verification events per control, control effectiveness rating/score per verification (e.g., pass/partial/fail or numerical score), count and percentage of control deficiencies identified, and remediation ticket creation and closure times for those deficiencies.
data_signals: control inventory records with control IDs and owners
impact analysis/verification reports and timestamps
control test results and effectiveness ratings per control
vulnerability or deficiency tickets linked to control IDs with created/closed timestamps
frequency schedule records showing when each control was last verified
trend logs of risk level or control score over time
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: repeatable verification artifacts (test scripts, evidence outputs) with timestamps and remediation tickets linking deficiencies to fixes and closure evidence.
Weak: occasional narrative summaries, single-point manual attestations, or verification without linking test results to remediation actions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes regular analyses to 'evaluate the effectiveness of existing cybersecurity measures' to proactively identify gaps, aligning with preventive objectives.

CM-4(2).differentiation_note: Unlike CM-4 which covers program-level impact analysis metrics, CM-4(2) focuses on validation of control effectiveness (scoring and remediation of control deficiencies) rather than environment-specific testing or general coverage.

CM-5.measurement_goal: Measure enforcement and review of change access controls by reporting the percentage of change actions executed by authorized roles, the count of unauthorized change attempts, and the frequency of access reviews completed on schedule.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control where coverage and compliance (percent of changes by authorized roles, percent of access reviews completed) are most meaningful; counts of unauthorized attempts supplement coverage metrics and trends show ongoing effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of change actions (by system/component) performed by users with approved change roles, count of unauthorized change attempts (attempts blocked or flagged), and timestamps/frequency of completed access reviews against the documented change management policy.
data_signals: change execution logs with actor identity and role
role-based access control (RBAC) role assignment records
access review completion timestamps and reviewer IDs
unauthorized change attempt or failed authorization events
change request and approval records
MFA authentication logs for users with change privileges
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, automated change execution logs correlated to RBAC assignments and MFA events are strong evidence.
Periodic manual screenshots or one-off approval emails are weak evidence compared with automated logs and systematic access reviews.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Description repeatedly frames the control as limiting who can make changes and establishing RBAC and policies, which are preventive measures (phrases: 'preventive control', 'limit and manage who can make changes', 'only authorized personnel').

CM-5.differentiation_note: This is the general, overarching access-for-change control focused on role definitions, policy, and review frequency — sibling controls add automation, signatures, dual approval, or environment-specific restrictions that require different signals.

CM-5(1).measurement_goal: Demonstrate automated enforcement of change access by measuring the percentage of change requests subject to automated policy enforcement, count of enforcement events (blocks/denies), and completeness of audit records for every enforced change.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control emphasizing automation and audit records, percent coverage of automated enforcement (percent of change attempts processed by enforcement tooling) is primary; counts of denied attempts and trends of enforcement events supplement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of change actions routed through automated enforcement tools, count of enforcement/deny events by tool, and completeness of audit records (audit log entries with user, timestamp, action, approval status) for each change.
data_signals: automated enforcement tool logs showing policy decisions (allow/deny)
audit trail entries for change requests including approver IDs and timestamps
integration records between IAM and change management system
counts of denied or blocked change attempts
timestamps of automated review/approval actions
records of average time to review access requests (from submission to decision)
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-generated enforcement logs and immutable audit records (tamper-evident) are strong evidence.
Manual, ad-hoc approvals recorded only in emails or spreadsheets are weak compared with integrated enforcement and audit logs.

affinity_keywords: percentage of changes made by unauthorized users
average time to review access requests

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control explicitly requires automated enforcement and audit logs to ensure only authorized personnel can make changes ('automated tools to enforce access controls', 'audit logs').

CM-5(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on automation and auditable enforcement records (tool-generated allow/deny and audit trails) versus the parent control which includes policy and manual reviews.

CM-5(2).measurement_goal: Measure review coverage and timeliness by reporting the count of changes reviewed and approved, the percentage of changes with documented approvals, and average time-to-approval for all system changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control focused on counting reviewed/approved changes and detections of unauthorized changes; counts of reviewed changes and unauthorized incidents are most directly actionable, with percentage coverage as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of change records processed, count of changes with documented approval entries, timestamps for submission and approval to compute approval times, and count of unauthorized or unreviewed changes discovered by audits.
data_signals: change request submission records with unique IDs and timestamps
approval records (approver ID, approval timestamp, approval decision)
audit trail of implemented changes vs. approved change list
exceptions or retroactive authorization records
time-to-approval per change
records of unauthorized changes identified in audits
evidence_strength_indicators: Consistent, system-generated change request and approval records for every change are strong evidence.
Spotty manual records or post-facto approvals without automation are weak evidence for demonstrating systematic review of 'all changes'.

affinity_keywords: number of changes reviewed and approved

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control explicitly described as a detective control requiring systematic review and authorization of changes ('detective control', 'reviewed and authorized before implementation').

CM-5(2).differentiation_note: This sibling is specifically detective — measuring review and approval events and detecting unauthorized changes — unlike preventive siblings that enforce restrictions before changes occur.

CM-5(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of changes (code, components, binaries) that include valid cryptographic signatures and track the count of unsigned or invalidly signed components deployed to production.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive requirement to enforce digital signatures lends itself to percentage coverage (percent of deployed artifacts with valid signatures); counts of unsigned artifacts and trend show gaps over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of deployed components/artifacts with valid cryptographic signatures, count of artifacts with missing or invalid signatures, and timestamps of signing plus signer identity mapped to authorized signers in IAM.
data_signals: artifact metadata including signature presence and signature validation result
code-signing certificate records and signer identity
deployment logs showing artifact hash and signature verification
version control records with signed commits/tags
audit records of signed component reviews
records of training or policy on code signing
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated signature verification at build/deploy time with logged validation results is strong evidence.
Manual checklist attestations or post-deployment retrospective signing are weak compared to pre-deployment cryptographic verification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control mandates cryptographic signatures and preventing unauthorized modifications ('use of cryptographic signatures for all changes must be enforced'), which are preventive measures.

CM-5(3).differentiation_note: Uniquely focused on cryptographic provenance (signatures) for components — measurements must capture signature metadata and verification results rather than general access or approval counts.

CM-5(4).measurement_goal: Report the percentage of scoped changes that received the required dual approvals, count of changes executed with single-approver exceptions, and average approval lead time for dual-authorized changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Dual-authorization is a preventive governance control where percent compliance (percent of required changes with two approvers) best demonstrates control effectiveness; counts of exceptions and trends are useful additional signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of changes within the defined scope that have two distinct approver identities logged, count of single-approver changes that should have had dual authorization, and timestamps for both approvals to verify sequences.
data_signals: change request records with approver IDs and approval timestamps
policy definition of scope requiring dual authorization
exception records or documented waivers for single-approver changes
automated logs showing enforcement of dual-approval workflow
audit logs showing review of dual-approval adherence
evidence_strength_indicators: System-enforced dual-approval workflows producing immutable logs are strong evidence.
Manual attestations or email approvals lacking system linkage to change records are weak evidence of dual authorization compliance.

affinity_keywords: two or more individuals to approve
automated tools to facilitate tracking and logging
scope of changes that require dual authorization

control_type_classification.reasoning: Described as requiring two or more approvers and preventing unilateral control over changes ('preventive control', 'dual authorization'), indicating a preventive governance control.

CM-5(4).differentiation_note: Distinct from other controls by requiring and measuring multiple approvers per change — measurement must capture multi-actor approval records and exceptions, not just single approver counts.

CM-5(5).measurement_goal: Measure least-privilege enforcement in production by reporting the percentage of production accounts with permissions matching job-role templates, number of active highly privileged accounts, and frequency of production access reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Privilege limitation is preventive; percent of production accounts conforming to least-privilege (role templates) is the key coverage metric, with counts of privileged accounts and trends showing control drift.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of production/operational accounts whose assigned permissions match approved role-based templates (least privilege), counts of accounts with elevated privileges, and timestamps of last access reviews for production access.
data_signals: IAM role and permission assignments for production accounts
job-role permission templates and mapping records
access review logs for production environment
active privileged account inventory and last use timestamps
access request and approval records for production access
evidence_strength_indicators: Systemic IAM reports showing role-to-permission conformity and automated access-review evidence are strong.
Ad-hoc spreadsheets or verbal confirmations without IAM mapping are weak evidence for least-privilege enforcement.

affinity_keywords: Privilege Limitation for Production and Operation

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control prescribes restricting access by job function and enforcing least privilege in production (phrases: 'limit access', 'principle of least privilege'), characteristic of preventive controls.

CM-5(5).differentiation_note: Targets production/operational environment privilege minimization and requires IAM permission-to-role conformity metrics, unlike siblings that focus on approvals, signing, or library privileges.

CM-5(6).measurement_goal: Measure control over system library changes by reporting the count of library modification events by authorized users, percentage of libraries with restricted write privileges, and number of unauthorized library change attempts detected.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control aiming for coverage: percentage of critical libraries with restricted write privileges demonstrates enforcement; counts of modification events and unauthorized attempts provide detective context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical/system libraries whose write/modify privileges are limited to an approved list of identities, count of library modification events and who performed them, and count of failed/denied attempts to change library files.
data_signals: library file access control lists and permission assignments
file system or repository modification logs with actor identity and timestamps
inventory of critical libraries and their access policies
denied/blocked library modification events
approval records for granted library access requests
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated file-system or repository logs showing only approved identities modifying libraries are strong evidence.
Manual records of who 'should' have access without corroborating logs are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text focuses on limiting who can modify libraries and setting approval processes (preventive measures to avoid unauthorized modification).

CM-5(6).differentiation_note: Specifically about controlling write/modify privileges on system libraries and tracking modification events — measurement must inspect repository/file ACLs and modification logs rather than general change approvals.

CM-5(7).measurement_goal: Demonstrate automated enforcement of change-related security safeguards by measuring the percentage of security safeguards applied automatically during change workflows, count of automated enforcement events, and number of manual overrides or exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive automation is best evidenced by percent coverage of automatic safeguard implementation; counts of enforcement events and overrides help quantify operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of change requests where security safeguards were automatically enforced (e.g., enforced RBAC checks, policy gates), count of automated enforcement or blocking events, and count/timestamps of manual overrides with justification.
data_signals: automation tool logs (policy gate pass/fail) for change workflows
change management system records showing enforcement steps applied
list of personnel with change permissions and inventory of who was allowed automatic implementation
records of manual overrides and documented justification
logs of change approvals including automated safeguard results
evidence_strength_indicators: Immutable automation logs showing enforcement decisions per change are strong evidence.
Relying solely on manual attestations that safeguards were applied without automation logs is weak evidence.

affinity_keywords: inventory of personnel with change permissions
integrating change management processes with security protocols
time taken to approve change requests

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control requires automated enforcement of access policies and security safeguards ('employing automated tools to enforce these restrictions'), which are preventive controls.

CM-5(7).differentiation_note: Emphasizes automation of security safeguards across change workflows — measurements must capture automation tool outputs, enforcement events, and manual overrides distinct from non-automated approval measures.

CM-6.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems that conform to documented security baselines, the count of configuration deviations detected, and the average time to remediate deviations so an auditor can verify baseline adherence and remediation effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about baseline adherence and coverage (best evidenced by percent compliant across assets); counts of deviations and trends over time supplement percent compliance to show volume and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percent of inventoried assets whose active configuration matches the approved baseline (by baseline ID), count of distinct deviation events per asset, and mean/median time from deviation detection timestamp to remediation completion timestamp; include asset criticality and baseline version in grouping.
data_signals: approved baseline identifier and version
asset inventory records with asset IDs and criticality
configuration scan results showing baseline match/mismatch (timestamped)
configuration deviation events with detection timestamp and remediation timestamp
configuration change request IDs and approval logs
configuration repository / version-controlled config file hashes
audit report timestamps and findings
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated configuration scan logs mapped to baseline version (strong)
Version-controlled configuration files and change approvals showing remediation actions (strong)
Periodic manual checklists or one-off screenshots without logs (weak)
Inconsistent asset inventory or missing baseline identifiers (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes enforcing standardized configurations and adherence to security baselines (preventive aim to reduce misconfiguration).

CM-6.differentiation_note: This core control focuses on overall baseline adherence and metrics across all systems, whereas siblings concentrate on automation, detection, response, or conformance demonstration specifics.

CM-6(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems with automated baseline verification enabled, the frequency of automated compliance checks per system, and the count and remediation time of deviations flagged by automated tools.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated management and verification emphasize coverage and compliance rates (percent of systems under automation); counts of failed checks and trend show operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Report percent of systems enrolled in the centralized configuration management database (CMDB) with automated compliance checks active, average frequency of automated checks per system per day/week, counts of automated compliance check failures by baseline rule ID, and time from automated alert to remediation ticket creation and closure.
data_signals: CMDB records indicating automated check status per asset
automated compliance check execution logs with timestamps and rule IDs
compliance check pass/fail results per asset per baseline rule
integration logs with incident response / ticketing system (ticket ID, create/close timestamps)
baseline version applied and 'least functionality' baseline identifiers
frequency of configuration changes detected by automated system
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated compliance-check execution logs tied to CMDB asset IDs and baseline version (strong)
Automated failure -> ticket creation with timestamps showing remediation workflow (strong)
Manual spreadsheets noting some automated checks but lacking execution timestamps (weak)
Automated checks that do not link to a central baseline version (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes automated management and verification to maintain secure configurations (preventing drift and misconfiguration).

CM-6(1).differentiation_note: Unlike CM-6 (general baseline adherence), CM-6(1) emphasizes automation coverage, execution frequency of automated checks, and integration with response workflows as primary measurement needs.

CM-6(2).measurement_goal: Measure the count of detected unauthorized configuration changes, the detection latency (time from change to detection), and the rate of unauthorized changes remediated within the organization's policy-defined timeframe.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires measuring response effectiveness (remediation and closure rates and time-based performance), so rate-based metrics (remediation within policy windows) are primary, supported by counts and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count unauthorized change events detected, compute median/95th percentile detection latency (detection_timestamp - actual change_timestamp or change log timestamp), and calculate percent of unauthorized-change incidents remediated within policy timeframe (remediation_timestamp - detection_timestamp). Include change request IDs, approval status, and incident ticket linkage for each event.
data_signals: change event logs with change timestamps and actor identity
unauthorized change alert records with detection_timestamp
SIEM correlation alerts referencing configuration change events
file integrity monitoring (FIM) alert records
change request records and approval logs (CR IDs)
incident/ticket records linking detection to remediation with open/close timestamps
training/acknowledgement logs for personnel (for audit of process adherence)
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time alert records with linked change logs, incident ticket creation, and remediation closure (strong)
SIEM-integrated alerts that include actor identity and change context (strong)
Ad-hoc manual discovery of changes without logs or lacking timestamps (weak)
Detection reports without linked remediation tickets (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on identifying and addressing unauthorized changes promptly (corrective activities to restore secure state).

CM-6(2).differentiation_note: This control is distinct in its emphasis on response and remediation metrics (detection latency and remediation rates) rather than general compliance coverage or automated verification.

CM-6(3).measurement_goal: Count and classify unauthorized change detection events, measure detection latency per event, and track false positive/true positive rates to demonstrate the effectiveness of detection mechanisms.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control, the primary need is to quantify detection events (counts) and detection rates; rates and trends then measure effectiveness and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Produce counts of unauthorized-change detection alerts (grouped by asset and rule), detection latency (time between actual change and detection alert), and detection quality metrics (false positive and true positive counts). Include mapping to baseline ID and monitoring tool that generated the alert.
data_signals: file integrity monitoring alerts with file/path and hash changes
configuration change detection events with asset ID and timestamp
SIEM alert records with correlation rule ID
detection tool coverage list (which assets/rules are monitored)
investigation outcomes indicating whether an alert was unauthorized vs authorized (triage results)
alert severity and type
average time to acknowledge detection alerts
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous FIM/SIEM detection logs with linked investigation outcomes (strong)
Asset coverage report showing which hosts are monitored (strong)
Periodic manual spot checks without detection logs (weak)
High volume of untriaged alerts lacking disposition (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly states detection and alerting of unauthorized changes (detective purpose).

CM-6(3).differentiation_note: This sibling is narrowly focused on detection and alert metrics (counts, detection latency, false positive rates) rather than remediation or automation coverage.

CM-6(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems that conform to the approved security baseline, track the number of non-compliance findings from audits, and report remediation completion rates for non-conformance issues to demonstrate conformance over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Conformance is best expressed as percent of systems conforming to baselines; counts of non-compliance findings and trends show audit results and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Report percent conforming = (number of assets with configuration matching approved baseline / total scoped assets) by baseline and by audit cycle, list of non-compliance findings with severity and timestamps, and remediation completion timestamps and evidence (e.g., updated config hash and approval log) for each finding.
data_signals: audit reports with finding IDs, severity, and timestamps
configuration scan results mapped to baseline ID and version
percentage of systems conforming per baseline and per asset type
non-compliance remediation tickets with create/close timestamps
configuration change documentation and justification logs
automated enforcement tool evidence (policy application logs)
version-controlled configuration artifacts
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated scan results and audit reports aligned to baseline version with remediation evidence (strong)
Complete audit trails showing justification and approved changes (strong)
Single-point-in-time screenshots or unlinked manual reports (weak)
Audit findings without remediation evidence or timestamps (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires demonstrating alignment to baselines and enforcing conformance (preventive assurance of secure configurations).

CM-6(4).differentiation_note: This control emphasizes audit evidence and reporting of conformance and non-compliance remediation, distinguishing it from automated verification (CM-6(1)) and detection/response controls (CM-6(2)/(3)).

CM-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems conforming to the organization’s least-functionality baseline (services/ports/protocols disabled where non-essential), plus counts of disabled unnecessary services and counts of unauthorized access attempts attributable to non-baseline functionality.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: CM-7 is a preventive control that verifies coverage/compliance with a baseline; percentage metrics (coverage/compliance %) are most useful, while counts (disabled services, unauthorized attempts) and trends show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of hosts/systems with configurations matching the documented least-functionality baseline; count of non-baseline services present per system; number of configuration reviews completed per period; count of unauthorized access attempts correlated to non-baseline features.
data_signals: system baseline configuration manifest (services, ports, protocols) per host
inventory of enabled services/processes per system with timestamps
disabled/unnecessary service count per asset
configuration review audit records and timestamps
blocked or logged unauthorized access attempts referencing disabled features
application whitelisting / policy enforcement logs
change/configuration management tickets referencing baseline deviations
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated configuration drift scans and continuous enforcement logs are strong evidence.
Periodic manual checklists or one-off screenshots are weak evidence compared to automated, timestamped manifests.
Correlation of unauthorized access attempts to specific non-baseline services strengthens evidence; absence of correlation weakens it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on limiting capabilities, maintaining baselines, and disabling non-essential services (preventive measures).

CM-7.differentiation_note: CM-7 is the umbrella baseline enforcement control — it focuses on overall baseline compliance and aggregated coverage metrics versus the more specific execution, exception, or hardware-focused subcontrols.

CM-7(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems for which a documented periodic least-functionality review was completed on schedule and the count of non-compliant functions discovered per review cycle, plus median time-to-remediate discovered deviations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Periodic review is preventive/assurance-focused: percentage of systems reviewed on schedule shows coverage; counts and trends show deviations and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems with a completed least-functionality review within the defined review period; number of unauthorized services/ports identified per review; time between detection in review and remediation closure (MTTR for review findings).
data_signals: periodic review schedule and completion records (review timestamps)
list of detected non-essential services/ports from each review
remediation ticket timestamps and resolution status for review findings
approved/disallowed functions list maintained per review cycle
audit reports summarizing review findings
evidence_strength_indicators: Formal review records with timestamps and linked remediation tickets are strong evidence.
Ad-hoc notes or undocumented verbal confirmations are weak evidence.
Consistent reduction in non-compliant findings across review cycles indicates effectiveness.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates regular reviews and validation of configurations to prevent non-essential functions from remaining enabled (preventive assurance).

CM-7(1).differentiation_note: CM-7(1) focuses specifically on scheduled review activities and their completion/compliance metrics rather than continuous enforcement or execution blocking.

CM-7(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of endpoints and servers enforcing application control (whitelist) and the count/rate of unauthorized program execution attempts blocked per time period, plus time-to-add-approved-apps to whitelist.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control requiring coverage (whitelisting in place), percentage coverage of enforcement is primary; counts/rates of blocked attempts prove operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of managed endpoints/servers with application control (whitelist) enforced; number of unauthorized execution attempts blocked per endpoint and aggregated; time from an exception request to whitelist inclusion.
data_signals: application whitelisting enforcement status per endpoint
blocked execution attempt logs with process/hash and timestamp
software inventory and approved application list
exception/approval tickets and timestamps for newly authorized apps
EDR/AV telemetry showing blocked/terminated processes
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time enforcement logs (blocked execution events with process hash and host) are strong evidence.
Periodic manual inventories without enforcement logs are weak evidence.
Low false-positive block rates and short exception turnaround times strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control emphasises preventing execution of unauthorized software via whitelisting and enforcement technologies (preventive enforcement).

CM-7(2).differentiation_note: CM-7(2) is execution-focused: it measures enforcement coverage and blocked execution events rather than baseline configuration reviews or exception processes.

CM-7(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems registered against the approved baseline (registration compliance) and the percentage of personnel/roles operating within least-functionality permissions, plus counts of unauthorized functionalities detected during audits.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Registration/compliance is a preventive coverage assurance; percentage of registered systems and percent adhering to baseline are primary, with counts of deviations and trend analysis as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of systems with an active registration/baseline record and percent of systems currently compliant with their registered baseline; count of unauthorized functionalities found per audit and remediation age for those findings.
data_signals: system registration/asset records (registration timestamp, owner, baseline ID)
baseline adherence scan results per system
audit/compliance findings and severity
access control/permission assignments and role mapping
change requests tied to baseline updates
evidence_strength_indicators: Linked registration records with automated adherence scans and audit logs are strong evidence.
Unlinked spreadsheets or ad-hoc lists without scan correlation are weak.
Consistent reduction in audit findings over review cycles strengthens confidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on maintaining registries/baselines and restricting capabilities before systems operate (preventive governance).

CM-7(3).differentiation_note: CM-7(3) emphasizes registration and documented baselines (asset-to-baseline mapping) and compliance metrics rather than runtime blocking or exception models.

CM-7(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of endpoints enforcing deny-by-exception policies (only explicitly allowed software runs) and the count/rate of unauthorized installation/execution attempts prevented, plus incident counts tied to unauthorized software.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Deny-by-exception requires enforcement coverage metrics (percentage) as primary; counts/rates of denied attempts demonstrate operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of managed endpoints where deny-by-exception application control is active; number of unauthorized installation/execution attempts blocked per period; incidents of malware attributable to unauthorized installs.
data_signals: application control policy enforcement status per device
blocked installation/execution logs with process/hash and host
malware incidents correlated to unauthorized software
approved application inventory and versions
software audit records
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous enforcement logs with blocked event details and linked incident records are strong evidence.
Relying solely on periodic inventories without enforcement event logs is weak.
Low numbers of successful unauthorized installs and quick remediation times indicate strong control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control enforces a deny-by-exception policy to prevent unauthorized software from executing (preventive enforcement).

CM-7(4).differentiation_note: CM-7(4) is specifically enforcement-first (deny-by-default) and requires continuous blocking logs, unlike exception/allow models which measure approval processes.

CM-7(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of endpoints governed by an allow-by-exception policy and the rate and age of exception approvals (requests to install/run software), plus time-to-remediate unauthorized apps found during audits.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Allow-by-exception is preventive but process-driven: percentage coverage of controlled endpoints is primary; counts of exception requests and trend of approvals/denials are useful secondaries.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets under allow-by-exception controls; count of software exception requests and average approval time; number of unauthorized installations detected and time-to-remediate.
data_signals: exception/approval workflow records (request, reason, approver, timestamps)
approved software list and versions
installation logs showing authorized vs unauthorized installs
audit findings of unauthorized applications
frequency of software audits
evidence_strength_indicators: Linked exception workflow tickets with installation logs and short approval turnaround are strong evidence.
Informal approvals (email without tracking) are weak evidence.
A reducing trend in unauthorized installs and faster approval times indicates maturity.

control_type_classification.reasoning: This control limits installs via an approval process (preventive via governance and controlled exceptions).

CM-7(5).differentiation_note: CM-7(5) focuses on the exception approval process and associated metrics (request counts and approval times) rather than strict runtime blocking.

CM-7(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of protected/contained environments (containers, sandboxes, restricted VMs) enforcing least-privilege settings and the count of unauthorized configuration changes or privilege escalations within those environments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Confined environments are preventive and access-focused: percent of environments configured with limited privileges is primary; counts of unauthorized changes and privilege escalations indicate failures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of confined environments with enforced limited-privilege configurations (e.g., RBAC/policies applied); number of unauthorized configuration changes or privilege escalations detected; time-to-revert/mitigate unauthorized changes.
data_signals: inventory of confined/protected environments with applied privilege policies
RBAC/permission assignments and recent changes
configuration change logs in confined environments
alerts for privilege escalation attempts
audit findings for confined environment settings
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated policy enforcement logs and alerts for privilege escalation are strong evidence.
Manual spot-checks without change-log correlation are weak evidence.
Low frequency of privilege escalations and rapid rollback actions indicate strong control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires restricting privileges and capabilities in confined environments to prevent exploitation (preventive access control).

CM-7(6).differentiation_note: CM-7(6) is focused specifically on confined/protected environment configurations and privilege restrictions rather than broad application whitelisting or hardware controls.

CM-7(7).measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of unauthorized code execution attempts within protected environments and the percentage of allowed code/processes enforced by policy (whitelist) inside those environments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This subcontrol is detective/preventive for execution events: counts of unauthorized execution attempts are primary to detect events; percentage of allowed code enforcement shows coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of unauthorized or blocked code-execution attempts in protected environments per period; percentage of processes in protected environments that match approved/allowed code lists; mean time to respond to blocked execution incidents.
data_signals: protected environment process execution logs with process hash and host
blocked/terminated execution events and reason codes
approved application/process list for protected environments
incident/response tickets for unauthorized execution
policy enforcement status in protected environments
evidence_strength_indicators: Detailed execution logs with process hashes and enforcement outcomes are strong evidence.
Absence of logging or reliance on infrequent manual checks is weak evidence.
Lower counts of unauthorized execution attempts and rapid incident response improve evidence strength.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on tracking and blocking unauthorized code execution events (detective with enforcement); it emphasizes monitoring attempts and logging events.

CM-7(7).differentiation_note: CM-7(7) is event-detection-focused inside protected environments (counts of execution attempts) versus broader endpoint/application policy coverage.

CM-7(8).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of hosts enforcing allowed-binary policies (only approved binaries may execute) and the count of unauthorized binary execution attempts and discovered unapproved executables during audits.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventing unauthorized binaries is coverage-driven: percent of hosts enforcing allowed-binary lists is primary; counts of blocked executions and distribution of block events across hosts are useful secondaries.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of managed hosts with allowed-binary enforcement enabled; count of blocked or flagged binary/machine-executable runs per host; inventory of unapproved binaries discovered in scans and their distribution by host/OS.
data_signals: allowed-binary/whitelist records and enforcement status per host
execution logs showing binary hash, path, and block/allow decision
software inventory scans identifying unknown/unapproved binaries
audit records of executable installations
time-to-remediation for unapproved binaries
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated execution-policy logs with binary hashes and enforcement outcomes are strong evidence.
Incomplete inventories or occasional manual scans are weak evidence.
Low counts of successful unapproved binary executions and rapid remediation indicate strong control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates allowing only essential binaries and using whitelisting/enforcement technologies (preventive execution control).

CM-7(8).differentiation_note: CM-7(8) is specifically focused on binaries/executables (hash/path-level enforcement) rather than broader application or configuration baselines.

CM-7(9).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of environments/assets reconciled against an authorized hardware inventory, the count of unauthorized devices detected on the network, and mean time-to-isolate/remove unauthorized hardware.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Hardware authorization is coverage-driven (percent of assets reconciled) while counts and rates of detected unauthorized devices and remediation times show operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of environments and asset records reconciled with an authorized hardware inventory within the reporting period; number of unauthorized hardware devices discovered on the network or endpoints; time from detection to removal/isolation of unauthorized hardware.
data_signals: authorized hardware inventory records (model, serial, owner) with reconciliation timestamps
network discovery scans and device MAC addresses not matched in inventory
endpoint management logs showing connected peripherals/devices
incident tickets for unauthorized hardware detection and remediation timestamps
audit logs from regular hardware audits
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated discovery scans linked to inventory reconciliation and remediation tickets are strong evidence.
Relying solely on periodic manual audits without network/device logs is weak evidence.
