Fast detection-to-removal times and low counts of unauthorized devices indicate strong control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prevents risks by prohibiting unauthorized hardware via inventory and audits (preventive asset control).

CM-7(9).differentiation_note: CM-7(9) uniquely targets physical/hardware asset controls and device discovery/reconciliation metrics rather than software/configuration or execution controls.

CM-8.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of the system component inventory: percentage of all hardware and software assets recorded in the centralized inventory, average time between asset appearance and inventory entry (update latency), and count of inventory discrepancies found per audit period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires demonstrating coverage and completeness (e.g., percent of assets inventoried) and currency (update latency). Percentage expresses coverage/completeness, while counts and trends show raw discrepancy volumes and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percent of assets present in the central inventory vs. canonical asset inventory (or discovered assets), median/mean inventory update latency (time from asset detection to inventory record creation or last_seen), and number of discrepancies identified during each audit cycle (records with mismatched attributes).
data_signals: central inventory records (asset id, asset type, last_seen timestamp)
asset discovery scan execution timestamps and results
inventory update event logs (create/update/delete timestamps)
asset attributes: owner, location, status, criticality
audit discrepancy report entries (record id, audit timestamp, discrepancy type)
asset onboarding/decommissioning events
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated discovery logs and timestamped inventory update events correlated to discovery events (strong)
Regular reconciliation reports from automated scans with low discrepancy counts (strong)
Ad‑hoc spreadsheets or single point-in-time manual inventories without discovery logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing and maintaining an up-to-date inventory to prevent unmanaged assets ('establish and maintain a comprehensive inventory', 'updated in real-time'), which is primarily preventive.

CM-8.differentiation_note: This is the overarching inventory control focused on global coverage and currency; sibling controls add event-driven updates, automation, detection, and metadata (ownership/configuration) that require more specific signals.

CM-8(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of installation and removal events that result in a corresponding inventory update within the organization-defined timeframe and count of missed or delayed updates per change window.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control mandates updating the inventory during installation/removal events (coverage/compliance), so percentage of events updated on time is most direct; counts and trends help show raw volumes and improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of change tickets or installation/removal events that have an associated inventory create/update event within the defined timeframe (e.g., within X hours of installation/removal), and list of change events without matching inventory updates.
data_signals: change ticket IDs and timestamps (installation/removal event timestamp)
inventory update timestamps linked to change ticket IDs
asset onboarding/decommissioning events
CMDB synchronization logs
role/owner fields for changed assets
audit logs showing missed updates
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated change-to-inventory linkage (ticket -> inventory update event) with timestamps showing updates within policy window (strong)
Manual inventory edits without traceable change tickets or long update latency (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires a process that prevents untracked installations/removals by ensuring inventory updates during change events ('establish a systematic process for updating the inventory during the installation and removal').

CM-8(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on change-event driven inventory updates and linkage to change management records; measurement must correlate change tickets/events to inventory updates (not just overall coverage).

CM-8(2).measurement_goal: Measure automated discovery coverage and accuracy: percent of assets discovered automatically and recorded in the inventory, average time from discovery to inventory entry (update latency), and count of discovered-but-unrecorded assets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated maintenance emphasizes completeness/coverage (percent of assets properly inventoried by automated tools) so percentage is primary; counts/trends show volume and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of inventory entries created/updated by automated discovery per period, median discovery-to-inventory latency, and number of discovery results that fail to reconcile with the CMDB.
data_signals: asset discovery scan logs (scan id, timestamp, discovered asset identifiers)
inventory update events created by automated processes
last_seen/heartbeat timestamps per asset
CMDB integration success/failure logs
unreconciled discovery results list
scan schedule and frequency settings
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated discovery feeds with low unreconciled count and short discovery-to-update latency (strong)
Intermittent manual discovery or divergent manual records without machine-readable logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates automated continuous discovery and maintenance ('deploy automated asset discovery tools that continuously scan the network'), aimed at preventing gaps in the inventory.

CM-8(2).differentiation_note: Emphasizes automated, continuous scanning and reconciliation with CMDBs; measurement must focus on automation origin of inventory events and reconciliation failure rates.

CM-8(3).measurement_goal: Measure detection and remediation of unauthorized components: count of unauthorized components detected per period, percentage of detected unauthorized components isolated/removed within the required timeframe, and mean time to isolate/remove after detection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control that produces events (unauthorized component detections), so counts of detections are primary; rates (detection rate, remediation rate) and trends show effectiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of unauthorized component alerts (by detection source) per week, percent of those alerts that were validated and isolated/removed within the defined response timeframe, and time from alert to isolation/removal.
data_signals: unauthorized component detection alerts (alert id, detection timestamp, source)
approved/whitelist baseline inventory (approved component list)
detection-to-remediation timestamps (isolation/removal events)
weekly scan schedule and results
false positive/true positive classification for alerts
incident/response ticket IDs linked to detections
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated detection alerts tied to the approved baseline with timestamps and corresponding isolation/removal actions (strong)
Sporadic manual findings without automated alert history or no timestamps linking detection to remediation (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires automated detection and alerts for unauthorized components ('deploy automated scanning tools that conduct regular (at least weekly) assessments' and 'alerts are generated for any discrepancies'), which is detective in nature.

CM-8(3).differentiation_note: Specifically targets detection of unauthorized/rogue components and ties detection events to response actions; requires measurement of detection volumes and remediation timeliness rather than overall coverage.

CM-8(4).measurement_goal: Measure asset accountability: percent of inventory records that include a documented owner and contact, frequency of ownership reviews, and count of assets with missing or stale accountability information.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Accountability is expressed as coverage/completeness (percentage of assets with documented owners); counts and trends show outstanding gaps and improvements over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of inventory entries containing a non-empty owner field and last ownership verification timestamp; number of assets flagged in an ownership audit as lacking documented accountability, and time since last ownership review per asset.
data_signals: inventory records with owner and owner_contact fields
ownership assignment timestamps
ownership review logs and review cycle timestamps
audit reports showing assets without assigned owners
asset lifecycle events linked to owner changes
evidence_strength_indicators: CMDB entries with stable owner id, signed owner acceptance records, and periodic review logs (strong)
Unverified owner fields or free-text owner fields without verification (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring assignment and documentation of asset owners ('assigning ownership for each asset to ensure accountability') is preventive because it reduces the chance assets remain unmanaged.

CM-8(4).differentiation_note: Focuses on metadata (ownership/accountability) for assets; measurement must verify presence and recency of owner attribution, not just asset existence.

CM-8(5).measurement_goal: Measure duplicate accounting: count and percentage of inventory records flagged as duplicates, percent of duplicates resolved within the target timeframe, and percentage of assets with unique identifiers (serial number/asset tag).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Prevention of duplicates is best expressed as percentage coverage of unique identifiers (percent of assets with unique IDs) and percent duplicates; counts show raw duplicate volumes and trends show reconciliation effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of inventory records that contain a unique identifier (serial number/asset tag) and deduplicated unique asset count vs. total records; count of duplicate record groups identified and average time to reconcile/resolve duplicates.
data_signals: asset unique identifier fields (serial number, asset_tag)
automated duplicate detection logs (potential duplicate pairings)
duplicate resolution ticket IDs and timestamps
physical inventory reconciliation reports
frequency of inventory audits that include duplicate checks
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated duplicate detection with reconciled resolution tickets and cleared duplicate flags (strong)
Persistent duplicate records with no reconciliation evidence or manual inconsistent merges (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly prevents duplication by requiring unique identifiers and processes to avoid duplicate entries ('unique identifiers for each system component', 'prevent the duplication of entries').

CM-8(5).differentiation_note: Uniquely mandates prevention and reconciliation of duplicate records; measurements must focus on unique identifier presence and duplicate resolution SLAs (zero tolerance target).

CM-8(6).measurement_goal: Measure configuration compliance and approved deviations: percent of components compliant with established security baselines, number of approved deviations (with justification) per period, and mean time to approve deviations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires demonstrating conformity to baselines (percent compliant is primary); counts of approved deviations and trends show deviation workload and risk acceptance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of inventory components whose current configuration matches the defined baseline, count of components with approved deviations (with linked approval records), and time from deviation request to formal approval/authorization.
data_signals: configuration assessment scan results (component id, baseline id, compliance flag)
approved deviation records (deviation id, justification, approval timestamp)
configuration item hashes or config templates
change management tickets for deviation approvals
frequency of configuration assessments
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated continuous configuration assessments with linked approval records and documented justifications (strong)
Manual one-off deviation notes without approvals or stale baselines (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires assessing configurations against baselines and formally approving deviations ('assessed configurations against established security baselines' and 'formal process for reviewing and approving deviations'), which is preventive of misconfiguration risk.

CM-8(6).differentiation_note: Focuses on configuration compliance and governing deviations from baselines; measurement must link configuration scan results with formal deviation approval artifacts.

CM-8(7).measurement_goal: Measure central repository adoption and completeness: percent of discovered assets present in the centralized repository, frequency of repository updates, and number of integrations syncing inventory data successfully.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Centralization is demonstrated by percent coverage of the single repository (percentage); counts of syncs and integration failures explain operational reliability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets that are present in the centralized repository vs. authoritative discovery results, number of successful syncs from integrated systems per period and count of integration failures, and time to onboard new asset classes into the repository.
data_signals: central repository entries (asset id, last_updated timestamp)
asset discovery results vs. central repo reconciliation reports
integration/sync logs (success/failure, timestamps)
attribute completeness metrics (owner, location, type)
access control logs for the repository (who updated what)
evidence_strength_indicators: Single-source-of-truth repository with automated synchronization logs and low reconciliation delta (strong)
Multiple isolated inventories and manual consolidation reports (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Establishing a centralized authoritative inventory ('centralized repository') prevents fragmentation and supports other preventive processes, so classification is preventive.

CM-8(7).differentiation_note: Emphasizes single authoritative repository and integration/sync health; measurement must validate integration success and centralization rather than mere asset counts.

CM-8(8).measurement_goal: Measure asset location tracking coverage and accuracy: percent of assets with automated location telemetry or tags, time to detect a location change, and count of location discrepancies found during audits.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Location tracking is about coverage (percent of assets with tracked location) and accuracy; percentage shows coverage while counts and trends indicate discrepancies and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of inventory records containing an automated location attribute (network location, GPS/BLE tag, rack/room), median time from actual location change to inventory update (detection latency), and count of assets with location mismatch between telemetry and recorded location.
data_signals: asset location attribute in inventory (last_known_network_segment, gps_coordinates, physical_location)
tagging logs (asset_tag scans, RFID/BLE/GPS telemetry)
last_seen network/location timestamps
location discrepancy audit reports
time-series of location change events and corresponding inventory updates
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated telemetry (network location, GPS/BLE, tag scans) correlated with inventory updates and low discrepancy counts (strong)
Manual location spreadsheets or infrequent physical audits without telemetry (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring automated location tracking and tagging ('deploy automated asset discovery tools' and 'devices are tagged with unique identifiers for easy tracking') prevents untracked/unauthorized devices and is therefore preventive.

CM-8(8).differentiation_note: Specifically targets automated location telemetry and tag-based tracking; requires time-to-detect/location-accuracy signals rather than just presence in inventory.

CM-8(9).measurement_goal: Measure mapping completeness and accuracy: percentage of components assigned to a parent system, number of unassigned components, and reconciliation time for incorrect assignments discovered during audits.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Assignment completeness is best expressed as percentage of components mapped to systems; counts and trends surface unassigned components and mapping correction activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of inventory component records containing a system_assignment field (system id) and percent of components correctly mapped after audit reconciliation; list/count of unassigned components and average time to assign them to a system.
data_signals: component-to-system mapping records (component id -> system id)
mapping audit logs (discovered mismappings, timestamps)
unassigned component count
mapping correction ticket timestamps
system owner and function attributes
evidence_strength_indicators: Authoritative CMDB mappings with reconciliation logs and resolved mapping tickets (strong)
Loose free-text system fields without reconciliation evidence (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mapping components to systems ('ensure that each component is accurately assigned to its respective system') prevents gaps in visibility and supports downstream preventive controls, so it is preventive.

CM-8(9).differentiation_note: Requires verifying component-to-system relationships and measurement of mapping accuracy; unlike general inventory coverage, this control requires relational integrity checks between components and systems.

CM-9.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage of approved baseline configurations in the CMDB (percentage of assets with an approved baseline), the count and rate of detected unauthorized configuration changes, the average time to approve change requests, and the frequency and coverage of configuration audits to demonstrate the CMP is operating.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and documented/approved configurations — percentage metrics (coverage/compliance) best show whether configurations are documented and approved; counts and rates quantify unauthorized changes and approval activity and trends show ongoing effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of hardware and software assets recorded in the CMDB with an approved baseline_config_version and baseline_approval_timestamp; weekly count of detected unauthorized configuration changes correlated to asset IDs; mean time from change_request.submission_timestamp to change_request.approval_timestamp; schedule and execution timestamps for configuration audits and audit nonconformance counts.
data_signals: CMDB records: asset_id, baseline_config_version, baseline_approval_timestamp, last_configuration_update
change request records: request_id, submission_timestamp, approval_timestamp, approver_id, change_status
configuration change detection logs/alerts: event_id, timestamp, changed_item_id, change_author, change_diff
version control records for configuration artifacts: commit_id, tag/version, timestamp, author
configuration audit reports: audit_timestamp, audited_item_ids, nonconformance_count, findings
asset inventory with asset_id, asset_type, owner_id, criticality_tier, management_scope
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated change-detection logs correlated to CMDB baseline records and signed approval records (timestamped approver_id) showing end-to-end traceability.
Strong: high CMDB completeness (low untracked asset count) and version-controlled baselines.
Weak: periodic manual spreadsheets or one-off audit snapshots without linked change approvals or automated change logs.
Weak: missing approver IDs, undocumented baseline versions, or inability to link detected changes to change requests.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes a plan and baselines to prevent unauthorized or uncontrolled changes ('establishes a systematic approach to managing and controlling changes').

CM-9.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on the CMP artifact, CMDB coverage, baseline versioning, and automated change monitoring (technical and system-level coverage) rather than assigning who performs those tasks.

CM-9(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of configuration items and systems with an assigned, documented owner and role responsible for baseline maintenance and the completion rate of assigned configuration tasks (e.g., reviews, remediations) over time; measure time-to-remediate configuration issues by assigned personnel.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Assigning responsibility is preventive — percentage coverage of assigned owners and training completion shows accountability; counts and trends track assigned-task throughput and remediation activity; scores can capture role competency or assignment completeness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of configuration items (by asset_id) with owner_id and role_assignment_date in the CMDB or responsibility matrix; count of overdue assigned configuration-review tasks and count of remediation tickets linked to assigned owners; completion timestamps for required training or role on-boarding for assigned personnel.
data_signals: configuration item inventory: item_id, owner_id, role_assignment_date, baseline_version
responsibility matrix / role documents: role_id, responsibilities_list, effective_date
audit logs of responsibility reviews: review_id, review_date, reviewer_id, findings, items_checked
ticketing records linking configuration issues to owner_id: ticket_id, created_timestamp, assigned_owner_id, remediation_timestamp, status
training records: personnel_id, course_id, completion_timestamp, certification_status
periodic compliance/assignment reports: assignment_coverage_percentage, overdue_task_count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: timestamped owner assignments in the CMDB linked to ticketing records and periodic review logs showing owners executed assigned tasks.
Strong: training completion records tied to assigned personnel and updated responsibility matrices.
Weak: informal email notes assigning responsibility, or unlinked spreadsheets without timestamps or CMDB linkage.
Weak: lack of periodic review evidence or inability to connect remediation tickets to a documented owner.

affinity_keywords: compliance with the configuration management plan

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires defining and assigning roles and responsibilities to prevent configuration drift and unauthorized changes ('defining and assigning roles and responsibilities related to configuration management').

CM-9(1).differentiation_note: This control uniquely targets human accountability and role coverage (owner assignments, training, and reviews) rather than technical configuration coverage or automated change detection.

CM-10.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of approved-software policies: percentage of endpoints with application whitelisting/configuration enforcement enabled, count of unauthorized software installation attempts detected and blocked per period, and rate of software inventory coverage (assets inventoried vs. total).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (enforce only-approved software). Percentage metrics (coverage/compliance %) directly show enforcement coverage; counts and rates capture unauthorized install attempts and blocking events; trending demonstrates improvement or degradation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute (a) percentage of managed endpoints with application whitelisting or equivalent enforcement enabled, (b) weekly count of blocked or flagged unauthorized software installation attempts with endpoint and user context, and (c) software inventory coverage ratio (number of assets in canonical inventory / total managed assets) plus time since last inventory update. Also capture policy acknowledgement receipts and frequency of compliance audits.
data_signals: endpoint configuration records indicating application whitelisting enabled (per device, date)
blocked installation alerts / prevention logs (process, executable, user, timestamp, endpoint ID)
software inventory entries with asset ID, installed application name/version, last scanned timestamp
unauthorized software detection count per week (EDR/XDR/MDM)
policy acknowledgement logs for employees (user, timestamp)
compliance audit records and findings (audit date, scope, findings)
remediation/ticket records for unauthorized installs (ticket ID, opened/closed timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated endpoint telemetry showing whitelisting enabled plus continuous prevention/block logs and a canonical software inventory with recent scan timestamps
Weak: annual manual inventory spreadsheets, screenshots of settings, or single point-in-time audit reports without prevention/block logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes preventing unauthorized installations using application whitelisting and enforcement of policies (phrases: 'prevent unauthorized or inappropriate software installations', 'application whitelisting', 'allow only approved software to run').

CM-10.differentiation_note: This control covers organization-wide prevention and enforcement (whitelisting, inventory coverage, blocked-install counts) for all software, unlike CM-10(1) which focuses specifically on vetting and managing open-source components and their vulnerability lifecycle.

CM-10(1).measurement_goal: Measure the vetting, approval and ongoing vulnerability management of OSS: percentage of projects/components in use that are approved and inventoried, count of OSS vulnerability disclosures affecting approved components, and mean time to remediate OSS vulnerabilities detected by SCA tools.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control focused on ensuring only vetted OSS is used, percentage metrics (approved vs used, inventory coverage) show policy adherence; counts and trends capture vulnerability disclosures and remediation volume and velocity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure (a) percentage of deployed codebases/components with an entry in the approved OSS inventory, (b) count of new vulnerability disclosures for approved OSS per month and associated CVE identifiers, and (c) mean and median time-to-remediate OSS vulnerabilities from discovery to fix/mitigation. Include SCA scan frequency and results per project and approval/denial decision records for OSS requests.
data_signals: approved OSS inventory records (component name, version, approver, approval date)
SCA scan results and timestamps (component, CVE ID, severity/CVSS, project ID)
vulnerability disclosure counts for approved OSS (CVE list, disclosure date)
OSS approval request records with outcome (approved/denied), reviewer, and timestamp
remediation ticket timestamps for OSS vulnerabilities (opened, fixed, closed)
licensing assessment records (license type, approval status)
integration points in SDLC (pull request or build scan logs showing SCA results)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated SCA scan logs integrated into CI/CD with timestamped findings, an authoritative approved-OSS inventory tied to deployed artifacts, and remediation tickets showing fix timestamps
Weak: manual approvals or spreadsheets without linkage to builds, ad-hoc developer attestations, or infrequent SCA scans

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes establishing policies, vetting, and approving OSS and integrating SCA into processes (phrases: 'establish and enforce restrictions', 'vetted and approved OSS', 'Software Composition Analysis (SCA)').

CM-10(1).differentiation_note: This control specifically targets open-source component governance, SCA integration, licensing vetting, and remediation timelines — measurement must include SCA scan results, CVE tracking, and OSS approval decision records rather than general endpoint whitelisting and block logs.

CM-11.measurement_goal: Measure adherence to the approved-software policy by tracking whitelist coverage across endpoints, count of unauthorized installation events, percentage of software requests processed through the approval workflow, and average time to detect/respond to unauthorized installs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control emphasizes policy and coverage (approved software list, inventory, and percentage of requests processed), so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rates) are primary; counts and trends (unauthorized installs, response time) are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure the percent of endpoints with only approved software installed (whitelist compliance), count of unauthorized installation events by endpoint and user per reporting period, number and timestamps of software approval requests processed vs. submitted, and mean time from detection to remediation for unauthorized installs.
data_signals: inventory of approved software (whitelist) records
endpoint software inventory snapshots (installed application name, version, hash, endpoint ID, timestamp)
installation event logs (process name, user, endpoint, timestamp, installer context)
number of software approval requests and approval/denial timestamps
unauthorized installation detection counts
time-to-detect / time-to-remediate timestamps for detected unauthorized installs
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated endpoint inventory and installation event logs correlated to the official whitelist are strong evidence.
Counts of unauthorized installs with full event metadata (user, endpoint, binary hash, timestamp) are strong; occasional manual audit reports or one-off screenshots are weak evidence.
Tracking of approval-request lifecycle (request -> approval/denial timestamps) demonstrates process enforcement; absence of request logs weakens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing policy, whitelists, approval workflows and preventing unauthorized installations, which are preventive controls.

CM-11.differentiation_note: This baseline control focuses on policy, inventory and overall compliance coverage (whitelist/approval workflows) rather than real-time alerting or privileged-install rules, so measurement centers on coverage and process metrics.

CM-11(1).measurement_goal: Measure real-time detection effectiveness by counting alerts for unauthorized installations, percent of endpoints generating alerts vs. total endpoints, and mean time from alert generation to acknowledgement and remediation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control emphasizes generating alerts and real-time monitoring (EDR alerts), so event counts and alert rates are primary to show detection volume; percentages (compliance rate) and trends provide coverage and trajectory context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count unauthorized-installation alerts generated by EDR and application control systems per endpoint and per day, measure the percentage of endpoints with alerting enabled, record timestamps for alert generation, acknowledgement, and remediation to compute time-to-response metrics.
data_signals: EDR/endpoint alert logs for unauthorized installation events (process name, binary hash, user, endpoint ID, timestamp)
configuration records showing application control / group policy status (enabled/disabled) per endpoint
alert acknowledgement and incident ticket timestamps
percentage of systems compliant with approved software list
frequency of unauthorized software detections by endpoint
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time EDR alert logs with full event metadata and linked incident tickets are strong evidence.
Automated, system-generated alerts correlated to the whitelist and blocked executions are stronger than manual reports or periodic scans alone.
High false-positive rates or alerts without remediation/ticketing weaken evidence of effective detection.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Although labelled preventive in the input, the description explicitly prescribes real-time monitoring and generation of alerts (EDR monitoring, alerts for unauthorized software), which are detective activities.

CM-11(1).differentiation_note: This control is specifically about real-time alerting and detection (EDR and application control alerts), so measurements emphasize alert counts, alert coverage, and time-to-respond rather than policy coverage or approval workflows.

CM-11(2).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement of privileged-installation restrictions by tracking the percentage of software installations performed by authorized privileged accounts, count of installation attempts by non-authorized users, and the average approval turnaround time for privileged installation requests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control restricts installation to authorized personnel, so percentage metrics (share of installs by privileged/authorized users, percent of endpoints enforcing privileged-install rules) directly show enforcement; counts and trends for unauthorized attempts are useful secondary signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure percent of software installs executed by authorized privileged accounts versus total installs, count unauthorized installation attempts and their origin (user, endpoint), and record approval/denial timestamps to compute mean time to approve/deny privileged installation requests.
data_signals: installation event logs indicating invoking user account and elevation/privilege context (user ID, privileged flag, endpoint ID, timestamp)
list of accounts authorized to install software (policy/vet list)
software approval request records (requester, approver, request timestamp, decision timestamp)
counts of denied or blocked privileged installation attempts
endpoint management / software restriction policy settings (who can install)
evidence_strength_indicators: Correlated installation events with the authorized-installer list and approval ticket records are strong evidence.
System-enforced blocking of installations by non-authorized accounts (with event logs) is stronger than evidence relying on manual approvals alone.
Absence of identity context on install events (missing user or privilege flag) weakens the evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates restricting installation to authorized personnel and enforcing policies (whitelist, software restriction) which are preventive controls limiting actions prior to occurrence.

CM-11(2).differentiation_note: Unlike the general CM-11 policy or the alerting control, this control specifically targets privileged/installers and approval turnaround — measurement must link install events to identity/privilege context and approval records.

CM-11(3).measurement_goal: Measure automated enforcement coverage (percent of endpoints with application control/whitelisting enabled), count of automatically blocked installation attempts, and mean time from automated detection to remediation for unauthorized software.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control emphasizes deployment of automated tools and enforcement across endpoints; percentage (coverage of enforcement) is the primary indicator, while counts/rates of blocked installations and remediation velocity are important secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure percent of endpoints with automated enforcement (whitelisting/application control) active, count of blocked installation/execution events by enforcement tools, timestamps of detection and automated remediation actions, and compliance rate of automated inventory scans against the approved-software list.
data_signals: configuration inventory showing enforcement/whitelisting enabled per endpoint
blocked-installation event logs from whitelisting/application control solutions (binary hash, process, endpoint, timestamp)
EDR remediation action logs (quarantine, uninstall, rollback) with timestamps
automated inventory scan results correlated to approved-software list
counts of remediation actions initiated automatically
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated enforcement logs showing blocked installs with remediation actions and endpoint enforcement state are strong evidence.
Continuous telemetry demonstrating enforcement enabled across endpoints is stronger than periodic manual scan results.
Evidence lacking automated remediation steps or enforcement configuration per endpoint is weak.

affinity_keywords: identify and remediate unauthorized software in real-time

control_type_classification.reasoning: Although it includes monitoring, the emphasis on automated enforcement and whitelisting to prevent unapproved installs classifies it primarily as preventive.

CM-11(3).differentiation_note: This control is about automated, system-enforced blocking and remediation across endpoints — metrics must prove enforcement coverage and automated remediation actions rather than manual approvals or alert-only detection.

CM-12.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of sensitive and critical data assets that are stored, processed, and transmitted only in policy-approved secure locations and track counts and remediation time for any instances of sensitive data found in unauthorized locations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage/compliance: percentage metrics (coverage of sensitive data in approved locations) best demonstrate extent of compliance; counts and trends show violations and remediation velocity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of sensitive records/assets (by classification level) whose storage location attribute matches an approved secure location list, plus the count of unauthorized storage instances and mean time from detection to remediation for each violation. Also measure frequency of audits and DLP-triggered blocked transfers to unapproved destinations.
data_signals: data inventory records with classification label and storage_location attribute
approved storage locations / policy-approved location list (registry)
DLP alerts showing blocked or allowed transfers with destination and timestamp
cloud storage object metadata including region/residency tags and encryption-at-rest status
audit reports / scan results showing discovered sensitive data in unapproved locations
remediation ticket timestamps (detection -> remediation completion)
frequency and timestamps of data audits/scans
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated inventory + DLP logs correlated to the inventory (strong).
Reconciled storage metadata showing approved location tags for each sensitive asset (strong).
One-off manual audit reports without automated discovery coverage (weak).
Sparse sampling or screenshots of a few files/locations rather than full-scope scan results (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly states 'Control Type: Preventive' and focuses on ensuring data is placed only in approved secure environments (preventing unauthorized storage/transmission).

CM-12.differentiation_note: This control focuses on policy and compliance of where data resides (residency, approved locations, and remediation of violations) rather than the tooling or classification accuracy used to discover that data.

CM-12(1).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and accuracy of automated discovery/classification tools: percent of sensitive data objects discovered and correctly classified, scan frequency coverage across on‑prem/cloud/endpoints, and mean time to respond to data location requests generated by discovery.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control centered on discovery and coverage, percentage metrics (discovery coverage, classification accuracy) are most informative; counts and rates capture scan frequency and request-response times.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Coverage percentage of data assets scanned (by storage type and environment), classification accuracy rate (true positive rate from validation samples), frequency of automated scans per environment, and average time to satisfy data location requests returned by the toolchain.
data_signals: discovery/scan execution logs with timestamps and target scope (on-prem/cloud/endpoints)
object-level classification labels produced by the tool (sensitivity tag per file/object)
validation records / manual review results indicating true positives/false negatives
scan coverage reports mapping discovered objects to asset inventory/CMDB entries
configuration and policy rules used by the tool (which patterns/PII types are recognized)
counts and timestamps of data location requests and tool response times
integration logs between discovery tools and inventory/CMDB
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated continuous scan logs correlated with validation sample results and CMDB reconciliations (strong).
High-frequency scans with audited low false-negative rates from spot validation (strong).
Tool logs without mapping to an authoritative inventory or without validation samples (weak).
Ad-hoc scans or only periodic/manual classification without automation (weak).

affinity_keywords: time taken to respond to data location requests

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes deploying automated discovery/classification tools to prevent misplacement of sensitive data, which is preventive in nature (reduces chance of unauthorized storage).

CM-12(1).differentiation_note: Unlike CM-12 which is about policy and approved storage locations, CM-12(1) is specifically about the automated discovery/classification tooling — measurement must include tool coverage, classification accuracy, and response times rather than just storage-location compliance.

CM-13.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of the organization's data action maps by counting documented data flows and assets, the percentage of data assets classified by sensitivity, the percentage of assets with assigned data owners, and the frequency/recency of access-control and mapping reviews against policy requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about coverage and completeness (best measured as percentages such as percent of assets mapped/classified and percent with owners). Counts (number of flows/assets) and trends (changes in coverage over time) provide supporting evidence, while rates measure review frequency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Produce metrics that can be queried from the data-inventory/CMDB and governance tools: (1) count of data assets discovered and count of documented data flows (create/store/process/transmit) per asset; (2) percentage of those assets with a sensitivity classification and an assigned data owner; (3) age distribution and frequency of last mapping/access-control review per asset; (4) DLP/policy enforcement coverage percentage for mapped assets. Include timestamps so measurements can be trended and compared to policy thresholds.
data_signals: data inventory records (asset id, storage location, last discovered timestamp)
documented data flow entries (source, destination, protocol, action: created/stored/processed/transmitted)
sensitivity/classification attribute per data asset
data owner assignment records (owner id, attestation timestamp)
access-control review timestamps and review outcome notes
DLP policy enforcement logs mapped to asset identifiers
mapping coverage ratio: documented flows / discovered data stores
overdue mapping/review list (assets with last review > policy threshold)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Centralized, timestamped data-inventory entries and formal data-flow diagrams stored in a governance system with automated linkage to access-control and DLP logs; regular automated attestations by data owners.
Weak: Ad-hoc spreadsheets, multi-month-old diagrams, or interview-only evidence without timestamps or linkage to enforcement logs.
Strong: Continuous correlation between DLP enforcement logs and mapped assets versus unmapped assets producing incidents.
Weak: One-time mapping reports or narrative summaries without asset-level identifiers or timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly states 'Data Action Mapping is a preventive control' and focuses on identifying/documenting flows and establishing controls to prevent breaches or unauthorized access.

CM-13.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about mapping and classification coverage (documented flows, owners, classification, review cadence) rather than detection or incident response; measurement must focus on coverage, ownership and recency of mapping artifacts rather than alert volumes.

CM-14.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of deployable software artifacts (applications, libraries, container images) that are cryptographically signed by an authorized key before deployment, the count/rate of signing or verification failures that block or allow deployment, and the mean time to remediate unsigned or failed-signature components.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage/compliance (percent of components signed). Secondary count/rate metrics capture volumes of unsigned artifacts and verification failure rates and trend shows improvement or regressions over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (number of artifacts deployed that bear a valid signature from an authorized key) / (total number of artifacts attempted for deployment) as a percentage, track the count and timestamps of signature verification failures during CI/CD and at runtime, and record time from detection of an unsigned/invalid-signature artifact to remediation (deployment block removed or artifact replaced with signed version). Include artifact metadata (artifact id, digest, image tag), signer identity, signing key id, and verification result/timestamp.
data_signals: CI/CD pipeline policy enforcement logs indicating artifact signature check results (pass/fail) with timestamps
artifact inventory records: artifact id, digest, type (application/library/container image), and signature metadata (signer, key id, algorithm)
runtime signature verification logs from deploy agents or container runtimes (verification result, timestamp, host/cluster id)
deployment events showing blocked or allowed deployments with reason (unsigned, invalid signature, key revoked)
key management access and usage logs for signing keys (key id, rotate/revoke events, issuance timestamp)
records of remediation actions for unsigned artifacts (remediation start/end timestamps, responsible owner)
code-signing policy document and configured policy thresholds in CI/CD (allowed algorithms, accepted key ids)
inventory of signature verification failures and failure reasons (expired key, revoked certificate, malformed signature)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous automated CI/CD enforcement logs showing signature checks for every artifact, accompanied by artifact inventory entries with signature metadata and runtime verification logs.
Strong: Key management logs showing authorized key usage and rotation/revocation aligned with policy.
Weak: Periodic manual attestations or one-off signed-sample reports without pipeline enforcement logs.
Weak: Screenshots or ad-hoc lists of signed artifacts that lack verifiable timestamps, artifact digests, or signer key identifiers.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly labels the control as preventive and requires blocking deployment of unsigned components and enforcing signing prior to deployment.

CM-14.differentiation_note: This control specifically mandates cryptographic signing and verification of artifacts before deployment (focus on signing coverage, verification failures, and key management), distinct from sibling controls that might address runtime integrity monitoring, vulnerability scanning, or patch management.

CP-1.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness, currency, and adoption of documented cybersecurity policies and procedures: (1) percentage of required policy documents with assigned owners and up-to-date review dates; (2) percentage of employees who completed mandatory policy-related training and acknowledged policies; and (3) count and rate of policy violations and audit findings mapped to specific policies and remediation status.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control focuses on coverage and adherence (e.g., 'compliance rates', 'training completion rates'), so percentage metrics best express coverage and completion; counts and trends are useful to show violation volumes and progress over time, and a score can aggregate policy maturity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of required policy documents that have an assigned owner, a recorded last-review timestamp within the defined review cycle, and a published current version in the policy repository. 2) Percentage of employees who completed required policy training and electronically acknowledged relevant policies within the required timeframe. 3) Count and status of policy violations and audit findings mapped to the policy identifier, with timestamps and remediation closure dates so remediation rate and timeliness can be calculated.
data_signals: policy document registry entries (policy_id, owner, version, last_review_date, status)
risk assessment outputs mapping identified risks to specific policies
policy-to-regulation mapping records (policy_id -> regulatory_requirement_id)
training records (employee_id, course_id, completion_timestamp, assessment_score)
employee policy acknowledgement receipts (employee_id, policy_id, acknowledgement_timestamp)
incident/violation reports referencing policy_id (report_id, timestamp, severity, remediation_status, remediation_timestamp)
audit findings records linked to policy_id (finding_id, date, severity, remediated_flag, remediation_date)
policy change log/governance meeting minutes (change_id, author, change_timestamp, approver)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated policy registry with owner and last_review_date, machine-readable policy-to-requirement mapping, centralized training system with completion timestamps and assessment scores, and incident/audit records linked to policy IDs enabling trend analysis.

Weak: one-off emailed policy attachments without acknowledgement receipts, manual spreadsheets of policy versions, or a single annual training completion report with no timestamps or mapping to policies.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control defines and enforces policies and training to reduce human error and non-compliance, which are inherently preventive measures ('establishes a framework', 'ensuring that all employees understand their roles').

CP-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on documentation, governance, training adoption and policy lifecycle metrics (owners, reviews, acknowledgements, violations) rather than technical configuration or detection metrics used by sibling operational controls.

CP-2.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, currency, and operational effectiveness of the organization's contingency plan by tracking plan versioning, frequency of tests (tabletop/exercises), incident response time, and recovery effectiveness (RTO/RPO achievement rates).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control centers on coverage and completeness (presence of a current plan and percent of components tested/covered) so percentage (coverage/compliance) is primary; counts of exercises and response rates are useful secondary metrics to show activity and timeliness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track contingency plan artifact metadata (version, last update), the count and dates of tabletop/exercise events and attendees, per-incident response start and recovery completion timestamps, and RTO/RPO targets vs actuals by critical asset or service.
data_signals: contingency plan document version and last updated timestamp
business impact analysis (BIA) records
tabletop/simulation schedule, attendance lists and after-action reports
incident detection timestamp and incident response start timestamp
recovery completion timestamps and RTO/RPO targets per service
test success/failure outcomes and remediation items
frequency of plan review/updates (audit trail)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated logs showing incident detection-to-response timelines and recorded recovery timestamps are strong evidence;
regular, documented tabletop exercises with after-action reports and tracked remediation items strengthen evidence;
A single annual plan document without test artifacts or dated updates is weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on preparing and maintaining a plan (development, training, scheduled reviews), which is primarily preventive in reducing impact of incidents.

CP-2.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overarching contingency plan readiness and effectiveness metrics (plan currency, testing, RTO/RPO achievement) rather than specialized sub-elements like capacity or alternate sites.

CP-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the degree of integration between the contingency plan and related plans (incident response, disaster recovery, business continuity) by counting joint reviews/exercises, tracking aligned roles/responsibilities, and measuring cross-plan update frequency and consistency.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control emphasizes frequency and occurrence of coordination activities (meetings, joint exercises), so counts of coordination events are primary; percentage alignment/completion of cross-plan actions complements counts.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count scheduled coordination meetings and joint exercise events, capture cross-reference links between plan documents (e.g., incident response and contingency plan sections), and measure the percentage of shared roles/responsibilities that are documented and confirmed by stakeholders.
data_signals: inventory of related plans with cross-reference fields (incident response, DR, BCP)
calendar records of coordination meetings and joint exercises
attendance rosters and meeting minutes
documented roles and responsibilities lists mapped across plans
plan update change logs showing synchronized revisions
post-exercise after-action reports referencing multiple plans
evidence_strength_indicators: Documented joint exercises with minutes showing coordinated actions and synchronized plan updates are strong evidence;
Regular meeting schedules and attendance with linked document revisions are stronger than ad-hoc or undocumented coordination;
One-off email confirmations without cross-referenced plan updates are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires alignment and planning across documents and stakeholders (coordination, scheduled reviews), which is a preventive governance activity.

CP-2(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on cross-plan integration and joint exercise/counts of coordination activities versus general plan readiness or capacity metrics.

CP-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure capacity readiness by tracking resource capacity assessments, thresholds that trigger contingency actions, and RTO/RPO attainment relative to projected resource requirements during disruptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Capacity controls require coverage and adequacy measures (percent of services meeting capacity thresholds); counts of capacity assessments and trend analysis of capacity utilization are valuable secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure percent of critical services with documented capacity assessments and thresholds, count of resources provisioned versus required during tests, and record RTO/RPO performance under simulated load compared to capacity thresholds.
data_signals: capacity assessment reports per service (CPU, memory, bandwidth, storage)
defined capacity thresholds and trigger conditions
provisioned resource counts and configuration baselines
load/scale test results and utilization graphs
RTO/RPO test results under capacity constraints
frequency and dates of capacity reassessments
evidence_strength_indicators: Regular load test results showing services meet capacity thresholds and documented trigger conditions are strong evidence;
Automated monitoring showing headroom against thresholds over time is strong;
A one-time capacity estimate without test evidence is weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates planning and thresholds to avoid outages (capacity assessments and triggers), which is preventive in nature.

CP-2(2).differentiation_note: This control is distinct because it requires measurable capacity thresholds and load-test evidence rather than just plan documents or coordination events.

CP-2(3).measurement_goal: Measure recovery performance by recording time-to-resume metrics (per critical business function), RTO/RPO achievement rates, and success rates of recovery procedures during exercises and real incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control targets recovery performance (how quickly functions are resumed), so rates (e.g., MTTR, percent of services meeting RTO per incident) are primary; counts of successful restores and trend analysis are complementary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Capture incident start and service resume timestamps per business function to compute MTTR/percent meeting RTO, record RPO attainment per data set, and log recovery procedure execution outcomes during tests and incidents.
data_signals: incident detection and recovery completion timestamps by service
RTO and RPO targets mapped to services
post-incident after-action reports documenting recovery steps
tabletop/simulation recovery exercise results and pass/fail
percentage of critical functions resumed within target windows
communication logs during recovery (alerts, stakeholder notifications)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated timestamps showing detection-to-recovery intervals and system state changes are strong evidence;
Documented exercise outcomes with measured resume times strengthen evidence;
Qualitative statements without timestamped recovery logs are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is focused on restoring operations after disruptions (measuring time to resume and effectiveness of recovery), which is corrective.

CP-2(3).differentiation_note: Unlike planning or coordination controls, this entry emphasizes time-based recovery performance (MTTR/RTO attainment) and measurable resume timelines per function.

CP-2(4).measurement_goal: Demonstrate capability to restore all mission and business functions by measuring success rate of full-restore tests, time-to-full-resumption, and frequency of plan updates driven by lessons learned.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires measuring plan readiness and success of full restoration; percentage of successful full-restores and percent of functions restored within RTO are most indicative, with counts of full-restore tests as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure percent of full-restore tests that achieved complete restoration of all mission functions within target timeframes, track time-to-full-resumption for each test, and record plan updates resulting from test findings.
data_signals: full-restore test schedules and results
time-to-full-resumption timestamps per test
success/failure flags for each mission function restored
RTO/RPO mapping and attainment per function
plan revision logs post-test
frequency of full-restore tests conducted
evidence_strength_indicators: Multiple automated test runs with measured full-resume times and documented remediation actions are strong evidence;
One-off successful restores without documented repeatable procedures are weaker evidence.

affinity_keywords: Resume All Mission and Business Functions

control_type_classification.reasoning: Although measuring recovery, the control (as labeled) emphasizes preparedness to resume all functions and regular testing, aligning with preventive readiness.

CP-2(4).differentiation_note: This focuses on evidence for complete/full-function restoration (end-to-end full-restore tests) rather than partial/function-specific recovery metrics.

CP-2(5).measurement_goal: Measure the organization's ability to continue mission and business functions under disruption by tracking percent of critical functions maintained within RTOs/RPOs, frequency/effectiveness of continuity exercises, and documentation of role execution during incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Continuity is assessed by coverage/attainment rates (percent of functions continued within targets); remediation and recovery rates (MTTR) are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of critical functions that could continue operation during tests and actual incidents within target RTO/RPO, track exercise frequency and participation, and log documented continuity procedure activations and outcomes.
data_signals: continuity exercise schedules and results
service availability metrics during simulated disruptions
RTO/RPO attainment per critical function
activation logs of continuity procedures
attendance and role execution records from exercises
incidents where continuity procedures were used and outcomes
evidence_strength_indicators: System availability and continuity activation logs across multiple exercises/incidents are strong evidence;
Self-reported or anecdotal accounts without timestamped system evidence are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: This control emphasizes planning and preparedness to maintain functions during disruptions (procedures, exercises), which is preventive.

CP-2(5).differentiation_note: Emphasizes continuity (maintaining operations during disruption) and measures of sustained availability rather than full post-incident restoration.

CP-2(6).measurement_goal: Demonstrate alternate site readiness by measuring failover activation time, success rate of site switch tests, data integrity at alternate sites (RPO attainment), and frequency of validation drills for alternate processing/storage.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Failover and alternate-site controls are best measured by rates (failover activation time, success rates) with percent coverage of systems replicated to alternate sites as a complementary metric.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Record failover trigger and completion timestamps for alternate site activations, measure success/failure of data integrity checks and RPO attainment at alternate site, and count successful site failover tests versus attempts.
data_signals: failover activation timestamps and completion timestamps
alternate site replication status and last-sync timestamps
RPO/RTO results for services on alternate site
failover test schedules and pass/fail outcomes
infrastructure configuration parity checks between primary and alternate
third-party continuity contract clauses and test evidence
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated failover logs with timestamps and data integrity verification are strong evidence;
Regular successful failover drills with documented outcomes are strong;
Documented plans without tested failovers are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates establishing and validating alternate sites and infrastructure to prevent total service loss, a preventive resilience activity.

CP-2(6).differentiation_note: Specifically requires evidence of alternate-site readiness and failover performance (replication, failover times), unlike general recovery planning controls.

CP-2(7).measurement_goal: Measure the level of coordination with external service providers by tracking frequency of plan-sharing and joint exercises, percentage of critical dependencies covered by provider continuity plans, and measured outcomes of joint incident responses.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Coordination is evidenced through counts of coordination meetings and joint exercises; percent of critical dependencies with aligned provider plans is a key secondary metric.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count coordination meetings and joint exercises with external providers, record contract clauses for continuity and service-level continuity commitments, and measure percent of critical dependencies with validated provider continuity plans and test outcomes.
data_signals: records of coordination meetings and joint exercise schedules and minutes
contracts and SLAs with continuity/disaster recovery clauses
list of critical service dependencies and mapped provider continuity plans
results of joint exercises and post-incident coordination reviews
completion rate of required plan updates requested of providers
evidence_strength_indicators: Signed contracts with DR/continuity clauses and evidence of joint tested exercises are strong evidence;
Post-incident reviews showing coordinated actions are strong;
Informal email exchanges without contractual or test evidence are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing coordination processes and contractual alignment with providers prior to incidents, a preventive governance activity.

CP-2(7).differentiation_note: Targets third-party coordination and contractual/test evidence with providers, unlike internal-only plan readiness controls.

CP-2(8).measurement_goal: Measure accuracy and coverage of the critical asset inventory by tracking percent of assets classified with criticality, percent with documented recovery plans (RTO/RPO), and frequency of inventory validation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Identification and prioritization require coverage metrics (percent of assets with classification and recovery plans); counts and distribution across criticality tiers help prioritize remediation and planning.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percent of total assets captured in the inventory with a criticality tag, percent of critical assets that have documented recovery plans including RTO/RPO, and track changes/validation timestamps to ensure inventory currency.
data_signals: asset inventory entries with criticality classification
BIA outputs mapping assets to business functions
documented recovery plans (RTO/RPO) per critical asset
audit logs of inventory updates and validation activities
percentage of critical assets with testable recovery plans
time since last validation for each critical asset
evidence_strength_indicators: A machine-readable asset inventory with criticality tags, linked recovery plans, and recent validation logs is strong evidence;
A partial spreadsheet list or undocumented classifications without recoverability details is weak.

affinity_keywords: percentage of critical assets with documented recovery plans

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is about identifying and prioritizing assets ahead of incidents to support planning, which is inherently preventive.

CP-2(8).differentiation_note: This control is uniquely about asset-level coverage and classification (inventory completeness and percent with recovery plans) rather than exercise or failover performance.

CP-3.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of the contingency training program by tracking training frequency, employee participation/completion rates, post-training assessment scores, and the change in incident response times before and after training.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control the primary focus is coverage and completion (percent of personnel trained/on-schedule). Secondary metrics capture event rates (drill frequency), assessment scores, and trends in response-time improvement referenced in the control text.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count training sessions per period and compute percentage of required personnel who completed required contingency training; capture per-person post-training assessment scores and record time-to-respond metrics from drills/simulations to compare before/after training windows.
data_signals: training session schedule and execution timestamps
attendance/roster records and completion certificates per employee
post-training assessment scores and pass/fail results
drill/simulation start and end timestamps with measured response times
participant feedback / after-action review notes
training curriculum version and last update timestamp
remediation or action-item counts from after-action reports with closure timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated LMS logs with timestamps, assessment scores, and drill telemetry showing measured response times before and after training.
Strong: documented after-action reports and closed action items tied to specific training events.
Weak: manual sign-in sheets or self-reported participation without assessment results.
Weak: one-off training records or annual-only exercises that do not show improvement over time.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control designed to ensure that personnel are adequately prepared' and emphasizes training and drills to reduce incident impact.

CP-3.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall training program effectiveness (coverage, assessment scores, and response-time improvement), whereas the sibling controls focus specifically on simulated scenarios (CP-3(1)) and on the mechanisms/environments used in training (CP-3(2)).

CP-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the realism and effectiveness of simulated incident exercises by tracking simulation frequency, cross-functional participation rates, measured detection and response times during simulations, post-exercise assessment outcomes, and closure of action items that update incident response plans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Primary percentage metrics (e.g., percent of required teams participating or percent of simulations with complete after-action reports) measure preventive coverage; secondary rate/count/score metrics capture detection/response times, exercise frequency, and exercise effectiveness scores called out in the control text.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Record each simulated event with timestamps and participant rosters; capture time-to-detect and time-to-respond for each simulation, post-exercise assessment scores, number of identified action items and their closure timelines, and any updates to incident response plans derived from the exercises.
data_signals: simulation event records with start/stop timestamps
participant rosters and functional roles present per simulation
measured detection time and response time during simulation
post-exercise assessment results and scoring rubrics
after-action reports and list of action items with open/close timestamps
changes to incident response plans or runbooks with revision timestamps
participant feedback/survey responses
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated simulation telemetry that logs detection and response timestamps, linked after-action reports, and tracked closure of action items.
Strong: measurable reduction in simulated detection/response times over successive periods.
Weak: tabletop notes without measured detection/response times or lacking participant role coverage.
Weak: anecdotal participant feedback without scored assessments or documented plan updates.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control text frames simulated events as proactive training to prepare personnel and mitigate unpreparedness, which is preventive in nature.

CP-3(1).differentiation_note: CP-3(1) requires measurement of simulation-specific detection/response times and after-action remediation tracking; unlike CP-3 it emphasizes realism of scenarios and measurable detection/response performance.

CP-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure the fidelity and coverage of training mechanisms by tracking the proportion of training exercises that use production-like or approved virtual environments/tools, the inventory and usage of those mechanisms, training content currency, and fidelity scores from participants and evaluators.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about coverage and fidelity of training mechanisms (percent of exercises using approved/realistic environments). Secondary scores measure fidelity/realism and counts track tool/infrastructure usage and updates.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage of training sessions executed in production-like or approved simulated environments; record the inventory of training tools/environments, fidelity evaluation scores for each exercise, training content version and last-updated timestamp, and frequency of environment/tool usage.
data_signals: training environment inventory (IDs, type: virtual/physical, provisioned config)
mapping of training session to environment ID and timestamps
environment configuration snapshots or baseline comparison to production
fidelity/effectiveness evaluation scores from observers or rubrics
training content version history and last update timestamps
count of training sessions using approved tools vs unapproved/manual setups
participant completion records tied to environment used
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: configuration snapshots showing environment parity with production and automated linkage between training session and environment IDs plus fidelity scoring.
Strong: regular updates to training content with timestamps and evidence of tooling usage consistent with NIST SP 800-84 guidance.
Weak: ad-hoc or undocumented training setups, manual descriptions of mechanisms without configuration evidence.
Weak: single annual environment-based exercise without ongoing verification of environment fidelity.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on building and maintaining realistic training mechanisms to prepare personnel, a forward-looking/preventive activity.

CP-3(2).differentiation_note: CP-3(2) specifically measures the quality and production-parity of the training environments and tools (fidelity and usage), unlike CP-3 which measures overall program effectiveness and CP-3(1) which measures simulation response performance.

CP-4.measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and types of contingency tests executed, the percentage of planned tests completed on schedule, median and distribution of time-to-restore (RTO) observed during tests, percentage of personnel who executed assigned roles successfully, and the count and closure rate of identified gaps per test cycle.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control that verifies tests occur and produce outcomes; counts of tests, gaps, and role completions are primary evidence, with percentages for coverage/completion and trending to show improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of contingency tests by type (tabletop, simulation, full-scale) and date; test completion status; per-test recovery durations (time from incident start to restored operations) mapped to affected systems; participant lists with role-execution success flags; identified gaps/issues with ticket IDs and closure timestamps. These data points should be queryable by test ID, date, and system.
data_signals: test execution records (test_id, type: tabletop|simulation|drill, start_time, end_time)
test plan objectives and scheduled date vs actual execution timestamp
participant roster and role execution status for each test
measured time-to-restore per affected system (RTO observed)
number of gaps/issues identified per test and remediation ticket IDs
remediation ticket open and close timestamps (to compute closure rate and time-to-fix)
test report artifacts and lessons-learned documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured test execution logs with timestamps, participant role completion flags, and linked remediation ticket lifecycle data for every test.
Weak: single summary reports or one-off slide decks without timestamps, participant lists, or ticket linkage; anecdotal notes from exercises.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on conducting and documenting tests (e.g., 'testing should be conducted at least annually', 'documenting the outcomes'), which makes it a control that detects preparedness gaps rather than directly preventing incidents.

CP-4.differentiation_note: This entry covers the broad, program-level measurement of contingency testing (frequency, role execution, and gap closure) rather than specialized scenarios like alternate sites, automation, or full reconstitution.

CP-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of contingency tests that include coordinated scenarios with incident response, disaster recovery, and business continuity plans; track stakeholder participation rates by role; and count cross-plan issues identified and remediated within defined timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective evidence requires counts (number of coordinated tests, cross-plan issues) and percentages (percent of tests with full stakeholder participation); trending shows improving alignment across plans.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of tests explicitly labeled as 'coordinated' (with linked incident response, disaster recovery, or business continuity plan IDs) per period; roster of stakeholder participants mapped to plan ownership; per-test list of cross-plan gaps and remediation tickets with timestamps to verify updates to each related plan.
data_signals: test records indicating related plan IDs (incident response, DR, BCP) included in the exercise
participant lists with department/plan ownership and attendance status
communication channel test results (e.g., notification send logs, receipt acknowledgements)
cross-plan issues identified and remediation ticket IDs
updates to plan documents (version, timestamp, author) following tests
test frequency schedule and actual execution timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: test artifacts explicitly reference and link updated incident response/DR/BCP documents and show participant attendance and follow-up plan updates.
Weak: tests described in narrative with no linkage to specific plan versions or absent participant/communication evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires conducting coordinated tests and documenting outcomes to detect alignment gaps across plans, so it functions as a detective verification activity.

CP-4(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on cross-plan coordination and evidence linking multiple plans and stakeholders, unlike the generic CP-4 which measures overall test effectiveness.
