CP-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure the time to activate the alternate processing site (activation latency), percentage of required hardware/software confirmed operational at the alternate site during tests, percentage of personnel trained for alternate-site operations, and count of gaps found and remediated related to alternate-site readiness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Counts of activation events and issues are primary detective evidence; percentages convey coverage (hardware/software operational, trained personnel) and trends show readiness improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-test activation records with timestamps: activation_start, services_brought_online timestamps, and full-availability timestamp; inventory of hardware and software status at alternate site during test; roster of trained personnel and role-execution success flags; list of gaps/issues and remediation ticket lifecycle to closure.
data_signals: alternate site activation log entries (activation_start_time, service_online_time, full_operational_time)
site inventory and configuration snapshots taken during tests
service health checks and connectivity test results from alternate site
participant training records and role-execution outcomes
backup restore logs for services hosted at alternate site
identified gaps/issues and remediation ticket IDs with close timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: activation logs with machine-readable timestamps, automated service health checks confirming functionality, and linked remediation tickets updating alternate-site configuration.
Weak: after-action narratives without activation timestamps, manual attestations of readiness, or no verification of software/hardware operability.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires testing and validating activation and readiness of an alternate processing site (e.g., 'activating the alternate processing site', 'regular testing'), which is a verification/detection activity.

CP-4(2).differentiation_note: Specifically targets measurable activation and operability of an alternate processing site (activation latency and site inventory), unlike other CP-4 entries that cover broader test program metrics.

CP-4(3).measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and success rate of automated contingency tests (automated test runs per period and percent passing), mean time to remediate issues discovered by automated tests, and trend in recurring failures detected by automation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated tests produce discrete runs and alerts (counts) and pass/fail rates; secondary rate and trend metrics capture effectiveness and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Automated test run logs with unique run IDs, start/end timestamps, scope (systems/services tested), test result (pass/fail), list of detected issues with alert IDs, and remediation ticket linkage including time-to-remediate. Also capture configuration of automated test schedules and tool versions.
data_signals: automated test execution logs (run_id, start_time, end_time, scope)
automated test results (pass/fail, failure reasons, affected assets)
alert/issue IDs generated by automated tests
remediation tickets linked to automated-test findings with open/close timestamps
automation tool configuration and schedule records
frequency of automated tests per asset or asset class
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-generated automated test logs with run IDs, deterministic pass/fail outcomes, and linked remediation tickets.
Weak: manual summaries of automated test activity or sporadic CSV exports without consistent run metadata or ticket linkage.

affinity_keywords: time taken to resolve identified issues

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates regular automated testing ('establish a schedule for automated testing', 'tools that simulate...'), indicating an automated detection/verification function.

CP-4(3).differentiation_note: Focuses on machine-generated evidence (automated test runs, pass/fail results, and linked tickets) and expects continuous, tool-level telemetry rather than manual exercise reports.

CP-4(4).measurement_goal: Measure per-test RTO and RPO achieved (median, 95th percentile), completeness of data recovery (percentage of systems/data restored), and count of discrepancies between planned and observed reconstitution steps; track participant feedback and corrective actions closed post-test.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective testing yields counts of full reconstitutions and distributions of measured RTO/RPO; percentages measure completeness of data/system recovery against objectives.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-reconstitution test logs recording RTO observed (service outage to restored), RPO observed (last recoverable timestamp), percent of systems restored successfully, backup restore logs and verification checksums, and after-action reports documenting discrepancies with prescribed recovery procedures and corrective ticket IDs.
data_signals: recovery time measurements per system (observed RTO timestamps)
recovery point measurements (RPO observed vs expected)
backup restore logs with checksums and success/failure status
percent of systems/data verified as restored successfully during test
after-action reports and participant feedback
corrective action tickets with open/close timestamps for reconstitution gaps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: detailed recovery logs with timestamps, integrity verification (checksums), and linked corrective action tickets showing closure.
Weak: high-level statements of 'recovered' without system-level verification, missing RTO/RPO timestamps, or absent data integrity checks.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires testing full recovery and reconstitution and measuring recovery objectives ('RTO' and 'RPO'), which are verification activities to detect gaps in recovery capability.

CP-4(4).differentiation_note: Specifically measures recovery objective attainment (RTO/RPO and data completeness) and integrity verification during full reconstitution tests, unlike general CP-4 which measures overall test program metrics.

CP-4(5).measurement_goal: Measure the frequency of self-challenge exercises, percent of contingency plan elements exercised, percentage of personnel trained and demonstrating role competency during challenges, and count and remediation rate of weaknesses discovered via self-challenges.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Self-challenge is investigative/detective; counts of exercises and discovered weaknesses are primary, with percentages to express coverage and competency and trends to show improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Records of self-challenge events (type, date, scope), mapping of exercised plan elements to plan sections, participant competency check results (pass/fail or score), list of weaknesses identified with ticket IDs and remediation timelines, and evidence of plan updates applied as a result of the challenge.
data_signals: self-challenge exercise logs (exercise_id, type: tabletop|simulation|drill, date)
mapping of exercised scenarios to contingency plan sections/controls
participant competency scores or role-execution flags
weakness/issues identified and remediation ticket IDs
plan version changes and update timestamps following exercises
frequency of self-challenge per plan or system
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured exercise logs linked to plan sections, participant competency evidence, and closed remediation tickets that resulted in documented plan updates.
Weak: informal or undocumented 'self-challenges' with no participant outcomes or remediation evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes proactively challenging and testing plans to identify weaknesses ('regular testing', 'identify weaknesses'), which is a detective verification activity to reveal gaps.

CP-4(5).differentiation_note: Emphasizes proactive, adversarial-style or self-challenge testing focused on personnel competency and discovering latent weaknesses, unlike automated or alternate-site specific tests.

CP-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of contingency plans reviewed and updated within the organization’s required cadence (e.g., annually or after significant changes), the count and completion rate of tabletop exercises/tests executed, and the percentage of corrective actions from tests and incidents closed within defined timeframes; additionally measure actual recovery times against planned recovery expectations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control verifies coverage and timeliness (e.g., percent of plans reviewed on schedule and percent of corrective actions closed). Counts of exercises/tests and trend analysis over time are useful secondary metrics to show ongoing program activity and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and timestamped records of plan review events per plan and plan owner, percent of plans with an update within the policy-defined period (e.g., within 12 months or after a change), count and dates of tabletop exercises with participant rosters, open corrective actions from tests/incidents with creation and close timestamps, and measured incident recovery durations compared to plan recovery expectations (RTO/RPO as recorded in the plan).
data_signals: contingency_plan_last_review_timestamp (per plan)
contingency_plan_next_review_due_date / review_schedule
contingency_plan_owner / stakeholder assignment records
tabletop_exercise_execution_dates and participant lists
test_results / exercise reports and documented lessons learned
corrective_action / remediation tickets from exercises/incidents with status and close timestamps
incident outage duration and time_taken_to_recover compared against plan RTOs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, versioned plan repository with review timestamps and signed approvals for each update
Strong: completed tabletop exercise reports with dated participant rosters and tracked corrective action tickets closed with timestamps
Strong: incident telemetry showing measured recovery durations that map to plan RTO fields
Weak: single undated or manually signed plan document without change history or dated exercise reports
Weak: anecdotal or undocumented claims of tests or updates without supporting tickets/logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description requires establishing a schedule for regular reviews, tests, and updates (preventive activities) such as 'establish a schedule' and 'regularly reviewed, tested, and updated.'

CP-5.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measuring review/update cadence, exercise execution and closure of corrective actions for contingency plans (focus on plan lifecycle and test evidence), differing from sibling controls that might focus only on backups, DR execution, or incident detection; if no siblings exist in the batch, the entry emphasizes cadence and test evidence as the unique measurement need.

CP-6.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical assets and datasets that are automatically backed up to a secure offsite alternate storage site, the count of successful backup transfers per period, and the time required to restore data from the alternate site during recovery tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive coverage control — auditors want percent coverage of critical data replicated offsite (coverage %) plus counts of successful transfers and trends in restore time to show ongoing effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each critical asset/dataset, count backup executions to the alternate site, record backup success/failure flags and timestamps, capture bytes transferred and encryption-at-rest/ in-transit status, and record restore durations (start/end) during recovery tests to compute median/percentile restore time.
data_signals: backup execution timestamp per asset to alternate storage site
backup success/failure status and error codes
bytes transferred / backup size per run
encryption-in-transit and encryption-at-rest flags for each backup
restore test start and end timestamps (restore duration)
count of incidents where recovery from alternate site was required
storage location identifier / alternate site ID and storage solution type (cloud or physical)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated backup/replication logs with per-asset timestamps and success codes are strong evidence;
regular documented restore test results showing measured restore durations are strong evidence;
annual/manual only backup records or single-point-in-time screenshots are weak evidence for ongoing effectiveness.

affinity_keywords: encryption of data in transit and at rest
number of incidents where data recovery was required

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates establishing an offsite site and automated backups to prevent data loss (phrases: 'offsite location', 'regularly scheduled data backups', 'automatically transfer').

CP-6.differentiation_note: High-level alternate storage coverage and backup success metrics (coverage %, backup counts, restore durations) versus sibling controls that focus on geographic separation, RTO/RPO objectives, or accessibility/replication health.

CP-6(1).measurement_goal: Demonstrate the percentage of critical datasets replicated to an alternate storage site that is geographically distinct from the primary site and track replication method usage (real-time mirroring vs scheduled backups) and last-sync age to prove the alternate site is not affected by the same local events.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive separation control; auditors want percent coverage of datasets stored in a geographically distinct site and counts of replication methods, with comparisons to policy-defined separation requirements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each critical dataset, capture the alternate site region/location metadata, replication method (real-time mirror or scheduled backup), last successful replication timestamp and replication lag, and documentation proving the alternate site is geographically distinct from the primary (site location/region tags or distance metric).
data_signals: alternate site region / location metadata per dataset
replication method indicator (real-time mirroring | scheduled backups)
last successful replication timestamp and replication lag (seconds/minutes/hours)
replication success/failure logs and error codes
documentation of site separation (region, facility ID, or distance statement)
records of risk assessments evaluating site separation
records of update frequency for data stored at alternate site
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-recorded replication logs with site-region metadata and low replication lag are strong evidence;
documented, signed risk assessments and manual attestations without machine logs are weaker evidence for ongoing geographic separation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing geographically distinct secondary storage and replication methods to prevent correlated loss ('geographically distinct', 'sufficiently distant', 'data replication methods').

CP-6(1).differentiation_note: Specifically measures geographic separation and replication method coverage (region metadata, replication lag) rather than general backup coverage or recovery timing.

CP-6(2).measurement_goal: Measure actual Recovery Time Objectives (actual RTO) and actual Recovery Point Objectives (actual RPO) achieved during recovery tests for critical systems, and the frequency and pass/fail rate of those tests against defined RTO/RPO targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control focuses on time-based objectives (RTO/RPO); trend metrics over repeated recovery tests are most useful to show sustained compliance and improvement; counts of tests and comparisons to targets are also required.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each recovery test, record test start and end timestamps to compute actual RTO, record the timestamp of last recoverable data to compute actual RPO (amount/time of data loss), map each test to business-criticality from the BIA, and capture test pass/fail outcomes and root-cause notes.
data_signals: recovery test start and end timestamps (per system/test)
measured data loss window or timestamp used to calculate actual RPO
defined RTO and RPO targets per system (policy/BIA)
test pass/fail flag and test run identifier
BIA criticality mapping per system
test frequency schedule and test run reports (test artifacts)
evidence_strength_indicators: Repeated, automated restore-test logs showing measured actual RTO/RPO vs targets across multiple runs are strong evidence;
single ad-hoc test reports or theoretical RTO/RPO statements without measured timestamps are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control defines recovery objectives and requires testing to ensure systems can be restored within defined timeframes (phrases: 'establish clear RTOs and RPOs', 'regular testing').

CP-6(2).differentiation_note: Focused on measured time-to-recover and data-loss metrics (actual RTO/RPO) and test pass rates, distinguishing it from sibling controls that focus on site selection or replication health.

CP-6(3).measurement_goal: Measure replication frequency, replication lag (last-sync age), and the percentage of critical datasets at the alternate site that meet the organization's acceptible RPO; track restore success rate and measured restore time from the alternate site during accessibility tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Accessibility is about ongoing replication health and up-to-dateness — percent of datasets within acceptable RPO is the best preventive coverage metric, supplemented by failure rates and trends in sync age.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per critical dataset and system, capture last replication timestamp (to compute sync age), frequency of successful replications, replication failure rate, percent of datasets whose last-sync age is within the acceptable RPO, and restore test durations and success/fail results to demonstrate accessibility.
data_signals: last replication timestamp per dataset (sync age)
replication frequency and schedule metadata
replication success/failure counts and error codes
percent of datasets within acceptable RPO threshold
restore test duration and success/failure logs
access control and encryption status for alternate site retrieval
capacity and availability metrics for alternate storage (e.g., storage utilization, service availability)
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time replication health telemetry and continuous sync-age dashboards are strong evidence;
only periodic manual accessibility checks or undocumented manual restores are weak evidence for ongoing accessibility.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires maintaining an accessible, synchronized alternate site and testing accessibility to prevent loss of access during disruptions ('ensure alternate storage site is always up-to-date', 'regular testing').

CP-6(3).differentiation_note: Specifically targets replication health, sync-age and accessibility metrics (last-sync, sync lag, restore success) rather than geographic separation or recovery objective timing.

CP-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical business functions covered by a documented, geographically distinct alternate processing site, and track failover test outcomes by comparing observed RTO and RPO against defined SLA targets and the number of successful drills per period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive readiness control is best evidenced by coverage/ compliance percentages (e.g., % of critical functions with an alternate site) with counts of tests/drills and trending of RTO/RPO over time to show improvement or degradation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of critical business functions with a documented alternate processing site and up-to-date disaster recovery plan; for each test/drill, record observed RTO and RPO, test outcome (success/failure), test date/time, and post-test corrective actions with completion dates to compare against SLA targets.
data_signals: inventory of critical business functions mapped to alternate processing sites
alternate site geographic location and distance metadata
disaster recovery plan version and last-updated timestamp
failover/drill execution timestamps and test results (success/failure)
observed recovery time objective (RTO) per test
observed recovery point objective (RPO) / replication lag per test
post-test corrective action items with status and completion dates
communication/role assignment logs for recovery personnel
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated test execution logs with measured RTO/RPO and pass/fail outcome (strong)
Regularly scheduled drills with documented after-action reports and completed remediation items (strong)
One-off manual test reports or undocumented tabletop exercises without timestamps or outcomes (weak)
Ambiguous documentation stating 'alternate site exists' without test evidence or measured RTO/RPO (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes establishing and testing an alternate site and readiness processes to prevent loss of availability, emphasizing preparedness and documented plans.

CP-7.differentiation_note: This is the parent control focused on overall readiness, coverage, and measured RTO/RPO performance versus SLA; its siblings focus on single aspects (separation, accessibility, priority, etc.).

CP-7(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical systems whose alternate processing sites are geographically distinct from the primary site per the organization's geographic-separation policy, and count instances of shared dependencies that could cause simultaneous disruption.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage/compliance metrics (percentage of systems meeting geographic separation) with counts of exceptions and distribution of dependency overlap to surface residual simultaneous-failure risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical assets with alternate sites located in different geographic regions or separate risk zones; number of assets whose alternate site shares physical utilities, network providers, or co-location facilities with the primary site (dependency overlap), including documentation showing reasons for any exceptions.
data_signals: asset-to-primary-site and asset-to-alternate-site location coordinates/region tags
dependency mapping: power provider, network provider, HVAC/provider IDs for primary and alternate sites
policy defining minimum geographic separation or separation criteria
exceptions register with business justification and approval timestamps
count of assets with shared physical/infrastructure dependencies
last assessment/test confirming geographic independence
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-readable asset location mapping and automated dependency comparisons showing zero shared dependencies (strong)
Formal approved exceptions documented with compensating controls (moderate)
Verbal assertions of separation without mapped dependencies or documentation (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement to establish geographic separation is a preventive design decision to avoid simultaneous disruption.

CP-7(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on geographic independence and shared-dependency mapping rather than general failover readiness or RTO/RPO performance.

CP-7(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of personnel and system access requirements validated for the alternate site (physical and remote) and count successful accessibility tests (personnel access, connectivity, credentialing) prior to activation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Accessibility is a preventive readiness requirement best measured by coverage/validation percentages (e.g., % of required access configurations tested) plus counts of successful access tests and trends over repeated drills.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of required personnel with validated access (physical badge, remote VPN/SSH credentials) at the alternate site, number of successful connectivity tests to critical systems from the alternate site, and timestamps of last access provisioning and validation events.
data_signals: list of personnel required for recovery and their access provisioning records for the alternate site
physical access approval logs and last-tested date/time
VPN/remote access test results and connection success/failure logs
credential provisioning timestamps and expiration dates
accessibility test drill records with outcomes
communication protocol confirmations and contact lists
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated access test logs and successful connection traces from the alternate site (strong)
Signed access rosters and dated provisioning records verified against policy (strong)
Unverified lists of names without access logs or test evidence (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Ensuring accessibility (access provisioning and connectivity) prevents failed activation of the alternate site and is a readiness/preventive activity.

CP-7(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures ability of people and systems to access and operate at the alternate site (access provisioning and connectivity), unlike sibling controls that measure geographic separation or RTO/RPO performance.

CP-7(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical services that have an assigned priority/tier and corresponding RTO/RPO at the alternate site, and track remediation/compliance rates where priority assignments are missing or unmet.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires verifying coverage of prioritized services – percentages of services with defined priority and SLA mapping are most appropriate, supplemented by counts of priority mismatches and trends in SLA compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of critical services with documented priority/tiering and associated RTO/RPO assigned for alternate-site operation; for each service, record last test result showing whether the service met its assigned RTO/RPO and count of priority-related exceptions with mitigation.
data_signals: service catalogue with priority/tier assignments
documented RTO and RPO per service
test/drill results per service with observed RTO/RPO
exceptions or downgrade notifications with approvals
service dependency maps showing prioritized service components
completion status of mitigation actions for missed priorities
evidence_strength_indicators: Service-level test records showing RTO/RPO met per assigned priority (strong)
Service catalogue entry exists but no test evidence or last-test older than policy window (weak)
Approved exceptions with compensating controls documented (moderate)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Defining and enforcing priority of service at the alternate site is a preventive planning activity to ensure critical services are restored per business needs.

CP-7(3).differentiation_note: Concentrates on service-level prioritization and SLA mapping (RTO/RPO per service) rather than site-level readiness or access.

CP-7(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of alternate-site preparation checklist items completed (power, network, systems provisioning, data replication) and track mean time to prepare/activate the alternate site during tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preparation is a preventive readiness activity best expressed as percent-complete for checklists and activation-readiness, with rates (time-to-activate) as a secondary corrective/performance measure.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent completion of a predefined alternate-site preparation checklist (infrastructure provisioning, data replication, configuration synchronization) prior to activation, plus recorded activation time from initiation to operational readiness for each test and whether the activation met target times.
data_signals: alternate-site preparation checklist items and completion timestamps
infrastructure provisioning logs (VM provisioning, storage allocation) with timestamps
data replication status and last-known replication lag
configuration sync records (configuration drift checks) with pass/fail
activation initiation and operational-ready timestamps per drill
staff assignment and training completion records
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated provisioning and replication logs with timestamps proving checklist items completed before activation (strong)
Detailed activation time series across multiple drills showing consistency (strong)
Checklist with manual completion marks but no timestamps or supporting logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Preparation and documented readiness activities aim to prevent failed activations and ensure the alternate site can be used when needed.

CP-7(4).differentiation_note: Emphasizes checklist completion and activation timing (time-to-ready) rather than geographic separation or service prioritization.

CP-7(5).measurement_goal: Because this control was withdrawn and incorporated into CP-7, measurement should be folded into CP-7: measure that alternate site implements equivalent information security safeguards (percent of required security controls implemented at the alternate site compared to primary).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Verifying equivalent safeguards is preventive and best expressed as coverage percentages or a control-implementation score comparing alternate vs primary site security controls.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of required information security controls (encryption at rest/in transit, access controls, logging) implemented and configured at the alternate site compared to the primary site, with evidence from configuration baselines and audit logs.
data_signals: control implementation matrix mapping security controls between primary and alternate site
configuration baselines for systems at alternate site (encryption, logging, access controls)
audit/logging configuration records and retention settings
gap analysis findings and remediation action items
evidence of monitoring/alerting configured at alternate site
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated configuration scans and audit logs showing parity between primary and alternate site (strong)
High-level statements that safeguards are 'equivalent' without technical evidence (weak)
Remediation plans with target dates but no implemented controls yet (moderate)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Ensuring equivalent security safeguards at an alternate site is preventive; the control text indicates it was folded into CP-7 but still represents preventive coverage requirements.

CP-7(5).differentiation_note: This entry is marked withdrawn and should be measured as part of CP-7; its unique focus is parity of information-security controls between sites rather than general DR readiness.

CP-7(6).measurement_goal: Measure time-in-failover (hours/days), percent of critical functions fully operational at alternate site after extended failover, and the number of unresolved actions preventing return to the primary site to evidence readiness for inability-to-return scenarios.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This corrective/operational control measures how the organization performs during prolonged failover: rates (duration, MTTR, percent restored over time) directly show response and sustainment capability, with counts of outstanding remediation items as secondary signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Time elapsed since failover initiation (time-in-failover), percent of critical business functions achieving operational status at the alternate site over time, counts of outstanding barriers to returning to primary site, and documented decisions/triggers for extended relocation.
data_signals: failover initiation timestamp and current/closure timestamp
operational-status per critical function with timestamps (up/down/partial)
number and description of unresolved technical or regulatory issues preventing return
data migration completion status and remaining bytes to synchronize
communications/decision logs authorizing extended stay at alternate site
resource utilization and capacity metrics at alternate site during extended operations
evidence_strength_indicators: Time-series operational-status dashboards showing recovery progression and resolution of blocking issues (strong)
Formal decision logs and change approvals authorizing extended relocation (strong)
Ad-hoc notes or unstructured emails without timestamps or formal decisions (weak)

affinity_keywords: Inability to Return to Primary Site

control_type_classification.reasoning: This control addresses sustained operations and decisions when the primary site cannot be returned to, which is an operational/corrective activity focused on response and sustainment.

CP-7(6).differentiation_note: Targets prolonged/unbounded failover conditions and sustainment metrics (time-in-failover, unresolved blockers), unlike siblings focused on initial readiness, separation, accessibility, or priority.

CP-8.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of telecommunications resilience: percent of critical telecommunications services with documented redundancy or alternate provider arrangements, count of outages with no alternate path, and average failover time during telecom incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive (ensure telecommunications services are available and backed by arrangements), so coverage/percentage metrics (coverage of redundancy/alternate arrangements) best show compliance; counts of outages and trending of failover times supplement coverage data.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Fraction of critical telecommunications services (by service or circuit) that have documented alternate paths or provider arrangements, along with counts of telecom incidents where no alternate service was available and measured failover times for those incidents.
data_signals: inventory of telecommunications services/circuits with criticality tag
documented provider arrangements / service agreement entries referencing alternate services
network/topology diagrams showing redundant paths or lack thereof
telecom outage incident records with timestamps and impacted circuit IDs
measured failover / switchover timestamps (start and completion) from incident logs
records of activation of alternate services (activation timestamps and provider IDs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory cross-referenced with provider agreements and machine-readable failover timestamps from incident/monitoring systems; Weak: one-off manual attestations or undocumented diagrams.
Strong: continuous monitoring of failover events and recorded activation timestamps; Weak: only anecdotal or single test reports without logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control aims to ensure telecommunications services are supported by redundancy and arrangements (prevention of service loss), consistent with preventive controls.

CP-8.differentiation_note: Broad infrastructure-level measurement of telecommunications coverage and failover capacity; siblings focus on specific aspects (priority, SPOF, provider separation, provider plans, and testing).

CP-8(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of critical communications/services with documented priority-of-service provisions in provider agreements and the count/rate of priority activations during incidents compared to expected SLA provisions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement is preventive (ensuring priority provisioning exists); percent coverage of services with priority clauses shows compliance, while activation rate/count of priority events validates operability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of listed critical telecommunications services that include documented priority-of-service provisions in provider contracts, plus counts of actual priority activations and whether activations met expected SLA behavior.
data_signals: provider contract excerpts or SLA clauses stating 'priority of service' or equivalent
service inventory entries flagged as having 'priority' provisioning
records of priority activation events with timestamps
incident records showing application of priority provisioning during outages or congestion
test results validating priority handling (if available)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable SLA clauses linked to service inventory and activation logs showing priority applied; Weak: legal contract references without recorded activations or test evidence.
Strong: repeated successful priority activations in tests or incidents; Weak: single manual attestations.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement requires arrangements and provisions (establishing priority), which is preventive by design; evidence includes contracts and activation logs.

CP-8(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on contractual/operational 'priority' arrangements and their activation, unlike other siblings that focus on redundancy, provider separation, or testing.

CP-8(2).measurement_goal: Measure the count and percentage of telecommunications components (circuits, routers, provider links) identified as single points of failure (SPOFs) and the remediation status/time-to-mitigate for each identified SPOF.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percentage of SPOFs mitigated (coverage) is the clearest indicator; raw counts and trends show remediation progress and remaining risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of critical telecom components with identified SPOF status that have mitigation implemented (redundant path/provider) and the number of outstanding SPOFs with open remediation tickets and remediation aging.
data_signals: inventory/list of identified single points of failure for telecom components
status fields for each SPOF (mitigated, planned, unmitigated)
remediation ticket IDs and timestamps (creation, update, closure)
network topology diagrams highlighting single versus redundant paths
incident records where an identified SPOF caused or contributed to an outage
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated SPOF inventory tied to topology data and closed remediation tickets with implementation evidence; Weak: informal lists or undocumented mitigations without topology confirmation.
Strong: correlation of resolved SPOFs with reduced incident counts; Weak: no linkage between SPOF inventory and incident history.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement requires identification and mitigation of SPOFs (preventative actions to avoid telecom outages).

CP-8(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures identification and remediation of single points of failure, unlike siblings that measure provider separation, contractual priority, contingency plans, or testing.

CP-8(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of critical telecommunications services with separation between primary and alternate providers (provider diversity) and count of services lacking provider separation per site/location.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percent coverage of provider separation shows whether alternate providers are in place; counts and distribution (e.g., by site) reveal concentration risks.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical circuits/sites that have documented separation of primary and alternate providers (distinct provider IDs and physically/route-diverse paths), plus counts of locations with single-provider dependency.
data_signals: mapping of service/circuit to primary provider and alternate provider IDs
evidence of physical/route separation (route diversity diagrams or provider statements)
service inventory annotated with 'primary' and 'alternate' provider fields
contracts or SLAs showing different providers assigned as primary/alternate
records of past failovers that used alternate providers (timestamps and provider IDs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: provider mapping that shows different provider IDs and route diversity documents plus successful alternate-provider failover events; Weak: claimed alternates in spreadsheets without contractual or topological proof.
Strong: consistent alternate-provider coverage across all critical sites; Weak: alternates only for a subset or only vendor-asserted (no documentation).

affinity_keywords: Separation of Primary and Alternate Providers

control_type_classification.reasoning: This enhancement prescribes separation/diversity between primary and alternate providers to prevent single-provider failure, a preventive control characteristic.

CP-8(3).differentiation_note: Targets provider diversity specifically (primary vs alternate) and requires provider-level mapping, unlike the SPOF enhancement which focuses on component redundancy within networks.

CP-8(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of telecommunications providers that maintain a documented contingency plan shared with the organization, and track the currency and last-test date of those plans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive/control-documented: percent of providers with documented contingency plans and recent validation is the clearest indicator; counts and trends show plan currency and coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of contracted telecommunications providers with a documented contingency plan on file (including contact lists and recovery steps), along with the last update/test date for each plan and any gaps noted.
data_signals: provider contingency plan documents on file (per provider)
document metadata: last updated date and version
provider contact lists and escalation procedures
evidence of plan distribution/acknowledgement between provider and organization
records of plan reviews or exercises (dates, outcomes)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed provider contingency plans with recent update dates and evidence they were shared/acknowledged; Weak: absence of dated documents or only high-level assurances without plans.
Strong: linked test/exercise records validating the plan; Weak: plans exist but have never been reviewed or exercised.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement requires providers to maintain contingency plans and share them (establishing preparedness), a preventive process.

CP-8(4).differentiation_note: Centers on contractual/documentary evidence from providers (plans and contact lists) rather than technical topologies or test execution data required by other siblings.

CP-8(5).measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and success rate of alternate telecommunications service failover tests (tests per period and percent of successful failovers), and distribution of measured failover times against acceptance criteria.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective/testing enhancement: success/failure rates of alternate-service tests indicate operational effectiveness; counts of tests and distribution of failover times provide additional granularity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Test execution frequency and pass/fail rate for alternate telecommunication service tests, including per-test measured failover start and completion timestamps to compute failover durations and compare to acceptance criteria.
data_signals: test schedule and planned tests for alternate telecommunications services
test execution logs with timestamps and test IDs
test outcome records (pass/fail) and remarks
failover switchover timestamps (start and completion) recorded during tests
post-test remediation or finding tickets generated from failed tests
evidence that tests used the documented alternate provider/path
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated test logs with timestamps and measured failover durations tied to test IDs and provider IDs; Weak: ad-hoc manual test reports or unlogged drills.
Strong: consistent periodic testing with high success rate and corrective action on failures; Weak: infrequent tests with unresolved failures.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly requires testing of alternate services (detecting whether alternate paths/providers operate as expected), a detective activity.

CP-8(5).differentiation_note: Focused on operational test execution metrics (frequency, pass rate, failover time) rather than contractual or topology evidence required by other controls.

CP-9.measurement_goal: Measure backup coverage and success across all systems (percent of systems with recent successful backups), volume/count of successful and failed backups, and time-based recovery readiness (RTO/RPO evidence) to demonstrate backups are performed and available when needed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: CP-9 is primarily preventive (ensuring backups exist and meet policy); percent coverage of assets and percent successful backups show preventive coverage, while counts and trends provide operational detail (backup volume, failures over time).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of production systems with at least one successful backup within the policy window, counts of backup executions and failures by system, backup retention location mapping, and measured recovery time objectives demonstrated via restore logs and restore timestamps.
data_signals: backup execution logs with timestamps and status (success/failure)
asset-to-backup-job mapping / backup inventory
last-successful-backup timestamp per asset
backup retention location metadata (on-site/off-site/alternate site)
restore test timestamps and results (if available)
backup size and frequency records
backup failure error codes and reason fields
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated backup execution logs showing continuous successful runs with timestamps (strong).
Comprehensive asset mapping demonstrating every production system mapped to a backup job (strong).
Ad-hoc manual screenshots or one-off reports without logs (weak).
Missing timestamps or only monthly summary reports (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'System Backup' indicates preventive measures to ensure backups exist and are available.

CP-9.differentiation_note: This top-level control covers overall backup operations and coverage; sibling controls focus on specific practices (testing, encryption, storage separation, authorization).

CP-9(1).measurement_goal: Measure the rate and outcomes of scheduled backup reliability and integrity tests: percent of scheduled integrity tests completed, count of integrity failures, and time-to-remediate integrity issues discovered by tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is detective (testing uncovers integrity/reliability issues); test pass/fail rates and counts of integrity failures are most useful, with percentages showing pass coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate of completed integrity/reliability tests per schedule, pass/fail outcomes per test and per backup job, timestamps of test execution, and remediation/ticket timestamps for failed tests.
data_signals: backup integrity test execution logs (hash/checksum verification results) with timestamps
test pass/fail status per backup job
scheduled-test calendar vs actual execution records
remediation ticket creation and closure timestamps tied to failed tests
integrity check output (checksums, verification logs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated integrity test logs (checksums, verification results) with linked remediation tickets and closure (strong).
Manual one-off test reports without timestamps or remediation linkage (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Testing for Reliability and Integrity' indicates tests that detect backup reliability/integrity issues rather than preventing them.

CP-9(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on integrity/reliability testing outcomes and remediation timelines rather than general backup coverage or encryption.

CP-9(2).measurement_goal: Measure restoration test success from sampled backups: percent of sampled restores completed successfully, count of sampled restore attempts, and time to detect and remediate failed sample restores.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control (restore testing reveals failures). Restore success/failure rates and counts of sampled restore attempts best evidence effectiveness, with trends showing reliability over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of scheduled sampled restores executed and successful (restore success rate), list of sample selection criteria, timestamps of restore starts/completions, and remediation records for failed restores.
data_signals: sampled restore execution logs with start/finish timestamps and status
restore test selection/sampling records
restore verification outputs (application/data validation)
failed restore error logs
remediation/action tickets linked to failed sampled restores
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated sampled restore logs showing verification steps and successful data validation across multiple sampling cycles (strong).
One-off manual restore notes without sampling records or verification evidence (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly describes testing restorations using sampling, which detects restore capability issues.

CP-9(2).differentiation_note: Specifically requires sampled restore test evidence and pass/fail rates rather than overall backup execution metrics.

CP-9(3).measurement_goal: Measure coverage of critical information stored in separate backup storage (percentage of identified critical data sets with backups stored in designated separate locations) and proof of physical/logical separation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires measuring coverage (percent of critical information placed in separate storage). Percent coverage shows compliance with the separation requirement; counts and comparisons validate mapping.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of tagged critical information/data sets whose backups exist in separate (designated) storage locations; mapping of critical information to storage location; configuration records showing separate storage identifiers and access controls.
data_signals: inventory of critical information/data sets
backup storage location metadata (separate storage identifiers)
data set-to-backup-location mapping records
access control lists for separate storage
storage provisioning/configuration records showing logical/physical separation
evidence_strength_indicators: Comprehensive inventory showing all critical information mapped to separate storage with configuration records (strong).
Partial lists or undocumented manual processes without storage mapping (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Separate Storage for Critical Information' is a preventive design to keep critical backups isolated from primary systems.

CP-9(3).differentiation_note: Uniquely requires mapping critical data sets to separately provisioned storage locations rather than general backup success metrics.

CP-9(4).measurement_goal: Although withdrawn and incorporated into CP-9, measure controls that prevent unauthorized modification of backups: percent of backups protected by write-once/immutable settings or access controls and count of unauthorized modification attempts detected.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Protection is preventive — percent of backups with immutable/write-protected configuration and percent of backup stores with enforced access controls show coverage; counts capture detected modification attempts.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of backup stores configured with immutability or explicit write/prohibit settings, number of access control exceptions for backup modification, and logs of attempted modifications with authorization outcomes.
data_signals: backup storage immutability configuration records (WORM, immutability flags)
access control policies for backup stores
audit logs showing attempted modifications or writes to backup objects
authorization/approval logs for modification events
change/configuration records showing incorporation into CP-9
evidence_strength_indicators: Immutable storage configuration and continuous audit logs showing no unauthorized write events (strong).
Policy statements without configuration evidence or without audit logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The wording 'Protection from Unauthorized Modification' implies preventive controls; confidence lowered because text notes it was withdrawn and incorporated into CP-9.

CP-9(4).differentiation_note: Although withdrawn, this entry focuses on immutability/access controls for backups — a configuration/authorization measurement need distinct from testing or storage location.

CP-9(5).measurement_goal: Measure successful transfer and availability of backups at alternate storage sites: percent of scheduled transfers completed successfully, count of failed transfers, and confirmation of accessibility at the alternate site.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive measure, percent of successful transfers to alternate sites indicates coverage; counts and trends provide operational visibility into transfer reliability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of scheduled backup transfers to designated alternate storage sites completed successfully within the expected window, transfer logs (start/finish/status), destination accessibility checks, and any transfer failure reasons.
data_signals: backup transfer job logs with timestamps and status
alternate site storage receipt/ingest logs
bandwidth/transfer error logs
transfer schedule vs actual execution records
accessibility/health checks at alternate site
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated transfer logs plus destination receipt confirmations and accessibility checks (strong).
Manual confirmation emails without transfer logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Transfer to Alternate Storage Site' prescribes preventive actions to ensure backups are replicated to alternate locations.

CP-9(5).differentiation_note: Requires measurement of transfer job success and destination accessibility specifically for alternate-site replication, distinct from local backup success or restore testing.

CP-9(6).measurement_goal: Measure redundancy and availability of secondary backup systems: percent of critical backups replicated to redundant secondary systems, uptime/availability of secondary backup systems, and failover test success rate.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive redundancy control, percent coverage of backups replicated to redundant systems shows protective coverage; trends and counts support operational availability analysis.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical backup data replicated to a configured redundant secondary system, secondary system availability/uptime metrics, replication lag times, and failover/fallback test results.
data_signals: replication job logs with timestamps and status
secondary system availability/uptime monitoring
replication lag metrics
failover/fallback test execution logs and outcomes
configuration records showing redundant secondary system assignments
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous replication logs, low/acceptable replication lag, and successful failover test records (strong).
Documented architecture diagrams without operational replication/uptime logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control 'Redundant Secondary System' is preventive by design to ensure backups exist in a redundant system.

CP-9(6).differentiation_note: Emphasizes measurement of replication coverage, replication lag and secondary system availability rather than single-site backup success or testing.

CP-9(7).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement of dual-authorization for backup deletions/destructions: percent of deletion/destruction events that required and recorded dual authorization, and count of deletion events executed with single or missing authorization.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by percent enforcement (how many deletion events complied) with counts of noncompliant events for incident follow-up.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of backup deletion/destruction operations that included two distinct authorized approvers logged, event logs with approver identities and timestamps, and counts of deletion events without dual authorization.
data_signals: backup deletion/destruction event logs with approver IDs and timestamps
approval workflow records showing two distinct approvals
change request or ticket records tied to deletion events
exceptions/override logs for deletion operations
audit logs showing deletion commands and resulting object states
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated approval workflow records where two distinct approvers are required and recorded for each deletion event (strong).
Ad-hoc emails or undocumented verbal approvals without logs (weak).

affinity_keywords: Dual Authorization for Deletion or Destruction

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement for 'Dual Authorization' for deletion/destruction is a preventive access-control mechanism to avoid unauthorized data removal.

CP-9(7).differentiation_note: Unique focus on approval workflow evidence and enforcement for destructive actions against backups, unlike other controls centered on creation, transfer, or encryption.

CP-9(8).measurement_goal: Measure cryptographic protection of backups: percentage of backups encrypted at rest and in transit, counts of backup objects missing cryptographic protection, and key management configuration evidence.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Cryptographic protection is preventive; percent of backups encrypted at rest/in transit indicates coverage, while counts of unencrypted backups and key-management scores add detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of backup objects with encryption at rest enabled (and encryption-in-transit for transfers), list/count of unencrypted backups, cryptographic algorithm metadata, and key management usage logs/configuration.
data_signals: backup object metadata indicating encryption-at-rest flag and algorithm
transfer logs showing TLS/SSH usage for in-transit transfers
key management system (KMS) usage logs (key creation, rotation, access)
inventory of backup objects with encryption status
configuration records for encryption policies applied to backup storage
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated backup metadata showing encryption flags plus KMS logs demonstrating proper key usage and rotation (strong).
Policy statements about encryption without object-level metadata or KMS logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The term 'Cryptographic Protection' implies preventive encryption controls for stored and transferred backups.

CP-9(8).differentiation_note: Specifically requires cryptographic and key-management evidence for backups (encryption flags, KMS logs), distinct from testing or transfer/replication metrics.

CP-10.measurement_goal: Measure the success and timeliness of system recovery and reconstitution activities: percent of critical system components successfully restored and reconstituted, mean/median time-to-recover per incident/test, and recovery test pass-rate over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Recovery and reconstitution are corrective activities where rates (e.g., recovery events per period, success rate) and percentages (percent of restores meeting target) best show effectiveness and adherence to recovery objectives.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count recovery executions (tests and real incidents) and derive time-to-recover per event; compute percent of critical components restored successfully and percent of recovery tests that pass acceptance criteria. Include mapping of recovered component identifiers to recovery plans and timestamps for start/end of recovery operations.
data_signals: recovery test execution records with start and end timestamps
incident recovery operation logs with component identifiers
reconstitution completion status per system component
recovery test pass/fail results and after-action reports
backup/snapshot availability and successful restore logs
post-recovery integrity checks or validation logs
recovery runbook version referenced during execution
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated recovery test logs with start/end timestamps and machine-readable pass/fail results are strong evidence
Continuous logging of recovery operations across all critical components is strong; periodic manual-only reports are weak
Mapping of each recovered component to a documented recovery plan strengthens evidence; undocumented or adhoc restores weaken it

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on recovery and reconstitution activities (corrective actions taken after disruption) as indicated by 'System Recovery and Reconstitution'.

CP-10.differentiation_note: This base control covers overall system-level recovery measurement (time, success rate, reconstitution completeness) rather than the specific enhancements like transaction recovery or component protection.

CP-10(1).measurement_goal: Demonstrate that contingency plan testing is executed and tracked: count of contingency plan tests per period, percent of tests completed per schedule, and percent of tests that meet acceptance criteria or have documented remediation actions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Testing effectiveness is best represented by test pass-rate (percentage) and test counts to show coverage and regularity; this control explicitly references testing activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and timestamp each contingency plan test, record test scope and systems exercised, capture pass/fail outcome and list of remediation actions with closure timestamps, and compare completed tests against scheduled tests per policy.
data_signals: contingency plan test schedule and execution records
test scope documentation listing systems/components tested
test results (pass/fail) and acceptance criteria
after-action reports and remediation tracking items
test participant and approver sign-offs
evidence of incorporation into CP-4 (where applicable)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, timestamped test execution records and after-action remediation logs are strong evidence
Tabletop-only summaries without recorded outcomes or remediation tracking are weak
Linkage of test results to documented plan revisions or CP-4 evidence strengthens the claim; absence of linkage weakens it

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement addresses testing of contingency plans (corrective readiness); however, the control text notes it was withdrawn and incorporated into CP-4, reducing direct applicability confidence.

CP-10(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement is specifically about testing the contingency plan and tracking test outcomes and remediation (and has been withdrawn/incorporated into CP-4), unlike sibling entries that focus on execution of recovery or component-level protections.

CP-10(2).measurement_goal: Measure transaction recovery effectiveness: count of transactions successfully recovered/replayed, percent of transactions recovered intact, and time-to-recover transactional state per incident/test.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Transaction recovery is an operational corrective activity where rates (recovery events, successful replay rate) and counts (transactions recovered) show effectiveness and fidelity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count transactional recovery operations and measure recovered transaction volume and success status; capture recovery start/end timestamps, transaction sequence or ledger positions restored, and integrity/validation results for recovered transactions.
data_signals: transaction log replay execution records with timestamps
number of transactions recovered per event
transaction reconciliation reports showing matched/unmatched counts
transaction integrity validation checksums or hashes
backup transaction log availability and restore success logs
error/exception logs during transaction replay
evidence_strength_indicators: Detailed transaction replay logs with sequence numbers and reconciliation reports are strong evidence
Manual estimates of recovered transaction counts without logs are weak
Automated integrity/hash verification for each recovered transaction strengthens evidence; lack of verification weakens it

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement focuses on restoring transactional data/state after disruption, an inherently corrective activity (transaction recovery/replay).

CP-10(2).differentiation_note: This entry targets transactional state recovery and integrity (transaction counts, replay logs, reconciliation), distinct from system-level restore timing or component hardening.

CP-10(3).measurement_goal: Demonstrate presence and effectiveness of compensating security controls used during recovery: percent of required compensating controls implemented for systems lacking primary recovery controls and test evidence showing compensating controls operated as intended.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Compensating controls are typically assessed by coverage and effectiveness (percentage of required compensating controls in place) and scored testing results; percentage and score best reflect coverage and effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Inventory compensating controls mapped to systems lacking primary recovery controls, record implementation status and test outcomes, and measure percent of compensating controls with successful test evidence and management approvals.
data_signals: compensating control inventory with implementation status
control test/execution results and timestamps
risk acceptance or mitigation approval records
scope mapping linking compensating controls to affected systems
periodic review/assessment reports of compensating controls
evidence of compensating control operation during a recovery event
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-readable inventory that maps compensating controls to affected systems and automated test logs are strong evidence
Only documented compensating controls without operational test evidence are weak
Formal risk acceptance records and periodic reassessments strengthen evidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly refers to 'Compensating Security Controls', indicating alternative controls used when primary recovery controls are not available (compensating classification).

CP-10(3).differentiation_note: This enhancement is unique because it measures alternative/compensating controls' coverage and test evidence rather than primary recovery execution metrics.

CP-10(4).measurement_goal: Measure compliance with restore time objectives: percent of restores completed within defined time periods (RTO), count of restores that exceeded the time period, and distribution of restore durations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The enhancement specifies restoring within a time period, so percentage of restores meeting the time target and distributions of restore durations best demonstrate SLA compliance and timeliness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each restore (test or incident) capture start and end timestamps, map to the defined time-period target, compute percent of events meeting the target, count overdue restores, and produce distribution (histogram) of restore durations by system criticality.
data_signals: restore operation start and end timestamps
defined time-period targets (per system or criticality tier)
record of system criticality or tier
overdue restore incident tickets and root cause notes
pass/fail acceptance for each restore
trend of restore durations over reporting periods
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated timestamps for each restore with linkage to policy-defined time targets are strong evidence
Ad-hoc or undocumented timing estimates are weak
Consistent trend data showing improvement or regression over multiple periods strengthens auditor confidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: This enhancement establishes requirements to restore systems within a time period (a corrective objective measuring recovery timeliness).

CP-10(4).differentiation_note: This enhancement is narrowly focused on timeliness metrics (percent meeting restore time targets and duration distributions), unlike siblings that emphasize transaction integrity or component protections.

CP-10(5).measurement_goal: Demonstrate failover capability readiness: percent of required systems with tested failover capability, failover test pass-rate, and time-to-failover measurements from detection to service continuity.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Failover capability is typically validated by the percentage of systems with a tested and functioning failover and by test pass-rates; percentage and rate metrics reflect readiness and operational success.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Record failover test executions and results per system, capture time-to-failover per event, track configuration/state replication health, and measure percent of systems with successful automated failover tests against expected capability list.
data_signals: failover test execution logs with start/end timestamps
automated failover execution success/failure results
replication/health-check metrics for primary and standby systems
configuration of failover mechanisms and documented test plans
time-to-failover (detection -> failover complete) measurements
evidence noting incorporation into SI-13 (withdrawn reference)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated failover test logs with measured time-to-failover and machine-readable success indicators are strong evidence
Tabletop descriptions of failover without executed failovers are weak
Consistent pass-rate over repeated tests strengthens evidence; single successful test is weaker

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement concerns executing failover to maintain continuity (a corrective capability); the confidence is reduced by the control's 'Withdrawn' note referencing SI-13.

CP-10(5).differentiation_note: This enhancement is focused on failover readiness and measured time-to-failover; it is distinct from transactional replay or component protection and is noted as withdrawn/incorporated elsewhere.

CP-10(6).measurement_goal: Measure protection and security posture of components during and after recovery: percent of restored components that meet configuration/hardening baselines, count of components requiring remediation post-restore, and vulnerability scan results post-recovery.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Protection of components is best shown as percentage compliance with protection baselines (hardening/configuration), plus counts of deviations and distributions of vulnerability severity post-recovery.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each component restored, capture configuration baseline comparison results, post-recovery vulnerability scan findings, whether security controls (e.g., agent, firewall rules) were enabled after restore, and count of components failing protection checks requiring remediation.
data_signals: post-restore configuration baseline comparison reports
vulnerability scan results (post-recovery) per component
security control activation logs (agents, firewall, EDR)
component inventory with restore timestamps
remediation tickets opened for components failing protection checks
hardening checklist conformance evidence
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated post-restore configuration scans and vulnerability scans per component are strong evidence
Manual sign-offs without machine-readable scan results are weak
Low count of remediation tickets and high percent baseline-conformant components strengthen evidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement focuses on ensuring components are protected (configured/hardened) during/after recovery, which is a preventive posture to avoid re-introducing vulnerabilities.

CP-10(6).differentiation_note: This enhancement is unique in measuring security posture of individual components after recovery (baseline conformance and vulnerability status), rather than system-level restore timeliness or transactional integrity.

CP-11.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of mission‑critical systems and stakeholders that have a documented, current alternate communications protocol and the count and outcomes of tests/exercises that demonstrate successful switch-over to those protocols within defined target times.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (ensuring alternate communications exist and cover required assets), so coverage percentage is most useful; counts of tests and trends in test outcomes/mean switch time are useful secondary measures to show operational readiness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of mission‑critical assets and stakeholders mapped to a documented alternate communications protocol, the percentage of those with a most‑recent test within the policy window, and measured switch‑over durations from primary to alternate protocol per test/event. Include test results (success/failure), timestamps, and procedure versioning so auditors can verify currency and effectiveness.
data_signals: asset inventory with criticality classification and 'alternate_communications_protocol' field
documented alternate communications procedures with version and last_modified timestamp
disaster recovery / contingency test execution logs (date, participants, outcome, notes)
failover/switch‑over event logs with timestamps and measured time-to-switch
network/device configuration snapshots showing alternate protocol enabled (and change history)
communications vendor/third‑party SLA and contact escalation records
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated test execution logs and measured switch durations with timestamps (strong)
Successful operational failover event logs that match documented procedure (strong)
Only tabletop exercise minutes or undated procedures without timestamps (weak)
Procedures present but no recorded tests or no mapping to asset inventory (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title (Alternate Communications Protocols) implies establishing preventative measures (alternate channels/protocols) to ensure communications continuity rather than detecting or correcting incidents.

CP-11.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on existence, coverage and testability of alternate communications protocols (documentation, mapping to assets, and test outcomes), unlike sibling contingent planning controls that may focus on data recovery or alternate processing sites; note: control description was not provided so keywords and signals are derived from the control name and common contingency testing expectations.

CP-12.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of critical systems configured to support a documented safe (minimal-functionality) mode, the frequency and results of safe-mode readiness tests, and the time and authorization trail to place a system into safe mode during an event.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (ensuring systems can enter a safe/minimal mode) so percentage metrics (coverage of systems configured and tested) best show compliance; counts and trends are useful to show test frequency and historical readiness, and rates show frequency of safe-mode activations or authorized entries.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of assets in the critical asset inventory that have safe-mode configuration present and compliant with the safe-mode policy; timestamps and outcomes of periodic safe-mode readiness tests per asset; access/authorization logs showing who initiated safe-mode transitions and associated change/incident tickets; time from declared need to successful safe-mode activation for exercised events.
data_signals: asset inventory entries flagged as 'safe mode supported' with configuration version
safe-mode configuration files or registry settings (per asset)
scheduled safe-mode test execution records and test outcome (pass/fail) with timestamps
safe-mode activation event logs with initiator identity and timestamp
change/authorization requests or incident tickets linked to safe-mode activations
time-to-safe-mode metrics (activation duration) per event
exceptions or mitigation plans for assets lacking safe-mode capability
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated configuration management records showing safe-mode settings across assets plus scheduled test logs and activation events tied to change/incident tickets; coverage >= policy threshold and regular passing tests.
Weak: one-time documentation or screenshots, ad-hoc manual tests without logs, or unlinked change requests that don't show activation timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Safe Mode' implies configuration and readiness to place systems into a limited-functionality state to prevent escalation, which is primarily preventive in nature.

CP-12.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets configuration, testing, and authorized activation of a system 'safe mode' (degraded/minimal functionality) rather than general backup, recovery, or full incident response processes, so measurements must focus on safe-mode support, test coverage, activation logs, and activation latency.

CP-13.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of critical systems and services that have documented, approved alternative security mechanisms and the frequency and success rate of tests or activations of those alternatives (e.g., failovers, temporary controls) against organizational policy requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive/continuity-focused so auditors need coverage/percentage metrics (percent of critical assets with alternatives). Counts of documented alternative mechanisms and trend metrics for periodic testing provide complementary evidence of maintenance and effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical assets/services with a documented and approved alternative security mechanism, plus count and pass-rate of tests/activations of those alternatives over the last N periods. Include asset identifier, mechanism type, approval timestamp, test execution timestamp, and test outcome to allow SLA/coverage calculations.
data_signals: inventory of critical systems/services (asset IDs and criticality tier)
documented alternative mechanism records (mechanism type, owner, approval date)
test execution logs for alternative mechanisms (timestamp, initiator, mechanism activated)
test results and outcomes (pass/fail, issues found, remediation actions)
failover/activation incident logs (time to activate, duration, manual vs automated)
change/approval records referencing alternative mechanism implementation
