policy mapping showing required coverage or exceptions (policy_threshold or approved exceptions)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated test execution logs and successful failover activations with timestamps and pass/fail outcomes across all critical assets on a regular schedule
Moderate: recent documented test reports and signed approvals but limited automation or inconsistent execution frequency
Weak: only one-off documentation or design diagrams without test execution logs or activation evidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing alternative mechanisms to prevent loss of security when primary controls are unavailable, so it functions as a preventive/continuity control.

CP-13.differentiation_note: Unlike sibling controls that measure primary control coverage or remediation, this control specifically requires measurement of documented fallback mechanisms and the testing/activation evidence for those alternatives (readiness and tested effectiveness).

IA-1.measurement_goal: Show that identity and access policies and procedures exist, are approved and owned, are distributed to affected personnel, and are reviewed/updated on the required schedule; specifically measure percent of IA policies current within review period, count of policies with an assigned owner, and percentage of staff who have acknowledged applicable procedures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control where coverage and currency are key (percent current, percent acknowledged). Counts and trending help show inventory and review cadence; a score can combine multiple policy hygiene signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Inventory of identity & access policy/procedure documents with version and last-review timestamp; for each document capture assigned owner, approval record, dissemination/acknowledgement evidence, and review schedule. Produce metrics: percent of IA policies reviewed within required period, count of policies without an owner, and percent of impacted personnel who have submitted procedural acknowledgements.
data_signals: policy/procedure document inventory with unique id, version, and last_review_timestamp
document approval records (approver id, approval timestamp)
document owner assignment fields (owner id/team)
distribution or access logs showing dissemination (email sends, document access events)
employee acknowledgment/attestation records with timestamps
policy review schedule entries and evidence of completed reviews
procedure implementation checklists or conformance assessment reports
training completion records mapped to specific procedures/policies
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable policy inventory with version history in a document management system, automated review reminders, and recorded employee acknowledgements or LMS completion tied to specific procedures.
Weak: a single static PDF on a shared drive with no version history, or infrequent manual attestations without timestamps tied to policies.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Establishing and maintaining policies and procedures is a preventive governance activity (the control name specifies 'Policy and Procedures').

IA-1.differentiation_note: Unlike technical access controls, this entry focuses on documentation, ownership, distribution, and review cadence metrics (policy hygiene and acknowledgement) rather than authentication events or access enforcement telemetry; limitation: control description was empty so keywords derive from the control name and standard policy lifecycle terms.

IA-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of organizational user accounts covered by required authentication controls (per policy), plus counts of authentication attempts by method and counts of failed/unauthorized authentication events to demonstrate enforcement and coverage.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive authentication control where auditors want coverage metrics (percentage of accounts protected) as primary evidence; counts and rates of auth events and trends over time help demonstrate enforcement and detection of exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of organizational user accounts with required authentication configuration (e.g., required auth methods recorded in directory), plus counts of authentication events by method (successful, failed, blocked) and the list/count of accounts without required authentication.
data_signals: user account records with configured authentication method
authentication event logs (timestamp, user, method, result)
enrollment status records for stronger authentication methods
privileged vs non-privileged account attribute in directory
local versus remote login indicators in auth logs
account provisioning and deprovisioning timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated directory records and continuous auth logs showing method enforcement (strong)
Periodic manual attestations or one-off screenshots without logs (weak)
Trend of decreasing failed/unauthorized auth attempts combined with full coverage (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes identification and authentication requirements for organizational users and is intended to prevent unauthorized access.

IA-2.differentiation_note: This is the high-level IA-2 control covering overall authentication requirements and coverage for organizational users; its measurements focus on population coverage and auth event volumes rather than device- or scenario-specific enhancements.

IA-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of privileged/local-access accounts that are subject to the IA-2(1) enhancement requirements (as implemented) and count local privileged authentication events to show enforcement.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As an IA-2 enhancement this is preventive (coverage of privileged/local accounts); percentage coverage is primary while counts/trends of auth events validate enforcement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of privileged and local-access accounts configured to meet the IA-2(1) enhancement (per policy), and counts of local privileged login attempts (success/failure) to demonstrate enforcement and exceptions.
data_signals: list of privileged accounts and classification flag
configuration records showing required auth method for privileged accounts
local login event logs for privileged accounts
authentication success/failure counts for local privileged logins
policy or control mapping showing which accounts are in scope
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated config records and continuous local-auth logs for privileged accounts (strong)
Ad-hoc spreadsheets or manual lists without event logs (weak)
Show of decreasing exception counts alongside full coverage (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: IA-2(1) is an enhancement addressing local/privileged account authentication requirements and is intended to prevent unauthorized local privileged access.

IA-2(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement narrows IA-2 to privileged/local-access accounts so measurement must focus on privileged account population and local login events rather than the general user population.

IA-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of non-privileged local accounts that comply with IA-2(2) authentication enhancements and count relevant local authentication events and failures for that population.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive enhancement focused on coverage for non-privileged local accounts; percentage coverage is primary, counts/trends of auth events provide validation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of non-privileged local accounts configured per IA-2(2) enhancement and counts of local authentication events (success/fail) for that set of accounts to show enforcement and exceptions.
data_signals: directory attribute marking non-privileged local accounts
authentication method configuration for those accounts
local login event logs with account classification
failed local authentication attempt counts by account
exceptions or waiver records for non-compliant accounts
evidence_strength_indicators: System-recorded account attributes and continuous local auth logs (strong)
One-time manual attestations or missing logs (weak)
Trend of declining exceptions together with high coverage (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: IA-2(2) targets authentication requirements for non-privileged local accounts and therefore functions as a preventive coverage control.

IA-2(2).differentiation_note: Differs from IA-2(1) by focusing measurement on non-privileged local accounts rather than privileged accounts; requires account classification filtering in metrics.

IA-2(3).measurement_goal: For a withdrawn enhancement, measure and document that the IA-2(3) requirements have been formally withdrawn and that its requirements are incorporated into IA-2(1) (policy mapping and control traceability).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Evidence that an enhancement is withdrawn is best shown by counts and mappings (policy documents, mappings) and comparisons to the incorporating control; a score or comparison shows completeness of incorporation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and traceability of policy/documentation artifacts showing IA-2(3) is marked withdrawn and mapped to IA-2(1), and presence of updated control implementation records referencing IA-2(1).
data_signals: policy/control revision records indicating 'Withdrawn'
control mapping documents showing 'incorporated into IA-2(1)'
change management/approval records for the withdrawal
updated implementation or procedure documents referencing IA-2(1)
evidence_strength_indicators: Official control baseline documentation and approved change records showing incorporation (strong)
Informal notes or unlabeled spreadsheets with no approvals (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The text states 'Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-2(1)', so it no longer operates as a standalone preventive enhancement; evidence is governance/traceability.

IA-2(3).differentiation_note: This entry addresses withdrawal and documentation of incorporation into IA-2(1) rather than technical authentication enforcement — measurement is governance/mapping focused.

IA-2(4).measurement_goal: Demonstrate that IA-2(4) has been withdrawn and that its content has been incorporated into IA-2(2) by producing policy mapping and change records.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Withdrawn enhancements are evidenced via document counts and mapping comparisons; counts of artifacts and mapping completeness are primary indicators.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of governance artifacts that show IA-2(4) marked 'Withdrawn' and mapping entries indicating incorporation into IA-2(2), plus change approval records.
data_signals: policy revision entries showing 'Withdrawn'
control mapping documents referencing IA-2(2)
change management approval logs
updated procedure or implementation documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Signed change approvals and updated baseline control documents (strong)
Unapproved or draft notes indicating withdrawal (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text explicitly marks this enhancement as withdrawn and incorporated into IA-2(2); measurement is governance-focused rather than preventive technical control evidence.

IA-2(4).differentiation_note: Focuses on verifying policy/control lifecycle actions (withdrawal and incorporation) rather than direct authentication enforcement metrics.

IA-2(5).measurement_goal: Measure percentage coverage and counts for whatever IA-2(5) enhancement requires in the implementation (track configured accounts in scope and the enforcement event counts) — treat as an additional authentication enhancement with coverage and enforcement metrics.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As an IA-2 enhancement it is preventive; coverage percentage is primary while counts and trends validate enforcement and exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of accounts and systems configured per the IA-2(5) enhancement (per policy) and counts of related authentication events to show enforcement and exceptions.
data_signals: policy or enhancement specification for IA-2(5)
system configuration records showing applied enhancement
authentication event logs correlated to systems/accounts in scope
exception/waiver records for accounts not compliant
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated configuration records and continuous auth logs tied to the enhancement (strong)
Only policy text without implementation evidence (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: IA-2(5) is an enhancement to IA-2 and thus intended to prevent improper authentication through additional requirements.

IA-2(5).differentiation_note: Undefined in input, so this entry treats IA-2(5) generically as an enhancement requiring coverage and enforcement evidence rather than a specific scenario.

IA-2(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of account accesses that require use of a separate device as specified by IA-2(6), plus counts of accesses where separate-device assertions are missing or bypassed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive enhancement is about enforcing use of a separate device; percentage coverage of configured separate-device access controls is primary, with counts of bypasses/exceptions as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of in-scope accounts/systems configured to require a separate device for access (device-binding entries) and counts of access events where the separate-device requirement was satisfied vs bypassed (with device identifier present/absent).
data_signals: device enrollment/registration records (device IDs linked to user accounts)
access event logs with device identifier and source type
policy/configuration records requiring separate-device access
exceptions or waiver logs for separate-device requirement bypasses
authentication method details showing device-based step
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous access logs with device identifiers and enforcement flags (strong)
Manual attestations without device-linked logs (weak)
Low bypass counts and high registered-device coverage (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: IA-2(6) prescribes an access restriction (use of a separate device) to prevent unauthorized access and therefore is preventive.

IA-2(6).differentiation_note: Specifically measures device-bound access enforcement (device enrollment and device-present logging), unlike other IA-2 enhancements that focus on account classification or local access.

IA-2(7).measurement_goal: Show that IA-2(7) is withdrawn and incorporated into IA-2(6) by providing governance artifacts and updated control mappings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Withdrawn enhancement evidence is governance-oriented: counts of policy artifacts and comparisons showing incorporation into IA-2(6) are most useful.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of formal documentation entries indicating IA-2(7) marked 'Withdrawn' and mapped to IA-2(6), plus updated procedure/control implementation documents referencing IA-2(6).
data_signals: policy/control baseline revisions citing 'Withdrawn'
control mapping documents referencing IA-2(6)
change management approvals and timestamps
updated implementation or procedural references
evidence_strength_indicators: Approved change records and updated baseline documents (strong)
Draft notes lacking approvals (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Explicit 'Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-2(6)' indicates governance/traceability evidence rather than an active preventive control.

IA-2(7).differentiation_note: Governance/tracing entry: verifies that the withdrawn enhancement has been assimilated into IA-2(6), unlike live enhancements which require technical enforcement metrics.

IA-2(8).measurement_goal: Measure percentage of account accesses using replay-resistant authentication mechanisms and count authentication attempts flagged/rejected as replay attacks to demonstrate replay-resistance enforcement.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive enhancement requires coverage measurement (percentage of accesses using replay-resistant methods) with counts/rates of replay-detection/rejection events to validate effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of in-scope systems and accounts configured to require replay-resistant mechanisms, counts of authentication attempts flagged as replay/replay-rejected, and counts of successful authentications using replay-resistant proofs.
data_signals: system configuration indicating replay-resistant auth enabled
authentication logs with replay-detection flags and rejection codes
counts of replay-rejected authentication attempts (timestamped)
device/session nonces and timestamps correlated to auth events
policy/specification for replay-resistant requirements
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous auth logs with replay-detection flags and nonces/timestamps (strong)
No replay-detection fields in logs or only periodic tests (weak)
Declining replay-rejection incidents after enforcement (strong)

control_type_classification.reasoning: IA-2(8) requires use of replay-resistant authentication methods to prevent replay attacks, a preventive technical enforcement control.

IA-2(8).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on replay-resistance — metrics need cryptographic/auth-log fields (nonces, flags) and replay-rejection counts rather than general auth coverage.

IA-2(9).measurement_goal: Provide evidence that IA-2(9) has been withdrawn and incorporated into IA-2(8) by producing governance documentation and mapping artifacts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Demonstrating withdrawal/incorporation is best done with counts of governance artifacts and comparison of mappings; a completeness score may be useful.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of authoritative documents showing IA-2(9) marked 'Withdrawn' and mapped to IA-2(8), plus change approval logs and updated control baselines.
data_signals: policy revision records noting 'Withdrawn'
control mapping/migration documents referencing IA-2(8)
change management approvals and timestamps
updated procedural/implementation documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Approved baseline documents and mapped control implementation references (strong)
Unapproved notes or missing mapping artifacts (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Labelled 'Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-2(8)', so primary evidence is governance mapping rather than an operational preventive control.

IA-2(9).differentiation_note: This is a governance/mapping entry confirming withdrawal and incorporation into IA-2(8) rather than a live technical control; measurement focuses on documentation completeness.

IA-2(10).measurement_goal: Measure SSO deployment and enforcement across organizational users by tracking percentage of applications integrated with approved SSO, percentage of organizational user authentications via the corporate SSO, and counts of SSO-related authentication failures or assertion errors.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and enforcement (SSO enabled/configured across apps)—percentage best shows coverage; counts/rates show event volumes and error rates for operational health and trending for adoption over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of enterprise applications configured to use the approved SSO provider, proportion of organizational user authentications performed via SSO vs non-SSO, SSO authentication failure/error counts and timestamps, and SSO configuration validation (IdP metadata validity) per application.
data_signals: list of applications with SSO integration flag and integration timestamp
SSO authentication event logs (timestamp, user_id, application_id, result)
SSO assertion/response error logs (SAML/OIDC error codes and timestamps)
IdP/SSO metadata validation results and last-validated timestamp
percentage of active users authenticating via SSO per period
SSO session token lifetimes and revocation events
SSO configuration change audit entries (who/when/what)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated SSO integration inventory plus continuous SSO authentication logs = strong evidence
Periodic manual checklists or one-off screenshots = weak evidence
Cryptographic validation of IdP metadata and automated assertion error parsing = strong
Ad-hoc or sampled reports without raw authentication logs = weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates use of Single Sign-on for organizational users (a configuration/enforcement activity), which is preventive by design.

IA-2(10).differentiation_note: This control specifically targets centralized SSO integration and operational monitoring of SSO flows, unlike sibling IA controls that address other authentication mechanisms (PIV, out-of-band).

IA-2(11).measurement_goal: For historical/trace purposes, measure enforcement and documentation that remote access from a separate device was required and validated: the percentage of remote access sessions originating from validated separate devices and counts of policy exceptions or overrides.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control (remote access from separate devices), coverage and enforcement percentages show whether sessions comply; counts of non-compliant sessions show exceptions and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of remote access sessions that present validated separate-device identifiers or attestation evidence, count of remote sessions blocked for failing separate-device checks, and records of exceptions or approvals tied to specific sessions and users.
data_signals: remote access session logs with device_type/device_id and source indicators
device attestation/posture check results (pass/fail, timestamp)
VPN/remote-access policy enforcement logs (blocked/allowed decisions)
remote access exception/approval records (who approved, justification, timestamp)
session counts by device classification (separate vs non-separate)
configuration entries showing requirement for separate-device enforcement (policy record)
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time device attestation and continuous session enforcement logs = strong evidence
Manual sign-offs or narrative records without session logs = weak
High ratio of automated enforcement failures documented with remediation = strong
Only policy text without operational logs = weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control (noting it is 'Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-2(6)') historically required preventing remote access unless from a separate device, which is a preventive enforcement requirement.

IA-2(11).differentiation_note: Marked withdrawn and incorporated into IA-2(6); its unique measurement need is device-level attestation and per-session device-origin evidence, unlike the SSO and PIV controls.

IA-2(12).measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and timeliness of accepting PIV credentials by tracking count and percentage of organizational users with accepted PIV credentials recorded, time-to-acceptance per user, and validation status of PIV credential bindings to user accounts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Acceptance of PIV credentials is a preventive onboarding/credentialing process; percentage coverage (users with accepted PIV) best shows policy compliance, while counts and trends show adoption and timeliness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage and count of organizational user accounts that have an accepted PIV credential bound, timestamps of PIV acceptance events per user, results of certificate/credential validation (serial, issuer, validity period), and records of revocation or rejection actions.
data_signals: PIV acceptance event records (user_id, credential_serial, acceptance_timestamp, approver)
user account records with PIV binding flag and binding_timestamp
PIV certificate validation results (issuer, serial number, validity status)
PIV revocation/checklist logs (CRL/OCSP check timestamps and results)
time-to-acceptance metric per onboarding event
list of users without accepted PIV credentials
evidence_strength_indicators: Cryptographic validation of PIV certificates and automated acceptance event logs = strong
Paper records or uncontrolled spreadsheets without certificate details = weak
Complete binding entries with timestamps in the identity provider = strong
Only HR/onboarding notes without certificate serials = weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control concerns accepting and validating PIV credentials for users—a preventive credentialing step to ensure only valid PIVs are bound to accounts.

IA-2(12).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on PIV credential acceptance and certificate validation/timeliness, unlike other IA-2 variants that address SSO or out-of-band authentication mechanisms.

IA-2(13).measurement_goal: Measure usage, success, and enforcement of out-of-band authentication for organizational users by counting out-of-band authentication challenges issued, success/failure rates, and percentage of privileged actions protected by out-of-band verification.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Out-of-band authentication is a preventive control that requires coverage and enforcement; percentage shows coverage of protected actions, while rates/counts capture challenge volumes and failure rates for operational visibility.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of targeted authentication events (e.g., privileged operations, high-risk logins) that invoked out-of-band authentication, counts of out-of-band challenges issued and their success/failure outcomes with timestamps, and policy records mapping which actions require out-of-band verification.
data_signals: out-of-band authentication challenge logs (challenge_id, user_id, timestamp, channel)
challenge outcome records (success/fail, response_time, failure_reason)
mapping of actions/resources to out-of-band requirement (policy/config table)
counts of privileged operations protected by out-of-band checks
exceptions/override records for skipped out-of-band authentication
authentication flow logs linking primary auth and out-of-band step
evidence_strength_indicators: Complete automated logs correlating primary auth with out-of-band challenge/response = strong
Manual attestations that out-of-band was used without logs = weak
High success rate but many overrides documented = medium (indicates operational issues)
Raw challenge/response cryptographic proof or token validation = strong

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires using an out-of-band authentication method to verify users (a preventive verification/enforcement mechanism).

IA-2(13).differentiation_note: Unlike the SSO control, this one measures issuance and outcomes of secondary/out-of-band challenges (per-event challenge metrics) rather than centralized federation coverage.

IA-3.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of device identity assignment and authentication: the percentage of managed devices with a unique, recorded device identifier and the proportion of device connections authenticated using approved methods; plus counts of authentication attempts and failures from unidentified devices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: IA-3 is primarily a preventive control (ensuring devices are identified and authenticated); percentage metrics show coverage/compliance while counts and trends capture event volumes and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of devices in the authoritative device inventory that have an assigned unique device identifier and at least one successful authentication event recorded in the last 30 days; count of authentication attempts from devices not present in the inventory and count of failed authentication attempts by device identifier.
data_signals: device inventory records with unique_device_identifier
authentication event logs (timestamp, device_identifier, result, auth_method)
device onboarding/enrollment records
list of approved authentication methods per device
failed authentication attempt logs filtered by unknown device identifiers
last-authenticated timestamp per device
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, continuous authentication logs that include device identifiers mapped to an authoritative device inventory and show successful mutual authentication events.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or periodic screenshots of enrollment lists, or authentication logs without reliable device identifier fields.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Device Identification and Authentication' indicates measures to prevent unauthorized device access by requiring identification and authentication.

IA-3.differentiation_note: This base control covers general device identity assignment and authentication coverage; its siblings add specific mechanisms (cryptographic mutual auth, dynamic address handling, attestation) that require different certificate/attestation/DHCP mapping signals.

IA-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the deployment and effectiveness of cryptographic bidirectional authentication: percentage of device-to-service and service-to-device connections using approved mutual (bidirectional) cryptographic authentication (e.g., client certificates or mutual TLS), plus counts of failed mutual-auth handshakes and percentage of devices with valid client credentials/certificates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control focused on cryptographic mutual authentication, coverage percentage (how many connections/devices use approved cryptographic auth) is most important; counts of failed mutual-auth attempts and trends help detect misconfigurations or attacks.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of inbound and outbound device connections that negotiated bidirectional cryptographic authentication (e.g., mutual TLS) over a defined period; count of mutual-authentication handshake failures by client certificate and by endpoint; percent of devices with valid, non-expired, non-revoked client certificates.
data_signals: TLS handshake logs indicating mutual authentication (client_cert_present, handshake_result)
certificate inventory (subject, serial, issuer, expiration, revocation status)
client certificate issuance and revocation events
list of approved cryptographic algorithms and cipher suites in use
counts of failed mutual-auth handshakes grouped by device identifier
configuration records showing enabled mutual authentication on endpoints/services
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated TLS/SSL handshake logs and certificate inventory showing mutual authentication was negotiated and validated against an authoritative PKI and revocation list.
Weak: ad-hoc test reports or single-session captures that do not demonstrate sustained mutual-auth coverage across services and devices.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Cryptographic Bidirectional Authentication' implies prevention of unauthorized connections by requiring mutual cryptographic authentication between peers.

IA-3(1).differentiation_note: Focused specifically on cryptographic/mutual authentication mechanisms (certificates, mutual TLS) and therefore requires certificate inventories and handshake logs not needed for the general IA-3 coverage.

IA-3(2).measurement_goal: This enhancement is Withdrawn ("Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-3(1)"): evidence should show that network-layer bidirectional cryptographic authentication requirements are implemented and covered under IA-3(1) — e.g., mutual-auth coverage for network services (VPN, TLS endpoints) and counts of failed network mutual-auth handshakes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Although withdrawn, the original emphasis was network-level bidirectional cryptographic authentication; percentage coverage of network services using mutual auth is the key preventive metric, with counts/trends for failures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of network services (VPNs, TLS listeners, network gateways) that require and negotiate bidirectional cryptographic authentication, and counts of failed network mutual-authentication attempts over time to demonstrate coverage and operational health.
data_signals: network TLS/VPN handshake logs indicating client certificate presence and handshake result
network service configuration records showing mutual-auth requirement
failed network mutual-authentication attempt logs
mapping of network endpoints to mutual-auth enforcement policy
note text: 'Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-3(1)'
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: comprehensive network handshake logs and enforced network service configs requiring mutual auth, matched to IA-3(1) certificate inventories.
Weak: policy statements claiming network mutual auth without network-level logs or enforcement evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input indicates 'Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-3(1)', but original phrasing 'Cryptographic Bidirectional Network Authentication' denotes a preventive requirement for network-layer mutual auth.

IA-3(2).differentiation_note: Marked Withdrawn and subsumed by IA-3(1); measurements should validate coverage of network services specifically and reference IA-3(1) evidence rather than separate controls.

IA-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure how dynamic address allocation is handled without losing device identity: percentage of dynamic (DHCP-assigned) addresses that are mapped to an authoritative device identifier (e.g., MAC-to-device-id mapping) and count of access or authentication events where device identity could not be resolved because of dynamic addressing.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control aims to ensure device identity is preserved despite dynamic addressing; percentage coverage of IP-to-device mapping shows compliance, while counts and trends capture mapping failures and operational issues.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of DHCP or dynamic address allocation events that have an associated, persisted mapping to the authoritative device inventory (by MAC, device_id, or certificate subject); count of authentication or access logs where IP address could not be resolved to a device identifier during the period.
data_signals: DHCP lease logs (timestamp, assigned_ip, mac_address, lease_id)
IP-to-device mapping table entries (ip, device_id, mapping_timestamp)
device inventory records with MAC and device_id
authentication/access logs referencing IP and device identifier resolution status
counts of unresolved IP-to-device mappings
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, near-real-time DHCP logs correlated with an authoritative device inventory and automated IP-to-device mapping used in access control decisions.
Weak: periodic manual reconciliation of DHCP leases to inventory or logs that lack MAC/device identifier fields.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Dynamic Address Allocation' implies requirements to prevent identity loss caused by ephemeral IP addresses, a preventive measure to maintain device identification integrity.

IA-3(3).differentiation_note: This enhancement focuses on mapping ephemeral network addresses to persistent device identifiers and requires DHCP/lease and mapping signals rather than cryptographic or attestation data.

IA-3(4).measurement_goal: Measure attestation coverage and result trends: percentage of managed devices that perform and report attestation results within policy windows, number and rate of attestation failures or non-compliant reports, and mean time to remediate attestation failures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Device attestation is preventive (ensuring device integrity prior to access); coverage percentage indicates how many devices are attesting as required, while counts and trends show failures and remediation progression.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of devices that submitted a successful attestation report within the attestation policy window (e.g., last 24 hours), counts of attestation failures or invalid attestation tokens, and timestamps showing remediation actions taken after failed attestation.
data_signals: attestation report logs (device_id, timestamp, attestation_result, measurement_hash)
attestation validation events (pass/fail, reason codes)
attestation certificate/token inventory and validity (expiration, revocation)
remediation ticket or workflow entries linked to attestation failures
time since last successful attestation per device
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous attestation reports and automated validation logs with linked remediation actions and timestamps.
Weak: single periodic attestation snapshots or manual statements without machine-readable attestation logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The label 'Device Attestation' indicates preventive validation of device integrity before permitting access.

IA-3(4).differentiation_note: Device attestation requires integrity measurement and attestation token validation signals (attestation reports, measurement hashes) distinct from authentication or address-mapping signals used by sibling controls.

IA-4.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and timeliness of identifier provisioning and deprovisioning, the rate of unique identifier collisions, and the percentage of accounts with current status attributes (active/disabled/terminated).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Identifier management is primarily preventive — auditors need coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., percent of accounts provisioned/deprovisioned according to policy); counts and rates (collision counts, provision/deprovision rates) provide additional operational context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of accounts with unique identifiers and correctly updated status attributes (active/disabled/terminated) within policy SLA; count of identifier collisions and count/rate of manual provisioning actions versus automated provisioning, grouped by identity source.
data_signals: user account creation records with timestamps and identity source
account disablement/deprovision timestamps and triggering event
unique identifier assignment logs (identifier values)
collision/error logs for duplicate identifier assignments
provisioning workflow audit logs showing automated vs manual actions
account status attribute values and last-modified timestamps
orphaned/unused account reports (last-login timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated provisioning/deprovisioning logs with timestamps and correlated HR/system events are strong evidence; occasional manual spreadsheets or one-off screenshots are weak.
Low duplicate identifier collision count with automated enforcement is strong; evidence showing many manual overrides is weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control concerns establishing and maintaining identifiers and lifecycle actions (provisioning/deprovisioning), which are preventive in nature.

IA-4.differentiation_note: This is the broad lifecycle control covering uniqueness and timeliness across all identifiers, unlike siblings that focus on specific identifier types or processes (e.g., pseudonymous IDs or cross-organization mapping).

IA-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of external-facing artefacts (URLs, API responses, public profiles) that do not expose internal account identifiers, and the count of public identifier exposure incidents detected.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage/compliance (percent of public surfaces free of account IDs); counts of exposures and distribution by surface (API, web UI) provide operational detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of monitored public endpoints (web pages, APIs, public profiles) that have been scanned and confirmed not to contain internal account identifiers; count of detected exposures and time-to-remediation for each exposure.
data_signals: web server response samples for public endpoints (content snippets, URLs)
API response payload logs for public/anonymous requests
public profile/exported user data samples
data classification tags indicating 'account identifier' fields
exposure incident reports and remediation tickets
code review or static analysis findings referencing identifiers in public responses
scan results from privacy/scrubbing tools
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated scans of public endpoints and API responses demonstrating zero occurrences are strong evidence; ad‑hoc manual spot-check screenshots are weak.
Tracked incidents with remediation evidence (ticket, code fix, deploy) strengthen evidence versus undocumented fixes.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement to prohibit account identifiers from being public is a preventive control to stop identifier exposure.

IA-4(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on preventing external/public exposure of identifiers, unlike the parent IA-4 which covers overall lifecycle and uniqueness.

IA-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of identity issuance or high-privilege account changes that have documented supervisor authorization and the count of identity actions lacking authorization.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Supervisor authorization is a preventive control; auditors expect percentage compliance (authorizations applied) and counts of unauthorized actions as secondary evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of account creations and privilege escalations that include a recorded supervisor authorization artifact (approval ticket, signed form, or IAM workflow approval) within the required process.
data_signals: provisioning workflow approval records (ticket IDs, approver ID, timestamp)
privilege escalation request and approval logs
HR onboarding records linked to provisioning actions
exceptions/override logs documenting missing approvals
IA-12(1) cross-reference notes indicating incorporation
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-recorded approver IDs and timestamps in the provisioning system are strong; email threads or undocumented verbal approvals are weak.
Low count of exceptions with documented compensating controls strengthens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control demands supervisory approval for identity actions (preventive), but the description indicates it is withdrawn/incorporated elsewhere, reducing confidence in standalone enforcement.

IA-4(2).differentiation_note: Although withdrawn, this entry isolates measurement of supervisor/approval artifacts for identity issuance — a governance-focused measurement differing from lifecycle/technical enforcement controls.

IA-4(3).measurement_goal: Measure whether multiple accepted forms of identity certification are documented and used where required, and the percentage of identity proofing events that used an approved certification form.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control about acceptable forms of certification, primary measurements are coverage/compliance percentages; counts/trends show Adoption and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of identity proofing/issuance events that used an approved certification method from policy (e.g., document types, credential checks); count of events using non-approved methods and remediation actions taken.
data_signals: identity proofing event logs with certification form field (document type, method)
policy registry of approved certification methods
exceptions reports and approval tickets for alternative methods
incorporation note 'Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-12(2)'
evidence_strength_indicators: Structured proofing logs tied to each issuance event are strong; unstructured manual attestations without recorded method are weak.
Low exception rate with documented approvals increases evidence strength.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes methods for identity certification (preventive); description marks it as withdrawn/incorporated which lowers standalone confidence.

IA-4(3).differentiation_note: Focuses on the proofing/certification methods used during identity issuance rather than lifecycle events or identifier formatting/exposure.

IA-4(4).measurement_goal: Measure the timeliness and coverage of updating user status attributes (active, suspended, terminated) and the count of accounts with stale or incorrect status beyond SLA.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires measuring coverage and timeliness (percent updated within SLA); rates of status-change events and counts of stale accounts give operational context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of user status changes (termination, suspension) that are reflected in IAM within the defined SLA (e.g., within X hours of HR event); count of accounts with last-status-update older than SLA and number of failed synchronizations.
data_signals: HR termination/suspension event timestamps
IAM account status change timestamps and actor
synchronization job run logs between HR and IAM
failed sync/error logs and reconciliation reports
stale account reports (last-login, last-status-modified)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, timestamped HR-to-IAM sync logs showing successful status updates within SLA are strong; manual updates without traceability or long reconciliation delays are weak.
Low failed-sync counts and documented reconciliations strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Updating and enforcing user status prevents unauthorized access by ensuring accounts reflect current employment/affiliation, a preventive function.

IA-4(4).differentiation_note: Targets the correctness and timeliness of status attributes specifically, unlike sibling controls focused on identifier format, federation, or pseudonymity.

IA-4(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of provisioning/deprovisioning and identifier updates handled by automated dynamic processes, mean time to revoke access for terminated users, and count of manual exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Dynamic management emphasizes automated enforcement and coverage (percentage automated); rates (time-to-revoke) and counts of manual overrides provide remediation context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of lifecycle events (provision, modify, deprovision) executed by automated workflows versus manual actions; median/mean time to revoke access after termination events; count of manual exceptions requiring human intervention.
data_signals: automation workflow execution logs with event type and timestamps
manual provisioning ticket logs and timestamps
time delta between HR termination event and access revocation
automation failure logs and retry counts
reconciliation reports showing automated vs manual percent
evidence_strength_indicators: Complete automation logs with event correlation to HR events and short MTTR are strong; spreadsheets or undocumented manual revocations are weak.
High automation success rate and low manual exception counts strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires dynamic/automated management of identifiers and lifecycle events, which is preventive by enforcing correct state programmatically.

IA-4(5).differentiation_note: Emphasizes automation and dynamic workflows for identifier lifecycle, different from static policy or proofing-focused siblings.

IA-4(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of cross-organization (federated/external) identities covered by mapping, trust and lifecycle controls, and count federation assertion errors or mapping mismatches.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Cross-organization management is preventive and requires coverage metrics (percent of federated identities under control); counts of assertion errors/mismatches provide operational signal.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of external/federated identities that have documented mapping to internal identifiers and are subject to lifecycle controls; count of failed federation assertions, mapping mismatches, and stale trust relationships.
data_signals: SAML/OIDC trust relationship metadata (entity IDs, metadata timestamps)
attribute mapping configuration records (RP-to-internal attribute maps)
federation assertion logs and error codes
provisioning/synchronization logs for federated accounts
trust certificate/metadata expiration reports
mapping mismatch/error ticket logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Up-to-date trust metadata and automated assertion logs with low error rates are strong; undocumented manual mappings or expired metadata are weak.
Low rate of mapping mismatches and timely remediation tickets strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Managing identifiers across organizations (federation, mapping, trust) is preventive to avoid improper access from external identities.

IA-4(6).differentiation_note: Focuses on federated/cross-organization identity mapping and trust, unlike internal lifecycle or pseudonymous-ID controls.

IA-4(7).measurement_goal: Measure whether in-person registration requirements (where applicable) are documented and the percentage of identity issuances requiring in-person proofing that have corresponding recorded in-person proofing artifacts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: In-person registration is a preventive proofing control; primary evidence is percent compliance (events with in-person artifact), with counts/trends showing usage and exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of identity provisioning events that required in-person registration which include documented proofing artifacts (registration forms, witness signatures) and count of exceptions where alternate proofing was used.
data_signals: in-person registration records and timestamps
proofing artifact storage entries (scanned IDs, witness logs)
policy note 'Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-12(4)'
exceptions and approval tickets for non‑in‑person proofing
evidence_strength_indicators: Securely stored, timestamped proofing artifacts tied to an issuance event are strong; undocumented verbal attestations or missing artifacts are weak.
Low exception rate with documented approvals strengthens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Originally preventive (in-person proofing), but the description shows it was withdrawn/incorporated elsewhere, reducing confidence in standalone applicability.

IA-4(7).differentiation_note: Specifically measures in-person proofing artifacts for issuance events — narrower than other identifier lifecycle controls and noted as withdrawn/incorporated elsewhere.

IA-4(8).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of relying parties/services that receive pairwise (per-RP) pseudonymous identifiers instead of a global account identifier, and count observable correlation incidents across relying parties.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control focuses on coverage (what percent of RPs use pairwise IDs); counts of correlation incidents and distribution by RP help show residual linkage risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of configured relying parties that are issued pairwise/pseudonymous identifiers, the mapping records showing RP-specific identifier values, and count of logs showing cross-RP identifier reuse or correlation.
data_signals: identifier issuance logs with RP identifier mapping (RP ID -> pseudonymous identifier)
pseudonym mapping tables and access control to mapping store
service configuration records indicating pairwise identifier enabled
audit logs showing identifier values returned to each RP
correlation incident reports where same identifier appears across RPs
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated issuance logs and protected mapping stores proving per-RP uniqueness are strong; static lists or manual obfuscation with no mapping logs are weak.
Zero cross-RP correlation incidents with locked mapping access is strong evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring pairwise/pseudonymous identifiers prevents cross-RP correlation and is therefore preventive.

IA-4(8).differentiation_note: Unique focus on per-relying-party pseudonymity and mapping records, rather than general identifier lifecycle or federation mapping.

IA-4(9).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of identity attributes that are classified and protected according to policy (e.g., encryption, access control), and the count/rate of unauthorized attribute modification events.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Attribute maintenance/protection is preventive: auditors want coverage percentages (attributes classified/protected) and counts/trends of unauthorized modifications as secondary detective signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical identity attributes (e.g., email, role, clearance) stored with required protections (encryption at rest, ACLs) and percent of attribute changes that have authenticated, authorized modification records; counts of unauthorized or anomalous attribute changes.
data_signals: attribute store configuration (encryption flags, ACLs) per attribute
attribute classification registry listing sensitivity and protection requirement
attribute change logs with actor, timestamp, and justification
access control enforcement logs for attribute store reads/writes
incident reports for unauthorized attribute modifications
evidence_strength_indicators: System-enforced encryption and ACL metadata plus immutable audit logs of attribute changes are strong; manual spreadsheets of attributes or missing access logs are weak.
Low unauthorized modification counts and timely investigation tickets strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: This control enforces protection and maintenance of identity attributes (preventive), requiring controls like encryption and ACLs.

IA-4(9).differentiation_note: Specifically measures classification, protection, and authorized change of identity attributes, distinct from identifier format, pseudonymity, or federation concerns.

IA-5.measurement_goal: Measure coverage of authenticator lifecycle controls: percentage of issued authenticators tracked (creation/issuance/change/revocation), percentage encrypted or protected per policy, and percentage of authentications using approved authenticator types.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: IA-5 is primarily preventive (coverage/compliance of authenticator controls), so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance) best show adherence; counts and trends support operational insight (numbers of issuances, revocations, and changes over time).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of authenticators (by type) with complete lifecycle records (user_id, authenticator_type, issuance_timestamp, change_timestamp, revocation_timestamp), encryption/protection status for stored authenticators, and proportion of authentications that used approved authenticators versus deprecated/unauthorized methods.
data_signals: authenticator issuance records (user_id, authenticator_type, issuance_timestamp)
authenticator change/rotation timestamps and change rationale
authenticator revocation/disablement logs
authentication logs with authenticator_type used (password, PK, token, MFA)
storage/encryption status for authenticator secrets (KMS/encryption flag)
authenticator inventory mapping to assets/accounts
policy mapping table of approved authenticator types
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, automated lifecycle logs showing issuance → change → revocation for every credential and encryption status flags.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or one-off screenshots of credential issuance without timestamps or automation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: IA-5 describes management and protection of authenticators (prevention of misuse) so it is preventive.

IA-5.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall lifecycle and coverage of authenticators across the organization, whereas child enhancements target specific authenticator technologies or processes.

IA-5(1).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement and coverage of password policies: percent of accounts subject to the configured password policy (length/complexity/expiration/reuse), count of password policy violations, and rate of password resets due to policy non-compliance or compromise.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Password-based controls are preventive and best evidenced by compliance/coverage percentages (policy enforcement); counts and rates show violation volumes and reset activity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of user accounts with password attributes that meet policy (minimum length, complexity rules, history/reuse settings, expiration) and counts of accounts violating password policy, plus password change/reset events with timestamps and triggers (user-initiated, admin-forced, compromise remediations).
data_signals: password policy configuration (min_length, complexity flags, expiration_days, reuse_history)
user account password attribute values (hashing algorithm, last_changed timestamp)
password change/reset event logs (user_id, timestamp, reason)
password policy violation logs/alerts
failed authentication attempts leading to resets
authenticator storage hashing/encryption metadata
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated system enforcement evidence (authentication system configuration showing enforced settings + continuous logs of password changes and policy violations).
Weak: manual attestations or one-off exported lists without linkage to enforcement configuration or change-event timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement focuses on password-based authentication controls (policy enforcement), which are preventive measures.

IA-5(1).differentiation_note: This entry specifically measures password policy enforcement and password lifecycle signals, unlike sibling entries that address non-password authenticators or delivery processes.

IA-5(2).measurement_goal: Measure management and protection of public-key authenticators: percentage of key pairs with documented ownership and lifecycle records, percentage of private keys stored/protected per policy (hardware-backed or encrypted), and rate of key rotations/revocations within required timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Public-key management is preventive; percentage coverage of protected keys and documented lifecycle demonstrates compliance, while counts and trends show rotation/revocation activity and key distribution.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of public/private key pairs with recorded owner, creation timestamp, storage protection method (HSM, KMS, encrypted at rest), rotation timestamp, and revocation status; counts of unprotected private keys and key revocations with reasons.
data_signals: certificate/key inventory (subject, owner, key_id, creation_timestamp)
key protection metadata (HSM/KMS usage, encryption_at_rest flag)
key rotation and revocation logs (key_id, timestamp, initiator, reason)
TLS/SSH certificate issuance records and expiry dates
authentication logs showing usage of public-key methods (user/key_id, timestamp)
private key storage location listings
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated key inventory and HSM/KMS logs showing protection and rotation events tied to key_ids.
Weak: scattered certificate spreadsheets without verifiable rotation/revocation event logs or evidence of protected storage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: IA-5(2) prescribes management/protection of public-key authenticators (preventing key compromise), so it is preventive.

IA-5(2).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on PKI/key lifecycle and protection metrics (HSM/KMS, rotations, revocations) instead of password or delivery controls.

IA-5(3).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of initial authenticator registrations that follow the approved in-person or trusted-third-party registration process (documented identity proofing and handover) and count exceptions processed via alternate methods.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement (registration process) is preventive; percentage of registrations following the approved process indicates compliance while counts of exceptions show risk exposure.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of authenticator registrations that include documented identity-proofing evidence (registrar_id, method=in_person|trusted_party, timestamp, delivery confirmation) and count of registrations using alternate/exception processes with approvals.
data_signals: registration transaction records (user_id, registrar_id, registration_method, timestamp)
identity-proofing documentation references (scan_id, verifier)
delivery/hand-over logs (delivery_method, confirmation_timestamp)
exception approval records for remote or alternate registration
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: linked registration transactions with scanned proof-of-identity, registrar signatures, and delivery confirmation stored in the system.
Weak: verbal attestations or spreadsheets listing registered users without proof artifacts or timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text indicates in-person/trusted registration (a preventive identity-proofing process); classification confidence reduced because the control is withdrawn per its description.

IA-5(3).differentiation_note: Covers the initial identity-proofing and registration method (in-person/trusted third party) and requires evidence of registration process adherence — distinct from technical protections of authenticators.

IA-5(4).measurement_goal: Measure use and coverage of automated password-strength enforcement: percent of authentication systems integrated with automated strength checks and percent of password changes evaluated and blocked when strength criteria not met.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automation of password strength is preventive; percentage coverage of integrated strength checks shows compliance while counts of blocked changes indicate enforcement activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of authentication endpoints applying automated password strength evaluation (integration flag) and count of password set/change attempts blocked for failing strength checks, with timestamps and user identifiers.
data_signals: authentication endpoint configuration (strength_check_enabled flag)
password set/change attempt logs (user_id, timestamp, result: accepted/blocked, failure_reason)
strength evaluation logs (score, rules triggered)
integration logs between authentication service and strength service
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system logs showing each password change evaluated by the automated strength engine with blocking events recorded.
Weak: manual guidance documents or occasional manual checks without integration logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Enhancement mandates automated password strength enforcement (preventive); confidence slightly lower because the enhancement text notes it was incorporated into IA-5(1).

IA-5(4).differentiation_note: Targets automation of password strength enforcement specifically (system integration and blocked-change events) rather than general password policy coverage.

IA-5(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of credentials that were changed (rotated/reset) prior to delivery to the user and count of delivery events where pre-delivery change did not occur (exceptions).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive process is best shown by percentage of deliveries complying with the prior-change requirement; counts of exceptions and comparisons against policy thresholds provide additional context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of credential delivery events where a pre-delivery change/rotation record exists (change_timestamp < delivery_timestamp) including user_id, delivery_method, change_initiator, and delivery timestamp; count of exceptions with documented approvals.
data_signals: credential change/rotation records (credential_id, change_timestamp, initiator)
credential delivery logs (credential_id, delivery_timestamp, delivery_method, recipient)
exception/approval records for skipped pre-delivery change
mapping of credential_id to user account
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: linked change and delivery events showing change occurred prior to delivery for each issuance, with no outstanding exceptions.
Weak: attestations or post-hoc statements without timestamps proving sequence change -> delivery.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires changing authenticators prior to delivery (a preventive issuance control), so it is preventive.

IA-5(5).differentiation_note: Unique measurement need is sequencing evidence (change event before delivery event) rather than general lifecycle coverage.

IA-5(6).measurement_goal: Measure protection controls for stored authenticators: percentage of authenticators stored with approved protection (encryption/HSM/KMS), count of authenticator secret exposures, and percentage of systems enforcing access controls to authenticator storage.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Protection of authenticators is preventive; coverage percentages (protected vs unprotected) are primary indicators while counts of exposures and distributions of storage types provide operational detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of authenticator secrets stored with approved protection mechanisms (HSM/KMS/encrypted_at_rest) and counts of unauthorized access attempts or confirmed exposures, plus mapping of storage locations to protection status.
data_signals: authenticator storage inventory (location, protection_method, encryption_flag)
KMS/HSM usage logs (key_id, access events)
access control lists and IAM policies for authenticator stores
incident/exposure logs referencing authenticator secrets
backup/encryption key management records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventories and KMS/HSM logs showing protection and access controls plus zero exposures.
Weak: manual inventories or policy documents without system-level evidence of encryption or access controls.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement mandates protection measures for authenticators (storage/encryption/access controls), a preventive requirement.

IA-5(6).differentiation_note: Focuses narrowly on storage and protection controls for authenticators (encryption, KMS/HSM, access policies) unlike lifecycle or issuance controls.

IA-5(7).measurement_goal: Demonstrate zero instances of embedded, unencrypted, static authenticators on hardware/software assets and count any exceptions with compensating controls documented.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a zero-tolerance preventive requirement; counts of embedded/unencrypted static authenticators (ideally zero) directly evidence compliance, with percentages showing prevalence across assets.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of assets containing embedded static authenticators and count of those where the authenticators are unencrypted; inventory of assets scanned with results and any documented compensating controls or approved exceptions.
data_signals: software/firmware inventory scan results indicating embedded credentials
configuration management database (CMDB) asset attributes referencing embedded authenticators
encryption status flags for embedded secrets
exception/compensating control approval records
vulnerability scan findings referencing static credentials
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated scans and configuration management showing zero assets with unencrypted embedded static authenticators and no outstanding exceptions.
Weak: ad-hoc manual code reviews or vendor attestations without automated scan results.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prohibits embedded unencrypted static authenticators (a preventive zero-tolerance requirement).

IA-5(7).differentiation_note: This entry is explicitly a zero-tolerance prohibition (count-based) of embedded unencrypted static authenticators, unlike other entries that focus on lifecycle or policy coverage.

IA-5(8).measurement_goal: Measure the number and percentage of users with multiple system accounts (per policy), justify exceptions, and track mapping of accounts to individual authenticators to detect reuse or shared authenticators.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Managing multiple accounts is preventive; percentage of users with multiple accounts vs policy expectations evidences policy adherence and risk exposure, with counts and distribution across systems adding detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of user principals that have more than one system account across monitored systems, inventory linking user_id to all account_ids, documented exceptions/approvals for multiple accounts, and count of shared authenticators across accounts.
data_signals: account inventory mapping (user_id -> account_ids, system)
authentication logs showing authenticator identifiers used across accounts
exception/approval records for multiple accounts
orphaned account lists and account-owner reconciliation reports
provisioning/deprovisioning event logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated account-to-user mapping and reconciliation reports showing coverage and exceptions, plus low rates of shared authenticators.
Weak: manual lists or partial inventories without reconciliation to authoritative identity sources.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement concerns management/restriction of multiple accounts (preventive governance to limit account proliferation).

IA-5(8).differentiation_note: Measures user-to-account mapping and reconciliation to detect account proliferation and shared authenticators, unlike other entries focused on authenticator protection or issuance sequencing.

IA-5(9).measurement_goal: Measure security and governance of federated credential usage: percentage of federated trusts documented and approved, count of SSO/SAML/OIDC trust relationships with validated configuration (assertion attributes, signing certs), and rate of federated credential revocations or trust removals.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Federation controls are preventive; percentage of federated relationships that meet documented security and configuration standards shows compliance while counts/trends of trusts and revocations show operational risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of federated trust relationships with documented metadata (entity_id, partner, assertion attributes, signing certificates, approved use) and counts of failed or anomalous federated authentications, plus revocation/removal events for federated credentials.
data_signals: federation trust inventory (partner, entity_id, protocol: SAML/OIDC, metadata_timestamp)
federation configuration validation results (assertion attribute mappings, audience, signing cert validity)
SSO authentication logs (assertion_id, partner_entity_id, timestamp, outcome)
trust revocation/removal logs
exception/approval records for federated partners
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated federation inventory and periodic configuration validation reports plus continuous SSO logs showing only approved assertion attributes and valid signatures.
Weak: manual partner lists or undocumented trust relationships without configuration validation or logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: IA-5(9) prescribes management of federated credentials (trust and configuration controls), which are preventive.

IA-5(9).differentiation_note: Specifically targets federated identity/trust configuration and governance (federation metadata, assertion attributes), not general authenticator storage or password policies.

IA-5(10).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of identities/services using dynamic (ephemeral) credential binding, the count and rate of dynamic credential issuance events, and enforcement of configured TTLs/rotation for bound credentials.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive/control-coverage requirement (use of dynamic binding) so percentage of applicable services using dynamic binding is most useful; counts of issuance events and trends show operation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of managed identities and service endpoints that have dynamic credential binding enabled; count and timestamps of credential binding/issuance events; configured TTL/rotation values and enforcement failures (overdue rotations).
data_signals: credential binding configuration per service (enabled/disabled)
ephemeral credential issuance timestamps and issuer identity
credential TTL/rotation configuration values
binding event logs (service-account -> credential mapping)
failed/expired binding enforcement events
automation runbook/execution records that perform binding
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated issuance logs with timestamps and service identifiers (strong);
Configuration inventory showing binding enabled for each service (strong);
Ad-hoc screenshots or one-off attestations without logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name requires enabling and managing dynamic credential binding (a control to prevent long-lived credentials), which is preventive in nature.

IA-5(10).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on dynamic/ephemeral credential issuance and enforcement (coverage and TTLs), unlike other IA-5 siblings that address token types, biometric metrics, PKI stores or issuance processes.

IA-5(11).measurement_goal: Show the percentage of applicable user accounts and systems configured to accept and require hardware token-based authentication where mandated, and track token provisioning and deprovisioning events against policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This withdrawn control (incorporated into IA-2 variants) relates to coverage and configuration of hardware tokens; auditors need coverage percentages and counts of provisioning/deprovisioning events.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of user accounts/systems required by policy that have hardware token authentication configured; counts and timestamps of hardware token provisioning, replacement, and revocation events; exceptions granted.
data_signals: hardware token provisioning logs
authentication method configuration per account
token revocation/deactivation timestamps
enrollment/issuance records
exceptions/waiver approval records
evidence_strength_indicators: Device enrollment and revocation logs with account mappings (strong);
Periodic attestations listing enrolled tokens without issuance/revocation logs (weaker).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control governs deployment and configuration of hardware-token authentication to prevent unauthorized access, a preventive requirement.

IA-5(11).differentiation_note: Targets hardware-token specific enrollment/configuration and lifecycle (provision/deprovision) rather than other authenticator types or detection/performance metrics.

IA-5(12).measurement_goal: Measure biometric system performance using standard metrics (false acceptance rate, false rejection rate, equal error rate) and the rate/count of biometric authentication failures and overrides, compared to policy thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Biometric performance requires rates (FAR/FRR) and error counts to detect acceptance/rejection issues; percentages and trends show performance over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Counts and rates of biometric authentication attempts, successful authentications, false accepts and false rejects; computed FAR, FRR, EER; number and timestamps of operator overrides or fallback authentications.
data_signals: biometric authentication attempt logs with outcome
FAR (false acceptance) calculations over sample windows
FRR (false rejection) calculations over sample windows
operator override/fallback authentication events
sample sizes and enrollment quality metrics
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated aggregated FAR/FRR metrics computed from raw attempt logs (strong);
Manufacturer claims or single test reports without operational attempt logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control measures biometric authentication performance (FAR/FRR), which is primarily detective—monitoring effectiveness of biometric authenticators.

IA-5(12).differentiation_note: Focuses on biometric accuracy/performance metrics (FAR/FRR/EER) rather than enrollment, issuance, or trust-store management found in other siblings.

IA-5(13).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement of cached authenticator expiration by counting cached authenticator instances, rate of expirations enforced, and number of authentication events using expired cached credentials.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires measuring enforcement (occurrence rate of expired-cache usage) and counts of cached authenticators; rates of violations are most informative for enforcement effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of cached authenticator entries per system, configured cache expiration values, number and timestamps of authentications using cached (and expired) authenticators, and enforcement/failure events where expiration was not applied.
data_signals: cached authenticator inventory per host/service
cache expiration configuration values and timestamps
authentication logs indicating cache-hit vs fresh-auth
authentication events that used expired cached credentials
cache eviction/expiration events and failures
evidence_strength_indicators: System logs showing cache eviction and authentication source (cache vs live) for each attempt (strong);
Periodic attestations or one-off checks without continuous logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement enforces expiration policies for cached authenticators to prevent reuse of stale credentials, a preventive control.

IA-5(13).differentiation_note: Targets enforcement and monitoring of cached authenticator expiration specifically, unlike siblings focused on issuance, token types, or biometric measures.

IA-5(14).measurement_goal: Demonstrate management of PKI trust stores by measuring coverage of systems using approved trust stores, counts of trust store updates, presence of untrusted/expired certificates, and time-to-remediate trust store anomalies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Management of PKI trust stores is operationally tracked via counts (updates, anomalies) and coverage percentages; counts of untrusted/expired certs and update events are primary signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of systems with validated/approved trust store contents, timestamps and counts of trust store updates, inventory of certificates in trust stores including expiration dates and trust anchor provenance, and remediation events for untrusted/expired certificates.
data_signals: trust store contents dump per system (certificate list with issuer and expiry)
trust store update/change logs and timestamps
inventory of systems mapped to trust store version
alerts for expired or revoked certificates found in trust stores
remediation tickets and completion timestamps for trust anomalies
