evidence_strength_indicators: Automated periodic trust-store inventory exports with certificate metadata and update history (strong);
Manual spreadsheets without cryptographic evidence or timestamps (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Managing PKI trust stores prevents acceptance of untrusted certificates and misconfigurations, a preventive management control.

IA-5(14).differentiation_note: Specifically addresses contents and lifecycle of PKI trust stores (certificates and trust anchors) rather than authenticator issuance or biometric controls.

IA-5(15).measurement_goal: Demonstrate compliance with GSA-approved product/service requirements by measuring the proportion of deployed authenticators/products that are on the approved list and tracking exceptions and time-to-replace unapproved items.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A compliance/coverage control: percentage of deployed products on the GSA-approved list shows conformity; counts of exceptions and comparisons against the approved list are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of authenticator products and services in inventory that match the GSA-approved products/services list; counts and timestamps of exceptions/waivers and remediation or replacement actions for non-approved items.
data_signals: inventory of authenticator products/services with vendor and model
GSA-approved products/services list (versioned)
match/mismatch reports between inventory and approved list
exception/waiver records and approvals
remediation/replacement ticket timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated inventory reconciliation against a versioned approved list with evidence of remediation timelines (strong);
Self-reported lists without cross-reference to approved list (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: This is a governance/compliance control ensuring only approved products/services are used, which prevents noncompliant authenticator deployment.

IA-5(15).differentiation_note: Addresses conformity to an external approved-products list (GSA) and inventory reconciliation, unlike technical enforcement controls for tokens or cache expiration.

IA-5(16).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of authenticators issued via in-person or trusted external party processes, log issuance activities with verifier identity, and track exceptions and issuance anomalies against policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Issuance lifecycle is tracked via counts (issuances, revocations) and percentage of issuances following the in-person/external trusted process; counts provide forensic evidence of correct issuance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Counts and timestamps of authenticator issuance events labeled as in-person or trusted-external-party; identity and credentials of verifier/issuer; issuance approvals and recorded proof of identity; counts of exceptions or remote issuances.
data_signals: authenticator issuance logs with issuance method (in-person/trusted-external/remote)
issuer/verifier identity and signature records
proof-of-identity documentation attachments (scans/checklists)
issuance approval records and timestamps
exceptions/waiver logs and remediation actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Signed issuance records tied to verifier identity and scanned proof-of-identity documents (strong);
Oral attestations or undocumented issuance (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes secure issuance processes (in-person/trusted parties) to prevent improper authenticator issuance, making it preventive.

IA-5(16).differentiation_note: Concentrates on issuance process controls and verifier evidence (in-person/trusted external) as opposed to runtime authentication performance or token configuration.

IA-5(17).measurement_goal: Measure detection effectiveness by counting presentation-attack detection (PAD) alerts, the PAD detection rate, and false positive/false negative rates for biometric authenticators versus policy thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Presentation attack detection is a detective capability; detection and false-positive/negative rates (rates) are the primary evidence of operating effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Counts and rates of PAD events (detected attacks), PAD true-positive and false-positive counts, timestamps and sensor identifiers for PAD alerts, and follow-up/mitigation actions taken for confirmed attacks.
data_signals: PAD sensor/biometric system alert logs with outcome (attack detected/benign)
confirmed incident records tied to PAD alerts
false positive and false negative incident tags
timestamped mitigation or lockout actions after PAD detection
sensor firmware/version and liveness algorithm identifier
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous PAD alerts with confirmed incident correlation and low false-negative rates (strong);
One-off lab tests or vendor claims without operational PAD logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires detecting presentation attacks against biometrics (a monitoring/detection capability), which is detective.

IA-5(17).differentiation_note: Unlike biometric performance (IA-5(12)), this control focuses specifically on detection of spoof/presentation attacks and PAD alerting/response metrics.

IA-5(18).measurement_goal: Measure adoption and secure configuration of approved password managers by calculating the percentage of users covered, counts of stored credential vaults, and counts/rate of insecure configurations or vault compromise events.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Password manager controls are preventive (coverage/configuration); percentage of user coverage and counts of insecure configurations are primary; distribution of vault types provides additional detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of target user population with approved password manager installed and enrolled; counts of password vaults per user, configuration compliance (master password/encryption settings, MFA enabled), and incidents of vault compromise or credential export.
data_signals: endpoint inventory of password manager installations (user -> product/version)
enrollment/activation logs for password managers
configuration compliance snapshots (MFA enabled, encryption settings)
incident logs of vault compromise or unauthorized export
exception/waiver records for non-approved managers
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated inventory and configuration scans showing enrollment and secure settings (strong);
Self-reported adoption numbers without configuration evidence (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control governs the use and management of password managers to prevent credential misuse, a preventive control focused on coverage and secure configuration.

IA-5(18).differentiation_note: Specifically about adoption and secure configuration of password managers (coverage and vault security), rather than issuance, PKI, or biometric detection topics covered by siblings.

IA-6.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and count of authentication responses that expose detailed feedback (e.g., whether username or password was incorrect) versus generic responses, broken down by application/endpoint, and track occurrences of account-enumeration behaviors and informative error messages over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (minimizing information returned in authentication responses); percentage metrics (percent of attempts returning only generic feedback) directly show coverage, while counts/rates capture volume and frequency of problematic responses.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and classify authentication response events by response content (generic vs. detailed) per application/endpoint; compute percentage of authentication attempts that returned detailed or distinguishing error messages. Capture associated attributes: timestamp, source IP, attempted username, endpoint/URI, HTTP response code, response message template, and MFA/challenge state to allow filtering and grouping for policy compliance queries.
data_signals: authentication event logs containing timestamp, username, source IP, endpoint, HTTP response code, and response body or error message
application response templates or configuration that define authentication error messages
webserver/access logs showing authentication-related 4xx/401/403 responses with response bodies
MFA/challenge logs indicating whether challenge was issued or bypassed
account lockout event records with timestamps and triggering conditions
synthetic authentication test results showing returned messages per test case
penetration-test or security assessment findings noting account-enumeration or verbose error messages
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated authentication logs plus synthetic test runs demonstrating only generic error messages across all applications/endpoints; versioned response templates mapped to policy.
Moderate: periodic manual test screenshots or single-application logs showing generic responses but lacking full coverage.
Weak: one-off developer statements or screenshots without logs, or penetration-test reports without continuous logging evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Authentication Feedback' indicates prevention of informative feedback that could aid attackers (reducing information returned in auth responses), so it is primarily preventive.

IA-6.differentiation_note: Unlike other auth controls that measure credential strength or access approvals, this control requires content-level measurement of authentication responses and templates (response bodies and messages) rather than just counts of attempts or successful authentications.

IA-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of deployed cryptographic modules (HSMs, TPMs, software crypto libraries) that present valid, current authentication/attestation (e.g., certificates, signed firmware, FIPS validation) and the counts/rates of authentication failures or unauthorized modules detected over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage metrics (percent of modules authenticated/validated). Counts of failures and rates of unauthenticated module detections are useful secondary indicators of gaps or incidents.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A data engineer should produce: (1) an authoritative inventory of cryptographic modules with attributes (module_id, type, host, FIPS/validation identifier, firmware version, attestation certificate serial, last_attestation_timestamp), (2) the presence/validity status of attestation/certificate per module, and (3) counts and timestamps of authentication/attestation failures or unrecognized modules discovered by attestation/scan processes.
data_signals: cryptographic module inventory with module_id, type (HSM/TPM/software), host/resource owner
attestation certificate serials and validity timestamps (certificate expiration, revocation status)
firmware/digital signature verification results for module binaries
FIPS/validation identifier (e.g., FIPS 140-2/3 validation number) per module
attestation success/failure logs with timestamps and failure reasons
HSM/TPM access and connection logs showing module handshake/authentication events
unauthorized or unknown module detection events from periodic scans
configuration baseline checks showing that cryptographic modules use approved algorithms and key sizes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated attestation logs showing successful certificate/firmware signature validation per module with timestamps and correlation to authoritative inventory.
Moderate: periodic signed attestation reports or weekly scan summaries without per-event logs.
Weak: one-time manual attestations or self-reported inventories without cryptographic evidence of module identity or certificate validity.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title focuses on authenticating cryptographic modules prior to use, which prevents use of unauthorized modules (preventive function).

IA-7.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets validation and attestation of cryptographic modules (HSM/TPM/crypto libraries) and requires module-level attestation and certificate evidence, unlike sibling controls that may focus on user identity, access provisioning, or key lifecycle management.

IA-8.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of approved identification and authentication mechanisms for non-organizational users: percentage of systems that enforce approved auth methods for non-organizational users, and counts/rates of authentication attempts by non-organizational credentials (success/failure).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: IA-8 is primarily preventive (ensuring non-organizational users use approved auth methods) so coverage/percentage metrics (percent of systems configured correctly) are most useful; counts/rates of auth attempts and trends supplement to show enforcement and anomalies.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of enterprise systems that have configuration enforcing approved identification and authentication methods for non-organizational users, plus counts of authentication attempts (success/failure) tagged as non-organizational. Include system identity configuration records, auth policy assignments, and authentication event logs with user type metadata.
data_signals: system authentication policy configuration records indicating allowed auth methods for non-organizational users
inventory of systems with non-organizational user access enabled
authentication event logs (timestamp, username, user_type=non-organizational, auth_method, result)
exceptions or waiver records for non-compliant systems
enforcement status (enabled/disabled) per system
failed authentication counts for non-organizational credentials
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated auth logs correlated with system configuration records showing enforced approved methods; system inventories showing 100% of in-scope systems configured.
Weak: ad-hoc screenshots or one-off attestations without correlated logs or system config exports.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title focuses on identification and authentication for users, implying prevention of unauthorized access by enforcing approved auth mechanisms.

IA-8.differentiation_note: Parent control covering broad enforcement and coverage of authentication for non-organizational users; subordinate controls specify particular approved products, profiles, or credential types.

IA-8(1).measurement_goal: Demonstrate that supplemental IA-8(1) requirements for non-organizational user authentication are implemented by measuring the percentage of non-organizational user endpoints and services configured per IA-8 baseline and counts of auth events tied to those supplemental settings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As an IA-8 supplement, this is treated as preventive coverage verification (percentage of systems configured) with counts of auth events as supporting detective evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope systems and services (those allowing non-organizational user access) that have the supplemental IA-8(1) configurations applied, plus auth event counts from those systems indicating enforcement.
data_signals: inventory of systems permitting non-organizational user access
configuration flags or templates applied to each system for IA-8(1) supplemental requirements
authentication logs with user_type labeling
applied control version or profile identifier per system
exception/waiver records for systems not compliant
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated configuration exports showing supplemental settings applied across all in-scope systems plus continuous auth logs reflecting the configured behavior.
Weak: manual attestations without exportable config or lacking correlated auth logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Supplemental IA-8 parts typically add preventive configuration requirements to the main IA-8 control; name implies configuration enforcement.

IA-8(1).differentiation_note: This entry covers an IA-8 supplemental requirement (unknown label in name) and focuses on applying supplemental configuration across in-scope systems rather than product-specific acceptance or profile definitions.

IA-8(2).measurement_goal: Measure implementation and enforcement of IA-8(2) supplemental requirements: percent of systems that conform to IA-8(2) settings and counts of authentication events governed by IA-8(2) rules (success/failure), plus any exception records.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Supplemental preventive requirements are best evidenced by coverage percentages (percent of systems configured correctly) with rates/counts of auth events to show live enforcement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope systems with IA-8(2) supplemental profile/settings applied, plus authentication event logs that indicate enforcement of those settings for non-organizational users; include any records of incorporation (e.g., IA-8(3) noted as incorporated into IA-8(2)).
data_signals: system configuration exports showing IA-8(2) profile identifiers
profile assignment records
authentication logs with profile enforcement indicators
exception/waiver records referencing IA-8(2)
change history showing incorporation of IA-8(3) into IA-8(2)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: exported system configs and profile assignments showing IA-8(2) applied to all in-scope resources plus continuous auth logs.
Weak: narrative statements or one-time attestations without config exports or auth logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Textual note that IA-8(3) was incorporated into IA-8(2) and the nature of IA controls indicates configuration/preventive intent.

IA-8(2).differentiation_note: Focuses on the supplemental IA-8(2) profile/configuration application (including incorporation of IA-8(3)) rather than credential-specific acceptance or disassociation behavior.

IA-8(3).measurement_goal: Because IA-8(3) is withdrawn (incorporated into IA-8(2)), measure that FICAM-related product requirements formerly in IA-8(3) are addressed by IA-8(2): count of systems with FICAM-approved product registry mapping present and confirmation that IA-8(2) profiles enforce approved-product constraints.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Although withdrawn, auditors need coverage evidence (percentage of systems mapped to FICAM-approved product constraints now managed under IA-8(2)) — coverage metrics are primary with counts/comparisons to show incorporation completeness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of previously in-scope FICAM product instances that are now covered by IA-8(2) configurations; evidence includes product registry mappings, IA-8(2 profile application logs, and change records noting incorporation of IA-8(3) into IA-8(2).
data_signals: FICAM-approved product registry or inventory
mapping of product instances to IA-8(2 profiles
change management records referencing 'Withdrawn: incorporated into IA-8(2)'
configuration exports showing IA-8(2 enforcement on formerly IA-8(3) systems
exceptions or unresolved mappings
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: explicit mapping records and change tickets showing IA-8(3) items migrated into IA-8(2) plus continuous enforcement evidence.
Weak: only a statement that IA-8(3) was withdrawn without mapping or enforcement artifacts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Marked withdrawn and incorporated into IA-8(2); classification as compensating reflects that its original requirements are expected to be enforced elsewhere (IA-8(2)).

IA-8(3).differentiation_note: This entry is unique because IA-8(3) is withdrawn — measurement verifies migration/incorporation into IA-8(2) and that FICAM-product constraints are still enforced.

IA-8(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems and services enabling non-organizational user access that are assigned and enforcing defined authentication profiles (defined profiles), and count profile mismatches or unprofiled systems.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Defined-profile requirements are preventive; coverage percent of systems with assigned profiles is primary, with counts and distribution across environments to show gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope systems assigned a defined authentication profile, list of systems without a profile, and counts of auth events where the enforced behavior differs from the assigned profile; include profile assignment records and configuration exports.
data_signals: defined profile catalog (profile IDs and definitions)
profile assignment records per system/service
system configuration exports showing active profile
authentication logs indicating profile-enforced attributes (e.g., auth_method, min_strength)
unassigned system count
exceptions/waivers referencing profiles
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory showing profile assignments and config exports proving active enforcement plus auth logs consistent with profile constraints.
Weak: manual lists of profiles without configuration or runtime enforcement logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control name explicitly references use of defined profiles which implies preventive configuration to enforce auth behavior.

IA-8(4).differentiation_note: Specifically targets application and enforcement of defined authentication profiles, unlike sibling controls that focus on credential types or product lists.

IA-8(5).measurement_goal: Measure acceptance and validation of PIV-I credentials for non-organizational users: count of authentications using PIV-I, percentage of PIV-I authentications successfully validated, and number of systems configured to accept PIV-I credentials.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about accepting a specific credential type (PIV-I) — counts of PIV-I authentication events and validation success rates are the most direct evidence; percentage of systems configured to accept PIV-I is a useful preventive complement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Counts of authentication attempts using PIV-I credentials (success and failure), the percentage of in-scope systems configured to accept PIV-I, and validation result logs showing PIV-I credential validity checks (e.g., certificate validation).
data_signals: authentication logs tagged with credential_type=PIV-I (timestamp, user, result)
system configuration inventory showing 'accepts PIV-I' flag
certificate validation/OCSP/CRL check logs for PIV-I
enrollment/issuance records indicating accepted PIV-I providers
failed PIV-I validation counts and failure reasons
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous auth logs with explicit credential_type fields, and validation artifacts (certificate chain, OCSP responses) correlated to system configs that accept PIV-I.
Weak: statements that PIV-I is accepted without logs of validation or config exports.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control focuses on accepting and validating a specific credential type (PIV-I), which is a preventive configuration and validation requirement.

IA-8(5).differentiation_note: Targets credential-type acceptance and validation (PIV-I) specifically, requiring certificate validation logs and counts of PIV-I auths rather than general profile or product application.

IA-8(6).measurement_goal: Measure that non-organizational user identities/credentials can be disassociated from organizational accounts or sessions as required: counts of disassociation requests processed, average time to disassociate, and percentage of successful disassociations within policy SLA.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Disassociability involves corrective/detection activity (unlinking or disabling associations) so rate metrics (processing rate, mean time to disassociate) best capture responsiveness; counts and percentages show volume and compliance to SLA.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and rate of disassociation actions (requests and completed disassociations), mean time to disassociate an identity or credential from an account/session, and percentage completed within the policy SLA; include request logs, action audit records, and change tickets.
data_signals: disassociation request logs (timestamp, requester, target identity, justification)
audit logs of disassociation actions (who/when/what changed)
time-to-complete timestamps (request vs completion)
open/unresolved disassociation requests
associated change management or incident records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated request and audit logs with timestamps enabling MTTR calculation and SLA compliance rates.
Weak: manual emails or verbal confirmations without auditable timestamps and action logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Disassociability describes the ability to unlink or remove associations which is corrective in nature (responding to a requirement to remove association).

IA-8(6).differentiation_note: Focuses on the ability and process to remove associations (disassociability) with measurable processing times and audit trails, unlike siblings which focus on profiles, products, or credential acceptance.

IA-9.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of service-to-service identification and authentication controls: percentage of service connections enforcing authentication, count of service identities with valid credentials/certificates, and rate of authentication failures for service accounts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: IA-9 is a preventive control requiring coverage and enforcement; percentage (coverage/compliance) best shows whether service authentication is applied organization-wide, while counts and rates show volume and failure trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of active service-to-service connections that use approved authentication (mutual TLS, issued tokens, or federation) versus total service connections; count of active service identities and their credential/certificate validity and rotation timestamps; authentication success/failure events for service accounts over the audit period.
data_signals: service identity registry entries (service ID, owner, provisioning timestamp)
service account credential and certificate inventory with issuance and expiry timestamps
authentication event logs for service-to-service calls (timestamp, source service ID, destination service ID, auth method, result)
TLS/mTLS handshake logs and negotiated protocol/cipher (for service endpoints)
token issuance logs (JWT/OAuth: issuance time, subject, scopes)
service authentication failure counts and failure reasons
credential rotation/rotation request timestamps and automation execution logs
policy mapping of allowed authentication methods per service
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous authentication event logs correlated with a centralized service identity registry and automated certificate/token lifecycle records.
Weak: one-off configuration screenshots, manual inventories, or annual attestations without operational logs demonstrating runtime authentication.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Service Identification and Authentication' indicates preventive measures to ensure only authenticated services interact (authentication and identification are preventative).

IA-9.differentiation_note: This entry covers broad service identity and authentication coverage across the environment (registry, credentials, runtime auth events); the siblings focus specifically on information-exchange and decision-transmission variants of authentication.

IA-9(1).measurement_goal: Measure whether exchanged information between services is authenticated and integrity protected: percent of information exchanges that include an identity assertion or message signature, and count of exchange failures due to missing/invalid identity assertions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive enhancement focused on information exchange, percentage coverage (exchanges with proper identity assertions/signatures) shows compliance; counts and rates surface volumes and failures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of API/message exchanges that contain a valid identity assertion (signed token, message signature, or verified header) compared to total exchanges; counts of rejected or flagged exchanges due to missing or invalid assertions; evidence of employed exchange mechanisms (SAML/JWT signatures, message-level encryption).
data_signals: API gateway / message broker logs with identity assertion headers (e.g., Authorization, X-Identity, signature headers)
message signature verification logs (success/fail)
token validation logs (JWT SIDs, issuer, expiry, validation result)
counts of rejected/blocked exchanges due to authentication or integrity failures
encryption/TLS used indicator per exchange (protocol and cipher)
information exchange policy mapping (which exchanges require signed/authenticated messages)
audit records of message integrity checks
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous API/message broker logs showing identity assertions and successful signature validations correlated to policy mapping.
Weak: offline message sample inspections or checklist attestations without runtime exchange logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The parenthetical title 'Information Exchange' describes preventive requirements ensuring exchanged information is authenticated and integrity-protected before acceptance.

IA-9(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on the authentication and integrity of messages/information exchanged between services (message signatures, token validation) rather than general service credential coverage.

IA-9(2).measurement_goal: Measure authentication and integrity of transmitted authorization decisions: count and percent of decision transmissions that are cryptographically authenticated/signed and acknowledged by enforcement points, and rate of decision transmission failures or mismatches.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive enhancement requires verifying authenticated transmission of decisions; percentage coverage (decisions transmitted with signatures/secure channel) demonstrates compliance, while rates/counts show failures and volumes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of authorization decision transmissions (PDP→PEP, or service decision messages) that include authenticated assertions or signatures and receive enforcement acknowledgements; counts of unsigned or unauthenticated decision messages and failed acknowledgements; latency and retransmission counts for decision delivery where relevant.
data_signals: authorization decision transmission logs (timestamp, decision ID, source PDP ID, destination PEP ID, signature present)
decision signature verification results (success/fail)
PEP acknowledgement logs indicating enforcement of received decision
counts of decision transmission failures, retries, or mismatches between decision and enforcement
secure channel indicators for decision transport (e.g., TLS/mTLS usage)
policy mapping of required decision authentication mechanisms
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: end-to-end logs showing signed decision issuance, successful signature validation at enforcement point, and acknowledgement records.
Weak: manual attestations that decisions are signed without correlated runtime verification logs or enforcement acknowledgements.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Transmission of Decisions' indicates preventive controls to ensure authorization decisions are authenticated and securely transmitted (prevents unauthorized decisions being applied).

IA-9(2).differentiation_note: Targets secure, authenticated transmission and verification of authorization decisions (PDP→PEP flows) rather than general service identity or message-level authentication; measurement requires decision-specific logs and acknowledgement records.

IA-10.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of adaptive (risk-/context-based) authentication decisions: percent of high-risk authentication attempts that triggered step-up authentication or blocking, count of prevented unauthorized authentications, and policy coverage across applications and user populations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Adaptive authentication is primarily about coverage and correct enforcement (percent of risky attempts challenged or blocked), while counts and rates (attempt volumes, challenge counts) and trends (changes over time) provide supporting context for effectiveness and capacity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count authentication attempts with contextual risk attributes (risk score, location, device posture) and record whether an adaptive action (step-up MFA, block, additional verification) was applied; calculate percentage of high-risk attempts where adaptive policy triggered and the success/failure outcome; measure per-application policy coverage and policy-version timestamps to show enforcement scope and currency.
data_signals: authentication event logs (timestamp, user, result: success/fail)
authentication risk score or risk classification per event
context attributes: source IP, geolocation, device posture/agent, browser fingerprint
adaptive action taken per event (no action, step-up MFA, block, additional verification)
MFA challenge events and outcomes (challenge issued, challenge succeeded/failed)
policy identifier and version applied to each decision
count of authentication attempts labeled high-risk per period
overrides or bypass events and justification logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous, timestamped authentication events with embedded risk score, applied policy id/version, and recorded adaptive action (step-up/MFA/block) across all production applications.
Strong: Automated correlation between risk score and enforced action stored in logs/decision records enabling percent-of-high-risk-attempts-challenged calculation.
Weak: Periodic manual reports or screenshots of configuration without event-level logs or missing contextual attributes (device posture, geolocation, risk score).
Weak: Records that show policy exists but lack evidence of enforcement (no decision/action events).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Adaptive Authentication' describes proactive enforcement of authentication decisions (step-up/MFA/block) to prevent unauthorized access, so it is primarily preventive.

IA-10.differentiation_note: This control is focused on dynamic, context-driven enforcement (risk scores, device posture, step-up actions) rather than static access configuration or periodic reviews, so measurements must capture event-level context and applied adaptive decisions continuously.

IA-11.measurement_goal: Measure the enforcement and coverage of re-authentication controls by reporting the percentage of sessions and sensitive/privileged transactions that required and completed re-authentication per policy, and the count/rate of transactions that bypassed or failed re-authentication requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Re-authentication is a preventive enforcement control where coverage (percentage of sessions/transactions compliant) best demonstrates effectiveness; counts and rates of re-auth events and bypasses are useful secondary measures and trends show ongoing adherence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate (1) percentage of active sessions that triggered a re-authentication event within the policy window (by application and user role), (2) count and rate of sensitive or privileged transactions that occurred without a required re-authentication, (3) median and P90 time between initial authentication and enforced re-authentication, and (4) configuration settings for session timeout and re-auth triggers per application.
data_signals: reauthentication event logs (timestamp, user, application, transaction id, trigger reason)
session creation and termination records (session id, auth timestamp, last activity)
privileged/sensitive transaction audit logs with reauth-required flag
session timeout and re-authentication configuration settings per application
failed re-authentication attempts (counts, user, timestamp, error code)
exemptions/whitelists for re-authentication (policy exceptions)
user role / privilege mapping (to identify privileged transactions)
application enforcement status (enabled/disabled for re-auth)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous machine-generated re-authentication event logs correlated with session and transaction logs and enforced configuration settings (shows automated enforcement and coverage).
Moderate: periodic system reports showing re-auth counts and configuration snapshots (shows enforcement but not continuous coverage).
Weak: manual attestations, one-off screenshots, or single point-in-time tests (do not demonstrate ongoing enforcement or coverage).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Re-authentication' indicates a preventive enforcement mechanism that requires credential revalidation to prevent continued unauthorized access.

IA-11.differentiation_note: Unlike other identity controls that focus on initial authentication or access provisioning, IA-11 specifically requires measuring ongoing session/transaction-level enforcement and revalidation coverage (e.g., reauth events, timeout settings, and privileged transaction checks).

IA-12.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of new and changed accounts that have a recorded identity proofing event (evidence type, source, timestamp, verifier) and the count of accounts provisioned without an approved proofing record.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage/compliance percentages (what percent of accounts had proofing) with counts of exceptions and trends over time to show improvement or regression.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percentage of account provisioning events that reference a completed identity-proofing record (fields: proofing_method, evidence_type, evidence_identifier, verifier_id, proofing_timestamp, outcome). Also report the raw count of provisioning events lacking such a record and list of affected user IDs.
data_signals: account_creation_event (user_id, timestamp, created_by)
identity_proofing_record (user_id, proofing_method, evidence_type, evidence_id, verifier_id, proofing_timestamp, outcome)
proofing_document_metadata (document_type, document_number, issuer_country)
provisioning_request_id linked to proofing_record
proofing_outcome (passed/failed/conditional)
exception/waiver records for account creation without proofing
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped proofing records linked to each account and stored in an auditable system with verifier identity and evidence metadata.
Weak: isolated scanned documents or ad-hoc emails without consistent metadata, timestamps, or linkage to the account provisioning event.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control's primary purpose (identity proofing) prevents issuance of credentials without verified identity, so it functions as preventive.

IA-12.differentiation_note: This is the broad, baseline identity-proofing control covering all account provisioning; its measurements focus on coverage and linkage between proofing records and provisioning events, whereas siblings specify particular proofing modalities or approvals.

IA-12(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of identity-proofing exceptions that received documented supervisor authorization before account activation, and the time between exception request and supervisor approval.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive/compensating enhancement, auditors need coverage percentages (how many exceptions were authorized) plus counts and trends to surface policy exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of provisioning events that lacked standard proofing but have an associated documented supervisor authorization record (supervisor_id, authorization_timestamp, justification) and median time-to-authorization for those exceptions.
data_signals: proofing_exception_record (user_id, reason_code, exception_request_timestamp)
supervisor_authorization_record (supervisor_id, authorization_timestamp, justification_text, linked_exception_id)
account_activation_timestamp
linkage between provisioning_event and authorization_record
policy-defined allowable exception reasons
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured authorization records with supervisor identity, timestamp, and linked exception IDs stored in the access governance system.
Weak: verbal approvals or unlinked email approvals that cannot be programmatically matched to provisioning events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring supervisor authorization for exceptions prevents inappropriate account activation without proofing; classification as preventive is supported by 'authorization' and 'supervisor' in the enhancement title.

IA-12(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on measuring documented supervisory approvals for proofing exceptions rather than general proofing coverage.

IA-12(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of proofing records that include required identity evidence attributes (evidence type, source, unique identifier, issuing authority) and the distribution of evidence types used.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive enhancement requires verifying presence and quality of evidence fields; percentages show compliance and distributions show reliance on specific evidence types.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of identity_proofing_record entries that have required evidence fields populated (evidence_type, evidence_source, evidence_identifier, issuer) and counts by evidence_type; identify missing or nonconforming evidence entries.
data_signals: identity_proofing_record.evidence_type (e.g., passport, driver_license, electronic ID)
identity_proofing_record.evidence_source (self-attested, government, third-party)
identity_proofing_record.evidence_identifier
evidence_issuer and issuer_country
proofing_record_completeness_flag
nonconforming_evidence exceptions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured evidence metadata for each proofing record that matches allowed evidence_type and includes issuer and identifier.
Weak: free-text notes indicating evidence presented without standardized fields or missing issuer/identifier.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Specifying required evidence attributes ensures proofing completeness and prevents inadequate evidence from being accepted; this is preventive in nature.

IA-12(2).differentiation_note: Specifies measurement of evidence quality and completeness (fields and types) rather than whether proofing occurred at all.

IA-12(3).measurement_goal: Measure the rate and percentage of submitted identity evidence records that were validated/verified (automated or manual) and the outcome distribution (verified, failed, manual review required).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement requires tracking verification actions and outcomes (a detective/preventive mix) — rates of verification attempts and success/failure percentages best capture effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and rate of identity evidence verification attempts per proofing record, verification method (automated check, manual review, third-party assertion), verification outcome, and time-to-verification for each evidence item.
data_signals: evidence_verification_event (proofing_id, method, verifier_id, timestamp, outcome)
verification_method (automated, manual, third-party)
verification_outcome (verified/failed/needs_review)
time_from_submission_to_verification
escalation_records for failed or ambiguous verifications
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: logged verification events with method, timestamp, verifier id and outcome linked to proofing records.
Weak: undocumented manual checks or unsupported assertions without verifiable logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement focuses on validating/verifying evidence and recording outcomes, which detects weak or fraudulent evidence and therefore has a detective function.

IA-12(3).differentiation_note: Focuses on tracking verification actions and outcomes for evidence (who/when/how outcomes were determined), not merely presence of evidence.

IA-12(4).measurement_goal: Measure the count and percentage of proofing events conducted in-person vs. remote, and verify that in-person validations include verifier identity, location, and signed attestation recorded at the time of proofing.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: In-person proofing is preventive; auditors want coverage percentages (what share of required in-person proofing occurred) plus counts and trends to validate procedural adherence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of proofing_records flagged as 'in-person' that include verifier_id, verifier_affiliation, physical_location (or branch), signed_attestation (or scanned signature), and proofing_timestamp; count of in-person records missing any required field.
data_signals: identity_proofing_record.proofing_method == 'in-person'
in_person_verification_record (verifier_id, verifier_affiliation, location_id, timestamp, attestation_signature)
attendance or appointment logs
linked identity evidence presented at in-person event
missing_field exceptions for in-person records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured in-person verification entries with verifier identity, signed attestation and location recorded at the time of proofing.
Weak: retrospective attestations or undated scanned documents lacking linkage to a known verifier and timestamp.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mandating in-person validation is a preventive step to ensure identity authenticity before credential issuance, indicated by 'In-person Validation' in the control title.

IA-12(4).differentiation_note: Specifically measures attributes of in-person proofing events (verifier identity, location, signed attestation) rather than remote or evidence-only proofing.

IA-12(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of proofing events that include address confirmation using an approved method (documented address evidence, third-party verification, or mailed confirmation) and the count of accounts where address could not be confirmed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Address confirmation is preventive; measuring coverage as a percentage of proofing events with acceptable address confirmation and distribution of methods used is most useful.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of proofing_records that include an address_confirmation entry with confirmation_method (document, third-party verification, mailed token), confirmation_timestamp, and confirmation_result; count of records with unconfirmed or disputed addresses.
data_signals: identity_proofing_record.address_confirmed_flag
address_confirmation_record (user_id, method, timestamp, result)
address_evidence_document (type, issuer)
third_party_address_verification_response
mailed_confirmation_token_status
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured address confirmation records from approved methods with timestamps and results.
Weak: self-attested addresses without supporting evidence or unresolved mailed confirmation tokens.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Address confirmation prevents misuse by ensuring address validity before account issuance; the title explicitly references 'Address Confirmation' which is preventive.

IA-12(5).differentiation_note: Narrow focus on address-specific proofing: measurements must capture method and result of address confirmation rather than general identity evidence.

IA-12(6).measurement_goal: Measure the share and count of identities accepted based on externally-proofed assertions (third-party IdP or federal/state proofing) and track verification level/assurance associated with those external proofs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Accepting externally-proofed identities is an operational decision where rates (how often external proofs are accepted), percentages and trends show reliance on external sources and any change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate and percentage of new account proofing events that relied on external proofing artifacts (e.g., IdP assurance level, third-party proofing token) including external_provider_id, assurance_level, assertion_timestamp, and whether local verification was performed.
data_signals: external_proofing_assertion (user_id, provider_id, assurance_level, assertion_timestamp)
linked_account_provisioning_event
local_verification_performed_flag
provider_trust_policy mapping (which providers/assurance levels are acceptable)
exceptions where external assertion was accepted without meeting required assurance_level
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: cryptographically verifiable assertions from trusted providers with recorded assurance level and linkage to provisioning events.
Weak: acceptance based on emailed attestations or undocumented third-party statements without verifiable metadata.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Allowing externally-proofed identities is a preventive policy controlling how identities are accepted; the control title 'Accept Externally-proofed Identities' signals prevention of inadequate local proofing.

IA-12(6).differentiation_note: Measures reliance on and characteristics of external proofing sources (provider, assurance level, linkage) rather than internal proofing mechanics.

IR-1.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, currency, and dissemination of incident response policy and associated procedures: the percentage of required IR documents that are approved and within review cycle, the percentage of targeted personnel who have acknowledged or completed training on those procedures, and the count of policy updates and reviews within the mandated timeframe.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive governance control: auditors need coverage/completion percentages (document currency, acknowledgement, training completion). Counts and trends support drill-down (number of updates, review frequency) and a compliance score summarizes posture.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number and percentage of required incident response documents (policy, procedures, playbooks) that are present in the authoritative repository, have an approved version and last_review_date within the required review period, and have dissemination/acknowledgement records for the target audience; plus counts of updates and documented approvals over the past 12 months and training completion rates tied to the procedures.
data_signals: policy document identifier and version from document repository
policy document approval_date and approver
policy document last_review_date and next_review_due_date
document repository access/distribution logs (who accessed/received the policy)
employee acknowledgement receipts or signed attestation records tied to policy ID
incident response training enrollment and completion records mapped to employee IDs
record of policy change history (change timestamps and change author)
scheduled review/maintenance calendar entries for IR documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative, version-controlled repository entries with approval metadata and automated acknowledgement records; training LMS completion records linked to policy IDs and employee directories; audit trail of review events.
Weak: undated PDF copies on shared drives, one-off email attachments without acknowledgement, or manual notes of 'policy exists' without timestamps or approver identity.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes documented policies and procedures (preventive governance) to guide incident response activities, focusing on existence, dissemination, and currency.

IR-1.differentiation_note: This entry focuses specifically on measurement of policy/procedure existence, currency, approval and dissemination (document governance) rather than operational detection, response execution, or incident handling metrics.

IR-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required personnel who complete agreed incident response training on schedule, the count and frequency of incident response exercises conducted, and the proportion of exercise-derived corrective actions closed within policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: IR-2 is a preventive control focused on training coverage and currency — auditors need coverage ratios (percentage of staff trained) as primary evidence, with counts of exercises and trend/score to show quality and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of required roles that have completed the current incident response training within the required timeframe, the number of exercises completed per reporting period, and the count and closure rate of post-exercise corrective actions. Include role-to-training mapping and timestamps to allow grouping by role and recency.
data_signals: training assignment and completion records (user id, role, course id, completion timestamp)
required-role training roster (mapping of roles to required courses)
exercise schedule and execution logs (exercise id, date, facilitators)
after-action reports (AAR) existence and AAR action item records (id, owner, status, due date, closed timestamp)
assessment or quiz scores from training (user id, score, pass/fail)
training version or syllabus reference and effective date
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated LMS completion records with timestamps and role mapping are strong evidence.
Post-exercise AARs with tracked action-item closure are strong evidence of effective training.
Manual attendance sheets or one-off slide-deck acknowledgements are weak evidence for ongoing training effectiveness.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Name and intent focus on training to reduce incident likelihood and improve preparedness, which is preventive in nature.

IR-2.differentiation_note: This entry covers overall IR training program coverage and outcome tracking (completion %, exercise counts, AAR closure) rather than specifics of simulations, automation, or breach-focused drills addressed by its sub-controls.

IR-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the number and frequency of simulated incident events run, participant coverage (percentage of required staff who participated), exercise evaluation scores, and the rate at which exercise-identified deficiencies are remediated within policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Simulated events are intended to validate preparedness and participation; percentage (participation/completion and pass rates) shows coverage while counts and rates show exercise throughput and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count simulated event executions per period, measure participant attendance per required role and compute participation percentage, capture exercise evaluation/scoring results, and track AAR action items with timestamps to compute remediation rate and time-to-closure.
data_signals: simulation/exercise execution logs (exercise id, date, type: simulated event, scope)
participant rosters with role and attendance (user id, role, attended boolean, timestamp)
exercise evaluation scores or graded results (exercise id, participant id, score, evaluator)
after-action reports and listed findings/action items (item id, severity, owner, due date, closed timestamp)
detection and response timing observed in simulation (time-to-detect, time-to-contain recorded during exercise)
exercise schedule adherence vs. policy (planned vs. executed)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated exercise execution logs, participant attendance tied to identity, and measurable detection/containment timestamps provide strong evidence.
AARs without tracked action items or single anecdotal facilitator notes are weak evidence of effective simulated-event training.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control demands running simulated events to build/validate capability and readiness, which prevents or reduces impact of real incidents.

IR-2(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on metrics tied to simulated exercises (participation %, detection/containment times, AAR-driven remediation) versus general training coverage or automated environment availability.

IR-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure availability and use of automated training environments: percent of exercises executed using automated environments, time-to-provision for automated environments, and success rate of automated scenario executions and auto-assessments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated training environments are about coverage and reliability; percentage of exercises run via automation and availability uptime are primary indicators, with counts and trend to show usage and stability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of planned exercises that used automated training environments, average provisioning time for environments, execution success rate of automated scenarios, and auto-assessment score distributions to show automated training effectiveness.
data_signals: automation/orchestration job logs (job id, scenario id, start timestamp, end timestamp, success/failure)
environment provisioning timestamps and duration (request time, provisioned time, teardown time)
environment availability/uptime metrics for training infrastructure (host/service status)
count of automated scenarios executed per period (scenario id, run count)
auto-assessment results produced by automated environments (participant id, score, pass/fail)
failure/error logs for provisioning or scenario execution with failure reasons
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-generated automation logs with success/failure, timestamps, and unique scenario ids are strong evidence.
Ad hoc manual notes that an environment was 'available' without logs, or exercises claimed as 'automated' without orchestration logs, are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires automated environments to deliver training and exercises, which prevents lapses in preparedness and increases coverage through automation.

IR-2(2).differentiation_note: Targets infrastructure and automation signals (provisioning times, orchestration logs, uptime) rather than human participation or exercise evaluation metrics emphasized in sibling controls.

IR-2(3).measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and effectiveness of breach-focused exercises: percent of required stakeholders participating in breach drills, average detection-to-notification time observed in drills versus notification time targets, and the remediation/action-closure rate for breach-scenario findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Breach drills are judged by completion/participation percentages (notification coverage and stakeholder engagement) and time-based performance for notification/remediation, so percentage and rate metrics best evidence compliance and timeliness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track the percent of scheduled breach drills executed, participation percentage of required stakeholders (security, legal, PR, execs), detection-to-notification times observed during drills, and closure rates/times for breach-specific AAR action items compared to required notification time targets.
data_signals: breach drill execution logs (drill id, date, scope: 'breach')
notification timestamps captured during drill (detection timestamp, notification sent timestamp, recipient list)
stakeholder participation records (roles present, attendance timestamps)
after-action report items specific to breach scenarios (item id, severity, owner, due date, closed timestamp)
measured detection-to-notification intervals from drill telemetry
regulatory/notification checklist completion indicators (signed checklists or automated confirmations)
evidence_strength_indicators: Drill telemetry with precise detection and notification timestamps and recorded stakeholder confirmations is strong evidence.
Claims of 'breach drill completed' without notification timestamps, participant evidence, or AAR action tracking are weak evidence for breach readiness.

control_type_classification.reasoning: This control calls out breach-specific training and drills to ensure timely notification and response, which is preventive (reduces impact and ensures compliance readiness).

IR-2(3).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on breach-oriented performance metrics (notification timeliness, stakeholder notification coverage, breach AAR closure) unlike broader training or automation-focused siblings.

IR-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of scheduled incident response tests executed on-schedule and the count and severity of findings per test, plus the remediation completion rate for test-identified action items against policy timelines.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Testing programs are typically assessed by coverage/completion (percentage of tests completed on schedule) with counts of tests/findings as supportive metrics to show activity and trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of scheduled IR tests and count executed per reporting period, timestamps (scheduled start, actual start, end), test type (tabletop/exercise/live), number of findings by severity per test, existence of after-action reports, and remediation ticket status and closure timestamps for action items raised during tests.
data_signals: IR test schedule entries (test_id, scheduled_date, type)
IR test execution logs (test_id, actual_start_time, actual_end_time)
after-action reports / test_result artifacts (test_id, findings list, severity)
action item / remediation tickets linked to test_id (creation_time, assignee, status, close_time)
participant/role attendance lists for each test
test_plan version identifier and sign-off record
test completion status flags (completed/cancelled/postponed)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated test schedules and execution logs with timestamps and unique test IDs are strong evidence;
linked remediation tickets with timestamps and status updates are strong;
single point-in-time PowerPoint summaries or undocumented verbal reports are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control specifies running tests to ensure preparedness (a preventive assurance activity); auditors will look for coverage/completion metrics and remediation tracking.

IR-3.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall IR testing program (scheduling, execution, and remediation tracking) versus enhancements that focus specifically on automation, plan coordination, or continuous improvement metrics.

IR-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and success rate of automated incident response tests (automation run counts per asset/service), percent of detection/playbook coverage exercised automatically, and failure/error counts per automation run.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated testing is evidenced by execution counts and run outcomes; counts of automated runs and pass/fail outcomes are primary, with coverage percentages as supportive metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of automated test executions per automation playbook or detection rule per period, execution start/end timestamps, pass/fail status and error codes, mapping of automated tests to assets/services, and coverage percentage of IR playbooks exercised automatically.
data_signals: automation run logs (playbook_id, run_id, start_time, end_time, status)
automation error/failure logs and stack traces
mapping of playbook_id to detection / response use-case and asset/service list
metrics of automated test pass rate (successful_runs / total_runs)
schedules/configuration of automated tests (cron/CI job definitions)
test artifacts/results stored in SIEM or orchestration platform
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-generated automation run logs with run IDs, timestamps, and pass/fail codes are strong evidence;
ad-hoc manual screenshots or undocumented manual runs are weak evidence for automated testing claims.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Automated testing ensures ongoing preparedness and coverage through machine-executed tests, aligning with preventive measurement of coverage and execution rates.

IR-3(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on machine-executed/automated test runs, requiring machine-readable run logs and playbook-to-asset coverage mapping rather than manual exercise artifacts.

IR-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of incident response tests that include participation or scenario integration with related plans (e.g., BCP, DR, continuity), the count of joint exercises executed, and the existence of cross-referenced plan artifacts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Coordination is best shown as coverage/alignment percentages (what share of IR tests include related plans) with counts of joint exercises to show activity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of IR tests that explicitly reference or exercise related plans, attendance/representation by related plan owners per test, cross-reference matrix entries linking IR plan sections to BCP/DR/COOP plan sections, and records of integrated test scenarios and outcomes.
data_signals: joint exercise schedules and test IDs indicating participating plans
attendance rosters showing representation from BCP/DR/COOP owners
cross-reference matrix or plan version history linking IR plan to related plans
integrated exercise after-action reports (noting coordination issues)
issues/requirements identified for plan alignment (tickets or change requests)
evidence_strength_indicators: Formal cross-reference matrices and signed joint exercise after-action reports are strong evidence of coordination;
informal email threads without documented plan updates are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires aligning and coordinating IR tests with other plans (a preventive assurance activity to avoid gaps), so measurements focus on alignment coverage and joint-exercise counts.

IR-3(2).differentiation_note: This enhancement uniquely requires evidence that IR testing is integrated with other organizational plans (BCP/DR/etc.), so measurements must show cross-references, participant lists, and joint-exercise artifacts.

IR-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure the rate at which after-action findings from IR tests are addressed: count of action items opened per test, percent closed within required timeframe, mean time-to-close, and trend in repeat findings over successive tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Continuous improvement is assessed by remediation velocity and closure rates (rates) and by trends showing reduction in repeat findings or improved closure times.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of findings/action items generated by each test, timestamps for action item creation and closure, assignee and remediation ticket linkage, average and median time-to-close, and tags indicating incorporation of lessons into plan updates.
data_signals: after-action report findings list (finding_id, severity, recommended_action)
remediation/action item tickets linked to finding_id (create_time, assignee, status, close_time)
plan revision history with references to lessons learned
repeat finding indicators (finding fingerprinting across tests)
metrics dashboard showing time-to-close distribution
evidence_strength_indicators: Tracked remediation tickets with timestamps and direct links to the originating test finding are strong evidence of continuous improvement;
uncoupled slide decks that list findings without tracked remediation or plan updates are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: This enhancement focuses on taking corrective actions from test findings and improving processes, so classification as corrective is appropriate.

IR-3(3).differentiation_note: This enhancement is uniquely about remediation velocity and learning adoption (closure rates and plan updates), not about test execution or automation itself.

IR-4.measurement_goal: Measure incident detection and response effectiveness: count of incidents detected, median time-to-detect, median time-to-contain/resolve, and percentage of incidents closed within defined response SLAs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Incident Handling requires measuring response velocities (detection/response rates) and volumes; rate metrics (e.g., incidents responded per time / SLA compliance rate) best reflect detection and corrective performance, with counts and trends to show volume and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of incident records created per source, timestamps for detection/containment/closure per incident, incident severity label, and SLA target versus actual time-to-contain and time-to-resolve for each incident.
data_signals: incident ticket/record creation timestamps
incident detection alert timestamps (by sensor/source)
time-to-detect (detection_timestamp - incident_start or first alert)
time-to-contain and time-to-resolve per incident
incident severity/classification field
incident closure timestamps and resolution notes
SLA/response target definitions referenced by incident severity
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated incident telemetry and timestamped workflow logs (strong) vs. manual, one-off incident summaries (weak).
Complete per-incident timestamps and severity labels across all detection sources (strong) vs. sampled or retrospective reports without timestamps (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Incident Handling' emphasizes detecting and responding to incidents (detective & corrective); primary monitoring of detection/response metrics aligns with detective classification.

IR-4.differentiation_note: This control is the broad incident handling requirement — it focuses on overall detection and response timing and closure metrics, unlike siblings that target specific mechanisms (automation, reconfiguration, external correlation).

IR-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure the extent and effectiveness of automated incident handling: count of incidents with automated handling invoked, automation success rate, and mean time saved by automation compared with manual response.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated processes are best evidenced by counts of automation executions and coverage; percentages (success/failure rates) and rates (automation-triggered responses per time) show effectiveness and frequency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of automation/playbook executions tied to incident IDs, success/failure outcome for each execution, timestamps of automation start and completion, and classification of incidents where automation was applied.
data_signals: automation/runbook execution logs with timestamps
incident ID linkage to automation executions
automation success/failure status and error codes
time saved metric (automated completion time vs. manual completion time)
number of incidents with automation invoked vs. total incidents
automation coverage by incident severity/type
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-generated automation execution logs correlated to incident IDs and outcomes (strong) vs. claims or manual spreadsheets of automation usage (weak).
High automation coverage with low failure rate and auditable timestamps (strong) vs. sporadic automation without logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly references automated incident handling processes (actions taken to contain/resolve incidents), which are corrective mechanisms that act after detection.

IR-4(1).differentiation_note: Focused specifically on measuring automation executions and outcomes tied to incidents, not on overall incident volumes or manual response metrics.

IR-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure dynamic reconfiguration events used during incidents: count and success rate of reconfiguration actions, mean time-to-reconfigure, and percentage of incidents where dynamic reconfiguration was applied successfully.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Dynamic reconfiguration speaks to coverage and correctness (what portion of incidents used/benefitted from reconfiguration and whether those actions succeeded), so percentage metrics for coverage/success are primary with counts and trends to show frequency and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Records of configuration change events triggered by incident response (with timestamps), outcome status (applied/failed/rolled-back), affected systems/assets, and time from trigger to completion per reconfiguration event.
data_signals: configuration reconfiguration event logs with timestamps
triggering incident ID linkage
reconfiguration success/failure/rollback status
time-to-reconfigure (start to applied)
asset/system identifiers impacted
change approval or automated trigger metadata
evidence_strength_indicators: Immutable system configuration change records (automated orchestration logs) correlated to incidents (strong) vs. manual change notes or undocumented reconfigurations (weak).
High successful apply rate and documented rollback history (strong) vs. frequent failed or manual-only reconfigurations (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes dynamic reconfiguration as a response action to incidents (corrective), focusing on executing configuration changes to mitigate impact.

IR-4(2).differentiation_note: Targets measurement of configuration-change actions triggered during incidents (event-level reconfiguration logs and success rates), unlike other controls that focus on detection or automation broadly.

IR-4(3).measurement_goal: Measure continuity capabilities during incidents: service availability percentage during incident windows, compliance with RTO/RPO targets, and count of successful failovers or continuity actions executed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Continuity of operations is demonstrated by availability and recovery compliance; percentage metrics (availability %, RTO/RPO attainment) directly show whether continuity objectives are met, supplemented by counts of failovers and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Service availability during incident periods (uptime %) per critical service, RTO/RPO targets and measured recovery times per incident, and records of continuity actions (failover, degrade-to-backup) with timestamps and outcomes.
data_signals: service availability/uptime metrics during incident windows
RTO and RPO target definitions and measured recovery times
failover/fallback event logs with timestamps
continuity action execution logs and outcomes
affected service identifiers and criticality tier
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated availability metrics and time-stamped failover logs proving successful continuity with measured RTO/RPO attainment (strong) vs. anecdotal summaries or post-incident narratives without timestamps (weak).
Consistent attainment of SLA/RTO targets across incidents (strong) vs. single successful test only (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Continuity of operations focuses on maintaining availability and recovery targets (preventive/availability control) though it also involves corrective actions; primary evidence is coverage/attainment which aligns with preventive measurement.

IR-4(3).differentiation_note: Measures continuity and recovery attainment (availability %, RTO/RPO compliance) rather than detection or automated response mechanics.

IR-4(4).measurement_goal: Measure information correlation capability: count of correlated incidents/events, correlation rule coverage, and false positive/true positive rates for correlated alerts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Correlation capability is best evidenced by counts of correlated events and correlation outcomes; rates (true positive rate, false positive rate) and distributions by source provide quality and source attribution.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of raw events ingested, number of correlation engine matches producing correlated incidents, counts of correlation-rule triggers, and true/false positive classification for correlated alerts.
data_signals: event ingestion counts by source
correlation engine match logs with timestamps
correlated incident IDs and contributing event IDs
correlation rule IDs and versions applied
true positive / false positive labeling for correlated alerts
time from event ingestion to correlation output
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated correlation logs showing event-to-incident linkage and rule IDs (strong) vs. manual summaries of correlation results (weak).
Low false-positive rate and documented rule tuning history (strong) vs. high false-positive rate without tuning evidence (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Information correlation is focused on combining detection telemetry to identify incidents (detective), evident from 'Information Correlation' which describes correlating data to detect incidents.

IR-4(4).differentiation_note: Unique focus on measuring correlation engine performance and quality (correlated event counts and TP/FP rates) rather than response actions or automation counts.

IR-4(5).measurement_goal: Measure automatic disabling actions taken during incidents: count of automatic disables (accounts, services, endpoints), success/failure rate, and mean time from detection to disable action.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automatic disabling is operationalized by discrete disable events, so counts of disable events and percentages of successful disables (versus attempted) most directly evidence the control, with rates for timing.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Event logs showing automatic disable actions (account disables, service shutdowns, endpoint quarantines) with timestamps, triggering incident ID, result status (applied/failed/rolled-back), and time between trigger and completed disable.
data_signals: automatic disable event logs with timestamps and actor (automation/system)
target identifier (account ID, host ID, service ID)
disable outcome status (applied/failed/rolled-back)
triggering detection alert ID
time-from-detection-to-disable metric
rollback or re-enable event logs
evidence_strength_indicators: System-generated disable execution logs correlated to detection alerts (strong) vs. manually recorded disables without automation logs (weak).
High apply-success rate and documented rollback safety checks (strong) vs. frequent failed disables requiring manual intervention (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Automatic disabling is an action taken to stop or mitigate active incidents (corrective), as indicated by the phrase 'Automatic Disabling of System.'

IR-4(5).differentiation_note: Measures automated disable actions and their outcomes (counts and times) specifically, rather than general response or correlation metrics.
