evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of designated areas with an inventoried emergency lighting fixture that has a successful functional test within the policy test window; counts of fixtures with failed tests and time-to-repair per fixture. Include battery health/readings and last maintenance action per fixture to allow drilling from failure to remediation.
data_signals: inventory of emergency lighting fixtures by facility and room
last functional test timestamp per fixture
functional test result (pass/fail) per test run
battery voltage/health telemetry per fixture
maintenance/repair ticket entries linked to fixture ID with open and close timestamps
power supply failover / outage test logs (time to illumination)
illumination measurement (lux) readings from periodic tests
fixture operational status flag (in-service / out-of-service)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated test logs and telemetry showing pass/fail, battery health, and last test timestamps for each fixture across facilities; trending test-pass rates and repair MTTR.
Weak: single point-in-time inspection photos or manual checklist entries without timestamps, no inventory mapping to fixtures, or ad-hoc maintenance notes.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Emergency Lighting' implies prevention of unsafe conditions by ensuring lighting is present/operational, so measurement focuses on coverage and functional readiness.

PE-12.differentiation_note: This control addresses general emergency lighting installation, testing and maintenance across facilities (coverage, test pass rates, repair MTTR) rather than prioritization for critical mission areas.

PE-12(1).measurement_goal: Measure coverage and operational readiness of emergency lighting specifically for essential mission and business function areas: percent of mission-critical locations with functional emergency lighting and the time-to-illumination and remediation for any failures in those areas.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement targets mission-critical areas so percentage coverage and pass rates are primary to demonstrate required coverage; counts and rates (failures, MTTR) show operational impact and responsiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of identified essential mission and business function areas that have at least one verified operational emergency lighting fixture; per-fixture last-test result and battery health, plus time-to-repair for any failed fixtures located within mission-critical areas.
data_signals: mapping of 'essential mission/business function' areas to facility/room identifiers
inventory of emergency lighting fixtures tagged by criticality (mission-critical vs non-critical)
last functional test timestamp and result for fixtures in mission-critical areas
battery charge/health telemetry for mission-critical area fixtures
power failover / emergency event logs showing time-to-illumination in critical areas
maintenance ticket lifecycle for failures in mission-critical locations
overdue or failed-test counts filtered to essential mission/business functions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous telemetry and automated test records proving each mission-critical area has at least one operational fixture, plus outage event logs demonstrating illumination within acceptable timeframes.
Weak: checklist-based declarations that critical areas are covered without fixture-level test logs, or post-incident anecdotal reports without pre-existing test evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement specifies emergency lighting for essential mission/business functions (a preventative requirement to ensure continuity and safe egress in critical areas).

PE-12(1).differentiation_note: Unlike PE-12 which covers emergency lighting broadly, PE-12(1) narrows measurement to lighting that supports essential mission/business functions and requires stricter evidence (mapping, continuous telemetry, and full-coverage baseline).

PE-13.measurement_goal: Measure the presence, coverage, and operational status of fire protection systems across facilities (detection and suppression), plus the on-schedule completion rate of required inspections and tests to demonstrate the fire protection program is implemented and maintained.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PE-13 is a preventive control focused on coverage and maintenance of fire protection systems; percentage metrics (coverage, inspection completion) best show compliance, with counts and trends useful for event volumes and historical compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of facilities/areas with required fire detection and suppression systems installed and operational (functional test pass), plus percent of required inspections/tests completed on schedule. Ideal metrics include (1) count of facilities required vs. facilities covered, (2) latest operational test timestamp per device, and (3) inspection completion timestamps and remediation items tracked to closure.
data_signals: facility inventory with fire detection/suppression requirement flag
device inventory (detectors, alarm panels, suppression units) with serial and location
operational test results and timestamps for detection and suppression devices
inspection and maintenance records with scheduled date and completion timestamp
open deficiency/remediation ticket count originating from inspections
alarm activation and suppression activation event logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated device test logs and time-stamped inspection records are strong evidence; manual or unsigned checklist snapshots are weak.
Complete device inventory mapped to facility inventory is strong; missing asset-location linkage is weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title and sub-controls focus on providing and maintaining fire protection systems and inspections, which prevent loss from fire.

PE-13.differentiation_note: This entry covers the program-level measurement of fire protection coverage and inspection/compliance across facilities, whereas the sub-controls specify detection, suppression, activation/notification, or inspection-specific measurements.

PE-13(1).measurement_goal: Measure detection system operational availability and the effectiveness of automatic activation and notification: count alarm activations, percent of activations that generated successful notifications, and sensor uptime across monitored zones.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control, event counts (alarm activations) and rates (notification success rate) directly evidence detection/notification performance; percentage shows reliability of notifications.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of alarm/detection events and the notification delivery outcomes per event; device-level uptime and last self-test timestamp. Specific fields: alarm_event.timestamp, alarm_event.device_id, notification_delivery.status, notification_delivery.timestamp, device.last_self_test, device.uptime_percent.
data_signals: alarm activation event logs (timestamp, device_id, event_type)
notification delivery logs/receipts (recipient_id, status: delivered/failed, timestamp)
device uptime and last self-test timestamps for detection sensors
false alarm classification and root-cause notes
zone-to-device mapping (which facility/zone each sensor covers)
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time alarm and notification logs with delivery receipts and device self-test telemetry are strong evidence; ad-hoc manual confirmation or single test reports are weak.
High device uptime and regular successful self-tests strengthen evidence; frequent missed notifications or long notification latencies weaken it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Detection Systems — Automatic Activation and Notification' indicates the control detects fire events and notifies responders, a classic detective function.

PE-13(1).differentiation_note: Focused specifically on detection event logging and notification delivery metrics (activation counts, notification success), unlike other PE-13 entries that focus on suppression or inspections.

PE-13(2).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and operational reliability of automatic suppression systems and their ability to activate and notify: percent of required areas with functioning automatic suppression and count/rate of successful automatic activations with associated notifications.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control should be measured by percentage coverage/operational status of suppression systems, with counts of activations and trends for historical reliability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of required zones with installed, inspected, and operational automatic suppression systems (e.g., sprinklers, gas suppression), plus counts of suppression activations and notification outcomes. Specific data fields: suppression_unit.id, suppression_unit.status, last_inspection.timestamp, activation_event.timestamp, notification_delivery.status.
data_signals: suppression system inventory and zone coverage map
functional test and inspection records for suppression units (timestamps, results)
suppression activation event logs (time, unit_id, cause)
notification/event forwarding logs tied to suppression activations
open maintenance/remediation tickets resulting from failed suppression tests
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated activation event logs with correlated suppression sensor telemetry and notification receipts are strong evidence; single manual activation tests without logs are weak.
Comprehensive zone coverage mapping and recent successful functional tests increase evidence strength; gaps between required and installed systems weaken it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on automatic suppression system operation and activation/notification, preventing fire spread via suppression capabilities.

PE-13(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures suppression system coverage and activation reliability (automatic suppression + notification), distinct from PE-13(1) which targets detection and notification only.

PE-13(3).measurement_goal: Although withdrawn (incorporated into PE-13(2)), measurement should treat any legacy requirements as part of suppression coverage: percent of areas where automatic fire suppression capability (formerly required by PE-13(3)) is present and operational.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive suppression-related control (withdrawn into PE-13(2)), percentage coverage of suppression capability remains the primary measure, with counts for activations and trends for reliability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of facilities/areas that retain required automatic fire suppression capability, with device operational status and last functional test timestamp. Fields: suppression_asset.exists_flag, suppression_asset.operational_status, suppression_asset.last_test_date, remediation_ticket.count.
data_signals: suppression asset register indicating legacy PE-13(3) applicability
operational test and inspection logs for suppression devices
activation events (if any) tied to these systems
maintenance and remediation records
evidence_strength_indicators: Incorporation of PE-13(3) requirements into PE-13(2) documented and tests executed across assets is strong; lack of mapping from legacy requirements to current assets is weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The entry references automatic fire suppression (preventive capability) and is explicitly labeled 'preventive' in the input, though it is withdrawn and incorporated into PE-13(2).

PE-13(3).differentiation_note: This entry documents the withdrawn/legacy automatic suppression requirement and focuses on ensuring those legacy requirements are folded into PE-13(2) coverage; measurement maps legacy scope to current suppression assets.

PE-13(4).measurement_goal: Measure inspection and testing compliance: percent of required fire protection inspections, tests, and maintenance activities completed on schedule and mean time to remediate inspection findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Inspections are compliance activities where percentage of on-schedule completion and remediation closure rates indicate effectiveness; counts and trends support volume and improvement tracking.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of scheduled inspections and functional tests completed within their required timeframe, count of open findings from inspections, and mean time to remediate those findings. Concrete fields: inspection.schedule_date, inspection.completion_timestamp, inspection.result_code, finding.ticket_id, finding.open_timestamp, finding.close_timestamp.
data_signals: inspection schedule and completion records (timestamped)
test reports for detection and suppression devices
inspection findings list with severity and remediation ticket IDs
remediation ticket lifecycle (open/close timestamps, assignee)
recurring inspection frequency definitions per asset/area
evidence_strength_indicators: Time-stamped digital inspection reports and linked remediation tickets with closure evidence are strong; paper checklists or undocumented verbal confirmations are weak.
High percent on-time completion and short MTTR indicate strong control; many overdue inspections or stale open findings weaken evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name 'Inspections' implies periodic review and testing to detect deficiencies, classifying it primarily as a detective control.

PE-13(4).differentiation_note: This entry is inspection/testing-focused (schedule compliance and remediation MTTR) rather than measuring detection event handling or suppression activation coverage.

PE-14.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and operational status of environmental controls (facility HVAC, power continuity, fire/suppression, leak detection) across defined assets and count environmental incidents and control failures over time to demonstrate control presence and effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PE-14 is primarily a preventive control requiring coverage/compliance metrics (percentage of facilities/assets with required environmental controls). Counts of incidents and trends over time support detection of failures and long-term effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope facilities/assets that have active environmental controls deployed (HVAC monitoring, fire suppression, water-leak detection, UPS/generator monitoring) and the count and rate of environmental control failures or incidents per facility per reporting period; include timestamps and remediation outcome for each incident.
data_signals: facility inventory with environmental control installation flags (HVAC, fire suppression, leak sensors, UPS/generator)
sensor telemetry streams (temperature, humidity, smoke, water-leak) with timestamps
power event logs from UPS/generator test and failure records
fire suppression activation logs and incident reports
environmental control maintenance and configuration records
environmental incident/incident ticket timestamps and remediation completion timestamps
coverage ratio (assets with required controls / total in-scope assets)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated sensor telemetry and event logs with continuous timestamps (strong)
Cross-referenced facility inventory showing active sensors and recent health checks (strong)
Periodic manual inspection checklists without sensor logs (weak)
Single point-in-time photographs or ad-hoc attestations without logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Environmental Controls' implies preventive measures to protect facilities and assets from environmental hazards and ensure required controls are deployed.

PE-14.differentiation_note: This entry covers broad coverage and operational-status measurement across facilities — unlike the siblings, it focuses on overall deployment and incident counts rather than automation or alarm notification behavior.

PE-14(1).measurement_goal: Measure percent of in-scope environmental control points with automatic control capability enabled and the rate at which automatic controls execute expected actions (e.g., automatic shutdown, alarm escalation, HVAC setpoint adjustments) when thresholds are crossed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As an automatic/preventive enhancement, auditors want coverage percentages (how many control points have automation enabled) and rates/counts of automatic actions fired to verify automation operates as intended.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of required environmental control points (sensors/actuators) configured for automatic responses, along with counts and timestamps of automatic actions triggered per facility (with outcome success/failure) and linkage to the triggering sensor readings.
data_signals: inventory of sensors/actuators with 'automatic control' configuration flag
automatic action event logs (action type, timestamp, initiating sensor and value)
sensor telemetry at trigger time (temperature, humidity, smoke, leak)
action outcome/result logs (success/failure, retry attempts)
change records showing when automatic controls were enabled/modified
automated action-to-ticket correlation (whether action created a remediation ticket)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated action event logs correlated with sensor telemetry (strong)
Recorded success/failure of automated actions and retries (strong)
Manual operator notes without automatic event logs (weak)
Infrequent test logs without production execution records (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Automatic Controls' indicates preventive automated responses to environmental conditions, so measurement focuses on configuration coverage and successful automatic actions.

PE-14(1).differentiation_note: This entry specifically targets automated response configuration and execution (did automation exist and perform), unlike PE-14 which measures general coverage and incidents.

PE-14(2).measurement_goal: Measure the volume and timeliness of environmental alarms and notifications: count of alarms generated, percentage of alarms that generated notifications to responsible personnel, and mean time to acknowledge and respond to environmental alarms.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective/alerting control where counts and rates of alarm events, notification deliveries, and response actions are most informative; trends show whether alarm volume or response performance improves.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of environmental alarms generated per facility per period, percentage of alarms that resulted in notifications to on-call personnel, timestamps for alarm generation/notification/acknowledgement/response to compute MTTA/MTTR for environmental events.
data_signals: alarm event logs (alarm id, type, severity, timestamp, source sensor)
notification delivery logs (recipient id, channel, timestamp, delivery status)
acknowledgement and incident ticket timestamps (ack timestamp, response start, remediation complete)
alarm suppression or escalation policy records
alarm-to-remediation mapping (ticket id linked to alarm id)
alarm frequency by sensor/location
evidence_strength_indicators: End-to-end alarm logs with notification delivery receipts and acknowledgement timestamps (strong)
Correlation between alarm events and incident tickets with lifecycle timestamps (strong)
Spreadsheet logs of alarms without delivery/acknowledgement metadata (weak)
Ad-hoc manual confirmations without machine timestamps (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name 'Monitoring with Alarms and Notifications' explicitly describes detection and alerting functions, which are detective in nature.

PE-14(2).differentiation_note: This entry focuses on alert generation, notification delivery, and response timelines — distinct from PE-14's coverage and PE-14(1)'s automated control actions.

PE-15.measurement_goal: Show coverage and operational status of physical water-damage protections for critical facility areas: percent of critical areas with installed/maintained water-detection or mitigation controls, count of water-intrusion incidents by location, and time-to-detect/mitigate water events.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PE-15 is primarily a preventive physical-protection control — auditors need coverage/installation and maintenance coverage (best expressed as percentages). Counts and rates supplement with incident frequency and failure rates.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of critical facility zones instrumented or configured with water-detection / mitigation controls and last-test timestamps; count of water intrusion events per zone and timestamps for detection and mitigation; sensor/device uptime and maintenance/test frequency per device.
data_signals: facility area inventory with criticality classification (zone IDs)
water-detection device inventory (device ID, location, device type e.g., moisture sensor, float switch, sump pump)
device last-test/inspection timestamps from maintenance logs
device health/uptime telemetry or heartbeat timestamps
water-intrusion incident logs (detection timestamp, detection method, location)
remediation/mitigation action logs with start and end timestamps
sump pump activation and drain system status logs
inspection/checklist records showing periodic physical checks
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous device telemetry, last-test timestamps, automated health/uptime records and correlated incident logs showing end-to-end detection-to-mitigation timestamps.
Weak: One-off photos or undated manual inspection notes; annual penetration-style reports without device-level health/uptime or event logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Water Damage Protection' indicates measures intended to prevent or limit water damage (installation/maintenance of protections) rather than only post-fact detection.

PE-15.differentiation_note: Focuses on physical coverage, installation and maintenance of water-mitigation/detection controls (coverage and inspection evidence), not on automated alerting or telemetry which is addressed by the enhancement.

PE-15(1).measurement_goal: Demonstrate automated detection and alerting for water intrusion: sensor online percentage, rate/count of automated leak alerts (including false-positive rate), and median time from automated alert to acknowledgement/remediation action.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automation enhancement emphasizes detection/alerting behavior; rates (alerts per time, alert-to-acknowledge rates) best capture operational effectiveness, with percentages for sensor uptime/coverage and counts for incidents.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-device automated alert counts and timestamps, sensor online/heartbeat percentage over the audit period, time from automated alert timestamp to ticket/acknowledgement and to mitigation action, and false-positive rate of automated alerts.
data_signals: automated sensor alert logs (alert ID, device ID, timestamp, alert severity/type)
sensor heartbeat/connection logs showing uptime and last-seen timestamp
automation/runbook execution logs for automated mitigation actions
alert-to-acknowledgement timestamps (ticket or incident acknowledgement records)
incident remediation timestamps correlated to originating automated alert
false positive/false negative adjudication records from alert triage
sensor configuration and firmware/firmware-patch status
test/simulation exercise logs for automated detection
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous telemetry and automated alert logs with correlated ticketing/mitigation timestamps and false-positive adjudication records.
Weak: Manual spreadsheets of sensor locations or ad-hoc reports without machine-generated alert timestamps or automation execution logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement label 'Automation Support' implies automated detection/alerting capability (detective) that supports response, rather than solely preventive physical installation.

PE-15(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement specifically targets automated detection, alerting, and automated mitigation telemetry (continuous-machine signals) rather than manual/physical protection coverage or inspection records.

PE-16.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of delivery and removal events for system components that have documented authorization, a recorded chain-of-custody, and an associated inventory update; and count instances of undocumented or unauthorized removals within the reporting period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control requiring coverage/compliance evidence (percent of events with required documentation). Counts of unauthorized removals and trending of incidents are useful secondary measures to show frequency and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of delivery/removal events for all tracked system components that include (1) documented authorization, (2) chain-of-custody or custody transfer record, and (3) an authoritative inventory update within the defined process window; and the raw count of delivery/removal events lacking one or more of those items and associated incident records.
data_signals: delivery and removal logs with timestamps
authorization records/signatures for each delivery or removal
chain-of-custody or custody transfer forms
asset inventory change records (asset_id, prior_location, new_location, timestamp)
receiving/inspection reports indicating tamper or damage
shipping manifests and vendor/shipper receipts
discrepancy or incident reports tied to delivery/removal events
CCTV/entrance badge logs correlated to delivery/removal timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated logging that links delivery/removal events to authorization records and immediate inventory updates; matched CCTV/badge logs and signed custody forms.
Weak: periodic manual spreadsheets or one-off receipts without matching inventory updates, or annual attestations without event-level records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes controlling and documenting movement of system components (preventing unauthorized removal) so it functions primarily to prevent loss or tampering.

PE-16.differentiation_note: Unlike sibling controls that may focus on access control or logging, PE-16 specifically requires measurement of physical delivery/removal events, custody documentation, and inventory reconciliation — so metrics must link event records to asset inventory and custody paperwork.

PE-17.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical business functions and personnel with documented, tested alternate work site arrangements and the average recovery/access time observed during alternate-site activation tests versus the organization’s required recovery/access timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage-style metrics (percentage of critical functions/personnel covered). Counts (number of tests, sites) and trends (improvements in RTO/access time over time) provide useful secondary context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of critical business functions and personnel mapped to an alternate work site, timestamps of documented tests/exercises for each alternate site, measured recovery/access times (RTO/RPO or time-to-first-productivity) observed during tests, and contractual/SLA evidence for alternate site availability. These should be reported as percent coverage of critical functions and personnel with a passing test within the policy-defined period and average/median recovery/access times from the most recent tests.
data_signals: inventory of critical business functions with assigned alternate work site identifier
alternate site test/exercise execution timestamps and results (pass/fail)
measured recovery/access time per test (time from activation to available workspace/network)
staff roster with alternate-site access credentials and assigned equipment records
alternate site SLA/contract documents and stated availability targets
network/connectivity test results at alternate site (bandwidth/latency) during exercises
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated test logs and time-stamped measurements from scheduled, recurring alternate-site exercises (strong).
Signed SLA/contract documents that specify availability targets and proof of periodic validation (strong).
Ad-hoc anecdotal reports or single-point manual attestations without test artifacts (weak).
One-off tabletop discussion notes without measured activation time or objective test results (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name indicates establishing alternate work sites to maintain operations (a preventive availability/resilience measure) rather than event detection or remediation.

PE-17.differentiation_note: Unlike sibling controls focused on detection or access control, PE-17 specifically requires measurement of alternate-site readiness and tested access for personnel and functions; measurement must emphasize test results, coverage, and recovery/access timing rather than logs or vulnerability counts. Note: no detailed control description was provided, so focus_area selection is the closest match to infrastructure/readiness requirements.

PE-18.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of system components that have an authoritative, recorded physical or logical location (facility/site, data center, cloud region/availability zone) in the asset registry/CMDB and track changes/relocations against approved authorization records.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive coverage control: auditors need percent coverage of components with documented locations (percentage) plus counts of undocumented/overdue items and distribution of components by site/region to identify concentration and gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of total inventoryed system components and count/percentage of those with an authoritative location attribute (facility/site or cloud region/availability zone) recorded in the CMDB or asset registry; number of location-change requests and their approval timestamps; count of components with mismatched or missing location metadata.
data_signals: asset inventory records with location/site attribute (CMDB entries)
cloud resource tags: region and availability zone
data center / facility identifiers assigned to system components
network topology records mapping components to physical sites
approved relocation/change request records with timestamps
physical facility authorization or hosting agreements referencing system components
exceptions or undocumented-component reports (undocumented asset count)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CMDB records and cloud tag enforcement showing location for >95% of assets, automated reconciliation between inventory and network topology, and approved relocation change records with timestamps.
Weak: periodic manual spreadsheets or one-time inventory reports without timestamps or change history; undocumented or inconsistent location tags across systems.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Location of System Components' implies preventing risks by documenting and controlling where components are placed (preventive coverage of asset locations).

PE-18.differentiation_note: This control is focused on authoritative inventory and location attributes for every system component (coverage and documentation), unlike siblings that may address facility-specific controls or other environmental protections.

PE-18(1).measurement_goal: Demonstrate that the 'Facility Site' enhancement has been retired and its requirements have been incorporated into PE-23 by measuring mapping completeness from PE-18(1) items to PE-23 requirements and documenting that no active procedures remain under PE-18(1).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Because the text indicates the enhancement was 'Withdrawn: incorporated into PE-23,' the primary measurement is completeness/coverage of the incorporation (percentage of PE-18(1) requirements mapped to PE-23) and counts of remaining artifacts referencing PE-18(1).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of PE-18(1)-specific procedures/policies and operational artifacts that remain active versus the count successfully migrated or documented under PE-23; timestamps of migration and evidence that facility-site items are tracked under PE-23.
data_signals: control catalog mapping showing PE-18(1) -> PE-23 incorporation
"Withdrawn: incorporated into PE-23." text present in control documentation revisions
policy/procedure documents updated to reference PE-23 instead of PE-18(1)
list of operational artifacts (checklists, CMDB fields) migrated to PE-23 scope
open action items or remediation tickets still referencing PE-18(1)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative control catalog and updated policy documents explicitly showing 'incorporated into PE-23' with migration dates and no operational artifacts remaining under PE-18(1).
Weak: informal notes saying 'withdrawn' without updated procedures, or mixed references to PE-18(1) and PE-23 across documentation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Originally a preventive enhancement (facility/site placement of components), but current description states it is withdrawn and incorporated into PE-23, so classification is preventive conceptually but operationally deprecated.

PE-18(1).differentiation_note: This entry documents the withdrawn enhancement specific to 'Facility Site' and focuses on verifying migration to PE-23 rather than measuring location coverage itself; measurement should map artifacts to PE-23 rather than duplicate PE-18 coverage metrics.

PE-19.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of measures that prevent compromising emanations and information leakage (e.g., percent of in-scope systems with emission-mitigation controls applied, count of detected compromising-emanation incidents, and time-to-remediate emission-control deficiencies).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PE-19 is primarily preventive (coverage of emission controls) so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance %) best show how many systems meet emission-protection requirements; counts of incidents and trending of detections are useful secondary measures for residual leakage and detection effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope devices/systems with documented and tested emission-mitigation controls (shielding, filtering, physical separation) and the count and details of any detected compromising emanation incidents including timestamps and remediation status; include records of EM emissions test results, installation/configuration of countermeasures, and corrective actions with remediation timestamps.
data_signals: EM/compromising-emanation test reports (date, device/system, test results)
inventory of systems/devices with emission-mitigation countermeasures (shielding/filtering status)
deployment records for countermeasures (installation date, configuration)
detected emanation incident logs (incident id, timestamp, affected system, detection mechanism)
remediation tickets and timestamps for emission-control deficiencies
wireless/egress signal monitoring alerts correlated to devices
policy/procedure evidence specifying emission-control requirements
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated monitoring alerts and continuous EM test logs mapped to asset inventory and remediation tickets showing timely fixes;
Moderate: periodic lab EM test reports with asset mapping but without continuous monitoring;
Weak: one-off manual statements or undocumented mitigation claims without test reports or remediation evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control focuses on preventing information leakage via emissions and implementing mitigation measures (shielding, countermeasures), which is preventive in nature.

PE-19.differentiation_note: Focuses on operational/technical emission controls and continuous detection of compromising emanations for systems and devices rather than policy-level requirements (which are covered by the PE-19 enhancement).

PE-19(1).measurement_goal: Measure existence, currency, and application of national-level emissions policies and procedures (e.g., percent of required policy elements present, evidence that procedures reference national emissions requirements, and number of systems assessed for compliance with those policies).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Enhancement is procedural — auditors need to see policy coverage and compliance rates (percentage of required policy elements and systems covered). Counts of policy reviews, assessments, and comparisons against national requirements are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Existence and currency of documented national emissions policies and procedures mapped to organizational assets, percent of in-scope systems referenced by the policy, records of policy dissemination/training, and counts of compliance assessments or exceptions with remediation evidence.
data_signals: official national emissions policy documents and revision history (document id, effective date)
organizational procedures referencing national emissions requirements (procedure doc, distribution list)
mapping of policy requirements to in-scope systems/assets
records of policy dissemination and training attendance
compliance assessment reports against national emissions policies
exceptions/waivers recorded with justification and remediation plans
change log of policy updates and approval records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal published policy documents with version history, asset mapping, documented assessments against national requirements, and evidence of remediation for noncompliance;
Weak: informal guidance emails or undocumented procedures without distribution, assessments, or remediation records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: This enhancement requires establishment and application of national-level emissions policies and procedures, which is a preventive governance control.

PE-19(1).differentiation_note: This entry focuses on policy existence, currency, dissemination and compliance assessments tied to national emissions requirements, not the technical detection/remediation of emanations covered by the base PE-19 control.

PE-20.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and freshness of the authoritative asset inventory (percentage of assets tracked), the frequency of discovery/monitoring events, and the count of unauthorized or untracked assets detected and onboarded within required timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Asset tracking requires coverage metrics (percentage of assets in the authoritative inventory) as the primary verifier of effectiveness; counts (unauthorized assets, missing records), trends (inventory growth/removal), and rates (time-to-onboard) provide supporting operational detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percentage of enterprise assets present in the authoritative inventory (CMDB/asset database) with a last-observed timestamp within the policy window (e.g., 7 days). Count unauthorized/untracked devices discovered by network/endpoint discovery per period and measure time from initial detection to creation/update of the CMDB record. Report agent heartbeat/telemetry coverage and reconciliation discrepancy counts between discovery scans and CMDB.
data_signals: authoritative asset inventory (CMDB) records with asset_id, asset_type, owner, classification
last_observed / last_seen timestamp per asset (from discovery or agent telemetry)
network discovery scan logs (device IP/MAC, discovery timestamp)
endpoint agent heartbeat logs (agent_id, last_checkin, version)
unauthorized or untracked asset detection alerts with detection timestamp
asset onboarding events (creation timestamp in CMDB) and reconciliation change logs
asset classification/completeness flags (missing owner, missing tag)
reconciliation report: number of discovered assets not present in CMDB
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated discovery and agent heartbeat logs reconciled to CMDB showing >90-100% coverage and short time-to-onboard for newly discovered assets
Strong: scheduled automated reconciliation reports and low discrepancy counts over time
Weak: periodic manual spreadsheets, ad-hoc discovery scans, or single point-in-time inventories without timestamps
Weak: CMDB entries without last_observed timestamps or without reconciliation to discovery logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title emphasizes 'monitoring' and 'tracking', which implies ongoing detection of asset presence and changes (detective); tracking also supports preventive completeness but primary operation is continuous detection.

PE-20.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on continuous discovery, inventory completeness, and reconciliation (asset coverage and freshness) rather than vulnerability remediation or access control; measurement needs center on CMDB reconciliation, discovery logs, and agent heartbeats. Note: control description was not provided; keywords and signals are inferred from the control name.

PE-21.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and operational status of EMP (electromagnetic pulse) protective measures for critical facilities and assets: percentage of critical assets located in EMP-hardened zones, count of installed EMP mitigation devices and verified testing events, and the rate of successful shielding/grounding test outcomes versus failures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PE-21 is fundamentally preventive (protecting assets from EMP); auditors need coverage/coverage percentage (assets with EMP protection). Counts of devices and trending test results are useful secondary measures to show ongoing validation and remediation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A data-set mapping critical assets to facility zones with an EMP_hardening flag, test/inspection records for shielding and grounding that include timestamps and outcomes, and inventory records of installed EMP mitigation equipment with installation and maintenance dates. Combine these to report percent coverage of critical assets, number of failed vs passed integrity tests, and the time-since-last-test per asset/facility.
data_signals: asset inventory records with EMP_hardening_flag and criticality_tier
facility shielding test reports (test_type, timestamp, tester, pass/fail, measured attenuation)
grounding/continuity test logs (timestamp, resistance measurements)
installed mitigation device inventory (device_type, model, install_date, maintenance_date)
maintenance/inspection work orders for EMP-related components (creation/completion timestamps)
backup/off-site redundancy test logs (activation/drill timestamps and success status)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: recurring, timestamped shielding and grounding test logs tied to specific asset IDs and signed by testers; automated monitoring outputs (e.g., continuous attenuation sensors) where deployed.
Moderate: recent maintenance work orders and photographic evidence of installed shielding without measured test results.
Weak: design/installation drawings or one-time vendor attestations without post-installation test records or periodic re-tests.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name indicates measures to protect assets from EMP effects (hardening/shielding), which are preventive controls implemented before an event.

PE-21.differentiation_note: This control is focused on physical/infrastructure hardening and periodic integrity testing for EMP hazards (not software vulnerability scanning or access controls); measurement must emphasize asset-to-facility mapping, physical test records, and device inventories. Note: the control description was not provided so keywords and signals are inferred from the control name and typical PE-21 requirements.

PE-22.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and accuracy of unique identification/marking for system components (physical and logical) by reporting the percentage of components that have an assigned identifier/marking linked to the asset inventory and the count of unmarked or mismarked components discovered during audits.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Component marking is primarily about coverage and conformity (what proportion of components are properly marked), so percentage is the most useful metric; counts of unmarked items and trends over time help show remediation progress and residual risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of components (by inventory record) that have a verified marking/identifier field populated and linked to a photo/RFID/serial in the asset registry; count unmarked components and record last verification timestamps and any approved exceptions. Include audit results showing reconciliations between physical/virtual scans and the inventory.
data_signals: asset registry records with marking/identifier field (serial, tag_id, barcode, RFID)
label/tag photos or scan logs (timestamped) linked to asset IDs
RFID/barcode scan event logs showing tag discovery and location
inventory reconciliation reports showing matched vs unmatched assets
inventory audit results with timestamps and auditor identifiers
change request or configuration item records referencing component identifier
exception/waiver records for unmarked components with approval metadata
last verification/validation timestamp per asset
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous or frequent automated scan logs (RFID/barcode) reconciled against the asset registry and photo evidence with timestamps; high percentage coverage and decreasing trend of unmarked items.
Weak: one-time manual photos or a static inventory list without reconciliation, or exception lists without approvals or verification timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Marking components is intended to prevent misidentification and support asset control, so it functions as a preventive control.

PE-22.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on physical/logical identification coverage and inventory linkage (marking/labels and verification), not on asset lifecycle or vulnerability remediation; limited original description required inferring measurement items from the control name.

PE-23.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of organizational facilities that have completed a documented facility location risk assessment and meet defined location criteria (environmental hazard, proximity to threats, redundancy requirements), and count facilities with identified location-based deficiencies requiring remediation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control focused on coverage and compliance, percentage metrics (e.g., % of facilities meeting location criteria) directly show coverage; counts and distributions of non-compliant facilities and trending of assessment results help prioritize remediation and show change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A facility-level dataset where each facility record includes: assessment completion timestamp, assessment score or risk-rating, checklist results for location criteria (e.g., flood/seismic risk, proximity to hazardous sites), mitigation status, and remediation timestamps. The primary metric is the percent of facilities with a completed assessment and rated as compliant against the organization's location policy.
data_signals: facility inventory records (facility_id, address, criticality tier)
facility location risk assessment reports (assessment_id, completed_timestamp, assessor)
assessment score or risk-rating by facility
checklist results for environmental hazards (flood, earthquake, fire) per facility
distance/proximity measurements to identified hazards or critical infrastructure
mitigation/remediation action items with status and remediation timestamps
signed facility compliance attestations or acceptance records
facility change/relocation approval records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal, dated risk assessment reports per facility with structured scores and signed attestations; geospatial hazard data or third-party hazard ratings linked to facility records; tracked remediation items with closure timestamps.
Weak: one-off narrative statements, unsourced location descriptions, or undated floor plans without formal assessment scores or remediation tracking.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Facility Location' implies controls aimed at preventing location-related risks by selecting/assessing sites and requiring criteria compliance, which is a preventive measure.

PE-23.differentiation_note: This control focuses on site-selection and location risk assessment coverage (policy compliance and facility-level risk ratings), distinct from sibling controls that might address operational physical access, monitoring, or access reviews; note: description was not provided so keywords and measures are based on the control name 'Facility Location' and typical NIST treatment.

PL-1.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, currency, formal approval, dissemination, and periodic review of organizational policies and their associated procedures (e.g., percentage of required policies with current approved version, count of policies reviewed within the mandated review period, and percent of personnel who have acknowledged receipt of key policies).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PL-1 is a preventive governance control where auditors want coverage and compliance rates (e.g., percent of policies current and acknowledged). Counts of documents and trends over review cycles are useful secondary metrics to show completeness and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of required policy documents and the percentage that have a current approved version, recorded approver and approval date, and documented next review date; count and percentage of personnel who have signed/acknowledged main policies within the required timeframe; count of policy exceptions/waivers and their approval statuses.
data_signals: policy document repository entries with title, version, effective_date, next_review_date, and approver
document version history / change log entries with timestamps and author IDs
formal approval records (electronic signatures or approval workflow timestamps)
policy dissemination logs (distribution lists or notification timestamps)
employee policy acknowledgement records with user_id and timestamp
scheduled review calendar entries and completed review timestamps
policy exception/waiver requests and approval records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized document management system with immutable version history, recorded approver signatures, and automated acknowledgement tracking; documented next_review_date and completed review logs for each policy.
Weak: ad-hoc file shares with timestamps only, manual emails announcing policies without tracked acknowledgments, or single point-in-time snapshots of documents without version or approval metadata.

control_type_classification.reasoning: As a 'Policy and Procedures' control, it establishes governance to prevent noncompliant behavior by defining and requiring approved, documented policies and review processes.

PL-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on governance artifacts (existence, approval, currency, dissemination, and acknowledgements) rather than technical enforcement or incident detection; measurement must emphasize document metadata, review cadence, and personnel acknowledgements rather than technical logs.

PL-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of information systems that have an approved System Security and Privacy Plan (SSPP) on file that maps required security/privacy controls to system components, and track currency by measuring the age and last-review date distribution of those SSPPs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PL-2 is preventive (plans and documented controls). A percentage of systems with current SSPPs shows coverage; counts and trends show volume and currency over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) total number of systems in the authoritative system inventory, (2) number of those systems with an approved SSPP artifact, (3) approval date and last-review timestamp per SSPP, and (4) whether each SSPP maps required controls to system components or privacy requirements. Produce percent coverage and age distribution of SSPPs.
data_signals: authoritative system inventory entries (system_id, system_owner, system_tier)
SSPP document metadata (document_id, associated system_id, approval_date, last_review_date, approver)
SSPP artifact presence flag (boolean) per system
mapping table or checklist showing controls/privacy requirements mapped to system components
SSPP version history / change log timestamps
list/count of systems missing SSPP
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory showing SSPP attachments with signed approval dates and recent review timestamps (continuous/versioned artifacts);
Weak: single stored PDF without metadata or an undated draft SSPP discovered by manual search

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires documented plans (preventive documentation of controls and privacy measures) so classification is preventive.

PL-2.differentiation_note: This entry covers overall existence and currency of SSPPs across systems (coverage and review cadence), whereas the sub-controls focus on specific plan contents (CONOPS, architecture, coordination).

PL-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of systems whose SSPP includes a documented Concept of Operations (CONOPS) describing operational roles, mission use, and major system interactions, and track whether the CONOPS is formally approved or marked 'withdrawn/incorporated'.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Presence/coverage of a CONOPS artifact is a preventive coverage metric best expressed as a percentage of systems; counts and trends show how many systems include CONOPS and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each system, record whether a CONOPS document is attached to the SSPP (document_id, approval_status). Compute % of systems with an approved CONOPS, count of systems with CONOPS marked 'Withdrawn' or 'incorporated into PL-7', and track last-update timestamps.
data_signals: SSPP attachments list including CONOPS documents (document_id, system_id)
CONOPS document metadata (approval_status, approval_date, last_review_date)
CONOPS status notes (e.g., 'Withdrawn', 'incorporated into PL-7')
count of systems lacking CONOPS
change history for CONOPS documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative repository entries linking CONOPS artifacts to system SSPPs with approval metadata;
Weak: references to CONOPS in free-text system descriptions without an attached/approved document

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control targets required plan content (Concept of Operations) which prevents misunderstanding of operational behavior, consistent with preventive classification.

PL-2(1).differentiation_note: Unlike PL-2 which measures plan coverage and currency broadly, PL-2(1) specifically measures presence and approval status of CONOPS artifacts linked to SSPPs (and notes withdrawn/incorporation status).

PL-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of systems whose SSPP includes a documented functional architecture (e.g., diagrams or component lists) that describes system components and interfaces, and record the presence/absence of such artifacts and their last-update timestamps.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Functional architecture is an artifact whose coverage is best measured as a percentage of systems; counts and distribution by system tier show where architectural documentation is missing.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each system, capture whether a functional architecture artifact is attached to the SSPP (document type, diagram presence), the last updated date, and component/interface enumeration. Produce percent coverage and distribution across system criticality tiers.
data_signals: SSPP attachments list including functional architecture diagrams (document_id, system_id)
functional architecture metadata (diagram_present boolean, last_updated_date)
component inventory extracted from architecture (component_id, interface_list)
notes/status (e.g., 'Withdrawn', 'incorporated into PL-8')
count of systems without architecture documentation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: stored architecture diagrams tied to system IDs with versioning and timestamps;
Weak: informal whiteboard images or undocumented component lists without an authoritative attachment

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring functional architecture documentation is preventive (design-time control) to ensure systems are understood and configured correctly.

PL-2(2).differentiation_note: This control focuses on the inclusion of architecture diagrams/component inventories in SSPPs, unlike PL-2 which addresses overall plan existence and currency.

PL-2(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percent and count of systems that have documented coordination records in their SSPPs showing planned coordination with other organizational entities (e.g., roles, points-of-contact, coordination agreements), and track whether coordination items are active or marked withdrawn/incorporated.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Coordination and documented agreements are preventive artifacts; percent coverage shows how many systems have formal coordination, while counts and trends show volume and changes in coordination status.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each system, record whether the SSPP contains a coordination section (list of external/organizational entities, POCs, coordination agreements or notes), the status (active, withdrawn, incorporated into PL-2), and the last coordination update timestamp. Compute percent coverage and count of outstanding coordination actions.
data_signals: SSPP coordination sections (documented organizational entities, contact names/roles)
coordination agreement artifacts or references (document_id, system_id)
status notes (e.g., 'Withdrawn', 'incorporated into PL-2')
last coordination update timestamp
open coordination action items or dependencies count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed coordination agreements or recorded POC lists in an authoritative repository with timestamps;
Weak: ad-hoc email threads or undocumented verbal coordination referenced in notes

affinity_keywords: Plan and Coordinate with Other Organizational Entities

control_type_classification.reasoning: This control requires documented coordination (planning and agreements) to prevent gaps between organizational entities, fitting a preventive classification.

PL-2(3).differentiation_note: PL-2(3) uniquely measures documented inter-organizational coordination artifacts and their status, whereas the other PL-2 entries measure plan existence or specific plan content types (CONOPS, architecture).

PL-3.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that each system has a current System Security Plan (SSP) and that SSPs are updated and approved according to organizational policy (e.g., percentage of systems with SSPs updated within the required revision window and count of SSP updates and approvals in the period).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors will want coverage/adhesion measures (percentage of systems with current SSPs). Counts of updates and trends over time support evidence of ongoing maintenance and frequency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of systems with an SSP artifact, last_update_timestamp per SSP, number of SSP updates and approval signatures per system within the policy-defined period, and number of systems missing an SSP or with overdue updates compared to policy-defined revision frequency.
data_signals: System Security Plan document existence flag per system
SSP last_update_timestamp (ISO8601)
SSP version identifier
SSP approver identifier/signature and approval timestamp
SSP change_log entries (timestamp, author, change summary)
Policy-defined SSP revision frequency / required update interval
Count of systems scoped to an SSP vs. total in inventory
Overdue_SSP_count (systems with last_update older than policy interval)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized, versioned SSP repository with machine-readable last_update timestamps, approver metadata, and automated alignment to system inventory (enables continuous/periodic queries).
Weak: ad-hoc document snapshots, single screenshots of an SSP, or informal email approvals (point-in-time artifacts without version history).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Named 'System Security Plan Update' (preventive by design) and description states 'Withdrawn: incorporated into PL-2', indicating the original preventive purpose (maintaining SSPs) now resides in PL-2.

PL-3.differentiation_note: This control is withdrawn and measurements should be consolidated under PL-2; its unique measurement need is explicit tracking of SSP existence, versioning, update timestamps, and approvals rather than operational artifacts like scans or incident logs.

PL-4.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of Rules of Behavior issuance and acknowledgement (percentage of active accounts with a current signed acknowledgement), and count and trend of policy exceptions and enforcement actions to demonstrate the policy is communicated and enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive policy control, auditors need coverage/acceptance rates (percentage) of policy acknowledgements; counts of exceptions or enforcement actions and trending over time are useful secondary measures to show residual risk and enforcement activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure (1) the percentage of active employees/accounts with a current Rules of Behavior acknowledgement (by policy version) and acknowledgement timestamp; (2) the count of approved exceptions and outstanding exceptions; and (3) count and timestamps of enforcement actions or disciplinary records referencing the policy, all indexed by user and organizational unit so compliance queries can be built (e.g., SELECT count(*) WHERE acknowledgement IS NULL AND status='active').
data_signals: policy acknowledgement records (user_id, acknowledgement_timestamp, policy_version)
active user/account inventory (user_id, status, org_unit)
exceptions/waiver logs (exception_id, user_id, approval_timestamp, expiry_date, approver)
disciplinary/enforcement action tickets referencing Rules of Behavior (ticket_id, user_id, action_type, timestamp)
policy distribution records or communications log (distribution_timestamp, targeted_population)
training/completion records when Rules of Behavior is paired with training (user_id, course_id, completion_timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated acknowledgement records with timestamps tied to identity provider accounts and policy_version metadata and continuous exception logs;
Weak: annual manual sign-off spreadsheets or unsigned PDF distributions with no user mapping or timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control (Rules of Behavior) establishes required behavior and acknowledgements to prevent misuse, so it is primarily preventive.

PL-4.differentiation_note: This control is the organization-wide Rules of Behavior policy and focuses on measuring issuance, acknowledgement, exceptions, and enforcement; it differs from PL-4(1) which targets external/social media usage restrictions and monitoring of external application access.

PL-4(1).measurement_goal: Demonstrate enforcement and monitoring of social media and external site/application usage restrictions by measuring percentage of users subjected to the restrictions, counts of blocked/denied external application connections, and counts of confirmed violations or exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires verifying coverage (percentage of users/devices subject to the restriction and with enforcement applied); counts of blocked connections and distribution by application/domain help show enforcement scope and where violations concentrate.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure (1) the percentage of endpoints and user accounts covered by the social-media/external-app usage restrictions (based on allow/deny lists and policy assignment); (2) weekly counts of blocked/denied connections to prohibited social media/external application domains (with timestamps, source user/device, destination domain); and (3) counts and details of approved exceptions and documented violations (incident tickets) tied to policy breaches.
data_signals: access control / firewall/proxy block logs with domain/application and source_user/source_ip (timestamped)
web filter or CASB deny/allow logs (application_id, action=blocked/allowed, user_id, device_id, timestamp)
endpoint agent telemetry indicating blocked external application launches (device_id, process, timestamp)
policy assignment records mapping users/devices to social media/external app restrictions (policy_id, assigned_population)
exceptions/waiver logs for external application usage (exception_id, user_id, scope, expiry)
incident/ticket records for violations involving social media or external sites (ticket_id, user_id, description, remediation_status)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated proxy/CASB/firewall logs showing blocked connection events correlated to user and device identities and up-to-date policy assignment records;
Weak: manual reports of violations or periodic spreadsheets without timestamped enforcement log correlation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement enforces restrictions on social media and external application usage to prevent policy violations, which is a preventive enforcement control.

PL-4(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement specifically targets social media and external site/application usage and requires monitoring/enforcement log signals (proxy/CASB/firewall/endpoint), unlike the parent PL-4 which centers on overall Rules of Behavior acknowledgements and exceptions.

PL-5.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that the PL-5 Privacy Impact Assessment control has been formally withdrawn and that its requirements and responsibilities have been incorporated into RA-8 by measuring the completeness of mapping from PL-5 to RA-8 and the presence of versioned policy/control-catalog updates documenting the incorporation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A percentage metric (coverage/mapping completeness) directly shows how much of PL-5's scope was incorporated into RA-8; counts of mapped clauses and comparison of document versions help verify completeness and correctness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of PL-5 clauses or requirements that are mapped to RA-8 in the official traceability/mapping matrix; presence (yes/no) and version/timestamp of a control catalog or policy update marking PL-5 as withdrawn and referencing RA-8; count of change management records or tickets that effected the incorporation.
data_signals: official traceability/mapping matrix rows: PL-5 clause ID -> RA-8 clause ID
control catalog / policy document revision history entries referencing 'PL-5' and 'RA-8' with timestamps and authors
change management ticket(s) that record 'Withdrawn: incorporated into RA-8' with status and closure date
documented RA-8 text excerpt showing incorporated PL-5 requirements
number of PL-5 requirement items mapped vs. total PL-5 items (mapping completeness)
attestation or approval record from control owner indicating incorporation complete
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: versioned control catalog and policy documents that explicitly mark PL-5 as withdrawn and contain a traceability matrix mapping each PL-5 requirement to RA-8, plus closed change tickets.
Moderate: minutes or memos describing the incorporation without a full mapping matrix.
Weak: informal email statements or absence of versioned documents; legacy references to PL-5 remaining in operational procedures.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The input labels PL-5 as preventive and it concerns privacy impact assessments (a preventive activity); the description notes it is 'Withdrawn: incorporated into RA-8,' so classification remains preventive but confidence is reduced because the control text indicates reassignment.

PL-5.differentiation_note: Unlike operational PL controls, this entry is focused on confirming formal withdrawal and documented incorporation into RA-8 (mapping and policy/version evidence) rather than measuring operational assessment activity or outcomes.

PL-6.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that security-related activity planning formerly covered by PL-6 is captured and enforced under PL-2 by measuring the existence and coverage of planning artifacts, the mapping of PL-6 activity items into PL-2, and the percentage of required planning items with documented ownership and approval.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PL-6 is a preventive planning control (now incorporated into PL-2) so auditors need coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., percent of planning items migrated/covered). Counts (number of planning artifacts mapped) and trends (coverage over time) are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of security planning artifacts (plans, procedures, planning checklists) required by the PL family that are present in PL-2; existence of a mapping matrix showing each PL-6 activity mapped to a PL-2 artifact; timestamps and approver identities for planning approvals and version-control entries to show governance and currency.
data_signals: mapping matrix or traceability document: PL-6 activity → PL-2 artifact
list of security planning documents stored under PL-2 (document names, IDs)
document approval records (approver ID, approval timestamp, signature/version)
version control commits / change history for planning artifacts (commit timestamp, author)
document review or refresh frequency logs (last review date, next review due)
ownership records (document owner/role for each planning artifact)
coverage count: number of PL-6 items with PL-2 coverage vs. total PL-6 items
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: an explicit, versioned mapping matrix and PL-2 artifacts with signed approvals and recent review timestamps showing coverage of former PL-6 items.
Moderate: documented PL-2 plans that imply coverage but lack an explicit mapping or approvals.
Weak: verbal assertions or absent/dated planning documents without version control, approvals, or mapping to PL-6 items.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is a planning (preventive) control; the description states it was withdrawn and 'incorporated into PL-2', indicating planning/coverage preventative intent.

PL-6.differentiation_note: This control is withdrawn and unique among siblings because measurement focuses on verifying that PL-6 responsibilities are captured within PL-2 (a mapping/coverage verification task) rather than standalone PL-6 artifacts; limited source text requires assessing PL-2 artifacts for coverage.

PL-7.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that an approved, maintained Concept of Operations (ConOps) exists for each system/major application and measure coverage and currency: percentage of systems with an approved ConOps, age (days) since last ConOps review, and count of ConOps updates tied to change control or operational exercises.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive planning control, auditors need coverage and currency metrics (percent of systems with valid ConOps). Counts (documents, updates) and trends (review frequency) supplement coverage; a score can summarize maturity or completeness of ConOps content.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percent of systems/applications with an approved ConOps record in the document repository; for each ConOps record capture approver and approval timestamp, last_review_date, linked system/component identifiers, and number of operational scenarios or procedures documented and exercised. Also measure number of change-control tickets referencing the ConOps and training completions referencing ConOps procedures.
data_signals: ConOps document identifier in documentation repository
document metadata: approval signature/approver ID and approval timestamp
last_review_date / last_revision_timestamp for each ConOps
linked system/component inventory IDs referenced by each ConOps
scope/system coverage indicator (list or count of systems covered per ConOps)
change control/commit/ticket IDs that reference ConOps updates
operational exercise / test records referencing ConOps scenarios
training records citing ConOps procedures or roles
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated repository metadata showing approver, approval timestamp, last_review_date within defined review period, and explicit links from each ConOps to system inventory and change-control tickets; regular exercise/test artifacts that reference the ConOps.
Weak: a single, undated PDF or standalone document without approver metadata, no links to system inventory, and no change-control or training traces.

control_type_classification.reasoning: PL-7 is a planning control that requires documented ConOps to prevent operational gaps by defining expected operation and responsibilities.

PL-7.differentiation_note: This control focuses on the presence, approval, coverage and currency of ConOps documentation (planning artifacts) rather than technical enforcement or detection — measurement must map documents to system inventory and change/training artifacts, unlike sibling controls that may measure technical configuration or monitoring.

PL-8.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of documented security and privacy architectures for systems in-scope: percent of systems with an approved architecture artifact, count of architecture reviews completed per project, and number of design exceptions or unresolved architecture gaps.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that requires coverage and completeness (percent of systems with approved architecture artifacts). Counts of reviews and trend over time help demonstrate ongoing governance and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of in-scope systems with an approved security and privacy architecture document (or equivalent), count of architecture reviews completed within the last review cycle, and count of open architecture exceptions with approval timestamps and remediation plans.
data_signals: security and privacy architecture documents (document IDs, version, approval timestamp)
architecture review meeting minutes and reviewer attendance records
architecture approval records (approver identity, approval timestamp)
open architecture exceptions or risk acceptance records (IDs, justification, remediation due dates)
system inventory mapping to architecture document (system ID → architecture doc ID)
last review timestamp per architecture artifact
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory mapping showing each system linked to a versioned, approved architecture document and an audit trail of architecture reviews.
Weak: ad hoc or manual documents without approval timestamps, or single-point-in-time snapshots without linkage to system inventory.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name focuses on establishing architectures (a design-time activity) which is preventive in nature: it requires documented and approved architecture artifacts.

PL-8.differentiation_note: This is the broad governance-level architecture control covering existence, review, and approval of security/privacy architectures across systems, unlike the siblings which focus on specific design patterns (defense-in-depth) or supplier considerations.

PL-8(1).measurement_goal: Measure how many in-scope systems implement layered (defense-in-depth) controls as defined by architecture: percent of systems with documented control layers and evidence that at least the minimum required layers are implemented, plus counts of missing or single-point-of-failure layers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive architectural control, coverage/implementation percentages show whether layered defenses are in place; counts and distributions by layer show where layers are missing or concentrated.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of systems with a mapped set of defense-in-depth layers (e.g., perimeter, network segmentation, host controls, application controls, IAM, logging) and count of systems that meet the minimum required layers per policy; list of missing layers per system.
data_signals: architecture diagrams or layer-mapping artifacts (system ID → declared layers)
control implementation evidence by layer (firewall rulesets, segmentation configs, host hardening checklists, IAM policy attachments)
logging coverage per layer (log source IDs, ingestion timestamps)
exceptions or single-layer findings (records showing missing layers)
test or validation results for layered controls (penetration test findings, segmentation test outcomes)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated mappings linking each system to implemented control artifacts for each declared layer and periodic validation tests proving layer effectiveness.
Weak: high-level diagrams only, or manual assertions without implementation artifacts or test evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The parenthetical explicitly requires 'Defense in Depth' design, a preventive architecture requirement to ensure multiple control layers are designed and implemented.

PL-8(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on evidence that layered defenses are designed and implemented per system (layer mapping and implementation artifacts), unlike PL-8 which is about existence and governance of architecture documents generally.

PL-8(2).measurement_goal: Measure incorporation of supplier diversity and supplier-specific security/privacy considerations into architecture decisions: percent of supplier-dependent components with documented supplier diversity assessment and percent of supplier contracts that include architecture-security/privacy requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage (percentage of supplier relationships evaluated and contract clauses present). Counts of suppliers and comparisons against procurement policy are useful supplements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of supplier relationships for in-scope components that have a documented supplier diversity assessment and security/privacy architecture alignment (contract clause present, assessment completed), plus count of supplier contracts missing required architecture/security clauses.
data_signals: supplier inventory linked to system components (component ID → supplier ID)
supplier diversity attributes in procurement records (diverse supplier flag, classification)
supplier security/privacy assessment reports (assessment ID, completion timestamp, score)
