contract records showing presence of architecture/security/privacy clauses (clause flag, effective date)
procurement/onboarding checklists indicating architecture alignment review
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative supplier inventory linked to systems, signed contracts with required clauses, and completed assessment reports stored in a central repository.
Weak: procurement spreadsheets without linkage to architecture, or informal vendor discussions without documented assessment or contract clauses.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The parenthetical targets supplier diversity considerations within architecture decisions — a design/prevention requirement to ensure supplier selection and contracts address security/privacy.

PL-8(2).differentiation_note: Unlike the general architecture control, this enhancement specifically requires measuring supplier-related documentation, diversity attributes, and contract clauses tied to architecture and privacy/security requirements.

PL-9.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of in-scope assets under a central management platform, the timeliness and success rate of policy/configuration deployments pushed from that platform, and the count of unmanaged or out-of-date systems that bypass central management.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Central management is primarily about coverage and configuration compliance (best expressed as percentages of assets managed and policy deployment success). Counts of unmanaged devices and trends over time are useful secondary signals to show remediation and drift.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope assets enrolled in and reporting to the central management platform, deployment success rate of pushed policies/configurations (successful vs. attempted), and counts of assets classified as unmanaged or with configuration drift. Include timestamps for enrollment, policy push, and successful application so latency and overdue deployments can be measured.
data_signals: central management platform enrollment records (asset ID, enrollment timestamp)
managed asset inventory vs. authoritative asset inventory (asset ID, management status)
policy/configuration deployment records (policy ID, target group, push timestamp, apply timestamp, success/failure)
agent/heartbeat logs from managed endpoints (last check-in timestamp, version)
configuration drift detection alerts (asset ID, drifted setting, timestamp)
unmanaged device detection events from network/endpoint discovery (device fingerprint, discovery timestamp)
failed deployment/error logs with failure reason codes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated enrollment and heartbeat logs showing >95% of in-scope assets reporting, automated deployment records with success/failure outcomes and timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheet lists of managed systems or one-time screenshots of the management console without timestamps or machine identifiers.
Strong: machine-readable mappings between authoritative asset inventory and central management enrollment enabling automated coverage calculations; Weak: manual reconciliation reports.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Central Management' implies prevention of unmanaged/configuration-drift conditions by centralizing policy/configuration distribution and control.

PL-9.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on centralized enrollment, policy push success and coverage metrics (versus sibling controls that may focus on vulnerability scanning or logging); due to an empty control description the taxonomy relies on the control name and typical expectations of 'central management' — verify against the authoritative control text to refine keywords and risks.

PL-10.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of information systems and assets that are assigned to an approved security control baseline (including baseline version and approval), the count of documented baseline selection rationales, and the number and status of any approved baseline exceptions/waivers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Baseline selection is a preventive control concerned with coverage and completeness (percentage of systems mapped to approved baselines). Counts of baselines, exceptions, and trending of baseline review/adoption rates are useful secondary measures to show scope and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of information systems and assets with an assigned baseline_id and approved baseline_version, grouped by system impact level; percentage coverage of systems with an approved baseline; count and details of any baseline exception/waiver records including approval metadata; timestamp of last baseline review per baseline document.
data_signals: system inventory records with baseline_id and system impact/categorization
baseline document metadata (baseline_id, version, approval date, approving authority)
baseline selection rationale / tailoring documentation linked to baseline_id
exception/waiver records with approval status and expiration date
configuration compliance scan results mapped to baseline_version
baseline review schedule and last_review_timestamp
change requests or configuration change logs that reference baseline updates
asset criticality or impact level mapping used for baseline selection
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative, machine-readable system inventory with baseline_id and signed/recorded baseline approval dates, automated mapping from asset records to baseline documents, and configuration scan results showing conformance to the assigned baseline.
Strong: recorded exception/waiver approvals with expiration and compensating controls logged.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or unapproved draft baseline documents, manual email approvals without recorded metadata, or missing mapping between assets and baseline versions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Selecting and approving baselines is an upfront activity intended to prevent inconsistent control implementation (establishes required configurations and approvals).

PL-10.differentiation_note: This control focuses on formal assignment and approval of control baselines and mapping assets to those baselines (coverage and approvals), whereas sibling controls would measure execution of scans or remediation against baselines — PL-10’s unique need is authoritative mapping and documented selection rationale.

PL-11.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems/environments that have an approved, documented tailored security-control baseline (including rationale and assigned overlays/inheritance) and the count of tailored control deviations or waivers tracked against the organization baseline policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Baseline tailoring is a preventive governance activity that is best measured by coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., percent of systems with approved tailored baselines). Scores and counts help quantify quality and volume (number of waivers, number of tailored baselines) while trends show change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of systems/environments with a documented tailored baseline that includes tailoring rationale, assigned overlays/inheritance, and formal approval; count of active waivers or deviations; timestamped evidence that tailored baselines were applied to system configuration or CMDB entries. Metrics should be linkable to the system inventory and to the baseline document/version.
data_signals: tailored baseline document identifier and version
system inventory records linking systems to a baseline identifier
tailoring rationale or justification documents
formal approval artifacts (signed approval record, change ticket ID, or authorization memo)
waiver/deviation records with expiration and approver
configuration management database (CMDB) entries showing applied baseline version per system
baseline update and review timestamps
control-to-baseline mapping (which control was tailored and how)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formally approved tailored baseline documents linked to system inventory entries and CMDB configuration versions with timestamps and approver identities.
Strong: automated linkage between baseline version in source control/CMDB and deployed system configurations.
Weak: only a high-level policy or guidance document without per-system mapping, no approver signatures, or ad-hoc emailed approvals.
Weak: retrospective notes or informal justifications without tracked waivers or expiration controls.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing and approving tailored baselines and mappings (preventive governance to ensure correct controls are selected and authorized before deployment).

PL-11.differentiation_note: This control focuses on documented tailoring, approvals, and linkage to system inventory/configuration (policy and governance evidence) rather than runtime detection or remediation metrics used by sibling operational controls.

PM-1.measurement_goal: Demonstrate the existence, completeness, and currency of the organization's Information Security Program Plan by measuring: (1) presence of an approved program document, (2) percentage of required program sections implemented, (3) date of last formal review/approval, and (4) coverage of program roles/owners assigned against the required role list.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PM-1 is primarily a preventive governance control where a composite completeness/quality score best represents program maturity; percentage metrics show coverage (e.g., sections implemented, roles assigned) and trending shows improvements in program currency over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and timestamped attributes of the authoritative Information Security Program Plan artifact: version identifier and approval timestamp, checklist of required program sections and which are implemented, list of assigned role owners with attestation dates, scheduled review cycle entries and last review date, and links/references to supporting risk assessments or budgets cited by the plan.
data_signals: Information Security Program Plan document identifier (filename/ID) and version history
approval/endorsement record (approver name, role, approval timestamp)
program section coverage matrix (list of required sections vs implemented flags)
assigned security roles and owner entries with assignment dates
scheduled review cycle entries and last review/next review timestamps
references to risk assessment artifacts cited by the plan (IDs/timestamps)
resource or budget allocation entries linked to the program (budget line or ticket)
evidence of training or communication tied to the program (distribution list, training completion logs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: version-controlled program document with approval signatures, automated revision history, mapped coverage matrix showing all required sections implemented, documented role assignments and scheduled review cadence.
Weak: undated or unsigned documents, single draft file without versioning, ad-hoc email approvals, or absence of referenced risk assessments and resource assignments.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes an organizational program plan (policy/plan creation and maintenance), which is preventive by defining required controls, roles, and review cycles.

PM-1.differentiation_note: Unlike operational controls that measure detection or remediation, PM-1 requires evidence of program-level governance artifacts (document versioning, approvals, section coverage, assigned owners and scheduled reviews) rather than runtime event logs.

PM-2.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that an accountable information security program leader is formally designated, has documented authorities and responsibilities, and is actively performing governance duties (reporting, budget oversight, program reviews) across the organization.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control where coverage (percentage of organizational units with a designated security leader / documented role) is most useful; counts of appointments and trends in governance activity (meetings, reviews) provide supporting evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of organizational units (or business lines) with a formally documented information security program leader (appointment letter or job description) and the number/frequency of governance activities led by that leader (security program reviews, steering committee meetings, budget approvals) over the audit period.
data_signals: signed appointment letters or delegation of authority for the information security program leader
security program charter or documented role description with responsibilities and authorities
organizational chart showing reporting line for the security leader
minutes/attendance records of security governance or steering committee meetings
records of security program reviews and dates (review reports)
budget allocation or resource approval records for the security program
training/compliance records for the appointed leader (if applicable)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed appointment plus org chart, repeated governance meeting minutes and program review reports, and documented budget/resource approvals.
Weak: informal/verbal assignment, job posting without appointment, or single one-off report without recurring governance activity.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes an assigned leadership role and authorities to prevent gaps in program governance, which is inherently preventive.

PM-2.differentiation_note: This control uniquely requires measurement of formal role assignment, documented authorities, and ongoing governance activities (charter, appointment letters, meeting minutes) rather than technical controls or operational detection metrics.

PM-3.measurement_goal: Measure the adequacy and coverage of allocated information security and privacy resources: percent of required security/privacy FTEs filled, budget allocated for security/privacy versus required budget, and proportion of planned security/privacy projects and toolsets that are funded and staffed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PM-3 is a preventive control about providing resources; percentage metrics (coverage, budget fulfilment, roles filled) directly show coverage/compliance while counts (vacancies, funded projects) and trends show direction over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percent of required security/privacy FTEs filled (filled_FTEs / required_FTEs), percent of security/privacy budget allocated (allocated_budget / required_budget), count of funded vs planned security/privacy projects, training hours per security/privacy FTE, and time-to-fill for assigned security/privacy roles. Break down by organizational unit and criticality tier.
data_signals: documented resource plan or staffing model listing required security/privacy roles and FTE by role
personnel roster with job codes, incumbents, start dates and FTE allocation
budget line items for information security and privacy (allocated_budget, fiscal year)
project funding approvals and spend records for security/privacy initiatives
procurement/contracts for security/privacy tools and services with start/end dates
training enrollment and completion records for security/privacy personnel (hours completed)
time-to-fill records for security/privacy role vacancies
risk treatment plans with resource estimates and assigned funding
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal resource plan + regular budget line items and payroll/HR records showing filled FTEs and signed procurement/contracts covering security/privacy tools and services.
Strong: ongoing trend data showing budget and staffing meeting policy thresholds over multiple periods.
Weak: single approval email or one-off expense entries without a documented required-FTE baseline or program plan.
Weak: anecdotal statements of resourcing without corroborating HR, budget, or contract records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title centers on ensuring resources are provided (preventing resource shortfalls), so measurements should verify coverage and allocation rather than detect incidents.

PM-3.differentiation_note: This control is distinct from policy or process controls because it requires measuring resource adequacy (budget/FTEs/contracts/training) rather than policy existence or procedural steps; note: description was not provided so keywords and measures are inferred from the control name.

PM-4.measurement_goal: Measure the volume, status, and timeliness of POA&M (Plan of Action and Milestones) items: count of open vs closed items, percentage completed on schedule, mean time-to-remediate for prioritized items, and count/age of overdue high-severity items.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: POA&M effectiveness is primarily demonstrated by remediation velocity and closure rates (rate). Complementary metrics (percentage completed on time, counts of open/overdue items, and trends over time) show coverage and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track POA&M record entries including item identifier, severity/priority, assigned owner, planned milestone date(s), actual completion timestamp, current status (open/in-progress/closed), and any verification evidence; compute remediation rate, percent completed on schedule, MTTR by severity, and overdue item counts.
data_signals: POA&M item identifier and creation timestamp
assigned owner / responsible party for each POA&M item
priority or severity classification per POA&M item
planned milestone dates / target completion dates
actual completion timestamps and closure status
verification evidence attachments or validation test results for completed items
overdue POA&M item count and age (days past planned date)
notes/change history for POA&M items (status updates, reason for delay)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized POA&M system with immutable timestamps, owner assignment, and attached verification artifacts for each item; automated status change logs and reporting.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets without timestamps or evidence, manual status notes, or one-time reports lacking historical trend data.

Strong: consistent linkage between POA&M items and originating finding (vulnerability, control weakness) with severity labels; Weak: items lacking origin or severity making prioritization unverifiable.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Plan of Action and Milestones Process' indicates a process to track and close remediation actions, which is corrective in nature (tracks fixes and closure).

PM-4.differentiation_note: This control focuses on tracking and measuring remediation items and their timeliness (POA&M lifecycle), unlike sibling controls that might focus on identifying vulnerabilities or preventive configurations; measurement must capture assignment, milestones, evidence, and aging of remediation tasks.

PM-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of organizational systems and assets that are recorded in the authoritative inventory (by asset_id/hostname/IP) and the recency of inventory updates; additionally measure the count of unmanaged or undiscovered systems detected versus documented assets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive—auditors need coverage % (what portion of systems are inventoried) as the primary signal, with counts of missing/unmanaged assets and trends over time to show maintenance and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of known systems present in the authoritative inventory (asset records with asset_id, hostname, IP, owner, type) and the distribution of 'last_updated' timestamps; count of discovery-scan-identified assets not present in the inventory and time to reconcile them.
data_signals: authoritative inventory/CMDB records (asset_id, hostname, IP address, owner, system type, criticality, last_updated timestamp)
network/endpoint discovery scan outputs (discovered host list with IP, MAC, OS fingerprint, discovery timestamp)
reconciliation logs showing added/removed assets and reconciliation timestamps
unmanaged asset detection report (assets flagged as not in CMDB)
inventory completeness ratio by environment (cloud, on-prem, virtual)
inventory update frequency metrics (time since last inventory update per asset)
asset ownership fields populated vs. null count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, continuous discovery feeds reconciled to an authoritative CMDB with reconciliation timestamps and automated reconciliation workflows
Strong: inventory records include unique asset_id, owner, and last_updated timestamps and map to discovery outputs
Weak: annual manual spreadsheets or one-time inventories without discovery reconciliation
Weak: inventories lacking unique identifiers or without timestamps for updates

control_type_classification.reasoning: PM-5 establishes and maintains an inventory (proactive asset tracking), which prevents unknown/unmanaged systems from persisting.

PM-5.differentiation_note: This control focuses on maintaining an authoritative inventory of systems and coverage metrics (presence, recency, reconciliation) rather than on data classification or protection of information content.

PM-5(1).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of systems and data stores that have documented Personally Identifiable Information (PII) mapped to them (coverage of PII inventory), the count of distinct PII data elements inventoried, and the recency of PII inventory updates and classification.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is preventive/detection-focused for data protection—percent coverage of systems with PII inventory is the primary indicator; counts of PII elements and trends show scope and maintenance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of applications/systems with an associated PII record (mapping of data elements to system_id), count of unique PII data elements or PII-containing records inventoried, and timestamps for last classification/validation of PII mappings.
data_signals: PII/data element inventory records (data element id, description, sensitivity classification, associated system_id, storage location)
data flow diagrams or mapping files linking PII elements to systems/processes
PII inventory last_updated timestamps and change logs
access control lists or entitlement reports for systems that process/store PII
encryption/at-rest and in-transit protection status for PII-containing systems
retention schedule and data minimization flags associated with PII records
discovery results from DLP or data discovery tools identifying PII in storage locations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated data-discovery/DLP outputs reconciled to a maintained PII inventory with data element-to-system mappings and timestamps
Strong: inventory includes classification, owner, retention, and protection status for each PII element
Weak: manual lists or declarations without automated discovery or lacking mapping to systems and storage locations
Weak: PII inventory without timestamps or without access/protection attributes

control_type_classification.reasoning: PM-5(1) requires maintaining an inventory of PII (preventive control to ensure identification and protection of PII assets).

PM-5(1).differentiation_note: Unlike PM-5 (general system inventory), PM-5(1) specifically requires mapping and classification of PII data elements to systems and storage locations — measurement must include data-element level coverage and protection attributes (e.g., encryption, retention).

PM-6.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required performance measures (KPIs) that are formally defined with owner, data source, collection frequency, baseline and target, and the rate at which those measures are collected and reported on schedule; additionally report the count and trend of metric threshold breaches and corrective actions open against those breaches.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about establishing and operating performance measures—auditors want coverage and compliance rates (percentage) as the primary proof, with counts of defined KPIs and breach events and trends over time to show sustained operation and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of formal performance measures (KPIs) and percentage that include required metadata (owner, data source, collection frequency, baseline, target), percentage of KPIs with a data point collected within the expected reporting window, time series of KPI values and threshold-breach events, and count of corrective actions opened/closed in response to breaches.
data_signals: registry of defined KPIs with fields: KPI_id, name, owner, definition, data_source, collection_frequency, baseline_value, target_value
last_collection_timestamp per KPI
KPI value time series (timestamped measurements) per KPI
threshold/breach records (timestamp, KPI_id, breach_type, breach_value)
report distribution logs (report_id, KPI_list, generation_timestamp, recipients)
corrective_action tickets linked to KPI breaches (ticket_id, open_timestamp, close_timestamp, resolution)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated KPI time-series ingest with continuous timestamps, automated report generation logs, and ticketing links from breaches to corrective actions.
Moderate: periodic manual spreadsheets with timestamped snapshots and meeting minutes documenting KPI reviews.
Weak: a static list of KPIs without timestamps, owners, collection records, or evidence of recurring measurement and reporting.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes and governs performance measures and targets (preventive control) that ensure processes and security objectives are met before failures occur.

PM-6.differentiation_note: Unlike operational detection or remediation controls, PM-6 focuses on the existence, completeness and reporting cadence of performance measures and requires metadata and time-series evidence for each KPI rather than event-level detection logs.

PM-7.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of enterprise architecture artifacts and the percentage of projects and systems that have completed an architecture review/approval against the organization's EA policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Enterprise architecture compliance is best evidenced by coverage and adherence percentages (what proportion of systems/projects conform), with counts (number of reviewed projects) and trends over time to show improvement or regression.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active IT projects and in-scope systems that have a recorded enterprise architecture review/approval and date of last architecture artifact update; counts of architecture exceptions and approved deviations. Measurements should be grouped by system criticality and project phase to allow policy compliance queries.
data_signals: inventory of systems and projects with associated EA review status (approved/pending/none)
timestamped architecture artifact update logs (diagram/version/date)
records of architecture review approvals and reviewer identities
documented architecture exceptions or waiver approvals with expiration dates
system criticality or tier classification linked to EA records
project phase or lifecycle status associated with EA review timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated inventory showing each system/project mapped to an EA review record and recent artifact timestamp (strong);
ad-hoc manual attestations or one-off documents without linkage to a system inventory (weak);
presence of signed approvals and timestamps for EA reviews (strong) vs. unsigned or undocumented reviews (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes architecture governance and review processes (preventing non-conforming designs) which is inherently preventive.

PM-7.differentiation_note: PM-7 addresses organization-wide EA governance and review coverage, focusing on artifact currency and architecture approval rates rather than specific technical implementations or offloading decisions.

PM-7(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of offloaded services/assets that have documented architecture assessments, approved offloading rationale, and protective controls defined and implemented before or after offload.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Offloading controls are verified by coverage percentages (what share of offloaded services are assessed/approved) with counts of offloaded assets and trends to show remediation/closure of gaps over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of offloaded services/assets with a recorded architecture offloading assessment, date of assessment, approved justification, identified data flows, and implementation status of required controls; counts of offloads pending assessment and overdue remediation items.
data_signals: inventory of offloaded services/assets with offload assessment status
offloading assessment reports or decision records with timestamps
approved offloading rationale or waiver records
mapped data flow diagrams for offloaded services
list of required protective controls and their implementation status for each offload
overdue remediation / remediation completion timestamps for identified gaps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated linkage between offload inventory and formal assessment/approval records with timestamps (strong);
Manual notes or undocumented offloads without assessment records (weak);
Presence of implemented protective controls documented against each offload vs. mere documented intention (strong vs weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The supplemental control focuses on governing and approving offloading decisions and required controls to prevent improper transfers or insecure offloads, a preventive activity.

PM-7(1).differentiation_note: PM-7(1) is specifically about offloading decisions and controls for offloaded services (assessment, approvals, control implementation) whereas PM-7 is broader EA governance and artifact coverage.

PM-8.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of the documented Critical Infrastructure Plan: percentage of identified critical infrastructure assets and services included in the plan, frequency of plan updates and exercises, and count of critical assets lacking assigned recovery objectives or documented dependencies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive planning control, auditors need coverage and completeness metrics (percent of critical assets covered) plus counts of uncovered assets and trend of update frequency to show ongoing maintenance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent coverage = (number of critical infrastructure assets/services documented in the plan) / (total number of critical assets in the authoritative inventory). Also measure update cadence = time since last plan revision, count of plan review/exercise events per year, and count of assets missing RTO/RPO or dependency mapping.
data_signals: authoritative critical asset inventory (asset IDs, criticality tier)
plan document metadata and version history (timestamps, author, version)
mapping of assets/services included in the plan (asset IDs referenced in plan sections)
recovery objectives per asset/service (RTO/RPO fields)
plan review and exercise records (exercise dates, attendees, after-action reports)
dependency mapping or service dependency lists referenced by plan
assigned roles and responsibilities records linked to plan (owner IDs)
open issues or remediation items from plan exercises (issue count, status)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: version-controlled plan in repository with timestamped updates, automated linkage to authoritative asset inventory, RTO/RPO defined for each critical asset, and recent exercise reports with tracked remediation items.
Weak: single undated PDF of a plan, no version history, missing links to inventory, or only ad-hoc tabletop notes without tracked remediation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Title 'Critical Infrastructure Plan' indicates a planning/control document intended to prevent or mitigate impact, so classification as preventive is appropriate.

PM-8.differentiation_note: Focuses on planning completeness and currency for critical infrastructure (coverage, RTO/RPO, exercises) rather than operational detection or remediation metrics used by sibling controls.

PM-9.measurement_goal: Demonstrate the existence, approval, currency, and organization-wide adoption of a documented Risk Management Strategy by measuring (a) presence and versioning of the approved strategy document and (b) the percentage of required reviews/updates and organizational units aligned to the strategy on schedule.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive governance control, auditors need coverage and currency measures (percent of units covered, percent of on‑time reviews). Counts (documents, approvals) and trends (review cadence over time) provide complementary evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Record of an approved Risk Management Strategy document (document ID, version, approver, approval timestamp), the documented review cycle (required review interval) and last-review timestamps, mapping of organizational units/assets to the strategy, and the number of completed vs. required reviews or alignment activities per review cycle.
data_signals: Risk Management Strategy document identifier and version
approval record (approver identity, approval timestamp, signature or approval ticket)
document last review date and next review due date
documented review frequency / review cycle requirement
mapping table of organizational units or asset groups to the strategy
count of units with documented alignment or implementation plans
meeting minutes or governance committee approvals referencing the strategy
exceptions or waivers logged against strategy requirements
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: version-controlled strategy document with an approval record, automated review reminders, and a maintained mapping of org units/assets showing >90% alignment and on-time reviews.
Weak: undated drafts, emails without formal approval records, or anecdotal attestations from managers with no versioning or mapping to organizational units.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Risk Management Strategy' indicates a preventive governance control focused on establishing policy and direction (existence, approval, currency).

PM-9.differentiation_note: This control focuses on strategic documentation, approval, review cadence, and organization-wide alignment (policy/coverage metrics), unlike operational controls that measure technical detection or remediation activity; note: input lacked a full description so keywords and signals derive from the control title and typical strategy elements.

PM-10.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of access requests that have documented authorization approvals and are provisioned (or denied) in accordance with the organization's authorization policy and defined timelines, plus counts of unauthorized or orphaned accounts discovered during reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Authorization Process is primarily preventive — auditors need coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., percent of requests with valid approvals and timely provisioning). Counts and rates provide supporting visibility (e.g., number of unauthorized attempts, provisioning times) and trends show ongoing compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of access requests with a recorded approval and corresponding provisioning (or denial) event within the policy SLA: numerator = count of request_ids that have approval records and provisioning_timestamp within SLA; denominator = total access requests in the audit period. Also measure counts of accounts without approvals (orphaned accounts) and median time-to-provision (provisioning_timestamp - request_timestamp).
data_signals: access request records (request_id, requestor_id, requested_permissions, request_timestamp)
authorization approval records (approver_id, approval_timestamp, approval_decision, approval_evidence)
provisioning/change execution logs (provisioning_timestamp, change_ticket_id, target_account_id, change_agent_id)
role/permission assignment table (role_id, assigned_principal, assignment_timestamp, rationale)
access revocation/deprovisioning events (revocation_timestamp, initiator_id, revocation_reason)
periodic access review reports (review_id, reviewer, review_date, exceptions_found, remediation_actions)
orphaned/unused account report (account_id, last_login_timestamp, owner)
authorization policy version and SLA thresholds (policy_id, effective_date, provisioning_SLA_days)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, time-stamped request → approval → provisioning audit trail (request_id linking approval and provisioning events) and periodic review reports showing remediation of exceptions.
Strong: policy versioned SLA values tied to provisioning timestamps to verify timeliness.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or manual sign-off without time-stamps or links to provisioning logs.
Weak: point-in-time screenshots of approvals lacking linkage to provisioning events or missing identity fields.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Authorization Process' implies controls that prevent unauthorized access by requiring approvals and provisioning steps prior to granting access.

PM-10.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on the pre-authorization and provisioning lifecycle (approval records, provisioning timeliness, orphaned accounts) rather than post-facto detection or incident response; measurement must link request → approval → provisioning events and compare against policy SLAs. Note: control description was empty; keywords and signals are derived from the control name and typical authorization process artifacts — validate against the authoritative control text if available.

PM-11.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of documented mission statements and business process definitions: percentage of critical business processes with approved documentation, assigned owners, and evidence of periodic review against the organization's mission and objectives.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive—auditors need coverage/ completeness measures (percent of processes documented and aligned). Counts of processes and trend over review cycles are useful secondary signals to show scope and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of identified business processes and count of those classified as 'critical'; percentage of critical processes that have an approved mission-alignment statement, process documentation, assigned process owner, and a documented review within the last defined review period (e.g., 12 months). Also measure time since last review per process and number of processes lacking owner or approval.
data_signals: process inventory entries (process id, name, criticality classification)
mission statement / process alignment documents (document id, attached process id)
process documentation metadata (version, approval timestamp, approver identity)
process owner assignment records (owner id, role, contact)
process review records (review date, reviewer, outcome/comments)
policy mapping records showing alignment to organizational objectives
open items from process definition reviews (issue id, status)
time-since-last-review per process
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized process inventory with machine-readable metadata (owner, approval timestamp, version) and automated timestamps showing periodic reviews; evidence of alignment mappings to mission/objectives.
Weak: standalone undocumented or undated process documents, spreadsheets without approval metadata, or anecdotal attestations without timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title indicates establishing/defining mission and business processes (a preventive governance activity) rather than detecting incidents or performing remediation.

PM-11.differentiation_note: Unlike operational or technical controls, PM-11 focuses on governance-level documentation and alignment (coverage and currency of process definitions) rather than technical detection or remediation metrics; its unique need is measuring documentation completeness, ownership, and review cadence.

PM-12.measurement_goal: Measure program coverage and operational effectiveness: percentage of workforce and critical roles enrolled in the insider threat program, percentage completing required insider-threat training, count and rate of insider-related alerts/incidents detected, and mean time-to-detect and time-to-contain insider incidents against organizational policy thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PM-12 is programmatic and auditors will primarily want coverage/compliance ratios (e.g., % of employees covered, % training completion). Counts and rates (incident counts, detection rate) are secondary to show volume and effectiveness over time; trending shows program improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete measures an auditor would query: (1) percent of active employees and percent of privileged/critical roles enrolled in and assigned insider-threat controls; (2) percent of employees who completed mandated insider-threat awareness training within the required timeframe; (3) count of insider-related alerts and confirmed insider incidents per period and mean time-to-detect/contain for those incidents; (4) percent of privileged accounts with completed background investigations and frequency of privileged access reviews.
data_signals: insider threat program policy document identifier and approval timestamp
LMS training enrollment and completion records with user ID and completion timestamp
roster of employees/roles flagged as covered by the insider threat program (employee ID, role, coverage flag)
background investigation completion records for privileged/critical roles (user ID, investigation date, status)
user activity monitoring alerts tagged with 'insider' or behavioral indicators (alert ID, user ID, timestamp, alert type)
SIEM/UEBA event counts filtered for insider indicators and associated investigation outcomes
privileged access review logs and exception/mitigation records (review date, reviewer, findings)
insider incident reports and incident response timelines (detection timestamp, containment timestamp, closure)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated exports of LMS completions, SIEM/UEBA alert logs with correlated investigation outcomes, up-to-date roster linking employees to program coverage, and timestamped background investigation records.

Weak: one-off attestations, annual summary reports without timestamps, manual spreadsheets without cross-reference to HR/identity systems, or anecdotal incident descriptions without detection/containment timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: A program establishes policies, training, and preventative controls (programmatic emphasis), although it also includes detective elements like monitoring; the primary purpose is prevention through governance and training.

PM-12.differentiation_note: This entry is program-level (coverage, training, governance) rather than purely technical detection — measurements focus on percent coverage, training completion, and governance artifacts in addition to incident counts and detection timelines.

PM-13.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of role-based security and privacy skills across staff and contractors (percentage of personnel assigned required security/privacy competencies and completed required training/certifications) and the velocity of closing workforce competency gaps (counts and times-to-certify for assigned roles).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Workforce controls are primarily evidenced by coverage/fulfillment rates (percentage of personnel meeting role-based security/privacy requirements); counts of personnel and trends over time show remediation velocity and improvement; scores (competency/certification ratings) are useful for quality measurement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percentage of personnel (employees + contractors) with assigned security/privacy roles who have completed required role-based training and hold required certifications as of report date, the count of personnel with overdue or missing trainings, and the mean time from role assignment to training/certification completion by role.
data_signals: employee and contractor role assignments mapping to security/privacy responsibilities
learning management system (LMS) training assignment records with due dates and completion timestamps
training completion status and scores/certification records
competency matrix or role profile definitions listing required skills/certifications
background check/onboarding completion dates for security/privacy roles
overdue training counts by role and by individual
time-to-complete training from assignment (timestamps)
third-party/contractor training attestation records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated LMS records showing assigned courses, completion timestamps, and certification artifacts for all personnel; role-to-competency mapping stored in HR/identity systems.
Strong: continuous integration of HR role assignments with training automation producing timely assignments and completion enforcement.
Weak: one-off attestations or annual spreadsheets without timestamps or lacking linkage to role definitions.
Weak: manual sign-off without verifiable completion records or scanned certificates.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is focused on ensuring the workforce has required security/privacy skills and training (preventing gaps that could lead to incidents).

PM-13.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls in this batch, PM-13 uniquely requires measuring human-capability coverage (training/certification and role-to-competency mapping) rather than system configurations or detection events.

PM-14.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of the entity's testing program, workforce training completion and assessment outcomes, and operational monitoring coverage and detection performance — e.g., percentage of staff completing required training on schedule, count and results of control tests/exercises per period, and monitoring coverage and alert/detection rates for in-scope systems.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control centers on programmatic coverage (training completion, monitoring coverage) where percentage/coverage metrics best show compliance; counts and rates (test executions, alert volumes, detection rates) are useful secondary metrics to show activity and effectiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track training assignment and completion timestamps and pass/fail scores per employee role, scheduled vs. executed tests/exercises (date, scope, findings), monitoring coverage by asset (which assets/systems are instrumented), alert counts by severity and detection timestamps, and remediation ticketing tied to test/monitoring findings and training gaps. Capture policy-required frequencies/targets and compare actual performance.
data_signals: training assignment and completion logs (employee id, course id, completion timestamp)
training assessment scores and pass/fail results
list of required training by role and policy threshold
test/exercise schedule and execution records (penetration tests, tabletop exercises, control tests) with dates and outcomes
test results and findings reports (including severity and remediation status)
monitoring/telemetry coverage inventory (assets instrumented, agents deployed, sources onboarded)
monitoring alert and detection logs (timestamp, severity, alert type, source asset)
mean time to detect/acknowledge/resolve alerts and remediation ticket linkage
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated LMS logs showing role-based assignment and 100% on-time completion with assessment pass rates; automated monitoring telemetry showing >90% asset coverage and time-series alert/detection metrics; documented test plans and signed test reports with remediation tickets and closure timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc or one-off training attendance sheets, single point‑in‑time slide decks, manual notes from an exercise without artifacts, or monitoring claimed but without telemetry/coverage lists.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title emphasizes 'Training' and programmatic activities (preventive), while also containing testing and monitoring elements; measured primarily as coverage/completion aligns with preventive controls.

PM-14.differentiation_note: This control is program-level and combines three activities (testing, training, monitoring) so measurements must span workforce completion rates, scheduled/actual test executions, and monitoring coverage — unlike more narrowly scoped sibling controls that focus only on testing or only on monitoring.

PM-15.measurement_goal: Measure the organization's active memberships and participation in security and privacy groups/associations (count and percent active), and evidence that outputs from those groups (standards, guidance, joint initiatives) are reviewed and integrated into organizational policies and programs on a recurring basis.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive/governance control where coverage and integration rates are most useful (percentage of required associations joined/active and percent of association outputs adopted); counts of memberships and trends in participation over time are also valuable to show engagement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of security/privacy groups and associations where the organization holds active membership or formal affiliation; for each affiliation record: membership status (active/inactive), last participation date, number of participation events in the last 12 months, documented outputs received (e.g., guidance, standards), and evidence of organizational review or policy updates mapped to those outputs (policy change records or meeting minutes).
data_signals: formal membership roster or contract records for security/privacy associations (membership_id, start_date, status)
attendance logs or participation records (meeting dates, attendees, role: member/observer/presenter)
documented outputs received from associations (guidance documents, standards, advisories) with timestamps
policy update/change logs showing references to association outputs (change request id, change_date, rationale)
minutes of internal review meetings referencing association guidance (meeting_date, attendees, referenced_documents)
invoices or subscription records indicating active membership payments (invoice_date, amount, membership_period)
number of contributions/submissions to association working groups (submission_id, date, topic)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed membership agreements + regular attendance records + documented policy changes that cite association guidance; machine-readable logs linking association outputs to internal change requests.
Weak: one-time invoice or passive mailing-list subscription without attendance records or evidence of review/adoption.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title focuses on establishing/maintaining memberships and associations (preventive governance activity) that influence policy and practice.

PM-15.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls (vulnerability scans, logging), this control requires measurement of external engagement and evidence of integration of external guidance into internal policy — metrics must cover membership records and documented policy adoption rather than system telemetry.

PM-16.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of the organization’s Threat Awareness Program by tracking the percentage of personnel reached by formal threat briefings/training, the cadence of threat communications distributed, and outcome measures (survey scores or simulation results) that demonstrate increased awareness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A preventive awareness program is best evidenced by coverage/participation rates (percentage) with supporting counts of communications and trending to show program adoption and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of employees who completed required threat awareness events or trainings within the reporting period; count and timestamps of formal threat briefings distributed to defined populations; outcome measures such as average awareness-survey score or phishing-simulation click-rate by population.
data_signals: training completion records with employee ID and timestamp
attendance logs for threat briefings (date, population targeted)
number and timestamps of threat advisory/briefing distributions
awareness survey scores by employee and date
phishing or simulation click-rate and remediation completion timestamps
program schedule and required-frequency policy document
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized LMS records showing automated completion timestamps and course IDs plus correlated survey scores and simulation results; program schedule and distribution logs proving regular cadence.
Weak: ad‑hoc email announcements or a single annual slide deck without participant-level completion data or outcome measures.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes an awareness program (preventive activity) to reduce risk by informing personnel rather than detecting incidents.

PM-16.differentiation_note: Focuses on human-centered coverage and effectiveness (training, briefings, surveys) rather than technical indicator exchange or automated telemetry.

PM-16(1).measurement_goal: Measure the operation and reliability of automated threat intelligence sharing by counting indicators/messages published and consumed, tracking ingestion success/failure rates and latency, and measuring the number of downstream detections or matches derived from shared intelligence.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated sharing is operational/detective in nature; counts of indicators shared/ingested and rates (success, failure, latency) directly show whether automated exchange is functioning and producing detections.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of threat intelligence artifacts/messages published and consumed by automated feeds per period; ingestion success and failure counts with error codes; average processing latency from receipt to available consumption; count of IOC matches in detection systems attributable to shared indicators.
data_signals: TI feed publish logs (message ID, timestamp, source)
TI feed ingestion logs at consumers (message ID, timestamp, success/failure, error code)
indicator counts by type (IP, domain, hash) published and consumed
API/transport transaction success rate and error rate (per feed)
latency: time delta between outbound publish and inbound availability
number of detection/alert events that matched shared indicators (with timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable exchange logs (e.g., STIX/TAXII or API logs) showing successful publish and ingestion events with low latency and documented mapping to detections.
Weak: manual attestations or screenshots of subscriptions without machine logs, or occasional exported lists without ingestion status and latency data.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates automated sharing of threat intelligence which provides detection and situational awareness (operational/detective) via exchanged indicators and telemetry.

PM-16(1).differentiation_note: Unlike PM-16 (human awareness), this control requires machine-to-machine telemetry (feeds, API logs, indicator counts, latency and ingestion success) to demonstrate automated TI exchange is functioning.

PM-17.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of external systems (including third-party and cloud services) that process, store, or transmit Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) and verify they meet required protections (encryption, access controls, contractual CUI handling clauses) and continuous monitoring requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive coverage/control-compliance control — auditors want percent coverage of external systems that meet required protections (percentage). Counts of external systems and incident rates are useful secondary measures; third-party assurance scores help assess provider posture.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of external systems (cloud instances, SaaS tenants, partner-hosted systems) that are authorized to handle CUI, and the percentage of those systems with required controls applied: encryption at rest, encryption in transit, documented contract/Data Use Agreement with CUI clauses, configured access controls, and active monitoring/DLP. Measurements should include asset identifiers, owner, service provider, protection flags, and timestamps of last verification.
data_signals: inventory of external systems processing/storing/transmitting CUI (asset_id, owner, provider, environment)
classification labels indicating CUI on data stores and objects (bucket tags, document metadata)
encryption-at-rest configuration records for external storage (encryption enabled flag, KMS key id, key rotation date)
TLS/configuration records for data-in-transit (TLS enabled, cipher suite, cert expiry)
third-party contracts or Data Use Agreements with explicit CUI handling clauses (document reference, signed date)
access control lists/ACLs and identity entitlements for external systems (principals with access, last access timestamp)
DLP/egress monitoring events identifying CUI transfers to external endpoints (event timestamp, source asset, destination)
third-party assessment evidence (SOC2/ISO report references, attestation dates, provider risk score)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, continuously updated external-system inventory with CUI flags and protection-config checks is strong evidence;
Live logs showing encryption and access control enforcement (key usage logs, TLS handshake logs, ACL change events) are strong;
Signed, current contracts and third-party assessment reports that explicitly reference CUI handling are strong;
One-time manual attestations or annual statements without automated verification are weak evidence;
Screenshots or isolated point-in-time checks are weaker than continuous configuration and monitoring records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Protecting Controlled Unclassified Information on External Systems' implies preventive measures to ensure protections are applied before or while CUI is hosted/processed externally.

PM-17.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets external/third‑party/cloud systems handling CUI (inventory, contractual and technical protections) rather than internal system hardening or incident response — measurement must focus on provider controls, contracts, and cross-boundary monitoring.

PM-18.measurement_goal: Demonstrate an approved, versioned privacy program plan is established and maintained by measuring plan existence and approval, review cadence, percent coverage of data processing activities by the plan/inventory, completion rate of privacy impact assessments, and privacy training completion against policy targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PM-18 is primarily preventive (establishing a program and coverage), so percentage metrics (coverage, completion rates) best show compliance; counts and trends supplement with volume and change over time and a score can summarize program maturity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Presence of an approved privacy program document with version, approver, and approval_date. 2) Review cadence: number of plan reviews and last_review_date vs. required review frequency. 3) Coverage metrics: percent of systems/processes in the enterprise data inventory mapped to the privacy plan and percent of required DPIAs/PIAs completed. 4) Training: percent of targeted staff who completed privacy training within the required window. 5) Tracking of identified privacy remediation items (open/closed, remediation_time).
data_signals: approved_privacy_program_document (document_id, version, approval_date, approver)
privacy_program_review_log (review_date, reviewer, review_outcome, next_review_due)
data_inventory_coverage_by_system (system_id, includes_personal_data_flag, mapping_date)
privacy_impact_assessment_records (PIA_id, system_id, completion_date, owner, risk_rating)
privacy_training_completion_records (user_id, role, completion_date, curriculum_version)
privacy_policy_and_procedure_version_history (policy_id, effective_date, changes_summary)
privacy_remediation_tracker (finding_id, severity, assigned_owner, open_date, close_date)
tabletop_exercise_and_incident_response_playbook_updates (exercise_date, attendance, lessons_learned)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: an approved, versioned program document plus automated data inventory showing >90% system coverage, up-to-date PIA records, and training completion meeting policy thresholds with timestamps and owners.
Moderate: documented plan and some manual inventory/PIA records but with gaps in system coverage or irregular review dates.
Weak: single undated PDF or informal notes with no approval metadata, no inventory mapping, and no PIA or training records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name indicates establishing and maintaining a privacy program plan (preventive control) that sets policy, roles, and processes before incidents occur.

PM-18.differentiation_note: This control is program-level governance measuring plan existence, coverage and maintenance (reviews/training/PIAs) rather than operational detection or remediation metrics; it focuses on policy/documentation and coverage percentages. Note: control description was not provided, so keywords and measures are inferred from the control name.

PM-19.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that a named privacy program leader (or leaders) is formally appointed with documented responsibilities, reporting lines to senior management, and allocated resources; measure the coverage of appointments across organizational units and the delivery of privacy-program outputs (policies, metrics, meetings) tied to that role.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control where auditors expect coverage metrics (e.g., percent of org units with an appointed privacy lead). Counts (number of appointees), trends (appointments and program outputs over time), and a score (maturity/role-completeness) are useful secondary measures to show breadth and effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track presence and scope of appointment (job title, appointment date, organizational unit), documented role and responsibilities (job description or charter), reporting relationship (to which senior leader), allocated resources/budget, and measurable outputs tied to the role (privacy policy approvals, privacy metrics reports, scheduled privacy meetings). Produce percent coverage of organizational units with a designated privacy leader and a timeline of program outputs since appointment.
data_signals: formal appointment record or HR job title entry for privacy program lead with appointment timestamp
job description or charter document for the privacy role showing responsibilities and authority
org chart entry showing reporting line to senior management (executive sponsor or C-level)
budget/expense records or FTE allocation tied to the privacy role
scheduled privacy program meeting minutes or calendar entries with attendance by the privacy lead
published privacy policies or policy approval timestamps attributed to the privacy lead
privacy program KPI reports or metric dashboards produced by or owned by the privacy lead
training completion records for privacy staff reporting to the privacy lead
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed appointment letters, HR system entries, and org chart snapshots plus recurring KPI reports and meeting minutes showing ongoing activity.
Strong: documented budget/FTE allocations linked to the role and policy approvals where the privacy lead is the approver.
Weak: informal verbal assignment, one-off emails, or ad-hoc meeting notes without formal job descriptions, HR records, or consistent program outputs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes a leadership role and accountability (preventive governance) to reduce privacy failures by assigning responsibility and authority.

PM-19.differentiation_note: This control focuses on governance — verifying appointment, authority, and resourcing of a privacy leader — rather than technical or operational privacy controls (e.g., data handling, encryption), so measurements emphasize organizational records, coverage, and program outputs. If no direct match in focus areas exists, data_protection and governance_risk_compliance are the closest fits.

PM-20.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of dissemination of the organization's privacy program information: the percentage of required stakeholder groups who were notified and who acknowledged receipt within the policy timeframe, plus counts and recency of published privacy artifacts/updates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control verifies coverage (who received and acknowledged program information) so percentage (coverage/acknowledgement rate) is primary; counts of communications and trending of update frequency are useful secondary measures to show activity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of target stakeholders (by role or distribution list) who were sent the privacy program information and who returned an acknowledgement within the policy timeframe; count published privacy artifacts (policies, notices, updates) and record their publication timestamps and last-modified dates to demonstrate recency.
data_signals: privacy program policy/publication timestamp(s)
distribution list membership by stakeholder group
notification send logs (email/intranet post IDs) with timestamps
acknowledgement/receipt records (signed acknowledgements or LMS completion records)
publication/change log entries (document name, version, last-modified timestamp)
number of privacy communications issued per reporting period
training enrollment and completion records if privacy program communicated via training
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated distribution logs tied to a canonical distribution list and verified acknowledgement receipts or LMS completions; publication artifacts with immutable timestamps/versioning.
Moderate: system-generated intranet posts with view counts but no acknowledgement receipts.
Weak: ad-hoc manual emails or one-off screenshots without distribution lists or evidence of recipient acknowledgement.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name focuses on distributing privacy program information (a proactive activity to prevent privacy incidents), so it is classified as preventive.

PM-20.differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on measuring communication coverage, acknowledgements, and publication recency of privacy program materials (versus technical privacy controls such as access enforcement or data minimization).

PM-20(1).measurement_goal: Verify that any PM-20 enhancement or variant-specific dissemination mechanism is implemented: measure existence of the documented enhancement requirement and the percentage/count of target recipients reached and acknowledged via the specified channel(s).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Enhancements to dissemination are typically coverage-oriented; percentage coverage/acknowledgement demonstrates whether the enhancement meets its objective, with counts and trends supplementing to show activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Confirm presence of the specific enhancement requirement (documented policy clause) and measure the proportion of mandated audiences for that enhancement who were reached and provided acknowledgement; capture publication records for the channel(s) identified by the enhancement and timestamps of postings.
data_signals: policy or control enhancement clause text referencing PM-20(1)
channel publication records (website/intranet post IDs and last-modified timestamps)
distribution list or audience definition for the enhancement
notification/send logs for the enhancement channel
acknowledgement receipts or LMS completion tied to the enhancement
change log entry documenting implementation of PM-20(1)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: documented enhancement requirement plus automated channel publication records and acknowledgement receipts tied to defined audience lists.
Moderate: posting records showing accessibility but without explicit acknowledgement receipts.
Weak: undocumented practice or informal email threads without a documented enhancement requirement or audience definition.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Named as an enhancement to PM-20 (dissemination of privacy program information) and thus treated as preventive, but confidence is low due to missing descriptive text.

PM-20(1).differentiation_note: Treated as an enhancement/variant of PM-20 that requires measurement of a specific channel or mechanism; entry is conservative and low-confidence because the control description was not provided, so keywords and signals are inferred from the control code/name.

PM-21.measurement_goal: Measure the count and completeness of documented disclosures of personal or sensitive data (who received data, what data, purpose, and timestamp) and the percentage of disclosures that include required authorization/consent and contractual coverage.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires records of disclosure events (best measured as counts of disclosure transactions). Completeness and compliance are captured as percentages and trends over time to show sustained compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of outbound disclosures (by recipient and purpose) and count/percentage of those disclosures that include required attributes: timestamp, recipient identifier, data elements disclosed, legal basis/consent, and linked data-sharing agreement; plus trend of missing attributes and overdue post-disclosure notifications.
data_signals: disclosure audit log entries with timestamp, recipient identifier, purpose code, and data elements disclosed
system-generated transfer/transfer-job records (API transfer logs, secure file transfer logs, email gateway transfer records)
consent/authorization records linked to subject and disclosure transaction ID
data-sharing agreement metadata (contract id, permitted purposes) linked to recipient
DLP outbound transfer events correlated with disclosure records
retention and deletion records for disclosed data
exceptions or manual disclosure request approvals
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, immutable disclosure logs that include timestamp, recipient, purpose, data elements, and a link to consent/contract; continuous exportable logs for the full audit period.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or point-in-time summaries without transaction identifiers or lacking linkage to consent or contractual records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Accounting of Disclosures' implies recordkeeping and logging of disclosure events (detective evidence of past disclosures) rather than a preventive configuration.

PM-21.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on logging and verifying outbound disclosures (who, what, why, when) rather than access controls or prevention — measurement must emphasize disclosure transaction completeness and linkage to consent/contracts; note: control description was not provided so keywords are derived from the control name.

PM-22.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and trend of personally identifiable information (PII) records that meet defined data-quality thresholds (completeness, validity/accuracy, timeliness/currency, and required provenance/consent metadata) and the count and rate of quality exceptions remediated within policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive data-quality management control, auditors need coverage/threshold metrics (percentage of PII meeting quality targets) as primary evidence; data quality index scores, raw exception counts, and trends show health and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) total count of PII records per system; (2) percentage of PII records passing automated quality checks (completeness, validity, currency, provenance/consent flags) per data store; (3) count and rate of new quality exceptions detected and remediated within SLA windows; and (4) data-quality score (composite) per PII dataset over time.
data_signals: PII dataset inventory with record counts and system identifiers
automated data-quality check results (per-record validation pass/fail with timestamp)
field-level completeness rates for required PII attributes (e.g., name, SSN, birthdate, contact)
validation/fidelity check failures and error types (format, range, referential)
last-updated / source timestamp for PII records (currency)
consent/consent-status metadata flags tied to PII records
data lineage/provenance metadata indicating source system and ingestion timestamps
remediation ticket counts and timestamps linking quality exceptions to fixes and closure times
evidence_strength_indicators: continuous automated validation logs and per-record pass/fail history (strong) vs. one-off manual sampling (weak)
high coverage across all inventoried PII systems and attributes (strong) vs. single-system evidence (weak)
presence of linked remediation tickets and closure timestamps showing fixes (strong) vs. undocumented manual corrections (weak)
time-series data showing improving or stable quality scores (strong) vs. single point-in-time snapshot (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title focuses on 'Quality Management' of PII, indicating prevention of poor-quality PII through ongoing data quality controls rather than solely detecting incidents.

PM-22.differentiation_note: This control is specific to PII data quality (accuracy, completeness, currency, provenance/consent) rather than access controls or encryption; measurement must focus on record-level quality metrics and remediation linkage rather than access or logging coverage.

PM-23.measurement_goal: Measure governance coverage and activity: the percentage of data domains/assets with an assigned data owner/steward, the rate of governance meetings held per policy period and the percentage of data policies/standards reviewed and approved on schedule; plus counts of open governance action items and their closure rate against SLA.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control where auditors expect coverage and compliance rates (e.g., percent of data domains with owners, percent of policies reviewed). Counts (meetings, open action items) and trends over reporting periods are useful secondary measures to show ongoing activity and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete metrics an auditor would want: (1) percentage of defined data domains/assets that have an assigned and documented data owner/steward; (2) count of governance body meetings held per quarter and percent held per approved meeting schedule; (3) percentage of data policies/standards reviewed and re-approved within the policy-defined review cycle; (4) count of open governance action items and percent closed within the governance SLA.
data_signals: governance charter document with approval timestamp and version
data domain / data asset inventory records with assigned data owner/steward identifiers
policy and standard documents with creation/review/approval timestamps and approver IDs
governance meeting schedule records and meeting minutes/attendance rosters
decision log / action-item tracker entries with assignee, creation and closure timestamps
quarterly governance reports submitted to senior leadership (report timestamps and distribution list)
policy exception requests and approval/denial logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable inventories linking data domains to owner IDs, timestamped meeting minutes and decision logs, automated action-item tracking showing SLA compliance and closure timestamps.
Weak: one-off signed charter PDF without version history, informal email approvals, or anecdotal attestations of meetings without minutes or action-item tracking.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Establishing a governance body is a preventive organizational control that sets policies, roles and oversight to reduce data management and compliance risks.

PM-23.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls that measure scan coverage or detection events, this control requires measurement of organizational artifacts and process cadence (charter, assigned owners, meeting minutes, policy review schedules and action‑item closure rates).

PM-24.measurement_goal: Measure the existence and active operation of a Data Integrity Board by counting scheduled vs. held meetings, the number of data-integrity incidents and remediation actions reviewed by the board, and the percentage of board-directed remediation actions completed within defined timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Board oversight is a governance/preventive control focused on coverage and compliance (e.g., percent of scheduled meetings held, percent of board-directed remediations completed). Counts of meetings/incidents and trending of those counts are useful secondary measures to show activity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count scheduled board meetings vs. meetings held per audit period; attendance records; meeting minutes or decision logs with timestamps and references to data-integrity incidents; count of remediation/action items issued by the board with owner, due date, and completion timestamps; KPIs the board reviews (e.g., data discrepancy/reconciliation failure rate) and changes to data integrity policies approved by the board.
data_signals: board charter or governance document (PM-24 / Data Integrity Board)
scheduled meeting calendar entries with timestamps and agenda
meeting attendance records and attendee identifiers
meeting minutes / decisions log with timestamps and referenced incident IDs
data integrity incident register entries that indicate 'reviewed by board' flag
remediation/action tickets with created_date, assigned_owner, due_date, resolution_date, and link to board decision
policy update records showing approval by board with version and timestamp
data integrity KPI values over time (e.g., reconciliation failures, discrepancy rate) as presented to board
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: persistent, machine-readable artifacts — recurring calendar invites, meeting minutes with explicit decision/action IDs, remediation tickets with board-reference and resolution timestamps, and board-reviewed KPI time series.
