Weak: one-off summary reports or a single annual statement without meeting minutes, missing attendance records, or remediation actions lacking timestamps or board linkage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name indicates a governance/oversight board (preventive) that establishes and enforces data integrity policy and remediation priorities.

PM-24.differentiation_note: This control is governance/oversight-focused (board existence, meeting activity, policy approvals and tracking of board-directed remediations) rather than a technical integrity control; note: control description was empty so all keywords and measures are inferred from the control name 'Data Integrity Board'.

PM-25.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of testing, training, and research dataset provisioning events that do not contain unmasked personally identifiable information (PII), and count exceptions where production PII was copied or used without approved de-identification or formal exception.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control requiring coverage/adhere­nce measurements (percentage of datasets de-identified). Counts of exception events and rates of provisioning with PII are useful secondary observables to show volume and frequency of non‑compliant activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of dataset provisioning events for testing, training, and research in a defined period that used de-identified/synthetic data versus those that used identifiable production data; include counts of exception approvals and raw copies from production. Measurements should be grouped by environment (test/training/research), data owner/team, and asset criticality to allow SLA/policy comparisons.
data_signals: dataset provisioning request logs (timestamp, requester, environment tag: testing|training|research)
data classification labels on datasets (contains PII boolean, PII field list)
de-identification/masking operation logs (method, timestamp, dataset id)
synthetic data generation records (dataset id, generation timestamp)
records of approvals/exceptions for use of production PII (request id, approver, justification, expiration)
count of dataset copies from production to non-production (source asset id, target environment, timestamp)
policy registry indicating required minimization controls for each environment
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated provisioning logs showing masking/de-identification applied with cryptographic hashing or tokenization and matching dataset ids; exception approvals recorded in an access/request system with timestamps and expirations.
Weak: manual attestations or one-off spreadsheet lists claiming datasets are de-identified without accompanying masking logs or audited provisioning events.
Strong: continuous telemetry of provisioning events enabling percent-of-events calculations; Weak: a single point-in-time inventory snapshot without provisioning history.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text focuses on 'Minimization' of PII used in testing, training, and research, which is a preventive measure to reduce exposure of PII before events occur.

PM-25.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets minimizing PII in non-production dataset use (testing/training/research) rather than general data protection controls like encryption or retention; measurement must focus on dataset provisioning, de-identification operations, and documented exceptions. Note: no associated risks were provided, so risk-derived categories are unavailable.

PM-26.measurement_goal: Measure the volume and lifecycle of complaints plus process effectiveness: count of complaints received, percent acknowledged within the defined target timeframe, percent resolved within policy/SLA, mean time to resolution, escalation rate, and rate of repeat complaints to demonstrate the complaint management process is operating and closing issues.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Complaint management is primarily corrective/operational — auditors need rates (acknowledgement rate, resolution rate) to show timely handling; counts and percentages (completion %, volumes) and trends help show throughput and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of complaint records by submission timestamp and category; timestamps for submission, acknowledgement, escalation, and resolution (to compute acknowledgement time and time-to-resolution); SLA/policy thresholds per complaint category; resolution outcome and root-cause remediation flags; repeat/duplicate complaint links and post-resolution satisfaction where available.
data_signals: complaint_id (unique ticket identifier)
complaint_submission_timestamp
complaint_acknowledgement_timestamp
complaint_resolution_timestamp
complaint_category/classification
complaint_priority/severity
assigned_owner/team
escalation_flag and escalation_timestamp
SLA/policy_target by category (acknowledgement/resolution thresholds)
root_cause_remediation_closed_flag
repeat_or_duplicate_complaint_count
post-resolution customer_satisfaction_score (if captured)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous ticketing system logs that include immutable timestamps for submission, acknowledgement, escalation and resolution, linked SLA definitions, and closure notes with root-cause actions.
Moderate: periodic exported reports from a complaints system showing counts and timestamps but lacking full linkage to SLA definitions or root-cause remediation items.
Weak: isolated manual spreadsheets, ad-hoc emails or single point-in-time screenshots without consistent timestamps, owners, or linkage to policy thresholds.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name indicates a process to manage and resolve complaints (corrective action), implying measurement of handling and remediation rather than purely preventive detection.

PM-26.differentiation_note: This control focuses on process and lifecycle metrics (acknowledgement/resolution rates and timeliness) rather than detection, access, or technical configuration — measurement must emphasize ticket timestamps, SLA compliance, and closure/root-cause evidence.

PM-27.measurement_goal: Measure the volume, timeliness, and coverage of organizational privacy reports and notifications (e.g., periodic privacy program reports, breach notifications, DPIA completion reports) and the percentage delivered on the policy-defined cadence and to required recipients.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Privacy reporting is primarily evidenced by discrete artifacts (reports and notifications) so counts of reports/notifications are most direct; rates (timely delivery rate), percentages (coverage of required recipients or percent on-schedule), and trends (improvement over reporting cycles) add context on timeliness and compliance with cadence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of privacy reports issued per reporting period (by report type), timestamp of issuance, list of recipients, records of breach/incident notifications with send timestamps, count and completion timestamps for Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) or privacy assessments, and percentage of reports/notifications delivered within the policy-defined timeframe.
data_signals: privacy report issuance timestamps and report identifiers
report type metadata (program report, DPIA, breach notification)
distribution/recipient lists for each report (emails or system distribution records)
breach/incident notification records with regulatory notification timestamps
DPIA/privacy assessment completion records and timestamps
policy-defined reporting cadence or reporting SLA reference
approval or sign-off entries for published privacy reports
exceptions or overdue notification flags
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated issuance logs with timestamps and distribution receipts (strong) versus ad-hoc manual email chains or undated PDFs (weak)
Structured records showing SLA/cadence cross-checks (strong) versus single point-in-time attestations (weak)
Consistent trend of timely report delivery across multiple reporting periods (strong) versus one-time compliance (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Privacy Reporting' indicates monitoring and reporting activities (detective) that produce artifacts and notifications to demonstrate privacy posture and incidents have been reported.

PM-27.differentiation_note: This control is focused on program-level reporting, notification timeliness, and distribution/coverage of privacy artifacts rather than technical enforcement or remediation metrics; measurement must capture issuance and delivery metadata rather than system configuration states.

PM-28.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, coverage, and currency of formal risk-framing artifacts (risk appetite, tolerance, scope, decision criteria) across organizational units and the percentage of key decisions/processes mapped to the approved risk frame.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive governance control that requires demonstrating coverage and compliance (best shown as percentages of units/processes covered) and a score/trend to show maturity and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of organizational units/processes with an approved risk framing artifact; timestamped approval and last-review dates; number/percentage of strategic decisions, projects, or controls that reference the current risk frame; and a maturity/quality score for each artifact (completeness, stakeholder sign-off, alignment to objectives).
data_signals: risk framing artifact identifiers (document IDs)
risk appetite statement text and defined thresholds
risk tolerance thresholds per business unit/process
approval workflow records and approver IDs with timestamps
last review / revision timestamps for each artifact
coverage matrix mapping artifacts to business units/processes/assets
records of decisions/projects referencing the risk frame (meeting minutes, change requests, risk acceptance logs)
maturity/assessment scores or checklist completion for each artifact
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centrally stored, versioned risk-frame documents with automated approval workflows, recent review timestamps, and a full coverage matrix showing mapping to all business units and decisions.
Weak: ad-hoc email threads, unsigned draft documents, or a single high-level statement without unit-level coverage or evidence of use in decision records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Risk framing establishes appetite/tolerance and decision criteria to prevent inconsistent risk acceptance — a preventive governance activity.

PM-28.differentiation_note: This control focuses on defining and documenting the organization's risk frame (appetite/tolerance/decision criteria) and coverage mapping, unlike sibling controls that measure detection, remediation, or incident response; it requires documentation/coverage metrics rather than event counts.

PM-29.measurement_goal: Measure the presence and completeness of formally assigned risk-management leadership roles (positions with documented responsibilities and appointment evidence), the percentage of required roles filled, and leaders' completion of role-specific risk governance training and chartered meeting participation over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily about coverage and completeness (what percent of required leadership roles are assigned and documented), so percentage is most useful; counts of appointments and trends over time help show turnover and continuity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of defined risk-management leadership roles required by policy vs. count actually assigned and documented; timestamps of appointment letters or role assignment records; presence of documented role descriptions/charters; completion records for required risk-governance training by each assigned leader; attendance records for risk committee/oversight meetings.
data_signals: official role registry / list of required risk-management leadership positions
appointment letters or signed assignment records with dates and assignee identifiers
documented role descriptions/charters for each leadership role
HR / IAM records linking persons to leadership roles (employee ID, title, start date)
training completion records for role-specific risk governance training (user id, course id, completion timestamp)
risk committee meeting minutes and attendance logs (meeting date, attendees, agenda references)
policy mapping showing which roles are required by the risk management program
role vacancy / open-position records (unfilled role identifiers and duration)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated role registry entries linked to HR/IAM with appointment documents and signed acknowledgements, paired with training completion and regular meeting attendance logs.
Moderate: periodic spreadsheets or PDFs listing role holders and dated appointment letters without automated linkage to HR/IAM.
Weak: verbal confirmations or undated/unsigned role descriptions and no training or meeting attendance records; single point-in-time snapshot without history.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control defines assignment and documentation of leadership roles (establishing responsibilities and governance), which is preventive in nature because it aims to prevent governance gaps.

PM-29.differentiation_note: This control uniquely requires measuring formal role assignment, documented responsibilities, and leader-specific training/meeting participation rather than technical controls or incident processes; its measurement focuses on people/organizational coverage and documentation.

PM-30.measurement_goal: Demonstrate existence, scope, and operationalization of a Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM) strategy by measuring documented strategy artifacts (version, last update), percent of suppliers covered by the strategy, and percentage of supplier risk assessments completed against policy requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A preventive strategy control is best evidenced by coverage and compliance percentages (policy coverage, percent of suppliers assessed). Counts (number of assessed suppliers) and trending over time show adoption and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Presence and currency of the SCRM strategy document (document version, last updated timestamp), percent of total supplier population included in the supplier inventory tagged as in-scope by the strategy, percent of suppliers with completed risk assessments within the required timeframe, and percent of critical supplier contracts that include required SCRM clauses.
data_signals: SCRM strategy document metadata (existence, version, author, last_updated_timestamp)
supplier inventory records with in-scope flag and criticality classification
supplier risk assessment records with completion_timestamp and risk_rating
contract repository entries showing presence/absence of supply-chain risk clauses
policy requirement rows that define assessment frequency and coverage (policy_threshold)
register of mitigation plans for supplier risks with status and remediation_timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated supplier inventory joined to risk assessment outcomes and contract metadata showing coverage and up-to-date strategy document metadata; weak: only an approved strategy document stored without evidence of supplier coverage or executed assessments.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name specifies a 'Strategy' for Supply Chain Risk Management, indicating policy/strategy existence and coverage (preventive).

PM-30.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on the enterprise-level SCRM strategy existence, scope and coverage across the supplier population (document metadata, inventory coverage); it is distinct from the follow-on control which targets critical/mission-essential suppliers specifically.

PM-30(1).measurement_goal: Show that the SCRM strategy is applied to suppliers of critical or mission‑essential items by measuring the percentage of critical/mission‑essential suppliers assessed, number and severity distribution of identified supplier risks, remediation completion rate for critical supplier risks, and the presence of contractual or monitoring controls for those suppliers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive/detective hybrid focused on critical suppliers requires coverage percentages (percent of critical suppliers assessed or under contract clauses) plus distributions of risk severity and counts of remediation items to show scope and priority.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percent of suppliers classified as 'critical or mission-essential' in the supplier inventory, percent of those suppliers with completed and recent supply-chain risk assessments, distribution of supplier risk ratings (e.g., high/medium/low), counts and timestamps of remediation actions for high-risk findings, and presence of contractual SCRM clauses for these suppliers.
data_signals: supplier inventory records with 'critical' or 'mission-essential' tag and classification timestamp
risk assessment reports for critical suppliers with assessment_date and risk_score/risk_rating
distribution of supplier risk ratings (high/medium/low) for critical suppliers
remediation records for critical supplier findings with remediation_timestamp and status
contract metadata indicating presence of supply-chain risk management clauses for critical suppliers
monitoring/assurance activity logs (audit findings, third-party assessment reports) for critical suppliers
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: recurring automated assessments and third-party assessment reports tied to the critical supplier inventory and active remediation tracking; weak: one-off questionnaires or undocumented/manual notes without timestamps linking to the defined critical supplier population.

affinity_keywords: Suppliers of Critical or Mission-essential Items

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly scope-limits the SCRM strategy to 'Suppliers of Critical or Mission-essential Items', implying preventive coverage and prioritized assessment/controls for that supplier subset.

PM-30(1).differentiation_note: This control narrows the strategy to critical/mission-essential suppliers and therefore requires supplier-criticality-tagged inventory, prioritized assessments and remediation metrics — a narrower, higher‑assurance measurement need than the parent PM-30 entry.

PM-31.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, completeness, and operational effectiveness of the organization's continuous monitoring strategy by tracking telemetry/source coverage, frequency of collection and analysis, integration with detection/automation tooling, and documented policy/versioning — expressed as coverage percentage, frequency adherence, and strategy maturity score.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A monitoring strategy is primarily evidenced by coverage and compliance metrics (percent of assets/sources covered, percent of telemetry ingested). Supplement with counts (connected sensors, alerts), a maturity/score for strategy completeness, and trends to show coverage change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track: (1) existence and version of the documented continuous monitoring strategy/policy; (2) inventory of telemetry sources and their coverage status mapped to asset inventory and criticality; (3) ingestion/collection frequency and success rates for each source; (4) number of detection/automation integrations (SIEM, EDR, cloud-native logs) and their operational health; and (5) scheduled review/update cadence and evidence of recent reviews.
data_signals: documented continuous monitoring strategy/policy filename and version + last review timestamp
asset inventory mapped to telemetry source registry (asset_id -> telemetry_sources)
telemetry ingestion logs (source id, last successful ingestion timestamp, ingestion failure count)
coverage ratio: assets with at least one monitoring source / total assets, by criticality tier
collection frequency metrics per source (events/sec or daily files) and adherence to stated frequency
integration status records for SIEM/EDR/cloud logging (connected/disconnected, last heartbeat)
number of monitoring rules/alerts onboarded and alerts triggered during test scenarios
scheduled review/remediation tickets referencing the monitoring strategy (change history)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated continuous ingestion logs and monitoring-source heartbeats showing near-real-time data, complete asset-to-telemetry mapping, and an up-to-date strategy document with recent review/approval
Weak: one-off manual reports or screenshots, an out-of-date or missing strategy document, partial asset lists, or intermittent/manual ingestion with no health metrics

control_type_classification.reasoning: A documented strategy is a forward-looking governance control intended to ensure monitoring coverage and prevent blind spots (focus on policy, coverage, and guidance).

PM-31.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on program-level strategy, coverage, and telemetry pipeline health (policy existence, asset-to-telemetry mapping, ingestion health), rather than specific detection rules or incident response actions.

PM-32.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of documented authorized purposes for data and processing: percentage of data assets and processing activities with an assigned, approved purpose and the count/rate of processing events that are flagged or blocked for purpose-mismatch.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (ensuring purpose assignments exist and are enforced), so percentage coverage of assets/activities with approved purposes is most useful; counts of exceptions and trends over time are useful secondary indicators.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of records in the data inventory that include a validated 'purpose' attribute; count of processing activities mapped to an approved purpose; number and rate of processing events blocked or flagged due to purpose mismatch; timestamps and approver IDs for purpose-approval/change events.
data_signals: data inventory records containing a 'purpose' field (asset id, purpose, classification tier)
processing activity logs with purpose tags (activity id, timestamp, purpose_tag)
purpose-approval/change records (approval timestamp, approver_id, rationale)
consent or data use agreement records linked to datasets (consent id, scope, expiry)
access or processing denials/alerts where purpose mismatch is recorded (event id, reason='purpose_mismatch')
mapping table between processing activities and approved purposes (activity_id → purpose_id)
exception registers for ad-hoc purpose uses (request id, approval status, duration)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable purpose tags on inventory and processing logs, automated enforcement events (purpose-mismatch denials) and recorded approvals with timestamps and approver IDs.
Weak: manual spreadsheets listing purposes without timestamps or approver metadata, occasional one-off approvals recorded only in email.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Purposing' implies establishing and enforcing approved purposes for data processing, a preventive activity to limit unauthorized uses.

PM-32.differentiation_note: Entry is inferred from the control name only (description was not provided); measurement focuses specifically on purpose-assignment coverage and enforcement metrics (purpose tags, approvals, purpose-mismatch denials), which differs from sibling controls that might focus on access or retention rather than purpose-binding. Limitations: taxonomy is based on inferred intent due to missing control text.

PS-1.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of required policies and procedures: percentage of required policy documents present in the policy inventory that are approved, assigned an owner, and reviewed within the defined review cycle; plus the percentage of targeted personnel who have received/acknowledged the current policy versions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Policy existence and coverage are best expressed as percentages (coverage, approval, acknowledgement). Counts and trends are useful secondary measures to track number of documents and review cadence over time; a score can summarize overall policy hygiene.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of required policy/procedure artifacts vs. documented artifacts in the policy inventory; for each artifact capture approval status, owner, version, effective_date, last_review_date, scheduled_review_cycle and employee acknowledgement timestamps. Calculate percentage approved, percentage within review cycle, and percentage of targeted staff with acknowledgements.
data_signals: policy inventory records (policy_id, title, owner, version)
policy approval metadata (approver_id, approval_timestamp, approval_status)
policy effective_date and last_review_date fields
policy review_cycle or next_review_date
list of required policies mapped to business areas (required_policy_list)
employee policy acknowledgement logs (user_id, policy_id, acknowledgement_timestamp, acknowledgement_version)
distribution/delivery logs or communications (email ID, distribution_timestamp, recipient list)
policy change history/audit trail (who changed version, change_timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable policy inventory with approval timestamps, owners, and automated acknowledgement receipts mapped to user directories; review dates showing periodic updates per policy_cycle.
Strong: evidence of automated distribution and logged employee acknowledgements per policy version.
Weak: single-file repository or manual snapshots without approval metadata, no acknowledgement receipts, or undated/unsigned documents.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on establishing and maintaining policies and procedures (preventive), as implied by the control title 'Policy and Procedures' which requires documentation, approval and dissemination.

PS-1.differentiation_note: This control is about governance-level documentation, approval, maintenance, and dissemination of policies (ownership, review cycles, acknowledgements) rather than measuring technical enforcement or incident activities; its unique need is tracking policy inventory and review/acknowledgement metadata.

PS-2.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of active organizational positions that have a formal, recorded risk designation (sensitivity/risk level) and the timeliness of assigning that designation for new or changed positions; also track mapping from designation to required personnel security actions (e.g., background investigation levels).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive classification control — auditors need coverage rates (percentage of positions with a designation). Counts (number of undesignated positions), trends (coverage over time), and comparisons (against policy or prior period) are useful complementary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of active positions in the authoritative HR/position inventory and how many of those have a recorded risk designation field populated; mapping records that link each designation to required personnel security actions (e.g., background investigation level); timestamp of designation assignment for new/changed positions to compute time-to-designate.
data_signals: position inventory records with 'risk designation' field populated
total active position count from HR system
job description records with designation tags
mapping table: designation -> required background investigation level
timestamp of position creation/last-change and timestamp of designation assignment
exceptions or waivers logged for positions without designation
audit/change log entries showing designation assignments and approver identity
onboarding checklist entries referencing position designation requirements
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative HR system or CMDB with a populated, queryable 'risk designation' attribute and automated change logs; measurable time-to-designate derived from timestamps.
Weaker: ad-hoc spreadsheets or occasional manual reports without reliable timestamps or missing mapping to required personnel security actions.
Strong: mapping records that explicitly tie designations to required investigations/policies; Weak: narrative policy references without executable mapping.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Assigning a risk designation to positions is a proactive measure to ensure appropriate personnel security controls are applied (prevention of mismatched access/clearance).

PS-2.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on classifying positions (coverage and timeliness of designation and mapping to required personnel security actions), unlike other personnel controls that may measure training completion or incident response for personnel — the unique need is authoritative position-to-risk mapping and assignment timestamps.

PS-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of personnel who have completed required pre-employment and periodic screening checks and the count of individuals provisioned access before screening completion; measure time-to-complete screening per hire.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PS-3 is a preventive control establishing screening coverage; percent coverage shows compliance vs policy, while counts and trends show volumes and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of employees (new hires and existing personnel where required) with completed screening records (e.g., background checks, adjudication) prior to or at access provisioning; distribution of time (days) from hire date to screening completion; count of access grants issued before screening completion.
data_signals: personnel screening completion timestamp
screening status (pending / complete / failed / adjudicated)
hire/start date
access provisioning timestamp and resource targeted
screening type recorded (background check / suitability adjudication)
screening outcome or adjudication result
overdue screening count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong evidence: automated HR/identity system records with timestamps showing screening completed before access provisioning and adjudication records.
Weak evidence: manual checklists or ad-hoc attestations without timestamps or linkage to provisioning events.
Strong: system-enforced gating preventing role/resource provisioning until screening status = complete; Weak: retrospective manual approvals.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Personnel Screening' indicates preventive vetting to block unauthorized access before it occurs.

PS-3.differentiation_note: General personnel screening focuses on onboarding and ongoing screening coverage and timing; unlike the enhancements it is not limited to classified info, indoctrination, special information categories, or citizenship-specific checks.

PS-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of personnel with appropriate security clearances/adjudications for access to classified information and the count of access grants to classified resources lacking required clearance.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement is preventive: auditors need percent of staff with required clearances for classified information; counts identify exceptions and comparisons show mismatches between clearance and resource level.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of users assigned to roles/resources labeled 'classified' who possess an active, valid security clearance/adjudication; count of classified-resource accesses or provisionings where user clearance level is insufficient or missing.
data_signals: user security clearance level and expiration date
clearance adjudication outcome and timestamp
resource classification label (classified / secret / top secret)
access provisioning events for classified resources with timestamps
active user-to-role assignments mapped to resource classification
instances of access granted without matching clearance
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative clearance registry cross-referenced to access control lists showing no mismatches and system enforcement preventing provisioning without clearance.
Weak: spreadsheets of clearances not reconciled to access control systems or expired clearances without automated removal.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly ties screening to access for 'Classified Information', a precondition to granting access, so it is preventive.

PS-3(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on mapping personnel clearances/adjudications to classified information access — measurement must link clearance registry to classified resource provisioning, unlike the broader PS-3 entry.

PS-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of applicable personnel who have completed formal indoctrination/training required for their role and track time-to-completion vs policy timeframes; track repeat training/recertification rates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Indoctrination is preventive/administrative and auditors need percent-completion and trends over time to show coverage and recency of training.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of employees in roles requiring formal indoctrination with recorded training completion and timestamp within the required policy timeframe; time distribution from assignment to indoctrination completion and recurrence/compliance for required refresher intervals.
data_signals: training/indoctrination assignment records
training completion timestamps and learner IDs
required completion timeframe or policy definition
role-to-training mapping (which roles require indoctrination)
recertification/refresh completion records
overdue training counts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: LMS records with user IDs, completion timestamps, and course IDs mapped to role requirements and automated assignment; Weak: signed paper attendance sheets without linkage to role mappings or missing timestamps.
Strong: declining overdue counts and steady completion within policy timeframes; Weak: one-off attestations without historical trend.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrasing 'Formal Indoctrination' implies required training completed prior to or early in employment to prevent misuse, a preventive control.

PS-3(2).differentiation_note: This enhancement centers on training/indoctrination records and timing (LMS evidence) rather than background/adjudication records used in other PS-3 entries.

PS-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure coverage: percentage and count of personnel with required special screenings or approvals to access information designated as 'requiring special protective measures' and count of accesses or assignments that lack that screening.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is preventive—auditors need percent coverage for personnel authorized to access specially protected information and counts of exceptions/mismatches.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of users assigned to roles or groups that grant access to information labeled as requiring special protective measures who have completed the required special screening/approval; count of exceptions where such information was accessible to personnel without recorded special screening.
data_signals: resource/information classification labels indicating 'special protective measures'
user special-screening/approval records and timestamps
access control assignments linking users to specially protected information
exceptions or temporary access approvals with justification and expiry
overdue or missing special screening records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: mapped authoritative inventory of specially protected information linked to access control and screened personnel records with automated enforcement; Weak: unlinked lists of sensitive data and separate HR files not reconciled to access logs.
Strong: downward trend in exceptions; Weak: persistent manual exception approvals.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement specifies screening tied to protection of certain information categories, a preventive requirement to control access.

PS-3(3).differentiation_note: Unlike general screening, this control requires mapping personnel screening records to specific sensitive information classifications and measuring exceptions for those categories.

PS-3(4).measurement_goal: Measure the count and percentage of personnel who meet documented citizenship or eligibility requirements where required, and the number of role assignments/accessions denied or remediated for non-compliance.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Citizenship/eligibility checks are preventive and best represented as percent of applicable personnel meeting requirements; counts and distributions show scope and exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of personnel in positions that require specific citizenship/eligibility who have verified documentation on file (document type, verification timestamp), and count of access grants or hires where citizenship requirement was not met or was waived.
data_signals: citizenship verification document type and upload timestamp
role-to-citizenship-requirement mapping
hiring decision records indicating eligibility checks
waiver or exception records with rationale and approver
access provisioning records for roles with citizenship restrictions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: scanned/verifiable citizenship documents linked to HR records and automatically checked against role requirements before provisioning; Weak: verbal attestations or untracked manual approvals.
Strong: zero active assignments violating citizenship policies; Weak: waivers recorded but lack approver or expiry.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The label 'Citizenship Requirements' denotes precondition checks preventing ineligible personnel from being placed into roles, a preventive control.

PS-3(4).differentiation_note: This enhancement is uniquely focused on verifying citizenship/eligibility for role placement and access; measurement must link HR verification documents to role eligibility mappings and provisioning records.

PS-4.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and timeliness of deprovisioning actions after employee termination: count of terminated personnel, percentage with all assigned accounts and credentials disabled within the required SLA, and counts of outstanding assets or active sessions after termination.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PS-4 requires verifying coverage and timeliness of deprovisioning (coverage is best expressed as a percentage of terminations fully processed); counts (number of failures/overdues) and trends (MTTR over time) give operational context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each HR termination event, capture (1) termination event timestamp from HR system, (2) timestamps for account disablement/credential revocation in IAM, (3) badge/access card deactivation timestamp, (4) asset return receipt timestamps, and (5) any remaining active sessions or privileged access after termination; compute percentage meeting SLA and list overdue items with elapsed time.
data_signals: HR termination record id and termination_timestamp
IAM account disablement timestamps per account (user, privileged, service)
access badge/card deactivation logs with timestamps
remote/VPN session termination logs and session end timestamps
asset inventory records showing return_of_property and receipt_timestamp
privileged account revocation events
open/active sessions or credentials after termination (active session count)
deprovisioning approval/workflow completion records and error/failure logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated system-to-system logs linking HR termination event to IAM disable event with timestamps and delta <= SLA.
Strong: continuous logs showing zero active sessions and recorded asset returns within SLA.
Weak: ad-hoc screenshots or manually signed paper checklists without correlated timestamps or system logs.
Weak: periodic attestations without event-level proof of account disablement.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on removing access and assets upon termination (preventing continued access), so it is primarily preventive.

PS-4.differentiation_note: This entry covers the broad, organization-level measurement of termination processing (timeliness and completeness) across HR, IAM, badge and asset systems; the siblings focus on post-employment obligations and automated execution specifics.

PS-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure compliance with post-employment requirements: percentage of separated personnel with documented post-employment obligations applied (e.g., NDAs, legal holds) and percentage with no residual system access or active credentials after separation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Post-employment requirements are about coverage/compliance (percent of separations with required post-employment actions completed); counts and comparisons (e.g., retained vs required) provide further evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each separation, record whether post-employment requirements are applied: presence of signed/sealed post-employment agreements, legal hold/retention flags, documented data transfer or archival actions, and confirmation that all system accounts are revoked; produce a per-separation compliance flag and percentage compliant against policy.
data_signals: separation_event id and separation_date from HR system
signed post-employment agreement records (NDA_signed flag with timestamp)
legal hold or data retention indicator on employee record
archive or transfer logs for employee-owned data stores
IAM account status showing disabled/archived accounts
audit logs confirming no successful authentications after separation_date
records of continued obligations or restrictions applied post-employment
exceptions or documented approvals for retained access
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system-tracked post-employment obligation flags (NDA_signed, legal_hold) correlated with IAM disablement and no auth events after separation.
Strong: records showing archival or legal hold applied to ex-employee data stores where required.
Weak: manual attestations without system flags or lack of correlation between HR and IAM systems.
Weak: undated paper NDAs without linkage to separation record.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires applying post-employment obligations and ensuring access is not retained after separation, which prevents unauthorized access or misuse.

PS-4(1).differentiation_note: Unlike PS-4 (which measures deprovisioning actions and timeliness), PS-4(1) specifically measures application of post-employment legal/retention obligations and verification that no residual access or data privileges remain.

PS-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure the effectiveness of automated termination actions: percentage of HR termination events that trigger automated deprovisioning workflows which successfully disable all accounts within the SLA, plus counts and rates of automation failures or required manual interventions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated action success is best expressed as a percentage of terminations successfully handled by automation; counts and rates (failures, retries, manual interventions per period) provide operational detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each HR termination event, capture the automation trigger timestamp, list of automation tasks executed (account disables, token revocations, badge deactivations), automation completion timestamps, error codes for failed steps, and whether manual remediation was required; compute percentage successful automation and mean time from HR event to completed disablement.
data_signals: HR termination event id and event_timestamp
automation workflow trigger logs (trigger_timestamp, workflow_id)
per-step automation execution logs with completion timestamps (IAM_disable, token_revoke, badge_deactivate)
automation error and exception logs with error codes
manual intervention/ticket creation records linked to termination event
automation success/failure count per period
mean time delta between HR event and automation completion
retries and queued automation backlog count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: end-to-end machine logs showing HR event → automation trigger → per-account disable events with timestamps and success status within SLA.
Strong: low manual intervention rate and low retry/backlog counts.
Weak: reported automation success without per-step logs or unexplained manual tickets.
Weak: periodic manual reports of automation activity without event linkage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires automated removal of access upon termination (preventing continued access), thus it is primarily preventive and operationalized via automation.

PS-4(2).differentiation_note: This entry is narrowly focused on the automation pipeline and its success/failure metrics (triggering, per-step success, retries), unlike PS-4 which covers overall termination processing and PS-4(1) which covers legal/post-employment obligations.

PS-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of internal personnel transfers for which role/privilege changes were reviewed and implemented within the organization-defined timeframe, plus the count of transfer events that resulted in unauthorized access or orphaned accounts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control ensuring coverage and timely adjustments (best measured as percentage of transfers with completed access updates). Counts of transfer events and trends over time help detect volume and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage of HR-logged transfer events that have an associated IAM/provisioning change completed within the required timeframe, (2) count of transfer events with orphaned or excess privileges after transfer, and (3) count of incidents attributable to improper access following transfers. Include timestamps to correlate transfer request, approval, and provisioning changes.
data_signals: HR personnel transfer request and approval records with timestamps
Identity management (IAM) change logs showing role/privilege modifications with timestamps
Access provisioning/deprovisioning ticket records and resolution timestamps
Audit logs showing post-transfer access activity for transferred users
List of accounts flagged as orphaned or not reconciled after transfer
Policy-defined timeframe or SLA for processing transfers
Manager/supervisor approval records or workflow events
Incident or access violation records linked to recent transfers
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage between HR transfer record and IAM change log with matching timestamps showing provisioning completed within policy timeframe.
Strong: workflow ticket records with approver, implementer, and completion timestamp for each transfer.
Weak: ad-hoc emails or manual spreadsheets noting transfers without system log evidence of access changes.
Weak: periodic attestations alone without per-transfer IAM change records or reconciliation to HR events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on ensuring access/authorization are adjusted when personnel move roles, which prevents inappropriate access (prevention).

PS-5.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets internal personnel transfers and the timely adjustment of access/privileges (not initial onboarding or terminations), so evidence must correlate HR transfer records to IAM provisioning change logs.

PS-6.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of access agreements for all personnel with access to organizational systems and information (percentage signed and current), and count any personnel with missing or expired agreements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PS-6 requires demonstrating coverage (who has signed and current agreements) so percentage coverage is primary; counts of missing/overdue agreements and trends over time are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active personnel records that have a current, signed access agreement on file; count of personnel whose agreement is missing or expired; timestamped signature and agreement version to validate currency.
data_signals: signed access agreement records with signer and timestamp
agreement version or template identifier
personnel roster / active employee IDs
agreement expiration or renewal timestamps
list/count of personnel without a current signed agreement
repository audit trail showing upload/receipt of agreement
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR system records with signed PDF and cryptographic timestamp, linked to employee ID and agreement template.
Weak: ad hoc scanned paper forms with no signature timestamp or mismatched employee identifiers.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishment of agreements before or during access (preventive), implying coverage and currency of signed agreements.

PS-6.differentiation_note: This entry covers general coverage and currency of access agreements for all personnel, whereas the numbered enhancements target specific types of information or post-employment clauses.

PS-6(1).measurement_goal: No separate measurement required for PS-6(1); evidence of the requirement should be provided through the controls identified in PS-3 (mapping that PS-6(1) was incorporated into PS-3 and that PS-3 contains the required protections).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Although PS-6(1) is withdrawn and incorporated into PS-3, auditors will want to see mapping and coverage; percentage of mapped requirements implemented (via PS-3 evidence) is the most direct way to show incorporation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Presence of a documented control mapping that shows PS-6(1) requirements were incorporated into PS-3 and the proportion of those mapped requirements with supporting evidence in PS-3 artifacts.
data_signals: text noting 'Withdrawn: incorporated into PS-3' (control catalog)
control mapping matrix linking PS-6(1) clauses to PS-3
PS-3 policy or procedure documents containing the incorporated clauses
evidence artifacts referenced by PS-3 (e.g., signed agreements, policy excerpts)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: explicit control mapping and PS-3 artifacts that contain the language and evidence previously required by PS-6(1).
Weak: absence of mapping and only a note that PS-6(1) was withdrawn without supporting PS-3 evidence.

affinity_keywords: Access Agreements | Information Requiring Special Protection

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input labels PS-6(1) as preventive, but the description indicates it was withdrawn and its requirements are covered by PS-3; classification reflects original intent with lower confidence.

PS-6(1).differentiation_note: This entry is administrative: it documents that the enhancement was withdrawn and requires mapping to PS-3 rather than independent measurement of a distinct agreement requirement.

PS-6(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of personnel who have signed access agreements that explicitly address handling and protection of classified or specially protected information, and count any individuals with access to classified data lacking such agreements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control targets coverage of specialized agreements for classified information (a coverage metric), so percentage of relevant personnel with appropriate agreements is primary; counts of non-compliant accounts and trends in compliance are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of users with access to classified or specially protected information who have a signed agreement that contains the required special-protection clauses; inventory of users with classified access and cross-reference to agreement records.
data_signals: signed agreement records with clause identifiers for 'special protection' or 'classified'
inventory of assets/data items labeled 'classified' or 'special protection'
access authorization lists for classified systems
mapping of user IDs to classified access entitlements
count of users with classified access and missing/expired special-protection agreements
agreement clause version and signature timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized agreements repository with clause tagging indicating 'classified' coverage, linked to user entitlements for classified systems.
Weak: manual attestations with no linkage to classified access inventory or unsigned/undated paper forms.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement requires preventive contractual/administrative measures (agreements with special-protection language) to limit mishandling of classified information.

PS-6(2).differentiation_note: This entry focuses specifically on agreements tied to 'classified' or specially protected information and must be measured by mapping agreement clauses to classified access entitlements, unlike the general coverage measured by PS-6.

PS-6(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of access agreements that include required post-employment clauses and track the rate/count of former employees who retain access after separation (and timeliness of revocation) to demonstrate post-employment obligations are enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PS-6(3) requires agreements to contain post-employment requirements (coverage -> percentage). Operational enforcement (revocation timeliness, residual access) is best measured by rates/counts as secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active and historical personnel agreements that include explicit post-employment requirements (e.g., nondisclosure, return of assets) and counts/rates of terminated employees with any active accounts or access after separation, including time-to-revoke metrics.
data_signals: signed agreement records with 'post-employment' clause identifiers
agreement clause version and signature timestamp
employee termination dates from HR system
access revocation timestamps (account disablement, entitlement removal)
list/count of active accounts for terminated employee IDs
overdue revocation instances and time-to-revoke durations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: linked HR and IAM records showing termination date and automated revocation event with timestamp, plus agreement text confirming post-employment obligations.
Weak: manual exit checklists without timestamps or uncorrelated paper NDAs that cannot be matched to termination events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement mandates preventive contractual language (post-employment obligations) and implies measuring both agreement coverage and enforcement actions after employment.

PS-6(3).differentiation_note: This entry uniquely requires measuring both the presence of post-employment clauses in agreements and operational enforcement (revocation/timeliness) for separated personnel, unlike PS-6 which addresses general agreement coverage.

PS-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of external (contractor/third‑party) personnel who completed required pre‑access screening (background checks, identity verification) and signing of required agreements (NDAs), and the count and timeliness of access provisioning/deprovisioning events for external accounts against policy SLAs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily preventive: auditors need coverage percentages (e.g., percent of contractors with completed background checks/NDAs) to show policy compliance; counts and trends support volume, timeliness and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of external personnel records where required screening and documentation are complete prior to first access (background_check_completed_date <= access_provision_date and nda_signed_date <= access_provision_date). Also measure counts of external accounts provisioned and deprovisioned and the mean time-to-deprovision after contract end or termination.
data_signals: external_personnel_roster (contractor_id, name, employer, sponsor, contract_start_date, contract_end_date)
background_check_records (contractor_id, check_type, completion_date, result)
signed_agreement_records (contractor_id, document_type='NDA', signature_date, storage_location)
access_provisioning_logs (account_id, contractor_id, provision_timestamp, provisioner, assigned_entitlements)
access_deprovisioning_logs (account_id, contractor_id, deprovision_timestamp, reason)
privileged_access_assignments for external accounts (account_id, privilege_level, approval_record)
onboarding_offboarding_checklists and sponsor_approval_timestamps
training_completion_records when training required for external personnel (training_id, completion_date)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR/contractor roster joined with background_check_records and access_provisioning_logs showing completion timestamps prior to first access; signed NDA files with verifiable signature timestamps stored in a central repository; deprovisioning events with timestamps and audit trail.
Weak: manual attestations or one-off spreadsheets without timestamps, scanned PDFs without metadata, or post-hoc attestations that do not prove screening occurred before access was granted.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Focuses on preventing inappropriate access by requiring screening, agreements, and controlled provisioning before external personnel are granted access.

PS-7.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets external/third‑party personnel onboarding and access lifecycle (screening, NDA, provisioning/deprovisioning), unlike sibling controls that may focus on internal staff or technical access controls; measurement must combine HR/contract and access logs rather than only technical configuration scans.

PS-8.measurement_goal: Measure the rate and timeliness of documented personnel sanctions following verified security policy or procedure violations (by role and violation type), and the percentage of policy violations that received an approved sanction action within the organization-defined timeframe.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PS-8 is an enforcement/corrective control; auditors need rates (sanctions applied per violation) to show enforcement effectiveness, supplemented by counts of sanctions and percentages (coverage of violations sanctioned).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of verified security policy/procedure violations (grouped by violation type and role), count of those violations that resulted in documented sanction actions, timestamps linking incident detection to HR sanction initiation and closure, and the percentage of violations sanctioned within the organization-defined SLA.
data_signals: documented disciplinary action records (employee/contractor ID, sanction type, initiation and closure timestamps)
security policy/procedure violation incident records (incident ID, violation type, detecting system/person, timestamp)
mapping/linkage between incident ID and HR sanction record (reference IDs)
sanction outcome codes and severity (e.g., warning, suspension, termination)
policy acknowledgement/acceptance records (signed/dated acknowledgements per user)
sanction SLA target and actual time-to-initiate / time-to-close per case
recidivism counts (repeat violations per individual) over rolling periods
HR case closure/resolution notes and approval signatures
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, auditable HR sanction records linked to incident IDs with timestamps and approval metadata; SLA compliance measurements over time; consistent sanction outcome coding.

Weak: one-off HR memos or verbal records; aggregated counts without linkage to specific incident records or timestamps; sanctions recorded only in free-text personnel files without standardized fields.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing and enforcing sanctions for noncompliance, which is an enforcement/corrective activity (documenting and applying disciplinary actions).

PS-8.differentiation_note: Unlike controls that measure preventive coverage (e.g., training completion or access provisioning), PS-8 uniquely requires measurement of HR enforcement actions and the linkage/timeliness between security incidents and documented disciplinary outcomes.

PS-9.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of formal position/job descriptions that define security responsibilities and role-to-permission mappings: percent of active positions with up-to-date, signed position descriptions that include required security duties and mapped access privileges, and count of positions missing required elements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control verifies coverage and compliance (best expressed as a percentage of positions meeting requirements). Counts of missing/overdue descriptions and trends of review/update frequency are useful secondary measures to show remediation and maintenance over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active organizational positions that have a current (within policy review period) position description documenting role title, duties, assigned security responsibilities, and mapped access/privileges; plus counts of positions missing any of these required elements and timestamps of last review and approval.
data_signals: HR system canonical position list (position IDs, active/inactive flag)
position/job description documents (with metadata: last_modified_timestamp, author, approver)
position description approval records or signature records
fields indicating included security responsibilities or required duties checklist
role-to-permission / role-to-access mapping records linked to position ID
last_review_date per position
policy-required review frequency or approval checklist
count of positions with missing required elements
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR records showing position document metadata (last_modified_timestamp, approver) and explicit role-to-permission mappings for each active position.
Moderate: centralized position documents without automated linkage to access mappings (requires manual verification).
Weak: ad-hoc or sample job postings or informal descriptions lacking timestamps, approver, or linkage to assigned privileges.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Defining and maintaining position descriptions is a preventive measure to ensure roles and security duties are assigned and enforced.

PS-9.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls in the batch, PS-9 is documentation- and HR-process focused — measurement must target coverage and currency of position documents and explicit links to access assignments rather than system logs or vulnerability counts; focus areas chosen as closest fit given limited description text.

PT-1.measurement_goal: Measure the presence, completeness, and currency of documented policies and procedures: percentage of required policies that exist and are up-to-date, count of policies with current approval and review dates, and rate of employee acknowledgments for key policies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PT-1 is preventive (policies define expected behavior), so percentage metrics (policy coverage, percent of policies current) best show coverage; counts and trending indicate volume and change over time (number of policies issued, review cadence).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Inventory of policy documents with metadata (policy id, title, owner, version, last_review_date, next_review_due, approval_record), employee acknowledgment records linking user IDs to policy IDs and timestamps, documented exceptions and remediation actions, and scheduled review/compliance task completion timestamps.
data_signals: policy document inventory (policy_id, title, owner, version)
policy last_review_date and next_review_due timestamps
policy approval/sign-off records (approver_id, approval_timestamp)
employee policy acknowledgment receipts (user_id, policy_id, acknowledgment_timestamp)
policy exception log entries (exception_id, policy_id, approver_id, expiry_date)
scheduled review tasks and completion timestamps (task_id, policy_id, completed_timestamp)
mapping of policies to controls/process areas or systems
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized policy inventory with machine-readable metadata, signed approval records, automated acknowledgment capture, and scheduled review tasks showing on-time completions
Weak: scattered PDF files without metadata, ad-hoc manual approvals, or single point-in-time screenshots of a policy document

control_type_classification.reasoning: Named 'Policy and Procedures' and describing governance artifacts that set requirements, which are inherently preventive by design.

PT-1.differentiation_note: This entry focuses specifically on measurable existence, currency, approvals, and acknowledgments of policies (policy lifecycle) rather than operational controls — description was not provided, so keywords and measures are centered on the policy lifecycle and may need refinement when a fuller control text is available.

PT-2.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of PII processing activities that have documented authorization (approver, purpose, and timestamp) and the count of processing activities executed without a recorded authority; report time-to-approval for new processing requests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage/authorization of PII processing (coverage expressed as a percentage). Counts of unauthorized events and rates (e.g., approval latency) are useful secondary supporting metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of PII processing records in the data/process inventory that include a documented authorization record (approver ID, purpose, authorization timestamp) versus total PII processing activities; count of processing events with no authorization; median time between request and authorization.
data_signals: PII processing inventory entries with 'authorization_status' and 'authorization_timestamp'
processing request and approval logs (requester ID, approver ID, purpose, timestamps)
data processing agreement (DPA) or policy references linked to processing activity records
access control / role assignment records tied to processing activities
exception or waiver logs for processing activities (with approver and rationale)
audit entries showing processing events and corresponding authorization reference (if any)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory records and approval logs with approver IDs and timestamps that correlate to processing events
Strong: machine-readable links between processing events and their authorization records (no manual reconciliation required)
Weak: manual spreadsheets or attestations without timestamps or approver identifiers
Weak: one-off attestations that do not map to individual processing events

affinity_keywords: Authority to Process Personally Identifiable Information

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title requires documented authority to process PII, which by design prevents unauthorized processing and therefore is preventive.

PT-2.differentiation_note: Focuses on existence and completeness of documented authorizations for PII processing (approvals, purpose, timestamps) rather than metadata/tag coverage or enforcement automation used by the sibling controls.

PT-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of data assets and repositories that are tagged/classified as PII, the count of untagged assets containing PII, and the average time from discovery of PII to application of an appropriate tag.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage of tagging/classification across assets (best expressed as a percentage). Counts of untagged items and distribution of tags by repository provide complementary visibility and trending over time shows improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of inventoryed data objects, databases, and storage locations that have an explicit PII tag/label (tag type, tag timestamp, tagging method), plus count of discovered PII items that remain untagged and median time-to-tag from discovery.
data_signals: data inventory records with 'pii_tag' or 'classification' field and tag timestamp
automated classification/scan results showing identified PII and their tag state
count of untagged files/records flagged by classifiers
tagging method indicator (manual vs automated) per asset
tag exception/waiver records with approver and rationale
repository coverage map showing which systems have tagging enabled
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated classification outputs that annotate assets with PII tags and timestamps and feed back into the inventory
Strong: continuous monitoring that detects new untagged PII and triggers tagging workflows
Weak: periodic manual labeling exercises or one-time inventories without ongoing discovery
Weak: tags applied without traceable timestamps or owner metadata

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title focuses on tagging/classification of PII to ensure appropriate handling, which is a preventive measure to control data handling.

PT-2(1).differentiation_note: Specifically measures metadata/tag coverage and time-to-tag for PII assets (vs PT-2 which measures authorized processing records and PT-2(2) which measures automated enforcement).

PT-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of PII processing decisions (allow/deny/require-approval) enforced by automated policy engines, count of automated denials/blocks, and rate of manual overrides of automated decisions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automation is intended to prevent unauthorized processing by enforcing policy; percent coverage of processing actions by automation is the primary preventive metric, supplemented by counts of blocked events and override rates.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Proportion of PII processing actions subject to automated policy decisions (policy engine allow/deny) versus total processing actions; count of automated blocks, count and reasons for manual overrides, and median decision latency for automated approvals.
data_signals: policy engine / PDP decision logs (action, resource, decision, rule_id, timestamp)
automation rule set versions and effective dates
counts of automated allow / deny / require-approval events
manual override logs with approver ID, reason, and timestamp
coverage map linking systems/components to automation enforcement
SLA/latency metrics for automated decisions (average decision time)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, machine-generated decision logs correlated to processing events and showing rule_id and timestamps
Strong: low override rate with documented reasons and owner approvals
Weak: manual enforcement logs or sampled screenshots without systematic logging
Weak: automation rules without versioning or missing decision logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control emphasizes automation of authority/processing decisions, which prevents unauthorized processing through automated enforcement.

PT-2(2).differentiation_note: Emphasizes operational automation metrics (decision logs, rule coverage, override rates) rather than manual authorization records or static metadata/tag coverage used by the other sibling controls.

PT-3.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and count of data assets (datasets, tables, records) that contain personally identifiable information (PII) which have an assigned, approved, and recorded processing purpose in the data catalog; measure number of purpose exceptions and timeliness of purpose assignment.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive coverage/control completeness requirement (coverage % of PII assets with an assigned processing purpose); counts and trends help show scale and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of all identified PII-containing assets and count/percentage of those assets with a non-empty processing_purpose attribute recorded in the canonical data catalog or inventory; timestamps of purpose assignment and approver id to verify timeliness and authorization; list/count of exceptions or assets explicitly marked 'no purpose' with justification.
data_signals: data inventory / catalog entries with a 'processing_purpose' field
list/count of identified PII-containing datasets/tables/fields
timestamp of processing_purpose assignment per asset
approver or owner identifier for processing_purpose assignments
exceptions register or documented justifications for assets without purpose
policy mapping table linking allowed processing purposes to asset types
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated data-catalog records showing a non-empty processing_purpose field, with assignment timestamps and approver IDs for each PII asset and low exception counts.
Weak: periodic spreadsheets or one-off assessments without timestamps or approver metadata, or manual lists that are not reconciled to the canonical inventory.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control defines/assigns processing purposes for PII (prevents improper processing by requiring purpose assignment), indicated by the phrase 'Personally Identifiable Information Processing Purposes'.

PT-3.differentiation_note: PT-3 focuses on the existence and correctness of purpose definitions across all PII assets (policy and coverage), not on how tags are implemented or automated.

PT-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and accuracy of PII-specific data tagging: percentage of PII assets with correct PII/classification tags in the canonical catalog and counts of untagged or miscoded PII items, plus sample-based tag accuracy rate.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Tagging and classification are coverage and quality controls; percentage shows coverage while counts and distribution reveal where tags are missing or concentrated.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Total count of assets containing PII and count/percentage of those assets with at least one PII tag/classification label in the data catalog; number of manual tag overrides and results of periodic sample audits that measure tag accuracy (correct tag vs. ground truth).
data_signals: data catalog entries with PII tag/classification label
repository-level tag coverage ratio (tagged assets / identified PII assets)
tag creation/last-updated timestamps
manual tagging override logs and justification
sample audit results mapping tagged items to verified PII presence (accuracy samples)
untagged PII asset list
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated catalog synchronization showing tag presence and timestamps, plus regular audit sample results demonstrating high tag accuracy.
Weak: ad-hoc manual tagging spreadsheets without timestamps, or tags present only in local systems that are not reconciled to the canonical catalog.

affinity_keywords: "Personally Identifiable Information Processing Purposes | Data Tagging"

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires tagging/classification of PII (preventing untracked processing and enabling enforcement), as indicated by 'Data Tagging' in the control name.

PT-3(1).differentiation_note: PT-3(1) is specifically about the presence and quality of PII tags/classification on data assets (tag coverage and accuracy), whereas PT-3 is about recording processing purposes at the asset/policy level.

PT-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure the effectiveness and reliability of automated processes for assigning PII tags and processing purposes: percentage of tagging/purpose assignments performed automatically, automation success rate, failure count, and mean time from asset discovery to automated tagging/assignment.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automation effectiveness is best shown by percentage coverage of automated actions (what portion is automated) plus rates (success/failure) and counts for scale and troubleshooting.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of tagging/purpose-assignment actions and count/percentage performed by automation versus manually; automation job success/failure counts and timestamps; mean time between asset onboarding and automated tagging/purpose assignment; list of automation exceptions and remediation actions.
data_signals: automation job execution logs with timestamps
automated tagging / purpose-assignment event records (asset id, tag/purpose, method=automated/manual)
automation job success and failure counts and error codes
time delta from asset discovery/onboarding to automated tag/purpose assignment
exception or manual remediation queue for failed automation runs
automation configuration/version identifier
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-generated event logs showing automated tag/purpose assignments with success indicators and short time-to-tag metrics, and low exception queue counts.
Weak: occasional scripts or one-off manual annotations labeled 'automated' without machine logs, or long delays between discovery and tagging.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control emphasizes automated assignment and tagging for PII processing purposes (preventive by reducing manual omissions), as indicated by the term 'Automation' in the control name.

PT-3(2).differentiation_note: PT-3(2) is focused on the automation mechanics and reliability (job success rates, latency) of tagging/purpose-assignment, unlike PT-3(1) which is about tag coverage and accuracy regardless of method.

PT-4.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of subjects with documented, auditable consent records for each processing activity and track consent-related events (grants, updates, revocations) and their processing timeliness to demonstrate consent is obtained and enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Consent is primarily a coverage/compliance control (preventive) so percentage metrics (coverage of records with valid consent) are most useful; counts of events and trends over time supplement coverage data to show ongoing operation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of subjects (user IDs or data subjects) with a stored consent record that includes scope, timestamp, source, and version, plus counts and timestamps of consent grants/updates/revocations and processing completion flags per downstream system.
data_signals: consent record entries with user ID, timestamp, scope, and version
consent source (web form, API, just-in-time prompt, tailored template)
consent acceptance events (timestamped)
consent update events and version history
consent revocation events and revocation processing status
mapping of consent scopes to processing activities (consent-to-activity linkage)
downstream data processing flags indicating consent honored
