evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, tamper-evident consent records with timestamps, source, and linked processing-activity mapping; continuous logs showing enforcement in downstream systems.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or one-off screenshots of consent without timestamps, scope, or linkage to processing activities.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on obtaining and recording consent (prevention of unauthorized processing) as indicated by the name 'Consent' and emphasis on consent records and management.

PT-4.differentiation_note: This is the baseline consent control focused on recording and enforcing consent overall; its measurement emphasizes broad coverage and linkage of consent records to processing activities rather than specialized consent flavors or revocation processing.

PT-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of processing activities and user interactions that use tailored consent scopes/templates and verify that consent scopes stored match the intended tailored preferences for each subject and purpose.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Tailored consent is a coverage/accuracy control (preventive): auditors need percentage coverage and distribution of consent types to confirm correct tailoring; counts help track template usage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of consent records that include a tailored scope/template field matching the processing purpose, distribution of consent template usage by purpose, and counts of mismatches between declared processing purpose and stored consent scope.
data_signals: consent record with tailored template identifier
consent scope/purpose field values
mapping table of templates → allowed processing activities
user attribute or preference fields used to generate tailored consent
audit entries showing which template was presented
mismatch or exception logs when processing lacks matching tailored consent
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable consent templates linked to presented UI artifacts and processing purpose mapping, with automated checks flagging mismatches.
Weak: manual notes that a tailored consent was 'offered' without template identifiers or mapping to processing activities.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement specifies 'Tailored Consent' (design-time selection of consent scope) which is preventive—ensuring consent presented matches purpose before processing.

PT-4(1).differentiation_note: Unlike the base PT-4 which measures overall consent coverage, this enhancement requires measurement of template/scoped consent accuracy and distribution (template-level linkage), a finer-grained mapping between consent templates and processing purposes.

PT-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure how often just-in-time consent prompts are presented when a new processing action is about to occur and the prompt acceptance/decline rate and time-to-consent to demonstrate just-in-time delivery and subject decisioning are functioning.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Just-in-time consent involves event-driven prompts and user responses; rate metrics (prompts served per opportunity) and acceptance rates best capture operational effectiveness, with percentages for coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate of just-in-time consent prompts served per eligible processing event, the acceptance and decline counts and percentages, and time between prompt presentation and user response; also record whether processing was blocked until consent.
data_signals: just-in-time consent prompt events with timestamp and triggering action
prompt presentation success/failure logs
user response events (accept/decline) with timestamps
processing events indicating whether action proceeded or was blocked
prompt template or message identifier
acceptance rate by prompt type and by user cohort
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated event logs linking trigger → prompt → response → processing decision, with near-real-time metrics on prompt delivery and acceptance.
Weak: manual confirmation that prompts exist without event logs showing linkage or blocked processing when declined.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control ensures consent is obtained at the time of processing ('Just-in-time'), preventing unauthorized processing—a preventive functionality focused on prompt delivery and acceptance.

PT-4(2).differentiation_note: This enhancement focuses on event-driven prompt delivery and user response metrics (prompt rate and acceptance) unlike the baseline which emphasizes persistent consent records.

PT-4(3).measurement_goal: Measure the count and timeliness of consent revocations processed and the completeness of propagation of revocation decisions across all systems that use the subject's data (e.g., percent of systems updated within target time).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Revocation is corrective/operational: rate metrics (e.g., revocation processing velocity, propagation rate) measure how quickly and reliably revocations are enacted; counts and percentages show coverage and volume.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of revocation requests received, mean/median time to propagate revocation to each downstream system, percentage of systems updated within the policy-defined timeframe, and any failed propagation attempts with error reasons.
data_signals: revocation request events with user ID and timestamp
revocation processing completion timestamps per downstream system
propagation status flags for each integrated system
error/failure logs for propagation attempts
audit trails showing data access disabled or access tokens revoked
notifications to downstream owners confirming revocation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated end-to-end logs showing revocation request → propagation events → downstream system state change with timestamps and success codes.
Weak: manual confirmation emails or one-off database deletes without systematic propagation records or timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement concerns handling and enacting revocation after consent withdrawal (processing the change), which is corrective—it changes system state to remove consent-based access.

PT-4(3).differentiation_note: This enhancement is focused on operational execution after consent withdrawal (timeliness and propagation), unlike the baseline (which focuses on initial capture) and the other enhancements (which focus on tailoring and prompt delivery).

PT-5.measurement_goal: Measure the presence and completeness of the published privacy notice and the percentage of data-collection flows where the current privacy notice is displayed and acknowledged prior to or at time of collection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PT-5 is primarily a preventive control verifying coverage and completeness (coverage % and compliance % are most useful); counts of missing notices and trending over time are useful secondary signals to show improvement or regression.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of published data collection endpoints (web/app/forms/ APIs) that expose the current privacy notice version and the percentage of collection events with a recorded notice_shown and (if required) acknowledgement before data is accepted; plus the existence and last_updated timestamp of the canonical privacy notice document and a checklist showing required content elements are present.
data_signals: canonical_privacy_notice_page: version_identifier and last_updated_timestamp
privacy_notice_content_checklist (required elements present/absent)
UI telemetry event: privacy_notice_shown flag with timestamp and session_id
consent/acknowledgement records: user_id, notice_version, timestamp
count of data_collection_endpoints and list of endpoints missing notice
privacy_notice_change_log entries (author, change_summary, timestamp)
policy or published notice URL accessibility/HTTP status
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated UI/telemetry events that show privacy_notice_shown and consent/acknowledgement records tied to notice_version for each data-collection event; versioned canonical notice with change log.
Weak: one-off screenshots, manual attestations, or a single published page without events tying the notice to actual collection flows.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires publishing and presenting privacy information to data subjects (a preventative behavior to reduce privacy risk).

PT-5.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on the global published privacy notice (existence, versioning, and coverage across collection endpoints) rather than per-interaction timing or specific statutory statements.

PT-5(1).measurement_goal: Measure the rate and timeliness at which just‑in‑time (JIT) privacy notices are displayed to users immediately before the collection of personal data, and the acknowledgement/acceptance rate for those notices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective/preventive hybrid for interaction-level behavior, counts of JIT notice display events and failures are primary to demonstrate enforcement; percentages (display rate, acknowledgement rate) and trends show coverage and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of data-collection transactions and count of transactions where a just-in-time notice was displayed prior to collection, plus acknowledgement events and latency between notice display and data entry; include per-channel breakdown (web/mobile/API) and by collection type.
data_signals: UI telemetry event: jit_notice_displayed (timestamp, session_id, endpoint_id)
UI telemetry event: data_entry_started (timestamp, session_id) to validate ordering
acknowledgement/consent event: user_id, notice_version, timestamp
jit_notice_dismissal or opt_out events
per-endpoint mapping of endpoints requiring JIT notice (endpoint_id, requirement_flag)
failed_display or error logs where jit_notice_displayed is missing while requirement_flag=true
per-channel display counts (web, mobile, API) and timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous telemetry showing notice_displayed before any data_entry_started for every required endpoint, with acknowledgement tied to notice_version.
Weak: manual QA screenshots, periodic sampling, or developer attestations without event logs tying notice display to each collection event.

control_type_classification.reasoning: PT-5(1) mandates delivering notices at the point of collection (preventing unaware collection); measurement focuses on delivery events prior to data capture.

PT-5(1).differentiation_note: This entry is focused on interaction-level delivery and timing (JIT notices and their acknowledgement/ordering) rather than the canonical published notice or statutory statement inclusion.

PT-5(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required collection forms, templates, and communications that include the required Privacy Act Statement language and the completeness of each statement against the mandated content checklist.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive compliance requirement that is best expressed as coverage/completeness percentages (percent of forms with required Privacy Act Statements); counts of non‑compliant forms and comparisons to policy templates are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of form templates and communications that include a Privacy Act Statement where required, and for each such instance a checklist of required elements present (e.g., authority, purpose, disclosure consequences) along with template version and last_reviewed timestamp.
data_signals: form_template_registry: template_id, has_privacy_act_statement flag, statement_version, last_reviewed_timestamp
privacy_act_statement_content_checklist (authority, purpose, routine_use, disclosure consequences)
count of active forms mapped to templates missing the statement
published collection notices inventory with statement_presence boolean
change_log or approval records for Privacy Act Statement templates (approver, timestamp)
sampled form exports or automated content scans showing the statement text
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative form template registry showing has_privacy_act_statement=true and automated content scans verifying required elements for every active form.
Weak: verbal attestations or isolated examples of the statement without linkage to a canonical registry or automated verification across all forms.

control_type_classification.reasoning: PT-5(2) requires inclusion of a statutory Privacy Act Statement in collection instruments, which is a preventive compliance control ensuring required information is provided.

PT-5(2).differentiation_note: This entry specifically targets statutory Privacy Act Statement presence and completeness in forms/templates, rather than general privacy notices or just-in-time display behavior.

PT-6.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of System of Records Notices (SORN) across all systems of records: percent of systems with a published SORN, percent of SORNs current within the required review cycle, and count of systems lacking a SORN or with expired/overdue notices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive/documentation control — auditors need coverage and currency percentages (coverage % of systems with up-to-date notices) plus counts and trends to show remediation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of systems in the authoritative system registry vs. number with a linked SORN record (including publication_date and last_review_date), percent of SORNs with current effective_date within the policy review period, and count of SORNs missing publication or approval metadata.
data_signals: privacy registry entries linking system identifier to SORN
SORN publication_date and effective_date fields
SORN last_review_date and next_review_due timestamp
SORN version, approval signature or approver ID
publication URL or Federal Register publication proof
asset inventory system identifiers to reconcile coverage
list of record categories covered per SORN
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated privacy registry records with system IDs, publication URLs, signed approval metadata, and last_review_date timestamps; Weak: ad-hoc PDFs or draft notices without approval dates or no authoritative registry linkage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires producing and maintaining SORN documentation (publication and currency), which prevents inadequate notice by ensuring documentation exists and is current.

PT-6.differentiation_note: PT-6 is the high-level control about existence, publication and currency of SORNs across systems; its measurement focuses on coverage and review-status rather than the content of routine uses or exemptions.

PT-6(1).measurement_goal: Measure how many SORNs include documented routine uses and whether actual disclosures map to those declared routine uses: percent of SORNs with explicit routine uses, count and rate of data disclosures that align to a declared routine use, and count of disclosures without a corresponding routine use.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage (what percent of notices include routine uses) and runtime alignment (percent of disclosures matching declared routine uses), so percentage metrics are primary with counts/rates for event volumes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of SORNs that include a routine_uses section; count and rate of recorded disclosure events (data sharing records) that map to a declared routine use identifier; count of disclosure events with no matching routine_use entry and approval status for ad-hoc disclosures.
data_signals: SORN routine_uses text or structured routine_use identifiers
data disclosure logs / data sharing event records with recipient, purpose, timestamp
mapping table between disclosure event purpose and declared routine_use identifiers
approval records for ad-hoc disclosures tied to routine uses
audit trail entries showing disclosure justification and approver ID
last_review_date for routine_uses in the SORN
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous disclosure logs mapped to structured routine_use identifiers and automated matching reports; Weak: occasional manual attestations or undocumented ad-hoc disclosures without log entries or approvals.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires documenting routine uses and ensuring disclosures conform to them — a preventive/documentation control focused on declared permitted sharing and approvals.

PT-6(1).differentiation_note: PT-6(1) specifically measures documented routine uses and real-world disclosure alignment, unlike PT-6 which measures SORN coverage and currency.

PT-6(2).measurement_goal: Measure documentation and authorized use of exemption rules in SORNs: percent of SORNs that include documented exemption rules with legal authority cited, count of exemptions applied, and count of SORNs missing exemption rationale or approval.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about documented exemptions and authorized application; percentage coverage of documented exemptions is the primary verifier, with counts of exemptions and comparisons to authority/policy as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of SORNs that include an exemption_rules section with cited statutory/regulatory authority and approver metadata; count of exemption applications (instances where exemption was invoked) with justification and approver ID; count of SORNs missing exemption rationale.
data_signals: SORN exemption_rules text and cited authority (statute/regulation)
approval records or legal memoranda authorizing exemption
logs/records of instances where an exemption was applied (date, system, records affected)
last_review_date for exemption rules
policy mapping showing permitted exemptions and conditions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: SORN entries with explicit exemption text, statute citations, signed legal approval, and logged instances of exemption application; Weak: undocumented verbal exemptions, undocumented ad-hoc exemptions, or exemptions without legal authority citations.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Focuses on documenting and authorizing exemption rules in SORNs, preventing unauthorized invocation of privacy exemptions through documented authority and approvals.

PT-6(2).differentiation_note: PT-6(2) specifically targets documented exemption rules and authorized application of exemptions, unlike PT-6 which is about overall notice coverage and PT-6(1) which focuses on routine uses and disclosures.

PT-7.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and completeness of identification, inventorying, and classification of specified categories of personally identifiable information (PII) across systems, datasets, and data flows; report percent of data stores inventoried and percent of identified PII items labeled to category.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive and requires demonstrating coverage/completeness (percent of systems/datasets classified). Secondary counts (number of classified datasets), distributions (by category), and trends (coverage over time) help show scope and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of information assets (databases, file shares, buckets, applications) that are included in the data inventory and assigned a specific PII category; count of datasets with at least one PII category tag; distribution of identified PII by category; number of unclassified or unknown assets.
data_signals: data inventory entries with fields for 'Specific Categories of Personally Identifiable Information' or equivalent category tags
data discovery/classification scan results showing identified PII instances and assigned category
list of systems/applications with classification status (classified/unclassified)
count of datasets labeled vs. total datasets
metadata tags indicating PII category on storage objects (databases, S3 buckets, file shares)
records of classification exceptions or manual review notes
data flow diagrams or mapping entries that reference classified PII categories
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated discovery/classification engine outputs and metadata tags across all inventoryed assets (strong)
Systematic data inventory with category fields populated and versioned (strong)
Ad-hoc spreadsheets or one-off manual listings without timestamps or system metadata (weak)
Single-point-in-time manual attestations without automated scan corroboration (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on identifying and classifying categories of PII to prevent improper handling, which is inherently preventive (requires establishing inventory and labels).

PT-7.differentiation_note: This entry covers the general requirement to identify and inventory PII categories across the environment; sibling controls specify measurement for particular PII categories (e.g., SSNs, First Amendment information). Note: control description text was limited to the title; measurement guidance assumes inventory/classification obligations implied by the title.

PT-7(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems and datasets that have been discovered, labeled, and have appropriate protective controls applied for Social Security Numbers (SSNs); count access events and protective coverage (encryption, masking, access control) for SSN-containing records.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control specifying a high-risk PII category (SSNs), primary measurement is coverage percentage (how many assets with SSNs are inventoried/protected). Counts of SSN-containing datasets and rates of access events are useful secondary signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of discovered SSN-containing datasets that have applied mandatory protections (encryption at rest, masking, least-privilege access) and percent of SSN datasets included in the authoritative data inventory; counts of access events to SSN fields and number of unprotected SSN datasets.
data_signals: data discovery scan results listing instances of 'Social Security Numbers' in datasets
inventory records marked with 'Social Security Numbers' category
encryption-at-rest status for databases/tables containing SSNs
data masking/tokenization records or transformation logs for SSN fields
access control lists (ACLs) and role assignments for SSN-containing datasets
access event logs filtered for accesses to SSN fields (identity, timestamp, action)
exceptions and approved usage justifications for SSN access
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated discovery showing SSN instances plus system metadata and applied protection flags (strong)
Access logs with user, purpose, and success/failure for SSN-field accesses (strong)
Manual labeling without corroborating discovery scans or lack of protection metadata (weak)
Evidence of protection only in documentation without technical enforcement (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control names a specific sensitive PII category (SSNs), implying requirements to identify and apply protections to prevent unauthorized disclosure, which is preventive.

PT-7(1).differentiation_note: This entry narrows PT-7 to SSNs: measurement must focus on discovery of SSN instances and technical protections (encryption/masking/access) rather than only cataloging general PII categories.

PT-7(2).measurement_goal: Measure the inventory and classification coverage for datasets containing First Amendment information and the presence of handling controls (access restrictions, consent/justification records, minimization or redaction where required); report percent of First Amendment datasets inventoried and access events by purpose.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive measurement should show coverage (percentage of systems/datasets tagged as containing First Amendment information) and counts/access rates to demonstrate controls and unusual access patterns.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets and datasets containing First Amendment information that are listed in the data inventory and tagged appropriately; count and rate of access events to those datasets with documented purpose/consent; presence of minimization, redaction, or consent records linked to each dataset.
data_signals: inventory/classification entries labeled 'First Amendment Information' or equivalent
data discovery/classification outputs identifying First Amendment-related content
access logs showing accesses to First Amendment datasets with user, timestamp, and purpose
consent or lawful basis records associated with First Amendment datasets
data sharing agreements or redaction/minimization logs for First Amendment content
list of systems/applications that process or store First Amendment Information
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated classification outputs and linked consent/justification records for each dataset (strong)
Access logs that include documented purpose and business justification for accessing First Amendment information (strong)
Manual notes or undocumented classifications without consent or access justification (weak)
Lack of linkage between inventory tags and enforcement controls (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Naming a specific sensitive category (First Amendment information) implies obligations to identify and restrict handling to prevent misuse, so the control is preventive.

PT-7(2).differentiation_note: This entry targets measurement for First Amendment information specifically (inventory, consent/justification, access controls), which differs from PT-7's broader requirement to classify all PII categories and from PT-7(1)'s focus on SSNs. Limited description required conservative assumptions about required protective controls.

PT-8.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of active computer-matching activities that have a documented, approved matching agreement and required privacy safeguards, plus counts of matching executions and audit events showing enforcement of those agreements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PT-8 is primarily preventive — auditors need coverage/approval rates (percentage of matches with approved agreements). Counts of matching operations and trends over time help show ongoing compliance and enforcement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A queryable inventory of computer-matching programs (operation_id, systems involved, data elements matched, stated purpose) joined to a registry of matching agreements (agreement_id, approver, effective/expiry dates) and to audit logs of matching executions (timestamp, user/system, query id). Useful metrics: percent of active operations with a current approved agreement, count of matching executions without agreement, and rate of overdue agreement renewals.
data_signals: matching_operation_inventory records: operation_id, systems_involved, data_elements, purpose
matching_agreement_registry: agreement_id, parties, approver_id, approval_timestamp, effective_date, expiration_date
approval_timestamps and approver identifiers (who approved each matching agreement)
audit logs of matching executions: operation_id, execution_timestamp, initiating_user_or_system, query_parameters
records of privacy notices or subject notifications tied to a matching operation (notice_id, recipient, date)
data_sharing agreement documents and metadata (document_id, parties, scope of shared attributes)
retention/deletion logs for matched outputs (deletion_timestamp, retained_until, justification)
exceptions/waiver records and renewal/expiry status for matching agreements
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized, machine-readable registry of matching agreements linked to enforcement controls that block executions for unregistered matches and complete audit trails for each execution
Moderate: periodic attestations plus accessible agreement documents and matching inventories with timestamps
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or emailed approvals without execution logs or linkage between agreements and matching operations

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Computer Matching Requirements' implies controls that require pre-approval/agreements and safeguards before matching occurs, which is preventive in nature.

PT-8.differentiation_note: This control focuses on documentation/approval and oversight of computer-matching activities (agreements, registry, and audit linkage) rather than technical detection or remediation of incidents; its unique measurement need is verifying policy coverage and approved agreement linkage to executed matches.

RA-1.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of documented risk assessment policies and procedures: percent of required RA policies in a document repository with an owner, recorded last-review date within the required review cycle, and count of exceptions or overdue reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive governance control, auditors need coverage and currency percentages (policy coverage, review-on-schedule rates); counts of missing/overdue items and trends over review cycles help demonstrate sustained operation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of required risk-assessment policies and procedures stored in an authoritative repository that have an assigned owner, an approval record, and a last-reviewed timestamp within the policy-defined review period; counts of documents missing approval, missing owner, or with overdue reviews; log of exceptions and remediation actions.
data_signals: document repository entries for RA policies (document ID, title)
policy owner assignment fields (owner identity, team)
approval records (approver, approval timestamp, version)
document version history / last reviewed timestamps
policy review schedule or review-cycle metadata (required review interval)
acknowledgement records or distribution receipts (who received/read)
exception/waiver records and remediation tracking (request date, approval, closure date)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative document management system with immutable version history, time-stamped approvals, and automated review reminders showing continuous review activity.
Weak: ad-hoc PDFs or email threads without version history, or a single snapshot file labeled as 'policy' with no approval or review timestamps.
Strong: recorded owner and acknowledgement receipts for distribution; Weak: absence of owner or only informal distribution evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Policy and Procedures' indicates governance artifacts intended to prevent gaps in risk assessment processes by documenting required behavior and responsibilities.

RA-1.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on documentation completeness, ownership, approval and review-cycle metrics for RA policies; because the provided control text was empty beyond the title, keywords and signals are inferred from the control name and emphasize governance/documentation measurement rather than operational detection.

RA-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of information systems and information types that have an approved security categorization (impact level) documented, reviewed, and linked to system owners and security objectives; track time-since-last-categorization and number of uncategorized or overdue reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: RA-2 is a preventive control that requires coverage/assignment of impact levels to all systems, so percentage (coverage) shows compliance; counts and trending show volume and maintenance over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of cataloged information systems and information types with an official security categorization record (impact level assigned: low/moderate/high), the date of initial categorization, the date of most recent review, and the designated system owner/approver; compute percent coverage vs. total inventory and list systems past their review cycle.
data_signals: authoritative system inventory with unique system IDs
security categorization records showing assigned impact level (low/moderate/high)
categorization approval or sign-off metadata (approver ID and timestamp)
categorization creation and last-review timestamps
mapping of information types to systems
system owner / custodian assignments
overdue-categorization flags or review-cycle schedule
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated system inventory integrated with a categorization attribute and time-stamped approvals showing continuous updates and scheduled review automation.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or single point-in-time PDFs of categorizations with no timestamps, missing approver metadata, or no linkage to the canonical asset inventory.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes categorization (assignment of impact levels) for systems ahead of other activities, which prevents misclassification and drives downstream controls.

RA-2.differentiation_note: This control focuses on establishing and maintaining baseline categorizations (coverage and review cadence) across all systems, rather than prioritizing actions based on those impact levels.

RA-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of systems prioritized for assessment, monitoring, or remediation based on assigned impact levels (e.g., proportion of high-impact systems placed on accelerated assessment/remediation schedules) and track SLA adherence for prioritized actions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Prioritization is an enforcement/coverage requirement (preventive) — percent of prioritized systems shows coverage; rate (e.g., assessment/remediation throughput) and counts show execution against priority.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: List of systems with assigned impact levels and a prioritized status flag, schedule and timestamps for prioritized assessments/remediations, time-to-assessment/remediation for high-impact systems, and SLA or priority-policy definitions to compare against actual completion times.
data_signals: asset inventory with impact-level attribute and priority flag
prioritization policy or priority matrix (mapping impact level to priority actions/SLA)
scheduled assessment/remediation tasks for prioritized systems with timestamps
assessment/remediation completion timestamps and owner IDs
SLA or timeframe targets for high/moderate/low impact systems
overdue prioritized tasks count and age
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated prioritized work queues driven from the canonical impact-level field with time-stamped scheduling and completion records tied to SLAs/policy thresholds.
Weak: manual lists or undocumented prioritization where priority decisions are not linked to impact-level fields, lacking timestamps or SLA comparisons.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement requires prioritizing activities based on impact level (planning/assignment ahead of execution), which is a preventive organizational measure to direct resources.

RA-2(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement builds on RA-2 by requiring that impact levels be used to prioritize and schedule assessments/remediations — measurement must link impact-level attributes to prioritized actioning and SLA adherence rather than simply documenting categorizations.

RA-3.measurement_goal: Measure the count and severity-distribution of identified risks, percentage of assets/entities covered by the latest risk assessment, and the frequency of risk assessment updates to demonstrate the organization is identifying and tracking risks on an ongoing basis.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Risk assessment effectiveness is best evidenced by counts of identified risks and assessments plus percent coverage and trends over time showing detection and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of risk register entries created/updated per assessment cycle (grouped by severity/likelihood), percentage of assets/systems covered by the most recent assessment, and timestamped assessment execution records showing periodicity (e.g., last assessment date per asset group).
data_signals: risk register entries with creation and update timestamps
risk severity/likelihood ratings per entry
asset/system inventory linked to risk register (asset IDs and criticality)
risk assessment execution timestamps and assessor identifiers
assessment scope documents or questionnaires mapped to assets
remediation/mitigation action items and their status
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, timestamped risk register updates and linked asset IDs with frequent updates (strong);
Single annual PDF reports or undated narrative assessments without traceable entries (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires identifying and assessing risks (detective) as indicated by the term 'Risk Assessment' and measuring found risks and assessment frequency.

RA-3.differentiation_note: This entry covers the core organizational risk assessment processes and population-level measurement (counts, coverage, cadence) rather than specialized inputs (e.g., supply chain or predictive analytics) handled by its siblings.

RA-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure supplier coverage and risk exposure by counting assessed suppliers, percentage of critical suppliers with completed assessments, and the number of high-risk findings in supplier assessments to prove supply chain risks are identified and tracked.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Supply-chain assessment effectiveness is demonstrated by coverage percentages (percent of suppliers assessed) and counts of high-risk suppliers; trend shows improvement or deterioration over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of suppliers (by criticality tier) with completed supply-chain risk assessments within the policy timeframe, count of supplier assessments that returned high or critical risk findings, and timestamps of assessment completion linked to supplier identifiers.
data_signals: supplier inventory with criticality classification
supply-chain risk assessment records per supplier with completion timestamps
assessment findings categorized by severity for each supplier
contract clauses or attestations captured during assessment
mitigation/action items for supplier risks and their status
date of last reassessment or monitoring frequency for each supplier
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated supplier inventory linked to assessment records with required fields (strong);
Ad-hoc spreadsheets without consistent supplier identifiers or missing timestamps (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on assessing and identifying supply-chain risks (detective) as implied by 'Supply Chain Risk Assessment'.

RA-3(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on third-party/supplier population coverage and findings rather than enterprise asset risk coverage—measurement must link supplier IDs and assessment records.

RA-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure the number and diversity of intelligence sources integrated into risk assessments, the rate at which intelligence items are ingested and referenced in assessments, and the count of actionable intelligence items used to update risk ratings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective use of all-source intelligence is evidenced by counts of sources, ingested intelligence items, and how many items are applied to risk assessments; percentages show coverage of intelligence use.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of distinct intelligence sources/feed connectors actively ingested per period, number of intelligence items (alerts/indicators) ingested per period, and percentage of risk assessments that cite or incorporate at least one intelligence item.
data_signals: list of configured intelligence feed connectors and source identifiers
intelligence ingestion timestamps and item IDs
mapping of intelligence items to risk register entries or assessment notes
analyst references or tags indicating 'source' used in assessment documents
counts of actionable intelligence items (IOCs/TTPs) used to change risk ratings
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated ingestion logs with source IDs and item-to-risk-register mappings (strong);
Manual analyst summaries without source linkages or timestamps (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires using intelligence sources to inform assessments (detective) as implied by 'Use of All-source Intelligence'.

RA-3(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures integration and usage of external/internal intelligence sources in assessments, rather than assessment coverage or supply-chain specifics.

RA-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure the frequency of threat environment updates reflected in risk posture (count of threat updates applied), the number of risk register changes triggered by new threats, and time-to-update risk ratings after a new threat is identified.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Dynamic threat awareness is detective; counts of threat updates and triggered changes plus rates of updating demonstrate responsiveness to changing threats.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of documented threat advisories or threat feed updates that resulted in a change to risk ratings, timestamps of when threat intelligence was received and when risk register entries were updated, and mean time from threat receipt to risk posture update.
data_signals: threat advisory/alert ingestion timestamps
risk register change records with triggering event references
tickets or change logs linking threat items to risk updates
time delta between threat receipt and risk rating update
number of assets/references re-classified due to threat
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated linkage between threat feed events and risk register updates with timestamps (strong);
Unlinked narratives describing threat awareness without event timestamps (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control emphasizes awareness of changing threats and updating risk posture (detective), as signaled by 'Dynamic Threat Awareness'.

RA-3(3).differentiation_note: Emphasizes real-time/continuous detection and mapping of newly observed threats to risk posture, unlike static assessments or supplier-focused assessments.

RA-3(4).measurement_goal: Measure the operational use and performance of predictive analytics by counting predictive model outputs used in risk decisions, tracking prediction-to-incident correlation (predicted vs. observed incidents), and monitoring model accuracy/false-positive rates over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Predictive analytics should be measured by rates (e.g., prediction accuracy, true-positive rates) and model performance scores to show the effectiveness of forecasts used in risk assessment.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate of correct predictions (predicted incidents that materialized) over a defined period, count of predictions generated and acted upon, model performance metrics (precision/recall or equivalent score), and timestamps linking predictions to subsequent incident records.
data_signals: predictive model run outputs with prediction timestamps and IDs
mapping of prediction IDs to subsequent incident/alert IDs
model performance metrics (precision, recall, false-positive rate) per model version
model version and retrain timestamps
actions/tickets opened in response to predictions and their resolution status
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated logs mapping predictions to observed incidents with measurable accuracy and tracked model versions (strong);
Ad-hoc or retrospective claims of predictions without model outputs or incident linkage (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Using predictive analytics to foresee cyber events is a detective capability that identifies likely incidents, as indicated by 'Predictive Cyber Analytics'.

RA-3(4).differentiation_note: Focuses on analytic model outputs and prediction performance (accuracy and linkage to incidents), distinct from source-integration or manual risk assessment coverage.

RA-4.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that the RA-4 control has been retired and its requirements were incorporated into RA-3 by measuring the percent of RA-4 items documented as withdrawn and mapped to RA-3 and the presence of RA-3 policy/procedure updates and executed risk assessments that reflect those incorporated requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive (policy) control now withdrawn, a percentage metric (coverage/mapping rate) best shows how completely RA-4 requirements were incorporated into RA-3; counts of mapping entries and trends of RA-3 updates are useful secondary evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of RA-4 control items marked 'withdrawn' and the percentage of those items that have one-to-one mapping entries to RA-3, plus the presence and timestamped revision records of RA-3 policy/procedure documents reflecting the incorporation. Also measure the number and timestamps of risk assessments executed under RA-3 that reference the incorporated RA-4 requirements.
data_signals: control catalog entries for RA-4 with status 'Withdrawn' or equivalent
mapping table or traceability matrix entries linking RA-4 items to RA-3 sections
policy/procedure revision logs (RA-3) with document ID, revision date, and author indicating incorporation
change control or approval ticket records referencing 'incorporated into RA-3' or RA-4 retirement
risk assessment report timestamps and IDs showing assessments executed under RA-3 after incorporation
audit trail or control matrix showing RA-4 removed and references moved to RA-3
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: updated RA-3 policy documents with signed revision history plus an explicit traceability matrix mapping each RA-4 requirement to RA-3 and supporting change-control tickets.
Moderate: documented mappings without signed approvals or a single revised RA-3 document but limited assessment evidence.
Weak: only verbal statements or unversioned notes claiming incorporation, or absence of mapping and revision logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is labeled 'preventive' and the name 'Risk Assessment Update' implies policy/process prevention; description states it is withdrawn and incorporated into RA-3.

RA-4.differentiation_note: Unlike active RA controls that require operational monitoring or remediation metrics, this withdrawn control's unique measurement need is verification of formal retirement and documented incorporation into RA-3 (control-mapping and policy revision evidence).

RA-5.measurement_goal: Measure scan execution and coverage (which assets were scanned and when), the count of detected vulnerabilities broken down by severity, and the timeliness of scan frequency versus stated scanning policy to demonstrate ongoing monitoring and detection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: RA-5 is primarily a detective control (monitoring and scanning) so counts of detected vulnerabilities and scan executions are the most direct evidence; percentages (coverage) and trends (scan cadence/remediation over time) add completeness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of vulnerability scan executions per asset and timestamp, count of open vulnerabilities by asset and severity, and scan coverage percentage (assets scanned / total assets) compared to the scanning policy frequency.
data_signals: vulnerability scan execution timestamps and scanner instance IDs
asset inventory entries with asset ID and classification
vulnerability findings records (vulnerability ID, severity, scanner finding timestamp)
scan type metadata (credentialed vs. non-credentialed, authenticated vs. unauthenticated)
scan coverage ratio (scanned assets / total assets)
last-scan timestamp per asset
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated scan logs showing recurring executions and per-asset last-scan timestamps (strong).
High scan coverage percentage aligned to policy and low number of long-open vulnerabilities (strong).
Single ad-hoc scan reports or manual one-off exports without timestamps (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name 'Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning' explicitly indicates detection/monitoring of vulnerabilities.

RA-5.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall program-level monitoring: scan frequency, asset coverage, and counts of findings — while sibling controls focus on specific aspects like coverage depth, tool updates, or privileged access.

RA-5(1).measurement_goal: Measure whether vulnerability scanning tools are updated to current capability level (signature/engine/plug-in updates) and the frequency of those updates compared to the organization's update policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Despite being withdrawn into RA-5, the original requirement concerns tool update events (detective evidence). Counts of update events and rates of out-of-date tools directly show capability currency; coverage percentages and trends are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number and timestamps of vulnerability scanner engine/signature/plugin updates applied, inventory of scanner instances showing current engine/plugin versions, and percentage of scanners up-to-date against policy.
data_signals: scanner update event logs (timestamp, version applied)
scanner instance inventory with installed engine/version
policy-defined update frequency or required engine version
exceptions or failed update records
last successful update timestamp per scanner
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated update records per scanner with successful timestamps and version hashes (strong).
Manual statements that tools are 'up-to-date' without logs (weak).
Frequent failed update events or long intervals since last update (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input marks this as 'detective' and the text 'Update Tool Capability' implies monitoring of update events and tool-state evidence.

RA-5(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on scanner/tool update currency (engine/plugins) rather than scan coverage or findings; requires measuring tool-version and update events.

RA-5(2).measurement_goal: Measure whether the set of vulnerabilities and asset types to be scanned is maintained and updated per policy — percentage of policy-defined vulnerability categories included in active scan configurations and count of vulnerabilities intentionally excluded with documented justification.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control governs the scope/coverage configuration (preventive) so percentage coverage of the policy-defined vulnerability types and assets is the primary evidence; counts and trends are complementary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of policy-specified vulnerability categories and asset classes that are included in active scan configurations, and count of exclusions with documented business justification and review timestamps.
data_signals: active scanner configuration records (enabled plugin/check list)
policy list of vulnerabilities and asset classes to be scanned
documented exclusion list with justification and approver
scan policy revision history and timestamps
change logs showing updates to 'vulnerabilities to be scanned'
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated mapping of scanner configurations to the policy list showing near-100% alignment (strong).
Manual lists without timestamps or approvals (weak).
Well-documented exclusions with approved justification (acceptable evidence).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control's focus on updating which vulnerabilities are scanned implies a preventive/configuration control ensuring coverage is set correctly.

RA-5(2).differentiation_note: Targets the maintained list/scope of vulnerabilities and assets to scan (policy-to-configuration alignment), unlike siblings that focus on execution, depth, or analytics.

RA-5(3).measurement_goal: Measure breadth (percentage of assets/types included) and depth (percentage of checks executed per asset, credentialed vs. non-credentialed) of scanning coverage to demonstrate comprehensive detection capability.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control concerns coverage completeness (preventive/configurational), so percentage coverage metrics (assets covered, checks executed) are primary; distributions by asset tier and trending coverage improvements are useful secondary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets scanned at required scan depth (credentialed vs. non-credentialed), distribution of checks/plugins run per asset class, and count of asset types that are unscanned or scanned at reduced depth.
data_signals: asset inventory with asset type/class and criticality
scan job metadata indicating depth (credentialed/authenticated vs. unauthenticated)
per-asset list of checks/plugins executed
scan success/failure outcomes and error codes
coverage matrix: asset types vs. check/plugin execution
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated coverage matrix showing credentialed scans executed across all policy-critical assets (strong).
Point-in-time manual tests without per-asset depth metadata (weak).
High proportion of assets scanned unauthenticated when policy requires credentialed (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Breadth and Depth of Coverage' implies ensuring scan configurations prevent coverage gaps, a preventive coverage control.

RA-5(3).differentiation_note: Specifically measures coverage completeness (breadth & depth) — requiring per-asset depth metadata — whereas RA-5 focuses on general detection counts and scan frequency.

RA-5(4).measurement_goal: Measure detection of discoverable information (assets or services exposing sensitive data) by counting findings that identify discoverable/information-exposing conditions and the percentage of applications/assets scanned for discoverable information checks.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detecting exposed/discoverable information produces discrete findings (counts); percentages of assets scanned for these checks and trends of such findings are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of scan findings categorized as 'discoverable information' exposures (per asset/application), percentage of assets/applications where discoverable-information checks were included in scan profiles, and time since discovery to mitigation/open-ticket creation.
data_signals: scan finding records labeled discoverable-information or information-exposure
scan profile/checklist indicating discoverable-information tests
asset/application mapping to findings
finding timestamps and mitigation ticket IDs
percentage of applications included in discoverable-information scans
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated scan findings with discoverable-information classification and remediation ticket linkage (strong).
Manual ad-hoc discovery notes without timestamps or repeatable scan evidence (weak).
High percentage of assets never scanned for discoverable-information checks (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Discoverable Information' indicates scanning to detect exposed information, a detective activity.

RA-5(4).differentiation_note: Concentrates on discovery of exposed/discoverable information specifically (types of findings and check inclusion) rather than overall vulnerability counts or scanner configuration.

RA-5(5).measurement_goal: Measure presence and use of privileged/credentialed access for scans: percentage of critical assets scanned with privileged credentials and count of scan failures attributable to lack of privileged access.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control focuses on use of privileged credentials to achieve deeper scan results (preventive/coverage); percentage of critical assets scanned with privileged access is the most direct measure, with counts of failures as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical or policy-tiered assets scanned using privileged/credentialed accounts versus total critical assets, and count of scan jobs failing due to authentication/privilege errors with error codes.
data_signals: scan job metadata indicating credentialed/privileged flag
credential inventory used by scanner (account ID, privilege level)
scan error logs indicating authentication/privilege failures
asset criticality classification
approval records for use of privileged credentials
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated logs showing successful credentialed scans across critical assets and low authentication-failure counts (strong).
Manual statements that 'privileged access used' without linkage to scan logs (weak).
High counts of credential-related scan failures or many critical assets scanned unauthenticated (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The emphasis on 'Privileged Access' for scanning is about ensuring configuration and access prerequisites (preventive) so scans have required depth.

RA-5(5).differentiation_note: Uniquely requires measuring credential use and authentication outcomes for scans (privileged/credentialed coverage), distinct from general scan counts or coverage matrices.

RA-5(6).measurement_goal: Measure automated trend outputs: rates of new vs. remediated vulnerabilities over time, trend lines of open vulnerability counts by severity, and automated alerts triggered by anomalous increases in findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control explicitly specifies automated trend analyses, so trend metrics are primary; rates of detection/remediation and distributions by severity/supporting dimensions are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Time-series of open-vulnerability counts by severity, rate of new findings vs. remediated findings per period, and number of automated trend-analysis alerts generated for anomalous activity.
data_signals: time-stamped vulnerability records (created and closed timestamps)
vulnerability severity classification per finding
automated trend report outputs and alert logs
scheduled analytics job run timestamps and result summaries
anomaly detection alert records tied to vulnerability metrics
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, timestamped trend reports and alerting records showing persistent reductions or spikes (strong).
Static monthly summary PDFs without underlying time-series data (weak).
Trend analyses that can be reproduced from raw timestamps (strong).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Automated Trend Analyses' indicates ongoing detection and analytics to observe vulnerability trends (detective).

RA-5(6).differentiation_note: Focuses on analytics and time-series outputs (trending) rather than single-scan evidence or configuration settings.

RA-5(7).measurement_goal: Measure whether configuration/state changes relevant to vulnerabilities are tracked (inventory/configuration change records) and whether historical component/firmware/OS changes are available for vulnerability correlation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Originally incorporated into CM-8 and marked detective: counts of configuration change records and their availability for correlation are primary; trends in change volume and comparisons to baselines aid analysis.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of configuration change records per asset (with timestamps), percentage of assets with historic configuration/audit snapshots available, and ability to correlate recent changes to vulnerability discovery.
data_signals: configuration change logs with timestamps and actor IDs
component/firmware/OS inventory with historical snapshots
correlation records linking changes to vulnerability findings
audit/archive retention records showing historic logs retained
evidence_strength_indicators: Comprehensive, timestamped configuration history available for affected assets enabling direct correlation (strong).
Lack of historic snapshots or missing timestamps (weak).
Ad-hoc spreadsheets without immutable logs (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input labels it 'detective' (withdrawn into CM-8) and the content implies historical/audit detection capabilities.

RA-5(7).differentiation_note: Addresses historical configuration/audit retention and correlation capability (change history) rather than active scanning or analytic trend outputs.

RA-5(8).measurement_goal: Measure frequency and coverage of historic audit log reviews that are used to detect previously missed vulnerabilities or anomalous activity — count of reviewed log windows and counts of findings identified via historic-log review.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective activity (reviewing historic audit logs); counts of reviews, counts of findings originating from historic log review, and trends in detection via log review are the most direct evidentiary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of historic audit log review activities (with reviewer and timestamp), number of vulnerabilities or anomalies identified from historic log review, and percentage of systems whose logs were reviewed in the audit window.
data_signals: historic audit log review tickets/records (timestamp, reviewer, scope)
log retention index indicating available historical log ranges
findings originating from historic-log reviews with timestamps
coverage list of systems whose logs were included in reviews
evidence_strength_indicators: Documented, timestamped review artifacts linking specific findings to historic log entries (strong).
Statements that 'historic logs are reviewed' without artifacts (weak).
Regular scheduled reviews with increasing coverage (strong).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Review Historic Audit Logs' explicitly describes a detective review process of logs to identify issues.

RA-5(8).differentiation_note: Specifically measures log-review activities and their findings from historic logs, distinct from active scanning or trend analytics.

RA-5(9).measurement_goal: Measure penetration testing activities and analytic outputs: count of penetration tests performed, coverage of in-scope systems, and number of validated findings that were previously undetected by routine scanning.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Although withdrawn into CA-8 and labeled detective, penetration testing produces discrete findings; counts of tests and counts of validated findings are primary metrics with percentage coverage and trends as useful supplements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of penetration tests executed in the period, list of in-scope assets/systems tested, count of validated findings (and how many were not previously identified by scans), and remediation ticketing for findings.
data_signals: penetration test reports with scope and execution timestamps
in-scope asset list for each test
validated finding records and cross-reference to prior scan findings
remediation ticket IDs and remediation timestamps
test coverage percentage (in-scope assets / total critical assets)
evidence_strength_indicators: Full penetration test reports with validated findings and remediation evidence (strong).
High-level summaries without validated findings or remediation linkage (weak).
Pen tests repeated periodically with decreasing new findings (strong trend evidence).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Name 'Penetration Testing and Analyses' indicates detective testing to find vulnerabilities; input marks it withdrawn into CA-8 but retains detective nature.

RA-5(9).differentiation_note: Focuses on penetration testing outputs and validated findings (point-in-time tests) rather than continuous scanning or automated trend analyses.

RA-5(10).measurement_goal: Measure the extent to which vulnerability scan outputs are enriched and correlated with other telemetry (threat intelligence, detection alerts, patch state) by counting correlated findings and the percent of scan findings with enrichment or matching detection events.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a detective correlation control — auditors want counts of correlated events (how many scan findings linked to detections or intel); percentages (coverage/enrichment rate) and trends (improvement over time) are useful secondary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of vulnerability scan findings that have one or more correlation links (e.g., matched threat-intel indicators, SIEM/detection alerts, or patch-management records) and the percent of total findings enriched; measure mean time from scan detection to correlation/enrichment. Include identifiers to enable asset-level joins (scan_id, finding_id, asset_id).
data_signals: vulnerability scan result records (scan_id, timestamp, asset_id, finding_id, description)
scan finding identifiers mapped to external identifiers (CVE IDs or advisory IDs) from enrichment
threat intelligence match records linking intel indicators to scan findings (indicator_id, match_timestamp)
SIEM/detection alerts referencing same asset or CVE (alert_id, timestamp, related_finding_id)
patch-management state for affected assets (installed_version, missing_patches, last_patch_scan)
correlation/enrichment logs showing links created between scan findings and external data (enrichment_timestamp, source)
count of findings with zero enrichment or correlation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated correlation/enrichment logs showing per-finding links to threat intel or detection events and timestamped joins (machine-readable), enabling asset-level traceability.
Weak: ad-hoc manual reports or one-off spreadsheets that list correlated items without machine timestamps or linking identifiers.
Strong: high percentage of findings with enrichment (near policy_threshold) and low lag between scan and correlation; Weak: large backlog of un-enriched scan findings or long time-to-enrichment.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name emphasizes 'Monitoring and Scanning' and 'Correlate Scanning Information', indicating detection and correlation of scan outputs rather than prevention or remediation.

RA-5(10).differentiation_note: This control focuses on correlating scan outputs with external telemetry (enrichment and detection matches) rather than the act of scanning itself or remediation tracking — measurement must show linkage/enrichment per finding.

RA-5(11).measurement_goal: Measure adherence to the public disclosure program by tracking the count and percentage of vulnerability reports processed under the program, time-to-public-disclosure or advisory publication, and the percent of reports handled according to the program's policy timelines.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A disclosure program is policy-driven and preventive/control-oriented; primary metrics should express coverage/adherence (percent of reports handled per policy), with counts (reports, advisories) and trends (time-to-disclose over periods) as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of incoming vulnerability reports processed through the public disclosure program workflow within defined timelines, count of published advisories and responsible disclosure acknowledgements, and distribution of time from report receipt to public advisory publication. Include report_id, receipt_timestamp, disposition_timestamp, advisory_publication_timestamp, and policy SLA for disclosure.
data_signals: public disclosure program policy document or policy_identifier
vulnerability report intake records (report_id, receipt_timestamp, reporter_contact, severity)
disclosure workflow records (triage_timestamp, remediation_tracking_id, reviewer_id)
advisory/publication records (advisory_id, publication_timestamp, affected_products)
time-to-disclosure metrics per report (disclosure_timestamp - receipt_timestamp)
counts of reports acknowledged vs. reports published as advisories
exceptions or withheld-disclosure justification records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable intake and workflow logs with timestamps showing each report progressed through the public disclosure workflow and published advisories with timestamps.
Weak: informal email threads or occasional public advisories without structured intake records or SLA timestamps.
Strong: consistent percent adherence to the program policy and short, bounded time-to-disclosure distributions; Weak: many exceptions logged without documented justification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Public Disclosure Program' describes an established policy/process intended to govern disclosure behavior (preventive/governance) rather than purely detecting vulnerabilities.

RA-5(11).differentiation_note: Unlike the correlation-focused sibling, this control requires measurement of programmatic handling and publication timelines (policy adherence and publication counts) rather than telemetry enrichment or detection correlation.

RA-6.measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and coverage of technical surveillance countermeasure (TSCM) surveys across facilities/sensitive areas, count and classify detected covert devices or anomalous RF/wireless signals, and track remediation and chain-of-custody for discovered surveillance artifacts against policy-required survey frequency.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective activity is best evidenced by counts (number of surveys, devices found). Percentage coverage (facilities surveyed / total) and trend (surveys over time and findings trend) help demonstrate ongoing program effectiveness and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of completed TSCM surveys per facility/area per reporting period; list and count of detected surveillance devices or anomalous RF/wireless events with type, location, and detection timestamp; percent of required facilities surveyed (survey coverage); remediation tickets with detection-to-remediation timestamps and chain-of-custody records for recovered devices.
data_signals: TSCM survey reports (facility, date, scope, inspector)
survey execution timestamps and duration
facility/sensitive-area inventory and classification
detected device records (device type, MAC/frequency signature, location, detection timestamp)
RF/spectrum analysis logs (frequency, amplitude, timestamp, anomalies flagged)
remediation/mitigation tickets (open/close timestamps, remediation actions)
chain-of-custody logs for recovered devices/evidence
contractor/inspector qualification and engagement records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: dated, machine-generated RF spectrum logs plus signed TSCM reports and chain-of-custody entries showing detection and remediation timestamps; consistent survey schedules across facilities as defined by policy.
Weak: single ad-hoc or undated manual reports, undetailed statements of inspection without device logs, or absence of remediation timestamps and chain-of-custody.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires conducting surveys to detect technical surveillance devices and anomalous signals, which is inherently a detective activity (identification and detection).

RA-6.differentiation_note: Unlike vulnerability scanning controls, this control focuses on physical and RF/electronic detection activities (site surveys, spectrum logs, chain-of-custody) rather than software/asset vulnerability metrics; measurements must include physical survey reports and RF/spectrum data in addition to coverage metrics.

RA-7.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of recorded risks with an approved risk response (mitigate/accept/transfer/avoid) that have an implemented response plan and evidence of reduced residual risk within policy timeframes; also measure counts of accepted residual risks and average time-to-implement risk responses.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Risk response is primarily about coverage and completion of treatment plans (coverage % of risks with implemented responses). Counts of responses and trends over time help show throughput and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of risks in the risk register and the subset with documented, approved response strategies and an implemented remediation/mitigation change (with implementation timestamps and owners). Measure time delta between risk approval and implementation completion and compare pre- and post-implementation residual risk ratings.
data_signals: risk_register entries (risk_id, initial_risk_rating, description)
documented response strategy field (mitigate/accept/transfer/avoid) per risk
risk_response approval records (approver, approval_timestamp)
implementation plan records (tasks, owners, planned_completion_date, actual_completion_date)
post-implementation residual_risk_rating and validation/test results
risk_response SLA or policy timeline associated with each risk
exceptions or risk acceptance records (signed acceptance, date)
automation actions or remediation runbook execution logs (if automated)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured risk register with timestamps for approval and implementation, documented validation results showing reduced residual risk, and automated remediation/runbook execution logs.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets, email approvals without timestamps, or manual notes without validation of residual risk reduction.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Risk response focuses on selecting and implementing controls to reduce or treat risk (preventing realized loss), so it is primarily preventive in nature.

RA-7.differentiation_note: This control is about implementing and validating risk treatment actions (plans, approvals, implementation timestamps and residual risk ratings), differing from sibling controls that would focus on risk identification or continuous monitoring rather than treatment execution.

RA-8.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) across systems handling personal data: percent of in-scope systems with a completed, signed PIA; count of open privacy risks documented in PIAs; and time elapsed between system change and required PIA completion/approval.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: RA-8 is fundamentally about coverage and completeness (a preventive control), so percentage metrics (coverage/current PIAs completed) are primary; counts of documented risks and trending of reassessments over time are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope systems/applications with a current (date-stamped) PIA recorded in the PIA registry, the count of privacy risks and open mitigation action items per PIA, timestamps of PIA creation/review/approval, and whether PIA completion occurred prior to production deployment or major change.
data_signals: PIA registry entries with system/application identifier and PIA creation date
PIA approval records (approver identity, role e.g., privacy officer, approval timestamp, signed/acknowledged flag)
List of identified privacy risks per PIA (risk id, severity/impact, description)
Mitigation/action items linked to PIA (status, owner, creation and resolution timestamps)
System change or deployment records indicating PIA-required flag and associated PIA id
Last review/reassessment timestamp for each PIA and review periodicity
Dataflow or data mapping artifacts attached to the PIA documenting PII elements and processing activities
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized, dated PIA registry entries with approver signatures, linked mitigation tickets showing resolution timestamps, and pre-deployment checks that block release until PIA exists.
Weak: isolated PIA documents stored in personal folders, undated templates, or a one-time retrospective privacy memo without approval or linkage to change records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: PIAs are performed to identify and mitigate privacy risks before processing or deployment, so the control functions to prevent privacy incidents through assessment and approval.

RA-8.differentiation_note: This control is focused on governance-level privacy assessment, documentation, and approvals (PIA lifecycle and coverage), rather than technical controls (e.g., encryption or access enforcement) or detection activities; measurement must track PIA registry coverage, approvals, and mitigation tracking rather than technical telemetry.

RA-9.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of the organization’s criticality assignments: percentage of assets/systems with an assigned criticality level linked to a business process, distribution of assets by criticality, and timeliness of criticality reviews/updates against policy-defined review cycles.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a coverage/assignment control (how many assets have criticality assigned), so percentage best shows coverage; counts and distributions show volume and severity mix, and trend shows review cadence and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of assets/systems in the authoritative inventory, number and percentage with an assigned criticality label and business-process mapping, timestamp of last criticality review per asset, and count/percentage of assets with overdue or missing reviews relative to the policy review period.
data_signals: authoritative asset inventory records with a criticality/impact field
criticality assignment timestamp (date of last classification) per asset
business process or mission mapping table linking assets to processes
policy-defined criticality criteria and review frequency
change/CMDB records or change tickets that update criticality
list of assets with missing or null criticality labels
audit logs or approval records showing stakeholder sign-off of criticality
distribution of assets by criticality level (e.g., high/medium/low)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated asset inventory entries with persistent criticality fields, timestamps, and change history (CMDB/asset database) and documented stakeholder approvals;
Moderate: periodic exported reports from an authoritative inventory showing timestamps;
Weak: one-off spreadsheets or undocumented manual lists without timestamped change history or link to business processes.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Criticality analysis is an asset classification activity that prevents mis-prioritization of safeguards by ensuring assets are labeled and prioritized (classification/assignment functions).

RA-9.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on assigning and maintaining criticality/impact labels and their linkage to business processes and review cadence, unlike sibling RA controls that may focus on risk identification methods or vulnerability scanning coverage.

RA-10.measurement_goal: Measure the volume and cadence of threat-hunting engagements, the telemetry coverage used by hunts, the count and severity of validated findings discovered by hunts, and the time-to-escalation for hunt-identified threats so an auditor can verify proactive detection capability and operational effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Threat hunting is a detective/proactive detection activity — auditors most want counts of hunt engagements and findings (primary). Secondary metrics (rates of findings per hunt, telemetry coverage percentages, and trends over time) show effectiveness and improvement.
