evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of documented hunt engagements (with start/end timestamps and scope), telemetry source coverage percentage for each hunt, count of validated findings (by severity) from hunts, and time between hunt detection and escalation/ticket creation. Also collect hunt artifacts (queries, hypotheses, results) to demonstrate reproducibility.
data_signals: hunt engagement records (start timestamp, end timestamp, operator)
documented hunt hypothesis and hunt scope artifacts
telemetry source inventory and coverage percentage (per hunt: logs, endpoints, network, cloud telemetry)
detection alerts or findings generated by a hunt (with severity tags)
validated IOC / indicator lists produced from hunts
investigation/escalation ticket timestamps linked to hunt findings
automation/runbook executions used during hunt (script/query run logs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped hunt records and telemetry ingestion logs showing consistent coverage; documented hypotheses, queries, and validated findings with linked escalation tickets.
Weak: ad-hoc undocumented hunts, one-off screenshots, or summary emails without timestamps, telemetry coverage data, or linked investigation tickets.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Threat Hunting' describes a proactive detection activity focused on discovering threats (detective classification).

RA-10.differentiation_note: Unlike vulnerability-scanning or incident response controls, RA-10 requires measurement of proactive hunt cadence, hunt artifacts/hypotheses, and telemetry coverage — not just scan counts or response SLAs; note: control description was not provided so keywords and signals are inferred from the control name.

SA-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required security and privacy policies and procedures that are current, formally approved, have a documented owner and next-review date, and have evidence of dissemination/acknowledgement; and count outstanding policy exceptions and overdue reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SA-1 is a preventive governance control focused on coverage and currency of policy artifacts; percentages (coverage/currentness) are most useful to show compliance rates, with counts for outstanding items and trends to show review cadence over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of mandatory policy/procedure documents in the central repository that have: an approved version (approval record and timestamp), a current effective_date and next_review_date within policy-defined intervals, a documented owner, and at least one dissemination or acknowledgement record. Also measure count of policies overdue for review and count of open/approved exceptions.
data_signals: policy document metadata (title, version, effective_date, next_review_date, owner)
approval records (approver ID, approval timestamp, approval artifact)
policy change_log entries (change author, change timestamp, summary)
distribution/delivery logs and employee policy acknowledgement receipts (user ID, timestamp)
policy review meeting minutes or review completion records (reviewer, date, outcome)
policy exception register entries (exception ID, approver, expiration, status)
mapping of policy to applicable systems/processes (policy-to-control matrix)
policy repository access and modification audit trails
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized policy repository with structured metadata (effective_date, next_review_date, owner), signed approval records, automated acknowledgement receipts tied to user IDs, and an exceptions register.
Weak: scattered documents without metadata, undated or unsigned PDFs, or only periodic manual attestations without timestamps or distribution evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Policy and Procedures' implies establishing and maintaining governance artifacts to prevent nonconforming behavior, so it is classified preventive.

SA-1.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on governance-level documentation lifecycle and dissemination metrics (approval, review cadence, acknowledgements) rather than operational or technical control performance; measurement needs center on document metadata and distribution evidence.

SA-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required security controls, projects, and program activities that have documented, approved resource allocations (budget and assigned staffing) and the count and dollar value of security resources funded vs. planned.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about coverage and sufficiency of resources; auditors need percentage coverage (controls/projects with approved funding) plus counts of funded items and trends in funding over time to show sustained allocation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of mandated security controls and active security projects that have an approved budget line and an assigned resource owner/FTE; count and total dollar value of approved but unfunded controls/projects; trend in security staffing (FTE) and budget allocated vs. planned each fiscal period.
data_signals: approved security budget line items with amounts and fiscal year
signed budget approval documents or board/management approvals referencing security spending
resource allocation matrix mapping controls/projects to assigned owner and FTE/hours
staffing roster with security roles, headcount and FTE allocations
purchase orders and procurement records for security tools and services
project charters or business cases showing budget, start/end dates and assigned resources
monthly/quarterly financial reports showing security spend vs. allocated budget
risk treatment plans or control implementation plans referencing allocated funding
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: documented, approved budget line items and signed allocation records tied to specific controls/projects plus an up-to-date resource allocation matrix and continuous financial reporting
Weak: informal email commitments, verbal assurances, or undated estimates not tied to approved budgets or control assignments
Strong: measurable FTE allocations and purchase orders demonstrate actionable resourcing; Weak: absence of procurement records or only high-level program budgets without control-level mapping

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Allocation of Resources' indicates proactive provision of budget and staff to prevent gaps in control implementation (preventive intent).

SA-2.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls that measure detection or remediation, SA-2 requires measuring financial and staffing allocations (budget line items, FTE assignments, procurement records) rather than event or vulnerability counts.

SA-3.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of systems/projects that follow the documented System Development Life Cycle (SDLC) artifacts (requirements, design, test, deployment) and evidence that security activities are integrated across each phase (requirements traceability, code review, testing, release approvals).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SA-3 is a preventive/programmatic control — auditors want coverage/compliance percentages (percent of projects compliant with SDLC) along with counts of nonconforming projects and trends over time to show program adoption.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active projects/systems with completed SDLC artifacts (requirements doc, design review record, test plan, deployment/acceptance signoff) and percentage of projects with documented security tasks (threat model/code review/pen test) mapped to SDLC phases; include counts of exceptions or projects missing required artifacts.
data_signals: SDLC policy or standard version and effective date
project registry with SDLC phase flags per project
presence/absence of requirements documents, design reviews, test plans, release approval records in project artifact store
security artifact indicators: threat model files, code review records, penetration test reports
release pipeline approval logs (who approved, timestamp)
exception/waiver records for projects not following SDLC
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated project registry and CI/CD pipeline logs showing required approvals and artifact presence per release; weak: single-point-in-time manual attestations or ad-hoc spreadsheets without timestamps or links to artifacts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: SA-3 mandates lifecycle processes and integration of security activities (preventive program controls to ensure secure development).

SA-3.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall SDLC program and artifact coverage across projects — unlike the enhancements which target specific SDLC aspects (preproduction, use of live data, technology refresh).

SA-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of preproduction/test environments that are managed to baseline configuration and separation standards (inventoryed, access-controlled, and isolated from production) and count instances of production-to-preprod configuration drift or unauthorized access.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control focused on environment coverage and separation — primary metrics should be coverage percentages (proportion of preprod envs meeting management standards) with counts of nonconforming environments and distributions by environment type.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of preproduction environments that are inventoried, labeled as preprod, have documented separation from production, appropriate ACLs, and configuration baselines applied; counts of preprod environments using production credentials or live data copies without approvals.
data_signals: inventory of preproduction environments with environment type tag
network segmentation/configuration rules for preprod vs production
access control lists and recent access logs for preprod systems
configuration management baseline and drift reports for preprod
approved exceptions or waivers for preprod environments
instances of production credential usage or production network access from preprod
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CMDB + network segmentation and IAM enforcement logs showing continuous separation and denied unauthorized access; weak: manual lists or one-time checklists that do not show ongoing enforcement or drift detection.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly requires management of preproduction environments (separation/controls) which prevents unsafe use of test environments.

SA-3(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on preproduction environment separation, inventory and access/configuration controls rather than broad SDLC artifact coverage.

SA-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure the count and percentage of non-production environments and projects that contain live/operational/production data, and the percentage of those instances with required controls (data masking, approval records, documented justification).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about restricting and controlling use of live data in development/test — coverage percentages of masked/sanitized datasets and counts of unauthorized copies are most relevant, with trends showing reduction over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of non-production environments containing production data that have data protection controls applied (masking, encryption, approved justification), and counts of recent copies of operational data into non-production along with approval records and data sanitization artifacts.
data_signals: records of production data exports/copies to non-production (timestamps, source/destination)
data masking/sanitization job logs and status
data handling approval records or justification forms
lists of datasets flagged as 'operational' and their locations
exceptions/waivers permitting use of live data
access logs showing who accessed copied operational data
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated data export logs correlated with masking/sanitization run records and signed approvals; weak: developer statements or isolated spreadsheets without verifiable export/masking logs.

affinity_keywords: Use of Live or Operational Data

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement restricts use of live/operational data in development/test and requires controls (masking/approval), which are preventive measures.

SA-3(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures presence and protection of live/operational data in non-production environments — unlike SA-3(1) which focuses on environment separation broadly.

SA-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of assets and systems with an approved technology refresh plan or current lifecycle status (planned refresh date, vendor support status) and the count of systems past refresh/end-of-life without remediation plans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive/corporate governance control — primary coverage percentage of assets with current refresh plans or within supported life; counts of EOL systems and trends in remediation velocity are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems/assets with documented lifecycle status and scheduled refresh/upgrade dates, and counts of systems past vendor support or end-of-life without active remediation or replacement plans; include timestamps of last refresh activity.
data_signals: asset inventory with lifecycle status and planned refresh date
vendor support/EO L indicators for platforms and software
technology refresh schedules and approval records
patch/upgrade history and last upgrade timestamps
work orders or projects tied to refresh/replacement activities
exceptions/waivers for deferred refresh with business justification
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized asset lifecycle record tied to procurement/patching systems with scheduled tasks and completed refresh logs; weak: informal lists or undocumented refresh decisions without timestamps or approvals.

control_type_classification.reasoning: SA-3(3) requires planning and execution of technology refresh to prevent unsupported/obsolete technology, a preventive lifecycle control.

SA-3(3).differentiation_note: Targets asset lifecycle and refresh planning (EO L/support status and scheduled upgrades), distinct from SDLC artifact coverage and preproduction or data-use controls.

SA-4.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of acquisitions (contracts, procurements, and vendor-supplied products) that include required security requirements, vendor-provided security artefacts, and acceptance testing evidence; and count procured items without documented security controls or monitoring plans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (ensuring acquisitions include security requirements), so percentage (coverage/compliance rate) best shows whether procurement processes meet policy; counts and trends help show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of procurement actions that include required security clauses, required vendor security artefacts (functional properties, design docs, NIAP/IA evidence), and a signed acceptance that security requirements were validated prior to acceptance. Also provide counts of procured items missing any required artefact.
data_signals: procurement contract documents indicating included security clauses
procurement checklist fields populated (yes/no) for security artefacts
vendor security artefact uploads (design docs, functional properties, NIAP certificates)
acceptance test and evaluation records tied to procurement IDs
procured item inventory with security-artefact status
exemption or waiver records for missing artefacts
contractual SLAs or requirements referencing continuous monitoring plans
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage between procurement record and validated artefacts (signed design docs, NIAP certificate, acceptance test logs).
Moderate: vendor-provided attestations plus manual acceptance review notes.
Weak: email statements or unverified vendor marketing materials without acceptance test evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The acquisition process focuses on embedding security requirements and artefacts into procurement to prevent insecure products, per the control title 'Acquisition Process' and subordinate topics.

SA-4.differentiation_note: Top-level SA-4 covers overall procurement coverage and policy inclusion across all sub-areas; its measurement need is broad coverage (% of procurements meeting any acquisition security requirement) versus sub-controls that target specific artefacts or technical items.

SA-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of acquired systems/components for which vendor-supplied functional security properties (control behaviour, modes, dependencies) are documented and validated during acceptance testing.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is preventive (ensure required functional properties exist before acceptance), so coverage percentage shows compliance; counts and distribution by product type/criticality help prioritize remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of procured items with an attached, reviewed functional properties document (mapping controls to product functions) and the count of items where functional properties were tested and issues recorded.
data_signals: vendor functional properties/specification documents
security control mapping matrices linking controls to product functions
acceptance test results referencing functional properties
vendor attestations or evaluation reports confirming control functionality
open issues discovered during functional validation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed acceptance tests that reference functional property documents and pass criteria.
Moderate: vendor-supplied functional specs with internal reviewer notes but no test artifacts.
Weak: presence of a functional spec in procurement folder without review or test evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The sub-control requires that functional properties be provided/validated during acquisition to prevent procurement of components lacking required controls.

SA-4(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on vendor-supplied functional behaviour of controls and their validation at acceptance, unlike SA-4 which is broader procurement coverage.

SA-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure the share of acquisitions accompanied by design and implementation documentation that is reviewed for security (architecture diagrams, implementation notes) and the count of findings from secure design reviews prior to acceptance.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percentage of acquisitions with required design/implementation evidence demonstrates procurement-level security requirements; counts help track remediation items found in design review.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of procured systems/components with attached design and implementation artifacts that have completed a security design review, plus the count and severity of design review findings tied to procurement IDs.
data_signals: architecture and design documents submitted by vendor
secure design review checklists and outcome records
implementation diagrams and configuration notes
reviewer sign-off or exception records
design review findings and remediation status
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: completed security design review with signed evidence and remediated/accepted findings.
Moderate: checklist ticked but no detailed reviewer notes.
Weak: design docs present in file share with no review record.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires design and implementation information to be provided and reviewed before acceptance, which prevents insecure designs from being acquired.

SA-4(2).differentiation_note: Targets documentation and review of architecture/implementation specifically (vs. SA-4(1) which targets functional control properties).

SA-4(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of acquired software/products for which vendors provide evidence of secure development practices (SDLC artifacts, secure coding practices, vulnerability disclosure policies) and track the count of acquisitions lacking such evidence.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is preventive (ensuring secure development methods are required in acquisition) so percentage coverage is the most direct measure; counts and trend show volume and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of procured software or components that include SDLC evidence (secure development process description, build pipeline artefacts, SBOM, vulnerability disclosure policy) and counts of items without SDLC evidence.
data_signals: vendor SDLC policy or process documents
secure coding standard references
software bill of materials (SBOM) submissions
vulnerability disclosure and patching policies
third-party security assessment or penetration test reports
build/release pipeline evidence (signed builds)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: SBOMs, signed build artifacts, and third-party assessment reports linked to procurement ID.
Moderate: vendor SDLC policy and self-attestation with some supporting artifacts.
Weak: only marketing statements about secure development without artifacts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring development methods and practices from vendors is intended to prevent acquisition of software developed without security controls.

SA-4(3).differentiation_note: Specifically measures vendor development processes and artefacts (SDLC/SBOM/etc.) rather than design docs or functional control properties.

SA-4(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of procured components that are inventoried and assigned to specific systems (CMDB/asset management) at time of delivery, and identify unassigned components.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control (inventory/assignment) is best shown as coverage percentage of components assigned to systems; raw counts and comparisons to canonical inventory help identify gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of newly acquired components with CMDB entries that include 'assigned system' attribute populated and timestamped at acceptance, plus counts of components still unassigned after acceptance window.
data_signals: CMDB/asset inventory records with procurement ID and system-assignment field
acceptance/delivery records linking component to system
assignment timestamps (date/time assigned)
exceptions/waiver records
incorporation note: 'Withdrawn: incorporated into CM-8(9).' from description artifacts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CMDB entry created at provisioning with procurement linkage and assignment timestamp.
Moderate: manual asset spreadsheet updated within SLA with reviewer initials.
Weak: procurement invoices without system-assignment data or untracked assets.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates assignment of components to systems (inventory/CMDB), which prevents unmanaged components; classification is preventive though control is withdrawn and moved into CM-8(9).

SA-4(4).differentiation_note: Unique emphasis on asset assignment to systems (inventory/CMDB linkage) rather than documentation or secure properties; direct mapping of procured item to system is the measurement focus.

SA-4(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of acquisitions that include vendor-provided system/component/service configuration baselines or secure configuration guidance and the rate at which those baselines are validated before deployment.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control focused on configurations, a percentage coverage of acquisitions with secure configuration baselines is the clearest evidence; counts and trends show compliance volume and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of procured items that include a vendor-secure-configuration guide or baseline, and the count/rate of those baselines that pass pre-deployment configuration compliance scans.
data_signals: vendor secure configuration guides or baseline documents
pre-deployment configuration compliance scan results
hardening scripts or automation referenced in procurement
deviation/exception approvals for configuration differences
configuration review sign-off records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated pre-deployment compliance scan showing baseline applied and passing with documented deviations approved.
Moderate: manual configuration checklist and reviewer sign-off.
Weak: a vendor configuration guide present without validation evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires secure configurations to be provided and validated at acquisition/deployment time, intended to prevent insecure default configurations.

SA-4(5).differentiation_note: Concentrates on configuration artefacts and validation (baselines and compliance scans) rather than design or SDLC documents.

SA-4(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of acquisitions that mandate or include information assurance (IA) products and confirm the deployed configuration and integration of those IA products against vendor/assurance evidence.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: measuring coverage (percentage of acquisitions using IA products) shows adherence to procurement requirements; counts and comparisons to assurance evidence help validate conformity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of procured systems for which IA products are required/declared in procurement, and counts of deployments where IA products are installed/configured as per assurance documentation.
data_signals: procurement requirement fields indicating IA product use
list/inventory of IA products procured and deployed
IA product certification or evaluation documentation
deployment/configuration evidence showing IA products enabled
integration test results demonstrating IA functionality
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: deployed system configuration with IA product enabled plus certification evidence.
Moderate: procurement requirement present and vendor attestation but partial deployment evidence.
Weak: procurement mentions IA products but no deployment or config evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mandating IA products during acquisition prevents acceptance of systems lacking required assurance technologies, a preventive activity.

SA-4(6).differentiation_note: Specifically measures procurement and deployment of IA products and their assurance evidence, distinct from configuration baselines or SDLC requirements.

SA-4(7).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of procurements subject to NIAP protection profiles that include NIAP conformance evidence (certificates, evaluation reports) and track exceptions/waivers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percentage coverage of NIAP compliance among applicable procurements shows whether policy is applied; counts and comparisons track exceptions and nonconformant items.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of acquisitions that are in-scope for NIAP protection profiles and have NIAP conformance certificates or evaluation reports attached to procurement records; counts of waivers granted.
data_signals: procurement scope fields indicating NIAP applicability
NIAP conformance certificates or lab evaluation reports
vendor product NIAP evaluation documentation
procurement waiver or exception records
acceptance records referencing NIAP conformance
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: attached official NIAP certificate and acceptance sign-off.
Moderate: vendor-provided evaluation report without official certificate.
Weak: procurement text claiming NIAP compliance without supporting documentation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring NIAP-approved protection profiles and evidence during acquisition is intended to prevent nonconforming product procurement.

SA-4(7).differentiation_note: Narrowly focuses on NIAP protection profile conformance evidence and exceptions, unlike sibling controls which cover broader design, configuration, or development artefacts.

SA-4(8).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion (rate) of procured systems and services that include a documented continuous monitoring plan for controls and the percentage of those systems actively enrolled in monitoring tooling (alerts, telemetry).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective/ongoing monitoring control; rates (enrollment/monitoring event rates) best show operational monitoring coverage and activity; percentages show coverage and counts show scale.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate (proportion) of procured assets that have a continuous monitoring plan attached to procurement/contract and an active enrollment record in monitoring systems, plus counts of monitoring alerts generated attributable to those assets.
data_signals: continuous monitoring plan documents attached to procurement/contract
monitoring enrollment logs (asset enrolled timestamp)
control coverage mapping showing which controls are monitored per asset
monitoring tool alert/telemetry volumes for newly acquired assets
SLA clauses for monitoring and contract references
exceptions or gaps in monitoring coverage
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated monitoring enrollment and active telemetry from assets with plan referenced in contract and monitoring rules mapped to control IDs.
Moderate: monitoring plan exists and partial enrollment with intermittent telemetry.
Weak: monitoring plan text in procurement but no enrollment or telemetry evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires a continuous monitoring plan for controls in acquired systems, which is detective in nature (ongoing observation/detection).

SA-4(8).differentiation_note: Unique focus on operational monitoring plans and enrollment of procured assets into monitoring tools, rather than procurement-time documentation alone.

SA-4(9).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of procured systems for which functions, open ports, protocols, and services are documented and validated (pre-deployment port/service hardening), and count instances of undocumented/unauthorized services found in scans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires documentation and validation of allowed functions/ports/protocols/services; percentage coverage of documented/approved services is the primary indicator, with counts and distribution by criticality for prioritization.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of procured systems with an approved inventory of functions/ports/protocols/services and results of pre-deployment network/service scans showing no unauthorized services; and counts of discovered unauthorized ports/services per asset.
data_signals: documented list of allowed functions/ports/protocols/services attached to procurement
pre-deployment network/port/service scan results (Nmap/asset scanner)
service whitelist/blacklist records
configuration hardening checklists indicating disabled services
exceptions/approval records for allowed deviations
post-deployment scan follow-up and remediation records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: pre-deployment automated port/service scan matching approved inventory and remediation evidence for discrepancies.
Moderate: manual checklist and reviewer sign-off with spot scans.
Weak: procurement notes listing services but no scan or validation evidence.

affinity_keywords: Functions, Ports, Protocols, and Services in Use

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires documentation and validation of allowed functions/ports/protocols/services as part of acquisition to prevent deployment of unauthorized services.

SA-4(9).differentiation_note: Focused specifically on documenting and validating network/service-level elements (ports/protocols/services) for procured items, unlike other sub-controls which address documentation, SDLC, or monitoring.

SA-4(10).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of PIV-related acquisitions that are drawn from the organization's approved PIV products list and the count of acquisition events lacking documented approval; verify procurement/enforcement workflows block or escalate non-approved PIV product purchases.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive acquisition control (verify coverage/compliance), so percentage of acquisitions compliant with the approved PIV product list is most useful; counts and rates provide volume and exception trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of procurement/acquisition transactions for PIV-labeled products where the product identifier matches the organization's approved PIV products list, plus the raw count of non-conforming acquisitions and presence of approval workflow records for each acquisition.
data_signals: approved PIV products list (authoritative registry of approved product IDs/models)
acquisition/procurement transaction records referencing product IDs and timestamps
procurement approval workflow logs showing approval or rejection for PIV product purchases
inventory records of received PIV products matched to approved-list identifiers
exceptions/waiver records for non-approved PIV product use
product acceptance/certification or vendor attestation documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated procurement system enforcing the approved PIV products list with logged rejections/approvals and matching inventory receipts.
Weak: ad-hoc email approvals or occasional manual attestations without system linkage between purchase records and the approved list.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Acquisition Process | Use of Approved PIV Products' indicates a preventive control to ensure only approved PIV products are procured/used.

SA-4(10).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on procurement and enforcement against an 'approved PIV products' list (verification of acquisition compliance), unlike siblings that address record designation or ownership.

SA-4(11).measurement_goal: Measure the completeness of the authoritative 'system of records' inventory (percentage of systems storing regulated/authoritative records that are designated and documented) and count of systems lacking system-of-records designation or supporting documentation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive/governance-focused: auditors need coverage percentages (how many systems have authoritative system-of-records designation) and counts/trends to show remediation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems that hold organization records which have an assigned 'system of records' designation and supporting documentation (e.g., authoritative data schema, owner designation, policy mapping), and count of systems with unresolved designation gaps.
data_signals: system of records registry or inventory entries (system identifier, designation status)
system metadata showing record types stored and authoritative designation
data classification labels tied to system identifiers
system registration or onboarding artifacts documenting 'system of records' status
privacy/PII or records management assessments identifying authoritative systems
exceptions or waiver logs for systems not designated as authoritative
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized registry with machine-readable system-of-records flags and cross-referenced system IDs; mapped documentation (schema, owner) for each designated system.
Weak: separate spreadsheets or undocumented verbal assertions without traceable registry entries or system identifiers.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The label 'System of Records' implies establishing/designating authoritative systems — a preventive governance control to ensure records are assigned to known systems.

SA-4(11).differentiation_note: Unlike SA-4(10) which focuses on approved PIV product procurement, this control requires inventorying and designating authoritative record-holding systems; measurement centers on registry completeness rather than procurement compliance.

SA-4(12).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of data assets (or systems storing data) with an assigned, documented data owner/steward and track remediation velocity for unassigned assets until owner assignment is complete.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Data ownership is a preventive governance control; percentage coverage of assigned data owners is the primary indicator, with counts and trends to show progress and remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of data assets or systems with an assigned data owner/steward entry in the authoritative data asset registry, plus counts of unassigned assets and mean time to assign an owner after discovery.
data_signals: data asset registry entries with owner/steward fields populated
data classification records linked to assigned owners
data ownership assignment/change logs (timestamp, assignee)
data governance policy mapping showing owner responsibilities
open tasks or tickets for owner assignment remediation
contracts or role descriptions that designate ownership responsibilities
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: an authoritative data asset registry with owner IDs, timestamps for assignment, and automated linkage to classification and access controls.
Weak: isolated documents or informal lists of owners without timestamps or linkage to assets and no evidence of assignment workflow.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Data Ownership' indicates a preventive governance control to ensure data assets have designated owners/stewards responsible for records and decisions.

SA-4(12).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on assigning and tracking data owners/stewards for assets (coverage and timeliness of owner assignment), which is distinct from procurement compliance (SA-4(10)) and system designation (SA-4(11)).

SA-5.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of required system documentation: percent of systems with versioned architecture and design artifacts, presence of interface and source-code documentation, and count of documents overdue for review or lacking approval.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Documentation coverage and completeness are best expressed as percentages (percent of systems/components with required artifacts); counts of missing artifacts and trends of review activity are useful secondary measures to show remediation or degradation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each system, record presence/absence of required artifacts (architecture/high-level design, low-level design, interface specs, source-code documentation), document version and last-modified timestamp, approval/owner metadata, and scheduled review dates. Build metrics: percent of systems meeting documentation policy, count of missing artifacts, and mean time since last review.
data_signals: system inventory with links to documentation artifacts
artifact type presence flags (architecture, HLD, LLD, interface spec, source-code docs)
document version and last_modified timestamp from CM/repository
document owner and approval/approval timestamp metadata
document change history / commit logs in version control
scheduled_review_date and last_review_timestamp
missing_document_count by system
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: version-controlled artifacts with commit history, explicit owner, approval records, and recent review timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc files on shared drives without version history, missing approval metadata, and no scheduled review cadence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: System documentation is a preventive control that establishes design, configuration and operational expectations to prevent insecure or inconsistent implementations.

SA-5.differentiation_note: Covers overall documentation coverage and currency across all artifact types, whereas the sub-controls focus on specific artifact classes (functional properties, interfaces, HLD, LLD, source code).

SA-5(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of systems with documented functional properties of security controls (purpose, expected behavior, configuration settings and dependencies) and the count of controls lacking formal functional specifications or approvals.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Completeness of control functional documentation is a coverage problem best expressed as a percentage of controls/systems with required documentation; counts and trends help track remediation of gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each security control deployed in a system, record whether a functional specification exists, the specification's last_updated timestamp, configuration baseline references, and approval/owner metadata. Build metrics: percent of controls with functional specs, count of specs overdue for review, and time since last update.
data_signals: control functional specification documents or spreadsheets
control configuration baseline references
mapping of controls to system components
specification last_updated timestamp
owner and approval/approval timestamp metadata
control test plans or validation evidence
missing_functional_spec_count by control/system
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal control specifications stored in CM with owner and approval metadata and test/validation links.
Weak: informal descriptions in emails or undocumented tribal knowledge without versioning or approvals.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Documenting functional properties of controls prevents misconfiguration and misuse, a preventive activity; the control text emphasizes specification and properties.

SA-5(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on control-level functional specifications (behavior and configuration) rather than broader system design or source-code artifacts.

SA-5(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of external system interfaces documented with security-relevant information (authentication/authorization methods, data flow, agreed protections) and count of interfaces missing security specifications or third-party agreements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Interface security documentation is a coverage/completeness requirement (percent of interfaces properly documented); counts and trends track remediation of undocumented interfaces.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each external interface, record presence of an interface specification with security requirements, authentication method, data flow diagram, third-party agreement or SLA, last review timestamp, and test/validation evidence. Build metrics: percent of external interfaces with security specs, interface_count missing specs, and age since last review.
data_signals: external interface inventory (unique interface IDs)
interface security specification documents
data flow diagrams showing external boundaries
authentication/authorization method descriptions
third-party interface agreements / SLAs
interface testing / validation logs
interface_last_review_timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: inventory entries with linked signed third-party agreements, interface specs, and test evidence stored in CM.
Weak: undocumented or informally described integrations with no test evidence or agreement records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Documenting security-relevant external interfaces prevents insecure integrations and defines controls; the control name emphasizes interface-level security documentation.

SA-5(2).differentiation_note: Targets external interfaces and third-party integration documentation specifically, unlike sibling controls that address architecture or code-level documentation.

SA-5(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of systems with high-level design artifacts (architecture diagrams, component and trust-boundary descriptions) that are versioned and approved, and count systems lacking HLD artifacts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: High-level design coverage and approval status are coverage metrics best captured as percentages; counts of missing HLDs and trends show remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each system, capture presence of HLD artifacts (architecture diagrams, component lists, trust boundaries), artifact version, approval metadata, and date of last review; compute percent of systems with approved, current HLDs and count missing or stale artifacts.
data_signals: architecture diagram files linked to system IDs
component inventory and trust boundary descriptions
deployment and topology diagrams
HLD version and last_modified timestamp
approval/approver metadata
HLD_last_review_timestamp
stale_or_missing_HLD_count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: versioned architecture artifacts in CM with explicit approver records and recent reviews.
Weak: whiteboard snapshots, slide decks, or undocumented diagrams without versioning or approvals.

control_type_classification.reasoning: High-level design documentation defines architecture and trust boundaries to prevent insecure deployments; the name highlights design artifacts.

SA-5(3).differentiation_note: Specifically measures presence and currency of architecture-level artifacts (HLD) rather than detailed design, interface specs, or source code documentation.

SA-5(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of systems with low-level design (LLD) artifacts—detailed component/specification documents, sequence/interaction diagrams, and configuration baselines—that are linked to implementation and approved.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Completeness of LLD artifacts is a coverage metric (percent of systems with required LLDs); counts and trends of missing LLDs support remediation tracking.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each system component, record presence of LLD artifacts (interface definitions, sequence diagrams, configuration baselines), document version, traceability to requirements/code, and review/approval timestamps. Compute percent of components with compliant LLDs and count components missing LLDs or traceability.
data_signals: detailed design documents (LLD) linked to component IDs
interface definition files and sequence diagrams
configuration baseline definitions
traceability matrix mapping LLD to requirements/code
LLD version and last_review_timestamp
approval/approver metadata
missing_LLD_count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: traceability matrices and LLDs stored in CM with approvals, versioning, and links to build/artifact systems.
Weak: informal notes or undocumented component assumptions without traceability to implementation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Low-level design documentation prevents implementation errors by specifying detailed component behavior and configuration; description focuses on design/specification artifacts.

SA-5(4).differentiation_note: Targets granular design/specification artifacts and traceability to implementation, unlike HLD which is architecture-focused and SA-5(5) which is code-focused.

SA-5(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of code repositories with required documentation and developer artefacts (README/build/deploy instructions, inline comments, API docs), plus count of repositories missing owner or review history.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Source-code documentation coverage (percent of repositories meeting documentation standards) is best expressed as a percentage; counts of missing docs and trends in documentation updates are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each code repository, capture presence of README/build/deploy docs, inline comment density, API documentation, existence of contributor/owner metadata, pull request review and approval timestamps, and links between code and design artifacts. Compute percent of repositories compliant with documentation policy and average time since last documentation update.
data_signals: repository README and CONTRIBUTING files presence
inline comment density metrics (comments per KLOC)
build and deployment instructions stored in repo
API/docs generation artifacts (e.g., Javadoc, OpenAPI files)
code ownership metadata and repository maintainers list
pull request approvals and review timestamps
links from code to LLD/HLD artifacts
last_doc_update_timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: repositories with required docs, automated doc generation, PR-based reviews and approval records, and links to design artifacts.
Weak: repositories without README or maintainers, sparse inline comments, and no review history.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring source-code documentation and repository metadata is preventive because it reduces risk of insecure or unmaintainable code; the name explicitly targets source code artifacts.

SA-5(5).differentiation_note: Unique focus on code-repository artefacts, inline comments, and build/deploy documentation, rather than design documents or interface specifications.

SA-6.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of endpoints and systems that enforce the organization's approved-software policy (allowlist/blocklist) and count unauthorized software installations and approved-exception records to demonstrate restrictions are effective.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of systems enforcing the restriction) as primary evidence; counts and rates of unauthorized installs and comparisons to approved lists are useful secondary measures to show enforcement and exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: The ideal metric is the percentage of managed endpoints/systems that have enforcement enabled (allowlist/blocklist active) and the count of unauthorized software installation events and approved exceptions over a reporting period. Include mappings of installed software to the approved-software catalog (or CMDB) and timestamps of enforcement/installation events so remediation and exception handling can be validated.
data_signals: approved-software catalog / allowlist records
software inventory records per endpoint (installed package name, version, hash)
application allowlist/blocklist enforcement events (timestamp, endpoint id, result)
unauthorized software installation alerts (timestamp, user, endpoint)
change/CMDB records for software installation requests and approvals
exception/waiver approval records (approver, expiration date)
policy version and effective date for software usage restrictions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated enforcement logs plus up-to-date software inventory and CMDB mappings showing zero unauthorized software on managed endpoints.
Moderate: frequent (daily/weekly) inventory scans with follow-up remediation tickets and documented exceptions.
Weak: one-time manual inventories or legacy policy documents without enforcement logs or exception records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is labeled 'preventive' and the name 'Software Usage Restrictions' indicates controls that prevent unauthorized software through policy/enforcement.

SA-6.differentiation_note: Although SA-6 is withdrawn and incorporated into CM-10/SI-7, its unique measurement focus is on enforcement and coverage of approved-software restrictions (allowlist/blocklist and exception tracking) rather than broader configuration or patching metrics.

SA-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of endpoints enforcing policy to prevent user-installed software, count of unauthorized user-install attempts detected/blocked, and coverage of approved exception records against the total endpoint population.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of endpoints with install-restrictions enabled). Counts and rates of blocked/unauthorized install attempts and trends over time provide supporting context on control effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of managed endpoints with software-install restriction policies (MDM/GPO/agent) enabled; count and rate of user-initiated software installation attempts that were blocked or allowed; number and details of documented exceptions/approvals (ticket id, user, justification, expiry). Include policy configuration versions and endpoint mapping so coverage can be computed per asset.
data_signals: endpoint inventory with install-restriction flag (MDM/GPO/agent configuration)
installation attempt logs (user, username, endpoint ID, timestamp, process, source URL or installer hash)
block/deny events from endpoint protection or application control agents
application whitelist/allowlist and exceptions registry (ticket id, approver, expiry)
number of endpoints without install restriction enabled (unmanaged or misconfigured)
software installation approval records (change tickets, timestamps)
policy configuration/version records (which policy version applied to endpoint groups)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated enforcement logs (MDM/GPO + endpoint agent block events) are strong evidence
Up-to-date endpoint inventory mapping to policy versions strengthens evidence
Point-in-time manual inventories or occasional scans are weak evidence for ongoing preventative enforcement
High ratio of exceptions or repeated allowed installs weakens confidence in the control

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control (User-installed Software) is aimed at preventing user installation of software; description labels it withdrawn but explicitly marks it as preventive.

SA-7.differentiation_note: Unlike sibling controls that address configuration management broadly (CM-11) or system integrity (SI-7), this control specifically requires measurement of endpoint-level enforcement and exception tracking for user-installed software rather than general configuration baselines.

SA-8.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of system development projects that have documented and approved security and privacy engineering principles applied in design artifacts and the count of exceptions or deviations from those principles per review cycle.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage/adherence; auditors want percentages of projects covered and maturity scores, with counts of deviations and trends over time to show adoption.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of projects with an approved security & privacy engineering principles checklist attached to architecture/design artifacts, count of documented deviations/exceptions by project, and a principles-adherence score per project derived from review checklists.
data_signals: approved principles checklist attached to architecture/design artifacts
architecture/design review approval timestamps
project-level principles-adherence checklist scores
documented deviation/exception records referencing principles
number of projects lacking principles documentation
remediation/closure timestamps for exceptions
principles adoption trend by project cohort
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage between project records and an approved principles checklist plus time-stamped review approvals and closed exceptions; Weak: informal memos or one-off interviews without documented approvals

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing engineering principles (a preventive governance activity) to be applied during design and development.

SA-8.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall program-level adoption and enforcement of engineering principles (coverage and exceptions), whereas the SA-8(x) entries target measurements for specific individual principles.

SA-8(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of codebases or system components that have documented abstraction boundaries (interface specifications) and the count of abstraction-violation incidents detected in design and code reviews per release.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive requirement: auditors need coverage (% of components with clear abstractions) and counts of violations to detect where abstractions are not enforced.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of components with published interface/abstraction specifications and design-review acceptance, number of code review findings classified as 'abstraction boundary violations', and time-to-remediate those violations.
data_signals: interface/abstraction specification documents linked to component
design review acceptance/approval records
code review findings labeled 'abstraction violation'
automated static-analysis coupling/coupling-score metrics per module
remediation timestamps for abstraction violations
release packages missing abstraction docs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-accessible interface specifications and automated static-analysis showing low coupling with time-stamped review approvals; Weak: verbal sign-off or undated/design-whiteboard notes without linkage to artifacts

control_type_classification.reasoning: The principle 'Clear Abstractions' requires establishing and documenting boundaries up front (preventive design control).

SA-8(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on documented interface/abstraction boundaries and violations, unlike other enhancements which target different engineering properties (e.g., modularity or sharing).

SA-8(2).measurement_goal: Measure the count and percentage of components that reuse shared mechanisms for unrelated duties and the number of identified 'least-common-mechanism' violations flagged in architecture reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective counts of shared-mechanism instances and violations surface where a single mechanism is used across unrelated functions; percentage coverage shows how widespread the issue is.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of distinct mechanisms/services used by multiple unrelated subsystems, number of architecture review findings citing reuse as 'least common mechanism' risks, and percent of systems refactored to remove excessive sharing.
data_signals: inventory of mechanisms/services with consumer lists
architecture review findings tagged 'least common mechanism'
number of distinct subsystems sharing a mechanism
design/mitigation plans to segregate mechanisms
refactor/closure timestamps for mitigation tasks
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated service-consumer inventory showing a single mechanism with many unrelated consumers plus remediation tickets; Weak: anecdotal statements without an inventory of consumers

control_type_classification.reasoning: While primarily preventive as a design principle, evidence typically comes from detective artifacts (inventories and review findings) that identify over-shared mechanisms.

SA-8(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures over-sharing of mechanisms (service reuse across unrelated functions) rather than general sharing, requiring consumer inventories and architecture review findings.

SA-8(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems with documented modular design and layering (layer maps) and the count of incidents where layering boundaries were bypassed or violated during deployment or runtime.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: auditor needs percentage coverage of modular/layered designs and counts of layering violations to demonstrate enforcement effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems with published modularity/layering maps approved in design reviews, count of deployment/runtime events classified as 'layering boundary bypass', and remediation completion rate for boundary violations.
data_signals: modularity/layering diagrams attached to system design
design review approval records for layering
deployment/runtime logs showing cross-layer calls flagged as bypass
change requests that modify layer interfaces
closure timestamps for layering violation remediation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: mapped layering diagrams tied to monitored runtime enforcement logs and automated detection of cross-layer bypasses; Weak: unversioned diagrams without linkage to runtime controls

control_type_classification.reasoning: The principle requires designing systems with modular layers (a preventive architectural requirement) to limit exposure and coupling.

SA-8(3).differentiation_note: Targets measurement of modularity and enforcement of layer boundaries (both design documentation and runtime bypass detection), unlike other principles that focus on sharing or dependencies.

SA-8(4).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of components whose dependency graphs are documented and acyclic (or clearly ordered) and count the number of dependency cycles or unresolved ordering issues discovered in design scans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective metrics (counts of cycles and ordering issues) surface dependency-order problems; percentage of documented/ordered dependencies shows coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of dependency cycles detected by automated dependency scanners per system, percentage of components with documented dependency graphs and declared ordering, and time-to-resolve dependency-order issues.
data_signals: automated dependency graph exports per build
dependency-cycle detection reports
documented component dependency graphs
design review findings referencing ordering issues
remediation/change tickets for dependency fixes
build/failure logs caused by dependency order
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable dependency graphs and automated cycle detection integrated into CI with tickets for each issue; Weak: manually drawn graphs without automation or unresolved cycles

control_type_classification.reasoning: This principle is realized by detecting and documenting dependency orderings (dependency-cycle detection is a detective activity supporting preventive design).

SA-8(4).differentiation_note: Focuses on measuring dependency graphs and cycles (ordering constraints) rather than modularity or abstraction enforcement.

SA-8(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of access requests and access decisions that are mediated through approved centralized mediation mechanisms and the count of direct-access bypass incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: auditors expect coverage (% of access mediated) and counts of bypass incidents to show enforcement effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of access events routed through approved mediation services (e.g., policy enforcement points), count of recorded bypass or direct-access events, and time-to-close bypass incidents.
data_signals: access decision logs showing mediation service as decision point
number of access requests denied/allowed via mediation
audit records of direct-access bypass incidents
policy enforcement point (PEP/PDP) uptime and response logs
remediation tickets for bypass incidents
configuration records for mediation components
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous access logs demonstrating mediation for >X% of requests plus low direct-bypass counts; Weak: periodic manual attestations without log linkage

control_type_classification.reasoning: The principle mandates access be mediated by approved mechanisms (a preventive access-control design requirement).

SA-8(5).differentiation_note: Specifically measures mediation of access decisions (coverage and bypass counts) rather than broader access-policy or identity lifecycle controls.

SA-8(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of interfaces and services that only expose data/functions necessary for their role (least-sharing) and the count of reviewed and approved sharing exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: auditors need coverage (% of services following minimized sharing) plus counts of exceptions and trends showing reduction over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of APIs/services with documented least-sharing justification (data/function exposure rationale), count of sharing-exception approvals, and number of data-sharing events logged beyond intended scope.
data_signals: API/service exposure documentation and least-sharing rationale
formal approvals for sharing exceptions
access logs showing cross-service data flows
number of unapproved data-sharing events
remediation/change tickets for unnecessary exposures
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: API inventories with declared minimal exposure and automated flow logs confirming no unexpected sharing; Weak: undocumented assumptions about scope without flow logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The principle enforces limiting sharing at design time (a preventive design requirement to reduce exposure).

SA-8(6).differentiation_note: Targets data/function exposure minimization (API/service scope and exception handling) rather than mediation or dependency ordering.

SA-8(7).measurement_goal: Measure complexity metrics across systems (e.g., component coupling, cyclomatic complexity, configuration item counts) and track the trend in complexity-reduction actions and their closure rates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Complexity is best represented as distributions and trends (e.g., complexity score distributions across components) to show reduction efforts and hotspots.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Distribution of complexity metrics per component (coupling, cyclomatic complexity, number of configuration options), count of planned complexity-reduction tasks, and trend in mean complexity over time.
data_signals: static-analysis complexity metrics per module (cyclomatic complexity)
module coupling/interaction counts
configuration item counts per component
tickets for complexity-reduction activities
before/after complexity measurements tied to change tickets
design review findings referencing complexity
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated complexity metrics integrated into CI showing consistent downward trend after tracked remediation; Weak: single snapshot metrics or subjective developer claims

control_type_classification.reasoning: Reducing complexity is a preventive engineering requirement; measurement focuses on metrics and trends that show complexity reduction activities.

SA-8(7).differentiation_note: This entry emphasizes measurable complexity metrics and trends (distribution of complexity) rather than documentation or access/mediation specifics.

SA-8(8).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems that include documented evolvability (extensibility/upgrade) plans with security considerations and the count/time-to-complete of secure-change requests that implement evolvability features.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: coverage (% with evolvability plans) and trends in secure-change implementation indicate whether evolvability is being addressed securely.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems with documented secure-evolvability plans approved in design reviews, count of change requests implementing evolvability-related secure changes, and time-to-complete those secure changes.
data_signals: secure-evolvability / extensibility plan documents
design review approvals for evolvability
change requests/PRs labeled 'evolvability' with security checklist
remediation/implementation timestamps
post-change security test results tied to evolvability changes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: linked evolvability plans, approved design reviews, and change records with security test results; Weak: aspirational roadmap items without approved designs or test evidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: Secure evolvability is a preventive design principle requiring planning and controls for future changes.

SA-8(8).differentiation_note: Measures planning and secure-change implementation for evolvability (design and change tickets) rather than static design properties like abstractions or layering.

SA-8(9).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical system components that are designated and validated as 'trusted components' (with attestation/evidence) and the count of components lacking trusted attestations in critical paths.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: auditors need coverage (% of critical components with trust attestations) while counts identify gaps; a trust score can summarize component assurance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical components with documented trust attestations (e.g., signed firmware/validated supply-chain declaration), count of critical-path components without attestations, and time-to-validate missing attestations.
data_signals: component inventory marked with 'trusted' status
attestation documents (supply-chain, firmware signature, vendor trust statements)
design review notes identifying trusted components
tickets for obtaining/validating attestations
runtime metadata indicating component trust level
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed attestations and automated inventory tags tied to runtime metadata; Weak: informal vendor assurances without signed attestations or inventory linkage

control_type_classification.reasoning: Designating and validating trusted components is preventive, ensuring only verified components are used in critical systems.

SA-8(9).differentiation_note: Focuses on component-level trust attestations and inventory coverage, unlike sibling controls which address architecture, sharing, or complexity.

SA-8(10).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of system components and principals that are assigned and enforce a defined trust hierarchy (e.g., root/parent/child trust levels) and the count of violations where lower-trust principals exercised higher-trust operations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This engineering principle is preventive — auditors need coverage/percent compliance of components conforming to the trust hierarchy (percentage) plus counts of hierarchy violations and trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of components/principals mapped to a documented trust level and enforcing policies, and count of events where operations violated trust boundaries (e.g., lower-tier principal performing higher-tier action). Include timestamps, principal IDs, component IDs, and policy mapping.
data_signals: trust-level mapping registry (principal_id -> trust_level)
component configuration records showing enforced trust level
access/operation logs with principal_id, operation, target_component, timestamp
policy enforcement decisions (allow/deny) with reason codes
violation/event counts where action's required trust_level > principal trust_level
change history of trust-level assignments
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated trust-level enforcement logs and an authoritative trust mapping registry correlated with operation logs.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or one-off attestations without logged enforcement events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name describes an engineering principle to enforce a trust hierarchy, which prevents misuse by restricting privileges according to trust levels.

SA-8(10).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on presence and enforcement of a trust hierarchy (mapping and enforcement events) rather than general access or privilege counts.

SA-8(11).measurement_goal: Measure adherence to an inverse-modification threshold policy by counting unauthorized modification attempts against components whose modification must be restricted and tracking the percentage of components protected by tamper-resistance controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This principle implies monitoring modification attempts (detective counts) and coverage of tamper controls (percentage) to evidence enforcement and incidents over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of modification/patch/change attempts flagged as exceeding inverse-modification thresholds (e.g., larger-than-allowed changes or changes to protected components), and percent of protected components with tamper-detection controls enabled. Include timestamps, change request IDs, component IDs, change diff size/impact, and enforcement decisions.
data_signals: change request logs with size/impact metadata
configuration management records (protected_component flag)
tamper-detection or integrity-check logs (hash/checksum verification with timestamps)
unauthorized modification attempt alerts with principal_id and change diff
policy threshold definitions for modification limits
deployment/rollback records correlated with modification events
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated integrity checks and change-control logs showing blocked or flagged modification attempts and consistent tamper-detection coverage.
Weak: periodic manual inspections or undocumented change approvals without correlated logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The text implies controls that limit or block modifications (preventive enforcement and thresholds) to avoid unauthorized changes.

SA-8(11).differentiation_note: Emphasizes limits on modifications and tamper-resistance thresholds (counting modification attempts and integrity checks) rather than general access hierarchy or privilege modeling.

SA-8(12).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of system layers/components that are assigned protection levels in the defined protection hierarchy and the count of protection enforcement failures where protections were bypassed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Auditors need coverage metrics (percent of components assigned and enforcing protection levels) and counts of bypass/failure events to evidence hierarchical protection.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of infrastructure and application components mapped to a protection level and actively enforcing controls (e.g., network segmentation, host hardening), and count of incidents where protections were bypassed. Include component IDs, protection level assignment, enforcement configuration, and bypass incident logs.
data_signals: protection-level assignment registry (component_id -> protection_level)
network segmentation configuration records (VLANs, security groups) tied to protection levels
host hardening/configuration compliance reports
intrusion/bypass incident logs with evidence of protection failure
enforcement policy configuration snapshots
change records for protection-level mappings
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated configuration and segmentation inventories with enforcement logs showing successful denies and very low bypass counts.
Weak: manual diagrams or anecdotal evidence without enforcement logs or incident correlations.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires assigning protection levels and enforcing them across layers — a preventive engineering measure to reduce attack surface and enforce segmentation.

SA-8(12).differentiation_note: Unlike Hierarchical Trust (identity-focused), this entry measures enforcement of protection levels across infrastructure and network layers (segmentation, hardening, bypass incidents).

SA-8(13).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage reduction or proportion of system elements designated as security-critical (minimized security elements) and the count of security-critical elements that exceed the approved minimal footprint or complexity.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive principle is best evidenced by coverage/percentage (how many components adhere to minimization) plus counts/distributions of elements that remain large/complex and therefore risky.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of services/components designated as security-critical that conform to the documented minimal design (e.g., minimal codebase, minimal privileged modules), and counts of security elements exceeding approved complexity thresholds. Include component identifiers, complexity/size metrics (e.g., lines of code, attack surface measures), approval records, and hardening status.
data_signals: inventory of security-critical elements with minimal-design flag
codebase size metrics (LOC per component), dependency counts
attack-surface measurements (open ports, exposed APIs) per component
configuration/compliance snapshots showing disabled unused services
approval/whitelisting records for allowed security elements
vulnerability counts per component correlated with element size/complexity
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventories and code/attack-surface metrics showing decreasing distribution of large/complex security elements and documented approvals for exceptions.
Weak: manual lists of security elements or one-time refactor reports without continuous measurement of complexity and exposure.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The principle directs minimizing security-critical elements to reduce risk, which is preventive by design and measured via coverage and size/complexity metrics.

SA-8(13).differentiation_note: Targets reduction/minimization of security-critical elements and measurable attack-surface/complexity metrics, not trust hierarchies or privilege assignments.

SA-8(14).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of user and service accounts that conform to least-privilege role definitions, the count of privilege escalations, and the proportion of privileged accounts with just-in-time or time-bound privileges.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Least privilege is preventive; primary percent coverage of accounts meeting least-privilege policies is most useful, with counts of escalations and trending remediation velocity as secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of accounts and service principals whose assigned privileges match documented role definitions (role -> allowed actions) and the count/timestamps of privilege escalation events and exceptions. Include account_id, role_id, permissions list, assignment timestamp, review/attestation timestamps, and JIT/time-bound privilege records.
data_signals: account entitlement lists (account_id -> permission set)
role definition registry (role_id -> allowed actions)
access review completion timestamps and attestations
privilege escalation logs with request/approval metadata
just-in-time (JIT) privilege issuance records with expiration
privileged account inventory and last-used timestamps
policy exception records for elevated privileges
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated entitlements, role reconciliation, and JIT issuance logs showing time-bound privileges and low escalation counts.
Weak: manual spreadsheets of permissions or infrequent attestations without correlated enforcement logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Least privilege is a classic preventive control — it limits rights granted to principals and requires enforcement and attestation.

SA-8(14).differentiation_note: Specifically measures entitlement conformity and time-bound privilege practices (JIT), unlike other principles that focus on trust hierarchies or structural minimization.
