SA-8(15).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of permission grants that are conditioned on defined predicates (contextual attributes) and the count of permission grants that occurred without evaluating required predicates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Predicate-based permissions are about coverage of conditional checks (percentage) and counts of bypasses or unconditional grants (count) to prove enforcement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of access grants evaluated against required predicates (e.g., time-of-day, location, device posture) and count of grants where predicate evaluation was missing or failed. Include grant_id, principal_id, predicate evaluated (name/value), evaluation result, timestamp, and decision outcome.
data_signals: access decision logs with predicate evaluation details (predicate_name, input_values, result)
policy store defining predicates and required conditions per resource
access grant records with flags indicating predicate evaluation performed
exceptions/override logs where predicates were bypassed
device posture/origin attributes used in predicate evaluation (IP, device ID, posture score)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: real-time access decision logs showing predicate inputs and outcomes for all grants and low counts of overrides.
Weak: policy documents without decision logs or sporadic manual approvals lacking predicate evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Predicate permission requires access decisions to be conditioned on predicates (preventive enforcement of contextual constraints).

SA-8(15).differentiation_note: Focuses on conditional (predicate) evaluation recorded in access decision logs, rather than static role assignments or minimized elements.

SA-8(16).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of components that can self-assess and report their trustworthiness posture (integrity, configuration compliance, attestation) and the count of components failing self-attestation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Self-reliant trustworthiness is best summarized by a trustworthiness score per component and percent of components achieving required attestation levels; scores capture multi-dimensional posture.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-component trustworthiness score (composed of integrity verification, configuration compliance, vulnerability status, and attestation recency), percent of components meeting minimum trustworthiness threshold, and counts of failed self-attestations. Include component_id, attestation timestamp, integrity check results, configuration compliance results, and vulnerability snapshot.
data_signals: self-attestation reports with component_id and timestamp
integrity verification results (hash/TPM attestation)
configuration compliance scans per component
vulnerability status/patch level per component
trustworthiness scoring formula or policy threshold
failed attestation/alert logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated machine-attested reports (TPM/secure boot attestations) linked to component inventory and continuous compliance scans.
Weak: occasional manual attestations or self-reports without cryptographic integrity evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The principle requires components to self-assess and attest posture to prevent reliance on unverified components, a preventive design element.

SA-8(16).differentiation_note: Unique in requiring self-attestation and a composite trustworthiness score per component rather than access controls or predicate evaluations.

SA-8(17).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of composed/distributed systems whose component interactions satisfy defined security contracts (authentication, authorization, data handling) and count integration violations where interactions break contract guarantees.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: For distributed composition, percent of integrations complying with security contracts is primary (coverage), with counts of contract violations and distribution of violation types as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of service-to-service interactions that validate required security contracts (mutual auth, data encryption, schema validation) and count of interaction/contract violations (e.g., unauthenticated calls, plaintext transfers). Include service IDs, contract ID, interaction logs, authentication method, and payload handling details.
data_signals: service interaction logs with source_service, dest_service, auth method, encryption status
API gatekeeper/contract enforcement logs (accept/reject with reason)
service-level security contract registry (requirements per interaction)
integration test results validating contract compliance
incident logs for contract violations (unauthenticated requests, policy bypass)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated runtime enforcement logs and API gateway rejections mapped to a contract registry with low violation counts.
Weak: manual integration test reports without runtime enforcement or correlation to live interaction logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control asks for secure composition of distributed components via contracts and enforcement, which prevents insecure interactions.

SA-8(17).differentiation_note: Measures service-to-service contract compliance and runtime enforcement for distributed systems, unlike single-component trust or privilege controls.

SA-8(18).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of in-scope communication channels that use approved trusted channel protections (e.g., TLS with required cipher suites, mutual authentication) and count occurrences of untrusted/plaintext communications.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Trusted communication is evidenced by percent coverage of channels meeting crypto/protection standards and counts of plaintext/untrusted sessions; percentage shows coverage while counts show incidents.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of endpoints and channels (service endpoints, APIs, inter-host links) configured with approved protection (e.g., TLS 1.2+/approved ciphers, mutual TLS) and counts of observed unencrypted or downgraded sessions. Include endpoint IDs, TLS versions/cipher details, session logs, and certificate validation outcomes.
data_signals: TLS/SSL configuration inventory per endpoint (protocol versions, cipher suites)
network flow/session logs indicating encryption status and negotiated protocol
certificate validation logs (expiry, chain validation, revocation status)
API/endpoint configuration snapshots showing required transport protections
detected plaintext/unencrypted session logs
failed mutual-authentication attempt logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous network/session logs showing encrypted sessions with approved ciphers and automated configuration scans reporting full coverage.
Weak: attestations that TLS is required without observed session-level evidence or occasional manual checks.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Trusted channel requirements are preventive, mandating cryptographic protections to prevent eavesdropping and tampering.

SA-8(18).differentiation_note: Specifically measures transport-level protections (TLS/certificates, encrypted sessions) rather than higher-level access or composition contracts.

SA-8(19).measurement_goal: Measure the rate of protective actions/events (e.g., blocked attacks, automated policy enforcements) per unit time and the trend of protection coverage (percent of systems under continuous protection) to demonstrate ongoing, automated defense.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Continuous protection implies ongoing detection and automated enforcement; rates of protective events and trends in coverage over time best show continuous operation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate of automated protective events (blocks, quarantines, preventive policy enforcements) per hour/day, percent of assets covered by continuous protection mechanisms (EDR, WAF, IPS), and trend of those rates over time. Include event timestamp, asset_id, protective action type, and coverage inventory.
data_signals: protective action logs (block/quarantine/enforce) with timestamps and asset IDs
EDR/WAF/IPS coverage inventory (asset_id -> protection_enabled flag)
automation playbook execution logs for automated responses
false positive/false negative annotations for protection events
incident escalation logs showing automated vs manual responses
coverage change history (assets added/removed from protection)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: high-fidelity, continuous protective action logs correlated with broad automated coverage and measurable decreases in successful incidents.
Weak: periodic manual protection checks or one-off blocking actions without evidence of continuous automation and coverage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Although framed as an engineering principle, 'Continuous Protection' implies ongoing detection and automated protective actions (detective/preventive hybrid); classified as detective because measurement focuses on event rates.

SA-8(19).differentiation_note: Unique requirement for continuous, automated protective enforcement and runtime event rates rather than static design attributes or single-point policies.

SA-8(20).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and integrity of metadata applied to data assets: percentage of assets with required metadata fields present and the count of metadata modification events that lack proper authorization or integrity protection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive (ensuring metadata is present and protected); coverage expressed as a percentage (assets compliant with required metadata) is most meaningful, with counts of unauthorized changes and trends for monitoring drift.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of data assets (by dataset or object ID) that include the required metadata schema fields (e.g., owner, classification, retention) and count of metadata change events where change authorizers are missing or integrity checks fail.
data_signals: data asset inventory records with metadata schema completeness flags
list of required metadata fields per asset type (schema)
timestamps of metadata creation and modification
metadata change event logs with user identity and authorization token
metadata integrity checksums or signatures and verification results
counts of assets missing required metadata fields
alerts for unauthorized metadata modifications
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated inventory showing 100% schema-complete assets with signed metadata is strong evidence; periodic manual spreadsheets or spot-check screenshots are weak.
Continuous metadata change logs with signer/authorizer metadata are strong; absent or manually compiled lists are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name emphasizes 'Secure Metadata Management' which aims to ensure metadata is present and protected (preventive).

SA-8(20).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on metadata coverage and integrity for data assets, unlike sibling controls that address analysis, defaults, or recovery.

SA-8(21).measurement_goal: Count the frequency and findings of automated self-analysis/self-assessment runs and measure the rate of identified issues remediated within defined timeframes (e.g., findings per scan and closure rate).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Self-analysis is detective in nature; counts of analysis executions and findings (and closure counts/rates) are the clearest evidence of operation and effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of automated self-analysis runs per system/component per period, number of findings produced per run categorized by severity, and remediation/closure count and time-to-close for findings originating from self-analysis.
data_signals: self-analysis execution logs with timestamps and system identifiers
reported findings/issues from each self-analysis run with severity labels
ticketing/remediation records linked to self-analysis findings
timestamps of finding creation and remediation closure
policy indicating expected self-analysis cadence
counts of missed or failed self-analysis executions
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated self-analysis logs with structured findings and linked remediation tickets are strong; one-off manual assessment reports are weaker.
Consistent, frequent runs with decreasing unresolved findings indicate effective control; sporadic runs or undocumented results weaken evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Self-analysis' implies ongoing assessment/detection of issues (detective).

SA-8(21).differentiation_note: Emphasizes automated/self-driven assessments and their findings/closure rates, unlike siblings focused on metadata, defaults, or documentation.

SA-8(22).measurement_goal: Measure audit coverage and traceability: percentage of critical events with immutable audit trails and the count of audit entries that include principal identity, action, object, and timestamp.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Accountability and traceability are detective and rely on counts of audit events and coverage; counts of complete audit records and percentage coverage show both volume and completeness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of critical actions (by system/component) that have associated audit records containing actor identity, action performed, target object, timestamp, and integrity proof; percentage of systems with tamper-evident audit logs enabled.
data_signals: audit log entries with fields: user/principal, action, object/resource ID, timestamp
log integrity markers (signatures/checksums) and verification results
list of systems/services designated as critical requiring traceability
configuration flags showing audit logging enabled/disabled per system
counts of missing or incomplete audit records for critical events
access and export logs for audit stores
evidence_strength_indicators: WORM or cryptographically-signed audit logs with complete fields for each critical event are strong evidence; ad-hoc or manually retained logs without integrity protection are weak.
High continuous coverage of enabled logging across critical systems is strong; intermittent or sample logs are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Accountability and Traceability' indicates requirements for logging/audit trails to detect and reconstruct actions (detective).

SA-8(22).differentiation_note: Specifically targets audit log completeness and tamper-evidence for traceability, rather than metadata, defaults, or human factors.

SA-8(23).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of deployed systems and components using organization-approved secure default configurations and the count of deviations from default-hardening baselines.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Secure defaults are preventive; percentage coverage of systems configured to approved defaults is the clearest measure, with counts of deviations for prioritization.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of instances/services that match the approved secure baseline configuration (by baseline ID) and count of configuration deviations per baseline with time-since-deviation.
data_signals: approved secure baseline configuration definitions/catalog
configuration scan results per host/service with baseline match scores
count of systems flagged as non-conforming to secure defaults
change history for configuration deviations and approvals
patch/configuration management system records showing baseline applied
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated configuration-scanning reports showing baseline conformity across inventory are strong; single manual configuration snapshots are weak.
Low and decreasing non-conformance counts indicate effective secure defaults; persistent deviations indicate weak implementation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Secure Defaults' implies setting preventive baseline configurations to reduce risk before deployment.

SA-8(23).differentiation_note: Measures baseline conformity for configurations (secure defaults) rather than runtime detection or recovery processes.

SA-8(24).measurement_goal: Measure resilience and recovery effectiveness: mean time to recovery (MTTR) for security-related failures, count of failover activations, and percentage of systems with approved secure-failure modes implemented.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control involves corrective capabilities; response and recovery velocity metrics (rates such as MTTR and failover activation rates) best demonstrate effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: MTTR for security incidents and system failures, count of automatic failover or graceful-failure events per system, and percentage of critical systems configured with secure failure/recovery modes as defined in policy.
data_signals: incident tickets with start and recovery timestamps
failover/rollback activation logs with reason codes
system configuration flags indicating failure-mode behavior
runbooks/playbooks executed during recovery (timestamps and operators)
tests of failover/recovery with pass/fail results
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated recovery logs and tested runbooks with measured MTTR are strong; anecdotal or untested recovery plans are weak.
Regular disaster/failover test results with documented outcomes strengthen evidence; only theoretical documentation weakens it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Secure Failure and Recovery' emphasizes recovery from failures, a corrective activity measuring recovery effectiveness.

SA-8(24).differentiation_note: Targets recovery/resilience metrics (MTTR, failover counts) rather than preventative defaults or metadata management.

SA-8(25).measurement_goal: Measure that security design decisions consider economic trade-offs: percentage of design decisions with documented cost-benefit or risk-cost analysis and a score of economic-risk alignment for major projects.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Economic security requires qualitative scoring (risk-cost alignment) and percentage coverage of documented analyses; a score conveys adequacy while percentage shows coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of system design decisions or change requests that include a documented economic/risk-cost analysis, plus an aggregated economic-security score per project derived from documented inputs (cost, residual risk, expected loss).
data_signals: design/architecture documentation with attached economic analysis
change request records with cost-benefit/risk-cost attachments
project approval checklists indicating economic-security review completion
aggregated project 'economic-security' score or rating
percentage of projects passing economic-security gate
evidence_strength_indicators: Structured, stored economic analyses attached to each approved design or change request are strong; informal or undocumented decision notes are weak.
A scored registry of projects with economic-security ratings is strong evidence of systematic application; ad-hoc board discussions without records are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The focus on 'Economic Security' implies preventive governance to ensure decisions consider economic trade-offs before implementation.

SA-8(25).differentiation_note: Unique focus on documented economic/risk-cost analysis for design decisions, rather than technical configuration or logging concerns.

SA-8(26).measurement_goal: Measure that security measures do not unacceptably degrade performance: percentage of security controls with defined performance impact tests and trend of measured latency/throughput impact attributable to security controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Ensuring secure design meets performance expectations is preventive; percentage of controls with validated performance tests and trends of performance impact are appropriate measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of security controls or middleware components that have documented and executed performance-impact test results (latency, throughput), and time-series of measured performance metrics before/after security control changes.
data_signals: performance test results linked to security control implementations (latency/throughput numbers)
test plan artifacts indicating performance acceptance criteria
change records showing security control deployment timestamps
time-series telemetry (response time, CPU, memory) correlated with security control states
counts of incidents escalated due to performance regressions after security changes
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated telemetry correlating performance metrics to security-control state with repeated test runs is strong; one-off manual tests without baseline are weak.
Demonstrated regression testing and acceptance criteria enforcement strengthens evidence; undocumented or anecdotal performance claims are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Performance Security' suggests preventive validation that security controls meet performance requirements before deployment.

SA-8(26).differentiation_note: Targets measurable performance impact of security controls (latency/throughput tests) rather than operational logging or recovery capabilities.

SA-8(27).measurement_goal: Measure adoption of human-centered security practices: percentage of user-facing controls or interfaces that have undergone human-factors review/usability testing and count of user-reported security-usability issues per release.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Human-factored security is preventive: percentage coverage of interfaces with human factors review shows preventive integration; counts of usability-related security issues indicate residual risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of user-facing components with documented human-factors/usability security reviews and the count and trend of security-usability issues reported by users or during usability tests per release.
data_signals: human-factors/usability review reports linked to features
test results from user studies evaluating security flows
bug/ticket records for security-usability issues with severity and component tags
release notes indicating mitigation of usability-related security problems
training completion records for designers on human-factored security
evidence_strength_indicators: Structured usability study artifacts and tickets closed against identified issues are strong; anecdotal feedback or undocumented fixes are weak.
High percentage of features with human-factors reviews and declining user-reported issues indicate strong evidence; opposing trends weaken it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Human Factored Security' implies designing systems to prevent user errors and increase secure behavior (preventive).

SA-8(27).differentiation_note: Specifically measures human-factors/usability review coverage and user-reported security-usability issues, unlike technical-focused siblings.

SA-8(28).measurement_goal: Measure whether systems meet defined 'acceptable' security baselines: percentage of projects and components assessed against the acceptable-security criteria and an aggregated acceptability score across critical assets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Determining 'acceptable security' is often a scored assessment combining multiple criteria; a score plus percent compliance against acceptability gates is appropriate.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Aggregated acceptability score per project/component derived from checklist outcomes (vulnerability counts, config compliance, testing results) and percentage of critical assets that meet the organization's acceptable-security criteria.
data_signals: acceptable-security criteria/checklist definitions
assessment reports with pass/fail entries for each criterion
aggregated acceptability scores per asset/project
vulnerability counts and severity tied to acceptability assessments
percentage of assets achieving 'acceptable' status
evidence_strength_indicators: Formal scored assessments stored in a governance system that show pass/fail per criterion are strong; informal sign-offs without evidence are weak.
Consistent documentation linking assessment outcomes to remediation actions strengthens evidence; lack of traceability weakens it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement to define and meet 'Acceptable Security' implies preventive governance and assessment before or during deployment.

SA-8(28).differentiation_note: Focuses on scored acceptability against policy criteria (pass/fail and scores) rather than operational logs or recovery measures.

SA-8(29).measurement_goal: Measure procedure coverage and testability: percentage of critical security processes with documented, versioned procedures and count of procedures that have been tested/validated in the past 12 months.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Documented, repeatable procedures are preventive; percentage coverage of critical processes with documented and versioned procedures is most relevant, with counts of tested procedures as secondary evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of identified critical security processes that have current, versioned procedure documents and the count of those procedures with evidence of an executed test or tabletop in the last 12 months.
data_signals: catalog of critical security processes with procedure document links
procedure document metadata (version, author, last reviewed date)
test execution logs or tabletop exercise reports linked to procedures
change records referencing procedure updates
counts of procedures without versioning or without tests in policy window
evidence_strength_indicators: Centralized document repository with versioning and linked test execution artifacts is strong; scattered or unversioned documents are weak.
Recent, recurring tests with documented outcomes strengthen evidence; outdated or never-tested procedures are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title emphasizes documented and repeatable procedures, which are preventive controls to ensure consistent secure operations.

SA-8(29).differentiation_note: Measures documentation coverage and testing of procedures (versioning and exercise evidence), unlike siblings focused on metadata, usability, or recovery.

SA-8(30).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of engineering/design/change activities that followed approved security & privacy procedures (policy versioned), the count of documented procedural exceptions, and time-to-resolution for those exceptions to demonstrate procedural rigor.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Procedural rigor is about coverage/compliance with defined procedures (best measured as percent complete or compliance score); counts of exceptions and trend show volume and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of engineering/design/change tickets that include a link to an approved procedure version, completed procedure checklists, and required approvals; count and resolution timestamps of exception/waiver tickets tied to those activities.
data_signals: engineering/design/change ticket IDs with attached procedure version
procedure checklist completion flags per ticket
procedure/policy version identifier and effective date
exception/waiver ticket count and resolution timestamps
approval events audit trail (approver ID, timestamp, decision)
procedure training/completion records for engineers
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated workflow records (tickets with procedure-version fields and approval audit trails) are strong evidence; manual PDFs or occasional ad-hoc approvals without audit trails are weak.
Low and decreasing exception counts plus short resolution times strengthen evidence; one-off documented exceptions without remediation weaken it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Procedural Rigor' implies enforcing procedures to prevent insecure or non‑compliant engineering practices.

SA-8(30).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on adherence to defined procedures and exception handling for engineering activities, unlike siblings that target modification controls, documentation, or data minimization.

SA-8(31).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of system modifications that completed required secure-change steps (documented risk assessment, approvals, pre-production test gating) prior to production deployment, plus counts of unauthorized or emergency modifications and their remediation timelines.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Secure modification is about coverage of required pre-deployment controls (best expressed as percent compliance); counts/rates capture unauthorized or emergency change occurrences and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of change/deploy tickets that have a recorded risk assessment, required approvals, test results/pipeline gating, and a production deployment timestamp tied to the change ticket; count of changes deployed without required approvals and time to remediate or rollback.
data_signals: change ticket IDs with risk assessment attached
approval records (approver ID, role, timestamp) linked to change
CI/CD pipeline/gate pass/fail records referencing change ticket
production deployment timestamps tied to ticket/commit hash
unauthorized/emergency change logs and rollback events
configuration drift alerts post-deployment (detected vs expected)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated linkage between change tickets, commits, pipeline results, and deployment events is strong evidence; isolated manual sign-off notes or post-hoc justifications are weak.
Low counts of unauthorized/emergency changes and rapid remediation/rollback strengthen evidence; frequent out-of-band changes weaken it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Secure System Modification' implies controls to prevent insecure changes by requiring pre-deployment security steps and approvals.

SA-8(31).differentiation_note: Specifically targets the change/change-control lifecycle and pre-deployment security gating, unlike siblings that emphasize procedural adherence, documentation completeness, or data reduction.

SA-8(32).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of system components and engineering artifacts that have required security & privacy documentation (design docs, interface/data flow, procedure) that is current as-of policy-defined review period, plus counts of missing or outdated documents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Documentation sufficiency is about completeness and currency (coverage percentage); counts and trends capture backlog and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of listed system components with an associated approved design/documentation artifact and a last-reviewed timestamp within the policy review interval; count of components lacking required documentation or with documentation older than policy-defined age.
data_signals: document inventory entries mapped to system/component IDs
document type/classification (design, interface, privacy) and version
document last-modified and last-reviewed timestamps
document approval/owner metadata (approver ID, role)
list of components with missing or stale documentation
document change history/audit trail
evidence_strength_indicators: A maintained document inventory with automated last-modified timestamps and approval metadata is strong evidence; scattered local files without versioning or owner attestations are weak.
High coverage with recent review dates strengthens evidence; many stale documents or unknown owners weaken it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring sufficient documentation prevents misconfiguration and supports secure design; the title emphasizes completeness and currency of artifacts.

SA-8(32).differentiation_note: Targets the completeness and currency of engineering artifacts (documentation inventory and review) rather than procedural adherence or change-control execution.

SA-8(33).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of collected/stored elements that have a documented business justification and retention requirement (and the percent flagged for minimization), plus counts of unnecessary or redundant data elements removed over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Minimization is about coverage/compliance with retention and collection policies (best shown as percentage of elements justified/retained); counts and trends show reduction progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of inventoryed data elements/fields with a documented purpose and retention rule, count of fields flagged and removed as unnecessary, and mean time to implement minimization actions after identification.
data_signals: data inventory entries with purpose/justification field
data element retention policy mapping and retention timestamps
classification tags (sensitivity/privacy) associated with elements
records of minimization/field removal actions (ticket ID, timestamp)
counts of systems/datastores holding flagged redundant elements
data access or collection request logs showing unnecessary collection
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated data inventory with justification and retention fields plus recorded removal actions is strong; ad-hoc verbal decisions or undocumented deletions are weak.
Sustained downward trend in redundant/retained-but-unjustified elements strengthens evidence; static inventories with many unjustified elements weaken it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'Minimization' implies preventing unnecessary collection/retention of information through policy and design choices, a preventive engineering control.

SA-8(33).differentiation_note: Concentrates on reducing privacy/security surface (data/element justification and retention) rather than procedural adherence, change gating, or documentation completeness.

SA-10.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of developer-produced artifacts (source repositories, build outputs, firmware images) that are managed under an approved configuration management process and the count of artifacts lacking traceable provenance or CM records.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive (ensuring artifacts are under CM). Percentage shows coverage/compliance; counts and trends show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of developer artifacts (by repo, package, or build ID) that have CM repository entries, provenance metadata, and change tickets; plus the count of untracked artifacts and time-to-onboard untracked artifacts into CM.
data_signals: configuration management repository records (repository IDs, artifact IDs)
build pipeline logs with artifact identifiers and timestamps
provenance/metadata entries for artifacts (creator, commit hash, build ID)
change request/ticket links referencing artifact IDs
untracked artifact count from artifact stores
time to add artifact to CM from discovery (onboarding timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated CM repository records with artifact IDs and linked change tickets are strong evidence; ad-hoc spreadsheets or one-off manual inventories are weak.
Continuous ingestion of build logs into CM with automated reconciliation is stronger than periodic spot checks.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control enforces management of developer artifacts under CM (prevention of unmanaged changes).

SA-10.differentiation_note: High-level coverage of developer artifacts under CM; other sibling controls focus specifically on integrity verification, hardware, distribution, or personnel roles.

SA-10(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of software and firmware artifacts that have cryptographic integrity verification (signed checksums or signatures) and the count/rate of integrity verification failures detected during build and pre-release scans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percentage demonstrates coverage of integrity verification; counts/rates show failures or verification events detected.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of produced software/firmware images with attached cryptographic signatures and successful verification records in build/release pipelines; count of artifacts with failed or missing verification attempts.
data_signals: artifact signature presence (signed/unsigned flag)
cryptographic checksum/hash records (SHA256, etc.)
signature verification logs from CI/CD
build/release pipeline verification step results (pass/fail)
list of unsigned or unverifiable firmware/software images
verification failure/error codes and timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated signature verification in CI/CD with stored pass/fail logs is strong evidence; manual post-hoc checks or occasional spot verifications are weak.
Presence of cryptographic signatures that can be independently validated is stronger than proprietary checksum fields only stored in a single system.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires artifacts to be integrity-verified (signed/hashed) prior to acceptance or distribution, preventing tampered releases.

SA-10(1).differentiation_note: Specifically targets cryptographic verification of software/firmware artifacts, unlike SA-10 which addresses general CM coverage.

SA-10(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of development projects using approved alternative CM processes with documented exceptions, and count of alternative-process deployments lacking required controls or approvals.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: percentage measures adherence to approved CM process coverage; counts capture exception volume and nonconforming projects.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of projects that either follow standard CM or have an approved alternative CM process documented; count of projects operating with undocumented or unapproved alternative processes and time-to-approval.
data_signals: inventory of projects with CM process type (standard vs. alternative)
approved exception or alternative process documentation records
approval timestamps and approver identities
projects flagged as using alternative CM without approval
remediation/onboarding timestamps for non-approved cases
evidence_strength_indicators: Documented approvals and machine-readable process mappings are strong evidence; informal email agreements or undocumented practices are weak.
Automated tagging of project CM process type in project management systems is stronger than manual checklists.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control prescribes use of approved CM processes or documented alternatives to prevent unmanaged divergence.

SA-10(2).differentiation_note: Focuses on approved deviations/alternatives to the standard CM process and requires measuring approvals and exceptions, unlike siblings focused on integrity or distribution.

SA-10(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of hardware components with recorded integrity verification (attestation reports, firmware signatures) and the count of hardware integrity verification failures during acceptance or provisioning.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: percentage indicates coverage of hardware attestation/verification; counts/rates capture integrity failures or anomalies detected.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of procured and provisioned hardware items that have integrity verification records (attestation, signed firmware) and the count/timestamped logs of failed hardware integrity checks during provisioning or boot.
data_signals: hardware attestation reports or TPM attestation logs
firmware signature verification logs for hardware components
asset inventory entries with integrity-verification status
provisioning logs showing integrity check pass/fail
list of hardware with missing attestation or unverifiable firmware
evidence_strength_indicators: Cryptographic attestation records tied to hardware serials are strong; vendor statements without verifiable attestation are weak.
Automated provisioning checks that block non-attested hardware are stronger evidence than post-deployment manual inventories.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires integrity verification for hardware components (attestation/signed firmware) to prevent compromised hardware entering the environment.

SA-10(3).differentiation_note: Targets physical hardware verification and attestation records rather than software/firmware artifacts or distribution processes.

SA-10(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of build/generation environments that are designated as 'trusted' (hardened, isolated, and auditable) and the count of artifacts produced outside trusted generation environments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: percentage demonstrates how many builds occur in trusted environments; counts capture nonconforming artifact production events.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of builds/releases executed in approved/trusted build environments (identified by environment ID) and the count of artifacts produced in non-trusted environments, with timestamps and responsible user accounts.
data_signals: CI/CD pipeline environment classification (trusted vs. non-trusted)
build execution logs with environment ID, user, and timestamps
artifact provenance linking artifact to build environment
exceptions/waiver records for non-trusted builds
counts of artifacts produced outside trusted environments
evidence_strength_indicators: Immutable, auditable build logs linking artifact to a hardened/trusted build host are strong; developer-local builds without recorded provenance are weak.
Automatic enforcement that prevents release from non-trusted environments is stronger than retrospective labeling.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mandates generation of artifacts in trusted/hardened environments to prevent introduction of untrusted code during build.

SA-10(4).differentiation_note: Unique focus on the trustworthiness of the build/generation environment and measuring where artifacts are produced.

SA-10(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of released artifacts that have an auditable and correct mapping to version control commits (commit hash → artifact mapping) and count of mismatches or unmapped releases.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive/detective: percentage shows coverage of artifact-to-commit mapping; counts show mismatches; comparisons validate mapping correctness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of artifacts in releases that include a recorded mapping to a specific version control commit hash and build tag; count of releases where artifact cannot be traced to VCS commits and list of mapping discrepancies.
data_signals: release metadata containing commit hash and artifact ID
version control tags/releases linked to build artifacts
artifact manifest entries mapping to commit hashes
records of mapping verification checks (pass/fail)
list of unmapped or mismatched artifact→commit pairs
evidence_strength_indicators: Immutable mapping records (release manifest with cryptographic commit hash) are strong evidence; manual notes or memory-based mappings are weak.
Automated reconciliation between VCS and artifact registries is stronger than sample-based verification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Ensures artifacts are traceable to VCS commits before release, preventing untraceable or altered releases.

SA-10(5).differentiation_note: Specifically requires artifact-to-commit mapping verification (commit hashes), unlike other siblings that address signing, distribution, or environment trust.

SA-10(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of distributed artifacts that are delivered through trusted/distributed channels with verified integrity (signed packages, vetted repositories) and the count/rate of distribution events that used untrusted channels.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: percentage shows distribution via trusted channels; counts and rates measure unauthorized/untrusted distribution events.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of artifact distribution events using approved/trusted channels (signed package repositories, controlled CDNs) and count of distribution events (pushes, releases) to unapproved or public endpoints, including timestamps and responsible principals.
data_signals: distribution event logs with destination endpoint and channel classification (trusted/untrusted)
package signing verification records at distribution time
repository/registry access control logs (push/publish events)
list of distribution endpoints approved in policy
exceptions or emergency distribution approvals
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated enforcement (blocked publishes to unapproved registries) and signed-distribution logs are strong; ad-hoc FTP/USB transfers or email attachments are weak.
End-to-end signed package verification by consumers is stronger than trust based only on source IPs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires using trusted distribution channels and verifying distribution integrity to prevent unauthorized dissemination of artifacts.

SA-10(6).differentiation_note: Measures the security of artifact distribution channels and verifies that artifacts are delivered only through approved/trusted pathways.

SA-10(7).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of development projects that include documented involvement (approval or review) from designated security and privacy representatives during configuration management processes and count instances where releases occurred without such involvement.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: percentage indicates coverage of required security/privacy involvement; counts capture lapses and enable trending of compliance improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of projects/releases with recorded security and privacy representative review/approval entries linked to CM records, and count of releases lacking documented security/privacy sign-off.
data_signals: approval/acknowledgement records from security/privacy representatives tied to change tickets
project onboarding records listing assigned security/privacy representatives
release/change logs indicating reviewer IDs and timestamps
exceptions/waivers where representative involvement was bypassed
counts of releases missing security/privacy review
evidence_strength_indicators: Signed, timestamped approvals recorded in CM/change systems are strong; verbal approvals or undocumented reviews are weak.
Automatic gating of releases until representative approval is recorded is stronger evidence than post-release attestations.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires inclusion of security/privacy representatives in CM processes to prevent releases without required reviews (preventive control).

SA-10(7).differentiation_note: Focuses on human governance and documented reviewer involvement in CM, measuring sign-offs and reviews rather than technical artifact properties.

SA-12.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of supply chain protections: percent of critical suppliers with required security clauses/assessments, count of supply-chain incidents detected, and trend in procurement-related security exceptions over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SA-12 is a preventive, programmatic control — auditors will look for coverage/percent compliance across suppliers (primary), supported by counts of incidents and trends in exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of in-scope suppliers that have required security contractual clauses, completed risk assessments, and active monitoring; number of supply-chain related incidents detected per quarter; time-to-detect or remediate supply-chain incidents where applicable.
data_signals: supplier registry with criticality classification
contracts with security clause boolean (present/absent) and effective_date
supplier risk assessment reports and timestamps
supply-chain incident tickets/alerts with timestamps and root cause
procurement exceptions/waivers log
continuous monitoring feed for supplier behavior (if available)
remediation/mitigation action records with closure timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated supplier registry joined with contract clause flags and ongoing monitoring alerts (continuous integration).
Weak: manual attestations or one-off spreadsheets without timestamps or traceable contract links.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Supply Chain Protection' indicates programmatic, preventive measures to reduce supply-chain risk and ensure coverage across suppliers.

SA-12.differentiation_note: This is the overall program-level supply chain protection control covering coverage and incident metrics, whereas its sub-controls target specific supplier lifecycle activities (acquisition, shipping, diversity, etc.).

SA-12(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of procurements executed using approved acquisition strategies and tools, and count of procurements that deviate from mandated methods over a reporting period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive acquisition control, coverage and adherence (percentage of procurements following approved strategies) is the primary evidence; counts and trends show exceptions and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of procurement actions using approved acquisition strategies/tools (by dollar value and count), list and count of procurements that used non-approved methods, and timestamps tying procurement events to approved tools.
data_signals: procurement record with acquisition_strategy_id
approved-tools registry and tool identifiers used in each procurement event
procurement exception/waiver logs with justification
procurement timestamps and responsible approver IDs
dollar value and criticality of affected procurements
audit trail of tool usage (e.g., e-procurement system logs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system-of-record procurement logs showing acquisition_strategy_id and tool usage for each procurement event.
Weak: ad-hoc email approvals or manual notes without system-traceable proof of approved method usage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The entry title references acquisition strategies and tools, indicating preventive controls to enforce approved procurement methods.

SA-12(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on enforcing approved acquisition strategies/tools during procurement, unlike other sub-controls which focus on supplier reviews, shipping, or diversity.

SA-12(2).measurement_goal: Count the number of supplier security reviews completed versus scheduled, and measure the detection rate of supplier-related findings per review cycle.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is marked detective; metrics that count reviews completed and findings detected (counts/rates) directly evidence review activity and detection effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of supplier reviews completed per period, scheduled vs completed review counts, number and severity of findings discovered during reviews, and remediation follow-up items logged per supplier.
data_signals: supplier_review schedule and completion timestamps
supplier review reports with findings and severity labels
open vs closed review findings and remediation timestamps
coverage list of suppliers with last review date
reviewer identity and scope documentation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: documented reviews with signed reports, findings linked to remediation tickets and closure dates.
Weak: undocumented or informal review notes without timestamps or tracked remediation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name 'Supplier Reviews' and the provided type 'detective' indicate this control is intended to detect supplier weaknesses via periodic reviews.

SA-12(2).differentiation_note: This control is specifically about conducting and evidencing supplier reviews and findings, not about contractual clauses or procurement methods.

SA-12(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of shipments and warehousing arrangements using approved/trusted carriers and facilities, and count incidents of tampering or chain-of-custody breaches.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, coverage percentage of shipments/warehousing that use trusted providers shows compliance; counts of incidents supplement evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of inbound/outbound shipments and stored assets handled by approved carriers/warehouses, chain-of-custody logs for high-risk shipments, and count of reported shipping/warehouse incidents (loss, tamper).
data_signals: approved_carriers/warehouses registry with certification/approval_date
shipment manifests linked to carrier_id and tracking events
chain-of-custody logs and signatures with timestamps
incident reports for shipment tampering/loss
warehouse access logs or certificate of compliance
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: end-to-end shipment tracking records tied to approved_carrier IDs and signed chain-of-custody events.
Weak: paper manifests or ad-hoc tracking without persistent, verifiable chain-of-custody data.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title references trusted shipping/warehousing which are preventive measures to reduce supply-chain tampering or loss.

SA-12(3).differentiation_note: Targets logistics controls (shipping and warehousing) specifically, unlike sibling controls that target procurement, assessments, or intelligence.

SA-12(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical service/product categories with at least two qualified suppliers and track the count of single-supplier exposures by category.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control focused on supplier diversity; percent coverage of categories that meet diversification policy is the key metric, with counts of single points of failure.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of critical product/service categories with >=2 qualified suppliers, list/count of categories with single-supplier reliance, and contractual/technical barriers recorded for switching suppliers.
data_signals: supplier roster mapped to product/service category
supplier qualification records and qualification_date
supplier count per product/service category
single-supplier occurrence list with mitigation plans
contractual exclusivity or lead-time constraints documentation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: supplier-to-category mappings in a CMDB showing ≥2 qualified suppliers for critical categories and documented mitigation for single-supplier cases.
Weak: informal knowledge or undocumented supplier lists without qualification evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control's focus on 'Diversity of Suppliers' is preventive, reducing dependency and single-point-of-failure risk.

SA-12(4).differentiation_note: Uniquely requires supplier-to-category mappings and counts to show diversification, not just contract clauses or review activity.

SA-12(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of contracts and supplier engagements that include limitation-of-harm clauses and count the number of containment/segmentation controls applied to supplier-accessible systems.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: primary evidence is coverage (percentage) of contractual and technical controls that limit harm; counts of applied containment controls are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of supplier contracts containing limitation-of-harm clauses (e.g., liability, segmentation requirements), count of supplier-accessible systems with network segmentation or limiting controls, and incidents where limitation-of-harm measures mitigated impact.
data_signals: contract clause inventory with limitation_of_harm flag
system access inventories showing supplier access and segmentation status
network segmentation/configuration records for supplier-access systems
incident logs showing containment measures and impact reduction
policy definitions for acceptable limitation clauses
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable contract repository with clause flags and network configuration records showing segmentation applied to supplier-accessible systems.
Weak: scanned PDFs of contracts without structured clause indexing or missing linkage to systems where suppliers have access.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Limitation of Harm' indicates preventive measures (contractual and technical) to limit impact from supplier incidents.

SA-12(5).differentiation_note: Emphasizes contractual and technical containment measures (clauses and segmentation) rather than procurement methods or reviews.

SA-12(6).measurement_goal: Measure the rate and percentage of procurements completed within defined procurement time targets (procurement lead-time SLA) and count expedited or delayed procurements with security exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control focused on minimizing procurement time; percentage of procurements meeting lead-time SLAs is primary, with rates of expedited procurement as secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of procurements completed within the defined procurement-time SLA (by category/criticality), median/average procurement lead time, and count of procurements where security checks were skipped to accelerate procurement.
data_signals: procurement_request_timestamp and procurement_completion_timestamp
procurement SLA targets by category
procurements flagged as expedited or with waived security checks
procurement approver and justification notes
time-to-contract-signature metrics
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system-tracked procurement timestamps compared to SLA targets with documented security check status per procurement.
Weak: anecdotal statements about procurement speed without system timestamps or SLA mapping.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name targets minimizing procurement time (preventive process control) and the provided type is preventive.

SA-12(6).differentiation_note: Specifically measures procurement lead-times and SLA compliance, unlike sibling controls focused on contract content or supplier vetting.

SA-12(7).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of supplier selections/acceptances/updates that include completed pre-selection assessments and count suppliers onboarded without assessments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percent coverage of pre-selection assessments shows adherence; counts of unassessed selections provide visibility into gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of supplier selections/acceptances/updates where a documented pre-selection assessment exists (including date, assessor, and findings), number of suppliers accepted without assessment, and average time between selection and completion of assessment.
data_signals: supplier selection records with assessment_id linkage
pre-selection assessment reports and timestamps
supplier onboarding checklist completion flags
suppliers onboarded_without_assessment list and justification
assessment findings and severity
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured onboarding workflows that prevent moving to acceptance without an assessment record.
Weak: retroactive or ad-hoc assessments logged after supplier acceptance without evidence of pre-selection timing.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires assessments prior to selection/acceptance, a preventive gating activity to reduce supplier risk.

SA-12(7).differentiation_note: Focuses on timing and existence of pre-selection assessments (gatekeeping) rather than ongoing reviews or contractual content.

SA-12(8).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of high-risk supplier decisions informed by at least one all-source intelligence input and count intelligence alerts affecting supplier posture/action.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percent of supplier decisions using intelligence demonstrates policy adherence; counts of intelligence-derived actions indicate operationalization.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of high/critical supplier selections, renewals, or changes where an all-source intelligence input is recorded; count of intelligence alerts tied to supplier risk escalations and resulting actions.
data_signals: supplier decision records with linked intelligence_report_id
intelligence feed entries referencing supplier identifiers
alerts/tickets opened from intelligence that reference suppliers
documented actions taken in response to intelligence (e.g., suspension, enhanced monitoring)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: linkage between intelligence report IDs and supplier decision records with timestamps and documented actions.
Weak: intelligence used informally without records tying it to the decision-making process.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires intelligence inputs to inform supplier decisions, a preventive step to avoid risky supplier choices.

SA-12(8).differentiation_note: Unique in requiring measurable linkage between external/internal intelligence inputs and supplier lifecycle decisions, not just contractual or operational controls.

SA-12(9).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of supplier-integrated operations that meet defined operations security requirements (e.g., access controls, logging), and count operational security incidents attributable to supplier integrations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percent of supplier-integrated operations compliant with operations-security requirements is primary; incident counts are useful detective complements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of supplier-integrated systems that comply with operations security baselines (access controls, logging enabled, monitoring), number of supplier-attributable operational security incidents, and time to remediate such incidents.
data_signals: inventory of supplier-integrated systems with ops-security-baseline compliance flags
access control lists and supplier-related accounts log
system/application logging configuration and retention settings for supplier systems
operational incident reports tied to supplier integrations
remediation tickets with timestamps for supplier-related incidents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated configuration scans showing logging and access-control settings for supplier systems plus correlated incident tickets.
Weak: periodic manual attestations without system-level configuration evidence or incident linkage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name 'Operations Security' for supplier integrations implies preventive operational controls (access, logging) to reduce operational risk from suppliers.

SA-12(9).differentiation_note: Focused on operational/technical controls applied to supplier-integrated systems (access, logging, monitoring) rather than procurement, contract, or intelligence activities.

SA-12(10).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of incoming hardware and software components with validated provenance and integrity checks (attestation/signature) at intake, and count of items flagged as altered or non-genuine per intake period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control verifying coverage/compliance (percentage of components with successful validation). Counts of tamper events and trend over time are useful secondary signals to show failures and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of receipts (hardware/software) that completed automated integrity/provenance verification (e.g., cryptographic signature or attestation) within the required acceptance window, plus count of items that failed validation and their disposition timestamps.
data_signals: receipt/ingress records for components with timestamps
integrity verification results (signature/checksum/attestation) per component
component unique identifier / serial number registry
chain-of-custody / custody transfer event logs
tamper-detection alerts or physical seal break records
verification completion timestamps vs. receipt timestamps
disposition or quarantine ticket IDs for failed validations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped verification logs showing successful signature/attestation checks for each receipt, linked to asset IDs and ingestion events.
Weak: occasional manual acceptance notes or a one-off inspection report without machine-readable verification or linkage to the ingest event.

affinity_keywords: Validate as Genuine and Not Altered

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input metadata lists the control as preventive and the objective is to prevent altered or non-genuine components from entering the environment (phrases: 'Validate as Genuine and Not Altered').

SA-12(10).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on intake-time validation and tamper detection of components (provenance/integrity checks) versus other SA-12 siblings that focus on testing, agreements, or remediation processes.

SA-12(11).measurement_goal: Measure coverage and findings of penetration testing/analysis applied to supply-chain elements, processes, and actors: count of supply-chain penetration tests executed, distribution of findings by severity, and percentage of high-severity findings remediated within policy timelines.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, percentage metrics (coverage and remediation rate) show whether required testing and remediation coverage is met; counts and trends provide event volumes and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of penetration tests or security analyses conducted that include supply-chain elements/processes/actors, number and severity of findings per test, and remediation ticket counts/timestamps showing closure within policy-defined timeframes.
data_signals: penetration test reports with scope including supplier/system/component identifiers
test execution timestamps and tester identity/organization
finding records with severity classification (e.g., high/medium/low)
remediation ticket IDs linked to findings with open/close timestamps
test coverage matrix mapping supply-chain elements to most recent test date
authorizations/engagement approvals for tests (scope documents)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable test results mapped to asset/supplier inventories and automated tracking of finding → remediation tickets with timestamps.
Weak: narrative PDF test reports without mapping to supply-chain elements, or ad-hoc tests with no remediation tracking.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Listed as preventive and the control seeks to proactively identify weaknesses through penetration testing/analysis (phrases: 'Penetration Testing / Analysis of Elements, Processes, and Actors').

SA-12(11).differentiation_note: Emphasizes active testing and analysis of supply-chain elements (tests, findings, remediation rates) rather than document-based controls or inventory/provenance validation.

SA-12(12).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical suppliers and supply-chain relationships covered by signed inter-organizational agreements containing required security clauses, plus count of agreements missing mandatory clauses.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive contractual coverage is best expressed as percentage coverage of required agreements; counts of missing/expired agreements and comparison to required clause checklists are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of supplier/partner relationships classified as in-scope that have an active signed inter-organizational agreement containing the prescribed security clauses, and a count of in-scope suppliers lacking such agreements or with expired agreements.
data_signals: contract/agreement repository entries with supplier identifiers
security clause checklist mapping per agreement
agreement signature timestamps and expiry/renewal dates
supplier criticality classification or in-scope list
exceptions or waiver approval records
contract change/amendment history
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized contract repository with machine-readable clause inventory and signed timestamps showing active coverage for each in-scope supplier.
Weak: spreadsheets or manual notes without signed agreement copies or clause mapping, or verbal acknowledgements without documented agreements.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is specified as preventive and focuses on establishing contractual safeguards (phrase: 'Inter-organizational Agreements').

SA-12(12).differentiation_note: Targets contractual coverage and clause-level compliance with suppliers, distinct from technical validation, testing, or remediation-focused siblings.

SA-12(13).measurement_goal: Measure inventory completeness for critical information system components supplied via the supply chain and the percentage of those critical components with required protective controls applied (e.g., maintenance status, configuration baseline adherence).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control ensuring critical components are identified and protected, percentage coverage of inventory and configuration compliance is primary; distributions by criticality and trend over time are useful secondaries.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical information system components (per the official criticality classification) that appear in the authoritative asset inventory and have required protective controls/configuration baselines and maintenance assignments recorded.
data_signals: authoritative asset inventory with criticality tags
component-to-supplier mapping
configuration baseline compliance scan results for critical components
maintenance assignment records (MA-6 reference)
risk assessment linkage (RA-9 reference) for each critical component
patch/patch-status records for critical components
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory reconciliation showing all critical components mapped to controls and maintenance records with automated configuration compliance scans.
Weak: manual lists of critical components without automated control verification or outdated inventory spreadsheets.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input type is preventive and the control's purpose is to ensure critical components are identified and protected before compromise (phrase: 'Critical Information System Components').

SA-12(13).differentiation_note: Specifically requires identifying and ensuring protections for critical components (inventory and control-mapping), as opposed to agreements, testing, or traceability-focused siblings.

SA-12(14).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of supply-chain-sourced components that have unique identity tags and an end-to-end traceability record (provenance/trace logs) from supplier to deployment, and count of traceability gaps discovered.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about coverage (percentage of items with identity/traceability); counts of missing tags and trending of traceability completeness are useful to show operational posture.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of components with recorded unique identifiers and complete provenance/trace logs (supplier, shipment, receipt, custody transfers, installation) and count of components with incomplete or missing traceability entries.
data_signals: component unique identifier registry
supplier provenance records
shipment and receipt event logs with timestamps
installation/assignment records linking component ID to asset
traceability/telemetry logs (SR-4(1) / SR-4(2) references)
exceptions or manual traceability reconciliation records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: end-to-end, timestamped provenance records for each component linked to unique IDs and deployment events in an authoritative system.
Weak: ad-hoc or retrospective trace logs, or missing unique IDs that prevent automated reconciliation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is preventive and focuses on ensuring identity and traceability of supply-chain components to prevent misuse or substitution (phrase: 'Identity and Traceability').

SA-12(14).differentiation_note: Concentrates on unique identity tags and end-to-end provenance/traceability records rather than contractual, testing, or remediation processes.

SA-12(15).measurement_goal: Measure the remediation lifecycle for supply-chain-related weaknesses: count of supplier-sourced deficiencies reported, percent remediated within the organization's remediation policy timeframe, and mean time to remediate supplier deficiencies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a corrective/preventive hybrid focused on addressing weaknesses, remediation velocity (rate, e.g., fix rate or MTTR) is primary; counts and trends provide volume and improvement context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and rate of supplier-originated weakness/deficiency reports, timestamps for identification → remediation ticket creation → remediation closure, and the percent closed within the organization's remediation policy timeframe (or SLA if defined).
data_signals: weakness/deficiency reports tagged as 'supplier' origin
corrective action / remediation ticket records with open and close timestamps
root cause analysis documents
supplier corrective action plans and acceptance records
SLA or policy remediation timeframes (SR-3 reference)
exception/waiver approvals for unresolved items
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage of deficiency reports to remediation tickets with timestamps and supplier action plans showing closure within defined timeframes.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or email threads without ticketing records or clear timestamps tying identification to remediation closure.

affinity_keywords: Processes to Address Weaknesses or Deficiencies

control_type_classification.reasoning: Listed as preventive in the input and the control title emphasizes establishing processes to address weaknesses before they cause harm (phrase: 'Processes to Address Weaknesses or Deficiencies').

SA-12(15).differentiation_note: Targets the processes and metrics around identifying and closing supplier-related weaknesses (remediation lifecycle) rather than technical validation, testing, or contractual coverage.

SA-13.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that SA-13 (Trustworthiness) has been formally withdrawn and its requirements have been mapped and migrated into SA-8; measure the percentage of SA-13 legacy artifacts (policies, procedures, implementations) that are recorded as migrated to SA-8 and the count of outstanding unmapped SA-13 items.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive/governance withdrawal action, the primary evidence is coverage (percentage of legacy SA-13 artifacts migrated to SA-8). Counts of remaining items and comparisons to a mapping registry are useful secondary metrics to show completeness and gap status.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of artifacts (policy documents, procedure records, implementation records, system configurations) originally mapped to SA-13 that have an authoritative migration/mapping entry showing incorporation into SA-8; timestamps of migration, migration ticket IDs, and any approved exception records. Also capture the number of legacy SA-13 references remaining in policy/procedural documents.
data_signals: control repository entries with status field showing 'withdrawn' or 'migrated' for SA-13
mapping records linking SA-13 items to SA-8 (mapping table rows or documented mappings)
policy/procedure document versions and timestamps referencing SA-13 or SA-8
change request / migration ticket IDs and completion timestamps for SA-13 -> SA-8 actions
count of active implementation artifacts still labeled SA-13 in inventory
approved exception/waiver records for any unmigrated SA-13 items
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized control registry shows SA-13 status='withdrawn' with explicit mapping to SA-8, migration ticket IDs, timestamps, and approval signatures.
Weak: only scanned/archived PDF policy mentioning 'SA-13 withdrawn' without mapping records, or ad-hoc notes in emails.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input metadata lists the control type as 'preventive' and the control name 'Trustworthiness' implies preventive/governance requirements; description states 'Withdrawn: incorporated into SA-8' indicating administrative consolidation rather than an operational detection or correction action.

SA-13.differentiation_note: This entry differs from sibling operational controls because SA-13 is withdrawn — the measurement focus is on mapping/migration completeness into SA-8 rather than on implementing a standalone technical control; auditors should verify migration records rather than runtime telemetry.

SA-14.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that a documented criticality analysis exists for systems/components (asset-level or system-level), and measure the proportion of assets with an assessed criticality classification and documented mitigation/contingency ownership as incorporated into RA-9/SA-20.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about coverage and completeness (percent of assets with completed criticality analyses); counts and trends supplement by showing volume and progress over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets/systems with a documented criticality analysis artifact (document ID, date, owner) and presence of associated mitigation/contingency actions; count of assets lacking criticality classification; timestamps of last analysis and linkage to RA-9/SA-20 risk entries.
data_signals: asset/system inventory records with criticality classification field and timestamp
criticality_analysis document identifiers and last-modified timestamps
owner/approver fields for each criticality analysis
linkage records to RA-9 or SA-20 risk register entries
count of assets with no criticality entry (unassessed assets)
mitigation/contingency plan documents referenced per critical asset
date of last review/refresh for each analysis
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Signed/dated criticality analysis artifacts linked to the authoritative asset inventory and cross-referenced in the RA-9 risk register.
Moderate: Scoped spreadsheets or change tickets without clear owner or link to risk register.
Weak: High-level statements or withdrawn control notes without per-asset analysis or timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control (Criticality Analysis) is proactive — identifying critical assets and planning mitigations before events occur, as indicated by the control name and purpose.

SA-14.differentiation_note: This entry covers the high-level existence and coverage of a criticality analysis across assets/systems (general assessment coverage); the sibling SA-14(1) focuses specifically on single-source critical components and sourcing alternatives.
