SA-14(1).measurement_goal: Show the number and percent of critical components identified as having no viable alternative sourcing (single-source), and the percent of those components that have documented mitigation/diversification plans, approvals, and review dates as incorporated into SA-20.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive augmentation focused on sourcing, the most relevant metric is percent coverage (how many single-source critical components have mitigation plans); counts and trends show inventory size and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of critical components flagged as 'no viable alternative sourcing' and percentage of those with a documented mitigation/diversification plan, supplier alternative analysis, contractual constraints noted, and an assigned owner/mitigation timeline.
data_signals: component inventory records with 'no_alternative_sourcing' or 'single_source' flag
supplier/vendor list and number of unique vendors per component
supplier alternative search logs or evaluation documents
mitigation/diversification plan documents and associated timestamps
contractual exclusivity or sole-source clause records
risk acceptance or mitigation approval records (owner, date)
last review/assessment date for each flagged component
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated inventory entries marking single-source components with linked mitigation plans, supplier analyses, and review timestamps tied to SA-20 records.
Moderate: Manual lists of single-source components without formal mitigation ownership or review history.
Weak: References only to withdrawn control text or absence of any supplier/contractual evidence.

affinity_keywords: Critical Components with No Viable Alternative Sourcing

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control targets proactive identification and mitigation of single-source component supply risks (prevention of supply disruptions), per the control title and augmentation text.

SA-14(1).differentiation_note: Unlike SA-14 (general criticality analysis coverage), SA-14(1) specifically requires measurement of single-source critical components and whether those components have documented mitigation/diversification actions and supplier alternative analysis.

SA-15.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of development projects that have approved, current development process documentation, mandated standards, and an inventoried toolchain; and count projects lacking required process or tool controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control about coverage and adherence to policies; percentage (coverage) shows how many projects comply, while counts and trends show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active development projects with (a) documented development process approved by governance, (b) evidence of adherence to mandated standards, and (c) a recorded toolchain inventory. Also measure count of projects missing any of these items and trend of remediation actions over prior periods.
data_signals: project registry entries with approved development process document ID and approval timestamp
standards adoption checklist status per project (pass/fail) or policy compliance flags
toolchain inventory records linked to project (tool names, versions, integration date)
exceptions or waivers logged against projects
project creation and closure timestamps
remediation/closure timestamps for nonconformances
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable project registry + toolchain inventory and automated policy-compliance checks showing current approvals and no open exceptions.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or retrospective attestations without timestamps or linking to project artifacts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on establishing and maintaining development processes, standards, and tools which are preventive in nature.

SA-15.differentiation_note: This entry covers baseline governance of development processes and tool inventories across projects, whereas siblings focus on specific aspects like metrics, threat modeling, or automation.

SA-15(1).measurement_goal: Measure coverage and compliance of defined quality metrics across projects: percentage of projects reporting defined quality metrics (e.g., defect density, test coverage) and trends in those metrics against quality thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive/process control, auditors need coverage percentages (how many projects report metrics) and trends/distributions of metric values (quality over time).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of projects publishing required quality metrics (defect density, unit/integration test coverage, static analysis pass rate) per release, plus distributions and trend lines of those metric values by project and criticality tier.
data_signals: project release records with attached quality metric reports
defect density values (defects per KLOC) per release
test coverage percentage per repository/commit
static analysis results and pass/fail counts
date-stamped quality metric publications
project criticality classification
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CI artifacts publishing test-coverage and static-analysis metrics per build tied to the project and release.
Weak: manual summary spreadsheets published quarterly without per-release granularity or source artifacts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates quality metrics and measurement to ensure process quality before release, a preventive orientation.

SA-15(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on measurement and reporting of quality metrics (defect density, coverage) rather than broader process or tooling inventories.

SA-15(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of development pipelines and repositories integrated with security and privacy tracking tools, and count open tracked security/privacy items per project and release.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive/detective hybrid requires coverage percentages (integration coverage) and counts/rates of tracked findings to show operating effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of code repositories and CI/CD pipelines with active integrations to security/privacy tracking tools (SAST/DAST/SCA/privacy scanners), plus counts of open tracked issues by severity and average time-to-triage.
data_signals: repository CI/CD integration flags for SAST/DAST/SCA/privacy tools
security/privacy tracking tool findings with severity and timestamps
issue ticket IDs linked to findings and their status
time-to-triage and time-to-assign metrics per finding
coverage matrix of repositories vs. enabled tools
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated tool integrations publishing findings to a centralized tracker with timestamps and linkage to code commits.
Weak: manual scanning performed irregularly and results emailed without linkage to projects or tickets.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires integrating tracking tools into development pipelines to prevent insecure code from progressing; measurement of coverage is preventive.

SA-15(2).differentiation_note: This entry measures tool integration and active tracking of security/privacy findings, distinct from quality metrics or high-level process documentation.

SA-15(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of projects and system components that have an assigned criticality classification and an associated security requirement matrix, and track the count of unclassified assets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Criticality analysis is a preventive governance activity; percentage coverage of classification and distributions of criticality are most informative.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of projects, components, and assets with documented criticality classification and a mapped set of security controls/requirements; count of assets missing classification and time to classify.
data_signals: asset/project inventory entries with criticality tier assigned
security requirement matrices linked to each criticality tier
date of criticality assessment and assessor ID
unclassified asset count
remediation/assessment timestamps for classification
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative CMDB entries showing criticality tier and linked control requirements updated and enforced in provisioning workflows.
Weak: informal lists or undocumented/undated assessments.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Assigning criticality and mapping requirements prevents under-protection of critical systems and is therefore preventive.

SA-15(3).differentiation_note: Unique in requiring asset/project-level criticality classification and mapping to security requirements, unlike siblings that focus on tools, metrics, or automation.

SA-15(4).measurement_goal: NOTE: Withdrawn — measurement responsibility moved to SA-11(2). If retained locally, measure count and coverage of threat models and vulnerability analyses per project, and the percentage incorporated into design decisions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: When present, threat modeling and vulnerability analysis are detective/preventive activities where counts of models/analyses and percentage incorporated into design decisions are useful; control marked withdrawn so counts help show any residual practice.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of completed threat models and vulnerability analyses per project/release, percentage of designs changed as a result, and time between analysis and mitigation decision. If withdrawn, track linkage to SA-11(2) artifacts.
data_signals: threat model documents with project/release IDs and timestamps
vulnerability analysis reports and scan outputs
design change logs or PRs referencing threat model findings
traceability between threat models and mitigation tickets
references to SA-11(2) artifacts (if migration recorded)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal threat-model artifacts tied to code changes and ticketing systems showing mitigations implemented.
Weak: informal notes or disconnected reports without linkage to design decisions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Originally designed to prevent design-level vulnerabilities via threat modelling; description indicates withdrawal and migration to SA-11(2).

SA-15(4).differentiation_note: This entry is marked withdrawn and differs by referencing SA-11(2); its unique need is traceability from threat models to design changes, unlike sibling tool/process entries.

SA-15(5).measurement_goal: Measure the count and percentage reduction of exposed attack surface elements (open services, public endpoints, exposed ports) per application and track remediation rate for identified exposures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Attack-surface activities produce discrete findings (open endpoints, services) where counts and reduction percentages over time show effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of exposed endpoints/services/ports per application, percentage of exposures mitigated within defined timeframes, and trend of total exposed attack-surface elements across releases.
data_signals: external/internal port scan results with host/service identifiers
API/public endpoint inventory and exposure status
configuration baseline deviations exposing services
tickets created for exposure remediation and resolution timestamps
pre/post-release attack surface comparison snapshots
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated scanning and baseline comparisons run per build/release with tickets automatically created for new exposures.
Weak: single manual audit snapshots without historical comparisons.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Reducing attack surface is a preventive engineering practice to minimize exposure before exploitation.

SA-15(5).differentiation_note: Targets measurable exposures (endpoints/ports/services) and their reduction over time, unlike siblings focused on process documentation, metrics, or tooling.

SA-15(6).measurement_goal: Measure rate and coverage of continuous improvement activities: percentage of projects with documented post-release retrospectives identifying security/process improvements and percent of identified improvement actions implemented within target timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Continuous improvement is corrective/preventive; rate metrics (actions implemented per period) and coverage percentages demonstrate improvement velocity and adoption.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate of closed improvement actions per release or quarter, percentage of projects conducting documented retrospectives that produce actionable items, and time-to-complete improvement actions.
data_signals: retrospective/lessons-learned documents with project/release IDs and dates
improvement action items logged in ticketing system with status and resolution timestamps
percent of action items closed vs opened in period
links between prior incidents/vulnerabilities and resulting improvement actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linking from incidents and scans to tracked improvement actions with closure dates and verification artifacts.
Weak: narrative retrospectives without tracked action items or closure evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Continuous improvement tracks and implements corrective actions from past findings, aligning with corrective control behavior.

SA-15(6).differentiation_note: Focused on tracking and closing improvement actions from retrospectives and incidents, unlike siblings that measure tools, metrics, or classification.

SA-15(7).measurement_goal: Measure the count and frequency of automated vulnerability analyses executed per repository/release, coverage percentage of codebases under automated analysis, and the rate of findings triaged within SLA.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated vulnerability analysis generates discrete findings; counts of analyses and findings and rates (triage/fix) are most useful to evidence continuous detection.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of automated vulnerability scans/analyses executed per repository or component per day/week, percentage coverage of codebases integrated with automated analysis, counts of findings by severity and time-to-triage/fix.
data_signals: automated scan execution logs with repository/component and timestamp
coverage matrix listing repositories integrated with automated analysis tools
vulnerability finding records with severity, timestamp, and ticket linkage
time-to-triage and time-to-remediate for findings
scan failure/error logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous CI-triggered analyses with findings automatically pushed to ticketing and evidence of triage/fix within SLA.
Weak: occasional manual scans with unlinked results and no ticketing or timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Automated vulnerability analysis produces detections of security issues, making this primarily detective in function.

SA-15(7).differentiation_note: Specifically addresses automation and operational metrics of vulnerability analysis (execution frequency, coverage, triage rates), distinct from manual threat modeling or high-level process standards.

SA-15(8).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of projects consuming and reusing centralized threat and vulnerability intelligence artifacts (threat models, vulnerability signatures, mitigations) and count instances where reused intelligence led to prevented findings or faster remediation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive/detective hybrid is about reuse/coverage; percentage of projects reusing shared intelligence indicates scale, and counts show impact.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of projects/packages that import or reference canonical threat/vulnerability artifacts (e.g., centralized threat models, signatures, mitigation libraries) and count of prevented/accelerated remediations attributable to reused artifacts.
data_signals: references to centralized threat/vulnerability artifacts in project documentation or CI configuration
consumption logs (who/what pulled intelligence artifacts and when)
tickets or PRs referencing reused artifacts in mitigation
metrics linking reused artifacts to reduction in duplicate findings
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated artifact repository access logs plus references in code/PRs showing reuse and measurable reduction in duplicate work.
Weak: informal sharing via email or ad-hoc documents with no usage telemetry.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Encouraging reuse of threat/vulnerability info prevents repeated introduction of known issues and speeds mitigation, a preventive aim.

SA-15(8).differentiation_note: Emphasizes telemetry of reuse of shared intelligence (artifact consumption and impact), unlike siblings that focus on creating metrics, tools, or analyses.

SA-15(9).measurement_goal: NOTE: Withdrawn — measurement migrated to SA-3(2). If retained, measure percentage of development/testing activities that use sanitized/masked live data vs. production live data and count unauthorized uses.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: When controlling use of live data, auditors need coverage percentages (how many dev/test environments use masked data) and counts of unauthorized live-data uses; control is preventive and is marked withdrawn.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of test/development environments using masked/anonymized datasets, count of occurrences where live production data was used in non-production environments, and evidence of approvals for any exceptions.
data_signals: environment data classification flags (production, masked-test, synthetic)
data provisioning logs showing dataset source and timestamp
exception/approval records for live data use
audit logs showing access to production data from non-prod accounts
references to SA-3(2) artifacts if migrated
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated data-provisioning logs and masking-tool records showing synthetic/masked datasets in non-prod and automated blocking of live-data clones.
Weak: attestations that data was masked without proofs such as provisioning logs or masking reports.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control aims to prevent use of live production data in development/testing (preventive), though it is marked withdrawn and migrated to SA-3(2).

SA-15(9).differentiation_note: Marked withdrawn; uniquely concerns data masking and preventing live-data use in non-production environments, unlike other SA-15 entries focused on processes, tools, or vulnerability handling.

SA-15(10).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of development projects/components that include an incident response plan or linked IR artifacts in their development artifacts and the count of CI/CD/toolchain checks enforcing IR plan presence per release.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage (percentage of projects/components with IR plan integration); counts of projects and trend over time are useful secondary signals to show adoption velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active code repositories / projects that contain a linked Incident Response Plan artifact or documented IR runbook, and the count of pipeline/tooling checks that validate IR plan presence prior to release. Include timestamps showing when IR artifacts were added and CI/CD enforcement events.
data_signals: presence of 'Incident Response Plan' artifact in project repository (file path or link)
development process standard checklist items indicating 'Incident Response Plan' inclusion
CI/CD pipeline checks or gating events enforcing IR plan attachment
issue/ticket IDs linking component to IR plan or playbook
repository commit timestamps for IR plan artifacts
tool configuration records (e.g., templates, IaC) that include IR integration flags
audit entries showing approval/signoff of IR Plan in design documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CI/CD gate failures preventing release when IR plan artifact is missing, machine-readable links to IR plans in each component repo, and time-stamped approvals.
Weak: one-off manual attestations or a central policy document without per-project linkage, or occasional screenshots of plans.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text ties incident response planning into 'Development Process, Standards, and Tools', indicating a preventive integration of IR requirements into development artifacts and toolchains.

SA-15(10).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on integrating incident response planning into the development lifecycle and toolchain (presence and enforcement of IR artifacts), unlike siblings that focus on archival or PII minimization.

SA-15(11).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems and components that have a documented archive design and implemented archival controls (e.g., configured archive storage, retention schedule, and access controls) as required by development standards and tools.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage and compliance across systems/components (percentage of components with archival design); counts of archive-enabled components and distribution of archive configurations are useful supplements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems/components with an archived-system/component design document or configuration, evidence of retention schedules applied, and archive storage configuration (encryption, access ACLs). Include archive restore test results and tool configuration records.
data_signals: archive system or component design documents checked into project artifacts
component inventory field indicating 'archive' or 'archival required'
retention schedule records tied to components
archive storage configuration (encryption-at-rest flag, access control lists)
archive access and restore test logs
tool configuration templates for archiving (e.g., backup/archive tool settings)
timestamps of archival configuration deployment
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable component inventory with archive flag, automated enforcement in deployment pipelines, encrypted archive configuration and periodic restore test logs.
Weak: policy statements requiring archiving without per-component design docs or evidence of implemented configurations and restore tests.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement ties archival requirements into development 'Process, Standards, and Tools', indicating preventive design and implementation requirements for archive systems/components.

SA-15(11).differentiation_note: Targets archival design and implementation for systems/components (retention, storage configuration, restore testing) rather than incident response integration or PII minimization.

SA-15(12).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of applications/systems that apply data minimization requirements during design (e.g., reduced PII fields, use of pseudonymization/tokenization) and track the count of stored PII elements per system to demonstrate reduction over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control focused on coverage and design compliance, percentage of systems conforming to PII minimization standards is most appropriate; counts and trends show absolute PII volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of systems with completed data inventory and applied minimization controls (fields removed, pseudonymization/tokenization applied), plus the count of PII data elements stored per system and timestamps of minimization implementation to show reduction.
data_signals: data inventory records with 'Personally Identifiable Information' flags
design/doc signoffs referencing 'Minimize Personally Identifiable Information'
schema/field lists showing PII fields per system
evidence of pseudonymization/tokenization or redaction implementations
data flow diagrams showing eliminated PII collection points
change logs showing removal or transformation of PII fields
privacy-by-design checklist signoffs in development artifacts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated data discovery scans and schema inventories showing reduced PII field counts, cryptographic/tokenization controls applied, and signed design artifacts demonstrating minimization.
Weak: an organizational policy that states minimization without per-system inventories, or manual attestations without artifact-level evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly requires minimizing PII in 'Development Process, Standards, and Tools', which is a preventive design-time requirement to limit data collection and storage.

SA-15(12).differentiation_note: Specifically requires measuring design-time reduction of PII (data inventory, field counts, pseudonymization) as opposed to archival controls or incident response integration.

SA-16.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of development personnel who have completed required developer-provided training (by role), their assessment scores, timeliness of completion against policy timeframes, and the rate of up-to-date refresher completions to demonstrate the training program is applied to all developers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive training control, auditors expect coverage/ completion percentages (primary). Counts of enrolled/completed developers, assessment scores, and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show depth and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-developer records linking developer role to required training module(s), timestamps of enrollment and completion, assessment/test scores, certificate issuance, and refresher completion dates; compute percent of developers in each role who completed required training within the policy timeframe and average assessment score by cohort.
data_signals: developer roster with role/function mapping
training module IDs mapped to developer-required curricula
training enrollment timestamps
training completion timestamps and certificate IDs
assessment/test scores and pass/fail results per developer
refresher training completion dates and due dates
training delivery method (instructor-led, online) and version
policy-required completion timeframe or deadline identifiers
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated LMS logs that show enrollment, completion timestamps, module ID, and assessment score linked to unique developer IDs.
Moderate: HR or LMS exported reports showing completion but missing per-module assessment scores or role mapping.
Weak: Self-attestation or one-off signed statements without timestamps, module identifiers, or assessment results.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires providing training to developers (preventing insecure development practices), indicated by the control name 'Developer-provided Training.'

SA-16.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on developer-targeted training records and role-based completion metrics (vs. broader organizational training) and therefore requires mapping training modules to developer roles and measuring completion/assessment per developer.

SA-17.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of development projects with a documented security-and-privacy architecture, the count of those architectures that received formal review/approval, and the number of design-to-implementation traceability artifacts linking architecture decisions to implemented controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage metrics (percentage of projects with architecture docs) plus counts of reviews and trends showing sustained compliance over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of active projects that have a stored security/privacy architecture artifact and an associated review/approval timestamp, plus count of traceability links (design-id -> implementation-commit) per project and number of outstanding architecture review findings.
data_signals: architecture document artifacts (repository path, artifact id, version, timestamp)
design review/approval records (reviewer, approval timestamp, findings)
privacy impact assessment artifacts
traceability matrices linking design elements to implementation commits or tickets
design change logs and version history
open architecture review findings count by project
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable architecture artifacts with explicit traceability links and automated review timestamps; Weak: ad‑hoc Word/PDF files with no traceability or missing approval records

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing secure/privacy-aware architecture and design artifacts, which prevents insecure designs from being implemented.

SA-17.differentiation_note: This parent control requires broad measurement of documented security/privacy architecture coverage and approvals across projects, whereas its enhancements focus on specific design techniques (policy models, testability, least privilege, etc.).

SA-17(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems with a formal policy model (e.g., XACML, formal authorization model) defined and the count of policy definitions that are mapped to enforcement points (PDP/PAP/PEP) and unit tests verifying policy decisions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A preventive requirement: auditors want coverage (percentage of systems with formal policy models) and counts of policy artifacts and enforcement mappings; scores help surface quality of models.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope applications with a stored formal policy model artifact and the count of policies mapped to enforcement points, plus number of automated policy decision tests passing vs failing.
data_signals: formal policy model files (e.g., XACML, Rego) with repository paths
policy-to-enforcement mappings (PDP/PAP/PEP configuration records)
policy unit/integration test results (pass/fail, timestamp)
policy version and change history
applications lacking a policy model (negative list)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-executable policy artifacts with automated test suites and mapping records; Weak: informal text descriptions of policies without executable artifacts or tests

control_type_classification.reasoning: Specifying a formal policy model aims to prevent ambiguous authorization behavior by defining explicit policy artifacts and enforcement mappings.

SA-17(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement specifically requires measuring formal, machine-executable policy artifacts and their mapping to enforcement points, unlike sibling controls that focus on general architecture or informal correspondence.

SA-17(2).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and completeness of the inventory of security-relevant components in designs (percentage of components classified as security-relevant with defined security controls) and the count of components missing security control specifications.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of security-relevant components documented and specified) plus counts of missing items to drive remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of components in the architecture inventory flagged as security-relevant that have an associated security control specification (control id, rationale, owner), and the count of security-relevant components without specifications.
data_signals: component inventory records (component id, classification, criticality)
security control specification artifacts per component
owner and contact fields for each component
components flagged as 'security-relevant' without spec
inventory update timestamps and change logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: an authoritative, machine-readable component inventory with a security control field populated for every security-relevant component; Weak: spreadsheet lists or undocumented assumptions without control specs

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring identification and specification of security-relevant components prevents gaps in coverage and ensures controls are planned for each component.

SA-17(2).differentiation_note: Focuses on measurable inventory coverage and per-component security specs rather than overall architecture artifacts or policy modeling.

SA-17(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of critical components for which formal correspondence artifacts exist linking design models to implementation artifacts (e.g., formal proofs, model-checking results, or verified mappings) and the pass/fail results of those formal verification activities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive/detective hybrid is best evidenced by coverage percentages (how many components have formal correspondence) and scores representing verification quality.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of critical design elements with formal correspondence artifacts linked to implementation (artifact id -> commit id), count of formal verification runs and their pass/fail status, and outstanding verification findings per component.
data_signals: formal verification artifacts (proofs, model-checker outputs, certificates)
trace links between design model elements and implementation commits
verification run results (pass/fail, timestamp, tool used)
open verification findings and remediation tickets
list of critical components subject to formal correspondence
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-generated formal verification outputs with signed timestamps and automated trace links; Weak: manual attestations or offline notes claiming correspondence without artifacts

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mandating formal correspondence aims to prevent discrepancies between design and implementation by verifying conformance through formal artifacts.

SA-17(3).differentiation_note: Requires evidence of formal, tool-generated verification artifacts and their mapping to implementation, unlike informal correspondence which relies on reviews and ticket traces.

SA-17(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of design items that have informal correspondence artifacts (traceability matrices, design-review notes, code-review references) linking them to implementation, and the count of mismatches identified during reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control evidenced by coverage percentage (design-items with trace links) plus counts of discrepancies found in reviews.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of design artifacts with at least one trace link to implementation (pull request or ticket) and count of design-vs-implementation discrepancies raised in reviews, including timestamps and resolution status.
data_signals: design-to-code traceability matrix entries (design-id -> PR/ticket id)
code review comments referencing design
design review minutes and attendee lists
discrepancy/defect tickets created from reviews
resolution timestamps for discrepancies
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated traceability links (design id referenced in PRs) and tracked resolution of discrepancies; Weak: undocumented verbal correlations or untracked review outcomes

control_type_classification.reasoning: This enhancement requires establishing informal mapping and review processes to prevent untracked divergence between design and implementation.

SA-17(4).differentiation_note: Unlike the formal correspondence enhancement, this one permits informal artifacts (review notes, PR links) as evidence and so requires tracking of review outcomes and discrepancy tickets.

SA-17(5).measurement_goal: Measure architecture simplicity by a composite complexity score (e.g., normalized component count + dependency depth + average cyclomatic complexity of modules) and the trend in that score per project or component over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A preventive design quality control: a complexity score quantifies conceptual simplicity while distributions and trends show where simplicity improves or degrades.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A normalized architecture complexity score per project computed from component count, dependency graph depth, and average module complexity; distribution of scores across projects and trend over reporting periods to show simplification efforts.
data_signals: component count per architecture
dependency graph depth (max hop count)
module complexity metrics (e.g., cyclomatic complexity aggregated per component)
number of cross-component interfaces
architecture rationale documents describing simplification decisions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated extraction of dependency graphs and complexity metrics tied to architecture versions; Weak: subjective statements that a design is 'simple' without measurable metrics

control_type_classification.reasoning: Encouraging conceptually simple designs prevents unnecessary complexity that can introduce vulnerabilities and maintenance risk.

SA-17(5).differentiation_note: This enhancement requires quantitative complexity metrics and trending (a complexity score), unlike sibling controls that focus on policy artifacts or traceability.

SA-17(6).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of components that include test hooks/interfaces and meet minimum automated test coverage thresholds, plus the execution rate of automated test suites in CI/CD per release.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: coverage percentages (test hook presence, test coverage) are primary evidence; counts and trends show testing throughput and stability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of components with documented test interfaces and with automated unit/integration test coverage above policy thresholds, number of CI/CD pipeline runs that executed tests per release, and count of test failures by component.
data_signals: test interface documentation (API mocks, test endpoints)
automated test coverage reports (per component, % coverage)
CI/CD pipeline run logs showing test steps and pass/fail counts
test execution frequency per component
test environment orchestration definitions (docker-compose, k8s test manifests)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CI/CD logs with per-component coverage reports and test interface artifacts; Weak: manual test reports or ad hoc testing without automated evidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: Designing for testability prevents untestable implementations and is evidenced by concrete test coverage and CI/CD execution metrics.

SA-17(6).differentiation_note: Specifically measures testability artifacts and automated test coverage and execution frequency, unlike other enhancements that measure policy models or design simplicity.

SA-17(7).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of components and services that enforce least-privilege principles (e.g., default-deny ACLs, role-to-permission mappings) and the count of over-privileged roles or accounts identified during design reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: coverage percentage of least-privilege enforcement and counts of over-privileged artifacts provide direct evidence of adherence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of components whose design specifies least-privilege ACLs/roles and the count of design items where role-to-permission mappings exceed policy limits; include timestamps of design review findings and remediation tickets.
data_signals: role-to-permission mapping artifacts (design docs, IAM templates)
default ACL configurations in design artifacts
design review findings related to privilege
list/count of accounts/roles flagged as over-privileged
remediation/mitigation tickets and closure timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable IAM templates and design artifacts showing default-deny and minimal privileges with automated scans flagging deviations; Weak: subjective statements that least privilege was considered without mapping artifacts

control_type_classification.reasoning: Mandating design structure for least privilege prevents excessive privileges from being built into systems.

SA-17(7).differentiation_note: Focuses on design-time evidence of least-privilege (role mappings, default ACLs) and counts of over-privilege, rather than testing or policy-model artifacts.

SA-17(8).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of security controls and architecture enforcement rules that are automated/orchestrated in CI/CD or IaC pipelines and the success/failure rate of orchestration runs that enforce security policies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: coverage percentage of automated enforcement is primary; orchestration success/failure rate provides operational evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of security controls expressed as automatable policies (policy-as-code/IaC checks) that are executed in CI/CD/IaC pipelines, plus orchestration run success rate and counts of policy violations blocked by orchestration.
data_signals: CI/CD pipeline definitions that include security policy checks
policy-as-code artifacts and IaC scan results
orchestration run logs (success/failure, timestamp)
count of deployments blocked by orchestration due to policy violations
automated remediation runs and their outcomes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated pipeline logs showing policy-as-code enforcement and blocked deployments with timestamps; Weak: manual gate checks or undocumented orchestration steps

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring orchestration of security controls prevents human error by automating enforcement in deployment pipelines.

SA-17(8).differentiation_note: Measures automation coverage and enforcement outcomes in pipelines (policy-as-code and orchestration logs), unlike other enhancements that focus on policy models or test hooks.

SA-17(9).measurement_goal: Measure the distribution of independent implementations or vendor diversity for critical functions (count and distribution of distinct implementations), and track single‑point-of‑failure items identified in design reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: distribution shows how many distinct implementations/vendors exist per critical function; counts and trends indicate progress toward diversity/redundancy goals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Distribution of implementations per critical function (e.g., number of distinct vendor/product implementations), count of critical functions with only a single implementation (SPOF), and the number of design review findings that recommend diversification or redundancy.
data_signals: inventory mapping of critical functions to implementations/vendors
count of distinct implementations per function
design review SPOF findings and recommendations
redundancy architecture artifacts (failover plans, replication topologies)
roadmap items or tickets to introduce design diversity
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative inventory showing multiple independent implementations per critical function and resolved SPOF findings; Weak: statements that diversity is desired without inventory evidence or planned remediation

control_type_classification.reasoning: Design diversity reduces reliance on a single implementation and prevents systemic failures, a preventive architectural requirement.

SA-17(9).differentiation_note: Unique in requiring measurement of vendor/implementation distribution and explicit single-point-of-failure counts rather than policy or traceability artifacts.

SA-18.measurement_goal: Measure the volume and coverage of tamper-detection events and integrity checks across systems and devices (count of tamper alerts, devices with active tamper sensors, and successful integrity validations) and evidence that alerts are logged and escalated for investigation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control, event counts (tamper alerts) are the most direct evidence; detection rates and trends help show detection effectiveness and whether incidents are increasing or decreasing over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of tamper-detection events and integrity-check failures per device/system, presence and coverage of tamper-detection sensors (device-level coverage percentage), and timestamps showing alert generation and escalation. Include firmware/checksum validation results and whether alerts generated corresponding incident records.
data_signals: tamper alert/event logs (timestamp, device_id, sensor_type, alert_severity)
device inventory with tamper-sensor present flag
firmware checksum validation logs and validation timestamps
integrity check failure records and remediation tickets
alert escalation/incident ticket IDs linked to tamper events
configuration records showing tamper-detection enabled status
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated tamper-alert logs with device IDs, timestamps, and linked incident tickets; automated integrity checks with immutable logs.
Weak: one-off inspection reports, manual screenshots, or policy text without system-generated alert logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title explicitly references detection (Tamper Resistance and Detection) and input type is 'detective'.

SA-18.differentiation_note: This entry covers broad, operational tamper-detection monitoring and alerting across systems and devices (continuous event collection), whereas the siblings focus on lifecycle integration (SA-18(1)) or physical inspections (SA-18(2)).

SA-18(1).measurement_goal: Measure inclusion and verification of tamper-resistance and detection controls across SDLC phases: count of SDLC artifacts (design reviews, test plans, security requirements) that include tamper controls, and percentage of projects with tamper controls tested before release.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective classification suggests counting artifacts (design reviews/test results) and measuring percent coverage of projects; trends show whether SDLC integration is improving across releases.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of SDLC artifacts per project phase that reference tamper-resistance/detection requirements (design documents, threat models, test cases), number and percentage of builds/releases with tamper-detection test results, and timestamps showing when tamper controls were introduced or verified during the lifecycle.
data_signals: design review minutes/artifact records with tamper-control checklist entries
threat model outputs or security requirement documents referencing tamper resistance/detection
test case execution results for tamper-related tests (pass/fail, timestamp, build_id)
release notes indicating tamper-control implementation
build/deployment pipeline records showing tamper-check gates
change request/approval records referencing tamper-mitigation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable SDLC artifacts and automated pipeline gates that enforce tamper checks and produce traceable test results across multiple releases.
Weak: ad-hoc manual notes in design documents or unlinked inspection comments without timestamps or test evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control variant name references lifecycle phases and test/inspection integration; the provided type is 'detective', implying verification activities within SDLC.

SA-18(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on evidence artifacts inside the SDLC (design/test/release artifacts and pipeline gates) rather than runtime sensor data or physical inspections.

SA-18(2).measurement_goal: Measure inspection coverage and findings: number and percentage of systems/components inspected for tamper evidence, count of tamper findings from inspections, and time-to-remediate inspection findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective inspections produce discrete findings (counts). Measuring percent coverage of inspected systems and tracking trends in findings over time gives auditors clear evidence of inspection activity and outcomes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of scheduled vs completed inspections of systems/components, count of inspection findings classified as tamper/evidence of compromise, inspection report identifiers with timestamps and inspector identity, and remediation ticket linkage with resolution timestamps.
data_signals: inspection schedule records and completion timestamps
inspection reports/checklists with findings (finding_id, system_id, inspector_id, timestamp)
photographic/evidence attachments from inspections
tamper finding counts by system/component
remediation/work order records linked to inspection findings (creation and closure timestamps)
asset registry entries indicating last inspection date
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed inspection reports with timestamps, photographic evidence, and linked remediation tickets closed within policy SLA; automated import of inspection results into asset inventory.
Weak: sporadic manual notes, undocumented walk-throughs, or unaudited checklists without timestamps or linkage to remediation work.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name explicitly references inspection of systems or components (detection via inspection) and input type is 'detective'.

SA-18(2).differentiation_note: This variant emphasizes scheduled physical or component-level inspections and their discrete findings (point-in-time evidence and coverage) rather than SDLC artifacts or continuous sensor alerts.

SA-19.measurement_goal: Measure organizational implementation of a component authenticity program: percent of critical hardware components covered by authenticity controls, count of authenticity exceptions (e.g., counterfeit findings), and presence of documented incorporation into SR controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control the primary need is coverage/coverage percent (percentage) of components under an authenticity program; counts of exceptions and trends over time are useful secondary signals to show incidents and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of critical hardware inventory items with documented authenticity controls (e.g., supplier certificates, receipt inspection, anti-counterfeit scanning) and count of components identified as counterfeit or unauthentic in the reporting period; presence of policy mapping indicating incorporation into SR-series controls.
data_signals: hardware asset inventory with criticality flags and authenticity-control tags
supplier authenticity certificates / chain-of-custody records
component inspection/receipt logs with timestamps
counterfeit detection findings (count and details)
policy or control mapping documents referencing SR-11 / SR-12 incorporation
exceptions register for components flagged as unauthentic
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory records linked to receipt inspection and supplier certificates showing coverage percentage and timestamps; documented control mapping to SR controls.
Weak: only a written policy stating an authenticity program without inventories, inspection logs, or exception records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control's intent (component authenticity) is preventive — it seeks to ensure genuine components and avoid counterfeit introduction.

SA-19.differentiation_note: This entry covers the broad organizational authenticity program (coverage and policy mapping) rather than the specific sub-activities (training, scanning, disposal, or repair controls) handled by the sub-controls.

SA-19(1).measurement_goal: Measure training coverage and effectiveness for anti-counterfeit procedures: percentage of relevant staff trained on anti-counterfeit processes and count of training completions and assessment pass rates over a defined period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control focused on workforce preparedness — primary metric is percent of targeted population trained; counts and trends show program reach and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of target roles (procurement, receiving, repair technicians) with completed anti-counterfeit training and the count and pass-rate of training/assessments in the reporting period; training material version and date to demonstrate maintained currency.
data_signals: training roster with role mappings and completion timestamps
training assessment scores / pass rates
target population list (roles/job titles) for anti-counterfeit training
training material version and issuance date
attendance logs / LMS completion certificates
records linking trained personnel to inspection/repair activities
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: LMS records showing role-mapped completion, assessment scores, and linkage to operational duties; periodic refresher training logs.
Weak: policy statement claiming training exists without completion records or assessment results.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly targets training (preventive action) to reduce counterfeit introduction risk.

SA-19(1).differentiation_note: Focused specifically on workforce training and metrics (coverage, completion, assessment), unlike other siblings that address scanning, disposal, or configuration control.

SA-19(2).measurement_goal: Measure configuration control for serviced/repaired components: percentage of serviced components with verified post-repair configuration baselines and count of service events with documented configuration verification and tamper-evidence checks.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires verification/coverage (percentage) that repairs/service events followed configuration control procedures; event counts and trends show operational compliance and improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of repair/service records that include pre- and post-service configuration baselines, signed verification of authenticity post-repair, and tamper-evidence status; count of service events lacking configuration verification.
data_signals: service/repair tickets with configuration baseline snapshots (pre/post)
signed verification records from technicians
tamper-evidence inspection logs
serial number / firmware version checks before/after service
supplier or repair-provider certificates of authenticity
overdue or missing verification exception logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured repair-ticket data with pre/post configuration snapshots and signed verification entries linked to asset IDs.
Weak: ad-hoc notes or unstructured emails claiming verification without timestamps or asset linkage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement to control configuration during service/repair is preventive — ensuring items are not compromised during maintenance.

SA-19(2).differentiation_note: Targets configuration verification and tamper-evidence for repair/service workflows, unlike scanning or disposal which address intake or end-of-life handling.

SA-19(3).measurement_goal: Measure disposal controls for components: percent of disposed components with documented disposal records and chain-of-custody evidence, and count of disposal incidents lacking verified destruction or reassignment.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Disposal is a preventive/control activity where coverage percentage of disposed assets with proper documentation is the primary evidence; counts and trends capture exceptions and improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of disposed components with signed disposal records (method, date, witness), chain-of-custody documentation, and certificates of destruction or re-provisioning; count of disposals missing required documentation.
data_signals: disposal tickets/records with asset IDs and disposal method
certificate of destruction or transfer documents
chain-of-custody logs and timestamps
witness or approver signatures
inventory reconciliation showing removal
exceptions for undocumented disposals
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed, timestamped disposal records and certificates of destruction tied to asset IDs and inventory reconciliation.
Weak: verbal approvals or undocumented removals from inventory without supporting disposal evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Disposal controls are preventive measures to ensure end-of-life components are handled to prevent misuse or introduction of unauthentic parts.

SA-19(3).differentiation_note: Focused on end-of-life handling and documentary proof of disposal, distinct from training, scanning, or repair/configuration controls.

SA-19(4).measurement_goal: Measure anti-counterfeit scanning operations: percentage of incoming critical components scanned, count of scans that flagged potential counterfeit indicators, and mean time to quarantine on detection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive scanning coverage is best expressed as a percentage of incoming/critical components scanned; counts of positive detections and quarantine rates provide operational evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of incoming critical components scanned with anti-counterfeit tools, count of scan results flagged as suspicious, timestamps from scan logs to quarantine action to measure time-to-quarantine, and scanner configuration/firmware versions.
data_signals: anti-counterfeit scan logs with asset serial numbers and timestamps
incoming shipment manifests linked to scan results
scan-positive detection records (flags, reasons)
quarantine actions and timestamps
scanner configuration and firmware version history
false-positive/false-negative review records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated scan logs tied to shipment manifests and immediate quarantine actions with timestamps; maintained scanner configuration and firmware records.
Weak: manual paper checklists or occasional ad-hoc scans without machine-readable logs or linkage to inventory.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Anti-counterfeit scanning is a preventive detection/control step to prevent counterfeit components entering the environment.

SA-19(4).differentiation_note: Targets operational scanning and detection processes (coverage, detections, time-to-quarantine), distinct from training, repair/configuration, or disposal activities.

SA-20.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of critical components that underwent customized development under an approved secure development process (documented SDLC approval, code review, and security testing) and the count of critical components deployed without required customized-development approval or evidence of secure development artifacts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SA-20 is preventive: auditors will want coverage/ compliance percentages (e.g., percent of critical components with approved customized-development artifacts). Counts and rates (number of non‑compliant components, rate of approvals) are useful secondary measures to show volume and velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of assets marked as 'critical components' in the component inventory that have: (a) a recorded customized-development approval or contract, (b) SDLC artifacts or development plan, (c) code repository provenance (commits/build IDs), and (d) security test/vulnerability scan results recorded before production deployment. Also produce the count of critical components deployed without one or more of these artifacts.
data_signals: critical_component_inventory entries with custom_development flag
change request / approval records or development contract indicating authorized customized development
SDLC artifact timestamps (requirements/design/test plans) linked to component ID
code repository commit logs and build provenance (build IDs, artifact hashes)
code review / pull request approval records with approver and timestamp
software bill of materials (SBOM) or component manifest for the component
vulnerability scan/test results for the component with timestamps
deployment manifests / production deployment timestamps linked to component ID
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous CI/CD provenance (commit → build ID → deploy) and SBOMs tied to each critical component plus automated code-review and scan results;
Weak: ad-hoc self-attestation statements, one-off manual approvals, or annual attestations without artifact-level linkage to component IDs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title focuses on governing 'Customized Development' of 'Critical Components', implying prevention through approved processes and artifacts rather than post‑event detection.

SA-20.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets governance and measurable approval/artifact coverage for customized development of critical components (approval records, SDLC artifacts, provenance), unlike sibling controls that may focus on general vulnerability scanning or runtime detection.

SA-21.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of developers and code-contributing personnel who have completed required screening/vetting before receiving access to code repositories and production systems, and count outstanding unscreened developers and any adverse findings that affect access.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive personnel control — auditors want coverage/percent compliance (percent of developers screened). Counts (unscreened developers, adverse findings) and trends (screening coverage over time) supplement the percentage to show volume and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) percentage of active developers with a completed and verified screening record prior to initial privileged code access; (2) count of active developers lacking screening or with pending/overdue screening; (3) count of adverse screening findings and resulting access denials or mitigations. Include timestamps for hire, screening completion, verification, and access provisioning.
data_signals: HR screening completion records with timestamps (pre-employment and periodic re-screening)
list of individuals in developer roles / developer access roster
access provisioning events for code repositories and production systems with timestamp and linked screening status
screening validation/verification artifacts (signed forms, background check results, identity verification IDs)
exceptions/waiver records for developers granted access without completed screening
adverse finding records and resulting access revocation or mitigation actions
time delta between hire/start date and screening completion timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR-system screening records tied to access-provisioning logs showing screening timestamp precedes access grant.
Strong: system-enforced gating that blocks repository access until screening flag is true.
Weak: periodic manual spreadsheets or one-off attestations without linkage to access logs or timestamps.
Weak: retrospective attestations without timestamped verification preceding access provisioning.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Named 'Developer Screening' and focused on vetting personnel prior to access — this is a preventive control to stop unvetted developers from obtaining access.

SA-21.differentiation_note: This entry is the primary screening/vetting control for developers — it requires measurable pre-access screening coverage and linkage between screening records and access provisioning, unlike its withdrawn companion which focused on validation detail.

SA-21(1).measurement_goal: Confirm that screening validation activities (verification that required screening was performed and documented) are recorded and that any validation responsibilities are integrated into SA-21 processes; measure the percentage of screened developer records that contain validation artifacts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive validation enhancement, percent coverage of validated screening records is most useful; counts of validation exceptions and comparisons to the parent control (SA-21) implementation are useful to show incorporation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure the percent of developer screening records that include explicit validation/verification artifacts (e.g., verifier identity, validation date, source of check) and the count of records flagged as 'validation missing' or 'validation incomplete'. If withdrawn, evidence should show these validation elements are present within SA-21 records.
data_signals: screening validation entries (verifier name, validation timestamp)
screening record metadata showing incorporation into SA-21 process
records labeled 'Withdrawn' or documentation noting 'incorporated into SA-21'
counts of screening records missing validation artifacts
exceptions or waiver records for validation steps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: explicit validation fields in screening records linked to SA-21 process and automated verification logs.
Strong: documented change history showing SA-21(1) controls were folded into SA-21 records and process flows.
Weak: textual note 'incorporated' without corresponding validated screening artifacts or linkage to SA-21 records.
Weak: absence of verifier identity/timestamp in screening records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is labeled preventive and focuses on validating screening, but the description states 'Withdrawn: incorporated into SA-21,' so it's not an independent active control.

SA-21(1).differentiation_note: This sub-control is withdrawn and its measurement is expected to be encompassed by SA-21; its unique need was explicit validation artifacts for screening records rather than the broader screening coverage measured for SA-21.

SA-22.measurement_goal: Measure the presence and handling of unsupported system components by reporting the percentage of assets flagged as unsupported, the count of unsupported components with active mitigation (remediation tickets, isolation, or approved exceptions), and the time-to-remediate or compensate for newly identified unsupported components.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage/compliance evidence (coverage % of assets with supported components); counts of unsupported items and remediation trend give operational context and show progress over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of total managed assets that are in an 'unsupported' state (asset metadata flag), the count of unsupported components with an associated remediation ticket or compensating-control record, and median days between unsupported identification and remediation/mitigation. Include asset criticality so measurement can be segmented by risk tier.
data_signals: asset inventory records with 'support_status' or 'unsupported' flag
component end_of_life / EOL date fields
vulnerability scan results mapped to component version (to show exposure on unsupported components)
remediation ticket IDs / timestamps linked to the unsupported component
approved exception or compensating-control records for unsupported components
network isolation or segmentation configuration entries for quarantined unsupported assets
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated asset inventory with explicit support_status and EOL fields is strong evidence;
vulnerability scan mappings that show CVE exposure on unsupported components strengthen evidence;
Ad-hoc spreadsheets or one-off manual lists are weak evidence because they lack automated linkage to remediation tickets and timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Name and intent ('Unsupported System Components') imply preventive controls to avoid use of unsupported components and require inventory/mitigation coverage.

SA-22.differentiation_note: This entry covers the broad requirement to identify, track, and mitigate unsupported components across the estate (inventory, remediation, exceptions), unlike the sibling SA-22(1) which focuses on alternative support sources for specific unsupported items.

SA-22(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of unsupported components that have documented alternative support arrangements or approved compensating controls, and the timeliness of establishing those arrangements after identification of unsupported status.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Because this sub-control (alternative support) is preventive in nature, a coverage percentage (unsupported components with alternative support) shows compliance; counts and trends show operational follow-through and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percent of identified unsupported components that have a recorded alternative support agreement or approved compensating-control record, the count of unsupported components without alternative support, and the elapsed days from unsupported identification to alternative support approval or compensating control implementation.
data_signals: unsupported component registry entries with 'alternative_support' contract reference
scanned asset list flagged as 'unsupported' with detection timestamp
signed alternative support agreements or vendor contract metadata
approved compensating controls or exception records with approval timestamps
remediation/mitigation implementation timestamps linked to the unsupported component
evidence_strength_indicators: Signed alternative-support contracts and timestamps showing rapid assignment after identification are strong evidence;
Documented but unsigned vendor statements or verbal commitments are weak evidence;
Because the control text is 'Withdrawn: incorporated into SA-22', reliance on centralized SA-22 inventory and exception records is stronger than isolated artifacts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input labels the control as preventive and the title refers to providing alternative support to avoid risk from unsupported components; however the description notes it is withdrawn and incorporated into SA-22.

SA-22(1).differentiation_note: SA-22(1) specifically targets the existence of alternative/vendor support or compensating arrangements for unsupported components (contractual evidence), whereas SA-22 covers identification and remediation tracking more broadly; note SA-22(1) is withdrawn and its requirements are expected to be recorded under SA-22 artifacts.

SA-23.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of roles requiring specialization that have assigned personnel meeting documented specialization requirements (certifications, training, demonstrated skills), and track exceptions/waivers and recertification currency to show ongoing compliance with specialization requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (verifying coverage of required specialization), so percentage metrics (coverage %) best show compliance; counts (number of qualified personnel, exceptions) and trends (recertification over time) provide supporting context and scoring can aggregate multi-factor qualification status.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percentage of specialized roles that currently have assigned personnel who meet documented specialization requirements (match on required certification/training IDs and non-expired credentials). Also measure count of active waivers/exceptions and average time since last recertification for those in specialized roles.
data_signals: position descriptions listing required specialization or certification IDs
HR role assignment records linking employee IDs to specialized roles
training completion records with course IDs and completion timestamps
certification documents and expiration dates (credential metadata)
recertification / continuing education timestamps per employee
exception/waiver records with approval date and expiry
access entitlement mappings showing role-based privileges tied to specialized roles
last verification/audit timestamp for specialization compliance per role
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR/training system records showing current certification IDs, non-expired credentials, and role bindings; continuous directory mappings linking role to entitlement.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or one-off attestations without timestamps or expirations, or annual manual attestations only.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Specialization' implies establishing qualified/specialized personnel to prevent incorrect assignment or operation, which is preventive in nature.

SA-23.differentiation_note: Unlike configuration or logging controls, this control uniquely requires measurement of personnel qualifications and assignment coverage (training/certification currency and exception tracking) rather than technical configuration or event detection; measurement focuses on HR/training artifacts.

SC-1.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of formally documented policies and procedures: the percentage of required policy documents that exist, are approved, and have completed their scheduled reviews within the organization-defined review period, plus the count of exceptions or overdue documents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Policy controls are preventive — auditors need coverage and currency ratios (percentages) to show policy existence and compliance; counts of documents and trending of review timeliness support investigation and improvement tracking.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure: (1) total count of required policy/procedure documents vs. count of documents present in the policy repository; (2) percentage of those documents with a recorded approval and a review timestamp within the organization-defined review cycle; (3) count and list of documents overdue for review or with approved exceptions. Capture document identifiers, owners, approver IDs, version history, and review dates to allow auditor queries by policy type and owner.
data_signals: policy document repository record (document id, title)
document version history and timestamps (created, last_modified, last_review_date)
document approval metadata (approver id, approval timestamp, approval record)
document owner/responsible party field
policy review schedule or review frequency field (policy-defined review_period)
exceptions or waiver records (exception id, approver, expiration date)
distribution/acknowledgement records (who was notified / who acknowledged policy)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized policy repository with machine-readable metadata (approval timestamps, owner, review schedule) and automated review reminders and acknowledgement logs; ability to query historical versions and review events.
Weak: ad-hoc file shares or isolated PDF copies without approval timestamps, manual emails as proof of approval, or single point-in-time screenshots of a document.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control concerns establishing and maintaining policies and procedures, which is preventive by design (avoids or constrains insecure behavior) — indicated by terms 'Policy' and 'Procedures' in the control name.

SC-1.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on documentation, approval and review timeliness metrics (policy lifecycle measurement) rather than technical enforcement controls; its unique need is measuring policy coverage, owner assignment, and review currency rather than monitoring system events.

SC-2.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of system-level functions and interfaces that are isolated from user-level functionality and the count of user-initiated actions that reach system-level functionality (privilege boundary violations).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control where auditors want percentage coverage (e.g., % of interfaces that enforce separation). Counts and trends supplement by showing number of violations and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of interfaces and functions classified as 'system' that have enforcement controls preventing direct invocation by normal user accounts; count of user-originated requests that invoked system-level functions within a measurement window; inventory of interfaces labeled system vs user with enforced access control settings.
data_signals: interface inventory with privilege classification (system vs user)
access control lists / RBAC policies for each interface or API
audit logs of user-originated requests containing target function identifier and caller privilege
privilege escalation / unauthorized-invocation event counts
configuration records showing enforcement settings (e.g., management port ACLs, admin-only flags)
results of automated configuration scans identifying exposed system functions
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, continuous mapping of interface inventory to enforced access control policies and live audit logs is strong evidence.
Point-in-time screenshots, manual attestations, or undocumented checklists are weak evidence because they don't show ongoing enforcement or runtime blocking of user requests.

affinity_keywords: Separation of System and User Functionality

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control's title focuses on separation (a design/configuration activity) which prevents improper access to system functions.

SC-2.differentiation_note: This top-level SC-2 entry defines measurement of overall separation of system vs user functions (broad inventory and enforcement coverage), whereas sub-controls focus on specific interface or disassociation behaviors.

SC-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of exposed interfaces (UI, API, ports, services) that are limited to non-privileged functionality and the count of interfaces that inadvertently expose system-level operations to non-privileged users.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive augmentation is best evidenced by coverage percentages (how many interfaces are correctly limited), with counts of offending interfaces and distribution by system/component for prioritization.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of public or user-facing interfaces that are configured to expose only non-privileged functionality; count of interfaces where configuration or logs show system-level operations callable by non-privileged users; inventory of interface exposure by port/service/API with privilege label.
data_signals: exposed interface inventory (service name, port, API endpoint) with assigned privilege level
API gateway / WAF policies showing allowed operations per caller role
access control configuration for UI/management endpoints
audit logs showing API/UI calls that invoked system-level endpoints and caller identity
scan results flagging management interfaces reachable from user networks
policy mapping of interface -> allowed privilege set
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated discovery of interfaces with policy-enforced role restrictions and continuous audit logs showing zero system-level invocations by non-privileged users is strong evidence.
