An occasional manual inventory or single penetration-test finding is weak evidence because it may miss transient exposures or newly deployed interfaces.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The clause explicitly constrains interfaces for non-privileged users, indicating configuration and design controls to prevent exposure.

SC-2(1).differentiation_note: SC-2(1) narrows measurement to the exposure and enforcement of user-facing interfaces, unlike SC-2 which covers the broader separation of all system vs user functionality.

SC-2(2).measurement_goal: Measure the ability to disassociate or disable system-level functionality from user-facing components (e.g., count and success rate of disassociation actions, existence and coverage of feature flags/isolations enabling disassociation).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Disassociability is a preventive / resilience attribute best reflected as percent coverage of components that support safe disassociation and the rate/success of disassociation operations during tests or incidents.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of system components that support disassociation (e.g., feature flags, modular isolation) and the success rate of planned disassociation actions in test or real events; time to disassociate a system function when requested.
data_signals: inventory of components with disassociation controls (feature flags, module isolation, hot-disable capability)
test run results for disassociation procedures (pass/fail, timestamp)
audit logs of disassociation operations and operator identity
time-to-disassociate measurements from tests or incidents
configuration records showing isolation or decoupling settings
failed disassociation attempt counts and error reasons
evidence_strength_indicators: Regular, logged disassociation tests with measurable success rates and timestamps mapped to the component inventory are strong evidence.
Documentation describing theoretical disassociability without test evidence or only one-off manual exercises is weak evidence.

affinity_keywords: Disassociability of system and user functionality

control_type_classification.reasoning: The focus on making functions disassociable is a preventive design/control characteristic intended to avoid undesired coupling of user and system functions.

SC-2(2).differentiation_note: SC-2(2) specifically measures the capability to decouple or disable system functions (disassociability) and requires testable disassociation evidence, unlike SC-2 which measures general separation and SC-2(1) which focuses on interface exposure.

SC-3.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and enforcement of isolation for defined security functions: percent of security functions deployed in isolated environments (dedicated hosts, VMs, or containers), count of instances where non-security functionality co-resides with security functions, and number of exceptions to isolation policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SC-3 is preventive: auditors need coverage percentages (what percent of security functions are isolated) and counts of exceptions; trends show improvement or regressions over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of enumerated security functions that run on isolated hosts/environments vs total security functions, list of hosts/containers with both security and non-security processes, and exception records showing approved deviations. Include host identifiers, environment type, and timestamp of last verification.
data_signals: inventory of security functions and assigned host/container IDs
host/container configuration records indicating dedicated vs shared role
process/service lists on hosts showing presence of non-security services
isolation exception and approval tickets with timestamps
configuration management database (CMDB) tags for 'security-function' and 'shared/isolated'
periodic verification scan results showing co-residence of processes
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated CMDB/asset inventory showing up-to-date isolation tags and continuous verification scans (strong).
Single architecture diagram or one-off manual attestations without inventory correlation (weak).
Approved exception tickets tied to compensating controls with expiry dates (moderate).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Security Function Isolation' implies preventing interference by separating security functions, which is a preventive design/control.

SC-3.differentiation_note: This overarching control focuses on measuring coverage and exceptions across all security functions, whereas the SC-3 enhancements specify the particular mechanisms (hardware, flow control, modularity, layering) that realize isolation.

SC-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of security functions hosted on dedicated hardware or hardware-isolated environments and count of physical/hosting configurations that violate hardware separation requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Hardware separation is preventive and best evidenced by coverage percentages (dedicated hardware coverage) and counts of noncompliant hosts over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of listed security function instances running on dedicated physical hosts or hardware-isolated partitions versus total security function instances; list of hosts where hardware separation is not implemented with host IDs, hardware type, and justification; timestamps of last host role verification.
data_signals: host inventory with hardware classification (physical vs virtual) and role tags
asset-to-function mapping showing which security functions are assigned to which hardware
hypervisor/firmware configuration records indicating partitioning or hardware-based isolation
exceptions/waivers for shared hardware with approval and expiry
periodic host role verification scan results
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated reconciliation between asset inventory and function assignments confirming dedicated hardware (strong).
Manual lists or unsynchronized spreadsheets claiming dedicated hardware without verification (weak).
Approved waivers tied to compensating controls and revalidation dates (moderate).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Hardware Separation' indicates preventive controls that place security functions on separate hardware to prevent interference.

SC-3(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement narrows measurement to physical/hardware-level separation (hosts, partitions, firmware) rather than higher-level architectural or procedural isolation.

SC-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure deployment and enforcement of access/flow-control security functions: percent of traffic flows covered by access/flow-control enforcement points, count of flows bypassing controls, and number of security functions implementing flow-control as designed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control should be measured by coverage percentages of flows under enforcement and counts of bypasses; distribution metrics help show where flows are concentrated.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of defined network/application flows that traverse access/flow-control functions (firewalls, ACLs, proxies, microsegmentation), number of observed flows that bypass controls, and inventory of devices implementing access/flow-control with configuration snapshots and enforcement status.
data_signals: network flow logs (source/destination/port/protocol) correlated with enforcement device policies
firewall/ACL/proxy rule coverage mapping to defined flows
inventory of access/flow-control endpoints and their configuration versions
alerts or logs indicating flow bypass or failed enforcement
policy-to-implementation mapping documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous flow log correlation showing all required flows inspected/enforced (strong).
Static rule lists without flow-log validation (weak).
Periodic pen-testing showing bypasses but lacking continuous detection (moderate).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The inclusion of 'Access and Flow Control Functions' indicates preventive measures that enforce traffic and access segregation.

SC-3(2).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on measurement of traffic/access enforcement coverage and bypass detection, unlike sibling controls that emphasize hardware or module structure.

SC-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure the degree to which security-function hosts/components run only required security functionality: percent of security-function hosts with non-security services disabled, and count of unauthorized/nonessential services found on security hosts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive emphasis on minimizing nonsecurity functionality is best measured via coverage percentages (hosts compliant) and counts of offending services, with trends indicating improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of security hosts/containers where only approved security services/processes are running, list of nonapproved service/process instances on security hosts with host ID, process name, and detection timestamp, plus records of configuration hardening baselines applied.
data_signals: process/service inventory on security-designated hosts
approved service whitelist per host or role
configuration hardening baseline application records
system management/patching logs showing services disabled
discoveries from vulnerability scans highlighting extraneous services
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated continuous process inventories and enforcement of whitelists (strong).
Ad-hoc manual host inspections or single-time hardened images without drift detection (weak).
Hardening baselines plus periodic scans detecting drifts (moderate).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Minimize Nonsecurity Functionality' implies preventing nonessential functions from co-locating with security functions, a preventive control.

SC-3(3).differentiation_note: This enhancement requires inventory- and configuration-level measurements of running processes and whitelists on security hosts, distinct from hardware separation or flow-control coverage.

SC-3(4).measurement_goal: Measure modularity and coupling of security components: number of modules violating coupling thresholds, distribution of inter-module calls per module pair, and presence of high-cohesion indicators per module.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: While preventive in intent, measuring coupling is often count/distribution-oriented (counts of high-coupling dependencies and distributions of inter-module calls); trends show architectural improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of modules/components exceeding defined coupling thresholds (e.g., high number of external calls), dependency graph edges between security and non-security modules, and cohesion indicators such as proportion of intra-module calls vs inter-module calls with timestamps and code version.
data_signals: module/component dependency graphs from build artifacts or static analysis
counts of inter-module API calls and call-frequency matrices
code-repository module ownership and size metrics
design documents indicating intended module cohesiveness
change records showing refactors or modularization efforts
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated static analysis and dependency graphs aligned with coding standards (strong).
High-level architecture diagrams without artifact-backed dependency data (weak).
Periodic measurements after major releases with visible reduction in coupling (moderate).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The term 'Module Coupling and Cohesiveness' targets architectural design choices that prevent insecure tightly-coupled implementations, a preventive design control.

SC-3(4).differentiation_note: This enhancement requires software-architecture and code-level dependency metrics (coupling/cohesion) rather than host/infrastructure isolation measurements.

SC-3(5).measurement_goal: Measure presence and enforcement of layered security structures: count of required layers implemented for each security function, percentage of functions with complete layer stacks, and instances where layering is bypassed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Layering is preventive and best measured by percent coverage of required layers per function and counts of missing or bypassed layers; comparisons show alignment to policy-defined stacks.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of security functions for which the organization has implemented the required layered defenses (e.g., isolation layer, access/flow-control layer, monitoring/logging layer), count of functions with missing layers, and records of bypass incidents with timestamps and compensating controls.
data_signals: policy-defined layering checklist per security function
implementation evidence per layer (network segmentation configs, host hardening baselines, enforcement device configs, logging pipeline links)
mapping of security functions to implemented layers in CMDB
incidents/alerts showing bypass of expected layers
architecture diagrams showing layer placement
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated mapping showing each function's implemented layers with live validation (strong).
High-level diagrams asserting layered design without implementation artifacts (weak).
Periodic architecture reviews with partial verification artifacts (moderate).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement 'Layered Structures' prescribes architectural layering to prevent single-point failures, which is preventive by nature.

SC-3(5).differentiation_note: This enhancement is about verifying multi-layer architectural defenses are in place for each security function (layer coverage), rather than host separation or module cohesion.

SC-4.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of shared system resources that enforce separation controls (isolation, labeling, ACL/MAC) and count cross-domain or cross-tenant information flows or accesses that violate separation policies; report configuration compliance and detected unauthorized access events over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SC-4 is a preventive control about enforcing separation on shared resources; percentage coverage (e.g., percent of resources with enforced isolation/labels) shows coverage, while counts/rates of cross-domain accesses and distribution of enforcement states provide tactical evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of shared resources (VMs, containers, storage volumes, network segments) with enforced separation controls (ACLs, MAC labels, tenant tags) and count of detected cross-domain/cross-tenant access events over a reporting window. Include configuration timestamps, resource identifiers, enforcement policy identifiers, and enforcement outcomes (allowed/blocked).
data_signals: inventory of shared system resources with tenant/owner tags (resource_id, type, tenant_id)
configuration records showing isolation settings (ACL entries, MAC policy bindings, VM/container isolation flags)
access control enforcement logs indicating denied/allowed cross-domain accesses (timestamp, source_principal, target_resource, decision)
security labeling / MLS labels applied to resources (label name, label value, apply timestamp)
cross-tenant or cross-security-level data flow logs (flow start/end, source tenant, destination tenant, policy violation flag)
policy-to-resource mapping (which resources are required to have separation controls and their policy thresholds)
configuration drift/scan results showing resources out-of-compliance (resource_id, drift_reason, last_scan_timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated continuous configuration scans and enforcement logs showing live policy decisions and 100% mapping between policy and resource assignment.
Strong: MAC/MLS enforcement logs that block cross-level flows with timestamps and policy identifiers.
Weak: one-time manual spreadsheets listing intended isolation without logs of enforcement or automated scans.
Weak: ad-hoc penetration test reports alone that do not show ongoing enforcement or coverage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires preventing improper information flows in shared resources (enforcing separation/isolation), which is characteristic of preventive controls.

SC-4.differentiation_note: This entry covers broad enforcement and monitoring of separation on shared resources (coverage, configuration, and enforcement logs) — sibling controls narrow the focus to specific security levels or multilevel processing.

SC-4(1).measurement_goal: Because SC-4(1) is withdrawn and incorporated into SC-4, measure enforcement of security-level requirements as part of SC-4: percent of resources correctly labeled and enforcing security-level separation policy and count of accesses violating security-level constraints.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The withdrawn enhancement concerned 'Security Levels' — evidence should demonstrate coverage/percentage of resources with security-level enforcement; counts/rates of violations supply complementary detective evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of resources with assigned security-level labels and active enforcement of those labels (mapping of label -> enforcement rule), plus counts of attempted or successful accesses that violate security-level restrictions, including timestamps and source principals.
data_signals: resource security-level label assignments (resource_id, security_level, label_timestamp)
policy rules mapping security levels to allowed principals/actions
access enforcement logs indicating security-level checks (decision, reason, timestamp)
"Withdrawn: incorporated into SC-4." (control metadata indicating incorporation)
audit records showing remediation or reclassification actions for mis-labeled resources
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated label inventories and enforcement logs tied to policy rules with near real-time alerts for violations.
Weak: a text note stating 'incorporated into SC-4' without operational evidence of labeling or enforcement.

affinity_keywords: "Information in Shared System Resources | Security Levels"

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement targets prevention via security-level enforcement; classification is preventive though the entry is withdrawn and folded into SC-4.

SC-4(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement is withdrawn and specifically addressed security-level labeling/enforcement; its measurement is a scoped subset of SC-4 focusing on labels/security-level mappings.

SC-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure configuration and enforcement of multilevel or periods processing controls: percent of systems configured for required multilevel processing, counts of cross-level processing operations, and number of policy violations or declassification events against defined rules.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Multilevel/periods processing is preventive (correct configuration required); percentage coverage of systems configured for multilevel processing is primary, with counts of cross-level events and trends in violations as secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of applicable systems configured with multilevel processing controls or period-based separation (system_id, mlp_enabled flag, configuration timestamp), counts of cross-level processing attempts and declassification events (timestamp, origin_level, destination_level, decision), and trend of violations over time.
data_signals: system configuration records indicating multilevel/periods processing enabled (system_id, mlp_config, last_change)
access and process execution logs showing origin security level and target security level (process_id, user, origin_level, target_level, outcome)
declassification and sanitization event logs (event_id, resource_id, declassify_reason, approver, timestamp)
policy definitions for multilevel or periods processing (policy_id, allowed_flows, time_periods)
audit findings showing exceptions or waivers for multilevel processing
trend series of violation counts per period (daily/weekly)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system configuration inventories tied to enforcement logs that show policy decisions in real time and recorded declassification approvals.
Strong: continuous monitoring of process-level security labels and automated blocking of unauthorized cross-level processing.
Weak: ad-hoc documentation of intended multilevel processing without enforcement artifacts or logs of declassification.

affinity_keywords: "Information in Shared System Resources | Multilevel or Periods Processing"

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement focuses on preventing improper multilevel or period-based processing by requiring configurations and controls that block unauthorized cross-level processing.

SC-4(2).differentiation_note: SC-4(2) specifically targets multilevel/periods processing controls and declassification events, so measurements must capture label-aware process execution and declassification workflows rather than general resource isolation metrics.

SC-5.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and operational status of DoS protections for internet-facing and critical services: percentage of internet-facing assets routed through DDoS mitigation or rate-limiting controls, count of unprotected internet-facing endpoints, and number of DoS incidents detected and mitigated within defined thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SC-5 is primarily preventive (protecting services). Percentage measures (coverage of assets protected) show compliance with configuration/coverage requirements; counts and trends supplement with incident frequency and operational trend data.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of internet-facing and critical assets covered by DoS mitigation (on-box ACLs, scrubbing services, rate-limits) and count of DoS-related incidents with mitigation activation timestamps and resolution status; include provider mitigation agreements and configuration change timestamps to show active protection.
data_signals: inventory of internet-facing assets with protection tagging
network routing/ASN records showing traffic routed to scrubbing service
firewall/edge ACL and rate-limit configuration snapshots and change timestamps
DoS mitigation activation logs (time of activation, duration)
DoS incident logs with timestamps and mitigation outcome
provider mitigation/SLA documentation and effective dates
post-incident mitigation reports
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated routing and mitigation activation logs correlated to asset inventory are strong evidence.
One-time configuration screenshots or policy documents without recent activation/validation are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title emphasizes 'Protection', indicating preventative measures to block or reduce DoS impact.

SC-5.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall DoS protection posture (coverage and operational status) rather than the specific mechanisms (egress restrictions, capacity planning, or detection) addressed by the enhancements.

SC-5(1).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of systems and network egress points covered by controls that prevent outbound abuse: percentage of hosts and edge devices with egress filtering, rate-limiting, or outbound anomaly blocking in place; and count of outbound flows blocked as potential attack traffic.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control (restricting ability to attack other systems). Percentage coverage (hosts/egress points with egress controls) directly shows compliance; counts/rates of blocked outbound events provide operational evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of edge firewalls, routers, and host egress policies that implement outbound filtering/rate-limits and the count of outbound connections blocked/ratelimited per period; include timestamps of policy deployment and automated blocking events tied to host identifiers.
data_signals: egress firewall ACL configuration inventory with deployment timestamps
host-level outbound filtering/EDR egress policy records
blocked outbound connection logs (timestamp, source host, destination IP/port, rule triggered)
outbound traffic baseline and anomaly detection alerts
incident tickets or abuse reports citing internal hosts used for attacks
change history showing deployment of egress restrictions
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous firewall/EDR logs showing automated blocks correlated to host inventory are strong evidence.
Policy documents without deployed ACLs or absent blocking logs are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly targets restricting the 'Ability to Attack Other Systems', a preventive configuration and policy control.

SC-5(1).differentiation_note: Focuses on outbound/egress controls to prevent internal hosts from being used to attack others — measurement centers on egress filtering and blocked outbound events, unlike the general coverage or detection-focused siblings.

SC-5(2).measurement_goal: Measure infrastructure capacity and redundancy prepared for DoS: available scrubbing/bandwidth capacity vs. observed peak traffic, count of redundant upstream providers/routes, and results of failover/resilience tests showing service continuity under simulated load.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive/resilience control requires measuring coverage/capacity (percent of required capacity/resilience achieved). Trends and counts (failover events/tests) show operational readiness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Ratio or percentage of required mitigation/scrubbing/bandwidth capacity provisioned versus policy-required capacity and count of independent upstream providers/routes; include timestamps and outcomes of failover/load tests and measured peak traffic vs provisioned capacity.
data_signals: provisioned bandwidth/scrubbing capacity per service (Gbps) with effective dates
measured peak inbound traffic (per service/time window)
number of independent upstream providers/BGP peerings per service
failover and load test results with timestamps and success criteria
SLA or contract capacity/commitment documents
historical saturation/overload incidents and duration
evidence_strength_indicators: Recent automated capacity measurements and successful failover/load test results are strong evidence.
Plans or contracts without measurement of actual peak traffic or without executed failover tests are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement emphasizes capacity, bandwidth, and redundancy — configuration and provisioning actions aimed at preventing service disruption.

SC-5(2).differentiation_note: This entry targets capacity and resilience measurements (bandwidth and redundancy) rather than coverage of protective controls or detection metrics handled by sibling controls.

SC-5(3).measurement_goal: Measure detection coverage and effectiveness for DoS events: rate of DoS detections per attack event (detection rate), mean time to detect (MTTD) DoS conditions, count of DoS-related alerts, and false positive rate of DoS alerts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control (detection and monitoring). Rates (detection rate, MTTD expressed as a rate) and counts (alerts) are most useful to quantify detection effectiveness and timeliness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Detection rate and latency: number of DoS/anomalous traffic detections per known DoS event or per monitoring period, mean time from anomalous traffic onset to alert (MTTD), and counts of alerts with disposition (true positive/false positive). Include monitoring coverage (what prefixes/services are observed) and rule versions/timestamps.
data_signals: network flow/NetFlow/PCAP summaries indicating anomalous traffic patterns
IDS/IPS/monitoring DoS alert logs with timestamps and rule IDs
MTTD measurements (anomaly onset timestamp vs alert timestamp)
alert disposition records (true positive, false positive)
monitoring coverage inventory (monitored prefixes, ports, services)
detection rule/version and deployment timestamps
correlation of alerts to mitigation activation logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous telemetry (flow logs, IDS alerts) with disposition and timestamps correlated to mitigation actions is strong evidence.
Ad-hoc or manual detection reports without continuous telemetry or disposition data are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly names 'Detection and Monitoring', a classic detective function focused on alerting and telemetry.

SC-5(3).differentiation_note: Specifically measures detection coverage and timeliness (MTTD, alert rates) for DoS — distinct from SC-5's coverage focus and SC-5(2)'s capacity/resilience measurements.

SC-6.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of critical services and resources meeting defined availability targets (uptime/SLA), track outage counts and total outage duration, and evidence redundancy and successful failover testing to demonstrate continued resource availability.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Availability requirements are typically expressed as coverage or uptime targets (percent), while rates (outage frequency) and trends (uptime over time) provide complementary views for SLA compliance and operational improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute uptime percentage per service/resource over the audit period, count and total duration of unplanned outages per service, proportion of resources with redundancy configured (e.g., multi-AZ/replica count), and pass/fail results of scheduled failover tests. Include timestamps, affected resource identifiers, and SLA/target definitions so results can be compared to organizational thresholds.
data_signals: service uptime percentage from monitoring probes / synthetic transactions (per service, per region)
incident/outage records with start and end timestamps and impacted resource IDs
SLA or availability target definitions per service (policy threshold values)
redundancy/configuration inventory showing replicas, zones, backups, and failover configuration
failover and disaster recovery test results and timestamps (pass/fail, test playbook references)
capacity utilization and resource exhaustion events (CPU/memory/disk/network throttle events)
autoscaling and recovery event logs (scale-up/scale-down, instance replacement)
mean time to restore (MTTR) per outage and count of outages per period
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous telemetry (synthetic transactions and heartbeat monitors) and automated incident logs provide strong, high-confidence evidence
Recorded, repeatable failover test artifacts (scripts, results, timestamps) are stronger than a single ad-hoc test
Inconsistent or manual, point-in-time reports (one-off screenshots, annual summaries) are weak evidence for ongoing availability

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title indicates ensuring availability and resilience (preventive measures like redundancy and capacity planning) though no description was provided, so preventive is the best fit.

SC-6.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets continuous availability and resilience (uptime/SLA, redundancy, failover testing), unlike sibling controls that might focus on configuration hardening, patching, or access — measurement must emphasize uptime telemetry and failover test evidence.

SC-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of network boundaries (firewalls, gateways, proxies) that enforce approved boundary controls and the count of unauthorized inbound/outbound flows blocked by those controls over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SC-7 is primarily preventive (boundary controls and coverage); a coverage percentage indicates how much of the environment is protected. Counts and trends supplement with event volumes and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of perimeter and internal network boundary devices configured with approved rulebases and controls, plus counts of blocked unauthorized flows and number of approved exceptions (with expirations). Include per-boundary device rule default policy (deny/allow), last configuration change timestamp, and exception records.
data_signals: firewall rulebase exports (deny/allow/default policy, rule counts)
network ACL configurations with last-change timestamps
edge router/gateway routing and NAT tables
proxy configuration and egress filtering rules
network flow logs (NetFlow/IPFIX) showing blocked vs allowed flows
approved exception list with approver and expiry timestamps
boundary device inventory with device role and coverage mapping
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated exports of firewall/ACL configurations and flow logs mapped to an authoritative boundary device inventory are strong evidence.
Point-in-time screenshots or manual checklists are weak evidence because they don't prove ongoing enforcement.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on enforcing protections at network boundaries (preventing unauthorized flows), which is a preventive function.

SC-7.differentiation_note: This is the general boundary protection control covering perimeter and internal boundaries; its measurement focuses on overall coverage and block counts rather than specific subtopics like split tunneling or proxies.

SC-7(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical systems placed in physically separated subnetworks (or defined segmentation zones) and the count of unauthorized inter-subnetwork flows or exceptions allowing cross-segment traffic.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive segmentation control, percentage coverage of required segmentation is most useful; counts of cross-segment exceptions provide operational context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets classified as 'critical' that are mapped to physically separated subnetworks or segmentation zones per the segmentation policy, plus counts of inter-subnet ACL exceptions and timestamps of segmentation changes or tests.
data_signals: network segmentation inventory (VLAN/VRF mappings) by asset
physical network design and rack/cage mappings for data centers
inter-VLAN/zone ACLs and exception records with approvals
segmentation test/validation results (isolation test logs)
last-change timestamps for segmentation configurations
unauthorized cross-segment flow detections from flow logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated inventories linking asset criticality to segmentation plus isolation test logs are strong evidence.
High-level architectural diagrams without testing or inventory mappings are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires physical/network separation to prevent unauthorized access, a preventive measure; the name explicitly references separation.

SC-7(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on physical/network segmentation coverage for critical assets (segmentation mapping and isolation test evidence) rather than general perimeter controls.

SC-7(2).measurement_goal: Measure the count and percentage of externally reachable services that have approved boundary controls (WAF, edge ACLs, rate limiting) and the count of internet-exposed services not on the approved public service inventory.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control for managing public access; percentage of internet-exposed services covered by controls (and listed in inventory) is the primary proof, supplemented by counts of unapproved exposures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of internet-facing services that appear in the authorized public service inventory and have required boundary controls (WAF, edge ACLs) configured; counts of externally exposed IP/ports discovered by scanning that are not authorized.
data_signals: internet-reachable service inventory (DNS records, public IP list)
external port/protocol exposure scan results
WAF configuration and rule coverage for public services
edge firewall ACLs for public-facing subnets
external vulnerability scan results for public services
approved public service exceptions with expirations
Withdrawn: incorporated into SC-7
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated external scanning correlated with an authoritative public service inventory and WAF/firewall configs is strong evidence.
A static inventory list without external scanning or WAF logs is weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control is about limiting and governing public access to services (preventing unauthorized exposure).

SC-7(2).differentiation_note: Targets externally reachable/public services specifically — measurement requires external scans, WAF/proxy configurations and authoritative public-service inventory matching.

SC-7(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of network access points (VPN endpoints, Wi‑Fi APs, remote gateways, physical access links) that are configured with approved boundary controls and the count of unauthorized or rogue access points detected.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage of access points; percent coverage indicates how many access points meet required controls, while counts show rogue/unauthorized instances.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of configured access points (VPN endpoints, Wi‑Fi APs, remote gateways) that enforce boundary controls (802.1X, approved SSIDs, VPN split-tunnel settings) and counts of rogue/unauthorized APs discovered by scanning with timestamps and remediation status.
data_signals: VPN endpoint inventory and configuration (split tunneling, auth method)
wireless AP inventory with SSID, encryption, 802.1X status
rogue AP detection scan logs
RADIUS/802.1X authentication logs
open port and service listings on access devices
exception/approval records for non-standard AP configurations
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous discovery scans and centralized configuration reporting for access devices are strong evidence.
Ad-hoc manual inventories or occasional walk-throughs are weak evidence of ongoing control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on ensuring access points are configured to prevent unauthorized entry, a preventive activity.

SC-7(3).differentiation_note: Concentrates on access point devices (VPN, Wi‑Fi, gateways) and detection of rogue/unauthorized points, unlike general perimeter devices.

SC-7(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of external telecommunications services (leased lines, carrier services, managed circuits) that have documented security requirements (encryption, boundary controls) and the count of services lacking required contractual security clauses.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive controls for external telecom require verifying contractual and configuration coverage; percentage of services meeting requirements is primary, counts highlight gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of external telecommunications services mapped in an inventory that have required security configurations (encryption in transit, dedicated circuit isolation) and signed contractual/security SLAs; counts of services without documented security controls or contracts.
data_signals: external telecommunications service inventory (carrier, circuit ID, endpoints)
service contracts/SLA records with security clauses
encryption-in-transit configuration evidence for circuits
third-party access lists and privileged access records
traffic measurements showing use of telecom links
exceptions/waivers for telecom services lacking controls
evidence_strength_indicators: Signed contracts with explicit security clauses and technical configuration evidence (encryption, dedicated VLANs) are strong evidence.
Informal provider emails or verbal agreements without documented config are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires management and configuration of external telecom services to prevent insecure connections, a preventive control.

SC-7(4).differentiation_note: Targets third-party telecommunications services and contractual/configuration coverage rather than internal boundary devices or proxies.

SC-7(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of boundary devices enforcing a deny-by-default posture and the count of active allow-by-exception rules (with approvals and expirations) and the number of flows permitted by exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive intent (deny-by-default) suggests measuring percent of devices enforcing default-deny; counts of exceptions and permitted flows indicate policy deviations.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of firewalls/gateways configured with deny-by-default and the number and status of allow-by-exception rules including approver, justification, and expiration; count of flows observed that match exception rules.
data_signals: firewall/gateway default policy flag (deny/allow)
allow-by-exception rule records with approver and expiry
audit logs of rule creation/approval
network flow logs showing traffic permitted by exception rules
exception renewal/expiration history
rule change history
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated rulebase exports showing default-deny and machine-readable exception records with expiry and rights are strong evidence.
Manual spreadsheets of exceptions without linkage to rulebases and flow logs are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Explicitly about enforcing a deny-by-default posture and controlling exceptions, which is a preventive policy and configuration control.

SC-7(5).differentiation_note: Specifically measures enforcement of a deny-by-default policy and the lifecycle of allow-by-exception rules, unlike other SC-7 variants focused on device types or services.

SC-7(6).measurement_goal: Measure the mean time to detect and remediate boundary device failures and the percentage of boundary devices with automated failover or documented response procedures that were executed during incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control concerns response to recognized failures (corrective activity); MTTR and incident response rates are primary measures of effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate-based measures: MTTR for boundary device failures (time from failure detection to restoration), count of failure incidents, and percentage of devices with tested automated failover or documented response executed during incidents.
data_signals: boundary device failure incident logs/tickets with timestamps (detection, remediation start, resolution)
automatic failover test results and schedules
configuration snapshots showing redundancy/HA settings
post-incident reports describing executed response procedures
SLA/response-time targets for boundary device availability
evidence_strength_indicators: Integrated incident/ticket timestamps and automated failover test logs are strong evidence of response capability.
Narrative incident summaries without timestamps or test evidence are weak for measuring response performance.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name and withdrawn note refer to response to failures, which is corrective (restoration/response) rather than purely preventive.

SC-7(6).differentiation_note: Addresses post-failure response and availability (MTTR and failover testing), unlike other SC-7 entries that focus on preventive boundary configurations.

SC-7(7).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of remote access endpoints (users/devices) for which split tunneling is disabled per policy and the count of VPN sessions observed performing split-tunnel bypass.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control to block split tunneling; percent of endpoints conforming to policy is the key metric, with counts of violations showing operational risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of managed remote endpoints and VPN configurations that have split tunneling disabled and the count of VPN sessions or endpoint telemetry showing traffic bypassing the VPN tunnel, including timestamps and device IDs.
data_signals: VPN configuration per endpoint/user (split-tunneling setting)
VPN session logs showing routes and tunneled vs non-tunneled traffic
endpoint routing tables and telemetry indicating default gateway usage
remote device inventory with compliance status
exception approvals for split tunneling with expiry
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time VPN session logs correlated with endpoint telemetry showing no bypass are strong evidence.
Policy statements without configuration or session evidence are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prohibits split tunneling to prevent policy bypass — a preventive configuration control.

SC-7(7).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on remote access/VPN split-tunneling settings and detection of tunnel bypass, requiring endpoint and VPN-session telemetry.

SC-7(8).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of egress web/HTTP(S) traffic routed through authenticated proxy servers and the proxy authentication success rate; count endpoints observed making non-proxied egress connections.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive/detective control — primary metric is coverage percentage of egress routed via authenticated proxies; rates of auth failures and counts of non-proxy traffic support detection of gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of egress HTTP(S) connections that are observed in proxy logs with successful authentication, count of failed proxy authentications, and count of egress flows identified as bypassing proxy controls (with source host and timestamp).
data_signals: proxy authentication logs (user/device, success/failure, timestamp)
proxy access logs showing URLs and source IPs
gateway routing tables indicating forced proxy routing
network flow logs identifying non-proxy egress
proxy exception/whitelist records with approvals
evidence_strength_indicators: Comprehensive proxy logs showing authenticated sessions correlated to flow logs are strong evidence.
Lack of proxy logging or manual records without traffic correlation is weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring traffic routing to authenticated proxies is a preventive control that enforces routing and authentication at egress points.

SC-7(8).differentiation_note: Specifically measures routing of egress traffic through authenticated proxies and proxy auth coverage, unlike other entries that focus on firewalls or segmentation.

SC-7(9).measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of outgoing connections identified as threatening that were blocked or mitigated and the percentage reduction in malicious outbound connections over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This detective/preventive control focuses on detecting and restricting threatening outbound communications; counts of blocked malicious outbound events and their rates are primary indicators.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of outbound connections flagged as malicious/threatening and blocked by proxies/IDS/egress filters (with timestamps, source host, destination IP/domain, and threat classification), plus trend of blocked events over time to show effectiveness.
data_signals: proxy and IDS/IPS egress alerts with threat classification
DNS logs showing suspicious domain queries and blocks
outbound connection logs with threat intelligence hits (destination IP/domain)
blocked outbound port/protocol events from firewalls
quarantine/isolation actions taken for hosts initiating threatening outbound traffic
threat intelligence feed matches for outbound destinations
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous alerting logs from proxies/IDS correlated with threat intelligence and automated blocking actions are strong evidence.
Manual reviews or isolated IDS alerts without blocking or correlation are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on identifying and restricting threatening outbound communications, which is primarily detective (alerts/identification) with prevention via blocking.

SC-7(9).differentiation_note: Concentrates on detecting and blocking malicious outbound traffic (threat telemetry and blocking actions), rather than general boundary configuration or segmentation.

SC-7(10).measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of controls preventing data exfiltration by counting blocked/flagged outbound transfers of sensitive data and the percentage of egress flows inspected/enforced by boundary controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily preventive: auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of egress flows inspected / DLP policy coverage). Counts and rates of blocked exfiltration attempts are useful secondary detective metrics to show enforcement and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of outbound network flows that are subject to exfiltration controls (DLP/egress filtering) and a count of outbound connections or transfers containing sensitive-data classification labels that were blocked or flagged, including timestamps and destination IPs. Include configuration records showing egress rule existence and policy mappings to data classification.
data_signals: egress firewall/NGFW policy configuration entries and rule timestamps
DLP alert records with data classification tags and disposition (blocked/quarantined/allowed)
outbound flow logs with destination IP, port, bytes transferred, and associated policy match
blocked outbound connection counts and timestamps
policy coverage table: assets mapped to DLP/egress enforcement
exceptions/allowlist change records and approvals
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated egress logs and DLP alerts correlated to data classification and enforcement actions; policy configuration showing deny-by-default for sensitive-data flows.
Weak: periodic manual reports or single-point-in-time screenshots of rules without correlated flow/alert logs or absence of data-classification mapping.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Name explicitly uses 'Prevent Exfiltration' which indicates controls put in place to stop data leaving the boundary (preventive).

SC-7(10).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on blocking or inspecting outbound flows for data exfiltration rather than general inbound restrictions, host agents, or segmentation.

SC-7(11).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of ingress network entry points governed by deny-by-default policies and the count/rate of unauthorized incoming connection attempts that were blocked or dropped.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage percentages (how many ingress points enforce restrictions) with counts/rates of blocked incoming attempts as secondary detective evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of network ingress interfaces (firewall/SGs/load-balancers) that implement deny-by-default inbound rules, and counts of inbound connections denied or flagged as non-compliant, with rule identifiers and timestamps.
data_signals: firewall/security group inbound rule sets and timestamps for last change
load balancer listener configurations and allowed-source lists
inbound connection logs (accepted vs denied) with source IP, destination port, and reason code
IDS/IPS alerts for inbound anomalies
rule exception records and approvals
change requests for inbound rule modifications
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous deny/allow logs showing deny-by-default behavior and automated enforcement at ingress points; documented rule inventories mapped to ingress interfaces.
Weak: manual snapshots of firewall rules without denied-connection logs or untracked exceptions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Restrict Incoming Communications Traffic' indicates preventive configuration to limit inbound communications.

SC-7(11).differentiation_note: Targets inbound/ingress controls and deny-by-default enforcement for incoming traffic rather than outbound exfiltration, host agents, or isolation of security tools.

SC-7(12).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of hosts with active host-based boundary controls (HIPS/HIDS, host firewall rules or host-based filtering) and the count of host-detected blocked inbound/outbound connection attempts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control best evidenced by coverage percentage of hosts running and enforcing host-based protection; counts/trends of agent-detected blocks provide operational validation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of managed hosts with an active host-based protection agent (HIPS/HIDS/host firewall) reporting heartbeats and enforced rules, plus counts of host-generated block/alert events with timestamps and rule identifiers.
data_signals: endpoint agent inventory and enrollment status (agent version, last heartbeat)
host firewall/HIPS configuration snapshots and rule sets
host-detected connection block logs with source/destination and process owner
agent tamper or disablement events
deployment/compliance reports showing hosts not covered
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated endpoint agent telemetry (heartbeats, block events, tamper detection) showing active enforcement across hosts.
Weak: ad hoc manual host scans or occasional AV reports without continuous agent telemetry or missing agent enrollment data.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Label 'Host-based Protection' indicates preventive measures deployed on hosts to enforce boundary protections at the endpoint.

SC-7(12).differentiation_note: Measures host-level enforcement and agent coverage rather than perimeter firewall rules or network segmentation.

SC-7(13).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of security tools, management interfaces, and support components that are deployed on isolated management networks or segments and count unauthorized cross-network access events to those tools.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: auditors need coverage (percentage of security tools isolated). Count of cross-access attempts and comparison of actual segmentation vs intended segmentation are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of identified security tools and support components mapped to an isolated management network/segment (with VLAN/subnet identifiers), and counts of access attempts from non-management segments including timestamps and source identifiers. Include network ACL/VLAN configuration and access control lists for management plane isolation.
data_signals: asset inventory annotated as 'security tool' or 'management component' with network segment assignment
network segmentation config: VLANs, ACLs, route tables, security group rules
management-plane access logs (SSH/RDP/API) with source network and account context
change records modifying segmentation or management access
unauthorized access or cross-segment flow logs with drop/allow decisions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: up-to-date asset inventory linked to network segmentation configs and continuous access logs showing only allowed management-plane connections.
Weak: periodic topology diagrams or manual attestations without access-log correlation or missing mapping between assets and segments.

affinity_keywords: Isolation of Security Tools, Mechanisms, and Support Components

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Isolation of Security Tools...' describes a preventive segmentation/isolation requirement to keep security components separated.

SC-7(13).differentiation_note: Unique focus on isolating management/security tooling and support components (management plane) rather than general host or network boundary rules.

SC-7(14).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of network physical ports and connection points protected (disabled or secured) and the count of unauthorized physical connection attempts or discovered active unauthorized ports.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control best evidenced by coverage percentage (ports secured/disabled) and counts of unauthorized physical connection detections as secondary detective evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of switch/patch panel ports that are configured with port-security/disabled-by-default or are managed by NAC/802.1X, and counts of detected unauthorized physical connections or MACs on disabled ports with port and timestamp details.
data_signals: switch port configuration exports including port-security/port status
NAC/802.1X authentication logs with port identifiers and device MACs
DHCP lease logs mapping MAC to physical port where available
physical port inventory and patch panel labeling records
events of unauthorized port usage or tamper alerts from infrastructure monitoring
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated NAC/802.1X logs and switch port configs showing disabled/unmanaged ports and alerts for unauthorized connections; measured mappings between physical ports and device identities.
Weak: manual walkthroughs or static diagrams without logs tying device connections to physical ports.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Protect Against Unauthorized Physical Connections' indicates preventive controls (port disabling, NAC) to stop unauthorized physical attachment.

SC-7(14).differentiation_note: Specifically measures physical-layer protections (ports, patch panels, NAC) rather than logical/host or application protections.

SC-7(15).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of privileged network accesses that are forced through authorized controlled paths (jump hosts / bastions) with session logging enabled and count direct privileged access attempts bypassing controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive: auditors need coverage % of privileged sessions using approved networked privileged-access controls; counts/trends of bypass attempts validate enforcement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of privileged accounts/sessions that use approved networked access mechanisms (bastion/jump host, privileged access gateway) and the count of privileged session attempts originating outside authorized paths including timestamps, source, and target systems.
data_signals: privileged account inventory and assigned access paths (bastion/jump host mapping)
bastion/jump host session logs with user, source IP, destination, and session start/stop
VPN/gateway logs that tag privileged sessions
access control policy records requiring jump host usage for privileged access
alerts/blocks for direct RDP/SSH connections to privileged systems
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous bastion session logs correlated to privileged accounts showing 100% funneling through approved hosts and automated blocks for direct access attempts.
Weak: manual attestations of privileged access policy without session log correlation or uncovered one-off privileged direct connections.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Networked Privileged Accesses' implies prevention/enforcement of how privileged network access occurs (restrict to approved mechanisms).

SC-7(15).differentiation_note: Focuses on ensuring privileged sessions are routed through controlled networked mechanisms (bastions/jump hosts) rather than general privilege management or host hardening.

SC-7(16).measurement_goal: Measure counts of reconnaissance/discovery attempts detected and percentage of externally visible systems that disclose identifying information (banners, versions) compared to the authorized public asset inventory.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Although preventive, this control is often evidenced by detective metrics: counts of discovery attempts (scans) and percentages of systems exposing discovery information; count is primary to show detection volume.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of detected discovery/reconnaissance events (port scans, banner grabs) per period and the percentage of internet-facing systems that disclose identifying banners or version strings compared to the authorized public asset list.
data_signals: IDS/IPS/scan-detection alerts for port scanning and enumeration activities
honeypot or tarpit logs capturing scanning activity
web server and service banner/version exposure scans (banner grab results)
public asset inventory vs observed internet-facing endpoints
blocked/rejected reconnaissance connection logs with source IP and timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous detection alerts and automated banner-scan results correlated to asset inventory showing reduction/absence of exposed banners; blocking records for scanning sources.
Weak: one-off penetration test reports or manual scans without continuous detection or inventory correlation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: While the control aims to prevent discovery, measurable evidence commonly comes from detection of scanning/reconnaissance events ('Prevent Discovery' implies detection of discovery attempts and mitigation).

SC-7(16).differentiation_note: Addresses detection/prevention of system discovery activities (scans, banner exposure) rather than general ingress/egress controls or host protections.

SC-7(17).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of network traffic that is subject to protocol-aware validation and the count of malformed protocol frames or protocol-violations dropped by enforcement devices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: coverage percentage (how much traffic is validated by protocol enforcement) is primary; counts of malformed packets and distribution of violation types are useful secondary signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of ingress/egress traffic flows processed by protocol-aware enforcement (WAF/NGFW/IPS) and counts of dropped/detected malformed protocol packets with protocol type, drop reason, and timestamps.
data_signals: WAF/NGFW/IPS logs showing protocol validation events and drops
malformed packet/drop counts by protocol (HTTP, TLS, DNS, etc.) with timestamps
policy configuration for protocol parsing/enforcement and rule versions
alerts indicating protocol violations and remediation actions
traffic coverage mapping showing which segments pass through protocol-aware devices
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous enforcement device logs showing malformed packets dropped and policy coverage mapping demonstrating protocol-aware devices in-line for relevant traffic.
Weak: offline or sampling-based protocol analysis without enforced drops or lacking mapping of which traffic is enforced.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Automated Enforcement of Protocol Formats' describes preventive enforcement to validate/protect traffic at the protocol level.

SC-7(17).differentiation_note: Specifically requires protocol-aware validation/enforcement and measurable drops of malformed traffic, differing from general firewall filtering or host protections.

SC-7(18).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of failure events where systems entered a secure state (deny-by-default) and count incidents where failures resulted in insecure or permissive states.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Correct (preventive/corrective design) evidence is best shown by percentage of failures that resulted in secure states; counts of insecure-failure incidents help track exceptions and improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of observed system/component failure events that transition to the documented secure state (e.g., deny-by-default), plus a count and narrative of failure incidents that resulted in insecure fallback with timestamps and root-cause records.
data_signals: system failure and state-transition logs indicating secure vs insecure fallback
failover and fallback test results with pass/fail and timestamp
configuration settings indicating default-fail behavior
incident reports describing failures that produced insecure states
change records where fail-secure settings were modified
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: test logs and automated state-transition telemetry showing consistent fail-secure behavior and no incidents of insecure fallback.
Weak: policy statements or ad hoc tests without logs from real failure events or lacking incident correlation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The term 'Fail Secure' prescribes preventive system configuration to ensure failures result in secure states rather than permissive ones.

SC-7(18).differentiation_note: Unique focus on system behavior during failure modes and validating secure fallback, unlike other controls that focus on traffic filtering, segmentation, or host protections.

SC-7(19).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of connections from non-organizationally configured (unmanaged) hosts that are blocked and the count of allowed connections from such hosts, showing enforcement of posture-based admission controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control: percentage of blocked connections from unmanaged devices indicates coverage; counts/trends of bypasses are useful for incident detection and remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of attempted connections from devices not enrolled/configured by the organization that were blocked by NAC/firewall policies, plus a list/count of any allowed non-organizationally configured hosts with justifications and approvals.
data_signals: NAC posture assessment logs with device enrollment status and decision (allow/quarantine/block)
firewall logs showing blocked connections from unmanaged device IPs/MACs
MDM/endpoint management inventory indicating managed vs unmanaged devices
DHCP logs mapping device MAC/IP to enrollment status
exceptions or allowlist change records with approvals
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous NAC/MDM telemetry and firewall blocks correlated to device enrollment status showing automated blocking of unmanaged devices.
Weak: spot checks or periodic lists of unmanaged devices without enforcement logs linking blocks to policy decisions.

affinity_keywords: Block Communication from Non-organizationally Configured Hosts

control_type_classification.reasoning: The term 'Block Communication from Non-organizationally Configured Hosts' describes preventive enforcement of network access based on device configuration posture.

SC-7(19).differentiation_note: Specifically measures enforcement against unmanaged/unconfigured hosts (posture-based admission) rather than general inbound/outbound filtering or host-based agents.

SC-7(20).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of dynamic isolation/segregation controls: percent of eligible assets/flows with dynamic isolation policy applied and count of automated isolation events and enforcement failures over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and enforcement; percentage shows coverage of dynamic isolation while count and trend capture event volumes and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets/flows tagged as in-scope for dynamic isolation that have an active microsegmentation/dynamic isolation policy in place; count of automated isolation actions (policy-triggered) and count of enforcement failures or exceptions per period.
data_signals: inventory of assets/flows marked for dynamic isolation with timestamps
dynamic isolation/microsegmentation policy assignments per asset/flow
automation isolation event logs (timestamp, asset, action: isolate/unisolate)
policy enforcement failure/error logs and exception records
policy version/history and configuration deployment timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated isolation event logs correlated to asset inventory and deployed policy versions; weak: one-time architecture diagrams or manual change tickets without event logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Dynamic Isolation and Segregation' implies proactive segmentation to prevent undesired flows, a preventive control.

SC-7(20).differentiation_note: This control emphasizes automated/dynamic isolation events (automation and enforcement logs) unlike sibling controls that emphasize static subnet separation or domain-specific connections.

SC-7(21).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of system components placed into isolated network zones and the count of cross-zone connections that violate isolation policies (unauthorized connections or rules).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage metrics (percentage of components isolated) are primary; counts of cross-zone violations and trends provide operational detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of system components mapped to isolation zones per architecture standard, and count of active network connections between isolated zones that lack approved rules or exceptions; include timestamps for detection of violations.
data_signals: asset-to-zone mapping inventory with isolation zone tags
network ACLs and security group configurations per zone
inter-zone connection logs (flow records) with source/destination zone tags
approved exception lists and change request records for cross-zone connectivity
alerts for lateral movement or unauthorized cross-zone traffic
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated asset-zone mapping, continuous flow logs showing zero unauthorized cross-zone connections; weak: static network diagrams or periodic manual lists without flow validation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control phrase 'Isolation of System Components' denotes proactive separation of components to prevent unauthorized interaction, a preventive measure.

SC-7(21).differentiation_note: Focuses on isolating individual system components and detecting cross-zone connections, not on subnet/domain mapping or PII-specific flows.

SC-7(22).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of connections between distinct security domains that use dedicated subnets or VLANs and count misconfigured interfaces or cross-domain traffic not using the assigned subnet segmentation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage (percentage of domain-to-domain connections correctly placed on separate subnets) is primary; counts of misconfigurations and comparisons to expected mappings add diagnostic value.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of security-domain-to-domain links implemented on dedicated subnets/VLANs per the architecture standard; list and count of interfaces/routes that connect domains without the required subnet separation.
data_signals: network topology records showing subnet/VLAN assignments by security domain
routing table/interface configurations linking domains
firewall and ACL rules scoped per subnet/domain
flow logs showing traffic between domains with source/destination subnet IDs
network configuration change requests for domain connections
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory and flow correlation showing all domain connections use dedicated subnets; weak: architecture diagrams absent of flow validation or manual lists without config evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The directive to use separate subnets for different security domains is preventive segmentation to stop cross-domain exposure.

SC-7(22).differentiation_note: Specifically requires subnet/VLAN-level separation between security domains—measurement centers on subnet assignments and routing vs. component isolation or dynamic segregation.

SC-7(23).measurement_goal: Measure the rate/percentage of protocol validation failure events where sender feedback is suppressed as required and count occurrences where feedback containing validation details was returned.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, percentage of validation failures that had sender feedback disabled shows policy coverage; counts of erroneous feedback incidents are needed for operational remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of protocol validation failure events where response templates suppressed sender feedback vs total validation failures; count and examples of responses returned that include protocol validation details or verbose error messages.
data_signals: protocol validation failure logs with response behavior flags
application/server configuration entries controlling feedback on failure (disable flags)
HTTP/SMTP/other protocol response bodies for validation-failure incidents
incident or vulnerability tickets documenting feedback leakage events
change logs showing deployment of 'disable feedback' configurations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated validation logs showing 100% suppression of sender feedback and config enforcement records; weak: developer attestations or occasional test screenshots without system logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Instruction to 'Disable Sender Feedback' on protocol validation failures is a preventive setting to avoid information disclosure.

SC-7(23).differentiation_note: Unique measurement of protocol-level response behavior (suppression of feedback) rather than subnetting or connection-level segmentation used by sibling controls.

SC-7(24).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of network/data flows containing Personally Identifiable Information that traverse protected boundary controls (DLP, encryption, approved gateways) and count unprotected PII transfers or boundary exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage (percentage of PII flows protected) is primary; count/trend of unprotected PII transfers identifies incidents needing corrective action.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of detected PII-containing flows that were subject to approved boundary controls (DLP policy applied, encryption in transit, allowed gateway) versus total PII flows; count of PII transfers that bypassed boundary protections.
data_signals: data classification tags indicating 'Personally Identifiable Information' in inventory
DLP detection logs showing PII in network flows with policy outcome (block/allow/quarantine)
flow logs with PII markers and encryption-in-transit flags (TLS usage)
gateway/proxy logs showing PII egress/ingress with applied controls
exceptions/approved-transfer lists for PII movement
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous DLP+flow logs correlating PII classification to applied boundary controls; weak: ad-hoc manual reports or periodic scans without flow-level evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control ties boundary protection specifically to PII flows, a preventive requirement to block or control exposure.

SC-7(24).differentiation_note: This entry is data-focused: measurement must correlate data classification (PII) with boundary controls, unlike purely network-segmentation siblings.

SC-7(25).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of unclassified national security system connections that are routed through approved/authorized gateways and the count of unauthorized or unapproved connections.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage of authorized gateway usage (percentage) is primary; counts of unauthorized connections and comparisons to approved lists support compliance verification.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of connections tagged as 'Unclassified National Security System' that traverse approved gateways or boundary devices per policy, and count of connections not on the approved list with timestamps and source/destination.
data_signals: inventory of connections labeled 'Unclassified National Security System Connections'
approved gateway/connection registry with authorization timestamps
firewall/gateway connection logs showing source/destination and gateway used
audit records of approvals/authorizations for specific connections
alerts for new/unapproved connections classified as UNCLASSIFIED NSS
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated mapping of connection inventory to approved gateway registry and continuous gateway logs; weak: manual lists of approved connections without log correlation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Specifies how unclassified national security system connections must be handled (route through authorized boundaries) — a preventive access/control requirement.

SC-7(25).differentiation_note: Targets connections labeled specifically as 'Unclassified National Security System' and requires mapping to approved gateways, different from generic subnet isolation controls.

SC-7(26).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of classified national security system connections implemented via accredited or approved cross-domain solutions and the count of non-accredited connection instances.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage (percentage of classified connections using accredited solutions) measures compliance; counts of deviations surface urgent issues.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of classified connections that are implemented via accredited cross-domain solutions or approved boundary mechanisms; count and details of any classified connections lacking accreditation or approvals.
data_signals: connection inventory tagged 'Classified National Security System Connections'
accreditation/certification records for cross-domain solutions or gateways
gateway/configuration logs showing classified traffic flows
audit records of approvals for classified connections
alerts for classified connections detected outside accredited solutions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: accredited solution certificates plus continuous gateway logs mapping classified flows to accredited devices; weak: policy statements without accreditation evidence or flow logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requirement about using accredited connections for classified NSS denotes a preventive control to avoid unauthorized data paths.

SC-7(26).differentiation_note: High-sensitivity focus on classified NSS connections and accreditation evidence differentiates it from unclassified or generic subnet isolation controls.

SC-7(27).measurement_goal: Measure the percent of unclassified non-national-security-system connections that are isolated per architecture and the count of any miscategorized or improperly routed connections.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive coverage percentage shows how well non-NSS connections are isolated; counts detect misrouting or categorization errors.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of 'Unclassified Non-national Security System' connections placed into the approved isolated network segments and count of connections that are not compliant with segmentation expectations.
data_signals: connection inventory tagged 'Unclassified Non-national Security System Connections'
subnet/VLAN assignments and ACLs for those connections
flow logs showing actual routing for non-NSS connections
change request and approval records for non-NSS connectivity
alerts for mismatches between connection tag and routing/subnet
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous correlation of connection tags to subnet configuration and flow logs; weak: spreadsheets or occasional manual inventories lacking log correlation.
