hardware attestation/TPM quote logs and attestation success/failure results
policy enforcement logs from hardware/firmware/hypervisor (blocked/denied events with timestamps)
policy deployment records (policy id, version, deployment timestamp) for hardware enforcement modules
non-compliance scan results (hosts failing hardware enforcement checks) and remediation ticket identifiers
BIOS/UEFI configuration snapshots and change history
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated hardware attestation logs combined with CMDB records showing enforcement flags and real-time enforcement event streams.
Strong: integration between attestation results and policy enforcement logs demonstrating that failed attestation led to enforcement actions.
Weak: single point-in-time manual BIOS screenshots or ad-hoc attestations without automated collection or correlation to enforcement logs.
Weak: absence of correlating remediation tickets or stale CMDB entries that are not time-stamped.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title emphasizes 'hardware-enforced separation' and 'policy enforcement', which are preventive measures that stop unauthorized interactions at the hardware/firmware layer.

SC-49.differentiation_note: This control requires measuring hardware-level configuration and attestation plus enforcement event logs (hardware/firmware/hypervisor), unlike sibling controls that may focus on software-only isolation or network controls; it uniquely needs attestation and BIOS/firmware configuration signals.

SC-50.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of software-enforced separation controls by reporting the percentage of enforcement points (hosts, VMs, containers, network segments) with active policy enforcement, the count and rate of blocked policy violations, and the number of policy drift or exception events against the authoritative policy baseline.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires demonstrating coverage (percentage of enforcement points configured) and compliance to policy; counts and rates (blocked violations, exceptions) are useful secondary indicators while trends show ongoing effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of infrastructure enforcement points (by host/VM/container/network segment/service) with current policy engines active and up-to-date rule sets; daily count of enforcement decisions that blocked unauthorized flows or actions; timestamps of policy deployments and number of exception/override records compared to the policy baseline.
data_signals: policy engine / policy agent deployment records with host/container/VM identifiers and timestamps
enforcement decision logs (allow/deny) with reason codes, rule IDs, source/destination, and timestamps
policy version and rule-set checksum per enforcement point
number of blocked policy violations per enforcement point per day
exception/override records and their justification/approval timestamps
policy drift detection events (rule delta between baseline and deployed)
coverage matrix mapping assets/services to enforcement points
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous enforcement decision logs with rule IDs, policy version, and host identifiers correlated to an authoritative policy baseline; automated deployment records showing policy versions pushed and active.
Weak: ad hoc screenshots of configuration, manual attestations, or annual test results that do not show ongoing enforcement decisions or coverage per asset.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name 'Software-enforced Separation and Policy Enforcement' indicates controls that prevent undesired interactions by enforcing policy in software, so it is primarily preventive.

SC-50.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measuring software-based enforcement coverage and enforcement decision outcomes (blocked violations and policy drift), unlike sibling controls that might focus on detection or remediation; note: control description text was not provided, so measurement specifics are inferred from the control name and may need refinement when authoritative control text is available.

SC-51.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of in-scope hosts and devices that have vendor/hardware-enforced protections enabled (e.g., TPM/TPM2.0, secure boot, measured/verified boot, HSM-backed key storage) and track exceptions and attestations to demonstrate hardware protections are actively enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Hardware-protection controls are preventive and auditors expect coverage metrics (what percent of assets are protected). Supplementing with counts (number of noncompliant devices) and trends (coverage over time) shows scope and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A device-level coverage metric: count of in-scope assets with hardware protections enabled divided by total in-scope assets, plus counts of attestation/verification failures and HSM usage events. Include attributes: asset identifier, asset type, protection feature (TPM/secure boot/measured boot/HSM), protection status, attestation timestamp, and any exception ticket ID.
data_signals: asset inventory records with hardware feature flags (TPM present, TPM enabled, secure boot enabled)
UEFI/secure-boot status reports (secure boot DB/index, secure boot enablement timestamps)
TPM attestation logs and PCR measurement timestamps
HSM usage and key storage logs (which keys are HSM-backed)
measured/verified boot attestation results
device compliance/MDM reports showing hardware-enforced settings
exception/tracking tickets for assets without hardware protections and their approval/expiration
cryptographic operation logs showing keys stored/used from HSM vs. software keystore
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated attestation logs and centralized inventory showing continuous secure-boot/TPM/HSM status for >95% of in-scope assets with timestamps and verifiable hashes.
Weaker: periodic manual checklists, one-off screenshots, or self-reported device statements without attestation timestamps or centralized aggregation.
Strong: HSM access logs and key metadata proving keys are stored/used only in hardware-backed modules; Weak: policy statements claiming HSM use without usage logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title indicates enforcement of protections at the hardware level (prevents compromise of integrity/confidentiality), which aligns with preventive controls.

SC-51.differentiation_note: No detailed description or test criteria were provided; measurement focus was inferred from the control name 'Hardware-based Protection' — unlike software/configuration controls, this entry emphasizes hardware feature presence, attestation logs, and HSM key usage as unique measurement needs.

SI-1.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, completeness, ownership, and currency of required security policies and procedures (percent of required documents that are published, assigned an owner, and reviewed within the defined review period) and count outstanding policy approvals or distribution gaps.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Policy & procedures are preventive controls where coverage and currency (percent of required docs current and owned) are most informative; counts of missing/overdue items and trends of review cadence provide additional context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of required security policy and procedure documents that are (a) published in the official repository, (b) assigned a documented owner, and (c) have a recorded review date within the organizational review frequency. Additionally, count of policy documents pending approval, and distribution records showing which user groups have received/published versions.
data_signals: policy/document repository entries (title, id, version)
document owner / approver metadata
document last_review_date and next_review_due
document approval status and approval timestamps
policy distribution or acknowledgment logs (who received/acknowledged)
policy scope / applicability tags (systems, roles)
open policy approval requests or change requests count
version history / change log entries for each policy
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated repository records with structured metadata for owner, review dates, approval timestamps, and acknowledgment logs showing coverage by role/group.
Weak: single-point manual snapshots, unsupported spreadsheets, or undated PDF files lacking approval or review metadata.

control_type_classification.reasoning: As a 'Policy and Procedures' control it is intended to prevent gaps through documented requirements and responsibilities; measurement focuses on coverage and currency rather than event detection.

SI-1.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on governance documentation (existence, ownership, review cadence) rather than operational detection or remediation metrics; keywords and signals were inferred from the control title because the control description was not provided.

SI-2.measurement_goal: Measure remediation throughput and coverage: count and rate of identified flaws remediated (by severity and asset), mean time to remediate (MTTR), and percentage of open/overdue flaws against the organization's remediation policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SI-2 is a corrective remediation control where auditors need remediation velocity (rates) and coverage (percentages) over time to show issues are closing and SLAs/policy targets are met.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of discovered flaws and count/time-to-resolution for remediation actions per asset and per severity; timestamps for discovery, ticket creation and resolution; patch/deployment execution records showing applied fixes; number of overdue remediation items compared to policy remediation windows.
data_signals: flaw/vulnerability discovery records (scan or advisory feed timestamps)
open vulnerability count by severity and asset
remediation ticket creation and resolution timestamps (ticket id, assignee)
patch or fix deployment logs with asset id, version before/after, timestamp, and success/failure status
overdue remediation count and list (items past policy remediation window)
asset inventory with installed software/firmware version metadata
remediation policy windows / SLA targets
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated discovery + ticketed remediation records + patch deployment logs that show version change and success status.
Weak: ad-hoc manual reports or one-off penetration test results without timestamps linking discovery to remediation actions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Flaw Remediation' indicates corrective actions to fix identified flaws (remediation and time-to-remediate focus).

SI-2.differentiation_note: This is the high-level program control measuring remediation outcomes and velocity across all remedy activities; sibling controls focus on specific mechanisms (central management, automated status, patch tools, automatic updates, version removal).

SI-2(1).measurement_goal: Demonstrate centralized management of remediation activities by measuring the percentage of remediation actions tracked through the central PL-9 system and the count of remediation items not present in central management.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive/management control (explicitly marked preventive and 'Central Management'), auditors expect coverage/centralization percentages and counts of unmanaged items.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of identified flaws and remediation tickets that are entered and tracked in the centralized/PL-9 system; count of active remediation items missing a central-management ticket; timestamps showing central assignment and lifecycle.
data_signals: central management (PL-9) remediation ticket list
source discovery records cross-referenced to central ticket ids
count of remediation items not linked to PL-9
timestamps of central ticket creation and status transitions
system/process configuration showing central management enforcement
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated ingest/integration logs showing every discovery mapped to a PL-9 ticket and no unmatched items.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or occasional exports claiming centralization without cross-reference to discovery sources.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is labeled 'preventive' and the name 'Central Management' implies a preventive governance mechanism to ensure remediation activities are centrally tracked.

SI-2(1).differentiation_note: This subcontrol is about centralization and tracking of remediation (PL-9) rather than remediation speed or tooling; it requires evidence of centralized ticketing coverage.

SI-2(2).measurement_goal: Show automated, near-real-time status of remediation by measuring the count and rate of remediation status updates emitted by automation (e.g., patch orchestration) and the percentage of assets with up-to-date remediation status reported.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective/monitoring-oriented subcontrol (automated status) where counts of status events and percentage coverage are most useful to show monitoring is operating.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of automated remediation status events (success/failure) per asset per time window; timestamped status refresh rates; percentage of managed assets reporting current remediation status within the expected interval.
data_signals: automated remediation status event stream (timestamp, asset id, status)
last status update timestamp per asset
status type (applied, pending, failed)
integration logs between scanning/CMDB and remediation tool
percentage of assets with status older than allowed reporting interval
failure/error codes from remediation jobs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous machine-generated status events with asset IDs and timestamps and low proportion of stale statuses.
Weak: periodic manual status snapshots or emailed spreadsheets without machine timestamps or asset linkage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Automated Flaw Remediation Status' implies monitoring/detection of remediation state via automation rather than direct remediation action.

SI-2(2).differentiation_note: Focused on automated status/visibility of remediation (event streams and freshness) rather than the remediation actions or SLAs themselves.

SI-2(3).measurement_goal: Measure time-to-remediate: MTTR (median and mean) and the percentage of flaws remediated within benchmarked SLA windows, broken out by severity and asset tier.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This corrective metric requires rates/velocity (time-to-remediate measures and SLA compliance rates) and trending/comparison against benchmarks.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Time intervals between flaw discovery and remediation completion for each item (discovery timestamp, remediation completion timestamp), count/percentage remediated within benchmark/SLA windows, and MTTR aggregated by severity and asset criticality.
data_signals: discovery timestamp per flaw
remediation completion timestamp per flaw
severity or benchmark tier per flaw
SLA/benchmark definitions per severity
calculated MTTR (mean/median) by severity
count and percentage of items remediated within SLA vs overdue
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated timestamps from discovery and remediation systems with SLA definitions and regular reporting of MTTR by severity.
Weak: manually estimated remediation dates or undated remediation logs that cannot be tied to discovery timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title explicitly addresses 'Time to Remediate' and 'Benchmarks' indicating measurement of remediation speed and SLA compliance (corrective metrics).

SI-2(3).differentiation_note: This subcontrol is explicitly metric-focused on remediation timelines and SLA/benchmark compliance, unlike siblings that focus on tools or status.

SI-2(4).measurement_goal: Demonstrate patching automation coverage and effectiveness: percentage of target assets managed by patch tools, patch job success rate, and count of failed or skipped patch deployments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive/tooling control, auditors need coverage percentages (how many assets are managed) plus rates of successful deployments and distribution of failure reasons.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets covered by automated patch management tools, patch job counts and success/failure results per asset, scheduled vs executed patch job timestamps, and reasons for failures or exclusions.
data_signals: patch management tool inventory (which assets are enrolled)
patch job execution logs (job id, asset id, timestamp, result: success/failure/skipped)
patch deployment success rate
list of excluded assets and documented reasons
patch schedules and last-run timestamps
failure/error codes and remediation attempts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated enrollment records + job logs showing high success rates and low exclusion counts.
Weak: claims of tool usage without job-level logs or proof of asset enrollment.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name 'Automated Patch Management Tools' indicates a preventive control providing automated application of fixes to reduce future flaws.

SI-2(4).differentiation_note: This entry measures tooling enrollment and job-level patch success/failure, unlike SI-2(2) which measures status events and SI-2(3) which measures remediation timing.

SI-2(5).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of assets configured to receive automatic software/firmware updates and the rate at which automatic updates are applied successfully versus failed/skipped updates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage/enablement percentages (how many assets have automatic updates enabled) and rates of update application to prove automated updates function.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of devices/firmware endpoints with automatic updates enabled, count and rate of automatic updates applied per time period with success/failure status, and list of devices with automatic updates disabled.
data_signals: endpoint configuration records indicating 'automatic updates' enabled flag
update application logs (asset id, update id, timestamp, result)
rate of automatic update success vs failure
inventory of firmware devices with current vs latest firmware version
exceptions or manually-disabled automatic update lists and justification
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system configuration data showing automatic updates enabled plus machine logs of update installs and success codes.
Weak: policy statements that automatic updates are required but no configuration or update execution evidence exists.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Automatic Software and Firmware Updates' indicates a preventive control that automatically applies updates to prevent exploitable flaws.

SI-2(5).differentiation_note: This subcontrol focuses on automatic update enablement and application metrics for software/firmware, distinct from patch tooling enrollment or remediation timing.

SI-2(6).measurement_goal: Measure the inventory and reduction of legacy/previous software and firmware versions: count and percentage of assets running deprecated/previous versions and trend of removed previous versions over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive/hygiene control is best evidenced by coverage percentages (how many assets have been cleaned of old versions) and trends showing removal over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of assets running non-current or previous software/firmware versions, timestamps of decommission/removal actions, lists of removed previous versions, and exceptions with justification.
data_signals: software/firmware inventory with current vs installed version per asset
list of deprecated/previous versions defined by policy
removal/decommissioning action logs (who, when, asset id, version removed)
count of assets still running previous versions
exceptions/approved justifications for retaining previous versions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory showing all assets at current versions and action logs for removals; exceptions tracked formally.
Weak: occasional manual inventories or claims of removal without timestamped decommission records.

affinity_keywords: Removal of Previous Versions of Software and Firmware

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title explicitly requires removal of previous software/firmware versions, which is a preventive configuration hygiene control.

SI-2(6).differentiation_note: This subcontrol requires version-level inventory and removal evidence (decommission logs and trends) rather than patching or remediation ticket metrics.

SI-3.measurement_goal: Measure deployment and currency of malicious-code protections across managed hosts (percentage of hosts with up-to-date anti-malware agents), volume and severity of malware detections, and remediation completion rate and time-to-remediate for detected malicious code.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SI-3 is primarily preventive (verify coverage and currency of protections) so percentage (coverage/up-to-date %) is primary; counts of detections and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show effectiveness and changes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of endpoints/servers with an approved anti-malware agent installed and reporting a last-definition-update timestamp within the policy window; count and severity of malware detection alerts per host; timestamps for remediation actions and closure for each detection, correlated to host and agent version.
data_signals: endpoint anti-malware installation inventory (host, agent name, agent version)
last signature/definition update timestamp per agent
malware detection alerts (timestamp, host, detection name, severity)
quarantine/isolation events (timestamp, host, file/hash)
remediation ticket timestamps and closure status for detection incidents
exceptions/allowlist entries for anti-malware
agent telemetry health/heartbeat logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous agent telemetry and automated update timestamps with full coverage by asset inventory is strong evidence.
Periodic spreadsheets or annual reports without per-agent timestamps are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control's aim (Malicious Code Protection) is to prevent malware via deployed protections and timely updates, so it is primarily preventive.

SI-3.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall program-level measurements (coverage, currency, detection volume) rather than specific operational techniques (e.g., nonsignature detection or remote-command authentication) captured by siblings.

SI-3(1).measurement_goal: Verify centralized management exists for malicious-code protection (policy/application of centralized controls) and that management functions are in effect (percentage of agents managed centrally vs unmanaged).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Central management is a preventive/coverage control — measuring percent of agents under central management (coverage) is most useful; counts of unmanaged hosts are a useful secondary signal.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Proportion of endpoints and servers enrolled in and reporting to a centralized anti-malware management console, including last heartbeat and policy assignment; list of unmanaged hosts and reasons.
data_signals: management console enrollment list (host, enrolled=true/false)
last heartbeat timestamp to central manager per agent
policy assignment per host
unmanaged host inventory and exception justification
central policy change logs (who/when/what)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated enrollment/heartbeat records showing most hosts managed is strong evidence.
Manual inventories or statements 'management exists' without automated telemetry are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Named for central management (a preventive coverage/control function) and listed as preventive; description indicates it is a management/configuration control.

SI-3(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on centralized enrollment and management coverage of agents (coverage %) rather than detection/remediation metrics of the overall SI-3 program.

SI-3(2).measurement_goal: Demonstrate that anti-malware definitions/engines are set to update automatically and measure the percentage of agents that received automatic updates within the defined update window.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automatic updates are a preventive configuration; percentage of agents receiving updates on schedule is the primary evidence; trends of missed updates and counts of failures help diagnose issues.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of agents with automatic-update setting enabled and the distribution of last-update timestamps, plus counts of update failures and reasons.
data_signals: agent configuration: automatic-update enabled flag
last-definition-update timestamp per agent
update failure/error logs (host, error code, timestamp)
scheduled update job execution logs
policy describing acceptable update interval
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated agent telemetry showing successful automatic updates across hosts is strong evidence.
Manual checklists or occasional manual updates without automatic update telemetry are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Automatic update settings are a preventive configuration to keep signatures/current, implied by the control name and marked preventive.

SI-3(2).differentiation_note: Narrow focus on automatic update configuration and success rates rather than broader deployment, detection, or remediation coverage.

SI-3(3).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement of restrictions that prevent non-privileged users from disabling or modifying anti-malware protections (count of attempts and percentage of hosts where such restrictions are applied).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about coverage/enforcement — percentage of hosts with policy preventing non-privileged modifications is primary; counts of detected modification attempts are useful detective secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of managed hosts with endpoint protection configured so non-privileged accounts cannot disable/modify agents, plus audit logs of attempted disable/modify actions including actor and timestamp.
data_signals: endpoint configuration policy: restrict_modify_by_nonprivileged flag per host
audit logs of local user actions to stop/disable agent (timestamp, user, host)
role mapping (user -> privilege level)
exceptions/allowlist of local admin accounts
agent tamper-protection status
evidence_strength_indicators: Tamper-protection telemetry and audit logs catching attempted disables in real-time is strong evidence.
Manual attestations without audit logs or tamper-protection flags are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control restricts actions by non-privileged users (a preventive access-control measure); description notes incorporation into AC-6(10).

SI-3(3).differentiation_note: Specifically measures access/privilege enforcement around anti-malware agent modification, unlike siblings that measure updates, detection, or central management.

SI-3(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of malicious-code protection update operations performed by privileged accounts and count unauthorized update attempts by non-privileged users or processes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive access-control/configuration control (privileged-only updates) so percentage of updates done by privileged identities is primary; counts of unauthorized attempts and trend of such attempts are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of agent/update operations (package installs, definition updates) initiated by accounts with privileged roles, and a count and log of any update operations initiated by non-privileged accounts including timestamps and process identifiers.
data_signals: update operation logs (timestamp, host, initiating user/account, process, update type)
user-role mapping (account -> privileged/non-privileged)
privileged account list and change history
change management tickets authorizing updates
policy defining privileged roles allowed to update
evidence_strength_indicators: Authenticated update logs correlating operations to privileged accounts and change tickets are strong evidence.
High-level statements without per-operation audit logs are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Controlling who can perform updates is an access-control/preventive measure; the name explicitly describes privileged-only updates.

SI-3(4).differentiation_note: Unique focus on provenance and authorization of update operations (who performed the update) rather than update success or content.

SI-3(5).measurement_goal: Demonstrate controls over portable storage (percentage of hosts with portable-device controls enforced, count of blocked/allowed device attach events, and exceptions tracked).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Controls over portable storage are preventive (configuration/coverage): percent of hosts with device-control policy enforced is primary; counts of blocked device attaches are useful detective secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of endpoints with portable-storage device blocking or scanning enabled; counts of device attach events and number of blocked/allowed devices correlated to host and user, and exception records.
data_signals: device-control configuration per host (block/scan/allowlist)
device attach/detach logs (timestamp, host, device id, vendor)
blocked device events (timestamp, host, reason)
exceptions/whitelist entries for devices
policy referencing portable storage controls
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated device-control logs showing enforcement and blocked events are strong evidence.
Policy text without device telemetry or only periodic manual checks is weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is about preventing malware via portable media controls (preventive configuration), and is labeled withdrawn/incorporated into MP-7 but functionally preventive.

SI-3(5).differentiation_note: Targets removable-media/device control telemetry (attach/block counts and enforcement coverage) unlike siblings focused on updates or detection.

SI-3(6).measurement_goal: Measure execution and results of scheduled tests/verification of anti-malware controls: test execution rate (% of scheduled tests run), pass/fail rate, number of remediation actions created from failed tests, and time-to-remediate test failures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Testing/verification are detective assurance activities; rates (test execution rate, pass rate, remediation rate) best show whether verification cycles are happening and being acted on, with counts to detail incidents.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and schedule of verification tests executed (test id, target hosts), pass/fail result per test, number of failed-test remediation tickets and time from failure detection to remediation closure.
data_signals: test/exercise schedule and execution logs (test id, start/end, hosts covered)
test results (pass/fail, false positive/negative notes)
remediation tickets created from test failures (timestamps, assignee, closure)
test scripts/tool versions used for verification
policy frequency for testing/verification
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated test execution logs with machine-logged results and linked remediation tickets are strong evidence.
Ad-hoc/manual test reports without execution logs or repeatability are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Testing and verification are detective assurance activities that identify gaps (the name explicitly states testing/verification).

SI-3(6).differentiation_note: Emphasizes scheduled test execution and remediation from test results rather than production detection or update configuration.

SI-3(7).measurement_goal: Measure deployment and effectiveness of nonsignature (heuristic/behavioral) detection: percentage of hosts with nonsignature detection enabled, count of nonsignature alerts, and false-positive/false-negative rates from tuning/verification.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Nonsignature detection is detective; counts of alerts and alert rates are primary to show detections, with coverage percentage as secondary to show deployment.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of nonsignature/heuristic detection alerts by host and detection type, percentage of hosts with the nonsignature engine enabled and reporting, and measured rates of false positives identified during tuning exercises.
data_signals: nonsignature detection engine enabled flag per host
behavioral/heuristic alert logs (timestamp, host, detection name, score)
alert disposition (true positive/false positive) from analyst review
tuning/change logs for heuristic rules
agent version and capability flags
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous behavioral alert telemetry with analyst dispositions is strong evidence.
Relying only on signature detections or occasional test outputs without behavioral telemetry is weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Nonsignature-based detection is explicitly a detective detection capability (detects anomalous/behavioral indicators rather than preventing).

SI-3(7).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on behavioral/heuristic detection telemetry and disposition rates rather than signature/update/configuration coverage.

SI-3(8).measurement_goal: Count and rate of detected unauthorized command executions (unauthenticated or unauthorized command events) by host, and mean time to respond to such detections.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detecting unauthorized commands is a detective function; raw counts of unauthorized command events and rates (per host or per time) best show detection volume, with trends indicating escalation or improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of unauthorized command events (commands executed without proper auth or outside policy) with timestamps, initiating user/process and host; time from detection to containment/remediation; disposition and false-positive flags.
data_signals: command audit logs (timestamp, host, user/process, command)
authentication result for command execution (success/failure)
policy of authorized commands or command whitelists
detection alerts for unauthorized commands (timestamp, host, alert id)
incident/response ticket timestamps for each event
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time command/audit logs and correlated detection alerts with incident response tickets are strong.
Ad-hoc manual reviews or sampled logs without alert correlation are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name explicitly refers to detection of unauthorized commands, which is a detective monitoring capability.

SI-3(8).differentiation_note: Measures command-level audit/detection events and response times specifically, distinct from broader malware detection or update controls.

SI-3(9).measurement_goal: Confirm remote command execution channels require strong authentication: percentage of remote command channels enforcing authentication (and authorized accounts only) and count of unauthenticated remote command attempts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Authentication of remote commands is preventive (access control); percentage of channels enforcing authentication and authorized-account-only execution is primary; counts of unauthenticated attempts are secondary detective evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of hosts and remote access services configured to require authentication for remote command execution, logs of remote command authentication failures and unauthorized attempts, and mapping of which accounts are allowed to execute remote commands.
data_signals: remote-command service configuration (authentication required flag)
authentication logs for remote command sessions (success/failure, account, timestamp)
access control lists for remote command execution
unauthenticated/failed remote command attempt logs
change records for remote-command auth configuration
evidence_strength_indicators: Per-service authentication enforcement telemetry and failure logs are strong evidence.
Policy statements without configuration or authentication logs are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring authentication for remote command execution is an access-control/preventive measure (name indicates authenticate remote commands).

SI-3(9).differentiation_note: Specifically measures authentication enforcement for remote command execution (coverage and failure counts), not general malware detection or update policies.

SI-3(10).measurement_goal: Measure the volume and coverage of malicious-code analysis activities and protections: count of analysis/execution sandbox events, count of detected malicious artifacts (by file hash and verdict), percentage of endpoints/agents with up-to-date malicious-code protection, and median time from detection to quarantine/classification.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control emphasizes analysis/detection activities (analysis runs, detected artifacts) so absolute counts of analysis events and detections are most useful; rates and percentages help show detection density, coverage and update currency over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of malicious-code analysis executions (sandbox/detonation runs) per binary/file hash; count of distinct detected malicious artifacts and their verdicts; timestamped records of quarantines/remediation actions per detected artifact; percentage of endpoints with the protection agent installed and signature/indicator-update timestamps and versions.
data_signals: malicious-code analysis execution logs (sandbox/detonation) with timestamp, file hash, and analysis_id
analysis verdict/classification and confidence score per file hash
count of detected malicious artifacts (distinct file_hash) and detection timestamps
quarantine/remediation action logs with timestamps and actor (automated/manual)
endpoint protection agent installation and last-update timestamp per endpoint
threat/indicator feed update timestamps and signature/version number
automated-analysis failure/error logs (failed analysis runs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, timestamped automated analysis and detection logs (sandbox runs, verdicts, quarantines) correlated with file hashes and endpoint IDs; automated update/version history showing timely signature/indicator refreshes.
Weak: isolated manual analysis reports, one-off malware write-ups, or screenshots that do not show automated, repeatable analysis runs or endpoint coverage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title explicitly references 'Malicious Code Analysis', which implies detection and analysis activities rather than purely preventive configuration.

SI-3(10).differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of analysis/execution events and protection coverage (sandbox/verdict logs, quarantine actions) rather than broader malware policy or training metrics; note: full control text was not provided so taxonomy is derived from the control title.

SI-4.measurement_goal: Measure continuous collection and coverage of system events and security-relevant telemetry across assets, the volume of detected security events/alerts, and the proportion of critical assets with monitoring enabled and healthy log ingestion.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SI-4 is a detective monitoring control where event and alert volumes (counts) directly evidence detection; coverage and health are best expressed as percentages and rates to show completeness and ingestion success over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of security-relevant events ingested per host/service, number of hosts with monitoring agents reporting, success/failure ingestion timestamps, and count of generated security alerts by severity; plus percentage of critical assets with active monitoring agents.
data_signals: event/log ingestion timestamps and counts (per host/service)
monitoring agent installation/deployment inventory (host -> agent version -> status)
log-forwarder/collector success and failure rates
security alert counts by severity and source
monitoring coverage % of critical/high-value assets
alert timestamp and disposition (triaged/closed) for latency measurement
log retention and storage health indicators
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated log ingestion and centralized event store with timestamps and host identifiers — strong evidence
Integrated alerting with source and severity metadata and triage timestamps — strong
Ad-hoc screenshots or one-off export files without ingestion metadata — weak
Periodic manual attestations of monitoring coverage without ingestion metrics — weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'System Monitoring' indicates ongoing detection of system events and alerts, a classic detective function.

SI-4.differentiation_note: This is the parent control defining broad continuous event/telemetry collection and coverage; enhancements specify particular tool types, traffic types, responses, or testing needs.

SI-4(1).measurement_goal: Measure deployment and coverage of a system-wide intrusion detection capability: count of IDS/IPS sensors deployed, percentage of network segments and hosts covered by IDS, count of IDS detections and true positives by severity, and IDS alert-to-triage latency.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detection capabilities are evidenced by numbers of IDS detections and deployed sensors (counts); coverage and integration quality are best shown as percentages and rates.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of IDS sensors and their network/host coverage, counts of IDS alerts by signature/behavior and severity, true positive rate from IDS alerts (validated), and mean time from IDS alert generation to triage.
data_signals: IDS sensor inventory and deployment location (network segment/host association)
IDS alert logs with signature/behavior classification and timestamp
alert validation/triage outcomes (true positive, false positive)
IDS sensor health and uptime metrics
IDS rule/signature update timestamps and versions
alert-to-triage/response time measurements
evidence_strength_indicators: Centralized IDS alert stream with signature IDs, timestamps, and validated dispositions — strong
Regularly updated IDS signatures and documented coverage maps — strong
Isolated appliance logs without centralized correlation or validation — weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name explicitly requires an intrusion detection capability (detective) across the system, indicating detection-focused measurement.

SI-4(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on IDS/IPS deployment, rule updates, and detection performance versus the parent SI-4 which covers general event collection.

SI-4(2).measurement_goal: Measure the presence and effectiveness of automated, real-time analysis tools: count of incidents flagged by real-time analytics, rate of real-time correlation rules firing, and latency from event ingestion to automated analysis output.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective real-time analytics produce events and detections (counts); analysis throughput and latency are best tracked as rates and trends to show sustained performance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Counts of detections produced by automated real-time analysis engines, time delta between event ingestion and analytic output, number of active correlation rules, and % of real-time rules with recent successful executions.
data_signals: real-time analysis engine detection logs with timestamps
correlation rule execution counts and last-run timestamps
event-to-detection latency measurements
detections by analytic model/signature and severity
automated enrichment calls (threat intel lookups) and response outcomes
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated analytic engine logs with event IDs, timestamps, and outputs — strong
Recorded latency metrics for event→analysis→alert — strong
Manual notes about analysis without execution logs — weak

affinity_keywords: Automated Tools and Mechanisms for Real-time Analysis

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement emphasizes automated real-time analysis and correlation, which are detective activities producing alerts/detections.

SI-4(2).differentiation_note: Emphasizes real-time analytics and correlation engine outputs and latency rather than general log collection or IDS-specific detections.

SI-4(3).measurement_goal: Measure integration coverage between monitoring tools and central analysis/correlation platforms: percentage of monitoring sources forwarding events to central SIEM/analytics and count of automated data flows successfully processed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement requires measuring integration/coverage (percentage of tools integrated); counts of successful flows and rates of failed integrations supplement coverage metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of monitoring tools/sources (endpoints, network devices, cloud services) integrated with the central analysis platform, count of successful integration pipelines, and number of failed/errored integration runs with error types.
data_signals: inventory of monitoring tools and their integration status (connected/disconnected)
integration pipeline execution logs with success/failure counts
normalized event ingestion counts per integrated source
timestamps of last successful ingestion per source
error logs for failed parsers/connectors
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated connector logs showing continuous successful ingestion and normalized events — strong
Mapping of sources to ingestion pipelines with last-success timestamps — strong
Manual lists of integration plans without execution logs — weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement focuses on integrating monitoring tools for detection/analysis; measurement of integration coverage supports detection goals, so classified as detective.

SI-4(3).differentiation_note: Uniquely measures integration coverage and pipeline health between monitoring sources and central analytics rather than detection volume or IDS specifics.

SI-4(4).measurement_goal: Measure monitoring coverage and detection of inbound and outbound communications: count and rate of captured flow/log records for inbound/outbound traffic, percentage of network segments with flow collection enabled, and alerts generated from unusual outbound communications.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective monitoring of network communications yields event/flow counts (count) and rates; coverage of flow collection is usefully expressed as a percentage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Counts of flow/log records ingested for inbound and outbound traffic per network segment, percentage of segments and cloud VPCs with flow collection enabled, count of anomalous outbound connection alerts, and flow capture success rates.
data_signals: network flow logs (ingress/egress) with timestamps and src/dst metadata
per-segment/per-VPC flow ingestion counts
flow collection enablement inventory by network segment
anomalous outbound connection alerts and severity
flow collection failure/error logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Centralized flow log ingestion with per-segment counts and timestamps — strong
Alerts tied to flow records with source/destination and timestamps — strong
Manual network diagrams without ingestion evidence — weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: Monitoring inbound/outbound communications is a detection activity; the name specifies capturing traffic for detection.

SI-4(4).differentiation_note: Specifically targets monitoring of network ingress/egress traffic and flow capture coverage rather than host-level logs or IDS signature detections.

SI-4(5).measurement_goal: Measure volume, severity distribution, and disposition effectiveness of system-generated alerts: counts of alerts by source and severity, percentage triaged within target time, and false positive/true positive rates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Alert volumes and dispositions are naturally counts; coverage and timeliness (triage within target windows) are percentages and rates to show effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Counts of system-generated alerts by source and severity, triage/acknowledgement timestamps to compute time-to-triage, disposition labels (true positive/false positive), and % of alerts addressed within defined SLAs.
data_signals: alert logs with source, severity, timestamp, and unique ID
triage/acknowledgement and resolution timestamps
alert disposition labels (true positive/false positive/ignorable)
SLA target for triage and resolution
alert suppression or deduplication counts
evidence_strength_indicators: Centralized alert stream with per-alert lifecycle timestamps and dispositions — strong
Automated triage/acknowledgement records and SLA compliance metrics — strong
Ad-hoc spreadsheets of alert counts without per-alert metadata — weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement addresses management of system-generated alerts (detection outputs), a detective function focused on alert volume and disposition.

SI-4(5).differentiation_note: Focuses on alert lifecycle metrics (volume, triage timeliness, disposition) rather than raw log collection or integration coverage.

SI-4(6).measurement_goal: Note: withdrawn enhancement (incorporated into AC-6(10)); if measured historically, evidence would be the percentage of non-privileged accounts prevented from performing privileged monitoring actions and the count of policy violations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The enhancement focused on restricting non-privileged users (access control), so measurement would emphasize coverage/percent of accounts correctly restricted; percentage is most appropriate for coverage checks.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of non-privileged user accounts that lack privileged monitoring permissions, count of access policy violations where non-privileged users accessed monitoring controls, and audit logs showing attempted privileged actions by non-privileged users.
data_signals: user account role/privilege assignment records
access control policy definitions for monitoring functions
audit logs showing attempts to access monitoring configuration or data by non-privileged accounts
access violation/alert counts and timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated role/privilege inventories with evidence of denied actions in audit logs — strong
Manual attestations that policies exist without audit evidence — weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: Although the enhancement concerns access restriction (preventive), the input marks it as withdrawn and incorporated elsewhere; measurement historically would verify restrictions via detective audit logs.

SI-4(6).differentiation_note: This enhancement is withdrawn and its control intent moved to AC-6(10); its measurement focuses on access restriction evidence rather than monitoring telemetry.

SI-4(7).measurement_goal: Measure the existence and effectiveness of automated responses: rate and success percentage of automated playbooks executed in response to suspicious events, count of incidents mitigated automatically, and mean time to containment for automated actions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated response is corrective/preventive in nature; measuring execution success and remediation velocity (rates) and success percentages shows effectiveness of automated actions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and rate of automated playbook/execution runs triggered by suspicious events, percentage of runs that completed successfully and achieved intended containment, timestamps for trigger→action→resolution to compute time-to-containment.
data_signals: automation/orchestration execution logs with trigger event IDs and timestamps
automation success/failure status and error codes
incident records showing automated actions taken and containment outcomes
count of incidents fully remediated/contained by automation
time delta from alert generation to automated action and to containment
evidence_strength_indicators: Automation execution logs tied to originating alert IDs and documented containment results — strong
Playbooks documented but no execution logs or unmeasured manual steps — weak
High success rate and low time-to-containment — strong indicators of effective automation

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly requires automated response actions to suspicious events, which are corrective (remediation/containment) activities.

SI-4(7).differentiation_note: Unlike other SI-4 enhancements focused on detection and coverage, this one is centered on automated remediation actions and their success/timeliness.

SI-4(8).measurement_goal: Note: withdrawn (incorporated into SI-4). If measured, evidence would include access controls and integrity checks for monitoring data (counts of unauthorized access attempts, percentage of monitoring data encrypted at rest/in-transit).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Protection-focused measurements are typically coverage or compliance percentages (e.g., % of monitoring data encrypted) with counts of access attempts as supporting metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of monitoring/telemetry data encrypted at rest and in transit, count of unauthorized access attempts to monitoring repositories, and integrity verification logs for monitoring data stores.
data_signals: encryption-at-rest flags for monitoring storage buckets/DBs
TLS/secure transport enforcement logs for collectors
access control policy and ACLs for monitoring data stores
access audit logs showing access attempts and outcomes
data integrity verification/checksum logs
evidence_strength_indicators: System logs showing encryption enforcement and access denials with timestamps — strong
End-to-end documented encryption policy without technical enforcement evidence — weak

control_type_classification.reasoning: Although protective in intent, the input marks it withdrawn and incorporated into SI-4; measurement focuses on detecting unauthorized access and verifying protections (audit evidence), so classified as detective for monitoring of protection.

SI-4(8).differentiation_note: Withdrawn enhancement that would uniquely require measuring confidentiality/integrity controls for monitoring data rather than detection coverage or alerting.

SI-4(9).measurement_goal: Measure testing coverage and efficacy of monitoring tools: percentage of monitoring tools tested on schedule, pass/fail rate of monitoring functional tests, and number of defects found and remediated from tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Testing/control validation is best evidenced by percent-tested coverage and pass rates; counts of defects and trend over time help demonstrate improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of monitoring tools and mechanisms that have completed scheduled functional tests, counts of test failures and defects by tool, timestamps of test executions, and remediation completion rates for test-identified issues.
data_signals: test execution logs with tool identifier, test cases run, and timestamps
test pass/fail outcomes and failure reasons
scheduled test calendar and last-execution timestamps per tool
defect/issue tracker entries created from tests and remediation status
test coverage matrix mapping tests to monitoring capabilities
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated test execution logs with detailed failure reasons and linked remediation tickets — strong
Infrequent manual test reports without execution logs or remediation evidence — weak
Improving pass rates and declining defect counts over time — strong

affinity_keywords: Testing of Monitoring Tools and Mechanisms

control_type_classification.reasoning: Testing of monitoring tools is aimed at verifying detection capabilities (detective); metrics focus on test pass rates and defect remediation.

SI-4(9).differentiation_note: Uniquely requires scheduled validation/testing evidence and remediation tracking for monitoring tools rather than runtime alert/ingestion metrics.

SI-4(10).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and completeness of monitoring for encrypted communications: percentage of encrypted sessions for which metadata or decrypted inspection is available, and count of encrypted flows that are blind spots for monitoring.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about visibility/coverage (a percent of encrypted traffic observed); counts of blind flows and trends over time are useful supplements to show scale and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of encrypted communication sessions (per host/segment) with available monitoring metadata or decrypted inspection, plus count of encrypted flows lacking any observable metadata; grouped by network segment and sensor. Include sensor-to-host mapping and timestamps to show continuous coverage.
data_signals: encrypted session metadata capture logs (SNI, cipher suite, session timestamps)
flow record coverage counts for encrypted flows (NetFlow/IPFIX records with encryption flag)
decryption/appliance inspection logs with session IDs and timestamps
monitoring sensor inventory and coverage map (sensor -> segment)
list/count of encrypted sessions with no metadata ('blind spot' sessions)
sensor health and ingestion success/failure logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated flow/metadata capture and decryption appliance logs correlated to assets is strong evidence.
Point-in-time packet captures or one-off reports are weak evidence because they don't demonstrate ongoing visibility.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control's title emphasizes 'System Monitoring' and 'Visibility', which are detective activities that observe encrypted communications.

SI-4(10).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on monitoring coverage for encrypted traffic (visibility and blind spots) rather than general traffic anomaly detection or alert generation.

SI-4(11).measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of detected communications traffic anomalies, the proportion of anomalies that are investigated/escalated, and anomaly types by severity and source segment.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective control focused on identifying anomalies — counts of anomaly detections and rates (anomalies per hour/per segment) directly evidence operation; distributions show types/severity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of traffic-anomaly detection events (per detection rule/model) with source/destination, timestamp, anomaly category, and severity; percent of anomaly events that are triaged/escalated within defined timeframes; baseline comparison against historical traffic patterns.
data_signals: network flow anomaly detection alerts (with timestamp, source/destination, anomaly type)
IDS/IPS anomaly rule hit logs
anomaly model baseline metrics and thresholds
alert triage/escalation records (ticket IDs, timestamps)
anomaly severity classification and counts by type
volume/spike metrics (bytes/session) associated with anomalies
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated anomaly alerts with linked triage tickets and historical baseline comparisons are strong evidence.
Ad hoc analyst notes or one-off captures without baseline models are weak evidence for ongoing anomaly analysis.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly references analyzing traffic anomalies, a detective monitoring activity.

SI-4(11).differentiation_note: Targets detection and analysis of anomalous communications traffic specifically, rather than broad pattern correlation or alert automation.

SI-4(12).measurement_goal: Measure volume and quality of organization-generated automated alerts: count of automated alerts, alert-to-incident conversion rate, and false positive rate for automated alerts over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This detective control is about automated alerts; raw counts of alerts and rates (conversion, false-positive) are primary evidence; percentages show coverage/quality.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of automatically generated alerts (by source system and rule) and mappings to incidents/tickets; calculate alert-to-incident conversion rate and false positive rate (alerts closed as non-actionable) over rolling windows.
data_signals: alert generation logs with source system and rule ID
alert metadata (priority, timestamp, automation flag)
incident/ticket linkage records (alert -> incident ID, timestamps)
alert disposition labels (true positive, false positive, duplicate)
automation playbook execution logs triggered by alerts
alert suppression/aggregation configuration records
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated alert logs with persistent linkage to incident records and disposition tags are strong evidence.
Manual summary reports or unlinked alerts without disposition data are weak evidence for automated alert effectiveness.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on organization-generated automated alerts — a classic detective monitoring function producing observable events.

SI-4(12).differentiation_note: Specifically measures the automation and quality of system-generated alerts (volumes and conversion) rather than generic anomaly detection or correlation capabilities.

SI-4(13).measurement_goal: Measure detection coverage and effectiveness of traffic and event pattern analysis: count of pattern matches detected, percent of event streams included in pattern analysis, and trend of pattern match rates and false positives.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective analysis yields discrete pattern matches (counts) while coverage and evolution over time are best captured as percentages and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of detected pattern matches (per rule/model) with associated event counts, percent of ingestible event sources included in pattern engines, and trend of pattern match counts and false positives over time.
data_signals: event log ingestion metrics (source, records per minute)
pattern rule/library registry with version and coverage
pattern match logs (rule ID, matched event IDs, timestamp)
correlation engine outputs linking events into patterns
false positive/true positive disposition counts per pattern
event source inclusion matrix (which sources feed the pattern engine)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous correlation engine outputs with pattern library versioning and dispositions are strong evidence.
Static rule lists or manual pattern descriptions without match logs are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control language 'Analyze Traffic and Event Patterns' indicates ongoing detection and analysis of event streams (detective).

SI-4(13).differentiation_note: Focused on pattern-level analysis across events and traffic, requiring coverage metrics for event ingestion and pattern library match counts (unlike single-point anomaly detection).

SI-4(14).measurement_goal: Measure wireless intrusion detection coverage and detection outcomes: percentage of wireless coverage by IDS sensors, count of wireless intrusion alerts (rogue APs, suspicious associations), and mean time to investigate wireless alerts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Wireless IDS is detective; counts of wireless intrusion alerts and coverage percentages are primary indicators of operation and completeness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of wireless intrusion detection events (rogue AP, deauth storms, suspicious client associations) with sensor ID and timestamps; percentage of wireless coverage (area/SSID) monitored by active sensors and sensor operational status.
data_signals: wireless IDS alert logs with alert type and timestamp
rogue AP detection records (BSSID, channel, SSID)
client association anomaly logs
wireless sensor inventory and floor/area coverage mapping
sensor uptime/heartbeat and ingestion metrics
investigation/ticketing records for wireless alerts
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous wireless IDS logs correlated with a documented sensor coverage map and investigation tickets are strong evidence.
Occasional site surveys or manual snapshots without persistent logs are weak evidence for ongoing wireless intrusion detection.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly references 'Wireless Intrusion Detection', a monitoring/detection capability.

SI-4(14).differentiation_note: Specifically measures wireless sensor coverage and wireless-specific intrusion events rather than general network traffic anomalies or correlation.

SI-4(15).measurement_goal: Measure monitoring coverage and detected policy violations for communications crossing wireless and wireline boundaries: percentage of cross-domain flows observed and count of policy-violating cross-boundary connections.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control concerns visibility/coverage of wireless-to-wireline communications (a coverage-percent measurement is most relevant), with counts of violations as a secondary evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of flows traversing wireless-to-wireline gateways captured by monitoring systems and count of cross-boundary flows that violate segmentation or access policies; include gateway logs and firewall/bridge logs tied to timestamps and policy IDs.
data_signals: gateway flow logs for wireless-to-wireline traffic (source/destination, bytes, timestamps)
firewall/ACL logs for cross-boundary connections
segmentation policy records and violations
edge sensor ingestion metrics for cross-domain flows
list/count of cross-boundary connections flagged for policy violations
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous flow capture and policy enforcement logs for the wireless-wireline boundary are strong evidence.
Manual network diagrams or periodic tests without continuous logs are weak for proving ongoing monitoring.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes monitoring communications across wireless and wireline domains — an observational/detection activity.

SI-4(15).differentiation_note: Focuses on monitoring the specific boundary between wireless and wireline networks and measuring cross-boundary flow coverage and policy violations.

SI-4(16).measurement_goal: Measure the extent and timeliness of correlation: count of correlated incidents/events produced by correlation engines, correlation latency, and percent of incoming alerts/events that are enriched/correlated.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective control about correlating monitoring info — counts of correlated events/incidents and correlation rates are primary; latency trends are secondary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of events/alerts that are correlated into multi-event incidents (with correlation rule IDs), correlation processing latency (time from earliest event to correlation output), and percent of total event volume that is correlated/enriched.
data_signals: SIEM/Correlation engine output logs (correlation_id, constituent event IDs, rule ID, timestamp)
event ingestion counts by source
enrichment logs (threat intel lookups, asset/context enrichment)
correlation latency metrics (event timestamp to correlation timestamp)
percentage of alerts/events linked to a correlation incident
evidence_strength_indicators: Persistent correlation engine logs with rule IDs and linked event IDs plus low latency are strong evidence.
Manual spreadsheets or occasional correlation summaries without event-level linkage are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control refers to correlating monitoring information, which is a detective process that links observations into incidents.

SI-4(16).differentiation_note: Requires measurement of correlation coverage and latency (event-level linkage) whereas sibling controls focus on raw detection or pattern analysis.

SI-4(17).measurement_goal: Measure availability and completeness of integrated situational awareness: percent of required data sources ingested into situational dashboards and count/percentage of incidents with full contextual data available at triage.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Situational awareness focuses on integrated coverage and completeness (percent of sources/data), with counts of incidents having full context as a secondary measure.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of required data feeds (logs, alerts, asset context, threat intel) successfully ingested into situational dashboards; count and percentage of incidents where the dashboard provided complete contextual fields at time of triage.
data_signals: dashboard ingestion status per data source (success/failure, last ingest timestamp)
list of required data sources and their ingestion coverage
incident records indicating available contextual fields at triage
data latency metrics (time from event generation to dashboard availability)
dashboard uptime/availability metrics
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time ingestion logs showing source-level success and incident records populated from the dashboard are strong evidence.
Static screenshots or ad hoc exports without ingestion timestamps are weak evidence for integrated situational awareness.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Integrated situational awareness aggregates monitoring inputs for detection and response, a detective/response-enabling capability.

SI-4(17).differentiation_note: Emphasizes ingestion and dashboard/context completeness for incident triage, unlike sibling controls that focus on raw detection or correlation.

SI-4(18).measurement_goal: Measure detection of covert exfiltration: count of suspected exfiltration events, percent of outbound flows exceeding baseline thresholds flagged for investigation, and percent of flagged events investigated to closure.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective control focused on exfiltration — counts of suspected exfil events and rates relative to baseline capture the control's operation; percentages show investigation coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of covert-exfiltration detection alerts (DLP, DNS tunneling, unusual outbound transfer) with volume, destination, and timestamps; percent of outbound flows exceeding baseline thresholds that were flagged and investigated; time to investigate and close.
data_signals: DLP incident logs (data type, volume, source user, destination)
outbound flow volume and destination records with baseline comparisons
DNS logs indicating tunneling patterns
suspicious large file transfer logs (SFTP/FTP/HTTP POST) with hashes and destinations
exfiltration detection alerts with investigation/ticket linkage
destination reputation lookups and enrichment results
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous DLP/outbound flow logs tied to investigation records are strong evidence; sporadic scans or retrospective reports are weak.
Detection alerts that lack linked investigation tickets or context enrichment are weak evidence for effective exfiltration detection.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes analyzing traffic for covert exfiltration, a detective monitoring and data protection detection activity.

SI-4(18).differentiation_note: Specifically targets detection of covert data exfiltration (DLP/outbound anomalies) rather than general traffic anomalies or pattern correlation.

SI-4(19).measurement_goal: Measure indicators of individual-level risk: count of events indicating potential exposure or misuse of individual data (PII/identifiable data), percent of such events investigated, and trend in number of individuals affected over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective/control-cum-compliance measurement of incidents affecting individuals is best evidenced by counts (incidents, affected individuals) and trends over time; percentages show investigation coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of monitoring events that indicate risk to individuals (unauthorized access to PII, data export events involving identifiers), number of affected individual records per event, percent of events triaged/investigated within target timeframes, and remediation outcomes.
data_signals: access logs to data stores containing PII (user ID, record IDs accessed, timestamps)
data export and download logs with user and destination
DLP incidents tagged as containing personal data
incident/ticket records including number of individuals impacted
notification/response records (data subject notification, containment actions)
evidence_strength_indicators: Event logs linked to incident records that enumerate affected individual records and show remediation/notification are strong evidence.
High-level summaries without linkage to individual record counts or investigations are weak evidence of effective individual risk monitoring.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Risk for Individuals' under System Monitoring indicates detection of events that create individual risk (detective), with a compliance/response aspect.

SI-4(19).differentiation_note: Focuses on individual-level privacy/data exposure metrics (affected individuals, PII access) rather than network-level anomalies or system-wide event correlation.

SI-4(20).measurement_goal: Measure the presence and completeness of monitoring for privileged users: percent of privileged accounts with session/activity logging enabled, count of privileged-user audit events generated per time period, and count of anomalous privileged actions detected and investigated.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective monitoring control; primary evidence is event/alert volumes (counts). Coverage (percentage of privileged accounts monitored) and alert rates are useful secondaries to show completeness and alerting effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of privileged accounts in authoritative inventory; count of privileged-account audit/log events ingested per day; percentage of privileged accounts with monitoring/agent/audit enabled; count of anomalous privileged-user alerts and their disposition (investigated/resolved).
data_signals: authoritative privileged account inventory (account id, role, privilege level)
privileged-account audit/event logs (timestamp, user id, action, source IP)
privileged activity alert records (alert id, severity, timestamp, rule matched)
monitoring coverage report showing which privileged accounts have logging/agent enabled
investigation/closure tickets for privileged-user alerts (ticket id, status, resolution timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated ingestion of privileged-account audit logs with timestamps and user IDs is strong evidence.
Point-in-time screenshots or ad-hoc manual reports are weak evidence compared with sustained log streams and coverage reports.
High coverage percentage of privileged accounts with monitoring enabled and low counts of unanalyzed privileged alerts strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Name explicitly references 'System Monitoring' which is a monitoring/detection activity for privileged users, indicating a detective control.

SI-4(20).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on monitoring and auditing activity for privileged accounts (coverage and alert counts) rather than general system monitoring or network service discovery.

SI-4(21).measurement_goal: Measure monitoring coverage and anomalous activity for users in probationary periods: percentage of probationary accounts with elevated monitoring enabled, and count/rate of monitored anomalous events originating from probationary accounts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective monitoring control (monitoring probationary users). Primary metrics are event/alert counts; coverage percentage is a necessary secondary to demonstrate monitoring is applied to the population.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Authoritative list of probationary users with start/end dates; count of audit/log events attributed to probationary users; percentage of probationary users with enhanced monitoring/audit enabled; count of investigations opened for probationary-user alerts.
data_signals: probationary user roster (user id, probation start, probation end, department)
audit/event logs filtered by probationary user ids (timestamp, action, source)
monitoring configuration indicating which accounts have elevated logging
alerts triggered by probationary users with disposition (open/closed)
investigation ticket metadata for probationary-user incidents
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated correlation of probationary roster with incoming telemetry and consistent alert generation for anomalous probationary-user activity is strong evidence.
Relying solely on manual reviews of probationary accounts without log linkage is weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name centers on 'System Monitoring' for 'Probationary Periods', indicating detection/monitoring of user activity during probation.

SI-4(21).differentiation_note: Targets the subset of users in probationary periods and requires linking user status (probationary) to monitoring coverage and alerts, unlike generic privileged-user monitoring.

SI-4(22).measurement_goal: Measure detection and elimination of unauthorized network services: count of unauthorized services discovered on the network per period, percent of network assets running services not in the authorized service inventory, and time-to-remediate unauthorized service findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control (detection of unauthorized services); counts of unauthorized-service findings are primary. Coverage/percentage of assets with unauthorized services and trends over time are useful secondaries.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Authoritative inventory of authorized network services and ports; results from network/host service discovery scans; count of detected services not in the authorized inventory; timestamps for detection and remediation to compute mean time to remediate.
data_signals: authorized service inventory (service name, allowed ports, environments)
network/host service discovery/port scan results (asset id, open port, service signature, timestamp)
network flow/session records indicating service protocols
IDS/IPS alerts correlated to unauthorized service signatures
remediation ticket records for unauthorized service findings (created/resolved timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated continuous discovery scans correlated against an authoritative authorized-service inventory and quick remediation tickets are strong evidence.
Infrequent manual inventory checks or one-off scans without correlation to the authorized inventory are weak.
