control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly concerns 'System Monitoring' for 'Unauthorized Network Services' — a detection activity to find unauthorized services.

SI-4(22).differentiation_note: Specifically focuses on discovery and remediation of network services that are not authorized, requiring service inventory correlation and remediation timing metrics rather than general host telemetry coverage.

SI-4(23).measurement_goal: Measure host/endpoint monitoring coverage and telemetry health: percentage of hosts with host-based monitoring agents installed and reporting, count of host-based alerts generated, and count of hosts missing telemetry or with stale heartbeats.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Although detective in nature, this control implies a coverage requirement for host-based devices; percentage coverage is the most direct primary metric, with counts of alerts and stale hosts as useful secondaries.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Host inventory with agent installation status and last-checkin timestamp; percentage of hosts with telemetry forwarded to central monitoring; count of host-based IDS/HIDS alerts and count of hosts with missing/stale telemetry exceeding allowed thresholds.
data_signals: host inventory (host id, OS, role, agent installed flag)
agent heartbeat/last check-in timestamps per host
host-based telemetry ingestion logs (events per host, timestamps)
host-based alerts (alert id, host id, rule matched, timestamp)
list of hosts with missing/stale telemetry (host id, last seen)
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time agent heartbeats and continuous telemetry streams from hosts correlated to inventory showing >90-100% coverage is strong evidence.
Periodic manual surveys of agent installation without ingestion proof is weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name focuses on 'System Monitoring' of 'Host-based Devices', indicating detection via host telemetry and agent coverage.

SI-4(23).differentiation_note: Emphasizes host/endpoint agent deployment and telemetry health (coverage and stale heartbeats), which differs from user- or network-focused monitoring controls.

SI-4(24).measurement_goal: Measure ingestion and detection of Indicators of Compromise (IOCs): count of IOC feed items ingested, count of IOC matches/hits across telemetry, detection rate of IOCs, and mean time from IOC ingestion to first match detection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective/threat-intel control where IOC matches and alert counts (counts) are primary indicators; detection rate and trend are useful to assess effectiveness and timeliness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of IOC entries ingested from threat feeds; number of IOC matches in endpoint/network logs with timestamps and IOC value; time delta from IOC ingestion to first match; count of investigations opened for IOC hits.
data_signals: IOC feed ingestion logs (feed id, IOC type, IOC value, ingestion timestamp)
IOC match records (IOC value, asset id, telemetry type, match timestamp)
telemetry sources scanned for IOC (endpoint logs, network flows, proxy logs)
alerts/tickets created from IOC matches (ticket id, status, resolution timestamp)
counts of unique IOC hits per period
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated ingestion of IOC feeds correlated against continuous telemetry with timestamped IOC match records is strong evidence.
Manual, retrospective IOC hunts without ingestion logs or timestamped matches are weak evidence for ongoing IOC detection capability.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on monitoring for 'Indicators of Compromise', an inherently detective capability to identify malicious activity.

SI-4(24).differentiation_note: Specifically measures ingestion and match activity for threat intelligence/IOCs, requiring correlation across telemetry feeds and IOC feed ingestion timestamps — unlike broader anomaly detection controls.

SI-4(25).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of network traffic analysis: percent of critical network segments with flow/packet capture enabled, sampling rate and throughput of network-analysis pipelines, and count of actionable alerts from network traffic analysis per period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective capability focused on network traffic analysis, primary metrics are counts of alerts and captured sessions; percentage coverage of capture points is a key secondary metric to show optimization/coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Inventory of network capture/analysis points and which critical segments they cover; network flow and packet capture ingestion rates; sampling rates configured at capture points; count of NTA-generated alerts and their disposition; retention/processing latency for captures.
data_signals: network capture point inventory (capture point id, location, segments covered)
netflow/session capture rates (records per second, timestamped)
packet capture retention and ingestion logs (pcap file timestamps, sizes)
sampling rate configuration per capture point
NTA/Network-IDS alerts generated from traffic analysis (alert id, severity, timestamp)
processing latency metrics (time from capture to analysis/alert)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous network flow/packet capture ingestion with documented coverage per critical segment and low processing latency is strong evidence.
Intermittent manual captures or sampling without documented coverage metrics are weak evidence for optimized network traffic analysis.

affinity_keywords: System Monitoring | Optimize Network Traffic Analysis

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name calls for 'System Monitoring' that 'Optimize[s] Network Traffic Analysis', indicating ongoing detection through network telemetry.

SI-4(25).differentiation_note: Focused on network-capture coverage, sampling rates, and analysis pipeline performance (throughput/latency), rather than host-agent coverage or IOC ingestion.

SI-5.measurement_goal: Measure the volume and timeliness of security alerts/advisories received and disseminated, plus the percentage and timeliness of associated directives or advisory-driven actions implemented (e.g., tickets/patches) against organizational policy targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SI-5 is primarily about receiving and tracking discrete alerts/advisories (counts) and measuring coverage/implementation (percentages, rates); trending is useful to show improvement in handling.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of distinct security alerts/advisories received per source and per timeframe, time between alert receipt and internal dissemination, count and percent of advisories associated with an issued directive/action ticket, and time-to-implementation for those directives compared to policy-defined targets.
data_signals: alert/advisory ingestion timestamps (per advisory ID and source)
source identifier (vendor, mailing list, ISAC, CVE feed) for each advisory
internal dissemination logs (timestamped distribution events and recipients)
acknowledgement receipts or assignment records from responsible owners
remediation/action ticket IDs linked to advisory (creation and closure timestamps)
implementation timestamps (patch deployment, configuration change logs) tied to directive
overdue advisory/action count (tickets past policy response timeframe)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped ingestion logs and dissemination records correlated to remediation tickets and deployment logs; shows end-to-end handling.
Weak: ad-hoc email threads or manual spreadsheets without persistent timestamps or traceability from advisory to action.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on receiving and tracking security alerts/advisories (detective activity) and ensuring they are processed and acted upon.

SI-5.differentiation_note: This entry covers the end-to-end process of ingesting, disseminating, and implementing advisories/directives (including manual and tracked actions), not the automation mechanics addressed in SI-5(1).

SI-5(1).measurement_goal: Measure automated advisory ingestion and processing coverage, automation success rates (alert→ticket or alert→distribution), time-to-automated-dispatch, and false-positive rates for automated advisories handling.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated alerts produce discrete events (counts); success/failure and coverage are best expressed as rates/percentages and trended over time to prove reliability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of automated alerts/advisories ingested, number and percent successfully processed (e.g., auto-created tickets or auto-distributed), average processing latency from ingestion to automated action, and false-positive classification counts to evaluate automation quality.
data_signals: automated alert ingestion logs with timestamps and advisory IDs
automation-run logs showing processing outcome (success/failure) per alert
auto-ticket creation records (ticket ID, creation timestamp, linked advisory ID)
automation-to-distribution logs (recipients, timestamp)
processing latency per alert (ingestion → action) and SLA target
automation error/failure reasons and counts
false-positive/false-discovery labels for automated advisories
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-generated, timestamped ingestion and automation logs linking each advisory to automated actions (tickets/distribution) with low failure and false-positive rates.
Weak: manual reconciliation of automated alerts or manual batch processing without per-alert success/failure logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement specifies automated receipt and processing of alerts/advisories (detective instrumentation and automation of alert workflows).

SI-5(1).differentiation_note: Focused specifically on automation metrics (ingestion, processing success, latency, false positives) rather than manual dissemination or implementation tracking covered by SI-5.

SI-6.measurement_goal: Measure the number and outcome of verification tests executed against documented security and privacy functions, the coverage of functions with recent verification, and the count and age of unresolved failed verifications (with associated remediation tracking).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SI-6 is a verification (detective) control — auditors want event counts (tests executed, failures) as primary evidence, with coverage percentages and trends as secondary views of effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of verification/test executions per security/privacy function and per system component, test result status (pass/fail) with timestamps, mapping of each test to the function/component it exercises, and associated remediation ticket IDs and resolution timestamps for failed tests.
data_signals: security function test execution logs with timestamps and test IDs
test result records (pass/fail) and failure details
mapping table of security/privacy functions to system components/assets
last execution timestamp per verification test and per function
remediation ticket IDs, creation and closure timestamps for failed tests
automated test harness output and raw logs
test schedule/plan metadata and test owner assignments
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated test execution logs with persistent test IDs and timestamps are strong evidence.
A maintained mapping of functions to components and up-to-date last-execution timestamps is strong evidence of coverage.
One-off manual test reports or undated screenshots are weak evidence for ongoing verification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'Verification' and references to tests/results indicate this control is intended to detect whether security/privacy functions operate as intended.

SI-6.differentiation_note: Broad, program-level verification coverage across security and privacy functions (counts and coverage) rather than the specific mechanics of notification, automation orchestration, or report formatting covered by the sub-controls.

SI-6(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of failed security/function verification tests that generated automated notifications/alerts to the designated owners within the defined SLA, and the time-to-notify distribution for failed tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive/operational control (notification/prevention of unaddressed failures), the key measure is coverage/compliance (percentage of failures that produced notifications) with counts and trends as supporting metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of failed test events that generated an automated notification record, time delta between test failure timestamp and notification delivery timestamp, and list of recipients resolved against expected owner mappings.
data_signals: failed test event records with timestamps
notification/alerting system delivery logs with timestamps and recipient addresses
mapping of test IDs to designated owners/notification recipients
notification SLA or expected delivery window metadata
missed notification counts and exceptions
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated notification logs with timestamps and delivery receipts are strong evidence.
System-generated linking of test ID → notification → owner is strong; manual email threads or undocumented notifications are weak.
High percentage of immediate notifications (within SLA) is strong; sporadic manual notifications are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control (originally focused on notifying on failed tests) is intended to prevent unaddressed failures by ensuring alerts — a preventive objective, though the control text notes it was withdrawn and incorporated into SI-6.

SI-6(1).differentiation_note: Specifically measures delivery and timeliness of notifications for failed tests (now incorporated into SI-6), rather than broader verification coverage or automation orchestration.

SI-6(2).measurement_goal: Measure the count and success rate of automated distributed verification test runs across nodes/components, and the percentage of security functions with automated, distributed test coverage (orchestration success rate and failure reasons).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This sub-control emphasizes automated, distributed test execution (detective/actionable events) so counts of automated runs and success/failure rates are primary; percentages of coverage and orchestration success rates are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of automated distributed test executions per host/node, orchestration success/failure status per run, test harness/version used, and coverage (which functions/components were exercised by automated tests).
data_signals: automation orchestration logs with run IDs, node/host IDs and timestamps
test run records including success/failure codes and error messages
test harness version and configuration metadata
scheduled vs actual execution timestamps (scheduling metadata)
coverage mapping showing which functions/components were exercised by each distributed run
orchestration retry and timeout logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Machine-generated orchestration logs with node-level details and consistent run IDs are strong evidence.
High automated run success rate and complete coverage mapping is strong; intermittent manual orchestration or spotty node logs are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Automation Support for Distributed Testing' indicates active automated testing to verify functions, a detective activity measuring executed test events and results.

SI-6(2).differentiation_note: Focused on the automation/orchestration mechanics and node-level execution success of distributed tests rather than on notification or report formatting/completeness.

SI-6(3).measurement_goal: Measure the count and timeliness of verification reports produced for security/privacy function tests, the percent of tests with an associated verification report meeting required content criteria, and the distribution of pass/fail findings in those reports.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective/reporting controls are evidenced by artifacts — counts of generated reports and counts of timely/complete reports are primary, with percentages for completeness and trends for historical compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of verification reports generated per scheduled test, report generation timestamps relative to test completion, checklist of required report fields present, reviewer/approver sign-off records, and distribution/delivery logs for each report.
data_signals: verification report artifacts and metadata (report ID, generation timestamp)
report content checklist presence/flags (required fields present)
report delivery logs and recipient list
review/approval records (sign-off timestamps, reviewer IDs)
linkage of report to originating test execution ID and test result
report version history and change audit trail
evidence_strength_indicators: Structured, machine-generated reports with immutable timestamps and linked test IDs plus signed approvals are strong evidence.
Loose PDF reports without metadata, or manually compiled summaries, are weak evidence for consistent verification reporting.

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'Report Verification Results' implies generation of detection artifacts (reports) summarizing verification activity, which is a detective/reporting function.

SI-6(3).differentiation_note: Concentrates on the existence, timeliness, completeness and approval of verification reports (artifact quality) rather than test execution, notification, or automation orchestration.

SI-7.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and operational status of integrity controls for software, firmware, and information: percent of managed assets with integrity protection enabled, count of integrity violations detected, and mean time to detect integrity violations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As an overarching integrity control the auditor needs coverage metrics (percentage of assets with integrity controls enabled) plus event counts and detection/remediation trends to validate operation and maturity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets by asset class (servers, endpoints, network devices, cloud instances) that have configured integrity controls (file/firmware signing verification, runtime integrity agents) and the count/timestamps of integrity violations and remediation actions per asset. Include asset identifiers, control configuration state, last integrity scan/check timestamp, and remediation timelines.
data_signals: asset inventory with integrity_control_enabled flag
integrity check execution timestamps per asset
integrity violation/alert events with severity and timestamp
signed binary/firmware signature verification results
mean time to detect (MTTD) integrity violation per asset
mean time to remediate (MTTR) integrity violation per asset
coverage percentage by asset type
evidence_strength_indicators: automated integrity check logs and signed verification results with timestamps (strong)
centralized inventory showing integrity_control_enabled for all assets (strong)
ad-hoc manual attestations or single-point-in-time screenshots (weak)
infrequent/annual reports without event-level logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: This top-level SI-7 control defines integrity protections to prevent and detect unauthorized modification, implying preventive coverage requirements.

SI-7.differentiation_note: Top-level overview control requiring broad coverage and combined event/count/trend metrics across all subcontrols rather than focused measurements like alerting or cryptography.

SI-7(1).measurement_goal: Count and frequency of integrity checks executed and the percentage of assets with successful recent integrity verification: track per-asset last successful check, failures, and open verification issues.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Integrity checks are event-driven/detective; counts of checks and failed checks plus coverage percentages show operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of integrity check executions per asset and per asset class over the reporting period, last integrity check result per asset (pass/fail), and count of failed verifications grouped by file/firmware/component with timestamps and associated hashes/signature verifications.
data_signals: integrity_check_execution logs (asset_id, timestamp, result)
file/firmware hash values and expected hashes
signature verification results (valid/invalid)
failed_verification_count by asset and component
last_successful_verification_timestamp per asset
scan/check coverage percentage by asset type
evidence_strength_indicators: continuous automated integrity check logs with per-file/hash verification (strong)
signed verification manifests and timestamped results (strong)
periodic manual file inventories without hash verification (weak)
single point-in-time checks without historical logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes performing integrity checks and verification which are detective activities that generate observable events.

SI-7(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on executing and recording integrity verification operations (checks, hashes, signatures) rather than alerting, response, or central management.

SI-7(2).measurement_goal: Measure alerting coverage and timeliness: count of automated integrity violation notifications generated, percent of violations that produced notifications, and average time from violation detection to notification delivery.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This subcontrol concerns automated notifications (alert events) so counts and rates of alerts and notification success rates are primary to evidence operation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of integrity violation events detected and number of corresponding automated notifications generated and delivered, with timestamps for detection and notification delivery, notification channel, and delivery status (delivered/failed).
data_signals: integrity_violation_event records (event_id, asset_id, timestamp, severity)
notification events/logs (notification_id, event_id, timestamp, channel, status)
notification delivery success/failure counts
time_delta detection_to_notification per event
unnotified_violation_count
evidence_strength_indicators: automated event-to-notification linkage with timestamps in central logging (strong)
notification delivery receipts or callback confirmations (strong)
manual email threads or ticket creation without automated linkage (weak)
alerts documented only in spreadsheets without event correlation (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control specifies automated notifications for integrity violations which are detective functions generating alerts.

SI-7(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures the notification pipeline (detection → notification) rather than the detection mechanisms or remediation actions.

SI-7(3).measurement_goal: Measure deployment and configuration coverage of centrally managed integrity tools: percentage of target assets enrolled in the central integrity tooling and count of configuration drift instances detected against the centrally managed baseline.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Central management implies coverage and compliance; percentage of assets enrolled plus counts of drift/errors demonstrates effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets enrolled in the central integrity management platform, per-asset configuration compliance status against centrally defined integrity policies, and counts of agent health/communication failures and configuration drift events with timestamps.
data_signals: central_tooling_enrollment records (asset_id, enrolled_flag, timestamp)
agent_heartbeat/health logs
configuration_compliance results per asset
configuration_drift events (asset_id, config_item, expected_value, observed_value, timestamp)
enrollment_failure_reasons
evidence_strength_indicators: central management server logs showing active enrollment and policy rollout confirmations (strong)
agent health heartbeats and automated configuration compliance checks (strong)
manual asset lists claiming central management without enrollment logs (weak)
inconsistent or intermittent agent connectivity without replayable logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Emphasis on central management and tool deployment to enforce integrity controls indicates a preventive/coverage control.

SI-7(3).differentiation_note: Emphasizes central tooling enrollment and configuration compliance rather than checks, alerts, or cryptographic details.

SI-7(4).measurement_goal: For shipments/devices where applicable, measure the percentage of items using tamper-evident packaging and the count of tamper-evidence detections reported during receipt inspection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Packaging is a preventive physical control where coverage percentage (items shipped with tamper-evident packaging) and counts of tamper findings are most relevant.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of physical shipments/devices that used tamper-evident packaging per policy, count of received items flagged with tamper evidence, and inspection timestamps and disposition actions recorded. Include references to any policy or SR-9 linkage if applicable.
data_signals: shipping manifests indicating tamper_evident_packaging flag
receipt inspection logs with tamper_evidence_found boolean
disposition/actions taken for tamper-evident findings
policy references (SR-9 linkage)
evidence_strength_indicators: shipping manifests and receipt inspection logs with photos/timestamps (strong)
policy-mandated packaging records and control owner attestations (strong)
no documented packaging records and ad-hoc statements (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Tamper-evident packaging is a preventive physical control; confidence reduced because control text notes 'Withdrawn: incorporated into SR-9.'

SI-7(4).differentiation_note: Physical-shipping focused and marked as withdrawn; measurement targets shipments/receipt inspections rather than digital integrity mechanisms.

SI-7(5).measurement_goal: Measure automation coverage and response effectiveness: percent of integrity violations with an automated response executed, count of automated responses triggered, and mean time from detection to automated remediation completion.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automated response is corrective in nature; rates (percent of events auto-remediated) and counts of automated actions indicate operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number and percentage of integrity violation events that had an automated response action executed (e.g., rollback, quarantine, process kill), timestamps for detection and for action execution, success/failure status of the automated action, and escalation events for failed automation.
data_signals: integrity_violation_event records (event_id, timestamp, severity)
automation_action records (action_id, event_id, action_type, start_timestamp, end_timestamp, status)
automated_remediation_success_rate
escalation/ticket records for failed automated responses
time_delta detection_to_action
evidence_strength_indicators: automated action logs correlated to events with success metrics and rollback proofs (strong)
playbook execution traces and timestamps showing completed remediation (strong)
manual intervention logs without automation correlation (weak)
claims of automation without event-action linkage (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control explicitly describes automated responses to integrity violations, which are corrective actions intended to remediate detected issues.

SI-7(5).differentiation_note: Focuses on execution and success of automated remediation actions rather than detection or cryptographic verification.

SI-7(6).measurement_goal: Measure cryptographic integrity coverage and validation: percentage of software/firmware artifacts signed, count of verification failures, and distribution of cryptographic algorithms/strength in use.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Cryptographic protection is a preventive control focused on coverage (percentage signed) and algorithm usage distribution to ensure policy-aligned strength.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of deployed software and firmware artifacts that are cryptographically signed, count of verification failures on deployment/boot, cryptographic algorithm and key length distribution for signing, and timestamps of signature verification checks per artifact.
data_signals: artifact_manifest with signature_present flag (artifact_id, version, signature_algorithm)
signature_verification_logs (artifact_id, timestamp, result)
verification_failure_count by artifact and reason
cryptographic_algorithm and key_length distribution for signatures
policy mapping of approved algorithms
evidence_strength_indicators: signed artifact manifests with verifiable chain-of-trust and verification logs at deployment/boot (strong)
cryptographic algorithm inventory matching policy (strong)
unsigned artifacts noted only in manual lists (weak)
signing claimed without verifiable signatures or verification logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Specifies cryptographic protections (signing/verification) intended to prevent unauthorized modification of software/firmware, a preventive measure.

SI-7(6).differentiation_note: Specifically requires cryptographic signing and verification metrics (signature presence, algorithm strength) unlike other subcontrols that focus on checks, alerts, or automation.

SI-7(7).measurement_goal: Measure the integration effectiveness between detection and response systems: percent of detected integrity events that automatically map to response actions or playbooks, and median time from detection to response initiation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Integration is about event-to-action linkage; rates (percent of events with automated response) and timings best show integration effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage and count of integrity detection events that are correlated to automated or manual response actions (with timestamps), mapping of detection signatures to response playbooks, and time deltas for detection→response initiation plus success/failure of invoked responses.
data_signals: detection_event logs with correlated response_action_id
playbook mapping records (detection_signature -> playbook_id)
response_initiation timestamps per event
response_success/failure outcomes
unmapped_detection_event_count
evidence_strength_indicators: end-to-end event correlation logs showing automated invocation of playbooks (strong)
audit trails linking detection IDs to response action IDs with timestamps (strong)
separate detection and response logs without correlation (weak)
manual ticket creation without automated linkage (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Focuses on linking detection to response; primary function is detecting events and ensuring they trigger responses (detective with response integration).

SI-7(7).differentiation_note: Measures the mapping and automated handoff between detection systems and response actions, distinct from detection-only or response-only controls.

SI-7(8).measurement_goal: Measure audit logging coverage and retention for significant integrity events: count of significant integrity events logged, percent of events with complete audit records (timestamp, actor, artifact, action), and log retention/completeness compliance.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Auditing produces discrete events; counts of logged significant events and completeness percentages are primary to verify auditing capability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of significant integrity-related events (e.g., failed signature verification, tamper detection, unauthorized modification) recorded in audit logs, percent of those events containing required fields (timestamp, asset, actor, artifact, action), and log retention status versus policy.
data_signals: audit_log entries for integrity events (event_id, timestamp, asset_id, actor, artifact, action, severity)
audit_log completeness checks (required_fields_present)
log_retention/archival records and retention_period_compliance
number_of_unlogged_significant_events (from alternate detection sources)
evidence_strength_indicators: immutable, centrally stored audit logs with complete event fields and retention proofs (strong)
correlated detection events matched to audit entries (strong)
ad-hoc logs or manual entries missing fields or timestamps (weak)
audit log gaps or truncated logs without archive proof (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requires auditing significant events which is a detective activity producing evidence in logs.

SI-7(8).differentiation_note: Focused on the logging and audit completeness/retention for significant integrity events rather than detection, notification, or response mechanics.

SI-7(9).measurement_goal: Measure secure/verified boot coverage and failures: percentage of devices configured for verified boot and count/rate of boot-time verification failures with timestamps and remediation status.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Verified boot is a preventive configuration; percentage coverage across devices plus counts of verification failures are the key metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of devices (by class) with verified/secure boot enabled, boot-time signature verification results per device (pass/fail with timestamp), count of devices with boot verification failures, and remediation/patch status for failed devices.
data_signals: device configuration inventory with secure_boot_enabled flag
boot_verification logs (device_id, timestamp, result, artifact_hash)
boot_failure_count and failure_reasons
remediation/ticket records for failed boot verification
firmware_version and signing metadata
evidence_strength_indicators: boot-time verification logs and signed bootloader/firmware manifests correlated to device inventory (strong)
central reporting showing secure_boot_enabled for all managed devices (strong)
user-reported boot issues without verification logs (weak)
occasional manual checks without automated boot logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Verifying the boot process (secure/verified boot) is preventive to ensure only trusted firmware/software boots on devices.

SI-7(9).differentiation_note: Specifically targets boot-time integrity (secure/verified boot) metrics for devices, distinct from runtime integrity checks or cryptographic signing of artifacts.

SI-7(10).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of managed endpoints with boot firmware protections enabled and the count of unauthorized/unsigned boot firmware modifications detected per period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control verifying coverage (percentage of endpoints with boot firmware protections) while also requiring detective counts of integrity/unauthorized changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of inventoryed endpoints with verified boot firmware protection features enabled (e.g., secure boot, measured boot), plus counts of firmware modification events that are unsigned or fail cryptographic verification, with timestamps and asset identifiers.
data_signals: endpoint inventory with secure-boot/measured-boot flag
firmware protection configuration status per asset
cryptographic signature verification results for boot firmware
firmware modification/change event logs with timestamps and asset IDs
unauthorized or unsigned boot firmware detection alerts
BIOS/UEFI update approval records and associated hashes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated endpoint telemetry showing secure-boot/measured-boot enabled across inventory and continuous firmware integrity alerts; Weak: single point-in-time inventory spreadsheet or manual attestations without logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: Protecting boot firmware is a preventive activity to stop unauthorized code from executing at boot (control name: Protection of Boot Firmware).

SI-7(10).differentiation_note: Focused specifically on protections at system boot/firmware layer (secure/measured boot and firmware signature verification) rather than general code integrity or runtime protections.

SI-7(11).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of confined or sandboxed execution environments that have enforced limited privilege policies, and count privilege escalation or policy violations detected in those environments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control (labeled withdrawn but originally preventive), primary evidence is coverage/percentage of environments configured with least privilege; detection of violations is measured as counts.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of designated confined execution environments (containers, sandboxes, restricted VMs) configured with documented limited-privilege policies and counts of detected privilege escalation or policy-violation events with timestamps and affected environment IDs.
data_signals: inventory of confined/sandboxed environments (container/VM list)
privilege policy configuration records per environment
access/privilege-change event logs in confined environments
privilege escalation alerts or detections
policy enforcement logs from sandboxing/containment tools
approval records for exceptions to limited-privilege policies
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated enforcement logs and continuous privilege violation alerts for confined environments; Weak: manual checklists or one-time attestations without enforcement logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control name and withdrawn note indicate it was a preventive control enforcing limited privileges in confined environments.

SI-7(11).differentiation_note: Targets confined/sandboxed execution contexts and their privilege restrictions specifically, unlike sibling controls that focus on firmware, code authentication, or runtime integrity verification.

SI-7(12).measurement_goal: Measure verification coverage and failure counts: percentage of critical binaries/configuration files under automated integrity verification and the count/rate of integrity verification failures or tamper detections per period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control; primary metrics should count verification executions and failures while coverage percentages show scope of verification.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of integrity verification runs (per asset), count of verification failures (tamper/bit-rot/unexpected checksum changes) with asset IDs and timestamps, and percentage coverage of critical binaries/config files included in integrity verification.
data_signals: integrity verification execution logs with timestamps
list of monitored file/bin patterns and asset mapping
verification result statuses (pass/fail) and failure reasons
hashes/checksums baseline repository and comparison results
alerts for tamper/changed files and correlated process IDs
coverage ratio of critical files/binaries monitored vs. inventory
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated verification logs tied to baseline hashes and alerting for failures; Weak: ad-hoc manual file checks or single report without historical verification logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Integrity Verification' implies ongoing checks to detect unauthorized or unexpected changes, which is detective in nature.

SI-7(12).differentiation_note: Specifically centers on automated verification of file/binary integrity and detection of tampering, distinct from preventive code-authentication or boot-level protections.

SI-7(13).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of protected execution environments (designated secure enclaves/sandboxed runtimes) configured to prevent unauthorized code execution and count blocked or prevented unauthorized execution attempts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Originally preventive: verify configuration coverage of protected execution environments (percentage) and measure counts of prevented code-execution events.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of designated protected execution environments (secure enclaves, protected runtimes) with enforced policies preventing unauthorized code execution, plus counts of blocked/terminated unauthorized execution attempts with timestamps and process hashes.
data_signals: inventory of protected execution environments and their configuration status
policy enforcement logs showing blocked executions
process execution logs with code signature/hash
blocked/terminated execution event records with reason codes
approval/whitelist records for allowed executables
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: enforcement logs and automated blocking records tied to signed/whitelisted binaries; Weak: policy documents without enforcement or only manual intervention records

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control concerns preventing unauthorized code execution in protected environments (name: Code Execution in Protected Environments), which is preventive.

SI-7(13).differentiation_note: Concentrates on enforcing policies in protected runtimes to stop execution of unauthorized code, not on firmware or post-execution integrity verification.

SI-7(14).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of deployed binaries/machine-executable code that are cryptographically signed and the count of unsigned or altered executables detected in production environments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive emphasis on coverage (percentage of binaries signed) with detective counts for unsigned/altered binaries.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of deployed machine-executable binaries with valid signatures recorded in the release repository and count of detected unsigned/modified executables in runtime/production with associated hashes and timestamps.
data_signals: release repository records with binary signatures and hashes
deployment inventory mapping binaries to assets
runtime executable scanning logs showing signature verification results
alerts for unsigned or modified executables with asset and timestamp
software bill of materials (SBOM) entries for deployed binaries
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated signature verification in deployment pipelines and runtime that tie back to release hashes; Weak: manual code review or occasional checks without automated verification

control_type_classification.reasoning: Managing and authenticating machine-executable code before deployment is a preventive release-control activity (control name references binaries/executables).

SI-7(14).differentiation_note: Specific to management and authenticity of binary/machine-executable artifacts (signing and detection of unsigned/altered executables) rather than boot firmware or runtime app protection.

SI-7(15).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of code artifacts and packages authenticated (signed and validated) prior to deployment and the rate of authentication failures or mismatches detected during deployment/runtime.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, primary measurement is coverage (percentage of signed/authenticated artifacts); detective metrics (rates/counts) capture authentication failures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of build artifacts/packages with valid code signatures recorded in CI/CD artifacts store, and rate/count of signature verification failures or mismatches during deployment or runtime with artifact IDs and timestamps.
data_signals: CI/CD artifact metadata including signature and signer ID
signature validation logs during build and deployment
deployment rejection/validation failure events with reasons
artifact hashes and corresponding signing certificate chain
automated policy records requiring signature before deployment
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated signature enforcement in pipeline with logs of validation success/failure and signer certificate details; Weak: ad-hoc manual attestations or unsigned approved exceptions without logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: Code authentication (signing and validation) prevents unauthorized code deployment and execution, fitting a preventive classification.

SI-7(15).differentiation_note: Focuses on authentication (signing/validation) of code artifacts in build/deploy pipelines, distinct from post-deployment integrity verification or runtime protections.

SI-7(16).measurement_goal: Define and measure the coverage of any additional SI-7 family integrity controls implemented: percentage of assets covered by documented SI-7 supplemental controls and counts of control-specific detection/remediation events.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: With no explicit description, treat as preventive/coverage-focused: primary measurement should show coverage percentage of whatever supplemental integrity controls are in place, with counts for detected deviations.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets covered by any documented SI-7(16) supplemental integrity controls and counts of detected deviations or exceptions tied to those controls, including asset IDs, control names, and timestamps.
data_signals: inventory of assets mapped to SI-7 supplemental control implementations
implementation/configuration records for supplemental integrity controls
detection/exception logs tied to supplemental controls with timestamps
policy or control definition document stating scope of SI-7(16)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: documented policy plus automated telemetry showing enforcement and logs; Weak: unspecified control with no documentation or telemetry (low confidence)

control_type_classification.reasoning: No description provided; classified conservatively as compensating/other with low confidence pending clarification.

SI-7(16).differentiation_note: Placeholder for unspecified supplemental SI-7 requirements; measurement must be tailored once the specific control text is provided (current entry uses sibling terminology due to missing description).

SI-7(17).measurement_goal: Measure runtime application self-protection (RASP) coverage percentage across production applications and the count/rate of in-process attacks or exploit attempts blocked by RASP per time period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: RASP is both detective and preventive at runtime; primary metrics counting blocked exploit attempts and incidents are most indicative while coverage (%) is critical as a secondary measure.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of exploit/attack events detected and blocked by RASP per application per period, along with percentage of production applications with RASP enabled and telemetry showing action taken (block/log/alert) and relevant request/process context.
data_signals: RASP telemetry logs with event type, action (block/log), timestamp, and application ID
list/inventory of applications with RASP agent enabled
blocked exploit signatures or rule IDs correlated to events
false-positive/false-negative tuning records and exceptions
incident tickets generated from RASP detections
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous RASP telemetry showing blocked exploit attempts and automated mitigation actions; Weak: occasional WAF logs or post-incident reports without inline RASP enforcement

control_type_classification.reasoning: RASP detects and often blocks runtime attacks (control name: Runtime Application Self-protection), functioning primarily as a detective/runtime protection control.

SI-7(17).differentiation_note: Directly measures runtime, in-process protections and blocked exploit attempts at the application layer, unlike sibling controls focused on pre-deployment signing or firmware-level protections.

SI-8.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of spam protection for inbound messaging: percent of inbound email processed by spam controls, counts of messages blocked/quarantined, and false positive/false negative rates to demonstrate the control is preventing malicious or unwanted email delivery.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Spam protection is primarily preventive (blocking/filtering) so percentage metrics (e.g., percent of mail processed/blocked, false positive rate) best show coverage and efficacy; counts and rates supplement with volume and trend context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of inbound messages processed by spam filters and the proportion blocked or quarantined; counts of quarantined/blocked messages per day; false positive and false negative rates derived from user reports and adjudicated messages; timestamps and versions of the last definition/signature updates across each mail gateway.
data_signals: inbound email processing logs (message id, recipient, sender, timestamp, disposition)
blocked/quarantined message counts by day and rule
user-reported false positive/false negative adjudication records
filter/policy version and last update timestamp per gateway
filter rule activation and change audit logs
mail gateway uptime and processing success/failure logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, automated inbound email logs with dispositions and timestamps are strong evidence.
Automated block/quarantine counts tied to filter versions and update timestamps strengthen evidence.
Periodic manual screenshots or one-off reports are weak evidence because they don't prove ongoing operation or coverage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Spam Protection' implies blocking/filtering to prevent malicious email delivery (a preventive objective).

SI-8.differentiation_note: This is the broad, operational email-filtering control focused on efficacy and coverage (blocked counts, FP/FN rates), while siblings target specific capabilities (central management, update cadence, ML learning).

SI-8(1).measurement_goal: Demonstrate centralized management of spam controls by measuring the percent of mail gateways and email tenants under the central policy console, frequency and success of policy pushes, and count of unmanaged or out-of-sync email protection devices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Central-management controls are preventive and coverage-oriented; percentage of devices under central management and distribution of config versions show compliance and reduce configuration drift.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of email gateways, cloud tenants, and appliances managed by the central management console; timestamps and success/failure status of the last policy/config push; list and count of devices with configuration drift or not reporting to central management.
data_signals: central management console inventory of managed devices (device id, type, last-checkin)
policy/config push logs with timestamps and success/failure status
device configuration version vs desired configuration records
count of unmanaged or unreachable email gateways
audit logs of policy changes and administrative access to the central console
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated central-console inventories and sync success logs are strong evidence of centralized management.
Systematic records of devices out-of-sync with desired config show weak points.
Manual screenshots or one-time attestations are weak compared to ongoing sync/audit logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name indicates centralized policy/configuration to prevent misconfiguration, but the description notes 'Withdrawn: incorporated into PL-9,' reducing confidence in standalone applicability.

SI-8(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on centralized policy/configuration coverage and sync (device management coverage, policy push success) rather than general spam detection metrics.

SI-8(2).measurement_goal: Prove automatic updates for spam protection by measuring the freshness and success rate of definition/signature updates across email protection points: percent of gateways with definitions updated within the required interval, and count of missed or failed updates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automatic update controls are preventive; percentage of devices updated within policy windows shows compliance, while rates/counts show volume of update failures or missed schedules.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of mail gateways and spam-filtering endpoints with a definition/signature update within the policy window; update job success/failure counts and timestamps; number of endpoints with definition age exceeding policy thresholds.
data_signals: definition/signature feed timestamp and feed version per gateway
update job execution logs (start, end, success/failure) per endpoint
time delta since last successful update per device
count of missed/failed update attempts by day
policy requirement for update frequency (desired interval) and device compliance status
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated update logs with per-device timestamps and success codes are strong evidence.
Correlation of update version with detection performance strengthens evidence.
Ad-hoc manual update notes or single snapshots are weak evidence for ongoing automated updates.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Automatic Updates' indicates an automated preventive mechanism to keep spam detection artifacts current.

SI-8(2).differentiation_note: Targets timeliness and success of update delivery (definition/version freshness) rather than high-level block/detection metrics or configuration centralization.

SI-8(3).measurement_goal: Demonstrate the operation of continuous learning by measuring model retraining/deployment frequency, incorporation rate of user feedback into models, and changes in key detection performance metrics (false positive/negative rates, precision/recall) over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Continuous learning is detective and iterative; rates (e.g., feedback-incorporation rate, retrain frequency) and trends in performance metrics show whether learning improves detection and adapts to new spam patterns.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate of model retraining/deployment (retrain events per period), percent of user-reported suspicious messages incorporated into training data, and time series of detection performance metrics (false positive rate, false negative rate, precision/recall) by model version.
data_signals: model retrain and deployment timestamps with version identifiers
user feedback/appeal submission logs mapped to training dataset entries
validation/performance scores per model version (precision, recall, FPR, FNR)
number of feedback items processed and incorporated into training per period
A/B test or evaluation run results comparing model versions
automation pipeline logs for model training and CI/CD deployment
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated model versioning plus CI/CD deployment logs and measurable improvements in precision/recall across versions are strong evidence.
Traceability from user feedback through dataset inclusion to subsequent model performance is strong.
Offline research notes or isolated model evaluations without deployment history are weak evidence for an operational continuous-learning capability.

control_type_classification.reasoning: A 'Continuous Learning Capability' implies ongoing detection model refinement and feedback-driven detection improvements, which is detective in nature.

SI-8(3).differentiation_note: Specifically measures ML/feedback lifecycle (retrain frequency, feedback incorporation, model performance trends) rather than update cadence or central management coverage.

SI-9.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of information input restrictions across systems and applications (percentage of applications/configurations with input validation/whitelisting enabled) and the count and rate of blocked or mitigated malicious/invalid inputs and documented exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors primarily need coverage metrics (percentage of applications/systems that implement input restrictions). Counts and rates of blocked inputs or exceptions are useful secondary detective measures to show the controls are active.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of in-scope applications and system entry points that have documented and enabled input restrictions (e.g., input validation, allowlists, WAF rules) versus the total in-scope inventory; report counts and rates of blocked/filtered input events and documented exceptions with timestamps and remediation/approval records. Include configuration change records showing when input restrictions were applied or modified.
data_signals: inventory of in-scope applications and data entry points with unique IDs
configuration records indicating input validation/whitelist/blacklist settings per application
web application firewall (WAF) rule sets and deployment timestamps
logs of blocked or sanitized requests (timestamp, source IP, rule triggered, request URI)
exception/allowlist entries and approved change requests for input restrictions
configuration change history for input-validation controls (who/when/what)
policy or requirement document stating required input restriction controls
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated configuration scans showing input validation enabled for a high percentage of inventory + correlated WAF/block logs demonstrating active enforcement; change records and signed policy mapping to each config.
Weak: single manual attestations, screenshots, or one-off test results without inventory mapping or automated logs; absence of exception records or timestamps weakens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input restriction is labeled preventive and the description states it was incorporated into access controls (AC-2/AC-3/AC-5/AC-6), indicating the control's intent is to prevent unauthorized or malformed inputs.

SI-9.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on restrictions/validation of information inputs (coverage/configuration of input validation and enforcement) rather than broader account or session controls covered by the AC family; measurement must emphasize application-level validation coverage and enforcement logs. Note: the original control text is withdrawn and requirements are implemented under AC-2/AC-3/AC-5/AC-6, so mapping to those controls is necessary when sourcing evidence.

SI-10.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of input validation across applications and services: percent of input sources covered by validation rules, count/rate of validation failures and blocked inputs, and coverage of unit/integration tests that assert validation behavior.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Input validation is a preventive control; auditors need coverage metrics (percent of inputs protected) plus counts/rates of failures or blocks to show effectiveness and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of distinct input sources (APIs, web forms, file uploads, integrations) that have deployed input validation/whitelisting rules; counts of validation failures and blocked requests by source and error type; test coverage for input validation logic (unit/integration/fuzz tests) with timestamps.
data_signals: inventory of input endpoints with validation rule IDs and deployment timestamps
request validation result logs (endpoint, timestamp, source, pass/fail, failure_reason)
blocked request logs with rule identifier and classification (schema mismatch, unexpected type, size limit, disallowed characters)
unit/integration/fuzz test execution results for input validation modules (test id, pass/fail, timestamp)
schema/whitelist rule repository versions and change history
code analysis findings for input-handling functions (unsafe parsing, missing sanitization)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated request validation logs correlated with endpoint inventory and deployed rule versions
Strong: automated test suites and CI results proving validation behavior for each endpoint
Weak: occasional manual test reports or one-off screenshots that don't tie to production request logs
Weak: undocumented ad-hoc validation implemented only in some environments

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Information Input Validation' implies controls implemented to prevent malformed or malicious inputs from entering the system.

SI-10.differentiation_note: This is the broad coverage/control for input validation across systems — sibling controls focus on exceptions (manual overrides), error handling, timing, sources/formats, and injection-specific protections.

SI-10(1).measurement_goal: Measure frequency and governance of manual overrides to input validation: count of override events, percent of overrides with documented justification and approval, and average time between override request and approval/rollback.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Manual overrides are exceptions; counting override events and the percent with approvals/justifications provides the clearest evidence of controlled use and governance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of manual override events against input validation rules (by endpoint and actor), percent of those events with recorded justification and managerial/authorized approval, and timestamps for request/approval/closure to measure timeliness and reversibility.
data_signals: override event logs (endpoint, actor_id, timestamp, original validation rule id)
override approval records (approver_id, approval_timestamp, justification text, linked ticket id)
rollback or remediation action logs after override (actions taken, timestamps)
frequency trend of overrides per endpoint/service over time
policy or exception request repository entries referencing manual override capability
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: every override event is logged and linked to an approval record and remediation ticket in the tracking system
Strong: low and decreasing trend of overrides with timely approvals
Weak: ad-hoc emails or verbal approvals without recorded justification or linked tickets
Weak: overrides made directly in production without audit logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: A 'Manual Override Capability' is an exception/compensating control that permits bypassing preventive validation when managed and logged.

SI-10(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on governance, logging and approval of exceptions to validation rules, not on validation coverage itself.

SI-10(2).measurement_goal: Measure detection and remediation of input validation errors: count of validation errors reported, mean time to resolve validation errors, percent of validation errors reviewed and triaged within policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a corrective control: auditors need rates and velocity metrics (resolution rate, MTTR) to show errors are reviewed and resolved in a timely manner.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of distinct validation error occurrences grouped by type and endpoint, time from error detection to ticket creation and to remediation closure, and percent of errors triaged/assigned within the policy-defined timeframe.
data_signals: validation error logs with error_type, endpoint, timestamp, request_id
ticketing/tracking entries created for validation errors (ticket_id, created_timestamp, assigned_to)
time-to-resolution metrics for validation tickets (created_timestamp -> resolved_timestamp)
triage/priority labels assigned to each validation error
backlog of unreviewed/unresolved validation errors by age
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated pipeline from validation failure detection to ticket creation with timestamps and resolution records
Strong: MTTR and backlog trending showing timely remediation and decreasing aged errors
Weak: manual spreadsheets or one-off email threads without formal ticket lifecycle timestamps
Weak: resolved errors without root cause documentation or code changes

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement to 'Review and Resolve Errors' implies processes that correct or remediate validation failures after detection.

SI-10(2).differentiation_note: Targets post-detection lifecycle (triage, ticketing, remediation) rather than prevention or overrides.

SI-10(3).measurement_goal: Measure determinism and consistency of system behavior when faced with invalid inputs: percent of endpoints returning documented, consistent error responses and number of inconsistent/undefined behaviors found by automated tests (fuzzing/contract tests).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive control intent — auditors need coverage and consistency percentages (how many endpoints behave predictably) plus counts of inconsistent findings from tests.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of API endpoints and input-handling functions with documented and tested error/edge-case behavior; counts of inconsistent or undocumented responses discovered by contract tests and fuzzing, including stack traces or exception types.
data_signals: API contract/specification coverage report (endpoints with documented error responses)
automated contract test results (endpoint, test_id, pass/fail, timestamp)
fuzz testing output showing unexpected behaviors or crashes (endpoint, input_case, exception)
production exception/stack trace logs correlated to invalid input cases
error response mapping (status codes and body patterns) per endpoint
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated contract/fuzz tests run in CI with results linked to endpoint specifications and production exception logs
Strong: low count and decreasing trend of inconsistent behaviors across releases
Weak: manual test cases or anecdotal reports without automated, repeatable test evidence
Weak: undocumented error responses observed in production without corresponding test coverage

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring predictable behavior implies preventive design of input-handling that avoids unpredictable errors or leaks.

SI-10(3).differentiation_note: Focuses on the determinism and documented error responses for inputs, unlike other controls which focus on coverage, overrides, or remediation.

SI-10(4).measurement_goal: Measure defenses against timing-based input attacks: quantify endpoints with constant-time or timing-mitigated responses, measure variance in response time for accepted vs rejected inputs, and count timing-attack test failures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control needs coverage percentage (how many endpoints implement timing mitigations) and distributions of response time to detect timing channels.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of sensitive input-handling endpoints implemented with timing-mitigation techniques; distributions and variance of response times for valid vs invalid inputs; results and counts of timing-attack simulation tests per endpoint.
data_signals: inventory of endpoints annotated with timing-mitigation status (constant-time, randomized delay, equalized response)
response time samples for accepted vs rejected inputs (endpoint, timestamp, latency_ms)
timing-attack test results and artifacts (test id, endpoint, pass/fail, delta distribution)
code analysis records showing use of constant-time libraries or constructs
production monitoring metrics for latency variance correlated to input validation outcomes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated timing-simulation test runs with documented pass/fail and production telemetry showing minimal distinguishing latency
Strong: endpoint inventory showing applied mitigations and versioned deployment records
Weak: one-off manual timing measurements without systematic sampling
Weak: theoretical claims without test artifacts or production latency distributions

control_type_classification.reasoning: Controlling timing interactions is a design-time preventive measure to avoid timing channels or side-channel attacks.

SI-10(4).differentiation_note: Unique focus on timing characteristics and side-channel mitigation rather than functional validation or error handling.

SI-10(5).measurement_goal: Measure enforcement of input source and format restrictions: percent of inputs validated against trusted-source allowlists and approved format schemas, count of inputs blocked for untrusted source or disallowed format, and percent of endpoints with enforced source/format policies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage metrics (what fraction of inputs are checked for trusted source and approved format) and counts of blocks to demonstrate enforcement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of input channels configured to enforce trusted-source checks (IP allowlist, client certs, auth tokens) and schema/format validation; counts of blocked inputs by reason (untrusted source, invalid format) with endpoint and timestamp.
data_signals: trusted source allowlist/denylist inventory with deployment records
TLS client certificate validation logs and client identity assertions
content-type/schema validation logs (MIME/checksum/JSON schema validation failures)
blocked input logs with reason code (untrusted_source, invalid_format)
endpoint configuration records showing enabled source/format restrictions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: production enforcement logs correlated to an authoritative trusted-source list and schema repository
Strong: low number of blocked legitimate inputs and documented exceptions with approvals
Weak: manual checks or developer notes without enforcement logs
Weak: inconsistent format checks across environments

control_type_classification.reasoning: Restricting inputs to trusted sources and approved formats is a preventive configuration to stop untrusted or malformed data entering the system.

SI-10(5).differentiation_note: Specifically measures source and format enforcement (allowlists, schema checks) rather than general validation logic or error remediation.

SI-10(6).measurement_goal: Measure injection-prevention coverage and effectiveness: percent of data access/query code using parameterized queries/ORM safe APIs, count/rate of blocked injection attempts, and results of automated injection tests (e.g., SQLi, command injection).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Injection prevention is preventive; coverage percentages (safe coding usage) and counts of attempted/blocked injections plus trends provide evidence of control effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of database and command-invoking code paths that use parameterized statements or safe API calls; counts of blocked injection attempts by type and endpoint; results of static analysis and dynamic injection test suites identifying injection vulnerabilities and remediation timestamps.
data_signals: codebase analysis results indicating use of parameterized queries/prepared statements (file/module level)
WAF/IDS logs showing blocked injection attempts (timestamp, endpoint, payload signature)
dynamic application security testing (DAST) / injection test results (test id, vulnerability id, severity, remediation status)
vulnerability scanner findings specific to injection classes (SQLi, command injection) with CVSS or severity
deployment records showing remediation commits and release timestamps for injected fixes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated static and dynamic test pipelines detecting injection issues plus WAF/IDS logs showing blocked attempts and linked remediation commits
Strong: high percentage of code paths using parameterized APIs and decreasing injection findings over time
Weak: reliance solely on periodic penetration test reports without continuous detection or blocking logs
Weak: blocked requests logged but no linked remediation or code changes

control_type_classification.reasoning: The explicit goal of 'Injection Prevention' indicates preventive design and coding practices to stop injection attacks before they succeed.

SI-10(6).differentiation_note: Concentrates on injection-specific defenses (secure coding, WAF, DAST) and measurable reduction/blocking of injection attempts, unlike more general input validation controls.

SI-11.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of secure error-handling across applications and services: percentage of production applications configured to suppress debug/stack-trace output, count of error responses in logs that contain sensitive information, and mean time to remediate code/configuration findings that permit information disclosure via error messages.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Error-handling is primarily a preventive control (coverage/configuration) so percent coverage of secure configurations is the most useful primary metric; counts and rates of sensitive-error occurrences and remediation trends are useful secondary metrics to detect and track failures over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage of production applications/services with production error-handling configurations enabled (e.g., stack-trace suppression flag = true) and standardized error templates deployed; (2) count of error responses in the last 30/90 days whose response bodies or logs include sensitive data (PII/credentials) or raw stack traces; (3) SAST/Code-review findings for 'exposed stack trace' or 'debug info in production' and time-to-remediate for those findings.
data_signals: application configuration flags for error handling (e.g., show_stack_trace=false, debug=false) per service
application/exception logs containing stack traces and full exception messages with timestamps
HTTP response bodies and status codes for 4xx/5xx responses captured by monitoring or WAF
SAST/secure code review findings labeled for 'error handling', 'stack trace exposure', or 'debug info in production'
incident or ticket records for information disclosure caused by error messages
error-injection / fuzz test results that capture error response contents
WAF/IDS alerts correlated to abnormal error responses or information-leakage signatures
remediation timestamps for code/config fixes tied to error-handling findings
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, structured application logs and monitoring that show no sensitive content in error bodies plus automated SAST findings marked closed with timestamps and code-commit references.
Moderate: periodic code-review reports and test results showing suppression settings applied to some services but gaps in coverage.
Weak: ad-hoc screenshots or developer assertions without log evidence, single point-in-time manual tests, or annual penetration test notes alone.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Error Handling' implies preventive measures (suppress debug/stack-trace output and control error messages) that stop information disclosure before it occurs.

SI-11.differentiation_note: Unlike general logging or vulnerability scanning controls, this control specifically requires measurement of error-message content and suppression/configuration coverage (not just presence of logs), so instruments must inspect response bodies, config flags, and code-review findings. If this mapping seems narrow due to missing control text, the focus areas chosen approximate the likely intent from the control name.

SI-12.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of retention policies across the data inventory: percent of data assets with assigned retention schedules, count of records past retention, and percent of retention-schedule reviews completed on policy cadence.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Retention/management is primarily a coverage/compliance control (coverage % of assets with retention metadata). Counts and trends support auditors by showing volumes and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of records/assets in the authoritative data inventory that have retention-period metadata and an assigned retention schedule, plus counts of records that have exceeded retention and not been dispositioned. Include timestamps of retention assignment, last review date, and disposal actions for each record to enable SLA and aging calculations.
data_signals: authoritative data inventory rows with retention_period and retention_policy_id fields
retention_schedule registry (schedule_id, data_type, retention_period_days, effective_date)
record-level retention metadata (creation_timestamp, retention_expiration_timestamp, retention_status)
disposition/disposal action logs (record_id, action_type, action_timestamp, performed_by, verification_id)
retention policy versioning and policy effective dates
retention-schedule review logs (review_id, reviewer, review_date, review_outcome)
exception/waiver records for retention (exception_id, approved_by, expiry)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory with per-record retention metadata and automated disposition receipts (disposal logs with verification hashes) showing continuous enforcement.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or a standalone policy document without per-record metadata or disposal logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control specifies information management and retention (planning and assignment of retention schedules) which is primarily preventive: assigning and enforcing retention before violations occur.

SI-12.differentiation_note: This is the high-level, cross-cutting control for assigning and enforcing retention schedules across all information types; its measurement focuses on coverage and schedule assignment rather than PII minimization or disposal mechanics.

SI-12(1).measurement_goal: Measure how well collection and storage processes limit personally identifiable information (PII): percentage of data collection points that collect only required PII elements and counts of unnecessary PII elements removed per review cycle.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Limiting PII is a preventive coverage/compliance requirement best expressed as percent-compliant (forms/processes that adhere to allowed-element lists); counts and trends show volume of PII elements collected and reduction over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of data collection forms, APIs, and data stores that are mapped to an approved data element inventory with allowed PII elements only; count of PII element occurrences by dataset and number of PII elements removed or redacted after minimization reviews. Include mapping table between collection point and allowed-element list.
data_signals: data element inventory with pii_flag and allowed_for_purpose fields
catalog of collection points/forms/APIs with fields captured (form_id, field_name, data_type, pii_flag)
PII element counts per dataset (field-level counts, unique PII types)
data minimization review logs (review_id, collection_point_id, findings, remedial_actions, action_timestamp)
change logs showing removal/redaction of PII elements (field_removed, timestamp, approved_by)
consent/purpose justification records mapping PII elements to business purpose
