policy-approved allowed-PII element lists and effective_dates
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated data catalog with field-level PII flags and change history showing PII removals and approvals; linkage to approved allowed-element lists.
Weak: unsynchronized manual inventories or occasional manual reviews without field-level mapping or action logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement explicitly limits which PII elements are collected/stored (a prevention of excess collection) and requires controls/processes to enforce that limit.

SI-12(1).differentiation_note: This enhancement specifically targets limiting collection of PII elements (data minimization) and therefore requires field-level inventory and mapping metrics distinct from overall retention coverage or disposal verification.

SI-12(2).measurement_goal: Measure assignment and enforcement of retention schedules: percent of datasets/assets assigned to an approved retention schedule and count of automated enforcement actions triggered for expired data.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Assuming this enhancement concerns retention schedule enforcement, primary evidence is coverage (percentage of assets with schedules). Counts and trends show enforcement actions and exceptions over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of authoritative data assets and systems that have retention_schedule_id metadata assigned and active enforcement configured; count of expired-data enforcement events (quarantine, deletion, archive) with timestamps and result codes to show automated enforcement effectiveness.
data_signals: asset/data store registry with retention_schedule_id and retention_enforcement_flag
retention enforcement event logs (event_id, asset_id, event_type, event_timestamp, result_status)
expired-data queue counts and processing timestamps
exception and waiver logs for retention enforcement (exception_id, approved_until)
audit trail correlating retention expiration triggers to disposal/archive actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: consistent automated enforcement logs showing enforcement actions for expired items and low exception rates; linkage between expiration triggers and disposal outcomes.
Weak: manual enforcement via ad-hoc tickets or inconsistent application of schedules without machine-readable retention metadata.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Based on its placement under SI-12, this enhancement is assumed to require assignment/enforcement of retention schedules (preventive), but the provided name is missing/ambiguous so confidence is lower.

SI-12(2).differentiation_note: Name/description was not provided; this entry assumes a retention-schedule enforcement enhancement — measurement focuses on assigned schedules and enforcement events rather than PII minimization or disposal verification. Note: limitation due to missing control text.

SI-12(3).measurement_goal: Measure effectiveness and verification of information disposal: percent of expired/terminated records media sanitized or disposed per policy and count of verified secure deletions/destructions with supporting evidence.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Disposal is a compliance/coverage outcome (percent of expired items disposed and verified); counts of disposals and comparisons to expected disposals are useful secondary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of items that reached retention expiration and have a corresponding disposal/sanitization record (with verification artifact). Track disposal type (secure delete, cryptographic erase, physical destruction), disposal timestamp, operator, and verification token/hash to enable auditability.
data_signals: disposal/sanitization event logs (record_id, disposal_method, timestamp, operator_id, verification_token)
media destruction receipts (media_id, destruction_method, certificate_of_destruction)
secure-delete/log output with file-hash or wipe verification
retention expiration triggers correlating to disposal_event_ids
disposal exception approvals and retention extension logs
audit trail linking disposal to retention policy and schedule
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-generated disposal logs and cryptographic verification tokens or certificates of destruction linked to retention-expiration events.
Weak: occasional manual attestations or unsigned disposal notes without verifiable artifacts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The enhancement requires disposal/sanitization actions and verification when retention expires; this prevents unlawful retention and enforces policy, so it is primarily preventive.

SI-12(3).differentiation_note: This enhancement is specifically focused on the disposal/sanitization phase of the information lifecycle and requires verification artifacts (hashes, certificates), unlike the parent control which focuses on assignment and coverage of retention schedules.

SI-13.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical components with documented failure-prevention strategies (redundancy, standby, transfer/runbook) and track counts and outcomes of failover/transfer tests and real-world transfers to demonstrate readiness and prevention of predictable failures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive and auditors need coverage/completeness metrics (percentage of components covered); counts of tests and trend show ongoing execution and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical assets with an assigned failure-prevention mechanism (redundancy/standby/transfer procedure), count and pass-rate of scheduled failover/transfer tests per asset tier, and number of predictable-failure incidents where the mitigation succeeded or failed.
data_signals: component redundancy/standby inventory (asset id, tier, standby present boolean)
runbook/SOP presence and last-updated timestamp per component
scheduled failover/transfer test execution logs (timestamp, asset id, test type, pass/fail, duration)
automated health-check/heartbeat metrics for critical components
notification logs for failover/transfer events (recipient list, delivery status, timestamp)
incident records referencing predictable failure with mitigation outcome
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory + scheduled automated test logs + successful automated failover event logs; Weak: one-off manual test reports or undocumented manual notes

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Predictable Failure Prevention' indicates actions to prevent predictable failures (preventive controls).

SI-13.differentiation_note: This is the overarching preventive control covering multiple mechanisms (transfer, standby, failover); sibling controls focus on specific mechanisms (e.g., transfer or failover) and therefore require separate, more granular signals.

SI-13(1).measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of responsibility-transfer mechanisms: percentage of components with documented automated or manual responsibility-transfer processes and the count, success-rate, and average duration of actual transfer events/tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires demonstrating coverage (percentage of components with transfer mechanisms); counts/rates of transfer events/tests show operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical components that have an assigned responsibility-transfer mechanism (automated or manual), plus counts and success/failure outcomes of transfer events and test runs, with timestamps and involved component IDs to allow verification of proper handover.
data_signals: component responsibility mapping registry (component id -> owner/backup component)
transfer automation configuration entries (scripts, orchestration jobs)
transfer event logs (timestamp, source component, destination component, initiator, success/failure, duration)
manual transfer operation logs and operator id entries
transfer test results and test schedule records
runbook/SOP references invoked during transfer
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated transfer event logs with end-to-end success metrics and pre/post-state snapshots; Weak: undocumented manual handovers or ad-hoc email confirmations

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Transferring Component Responsibilities' implies preventive measures to ensure continuity through planned transfers of responsibility.

SI-13(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on the mechanisms and records for transferring responsibilities between components (ownership mapping and transfer events) rather than on standby hardware or failover execution.

SI-13(2).measurement_goal: For historical measurement mapping (this enhancement was withdrawn and incorporated into SI-7(16)): where applicable, measure the percentage of long-running processes that respect configured time limits (count of executions exceeding allowed unsupervised duration and alerts generated).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control defines a time-bound preventive requirement (process execution time limit) so coverage/adherence percentage is the most useful primary metric; counts of violations are useful secondary metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of processes with configured supervision/timeouts and count of process executions that exceeded the allowed unsupervised time limit, including timestamps, process IDs, host, and responsible owner; record of alerts or automated terminations triggered by breaches.
data_signals: process execution logs (process id, start time, end time, host, owner)
configured supervision/time-limit policies per process
timeout/termination events (timestamp, process id, reason)
alerts generated for processes exceeding time limit
mapping to SI-7(16) control references (where implemented)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated enforcement logs showing processes terminated/alerted at time-limit thresholds; Weak: written policies without enforcement logs or only periodic manual reviews

affinity_keywords: Time Limit on Process Execution Without Supervision

control_type_classification.reasoning: The name sets a preventive time-bound requirement (preventing unsupervised long-running processes), but the control is marked 'Withdrawn' and mapped to SI-7(16), lowering classification confidence.

SI-13(2).differentiation_note: This entry is a withdrawn enhancement focused on enforcement of time limits for unsupervised processes; measurement should instead be obtained from the current controlling requirement (SI-7(16)).

SI-13(3).measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and correctness of manual transfers between components: count of manual transfer operations, percentage executed with an approved runbook/SOP and post-transfer verification, and average time-to-complete for manual transfers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control about manual transfer procedures, percentage coverage of documented procedures and adherence is primary; counts and rates of manual transfers show operational volume and error rates.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of manual transfer events executed that reference an approved runbook/SOP and include post-transfer verification, plus counts of manual transfers, operator IDs, start/end timestamps, and any errors or rollbacks recorded.
data_signals: manual transfer operation logs (timestamp, operator id, source component, destination component)
associated change request or ticket ids
runbook/SOP reference and version invoked during transfer
post-transfer verification checks/results (success/failure)
rollbacks or remediation actions and timestamps
number of manual transfer errors or incidents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured manual-transfer logs with linked runbook versions and automated post-transfer verification results; Weak: email confirmations or undocumented manual notes

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly requires manual transfer procedures between components, which are preventive measures to avoid predictable failures.

SI-13(3).differentiation_note: Targets manual (human-executed) transfers specifically — measurement must capture operator actions, runbook invocation, and verification, unlike automated-transfer sibling controls.

SI-13(4).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical components that have standby components installed and notification subscriptions configured, plus counts of standby activation events and delivery success rates for notification messages.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive readiness is best expressed as coverage/percentage of components with standby and notifications configured; counts of activations and notification delivery rates provide operational validation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical components with a configured standby (asset id, standby id, last sync timestamp) and configured notification targets; counts and outcomes of standby activation events, and notification delivery logs including recipient and status.
data_signals: standby component inventory (primary id -> standby id, last sync time)
installation/configuration records for standby components (timestamp, installer, config version)
notification subscription/configuration records (who/what receives alerts)
notification delivery logs (timestamp, recipient, delivery status)
standby activation logs (timestamp, trigger, success/failure, time-to-activate)
health-check failure events that triggered standby activation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory + activation logs + notification delivery receipts; Weak: checklist stating standby exists without activation or delivery logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement to install standby components and configure notification is a preventive readiness control designed to mitigate predictable failures.

SI-13(4).differentiation_note: Specifically measures standby hardware/software presence and notification configuration/delivery, distinct from transfer or failover testing which measure transition mechanics and outcomes.

SI-13(5).measurement_goal: Measure failover readiness and performance: percentage of critical services with failover configured, failover success rate, and mean time to failover (MTTF) for executed failover events and tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive capability requires demonstrating coverage/readiness (percentage of services with failover configured); rates and trends of failover events show operational effectiveness and stability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical services with configured and tested failover, plus counts of failover events and tests with success/failure outcome, and mean time-to-failover measured from trigger to restoration of service.
data_signals: failover configuration inventory (service id -> failover configured boolean, config version)
scheduled failover test logs (timestamp, service id, result, duration)
automatic/manual failover event logs (trigger, timestamp, duration, success/failure)
mean time to failover (per event/test)
post-failover validation check results
incident/ticket records for failed failovers
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated failover event logs with precise timestamps, successful post-failover validation, and regular test evidence; Weak: declarative statements of failover capability without test outcomes or event logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing failover capability (a preventive readiness measure), implied by the title 'Failover Capability'.

SI-13(5).differentiation_note: Focuses on actual failover mechanisms and performance (success rates and MTTR) rather than inventory or transfer procedures; requires event/timing measurements to demonstrate capability.

SI-14.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of compute and storage resources configured and operating as non-persistent (ephemeral/stateless) per policy, the count of persistent-exception occurrences, and the distribution of instance lifetimes to demonstrate non-persistence enforcement.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (enforce non-persistence), so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rate) show policy adherence; counts and distributions show raw exception volumes and instance lifetime patterns for further analysis.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of provisioned instances and storage volumes flagged non-persistent versus total provisioned, count of persistent storage attach/mount events per instance, number of exceptions granted, and instance lifetime distribution (creation → termination timestamps) to confirm ephemeral behavior.
data_signals: instance provisioning records with non-persistent/stateless flag
instance creation and termination timestamps (lifecycle events)
block storage attach/detach events and persistent volume IDs
configuration items/templates indicating ephemeral image use
record of approved exceptions for persistent resources
automation/orchestration job runs enforcing non-persistence (timestamps & success/failure)
inventory of instances by persistence classification
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous lifecycle logs (creation and termination events) and automated enforcement job success rates are strong evidence.
Periodic manual reports or single point-in-time screenshots are weak evidence because they don't show ongoing non-persistence behavior.
High percentage of instances with non-persistent flags and near-zero persistent-attach events indicate strong control operation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Non-persistence' implies preventive configuration to avoid persistent state (enforce ephemeral resources) rather than detection or remediation.

SI-14.differentiation_note: This baseline SI-14 entry covers overall policy and enforcement around non-persistence across compute/storage; the enhancements below focus on specific mechanisms (trusted-image refresh, non-persistent information handling, and connectivity behaviors).

SI-14(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of deployed images and runtime instances that were refreshed or built from an approved 'trusted sources' list within the mandated refresh interval and count of instances running outdated/untrusted images.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive enhancement requiring images/instances be refreshed from trusted sources, percentage coverage of refreshed images vs total is the key compliance indicator; counts/trends show raw and temporal behavior.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of active images/instances with last-refresh timestamp within policy window and source marking that matches an approved trusted-sources registry; count of active instances using images not in the trusted list and timestamps of last refresh or rebuild.
data_signals: image build/registry metadata including source/owner and trust-tag
image last-refresh or build timestamps
list of approved trusted sources (registry IDs)
instance-to-image mapping (which image an instance was launched from)
inventory of active instances with image trust status
automation pipeline logs showing image refresh/build events and results
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated image build and refresh logs tied to an approved trusted-source registry with timestamps are strong evidence.
Manual attestations without artifact-level metadata or missing image-to-instance mapping are weak evidence.
Low count of instances using untrusted/outdated images strengthens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Refresh from Trusted Sources' indicates preventive controls to ensure only approved/trusted images are used and refreshed.

SI-14(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on image/source provenance and refresh cadence (trusted-image pipeline) rather than overall non-persistence configuration or data/network behavior.

SI-14(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of sessions and processes that do not persist sensitive information to durable storage (per policy), count of data writes to persistent stores flagged as sensitive, and number of policy violations where non-persistent information became persistent.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This enhancement prevents persistence of information; percentage metrics show compliance coverage (sessions/processes avoiding persistence), while counts/trends capture violations and remediation activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of application sessions/processes that write zero bytes of sensitive-classified data to persistent volumes, count of sensitive-data-write events to persistent stores, timestamps and user/process identifiers for each write, and number of exceptions or policy overrides recorded.
data_signals: data classification labels applied at ingestion and storage (sensitive/non-sensitive)
file write events to persistent volumes with process and user context
session/process identifiers with duration and write counts
storage mount events showing ephemeral vs persistent mounts
DLP (data loss prevention) or endpoint agent alerts for persistence of sensitive data
records of approved exceptions for persistent storage of information
evidence_strength_indicators: Real-time DLP/endpoint agent logs linking sensitive data classification to write events on persistent volumes are strong evidence.
Post-incident manual file scans without process/session correlation are weaker evidence for ongoing non-persistence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The term 'Non-persistent Information' implies preventive measures to avoid storing sensitive information persistently, rather than detecting after the fact.

SI-14(2).differentiation_note: Specifically addresses data persistence (writes of information to durable stores) and DLP-style signals, unlike the base SI-14 which covers compute/storage lifecycle more broadly.

SI-14(3).measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of network sessions and remote connections that terminate within configured ephemeral/session-lifetime limits, count of long-lived persistent connections exceeding policy, and number of network configuration items (ACLs, firewall rules) permitting persistent connectivity exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive enforcement of non-persistent connectivity is best measured by percentage of sessions complying with ephemeral/maximum-lifetime policies; counts and trends highlight exceptions and temporal patterns.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of network sessions/connections terminating within policy-defined maximum lifetimes, count and identifiers of sessions exceeding lifetime thresholds, timestamps for connection start/stop, and records of ACL/firewall rules that allow persistent sessions or have been temporarily modified.
data_signals: network session logs with start and end timestamps (flow logs, proxy logs)
connection duration distributions by protocol and port
firewall/ACL rule change history and exception records
VPN/session manager logs (active session counts and lifetimes)
orchestration/automation records that create temporary network rules
alerts for long-lived or reestablished persistent connections
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous flow/session logs with start/stop times and automated enforcement of max-session lifetimes are strong evidence.
Intermittent manual checks of firewall rules without session correlation are weak evidence for persistent-connection enforcement.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The label 'Non-persistent Connectivity' indicates preventive controls to limit session lifetimes and avoid long-lived network connections.

SI-14(3).differentiation_note: Targets network/session-level ephemeral behavior and session lifetime enforcement (flows and ACLs), distinguishing it from SI-14 which centers on resource lifecycle and SI-14(2) which focuses on data persistence.

SI-15.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of controls that filter, redact, or block outbound information: (1) percent of egress channels/endpoints with active output-filtering/DLP policies, (2) count and rate of blocked/redacted outbound events by severity/sensitive-data-type, and (3) instances of unauthorized sensitive data transmitted despite filtering.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control — auditors need coverage % (what proportion of egress points are protected). Counts and rates of blocked/redacted events and trends show effectiveness and detection of bypass attempts.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (A) percentage = number of egress interfaces/endpoints with active output-filtering rules / total egress interfaces; (B) daily/weekly counts of outbound events classified as blocked, quarantined, redacted, or allowed with sensitive-data match, grouped by data type and policy rule; (C) list of filter policy definitions with last-modified timestamps and enforcement scope (mail gateway, web proxy, cloud-storage egress, API egress).
data_signals: egress endpoint inventory (gateway, proxy, mail, cloud egress endpoints) with identifiers
filtering/DLP policy definitions and scope (rule id, match patterns, actions: block/quarantine/redact/allow)
DLP/event logs of outbound flows including timestamp, source endpoint, destination, matched rule id, matched data type, and enforcement action
count of blocked/quarantined/redacted outbound events per rule per time window
outbound transfer logs (file sizes, destinations) correlated with DLP matches
policy change and review records (policy id, author, last updated timestamp)
exceptions/whitelist records and approved bypass tickets with timestamps
incidents where sensitive data left the environment despite filters (incident id, root cause, remediation timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated DLP/egress logs with structured fields (rule id, action, data type) are strong evidence.
Configuration inventories showing filters applied to all egress endpoints are strong; periodic manual test reports without logs are weak evidence.
High counts of blocked events with matching reduction trends after rule tuning indicate effective filtering; repeated incidents with exceptions indicate weak control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name and intent focus on filtering and preventing unauthorized information release — a preventive enforcement control (filters/block/redact outbound data).

SI-15.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of outbound/egress filtering coverage and enforcement effectiveness (policy definitions, DLP events, blocked/redacted counts), unlike sibling controls that might focus on scanning, access, or encryption; limitation: description was minimal so keywords and signals reflect standard SI-15 output-filtering concepts.

SI-16.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems and deployed binaries with exploit-mitigation memory protections enabled (ASLR, DEP/NX, stack protection, CFI) and count of memory-corruption exploit attempts prevented or resulting in crashes; demonstrate continuous configuration coverage and runtime prevention effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive configuration control where auditors need coverage percentages (e.g., percent of hosts with ASLR/NX enabled). Supplement with counts of prevented exploit events and rates/distribution of affected binaries to show both coverage and operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of hosts/instances with ASLR enabled, percentage with DEP/NX active, percentage of deployed binaries compiled with stack-protector/CFI flags, count and timestamps of prevented execution events (DEP/ASLR triggered) and memory-corruption crash reports; correlate with asset inventory and CI/CD build metadata to show coverage per asset and per deployment pipeline.
data_signals: host configuration records: ASLR status per host/VM
process-level DEP/NX/non-executable memory flags
binary build metadata: presence of -fstack-protector, /GS, /SAFESEH, or CFI/Control Flow Guard flags
runtime prevention logs: DEP/ASLR/CFI blocked execution events with timestamps and host identifiers
crash reports labeled as memory corruption (stack/heap overflow, use-after-free) with hashes
vulnerability scanner or configuration scanner results noting missing exploit mitigations
CI/CD pipeline artifacts indicating compile/linker flags used per build
sandboxing/containerization status for high-risk processes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, continuous configuration scans and inventory showing ASLR/NX and stack-protector/CFI enabled across all in-scope hosts and CI/CD build metadata proving binaries were compiled with protections; runtime prevention logs showing blocked exploit attempts correlated to hosts.
Weak: one-off manual screenshots, single host checks, or annual penetration test reports without continuous config scan data or CI/CD build evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control's focus on memory protections (ASLR/DEP/stack protections/CFI) is designed to prevent exploitation of memory-corruption vulnerabilities, which is characteristic of a preventive control.

SI-16.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets runtime and build-time memory exploit mitigations (ASLR/DEP/stack/CFI) and requires both configuration/CI metadata coverage and runtime prevention logs, unlike sibling controls that may focus only on vulnerability discovery or patching.

SI-17.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and operational effectiveness of fail-safe procedures: percentage of critical systems with documented and implemented fail-safe procedures, count and success-rate of fail-safe activations, and distribution of time-to-safe-state after failure events.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Fail-safe procedures are primarily preventive (ensuring systems can enter a safe state) so coverage/implementation percentages are most useful; counts and rates of activations and success provide operational verification and trending shows reliability over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of critical assets with a documented fail-safe procedure and the timestamped records of fail-safe activations showing outcome (success/failure) and time-to-safe-state. Also measure scheduled test executions and pass/fail results per system, and the number of systems lacking documented procedures.
data_signals: inventory of critical systems with flag for documented fail-safe procedure
fail-safe activation logs with timestamps and outcome (success/failure)
time-to-safe-state or duration-to-stable-state per activation
scheduled fail-safe test execution records and pass/fail results
configuration records showing failover/redundancy settings
backup/restore verification logs tied to fail-safe procedures
change tickets or runbooks linking procedure versions to systems
post-incident reports noting fail-safe invocation and effectiveness
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated activation logs showing timestamps, outcomes, and measured time-to-safe-state across all critical assets; periodic automated test records with pass rates.
Weak: one-off tabletop exercise notes or undocumented manual reports without timestamps or outcome fields.
Strong: linkage between configuration (redundancy/failover) state and documented procedure versions; Weak: only policy text without implementation evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The title 'Fail-safe Procedures' implies actions to place systems into a safe state to prevent harm, which is preventative in nature.

SI-17.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on safe-state/failover procedure coverage and operational effectiveness (activations, success rates, time-to-safe-state), unlike sibling controls that may focus on detection, remediation, or vulnerability scanning.

SI-18.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of PII data quality operations: percentage of PII records with validated accuracy/metadata, count and age of unresolved quality exceptions, and rate of corrections or deletions applied to PII records over a reporting period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control aims to ensure PII data quality and coverage (best measured as percent of records meeting quality criteria); counts of exceptions and trending show operational workload and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of PII records that meet defined quality criteria (have required metadata, validated accuracy, and provenance) and the count/age distribution of open PII quality exceptions; track remediation actions (correction/deletion) with timestamps and actor IDs to show closure.
data_signals: PII inventory with record identifiers and data domain
data quality validation results per record (validation timestamp, validation checks passed/failed)
metadata presence flags (e.g., tag existence, provenance fields, source system)
quality exception/ticket logs (creation timestamp, severity, assigned owner, status)
remediation actions (correction/deletion) with before/after snapshots and timestamps
overdue/unresolved quality issue count and age distribution
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated continuous quality validation logs and record-level provenance snapshots are strong evidence.
Periodic manual spreadsheets or ad-hoc reports without record IDs are weak evidence.
High tag coverage plus declining open-exception trend indicates strong operation.
Single point-in-time reconciliations without remediation timestamps are weaker.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title emphasizes 'Quality Operations' for PII (preventing errors and ensuring accuracy), which is primarily preventive in nature.

SI-18.differentiation_note: This top-level control defines overall PII data quality objectives and coverage metrics; sibling controls focus on specific mechanisms (automation, tagging, collection, requests, notifications) that operationalize this goal.

SI-18(1).measurement_goal: Measure automation coverage and reliability for PII quality tasks: percentage of PII quality workflows automated, automation success/failure rates, and count of exceptions routed to human review per period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Automation support is about coverage and reliability—percent coverage of PII workflows shows preventive control reach; failure/exception rates (rate/count) show operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of defined PII quality workflows that are implemented as automated processes and the automation success rate (successful runs / total runs) with timestamps; count and reasons for automation exceptions escalated to manual handling.
data_signals: automation workflow registry (list of PII quality workflows and automation status)
automation execution logs (run ID, start/end timestamp, success/failure status)
exception/alert logs from automation runs with error codes and stack traces
count of records processed by automation vs processed manually
timestamped handoff entries where automation escalated to human review
automation configuration versions and change history
evidence_strength_indicators: Detailed automation execution logs with record-level IDs and success/failure outcomes are strong evidence.
Manual change logs or one-off scripts without execution history are weak evidence.
Low automation exception rates and high automated throughput indicate strong support.
Frequent manual interventions or undocumented automation scripts weaken evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Automation Support' implies enabling automated PII quality processes to prevent human error and enforce coverage, a preventive function.

SI-18(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on automation capability and reliability for PII quality tasks, not on tagging, collection policy, or request fulfillment which are handled by other siblings.

SI-18(2).measurement_goal: Measure tag coverage and correctness for PII: percentage of PII records with required data tags/metadata, count of mismatched or missing tags, and distribution of tags by data domain.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Data tagging is a coverage/compliance activity—percentage of records tagged correctly is the primary indicator; counts and distribution reveal scope and exception hotspots.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of records in the PII inventory that have required tags/metadata fields populated and valid (tag schema-compliant), plus lists/counts of untagged or mismatched records grouped by system/source.
data_signals: PII record metadata fields (tag keys and values)
tag schema definitions and required tag list
untagged PII record list (record ID, system, ingestion timestamp)
tag change logs (who/when changed tags)
tag validation error logs (schema mismatches)
tag coverage by data source/system
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated tag validation reports and record-level tag presence are strong evidence.
Manual spreadsheets of tagged samples are weaker evidence.
High tag coverage across all sources and low schema validation errors indicate strong control.
Inconsistent tag schemas across systems or frequent manual tag fixes indicate weak control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement to apply and manage data tags is a preventive control to ensure records are identifiable and handled correctly, implied by 'Data Tags'.

SI-18(2).differentiation_note: Specifically measures tagging/metadata coverage and correctness for PII records, whereas other siblings address automation, collection, and request handling.

SI-18(3).measurement_goal: Measure compliance of PII collection with policy: percentage of new PII collections that include required lawful-basis/consent metadata and minimal-collection justification, and count of collection events lacking required documentation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Collection compliance is primarily about coverage and correctness (percent of collections with required metadata/consent); counts and trends show operational volume and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of PII collection events (ingestion or intake) that record required collection metadata (source, purpose, lawful basis/consent flag, collection timestamp) and the count of collection events missing one or more required fields.
data_signals: collection event logs (event ID, timestamp, source system)
consent/lawful-basis records linked to collection event
collection purpose field and justification text
data minimization flags or collection field lists
ingestion pipeline configurations showing collected fields
list of collection events missing required metadata
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated ingestion logs that include required collection metadata for each record are strong evidence.
Samples or spot-checks without linkage to ingest logs are weak evidence.
Consistent presence of lawful-basis/consent fields and low missing-metadata counts indicate strong control.
High rates of missing collection metadata or undocumented sources indicate weak control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Collection' implies controls at the point of data intake to prevent unnecessary or undocumented PII collection, which is preventive.

SI-18(3).differentiation_note: Targets PII intake and compliance at collection time (consent, purpose, minimal fields), unlike siblings that address tagging, automation, or requests after collection.

SI-18(4).measurement_goal: Measure handling and timeliness of individual data subject requests: count of requests received by type, percentage fulfilled within the required timeframe, and average time-to-fulfill per request type.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is an operational corrective process; rates (e.g., fulfillment rate within timeframe) reflect responsiveness, with percentage and counts supporting context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of individual requests (access/correction/deletion) received and the rate completed within policy timeframes; measure time from request receipt to completion, verification steps executed, and reasons for denial if applicable.
data_signals: request intake logs (request ID, requester identity verification timestamp, request type)
request lifecycle timestamps (received, acknowledged, fulfilled/denied)
request outcome records (fulfilled, partially fulfilled, denied) with reason codes
correspondence logs with the requester (emails, portal messages)
SLA or policy timeframe definitions for each request type
evidence_strength_indicators: Structured request-tracking system with timestamped lifecycle events and linkage to actions taken is strong evidence.
Ad-hoc email threads without consolidated request IDs or timestamps are weak evidence.
High on-time fulfillment rate and documented verification steps indicate strong control.
Frequent delays, missing verification artifacts, or undocumented denials indicate weak control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Handling 'Individual Requests' (access/correction/deletion) is a corrective operational process to respond to data subject actions, implied by the name.

SI-18(4).differentiation_note: Focuses on operational metrics for processing individual data subject requests (timeliness and completeness), unlike siblings which focus on prevention (collection/tagging) or automation.

SI-18(5).measurement_goal: Measure execution and notification of corrections/deletions: count and rate of correction/deletion notices issued, percentage of requested corrections/deletions completed with audit trail, and average time-to-complete per notice.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Corrective actions are best evidenced by rates (completion per request) and counts (volume), with percentages showing proportion completed versus requested.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate and count of correction or deletion notices issued and executed with linked audit records (pre/post record snapshots), proof of notification to the individual, and timestamps for when correction/deletion actions were applied.
data_signals: correction/deletion request records (request ID, requester, timestamp)
action logs showing execution of correction/deletion (actor ID, action timestamp, system affected)
pre- and post-change snapshots or checksums
notification delivery logs to the requester (email/portal confirmation)
audit trail entries linking request to action and notification
evidence_strength_indicators: Linked request → action → notification audit trail with record snapshots is strong evidence.
Manual sign-off notes without system change logs are weak evidence.
High completion rate with timely notifications indicates strong control.
Missing pre/post snapshots or undelivered notifications indicate weak control.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Notice of Correction or Deletion' refers to performing and notifying corrective changes to PII, which is corrective by nature.

SI-18(5).differentiation_note: Specializes in measuring the execution and notification chain for corrective PII actions (corrections/deletions), distinct from request intake (SI-18(4)) and tagging/automation controls.

SI-19.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of datasets and data releases that have required de-identification applied (removal/masking/encryption/hashing/replacement or statistical methods) and count any data releases that include direct identifiers or exceed accepted re-identification risk thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SI-19 is a preventive program control: auditors typically want coverage percentages (what percent of datasets/releases are de-identified) plus counts and trends of exceptions and risk-score distributions to show ongoing compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of datasets (or releases) with a documented de-identification method applied before release; count of releases containing one or more direct identifiers; per-dataset re-identification risk score or metric and the number of exceptions with justification.
data_signals: dataset inventory with sensitivity and de-identification status
data release records and approval logs
transformation logs showing removal/masking/encryption/hashing/replacement operations per field
field-level metadata indicating 'direct identifier' flags and removal timestamps
re-identification risk assessment scores or statistical disclosure control reports
validation/test reports for de-identification procedures
exception/waiver records for any releases containing identifiers
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated transformation logs and per-release signed approval records plus generated risk-score outputs
Weak: manual spreadsheets or attestations without transformation logs or risk-score outputs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates de-identification to prevent disclosure of identifiers (preventive action).

SI-19.differentiation_note: This entry covers the overall de-identification program and is focused on coverage and exception counts; siblings target specific lifecycle phases or techniques (collection, archiving, DP, etc.).

SI-19(1).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of data ingestion/collection pipelines that apply required de-identification/minimization controls at time of collection and count incoming records that include direct identifier fields contrary to policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Collection-phase controls are preventive and best evidenced by coverage percentages of ingestion pipelines plus counts of non-compliant ingested records to track exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of active ingestion pipelines with configured de-identification steps (field removal/masking/encryption) and count of ingested records flagged with direct identifiers per ingestion job.
data_signals: ingest pipeline configuration records indicating de-identification steps
ingest execution logs with transformation timestamps
ingested-record audit logs showing fields present on ingestion
dataset field-level schema flags for 'direct identifier'
exceptions or incident tickets for improper collection
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated ingest logs showing applied transformations for every pipeline run
Weak: periodic manual checklists or ad-hoc developer notes without pipeline logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: Applying de-identification at collection prevents storage of identifiers and is thus preventive.

SI-19(1).differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on de-identification applied at data collection/ingest rather than later lifecycle stages (archiving or release).

SI-19(2).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of archived datasets that have de-identification applied consistent with retention policies and count archives containing direct identifiers or lacking de-identification metadata.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Archiving controls are preventive and evaluative over time; coverage percentage shows compliance, and trends show policy adherence across retention periods.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of archived datasets with recorded de-identification method and timestamp and count of archived records or datasets that still contain direct identifier fields contrary to archiving policy.
data_signals: archive inventory with de-identification status and retention classification
archival transformation logs showing masking/encryption/hash applied
archive metadata with de-identification method and date
audit logs of archive access and exports
exceptions/waivers for archived datasets containing identifiers
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-recorded archival transformations and metadata for each object with verifiable timestamps
Weak: manual archival records or undocumented deleted fields

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring de-identification before archiving prevents future disclosure of identifiers (preventive).

SI-19(2).differentiation_note: Targets archived/retained data specifically (retention lifecycle) rather than collection or release activities.

SI-19(3).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of data releases that were verified as de-identified before release and count releases where direct identifiers were present or re-identification risk exceeded acceptable thresholds at release time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Release-phase controls are preventive and audited at release time; coverage percentages and counts of non-compliant releases are the primary evidence types.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of releases with signed verification that required de-identification was applied and the count of releases with direct identifiers or that failed risk acceptance criteria at time of release.
data_signals: release approval logs with de-identification verification
pre-release transformation logs and snapshots
per-release re-identification risk assessment scores
release exception/waiver records
release artifact manifests listing removed/masked fields
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: pre-release automated checks and signed approval records tied to transformation logs
Weak: post-release attestations without pre-release artifacts or risk scores

control_type_classification.reasoning: Verifying de-identification prior to data release actively prevents disclosure of identifiers.

SI-19(3).differentiation_note: Specifically measures controls and evidence required at the point of data release (pre-release verification) rather than collection or storage.

SI-19(4).measurement_goal: Measure coverage and correctness of technical transformations (removal, masking, encryption, hashing, replacement) applied to direct identifier fields: percentage of records/fields transformed per policy and counts of transformation failures or reversible transformations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: These are preventive technical controls where percent coverage (fields/records transformed) is primary; counts and distributions show failure modes and methods used.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of direct-identifier fields transformed by the prescribed method per dataset and counts of transformation errors or instances where reversible transformations exist; include key-management evidence for encryption when used.
data_signals: transformation operation logs (removal/mask/hash/encrypt/replacement) with timestamps
field-level transformation status in dataset metadata
transformation error/failure logs and reasons
encryption key usage logs and key IDs for encrypted fields
hash algorithm and salt metadata for hashed identifiers
counts of records with untransformed direct-identifier fields
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, auditable transformation logs plus key management records and per-field metadata
Weak: ad-hoc manual masking in spreadsheets or undocumented scripts

control_type_classification.reasoning: Applying technical transformations to identifiers is an active preventive measure to prevent disclosure.

SI-19(4).differentiation_note: Focuses on the technical evidence of specific transformation operations (encryption, masking, hashing) rather than programmatic assessment or statistical methods.

SI-19(5).measurement_goal: Measure statistical disclosure risk across released datasets (e.g., k-anonymity/l-diversity/t-closeness metrics or equivalent) and the percentage of datasets meeting organizational disclosure-risk thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Statistical disclosure control is best evidenced by distributions of risk metrics (k-anonymity, re-identification risk) and percentages meeting thresholds; distributions show population-level risk characteristics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-dataset statistical disclosure metrics (e.g., k-anonymity / l-diversity / t-closeness values or equivalent risk score), percent of datasets within acceptable disclosure risk thresholds, and counts of datasets requiring additional suppression or generalization.
data_signals: statistical disclosure control reports with k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness metrics
per-dataset suppression/summarization rates
information loss or utility metrics associated with transformations
re-identification test outputs
dataset-level risk acceptance records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-generated disclosure-control metrics and suppression records per dataset
Weak: informal statements of 'de-identified' without supporting statistical metrics

control_type_classification.reasoning: Statistical disclosure controls are applied to prevent re-identification and thus are preventive in nature.

SI-19(5).differentiation_note: Centers on statistical risk metrics (k-anonymity/l-diversity/t-closeness) and utility tradeoffs, unlike sibling entries that focus on technical transforms or DP.

SI-19(6).measurement_goal: Measure compliance with differential privacy controls by tracking privacy budget usage and the proportion of query responses or datasets that apply DP mechanisms within organizational epsilon/delta thresholds; track the number of queries that consume privacy budget and remaining budget over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Differential privacy requires numeric assessment (epsilon/delta and privacy-budget scores); a compliance/DP score best captures whether DP parameters meet policy, with trends showing budget consumption.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-dataset or per-query DP parameters (epsilon, delta) applied, privacy-budget consumption over time, percent of queries/releases using DP, and count of queries/releases exceeding policy epsilon/delta thresholds.
data_signals: DP mechanism logs recording epsilon/delta per query or release
privacy-budget ledger showing consumption and remaining budget
query audit logs tied to DP application
policy threshold definitions for acceptable epsilon/delta
exception records where DP was not applied
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-recorded DP parameters and privacy-budget ledgers correlated to query logs
Weak: manual notes claiming DP application without recorded epsilon/delta or budget tracking

control_type_classification.reasoning: Differential privacy parameters and budget management are preventive measures that limit disclosure risk before it occurs.

SI-19(6).differentiation_note: Specifically measures DP parameter application and privacy-budget consumption rather than generic statistical or technical transformations.

SI-19(7).measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of de-identification algorithms and software components that have been validated (test coverage and pass rate) and count outstanding validation defects or unvalidated versions in use.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Validation evidence is a preventive assurance activity; percent validated components and counts of failing/untested versions are most useful for auditors.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of de-identification algorithms/software components with passing validation test suites and documentation; counts of validation defects, and number of deployed algorithm versions without validation.
data_signals: validation test reports and pass/fail results for algorithms/software
test coverage metrics and test-case catalogs
deployment registry showing algorithm/software versions in use
defect/issue tracker entries for validation failures
signed verification or acceptance records for validated components
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CI test results, versioned deployment registry and validation artifacts
Weak: undocumented manual tests or absent test artifacts

control_type_classification.reasoning: Validating algorithms/software ensures de-identification works as intended and prevents incorrect processing (preventive).

SI-19(7).differentiation_note: Focuses on validation/test evidence for algorithms and software rather than the operational application of transformations or statistical methods.

SI-19(8).measurement_goal: Count and rate of adversarial/motivated-intruder re-identification attempts and the success rate (number of successful re-identifications), and document severity of exposed identifiers to demonstrate detection of residual disclosure risk.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Motivated-intruder testing is detective: counts of tests and successful re-identifications (and success rate) are direct evidence of detection capability and residual risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of motivated-intruder/red-team tests executed, number of successful re-identifications per test, classification of records re-identified, and remediation actions taken for each success.
data_signals: red-team / motivated-intruder test reports
counts of successful re-identifications and details of re-identified fields
test timelines and methods used
remediation tickets and closure timestamps for issues found
post-test risk assessments and acceptance records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal, repeatable adversarial test reports with reproducible steps and remediation evidence
Weak: anecdotal or informal testing without documented results or follow-up remediation

control_type_classification.reasoning: Motivated-intruder testing detects whether de-identification has failed (detective activity).

SI-19(8).differentiation_note: This entry is detective and focused on adversarial testing outcomes (successful re-identifications), distinct from preventive coverage or technical transformation evidence in other siblings.

SI-20.measurement_goal: Measure instrumentation coverage of taint-tracking across applications, the count and rate of tainted-input events and taint-propagation alerts to sensitive sinks, and the percentage of taint findings that are blocked or remediated within policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Tainting produces discrete detection events (tainted-input events, propagation alerts, blocked attempts) so counts and rates are most useful; percentages (coverage, remediation %) and trends support coverage and improvement analysis.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of application components/instrumentation points with active taint-tracking; number and timestamps of tainted-input events by source; number of taint-propagation alerts where tainted data reached defined sensitive sinks; count of blocked tainting incidents and remediation/closure timestamps for recorded taint findings.
data_signals: taint-tracking instrumentation inventory (component id, enabled flag, version)
tainted input event logs (source, parameter, timestamp, request id)
taint-propagation alerts (source, sink, path, timestamp, severity)
blocked tainting actions or enforcement logs (policy id, action taken, timestamp)
sensitive sink inventory (DB write, file write, exec, external call) mapped to application components
dynamic taint analysis test results (test id, findings, timestamps)
static analysis taint/source-sink findings exported from SAST tools
remediation tickets for taint findings (ticket id, open/close timestamps, assignee)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated taint-tracking logs with source/sink detail and timestamps are strong evidence.
High instrumentation coverage (near 100%) and low counts of tainted-data-to-sink incidents indicate effective control.
Periodic manual test reports or single-point-in-time scans without continuous logs are weak evidence.
Remediation tickets with closure timestamps and reduced recurrence of identical propagation alerts strengthen evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Tainting' implies detection of tainted data flows and alerts when tainted input reaches sensitive sinks, which is primarily a detective activity.

SI-20.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on data-flow instrumentation and detection of tainted inputs reaching sensitive sinks (taint-tracking metrics, event counts, instrumentation coverage), unlike sibling controls that may address general vulnerability scanning or patching; note: control description was not provided, so taxonomy is inferred from the control name 'Tainting'.

SI-21.measurement_goal: Measure the timeliness and completeness of scheduled information refreshes (e.g., asset/inventory/configuration/data refresh) by reporting the percentage of items refreshed within the defined refresh window and counts of refresh failures or overdue refreshes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage/recency measures (percent of items refreshed on-schedule). Trends show improvement or degradation over time and counts capture failure volumes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of records/assets/configurations with a 'last refreshed' timestamp within the policy-defined refresh window, count of refresh jobs executed and failed, and mean time between refreshes grouped by asset type or data domain.
data_signals: item last_updated / last_refreshed timestamp
scheduled refresh job execution timestamps
refresh job success/failure status codes and error messages
count of items eligible for refresh (asset/inventory/configuration/data list)
overdue refresh count (items not refreshed within policy window)
mean time between refresh events per item or asset group
refresh frequency/schedule configuration (policy-defined refresh interval)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated refresh job logs with timestamps and success/failure codes (strong)
High coverage percentage (most items refreshed within policy window) and steady/improving trend (strong)
Ad-hoc/manual update notes or single point-in-time screenshots (weak)
Frequent failed refresh records or large overdue counts (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The term 'Refresh' implies periodic updates to keep information current, which is a preventive activity to avoid reliance on stale data.

SI-21.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on the timeliness and completeness of information refresh operations (recency/coverage) rather than on detection or remediation; description was minimal so measurements emphasize refresh timestamps, schedules, and success/failure counts.

SI-22.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of critical datasets and information flows that have multiple independent sources/providers or storage locations (>=2), the vendor/source concentration for critical information, and the count of single-source dependencies that create single points of failure.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Diversity is best expressed as coverage/percentage of assets meeting the multi-source requirement (primary). Counts of single-source items and distribution metrics (vendor concentration) are needed to quantify exposure and trends to show improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of critical datasets and information flows, number of distinct independent sources/providers per dataset, list of storage/replication locations per dataset, and timestamps of most recent ingests/replications. Produce a percentage of critical datasets with >=2 independent sources and a vendor-source concentration score (e.g., HHI or share by top-3 providers) and list of datasets with single-source dependency.
data_signals: catalog of datasets/information flows with criticality classification
source/provider identifier list per dataset (vendor IDs, internal source IDs)
replication/storage location records per dataset (region, cluster, provider)
ingest/replication timestamps and success/failure logs
provenance/metadata showing origin and transformation lineage
cross-source reconciliation or validation result logs
vendor concentration metrics (counts by provider per critical dataset)
list of datasets flagged as single-source or single-point-of-failure
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated provenance and ingestion logs showing distinct independent sources, documented replication locations, and reconciliation results over multiple periods.
Weak: one-time attestations, manual spreadsheets, or single-point backup snapshots without provenance metadata or regular verification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Information Diversity' implies preventive controls to avoid single-source dependency and improve resilience by ensuring multiple information sources.

SI-22.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about measuring multiplicity and independence of information sources and vendor concentration, not about access controls, logging, or remediation — it requires metrics on source counts and provenance rather than detection or patching metrics.

SI-23.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of sensitive information that is protected via approved fragmentation (or equivalent separation) techniques, the coverage of fragmentation controls across identified sensitive datasets, and the count of unauthorized reconstruction or reassembly attempts detected and remediated.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors will want coverage metrics (percent of sensitive data using fragmentation) as primary evidence; counts of reconstruction attempts and trends over time are useful secondary signals to show detection and remediation activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of datasets classified as sensitive that have an approved fragmentation or separation control applied; count of fragments per dataset and mapping of fragments to distinct storage zones/accounts; number of detected/unapproved reconstruction attempts and time-to-detect for those attempts. Measurement should be grouped by data classification tier and storage location.
data_signals: inventory/catalog of sensitive datasets with fragmentation status (fragmented / not fragmented)
fragment-to-storage-location mapping (fragment ID -> storage account/zone)
data classification tags and sensitivity level for each dataset
access logs for fragment objects (user, timestamp, operation, source IP)
alerts/logs for fragment-reassembly or linkage detection (reconstruction attempt alerts)
encryption/key-management events tied to fragments (key access, rotation)
policy approval or exception records for fragmentation implementation
incident records for unauthorized reconstruction or data reassembly
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory showing continuous coverage (fragmentation status) and machine-generated logs of fragment access + reassembly-detection alerts; separation of fragments across independent storage accounts or zones with distinct access controls.
Weak: periodic manual inventories, one-time documentation, or screenshots showing fragmentation design without machine-accessible logs or evidence of enforcement and monitoring.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Information Fragmentation' implies preventive measures to separate or fragment information to reduce disclosure risk (hence classified as preventive); confidence is limited due to missing control description.

SI-23.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measuring separation/fragmentation coverage and detection of reassembly attempts (unlike general data-protection controls that focus only on encryption or access), but the original control text was not provided so the taxonomy is inferred from the control name and therefore lower-confidence.

SR-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of required policies and procedures that are documented, approved with assigned owners, reviewed within the mandated review cycle, and accessible to relevant personnel; and measure exceptions/waivers and overdue reviews to demonstrate the program is operating.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage- and compliance-style measures (percentage of policies meeting requirements). Counts and trends provide context (how many, how overdue) and a score can summarize overall policy health.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A policy inventory table rows with metadata fields (policy_id, title, owner, approval_date, next_review_date, status, version) and derived metrics: percent of in-scope policies with valid owner and approval date, percent reviewed within the defined review cycle, count of overdue reviews, count of active exceptions/waivers, and number of personnel who completed policy-specific training.
data_signals: policy inventory records (policy_id, title, owner, approval_date, next_review_date, status, version)
policy revision history and approval signatures/timestamps
policy review activity logs (review_date, reviewer_id)
document repository access records showing availability/distribution
exceptions/waivers register (id, policy_ref, approver, expiry)
training completion records mapped to policy assignments
open/overdue policy review count and timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative machine-readable policy inventory with approval timestamps, owner IDs, and automated next_review reminders showing continuous updates and low overdue counts.
Weak: ad-hoc folder of documents or single snapshots (point-in-time PDFs) without revision history, owner metadata, or evidence of periodic review and approvals.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes documented policies and procedures which act to prevent noncompliant behavior and guide processes (policy/procedures are inherently preventive).

SR-1.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls that measure system state, this control requires measuring documentation completeness, review cycles, approvals and training coverage for policies — a governance/documentation-centric measurement set.

SR-2.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, currency, scope coverage, and operationalization of the Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM) plan: percentage of covered supplier tiers/assets, presence of an approved plan version and review cadence, and count of documented risk-assessments referenced by the plan.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A preventive documentation/control is best evidenced by coverage percentages (e.g., percent of suppliers/assets covered by the plan); counts (e.g., risk assessments) and trends (review cadence over time) complement coverage to show operationalization.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Presence of an approved SCRM plan (document id/version and approval timestamp), percent of supplier inventory (or supplier tiers) explicitly included in the plan, count of supplier risk assessments referenced by the plan, and timestamps of plan reviews/updates within the last 12 months compared to the policy-required review frequency.
data_signals: SCRM plan document metadata (document id, version, approval date)
plan review/update log entries with reviewer and timestamp
supplier inventory with coverage flag (in-scope per plan)
supplier tier classification referenced by plan
risk assessment artifacts referenced by the plan (count and dates)
plan distribution/acknowledgement records (who received/acknowledged plan)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: version-controlled SCRM plan stored in document management with automated review reminders, supplier inventory linkage showing % coverage, and recent review/approval timestamps within policy frequency.
Weak: single-point PDFs or emailed plan copies without version history, no supplier mapping, or only an initial plan with no subsequent reviews.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on a documented SCRM plan (preventive governance) — requirements like a 'plan' and its coverage are aimed at preventing supply-chain risk.

SR-2.differentiation_note: This control focuses on the documented SCRM plan itself (existence, scope and review cadence) rather than who executes it or how teams are organized.

SR-2(1).measurement_goal: Measure the establishment and operational readiness of the SCRM team: existence of an approved team charter, roster with assigned roles/owners, percent of critical suppliers with assigned SCRM owners, and evidence of recurring SCRM team meetings and training completion.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Establishing a team is a preventive control where coverage (percentage of suppliers with assigned owners, percent of roles filled) shows compliance; counts of appointments and meeting rates show operationalization.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Presence of an approved SCRM team charter (document id and approval date), roster count and percentage of required SCRM roles filled, count/percentage of critical suppliers with an assigned owner, number and frequency of SCRM team meetings in the past 12 months, and training completion records for team members.
data_signals: SCRM team charter/charter approval metadata (document id, approver, timestamp)
team roster with roles, start dates, and appointment letters
mapping of critical suppliers to assigned SCRM owners
meeting minutes or calendar invites showing cadence and attendance
training completion records for SCRM team members
escalation/communication contact lists for SCRM team
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: chartered team with role assignments in HR or IAM systems, automated mapping of critical suppliers to owners, regular meeting records and completed role-specific training.
Weak: informal team referenced in emails without charter, missing appointment documents, or inconsistent meeting records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Establish Scrm Team' indicates a preventive organizational control that assigns responsibilities and owners to reduce supply-chain risk.

SR-2(1).differentiation_note: Unlike SR-2 (the plan document), SR-2(1) is uniquely about personnel, role assignments, and operational readiness of the SCRM team (roster, charter, meetings, training).

SR-3.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of supply-chain controls by tracking the percentage of suppliers under defined security controls, the share of critical suppliers with completed risk assessments, supplier concentration metrics (single-/single-source counts), and counts of supplier-caused security incidents and remediation closures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SR-3 is a preventive control focused on coverage and process adherence; percentage metrics (coverage, compliance %) directly show how much of the supplier population is managed, while counts and trends show scale and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of active suppliers covered by formal supply-chain security controls and contracts, percent of critical/high-impact suppliers with a completed risk assessment within the past defined period, count of single-source suppliers for critical components, and count/closure timestamps of supplier-related security incidents and remediations.
data_signals: supplier registry with supplier IDs, criticality classification and spend tier
contract repository flagging presence and effective date of security/flow-down clauses per supplier
supplier risk assessment records with dates and risk scores
supplier incident logs with timestamps, severity and root-cause attribution
remediation plan records and remediation completion timestamps for supplier findings
supplier concentration metrics (top-N supplier spend ratios)
supplier audit reports and audit completion timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: High strength: automated supplier registry + contract database showing clause presence and timestamps, continuous supplier incident telemetry, and up-to-date risk assessment records for all critical suppliers.
Weak strength: ad-hoc spreadsheets, annual/manual reports, or one-off attestations without timestamps or corroborating incident/remediation logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Supply Chain Controls and Processes' describes establishment of controls/processes to prevent supply-chain risk, matching a preventive classification.

SR-3.differentiation_note: This is the broad, program-level control measuring overall supplier coverage and control effectiveness; sibling controls (SR-3(1)-(3)) focus on specific aspects (diversity, harm limitation, and contractual flow-down) that require different signals and metrics.

SR-3(1).measurement_goal: Measure supplier diversification by tracking the percentage of critical components or services with multiple qualified suppliers, supplier concentration (top-N spend) distribution, and the count of single-source suppliers for prioritized items.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: A preventive diversification control is best shown as percentages (coverage with ≥2 suppliers) and distributions (spend concentration), which demonstrate breadth and reliance across suppliers.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of critical components/services that have two or more qualified suppliers, the distribution of supplier spend across suppliers (top-5/top-10), and the count of single-source or sole-source suppliers for items designated critical.
data_signals: catalog of critical components/services mapped to supplier IDs
supplier qualification records (capability/qualification timestamps)
supplier count per component/service (number of qualified suppliers)
supplier spend by component and overall (top-N spend ratios)
procurement sourcing decisions and alternate-sourcing plans with dates
onboarding time and readiness records for alternate suppliers
evidence_strength_indicators: High strength: authoritative product-to-supplier mapping plus supplier qualification records and procurement tender artifacts showing alternates.
Weak strength: informal lists or claims of 'diversity' without qualification records or spend distribution data.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Diverse Supply Base' indicates active measures to prevent single-point supplier concentration, a preventive objective.

SR-3(1).differentiation_note: SR-3(1) specifically measures supplier diversification (alternate sourcing and concentration metrics) rather than general contract coverage or incident response capability.

SR-3(2).measurement_goal: Measure the organization's ability to limit harm from supplier compromise by tracking supplier-related incident containment performance (time-to-contain, containment success rate), percent of critical suppliers with tested containment/segmentation plans, and counts of mitigation actions executed against supplier-origin incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Limitation-of-harm is corrective/mitigative in nature; metrics that show containment rates, escalation and mitigation counts, and MTTR trends (rates) most directly evidence effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Rate and timing of containment actions for supplier-origin incidents: percent of supplier incidents contained within target time, mean/median time-to-contain (detection→containment), count of supplier-related mitigations (isolation, access revocation) and percent of critical suppliers with documented containment/segmentation plans and test records.
data_signals: incident ticket records with attribution flags for supplier involvement (timestamps for detection, containment, remediation)
